WHERE THE OLD FRIENDS MEET.

Affectionately inscribed to Mrs. Flizabeth Howard on her
	birthday, by her friend EMILY H. WOODMANSEE

		Cheering as the green oasis,
			Is this social treat,
		Pleasant in our recollection
		Is this place of our affection,
	 		Where the old friends meet.

		Dearer still the old friends' faces
			All so true and sweet;
		Like some loved, continued story
		Is the very lanscape glory,
			Where the old friends meet.

		Friends that in the pleasant sunshine,
			Friends that in the shade,
		With unselfish, loving kindness,
		And a hearty, willing mindness,
			Lent us timely aid.

		Friends like these who can forget them.
			Tho' seas rolled between?
		Recollection answers Never!
		Memory shall repeat forever
			What their love has been.

		But no gulf is now between us,
			Face to face we greet,
		One by one each hand is shaken,
		Nothing seems at all forsaken,
			Where the old friends meet.

		Gratefully my heart desireth
			Shall desire for aye,
		That of joy, our kind host's measure,
		May be equal to the pleasure,
			Felt by us to-day.

		Many years, may yet your birthday.
			Be with joy replete.
		Till old age—all ripe and hoary–
		Crowns you with a fitting glory,
			Where the old friends meet.
July 12, 1876.


TO MY FRIEND MRS. HOWARD, ON
		HER BIRTHDAY.

	Again your natal day awakes my lyre,
		And bids me sing
	A song of genuine friendship unto thee,
		Which here I bring.

	The harp within my heart of thousand strings
		Is tuned for thee!
	The dispason clear and full rings out
		To you from me.

	How many years have fled since first we met,
		In thrilling scenes!
	And memory indellby has left
		Her waking dreams!

	The vision yet in all these years fades not,
		I see as clear
	All that then I saw—all I then beheld.
		As tho' 'twere near.

	Since then the sea of life has o'er us rolled,
		Wave upon wave;
	But through God's help we've stemmed the rolling tide,
		HE stood to save.

	Yes, let it go—the stream of life flows on
		With noiseless roll,
	And day by day, and night by night, we do approach
		The final goal!

	But from the subject that I had in hand
		I have digressed—
	To wish you all that's good on this bright day,
		To wish yon blessed

	With all your heart desires, is what I wish;
		I full well know 
	You nothing crave but that a Saint may ask
		Or God bestow.

	Then be you blessed my sister ever kind,
		And with pure health;
	For health of body, and a mind that's sound;
		Is untold wealth.

	And if some wish above all others dwells within
		That heart of thine,
	Then may kind Heaven that boon on you bestow.
		So prays my rhyme.

							HANNAH T. KING.


A WEDDING TRIP.—Stumbling over the
bride's train.﻿


For the Exponent.
				TO HAL.

	And you are a school-teacher! you have
my sympathy, if you are indeed one of
that patiently suffering and imposed-upon
class, who plod steadily on, year after year,
and endure the whips and scorn of an out-
rageous fortune." But even this is better
than to fly to others that we know not of."
	But what could lead you to think that
you are older than I am, I can't imagine.
	You must be anti-quated, indeed! or are
you uncle-quated?
	Now don't presume to dictate to your
grandmother. Older than I indeed! I flat-
ter myself that there are few teachers in
this vicinity who possess a more profound
and extensive stock of information on the
subject of tea. (abbreviation of teaching,)
t[-]an I do.
	On the wall, just opposite to me, I see this
motto: "Be not wise in thine own eyes."
I read it with complacency, knowing that it
cannot apply to me. And now as an illus-
tration of the vast experience I have had, I
will just give you a piece of advice, which,
if you will follow you will find of great ben-
efit in every-day, practical work in the
school-room.
	For example: Two of your pupils come
to you with an apple—or some other edible,
perhaps a fine spring onion—each declaring
that it is his very own.
	Now how are you to decide the question
of which it belongs to? It is simply an im-
possibility. It may belong to both. Under
these trying circumstances, assume your
most severe and dignified manner, look
them steadily in the eye at the same time,
calmly take the disputed property and eat
it yourself. Then in a stern voice bid your
pupils return to their books. This will in-
spire confidence on their part, in your supe-
rior wisdom. They will at once see the jus-
tice of your action, and will undoubtedly
retire subdued to tears by this impartial
act of yours, and spend the rest of the day
in vain regrets for the lost apple. I am
sorry to say I have never read "Hannah
Jane." Judging from what I have read of
"Nasby's" writings it is rather on the con-
cise order like the "Widow Bedott's.
	You remember the poetry she made eu-
logizing the deacon, while she was sitting
under the spreading branches of an old
tree, and repeated them loud enough to be
heard by the deacon who was sitting on the
other side of the tree, preparing his ser-
mon for the coming sabbath. But of course
she didn't know it.
	Of course it is amusing to read such poe-
try, but one could scarcely become so
deeply engrossed in it as to become obliv-
ious to all mundane affairs.
	But really, it is almost too sultry to-day
to express an opinion on any subject. In
fact it fatigues me to write, so I shall short-
en this article, (it is slightly mixed, but all
owing to this extremely disagreeable weath-
er) by dropping part of my long name, and
sign myself, simply—
						OLD MAID.
	P. S. Although I have not the mascu-
line failing of curiosity, I should really like
to see you, just for fun.
[handwritten] Augusta Winters	O.M.


WOMAN'S VOICE.

		MILL CREEK WARD, July 14, 1876.
EDITOR EXPONENT:	Mill Creek Ward Relief Society is in a
flourishing condition and under the guid-


ance of the Bishop of the Ward, Reuben
Miller. Mrs. Mary McAllister, President,
Sisters Elizabeth Park and Ann Morgan,
Counselors, Sister Elizabeth Carlyle, Secre-
tary, Sister Orice Miller, Treasurer. Our
President is a woman that is alive to her
duty at all times, in looking after the wants
of the poor and needy; the Society owns a
good meeting house well furnished and car-
peted, and withal, very comfortable.
	I believe the meetings to be productive
of much good, and edifying to the sisters
who attend them; there are some of the
sisters who have been baptized for their
dead and they rejoice in the great and
glorious principles that have been revealed
in these the latter days; how great, grand
and noble the work of God is.
	Oh, let us try and be faithful and do our
part; we can help much. We want salva-
tion, and with the help of Israel's God we
will persevere by walking in the fear of the
Lord. Our enemies are assiduous in their
labors; they make a bugbear of Polygamy,
and I believe "a gnat would choke them
while a camel would ne'er be felt."
	I think if our would-be-friends would
look at home they would need to blush for
shame, instead of coming among this peo-
ple to reform them.
	The song of Polygamy has been sung so
much that it has become quite stale; some-
thing new by way of variety would be a
change. There is one thing they have got
to learn yet, and that is, "two are better than
one, and a three-fold cord is not quickly
broken." We do not believe that Polygamy
detracts from a woman's sphere, we can but
believe that it is Monogamy that degrades
her in this generation; we believe in Wo-
man's Rights, and it is her right to have a
husband and a home; we think there are
not many women that would refuse this
heaven-born gift; but it is truth we are
seeking, it is truth we love for itself alone
and truth we intend to follow. She needs
no breastworks to shield herself behind, but
proudly stands upon her own merits.

	"Oh! say what is truth, 'tis the fairest gem
	To which mortals or gods can aspire;
	And priceless the value of truth will be
	When the proud monarch's costliest diadem
	Is counted but dross and refuse."

				Respectfully,
									I. P. W.


WOMAN'S WOUNDED AFFECTIONS.

	Washington Irving wrote:—"As the
dove will clap its wings to its side and cov-
er and conceal the arrow that is prey-
ing on its vitals, so it is the nature of
woman to hide from the world the
pangs of wounded affection. With her the
desire of the heart has failed. The great
charm of existence is at an end. She neg-
lects all the cheerful exercises that gladden
the spirits, quicken the pulse and send the
tide of life in healthful currents through
the veins. Her rest is broken; the sweet
refreshment of sleep is poisoned by melan-
choly dreams; "dry sorrow drinks her
blood," until her feeble frame sinks under
the last external assailant. Look for her af-
ter a while and you find friendship weeping
over her untimely grave wondering that
one who but lately glowed with all the ra-
diance of health and beauty should now be
brought down to "darkness and the worm."
You will be told of some wintry chill,
some slight indisposition that laid her low
but no one knows the mental malady that
previously sapped her strength and made
her so easy a prey to the spoiler."

SALT LAKE CITY, AUGUST 1, 1876,


HOME HAPPINESS.

	There are some sweet words which never be-
come hackneyed, however much they may be
used; and HOME is one of them. "Home!"
"Our home!" But how do we understand the
term? Should the habitation in which jar-
ring, unkind expressions are common, where
love and confidence and tender sympathies are
wanting, be known by that sacred name? We
must admit that the dear word is too often thus
profaned. But as we take it, home means a
place of enduring cheer. A place of rest, of
quiet repose for the care-worn and weary. A
place of social freedom and gladness which
can never be found outside the hallowed pre-
cincts, in the cold, carless world.
	It is not perfect home if the strength and
vigor of manhood, with its protecting, furnish-
ing care be wanting; or if the calm, pure in-
fluence of womanly grace and gentleness be
not there; or lacking the sweet music of child-
ren's voices. But all these powers must be de-
lightfully and harmoniously blended to make
a home complete.
	Then there are certain individual character-
istics to be possessed by each member of a fam-
ily, and certain household rules to be observed,
which combine to make home what it should
be, really the dearest, brightest, happiest
place on earth. Among the very first princi-
ples to be inculcated, should be strict honesty
and industry. Where these two important
items are justly regarded, it is strange if all
the other pleasing virtues do not shine forth
with their proper lustre. Honesty and indus-
try are surely the founders of cheerfulness and
of order; they have much to do in a general
way, with temperance and with all the devo-
tional emotions. The enjoyment of all real
pleasure depends greatly upon the possession
and use of these principles.
	No bread is so sweet as that which is earned
by honest industry, no sleep so refreshing
as that won by the same blessed medium.
The boy who is sent to a "free school" and has
no concern about the finding of board and
clothes, can never prize the blessings of an ed-
ucation so fully as the one who pays the world
for all he receives of it by his own honest
labor. Says a great philosopher of the present
age, "we have a nation of thieves and murder-
ers. And why is this? Is it the man who is
raised a farmer that becomes a thief and a
murderer? No! Is it the one who has been
raised a mechanic or a mason? No! Such
men can find honest employment by which to
earn a living. But those who are sent to 'free
schools," and are then turned out upon the world
knowing nothing of work or how to sustain
themselves honestly, are the men who enter


into intrigues and commit crimes as a means
of obtaining a livlehood."
	Such statements as the above could not be
truly made of our nation had the homes
of the United States, for the past cen-
tury, exhibited the respect and honor which
should be shown to the great principles of hon-
esty and industry. The home of the truly in-
dustrious is generally the home of true happi-
ness, and such should be the home of every
Latter-day Saint.
				[handwritten] E. B. Wells


A WORD TO THE WISE IS
			SUFFICIENT.

	There are some convenient places for listening
to gossip, where you may have to near it if you
are pleased or otherwise, and all present can
put their own construction upon it, and make
of it perhaps a pretty heavy investment in
newspaper columns; it is strange that people
should forget when they are in certain public
places that there is anybody there except
themselves, those in whom they are interested,
or to whom they are addressing their conver-
sation. They will tell all their family affairs,
their neighbors affairs, and in fact, you can
hear almost anything about yourself or your
friends in street cars, or railroad cars etc; And
afterwards when somebody writes it and makes
a mountain out of the mole hill—these very
persons lift up their hands in astonishment
and wonder how they got the leading items.
That people talk too much is a very common
saying; certainly they should be careful how
they talk, but there is one other important
consideration and that is WHERE THEY TALK.
	We were on the point of asking a lady the
other day whether she imagined herself trav-
eling in her own family carriage, so freely did
she proclaim her domestic troubles and those
of her: neighbors to the occupants of the street
car.
	The opinions of people are various and it is
scarcely to be expected that a class of persons
gathered from different parts of the communi-
ty, and previously from different parts of the
world, and from all classes of society, should
draw the same, or even similar inferences
from a casual conversation. Then there is
in the organization, of perhaps a majority of
people at the present day a proneness to ex-
aggeration, and all these things considered is
it any wonder that people's private affairs are
often made public, and so re-constructed and
highly colored as to assume an altogether un-
familiar bearing? People who profess to live
conscientiously and to be guided by the spirit
of truth should certainly try to check any
spirit or influence of evil, and live in a differ-
ent element. And if people will talk scandal
or gossip let such understand they can have it
all to themselves.


[Column 1]
			HOME AFFAIRS.

	MRS. ELISE M. Shipp. who has been East
recently, studying in the Woman's Medical Col-
lege in Philadelphia, has opened a series of
lectures to the medical class of ladies in this
city, who have been engaged in the study of
medicine and obstetrics for some time. Dr. S.
B. Young has taken great pains in lecturing to
this class for a long time. He has labored with
a great deal of zeal and perseverance to give
them all the information possible in courses of
lectures. They commence every Saturday
morning at ten o'clock at the Office of Wo-
man's Exponent. Mrs. Shipp's lectures will
be given immediately after, from eleven to

[Column 2]
twelve. To all the sisters who are seeking
information in this branch of science, a gener-
al invitation is given.
	We publish below a brief synopsis of Mrs.
Shipp's Introductory lecture.
	She opens her lecture by soliciting the
leniency of those to whom she is speaking in
consideration of her inexperience, but is most
anxious if she possesses any knowledge which
will benefit them to aid them in the pursuit
they have undertaken and help to lighten their
tasks, if she can do this effectually, then she
will have gained her object. After speaking
at some length on the subject of medicine and
the qualifications necessary for the attainment
of any proficiency in the science, she says, "es-
pecially with us as a people there should be no
such word as 'fail," for in addition to the nat-
ural capabilities of mind, we have to aid us
faith and the spirit of revelation." She then
points out a few of the necessary pre-requisites
to the successful student of medicine.
	First a natural love for the profession, both
theoretically and practically.
	Second, a strong will and firm determina-
tion of purpose that no common obstacle can
hinder.
	Third, The nerves should be strong, with
power to control the emotional nature.
	Fourth, The physical system in the highest
state of healthful perfection.
	Fifth, Suitable text-books.
	Sixth, Competent teachers.
	Seventh, Clinical advantages.
	Eighth, Undisturbed time.
	These are only a few she thinks, yet they are
the most essential. Love for the profession,
she places at the head of the list, believing it
to be of the greatest importance, knowing that
love for any object or pursuit, is the best incen-
tive for earnest work and application. She
points out the many attractions the study pre-
sents to those who desire to gain truth and
light and to feast their own souls, and then
adds that to benefit and bless our fellow crea-
tures, is the greatest and grandest aim
of mortals; that in God's kingdom there
should be no thought of self-aggrandizement,
but the welfare of His people.
	She advises those who enter upon this or any
other field of labor to fortify themselves
against all interference and discouraging influ-
ences, and after saying many plain and excel-
lent things with regard to this, she says: "that
with a resolute will and faith in God, none can
surpass the women of Utah whom the world
pities so supremely." She has said so many
good things that it is hard work to cull out the
main points, but we have not space to publish
more than these few items, but will add her
closing remarks.
	"The teacher in any phase of life, assumes
great responsibility, for it is no small undertak-
ing to attempt to express ideas in the most for-
cible manner and present them to others in
that light which will soonest illuminate their
minds. Especially is it so when our inter-
views are limited to one a week. It has been a
subject of much thought and reflection with
me, how and what to give you that will prove
most beneficial; I desire to give you ideas thatwill prove the greatest practical benefit. The
long, hard names in Anatomy, in fact the dull
abstract ideas in any branch given as mere
facts only, with which to tax the memory
will prove of very little utility in your pres-
ent circumstances.

EDITOR EXPONENT:
	After spending many days at the great Cen-
tenial Exhibition in Philadelphia, of course
admiring the dif[--]rent specimens of the inven-


tive genius of ages, I was much surprised on
visining the Centennial Exhibition on Main
St. Salt Lake City, to find very many articles
comparing favorably with the best specimens
on exhibiiion at Philadelphia, though formed
out of the elements here in Utah. I have not
time to describe one of a hundred of these
truly fine and finished specimens of Utah in-
dustry, but would recommend any one doubt-
ing the comparison to go and see.
						W. O. CLARK.
	Ogden, July 17, 1876.
	"THE LIFE of Brigham Young, or Utah and
Her Founders," by E. W. Tullidge, is now com-
pleted and it is expected it will be for sale this
present month. We have had the pleasure
(through the courtesy of the author) to peruse
many of the proof sheets, and we believe it
to be a work that will counteract much of the 
prejudice now existing in regard to this peo-
ple. "One story is good, until another is told"
is an old adage, and Mr. Tullidge: has plainly
delineated the other side of the dark represen-
tations now extant concerning this people.
To those who really want to know the truth
we would recommend its perusal.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.	37


OBITUARIES.

				TOOELE CITY, July 27th, 1876.
	SISTER REBECCA DEW, wife of Elder Thomas
Dew, departed this life the 24th of June, 1876.
She was born Feb. 15th, 1812, in Wiltshire,
England, was first baptized in 1843, emigrated
to Nauvoo in the spring of 1844, and was driv
en out of Nauvoo with the rest of the Saints in
1846; she was faithful and patient in all
the trials she was called to pass through.
In 1852 she removed to Tooele, where she re-
sided until her death. She suffered many years
with a cancer in her breast, and for the last ten
months was confined more or less to her room.
She lived and died in full faith of the Gospel.
Sister Drew was second counselor in our Relief
Society, and rejoiced very much in the society
of the Saints and the principles of the gospel.
She was always on hand in every duty or en-
terprise necessary for the building up of the
kingdom of God. A large procession of the
members of the Relief Society followed the re-
mains to the cemetery. and in accordance with
her request, that beautiful hymn of Miss E. R.
Snow's' "Oh My Father," was sung over the
grave, and she was laid away in peace to await
the morning of the resurrection.
									COM.


HARRIET McEVER, born on the Isle of Mott,
Lake Champlain, State of Vermont, Sept. 3d,
1809; was married in 1828 to Horace Roberts in
the State of Illinois. Was baptized into the
Church of Latter-day Saints in 1838 with her
husband. They joined the Saints at Nauvoo,
1839, where they lived until their expulsion.
She came with them through all their subse-
quent journeyings. She came to Utah in 1850,
and settled in Provo, was one of the first set-
tlers. She passed through many trials. Indian
wars, grasshopper wars, and all the privations
attendant on the early settlement of this
country.	Her husband died Dec. 25, 1858, af-
ter an illness of nearly three years. She rais-
ed nine children, six of whom are now living.
	She died Feb. 27th, 1876, after an illness of
fourteen months, with tumor in the stomach.
	She was a woman of iutelligence and good
judgment, loved and respected by all who
knew her, a kind friend and faithful wife and
mother, charitable and upright in all her in-


tercourse, ever ready to minister to the sick,
or comfort the sorrowing. She was humble in
prosperity and uncomplaining in adversity.
She died in full faith of the Gospel and in
hopes of a glorious resurrection.
							M. J. T.
	Provo, July 11th, 1876.

	AT OXFORD, Oneida Co., Idaho, Julia,
daughter of Martha Eliza, and Benjamin
Boice, died May 11th, 1876, of consumption,
aged 16 years, 1 month, and two days. Her
sister, Alnora Eliza, died June 11th, 1876, of
the same disease, aged 8 years and 28 days.
	Their mother died of consumption three
years prior to their death.
							M. A. BOICE.


HOME AFFAIRS.

	WE HAD a very pleasant call on Saturday
last from Mrs. A. J. Dunniway, Editor of
the "New North West," Portland, Oregon;
this lady has been well known to the public
for some years as a lecturer, an author, and
an editor. She is on her way to Philadel-
phia to deliver a centennial address at the
Woman's Convention, on taxation without
representation.


THE CELEBRATION JUBILEE.

	The Twenty-Fourth of July, 1876, the
anniversary of the day on which the pio-
neers entered this valley, was celebrated at
the New Tabernacle by the citizens of this
city. The audience was immense, number-
ing, it was thought, between nine and ten
thousand people. The Sunday School chil-
dren, probably about four thousand, occu-
pied the central part of the building. The
opening prayer was offered by Elder John
Taylor. Prof. C. J. Thomas led the singing
by the children, who sang first, "In our
lovely Deseret." Elder Orson Pratt was
the first speaker: he gave a historical sketch
of the entrance of the pioneers into this
valley in 1847. John G. Midgley delivered
an appropriate address, mentioning the
many hardships of the first pioneers of the
country, of the progress which had been
made, of the duties of this generation born
and reared under these circumstances; of
the evil influences to be avoided by the
young, and the responsibility which must
eventually rest upon them. While Mr.
Midgley was speaking, President Brigham
Young, the Chief of the pioneers and the
honored and esteemed leader of the Latter-
day Saints, entered the Tabernacle, and
after listening for some time to the music
and singing, he arose and addressed the con-
gregation in an affecting and eloquent man-
ner. At the conclusion he blessed the peo-
ple in the name of Jesus Christ, and by the
authority of the right and privilege assigned
him by the Holy Priesthood. The entire
proceedings were characteristic of the occa-
sion, and those who arranged the affair in
so short a time deserve the thanks of the
community.

R. S. REPORTS.

PARIS, BEAR LAKE CO., IDAHO,
	June 11th, 1876.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	The Young Ladies Retrenchment Asso-
ciation of Paris was organized Feb. 19th,
1876, by Mrs. M. I. Horne. The officers
are as follows:

[COLUMN 2]

	Mrs. Deseret Rich, President,
	"	Caroline Humphreys,
	"	Harriet Williams,
	"	Adaline Passey,		Counselors.
	"	Anna Mowry,
	Miss Caddie Rich,
	"	Phebe Rich,
	"	Ursulia Pomeroy, Secretary.
	We hold our meetings once in three
weeks; they are always well attended, and
a good spirit prevails; all the young ladies
take a lively interest in the society. We
select pieces from the EXPONENT and other
good papers and books and read them which
proves to be a great benefit to us all. The
sisters of the Relief Society meet with us
occasionally, also President Rich and Bishop
Budge, they give counsel and advice which
is very encouraging. I really think that
most of the young ladies take more pleas-
ure in attending their Retrenchment meet-
ings than they do when attending balls or
theatres. For the past winter round-danc-
ing has been entirely abandoned in this
place; they had been favorite dances with
the young folks, it was hard for them to give
them up entirely at first; but the knowl-
edge that we were obeying counsel amply
repaid us for any regrets we might have
felt.
	We shall always remember Sister Horne
with respect and gratitude for her kind in-
structions and good advice while here, for
she truly had the interest and welfare of
the young at heart. The EXPONENT has
the best wishes of our society for its suc-
cess.
	URSULIA POMEROY, Sec'y.

	Eighth Annual Meeting of Sixteenth
Ward Relief Society.
	Sixteenth Ward School Rooms, S. L.
City, June 15, 1876. Meeting opened by
singing Arise O glorious Zion—Prayer by
Bishop F. G. Kesler. Singing, The Spirit
of God—Minutes of the previous meeting
read and accepted. Annual report as fol-
lows; beginning June 15, 1875, ending June
15th 1876:
Cash received............................. $ 106 75
Mdse. and produce.....................	69 75
Disbursements to poor, cash......	61 75
Mdse. and produce.....................	57 25
	Many good instructions were given and
Dianna Reid was ordained President of the
Society as we had been called to part with
our former President by death. Benedic-
tion by Geo. C. Riser.
	The ladies of the Relief Society had pre-
pared a splendid supper, and the young
ladies of the Retrenchment Association
waited on the tables; over one hundred par-
took of the supper, and it was indeed a
feast of spiritual as well as temporal food.
	Donated by the Society commencing June
15, 1868, ending June 15, 1876, $2000,47,-
95.
	PRESENDIA L. KIMBALL.
		Sec'y.
	Since our Annual Meeting we have had
a Widow's Party at the Sixteenth Ward
School Rooms; we gathered all the widows
in the ward and had a pleasant interview.
Bishop Kesler addressed us and we would
his words were written, for they were in-
deed like jewels. After we had partaken of
refreshments and all had been bountifully
supplied we scattered our picnic among the
poor of the ward, there were many baskets
full and it was a day of rejoicing for all
We were indeed much blessed in coming
together, and felt we were mutually bene-
fitted by each other's society.
	M. R. THOMPSON.


38		WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
			CORRESPONDENCE.

		SYDNEY, NEW SOUTH WALES,
			AUSTRALIA, May 27th, 1876.
MRS. EMMELINE WELLS:
		MY DEAR FRIEND AND SISTER.
	Your welcome and very interesting let-
ter of Feb. 19th came to hand on the 6th
inst., but not in time for me to reply by re-
turn mail, as the mail steamer for San Fran-
cisco left Sydney the same day.
	I tender you many thanks for your kind
token of remembrance to me in this dis-
tant land where amid strangers "my pil-
grim feet now roam," and where I am dil-
igently laboring to dispel the sable clouds
of ignorance, bigotry, superstition and er-
ror from the minds of a people who seem to
be fettered in the galling chains of Priest-
craft, and who, judging from my stand-
point, are living without light, liberty or
God. From what I had read, and pre-
viously heard of Australia, I had formed a
very favorable opinion of the people, the
climate, the productions and the general
character and resources of these colonies,
and when the S. S. Colima entered the Syd-
ney harbor, which, I believe is acknowl-
edged to be the finest—(except one—in the
world,) I felt that nothing which I had ever
read or heard of Australia, had been exagg-
erated or in the least overdrawn.
	It was on the evening of Dec. 25th, the
sea during the day had been unusually
rough, and the steamer had been rolling and
plunging amoug the huge billows at a fear-
ful rate, so much so, indeed, as to awaken
within me such kind and benevolent feel-
ings towards all animated things in gener-
al, and towards the inhabitants of the
ocean in particular, that I gave them every
morsel of my Christmas dinner, and tried
hard to get up my boots to throw over-
board. Under these circumstances we
passed the "Light-house," situated on the
perpendicular rocks at the entrance of
Port Jackson, about eight miles from Syd-
ney. After passing "Heads," as these
rocks are denominated, and on entering the
bay the sea suddenly became calm, the ves-
sel ceased to roll, and everything around
seemed transformed as if by magic, to the
most peaceful tranquility, and heavenly se-
renity; the gentle zephyrs like angels of
peace, laden with sweetest fragrance, from
Oleander and Orange groves, came out to
meet us, as if to congratulate us upon our
safe arrival, and bid us welcome to the
spicy shores of Australia. And as the beau-
tiful "Colima," like a great white swan,
glided majestically up this lovely bay, and
the bright lights gleamed from the villages,
which lay nestled among the hills on either
side, and coupled with this enchanting
scenery, the cheering thought, that my
wearisome, unpleasant, and monotonous
journey from San Francisco, over the great
Pacific Ocean, was at an end, and that with-
in a few hours, I should once more, set my
feet on "Terra Firma," presented to my mind
the most bewitchingly beautiful, and lovely
picture that I had ever before contempla-
ted; and my soul was filled with love and
admiration for Sydney, and for several hours
of that night, I felt as the Queen of Sheba
must have felt, when visiting and behold-
ing the greatness and glory of Solomon. I
really felt that the half had not been told of
this beautiful and wonderful country, But
every picture hath its light and shade,
"sunshine and shadow" are the characteris-
tics of this life, and I have always endeav-
or to look at the bright side of every pic-
ture for I experience more pleasure in look-

[Column 2]
ing at the silver lining of the clouds than
in contemplating the dark shadows beyond
that lovely lining; hence, my favorable im-
pressions relative to Australia. But a few
weeks residence in Sydney, changed very
materially my ideas of these colonies.
	The country suffered during seven or
eight months of the past year, from a most
blighting and destructive drouth, very lit-
tle rain having fallen during that period,
consequently, so far as the vegetable pro-
ductions of the country are concerned I saw
everything at a great disadvantage, and for
this I am inclined to make due and full al-
lowance; but I have looked, and still look,
but in vain, for the bright side of the
lovely Sydney picture I fancied, before
treading on Australian soil; for if there be
but little good, I would like to find that
little, and in the language of the Poet—
"Speak of all the best I can," and in jus-
tice to the colony of New South Wales, I
will say that I have seen many things here
to admire. The safe and beautiful Harbor,
the Post Office and many other fine build-
ings, the Public Parks, the Botanical Gar-
dens, the free Public Library and Reading
Rooms, and the excellent condition of the
public roads, are all worthy of commenda-
tion. Many tropical fruits and flowers at-
tain here (in ordinary seasons) great perfec-
tion, and I have found some kind, intelligent
and hospitable people in and around Syd-
ney. The women here as a general rule, are
superior to the men both in looks and intel-
lectual development and culture. There
are many handsome and intelligent women
in Sydney, but I cannot fully agree with
what is frequently said of them, "that for
superior beauty and accomplishments, they
are pre-eminent."
	Now a few words in relation to the dark
shadows which I am compelled to see, not-
withstanding my natural inclination to look
on the other side. The "soocial status" of
the citizens of this city, I cannot but re-
gard as most deplorable; drunkenness pre-
vails to an alarming degree, and what makes
this debasing practice doubly dangerous
and demoralizing, is the fact, that women
as well as men, are addicted to it. I have
seen more drunken women in one day in
Sydney than I ever saw in Salt Lake City,
during my residence there of twenty-one
years. Nearly every corner house in this
city, is a liquor saloon, where spirituous and
fermented liquors are retailed; and the ven-
ders of this destructive beverage, in nearly
all cases are women, and the wages of a
"Bar-maid" are generally graded or regula-
ted, by her personal beauty or attractions;
the finest looking girls, demand and re-
ceive the highest wages. Domestic infelic-
ity here is the rule, and peaceful, happy
homes the exception. Wife beating, is a
common practice among the "lords of crea-
tion" in this land; many men (in form,) in-
dulge in this elevating practice with as lit-
tle compunctions of conscience apparently,
as an intelligent father would gently chide
his child with words of admonition and
counsel.
	Such is the state of Sydney morals, and
society, and yet, they will prate about Utah
and the Mormons, and Priests and people
will roll their eyes, and lift their hands in
holy horror, while contemplating the awful
oppression and galling slavery of the wo-
men of Salt Lake City; whose condition in
reality, and temporal blessings compared
with the women of Sydney, are as light to
darkness, or as heaven to hell, to say noth-
ing of the hopes of a glorious resurrection,
eternal unions beyond the grave, and ever-
lasting glory and exaltation in the pres-

[Column 3]
ence of God our heavenly and eternal Fa-
ther.
	God bless you my dear Sister, and aid
you in your laudable efforts to disseminate
through the medium of the EXPONENT
those principles and doctrines, which when
understood and observed, will redeem men
and women from the thraldom of sin and
oppression, will banish evil, the bane of
society, from the human heart, will enable
God's children dwelling on the earth to at-
tain to the highest type of manhood and
womanhood, and will usher in that happy
day, when one law will bind all nations,
kindred, tongues and people, and that law
will be the law of universal Brotherhood,
then will the earth be prepared for Jesus
our elder brother and Savior, whose right it
is to come and reign King of Nations as
He now reigns King of Saints.
	I am as ever, your friend and brother, in
the Gospel of Peace,
						ISAAC GROO,
	P. S. Remember me kindly and in love
to your family and inquiring friends, and
accept my sincere thanks for the EXPO-
NENT.
							I. G.


A MOTHER'S LESSON TO YOUNG
LADIES UPON DRESS.

	The rich dress and costly ornaments that
become maturer life, and ceremonious par-
ties in large cities, are unsuited to the very
young, who need no such "foreign aid."
Some persons toil early and late and strain
every nerve, to procure an expensive gar-
ment, and think, that once arrayed in it,
they will look as well as some richer neigh-
bor, whose style of dress they wish to im-
itate; but they forget, that if it does not ac-
cord with their general style of living, if it
is out of harmony with other things, it will
so strike everybody, and this want of fit-
ness will prevent it looking well on them.
Let a true sense of propriety of the fitness
of things regulate all your habits of living
and dressing; and it will produce such a
beautiful harmony and consistency of char-
acter, as will throw a charm around you
that all will feel, though few may compre-
hend. Always consider well whether the
articles of dress which you wish to pur-
chase are suited to your age, your condi-
tion, your means; the climate, to the par-
ticular use to which you mean to put them;
and then let the principles of good taste
keep you from the extremes of the fashion,
and regulate the form, so as to combine util-
ity and beauty, while the known rules of
harmony in colors save you from shocking
the eye of the artist with incongruous mix-
tures.
	The agreeable effect that all wish to pro-
duce in the eyes of others, depends much
more on just, proportions in the parts, thus
forming a pleasing whole, than on little or-
namental additions, which, though pretty
in themselves, add nothing to the general
effect. Thus in making a pelisse, it is all-
important that the cape should be of the
right size and shape, to agree well with the
sleeves and back, and that the collar should
be well proportioned to the cape; but it mat-
ters very little whether it have one or two
rouleaus of satin or velvet around it, or
whether it have none; and so it is with a
thousand other little particulars, which take
much time in the making and add much to
the expense, without really adding any-
thing to the general good appearance. It is
a happy thing for the diffusion of good
taste, that it may, in many things, be cul-
tivated without any pecuniary expense. The


[COLUMN 1]

price of colored muslin or printed calico, is
the same whether the figure be pretty or
ugly; the ribbon that is tastefully disposed
upon the bonnet, costs no more than the
one that is ill arranged. The shawl is the
same in value whether it is dragged round
the shoulders like an Indian's blanket, or
worn in graceful folds. It costs no more to
buy colors that harmonize than those that
do not. Indeed, true taste will generally
be found on the side of economy, because
simplicity is;—the first cost is thus lessen-
ed, and garments that are really well shap-
ed, are longer in favor. The exercise of
good taste, therefore, need not be consider-
ed the privilege of the few; it may regulate
the toilet of her who earns what she ex-
pends upon it, as well as for her whose bills
are paid by a rich father; the more it is
studies the more good sense and simplicity
will be consulted, because these are includ-
ed in the principles of true taste.
	Whilst taste may be made to regulate the
dress of all, even of the working classes,
wealth, unaccompanied by it, cannot com-
mand its beautiful results. Your clothes
may be ordered of the best dressmaker, and
everything you wear may be of the most
costly materials, and in the latest Paris
fashion; and yet you may spoil your appear-
ance by your manner of putting them on,
and by wearing those articles together
which do not accord. The most expensive
dresses at a ball are seldom those that pro-
duce the best effect; and nothing so effect-
ually defeats its object, as an excess of or-
nament. Some persons seem to have an
inherent love of finery, and seem to cling
to it pertinaciously, even when their under-
standings are convinced that it is repug-
nant to the feelings of refined minds, and
that it is a trait common to all barbarous
tribes: they cannot reason upon their pref-
erences—they can only say, that what oth-
ers condemn as tawdry, looks pretty to
them. This persuasion generally takes
place very early, and is much to be regret-
ed, as it prevents the growth of purer prin-
ciples. We have often thought that the
very bad taste in which dolls are usually
dressed, may have something to do with
this early love of finery. Children have of-
ten a real affection for their puppets; and
when they are bedizened in all the colours
of the rainbow, and decked in all the odds
and ends of finery that can be stuck upon
them, the little dears learn, by this associa-
tion, to love this tawdry ornament: whereas
a well-dressed doll would have an import-
ant influence in establishing a correct taste
in the mind of the child. We once knew a
family where the dolls were all very neatly
dressed, like babies and little children, and
not as fine ladies, on purpose to make them
a more rational and useful source of amuse-
ment; and we would beg all young ladies
who dress dolls for little girls, to do it in
such a manner as not to foster a love of fin-
ery.
	We have heard a mother who guarded
her daughters against this bad taste by mak-
ing it one of their childish punishments to
weak a very tawdry cap full of feathers, and
flowers, and bows of ribbons of all colours.
Judging by what we sometimes see worn
by grown up people, we might suppose that
such a cap had been their reward in child-
hood rather than their punishment, and was
thus recommended to their best affections.
The love of finery is rarely cured, and forms
an insurmountable obstacle to the cultiva-
tion of pure taste. Whosoever is conscious
of possessing it, ought to mistrust her own
judgment in matters of taste, and be will-
ing to take the advice of others. No plain-

[COLUMN 2]

ness of dress can ever be construed to your
disadvantage; but ornamental additions,
which in their best state are a very doubt-
ful good, become a positive evil when de-
faced, or soiled, or tumbled. Shabby feath-
ers, and crushed or faded artificial flowers,
are an absolute disgrace to a lady's appear-
ance; while their total absence would never
be remarked. Next to soiled stockings,
soiled bonnet-caps are the most offensive
things in a young lady's dress; and the lat-
ter are but too common. Whatever ap-
proaches the face should be particularly
clean; and it is better always to wear those
materials which will wash than to use silk
net after it is at all sullied by wear. Color-
ed gauze hankerchiefs, that are worn till
they are faded, or sullied, or the color
changed by perspiration, are a disagreeable
sight, and will spoil the neat appearance of
a person, however well dressed otherwise;
cleanliness being the first requisite in a
lady's dress.
	Such qualities of mind are brought into
action in connection with dress that we
cannot wonder at its having great influence
on the opinions that are formed of us. Who,
that sees a young lady very carefully array-
ed at a ball, and finds her when at home,
and not expecting company, in a torn or
soiled dress, can fail to draw conclusions un-
favorable to the character of the individual?
If you have a friend who has lately be-
come insolvent, and you see his daughters
appear, soon after, in new and costly gar-
ments, what are your feelings? Is not all
admiration of their costume lost, in the
sense of its betraying a want of common
honesty, thus to spend the money that be-
longs to their father's creditors? And does
not this exhibition of lax principles do
them more harm than any personal decora-
tion can do them good? Dress is a very fair
index of a young woman's neatness, indus-
try, economy, good sense, modesty, and
good taste; and she, who is at all times, in
her private as well as in her public hours,
perfectly well dressed, according to all that
we include in that term, must have many
of the substantial qualities that constitute a
good character and are essential to domes-
tic comfort. The character is much more
shown in the style of dress that is worn ev-
ery day, than in that which is designed for
great occasions; and when we see a young
girl come down to the family breakfast
in an untidy wrapper, with her hair in pa-
pers and her feet slip-shod, and an old silk
handkerchief round her neck, we know
that she cannot be the neat, industrious,
and refined person whom we would like for
an inmate. We feel equally certain too,
that her chamber is not kept in good order,
and that she does not set a proper value up-
on time. However well a lady has appear-
ed at a party, we would recommend to a
young gentleman, before he makes up his
mind as to her domestic qualities, to ob-
serve her appearance at the breakfast-table,
when she expects to see only her own fam-
ily; and, if it be such as we have just de-
scribed, to beware how he prosecutes the
acquaintance.
	To begin the day well it is necessary to
rise early enough to perform all the ablu-
tions necessary to health and cleanliness,
and to be neatly and completely dressed be-
fore breakfast. Your morning dress should
always be such that you would not be
ashamed to be seen in, by any accidental
visitor; and a clean muslin collar, or ruffie,
of the plainest materials, give an impress-
ion of far greater neatness than a colored
handkerchief. If you are suitably dressed
or the business of the morning, you are

[COLUMN 3]

ready to enter upon it at once and much
time is saved; you are not discomposed by
unexpected guests; and, if particularly en-
gaged, you can wear the same dress all day.
Very yonng girls are apt to think that the
most important item in their dress is the
material of which their frocks are made,
whereas that is really of the least conse-
quence. Clean stockings, neat shoes and
strings, smooth, well-brushed hair, and
delicately clean hands, nails, and teeth,
would make them look more lady-like, and
better dressed, in a cheap calico, than they
would be in the finest merino, or most costly
French silk without these accompaniments.
Those things which are most essential to a
neat appearance, are most within the reach
of everybody, and therefore the neglect of
them is not to be excused. Everybody can
mend stockings and gloves, however old
they may be; everybody can avoid break-
ing shoe-strings by wearing them too tight,
or having them become untied, by tying
them in false knots. Clean hands and nails,
and well-brushed hair and teeth, it is with-
in everybody's power to possess; and with-
out constant attention to these particulars,
the most expensive garments will fail to
produce the effect you desire. The same
honesty and self-respect which would keep
you from making a saving that you are
ashamed of, should prevent you wearing
anything, even out of sight, that you would
be ashamed to have seen, if sudden indisposi-
tion caused it to be exposed before stran-
gers. We should gladly adopt every con-
trivance for saving time and abridging the
labour necessarily bestowed upon dress.
Those who know how to use the needle af-
ter the best fashion of our grandmothers'
days, may next acquire the art of slighting
certain parts of their work, where it will
not interfere with durability, and so save
some thousand superfluous stitches in a
week. If it has been the misfortune of
any of my readers to have grown up with-
out being made good needlewomen, the
sooner they undertake to supply this defi-
ciency the better. A woman who does not
know how to sew is as deficient in her ed-
ucation as a man who cannot write. Let
her condition in life be what it may, she
cannot be ignorant of the use of her needle,
without incommoding herself and others,
and without neglecting some important du-
ties. Besides this, there is, in this truly
feminine employment, a moral power which
is useful to the sex. There is a soothing
and sedative effect in needle-work; it com-
poses the nerves, and furnishes a corrective
for many of the little irritations of domes-
tic life. Let no woman think herself ex-
empt from the duty of "plying the polish-
ed shaft." In every situation of life, she
will find herself the better for an early and
thorough acquaintance with plain sewing.
	SELECTED.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.		39


LOVER'S MISTAKES.

	Many a man lives with a woman half a
lifetime without suspecting that the wife
of his bosom has really forgotten more than
he ever knew. Many a carpet-knight, who
plumes himself upon his wonderful skill in
smashing hearts, is being mentally meas-
ured and intellectually turned inside out by
the smiling girls whom he thinks he is
captivating. Many a veteran beau, who
pulls on his gloves to depart, feeling proudly
conscious of having made a profound im-
pression upon the soul of the belle who has
endured him for the evening, would be
wonderfully enlightened, if not edified,
could he hear the sigh of relief which es-
caped her lips when the clang of the door
announced his departure.


40		WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


HYGIENIC NOTES.

	Cure for Neuralgia:—Take horseraddish and
grate it, then mix in vinegar and apply it to the
temples if it is the face or head that is affected; to
the wrists if it is the arms or shoulders.
	Ivy Poisoning:—Bathe the affected, inflamed
or swollen parts of the body freely with sweet
spirits of nitre, three or four times during the
day; it is affirmed by those who have tried it that
it is a safe, simple, and effectual remedy.
	For Corns:—Take a lemon and roll it until it
becomes soft; then cut thin slices from it and bind
on the corns when retiring for the night; in the
morning if the corn is white and disintegrated,
pull it out, but never cut it. If one application
will not answer, keep on applying the lemon and
perseverance will accomplish it.
	Waking at Night:—Dr. Hall says if persons find
themselves inclined to wake up at any regular
hour in the night, the habit can be broken up in
three days, by getting up as soon as awake and
not retiring again until the usual hour; or by re-
tiring two hours later and rising two hours ear-
lier for three days in succession.
	Sleep for Children:—The rule for children's
sleep, says an eminent physician, is for children
between the ages of five and seven, about twelve
hours; children over that age ten hours and never
less than eight hours. The time for going to
sleep seven o'clock in summer and six in winter.
To adhere strictly to this rule, he observes,
would be decidedly beneficial to health, growth,
and development. The Doctor adds it would al-
so accustom them to a sense of punctuality and
obedience. It is a well-known fact that children
require more sleep than persons of maturer age.
If we would have a healthy race of men and wo-
men, we must give all possible attention to the
laws of life and health.


FLOWERS.

	THE best consistency is to be consistent
to one's self, by acting every day up to the
light of that day.
	To work out our own contentment we
should labor not so much to increase our
substance as to moderate our desires.
	PERSEVERE against discouragements.
Employ leisure in study, and always have
some work at hand. Be punctual and me-
thodical in business, and never procrasti-
nate. Maintain dignity without the ap-
pearance of pride.
	No person is so well qualified to discharge
the duties of life as one who has a tempera-
ment glowing and genial. Such a one above
all others, is able to bring to the discharge
of duty such happiness as is necessary to
persistency.
	GREATNESS lies not in being strong, but
in the right using of strength; and strength
is not used rightly when it serves only to
carry a man above his fellows for his own
solitary glory. He is the greatest whose
strength carries up the most hearts by the
attraction of his own.


FACTS AND FIGURES.

	FRENCH statistics show that woman is
six times less criminal against person, four
times less criminal against property, and
twice less hardened a criminal than man.
The recently published record also proves
that maternity is a better shield than pater-
nity against bad life. Of 1,000 female
criminals 261 are mothers, and of 1,000
male criminals 324 are fathers.


THE population of some of the largest
cities in the world at the present time, by
good authority, is as follows: Paris, 1,851,-
792; New York, 1,046,000; Berlin. 980,000;
Philadelphia, 800,000; Vienna, 676,791;
Naples, 451,000; Brooklyn, 450,000; Ham-
burg, 370,000; Rome, 256,153; Copenhagen,
199,000, Brussels 188,264; Florence, 176,-
000; Christana, 75,000.
	IN England and Wales there are 810 co-
operative societies with a membership of
357,821; a capital of £4,151,634; annual
sales amounting to about £14,000,000; with
profits £1,070,000; expenses of manage-
ment £601,000. In Scotland, 216 societies
with 54,431 members, £338,946 capital,
£2,062,000 sales, £155,000 profits, and £76,-
300 expenses of management.


FACT FOR DARWIN;—"What's your
name?" said an officer to a young colored
lad, who joined his ship at the cape. "Al-
goa Bay, sir," "Where were you born?"
"Wasn't born at all." "Wasn't born at all?"
"No Sir; was washed ashore in a storm!"

WOMAN'S EXPONENT.

VOL. 5.	SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, AUGUST 15, 1876.	NO. 6.


For the EXPONENT.
IT'S BETTER TO LAUGH THAN TO
CRY.

I am only a girl in the cold, proud world,
	Working from day to day;
But this is my plan, wherever I can,
	To brighten the lonely way.
I look around me where they stand,
	The weary and sad and meek,
I smile and offer a friendly hand,
	And these are the words I speak:
"It is better to work than to idle be,
	As it's better to live than to die;
To sustain one's self and thus be free,
	And its better to laugh than to cry."

I have a heart that is full of love,
	For those sorrowing words of dust,
And would brace them up while they drink life's cup,
	So bitter, though oft so just.
But I know that the Lord is over all,
	Who can every comfort bring;
So attention to this great truth I call,
	And this is the song I sing:
"It is better to work than to idle be,
	As it's better to live than to die;
To trust in God and His mercies see,
	And it's better to laugh than to cry."

I often think as the world moves on,
	And we trample and crowd and shove,
That a world like this, full of lovliness,
	Should be much fuller of love.
We all admit that it must be so,
	When God shall all things restore;
And this great, beautiful truth I know,
	And I love to keep telling it o'er:
"It is better to work than to idle be,
	As it's better to live than to die:
To help each other and all agree,
	And it's better to laugh than to cry."

There's plenty of work for us all, my friends,
	And blessings that all may earn,
If we'll hold to the plan, wherever we can,
	To offer a kindly turn.
I'm only a woman bearing a part,
	In the world's great, busy throng;
Yet I may comfort some sadder heart,
	By the sound of my cheery song:
"It is better to work than to idle be,
	As it's better to live than to die;
To assist the Lord in His wise decree,
	And it's better to laugh than to cry."

		A WORKING WOMAN.


[Column 1]
	NOT ABOVE THEIR MISUNDER-
			STANDING.

	Dr. Arnold said, thirty years ago: "It is
in vain to say that questions of religion
and politics are above the understanding of
the poorer classes: they are not above their
mis-understanding." This is the irrestible
fact in regard to women and politics; and
every advance in education increases the
weight of the fact. At the beginning of a
Presidential campaign, this consideration
presses more and more upon us. Every
man wishes, or professes to wish that the
whole community should be enlightened as
to measures and men. Woman as a power-
ful factor in this process, will be every-
where conciliated. But if you conciliate
them, why not enlighten them? And of
all modes of enlightenment, the best is
that which is to be found to lie in some ac-
tual participation in the pursuit.
	This is recognized, in some degree by the
most conservative. The study of Political
Economy is introduced into High Schools
and Academies for both sexes. The most
conservative authorities recommend that
"Nordhoff's Politics for young Americans"

[Column 2]
should be widely circulated among ingen-
ious youth. Mr. Wells writes: "Robinson
Cruesoe's Money" that all may read. Why
is all this, if not that the understanding of
the whole people may be reached, and their
misunderstanding corrected? And since
women will have opinions and will influ-
ence votes, why not include them in the
general instruction? If we look in the
newspapers, what questions do we find
most pressing? The Chinese question, the
Indian war, the Hamburg massacre. All
these questions are daily discussed at a hun-
dred thousand breakfast tables, and in most
of these discussions women take their part.
What they say may have much or little ar-
gumentative value, but its representative
value is enormous. If it does not exhibit
common sense it exhibits at least the com-
mon nonsense—a thing which is, as Helps
has pointed out, almost equally important
to know.
	And each of these questions, moreover,
will be taken up and followed out, by clear
heads among both sexes, to its ultimate
principles. The principles of civilization
itself,—the rights of different races and re-
ligions under our government—the bind-
ing force of treaties—the principle of State
rights as against centralized power, are all
involved in them. Nay, a few short steps
take one back to the theory of the Federal-
ist, that the few wise and good should gov-
ern the many—or the other theory involved
in Jefferson's pithy question. "It is said
that man cannot be trusted to govern him-
self; can he then be trusted to govern an-
other? And between these two there is a
great gulf fixed, of inheritance, temperance
and habit; and if man cannot yet bridge
this chasm how can woman?"
	That women are to vary among them-
selves, almost as much as men, in opinions
on public matters is plain from the divided
fears, of those who oppose their enfran-
chisement; orthordox Christians for in-
stance, opposing it lest women should prove
too radical; and free thinkers, lest they
should prove too conservative. But wo-
men have opinions on all these matters as
it is; they are radical or conservative, as it
is. What we claim is that just in propor-
tion as they have a recognized position,
they differ, as it is. What we claim is that
just in proportion as they have a recognized
position, they will be trained for that posi-
tion, and by educating their understand-
ing we shall best get rid of their misunder-
standing.—A. W. H.—Ex.


BUCKSTONE'S BENEFIT AT DRURY LANE
THEATRE, LONDON:—

	A benefit was tendered to the old and
celebrated Comedian, Buckstone of the
Haymarket Theatre, London, it came off on
the 8th of June-"The School for Scandal,"
was the play chosen-every character even
to the guests, being played by first class
characters, in fact the elite of the stage
came forward to honor their old friend; the
result-was one of the most wonderful per-
formances ever known. The net profits of
that single performance amounted to the
large sum of twelve hnndred pounds."
						H. T. K.


NOTES AND NEWS.

	EASTERN papers announce the death of
Sydney Rigdon at Friendship, Alleghany
County, New York, July 14. He was born
in Alleghany County, Pennsylvania, in
1793, and was about 83 years of age at his
death. Sydney Rigdon's name is familiar
with the people called Latter-day Saints; in
connection with the early history of the
church. It is said that he was unwavering
in his testimony concerning the truth of the
Book of Mormon.
	THE FIRST railroad in China has been
opened for traffic. The line is only five
miles long, connecting Shanghai and Kan-
gwan; it is intended to extend it to Woo-
sung, five miles farther. It is built on the
narrow guage system with British capital.
This opens a new field for American enter-
prise in China, undoubtedly they will soon
establish a vast railroad system.
	THE FORTY Fourth Congress has pro-
tracted its session very considerably owing
probably to the great variety of questions
before the house and the many wide differ-
ences of opinion upon them. It seems to
have been particularly hard work during
this session to come to any satisfactory de-
cision, and where haste or dispatch are used
at the close of a session in disposing of vexed
questions ruinous consequences are usually
the result.
	AT THE Universal Peace Convention, (an
international gathering) held in Philadel-
phia early in this present month, the fol-
lowing resolutions were adopted as pro-
posed by Julia Ward Howe.
	"Resolved, That this Convention do not
regard the late unhappy massacre of U. S.
troops under General Custer as requiring or
justifying any departure from the pacific
policy inaugurated by the President of the
United States. They believe this event, on
the contrary, to have been the result of an
attempt on the part of our troops to occupy
by force a territory formerly ceded to the
Indians and already long occupied by them;
and they would respectfully request the
Government to either complete the purchase
of the disputed Territory or secure to the
Indians its peaceable possession.
	"Resolved, That the friends of peace
should spare no pains to contribute to the
civilization of the Indian tribes on our
frontiers and elsewhere, that schools and
missions should be largely established
among them, and that their children should
be so trained and educated as [-]o become fit-
ted to participate in the rights and duties of 
American citizenship.
	"Resolved, That every effort should be
made by the Government of the United
States and the friends of peace in America,
to induce the Indian tribes devoted to hunt-
ing to support themselves by agriculture,
and to study the arts and appliances of civ-
ilized life, since the tendency of their pres-
ent mode of life is to excite their passions
which lead to deeds of cruelty, while the
tendency of agricultural pursuits is peace-
ful and humanizing.—EX.

R. S. REPORTS.

	The Senior and Junior Retrenchment
Meeting held in the Fourteenth Ward As-
sembly Rooms, Saturday, Aug. 7th, was
one of general instruction and good feeling,
Counselor Zina D. Young presiding: meet-
ing opened with the usual exercises and
reading of minutes by the young lady sec-
retaries; considerable improvement is mani-
fest from time to time in the reports from
the societies, any person who attends these
meetings can readily perceive the spiritual
culture that is gaining ground in the organ-
izations formed to promote spiritual devel-
opment of the young. Mrs. Zina D. Young
spoke of the testimony the faces of the sis-
ters brought to her by being present regu-
larly at these meetings, that they were
seeking after light and knowledge from on
high. Mrs. J. Howe, Coun. E. Howard,
Mrs. M. R. Thompson, Mrs. P. Kimball
and other sisters spoke of obeying counsel,
training and educating the young, home
manufacture and other subjects of interest.

	FAIRVIEW, June 16, 1876.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	By request of our president, Mrs. Mary
A. Pritchett, I send you the Eighth Annu-
al report of Fairview Relief Society. The
Society convened in the meeting house on
the 14th of June, 1876. Opened by sing-
ing—prayer by Bishop Tucker. Report
read by the Secretary, as follows: From Jan.
21st, 1876, till June 14th.
	Disbursed to the needy................... $ 20
	To St. George Temple....................	    38
	To Salt Lake Temple......................     16
	In Treasury.....................................     12
	Spirited remarks were made by our Bish-
op and others. Our President, Sister Prit-
chett exhorted the sisters to be united, said
she was pleased with what they had
done in so short a time. We have a great
mission to perform in helping to build up
the kingdom of God, and it is for us to be
up and doing. Several other sisters spoke,
and all seemed to enjoy the spirit of the
Lord. Dismissed by Bro, Sanderson.
	M. A. PRITCHETT, Pres.
		T.C. AVERY, Sec. 

	KAYSVILLE, July 10, 1876.
EDITOR EXPONENT
	The Young Ladies Retrenchment Asso-
ciation of Kaysville was organized May 16,
1876, by Sister Eliza R. Snow: officers as
follows:
	Mrs. Ada Williams, President,
	"	Maggie Allred,
	Miss Helen Hyde,
	"	Matilda Egbert		Counselors.
	"	Sarah Ann Webb,
	"	Martha Raymond,
	"	Mary Ann Layton, Secretary.
	Mrs. Celina Phillips, Assistant Secretary.
	Miss Elizabeth Williams, Treasurer.
	Seventy-five young ladies were admited
as members of the Society. Our meetings
are well attended, and all seem to take an
interest in the welfare of the Association.
	We have an energetic president who is
fast gaining the love and respect of the
young sisters. She is taking measures to
establish Home Industries, according to the
counsel of Sister Snow. At the organiza-
tion of the society, Sisters' Eliza R. Snow
and Rachel Grant gave us some excellent
instructions and advice, which we think
will leave a lasting impression on the
minds of all who were present.
	We gave a concert on the fourth of July,

[COLUMN 2]

assisted by a number of the young men,
from which we realized forty-two dollars,
which will be used for the establishment of
Home Industry.
	The Exponent is read with interest in
our meetings.
	With best wishes for the advancement of
every good work, we remain your sisters,
	HELEN HYDE,
		MARY A. LAYTON, Sec'y.

	We meet regularly, and speak of the
things pertaining to the work in which we
are engaged. Our President is an amiable
young lady, who is trying together with
her counselors, to honor her calling. We
have forty-seven members. We have ta-
ken a vote to refrain from round dancing;
we read selections from the EXPO-
NENT at every meeting and gain much in-
struction therefrom.
	Wishing the paper success, and all insti-
tutions of improvement, I remain your
Sister &c.,
		MARTHA A. MURRAY, Sec'y

	SOUTH COTTONWOOD, July llth, 1876. 
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	We have been rather negligent in for-
warding our report. Our soeiety is in a
very prosperous condition and we are try-
ing to do all the good we can. We held our
Annual Meeting on the 8th of June, there
was a full attendance. Bishop Joseph Raw-
lins and several of the teachers were pres-
ent and gave us some very good instruc-
tions. They were well satisfied and pleased
with the progress we had made, and the
way in which we conduct our meetings,
encouraged us to sustain all HOME MANU-
FACTURES and strive to be self-sustaining
in all things. Many interesting remarks
were made on very important subjects, but
would take up too much space to make
mention of them.

WOMAN'S VOICE.

EDITOR EXPONENT:
	This is my first attempt to write for the
EXPONENT and I do not expect to give any
one instruction or counsel, but I feel it
my duty to contribute my mite with the
rest of my sisters, and I know that if we
strive to do right and ask our Heavenly
Father to bless and aid us in our endeav-
ors to do good He will do so. I do not
have the same chance to improve my mind
and feast my soul that some of my sisters
have who live in the midst of the Saints,
and do not have the outside influences to
contend with that those living out here do.
But we can learn to do right and not follow
after the ways of the outside world. We
have a Sunday School, and it is in a pros-
perous condition. We have no young la-
dies association, because there are but very
few families and they are living quite a
distance apart. Swearing and drinking is
getting to be quite a habit with some of the
young people in this vicinity, most people
know that it is wrong and should refrain
from it. I am very glad to hear that round
dancing is being put down among our
young people. Boys and girls refrain from
using bad language, refined people do not
like to associate with young ladies who
use slang phrases, and young men who will
swear, they feel ashamed to be seen in their
company, they are shuned, and respected
by none. If we are acquainted with any of
this type, let us talk kindly to them, show
them how wrong it is, and try to persuade
them to better regulate their conversation
and their actions.
	We should honor and obey our parents
and teachers, and do our best to keep the
commandments of God in every particular.
How well we feel when we know that we
have done right, and how miserable when
conscious of having done wrong. There
are many young men and boys who think
it is a credit to them to smoke, but on the
contrary, it is a disgrace; it is a very bad
habit and the Latter-day Saints are counsel-
ed to refrain from it. "Do what is right,
let the consequence follow," when we do
this we are often ridiculed by our compan-
ions, but if the spirit of God is in our
hearts, and we know we are pleasing Him,
we do not care for their taunts and sneers.
Let us shun all evil companions, and seek
the company of those who can improve us.
I am not in the habit of giving counsel, but
I feel it my duty to speak about these bad
habits having noticed many of the evils
which arise from them. We should strive
to improve our minds and seek continually
to do our duty.
	We all have faults, some more than oth-
ers, but by constant prayerand watch-guard
over our actions, we will in time overcome
them. We must love all mankind, and
keep the GOLDEN RULE if we ever expect
to have a crown in the kingdom of Him
who is Omnipotent. We should pray con-
stantly to Him who keeps us from day to-
day. May God bless us all and preserve us
from evil, is my prayer in the name of Je-
sus.
	I remain your Sister, &c.
						J. A. MAXWELL.
	Little Spring Valley, Nev.

WOMAN'S EXPONENT.		43


LEARN TO KEEP HOUSE.

Beautiful maidens—aye nature's fair queens,
Some in your twenties, and some in your teens,
Seeking accomplishments worthy your aim.
Striving for learning, thirsting for fame;
Taking such pains with the style of your hair,
Keeping your [---]y complexions so fair:
Miss not this item in all your gay lives,
Learn to keep house, you may one day be wives.
			Learn to keep house.

Now your Adonis loves sweet moonlight walks,
Hand clasps, and kisses, and nice little talks;
Then, as plain Charlie, with burden of care,
He must subsist on more nourishing fare;
He'll come home at the set of the sun.
Heart-sick and weary, his working day done,
Thence let his slippered feet ne'er wish to roam,
Learn to keep house that you may keep home.
			Learn to keep house.

First in his eyes will be children and wife,
Joy of his joy, and life of his life,
Next his bright dwelling, his table, his meals—
Shrink not at what my pen trembling reveals,
Maidens romantic, the truth must be told.
Knowledge is better than silver or gold;
Then be prepared in the spring-time of health,
Learn to keep house, tho' surrounded by wealth.
			Learn to keep house.


[Column 1]
			A VISIT TO TEXAS.

	I left S. L. City, in company with the
missionaries who were going East, on
the 9th of Nov., 1875, to visit my relatives
in Texas, and had a very pleasant and in-
teresting time. On the Union Pacific R. R.
we met with a number of strangers who
manifested an interest in our people, and
wished to know concerning our doctrine &c.
They had stayed over Sunday in Salt Lake,
and had heard Orson Pratt preach and were
surprised to find the city so orderly and
quiet, spoke of the saloons being closed, re-
marked how unfavorably we had been rep-
resented abroad.
	I was surprised to find books distributed
on these cars containing misrepresentations
against the Mormons. I thought the U.
P. R. R. was too well patronized by the
Mormons to allow such insults to them; I did
not feel like being insulted by the distribution
of such literature, (especially as there was
no deduction made in the fare for a lady
missionary,) nevertheless it opened a field
for the missionaries, so that what was design-
ed for evil resulted in good.
	On our arrival at Omaha we parted with
the Elders who were going East and board-
ed the southern train by way of Council
Bluffs, St. Joseph, and Kansas cities for Dal-
las, Texas, which is near my mother's resi-
dence, arrived at the "Old Folks at Home,"
the seventh day after leaving here. To say
we had a joyful meeting and all talked at
once, will not begin to express the scene
that prevailed. My brother Thomas said it
reminded him of the resurrection, that
twenty years absence had not broken the
kindred ties; I answered, if you believe the
Gospel they have grown ten-fold stronger.
He replied he had not lost the testimony
he[-]eceived twenty years before, but was
anxious to forward the work and assist the
Elders; there were appointments made and
the work of preaching the everlasting Gos-
ple re-commenced in that lovely land of
Texas, for indeed it is a beautiful country,
and I felt constrained to prophesy that
there would yet be a Temple built in that
land and the Saints would possess it; the
good seed sown there by the Elders twenty
years ago has not all died out, and by dili-
gent care and cultivation it will yet take
root and grow. The meetings were well
attended, eight persons were baptized into
the church and others renewed their cov-
enants by baptism, and many were inter-
osted, believing and inquiring. I never

[Column 2]
felt better and stronger in my life than I
did when defending the character of this
people and the principles of our belief. I
attended all the meetings held by our Eld-
ers in Bosque County, Texas, and whenever
it was known that I was present, I was al-
ways requested by some of the audience to
speak and tell what I knew, and what had
induced me to live among such a despised
people. I never refused to respond and
there was a power with me whenever I
raised my voice to testify to the truth of
the Latter-day work. I was told in a bless-
ing given me by one of the Twelve Apos-
tles, that those who heard my testimony
should be pierced to the heart, and this
prophecy was literally fulfilled. Several
noted gentlemen remarked to my brother
that they never heard a more convincing
testimony, and that he should feel proud of
a sister who in face of opposition, and the
unpopularity of the doctrine in which she
believed, could thus stand up and defend it.
A gentleman said to me, Paul said it
was a shame for a woman to speak in
public." I replied, Paul also said, Priscil-
la and Aquilla labored with him in the
Gospel, and that Paul was not writing to
me, that my religion was between me and
my Father in heaven, that I had covenant-
ed with Him that if He would give me a
testimony of the truth I would be a faith-
ful witness to Him. He had fulfilled His
promise and now it remained for me to be
true to my covenant with Him. On one
occasion I was requested by a Universalian
minister to speak to the people on the sub-
ject of polygamy; this reverend gentleman
invited our Elders to preach to his congre-
gation, thinking to lay a snare for them as
was afterwards proven, but he was caught
in his own trap, for there was an editor
present who reported the discourses of
Elders' East and Greer, and published
them, which opened the way for Mr. East
to get an article published in the Bosque
Co. Herald, in which he invited the said
Rev. gentleman to prove the assertions he
had made by the Bible. At this meeting
there was the largest audience that had
ever been present, and I was called on to
speak at the close of the meeting; I told
them it reminded me of the Apostle Paul
when he made his defense before King
Agrippa, Paul had seen the power of God
made manifest (so had we) he had seen a
vision and testified of Jesus of Nazareth,
and the resurrection of the dead, (so do
we testify of that same Jesus, the gift of
the Holy Ghost bearing witness &c., and
we warn you to repent and fear God for the
hour of His judgment has come, and the
day is nigh at hand when this beautiful
land of yours will be laid waste by another
desolating war, and you will feel the chast-
ening hand of God and acknowledge Him
perchance when it is too late to gather out.
We contrasted the practises of the mono-
gamic cities of the world, where beautiful
women were led astray and excluded from
society, disowned by parents and kindred,
left to die without husband or child to
mourn for them, their names dishonored
and cast out as evil forever; compared this
with the Patriarchal order of marriage
where women are honorable wives and
mothers, the children considered an herit-
age from the Lord. You who so despise Po-
lygamy which of the two do you think the
Lord will own and bless?
	There are among the women of the Lat-
ter-day Saints, we are proud to say, those
who are self-sacrificing like holy women of
old, and as Josephine the first wife of Napo-
leon, who would make a sacrifice for the
the benefit of a nation, or the elevation of a

[Column 3]
people, rather than to gratify their own sel-
fish feelings and desires. To be self-sacrific-
ing is elevating and exalting, it was through
this principle that Abraham proved him-
self to be the friend of God.
	After bearing a faithful testimony, we bid
farewell to Texas and our kindred; Yes,
Fare well, "In the sweet by and by we may
meet on that beautiful shore."
	On the 25th of April, 1876, we started
for home, "Sweet Home," arrived in S. L.
City the 2d day of May, feeling we had
not labored in vain.
				WILLMIRTH EAST.


SPLINTERS.

		GENERAL.
	The Emperor of Brazil's hotel expenses
in this country are said to have been nearly
$40,000......Charles Campbell, the Vir-
gina historian, died a few days ago in the
insane asylum at Staunton......Vanderbilt
is very likely to recover......Miss Allison,
the engineer of machinery, in the Wo-
man's Pavillion at the Centennial is report-
ed very ill......Miss Martineau, recently de-
ceased, left an injunction in her will
against the publication of her private let-
ters......Clara Bauer, a German novel-writ-
er, died lately in Breslau; she was familiar
with Russian society, and her last work "A
Document," was a portrayal of the best
Russian society......New port, Vermont, on
the 4th of July, had music by a drum corps,
each member of which was over ninety
years of age. America has two thousand
actors, all trying to become renowned......
Bryant is said to be the richest as well as
the oldest poet now living.....Over three
millions of visitors have reviewed the cu-
riosities at the Centennial Exposition...... I
Capt. Gildersleeve will command the Amer-
ican rifle team in the Centennial matches.
		FOREIGN.
	Abdul Aziz's private jewelry, valued at
six million Turkish lbs., has been seized
and handed to the Finance Department for
sale.........Don Carlos is tall, handsome,
swarthy of complexion, dark hair, dark
moustache, and small imperial......Victor
Hugo is worth half a million of dollars and
is said to be as egotistical as he is rich......
Mrs. Swishelm writes that in Germany it is
the rule for gentlemen to bow first in the
matter of recognition......News from the
seat of war in the East is of a very confiict-
ing character, each side claiming victories.
All the powers except Russian have de-
clared Roumania's demands unreasonable
.......A Berlin mechanic has invented a
steam velocipede, the engine is heated with
petroleum, and placed on the back wheels
does not interfere with the driver.


ALEXANDRE Dumas, who had some rea-
son to complain of his plays being left on
the shelf, sat down one evening and wrote
an article in his newspaper against the
Theatre Francais. An ill-natured person
carried the news, the manager immediate-
ly altered his play bills. The next morning
people after reading the attack of the great
author on the Theatre Francais, turned to
see what the house was performing, they
read—"This evening 'Les Demoiselles de
St. Cyr,' by Alexandre Dumas, to be fol-
lowed by 'Le Mari de la Veuve,' by Alex-
andre Dumas. To-morrow evening, 'Made-
moiselle de Belle Isle,' by Alexandre Du-
mas, to be followed by 'Romulus,' by Alex-
andre Dumas."
