SALT LAKE CITY, AUGUST 15, 1876.


[COLUMN 1]

WOMEN TALKERS AND WOMEN
WRITERS.

	"But words are things; and a small drop of ink,
	Falling like dew upon a thought, produces
	That which makes thousands, perhaps millions think."
		BYRON.
	Then let women talk and write and think.
It is a common saying, women talk too much;
it may be that they do about that which is of
no real importance, but upon subjects which
should be well understood, by women possess-
ing common intelligence, there are a vast ma-
jority who do not converse at all; to them it is
all rubbish, nonsense and unwomanly. They
prefer to have a husband, father or brother, to
think and act and speak for them; to attend to
them, to support them and to defend them.
It is to educate this class of women upon such
essential points, that it becomes more than
ever necessary to speak, and write, and har-
angue. The vine and oak theory, that has
been so romantically written about by a cer-
tain class of authors, will answer perhaps in
exceptional cases, but how it will apply to the
world of mankind and womankind in general,
those who are observers of modern society
can easily perceive.
	In this wonderfully enlightened nineteenth
century when there is so much questioning
of rights and equality and supremacy. and in-
feriority and superiority, and submission and
place and calling, and influence and individu-
ality, it is but natural to express some interest
on the subjects which bring to their aid such a
variety of adjectives to qualify and determine
their general and relative interest.
	It is a well-authenticated fact and palpable
to all the world, that there are thousands of
women, who have no such substantial support
to cling to, as an oak in the form of a husband,
father or brother, and of those who have, there
are many, who suffer more than those who
have not, from intemperance and other causes,
consequently it follows, that as vines they
would trail upon the earth, and very naturally
be trod upon just as thousands of women are,
who have been taught and encouraged in de-
pendence instead of independence.
	Circumstances and training it is said form
the individual; certainly they tend to strength-
en and develop important characteristics.
Custom the irrepressible tyrant, has in the
past constrained parents to train their boys for
the battle of life, the girls to look forward to
marriage as the one great aim of their exist-
ence. Now custom, alias fashion, argues men
cannot afford wives they are too expensive a
luxury; then women must necessarily look
around for some avenue of support, some prac-
tical, paying kind of labor. These matters re-
quire serious thought, require words, require

[COLUMN 2]

actions,—which will determine some positive
demonstration of the subject in question.
	Women cannot talk too much about these
matters, if they do so understandingly to as-
sist in enlightening themselves and others.
	The world says Polygamy makes women in-
ferior to men—we think differently. Polyga-
my, gives women more time for thought, for
mental culture, more freedom of action, a
broader field of labor, inculcates liberality and
generosity, develops more fully the spiritual
elements of life, fosters purity of thought and
gives wider scope to benevolence, leads wo-
men more directly to God the fountain of all 
truth, knowledge, light and intelligence.
	If those who are anxious to promote higher
development, would consider the true prin-
ciples upon which to base marriage, they would
look closely into the order of plural marriage,
and some of the most momentous questions
that are puzzling the world at the present
time, might thus be properly adjusted. Then
let women talk, and write, and reason, and
think, and interchange their ideas with each
other and with men. God has endowed wo-
man with reasoning and intuitive powers—
certainly He intended her to use her own
agency.
	E. B. Wells


[Column 1]
			HOME AFFAIRS:

	WE ACKNOWLEDGE the favor of an elegant
bouquet of flowers from Mrs. Toms, we appre-
ciate these delightful and pleasant reminders
of the perfection of the works of God, and we
are grateful to the lady for her kind remem-
brance. The flowers were delicate and choice
ones, and the trailing vines, ferns and fancy
grasses gave an additional charm to their va-
riety and beauty.
	WE HAVE had several pleasant calls within
the past week from strangers, people of refine-
ment and culture. Almost invariably, they
express themselves pleased with the locality
and general appearance of the city, the moun-
tain scenery etc. Capt. Havelock, Mrs. Have-
lock, and Mrs. Rattray, English people, called
on Monday, Aug. 7th, and spent an hour [--]
two in a cheerful social way. They had been
under the impression that this people discour-
aged education, advancement, or progression
in any but industrial employments.
	THE BENEFIT tendered Mrs. Susie Maiden,
at the Theatre on Saturday evening, Aug. 5th,
was not so largely attended as the occasion de-
served. The selection of the programme for
the entertainment presented a most attrac-
tive bill, and those who profess so much ad-
miration for the drama ought to offer some in-
ducement and encouragement to HOME TAL-
ENT. Mrs. Marden as Peach Blossom was a
decided success, and merited the applause ac-
corded her, and the congratulation of her
friends for her exertions to please the public.
Several other parts were extremely well ren-
dered, particularly those performed by Miss
Colebrook and Miss Decker.
	Mrs. Louisa Pratt of Beaver, in this Terri-
tory has written her Autobiography which
she purposes publishing the present year.
The writer is in her 74th year, she has
kept a journal almost all her life, and has pre-
served a record of the most important events.
She has a prospectus printed and distributed
among her friends; in this she refers to some
of the main incidents of her history, of her
journey by land from the Atlantic to the Pa-
cific, before the days of cars and steam, of her
sojourn on the South Pacific Islands, where

[Column 2]
she resided for some time with her husband
and family, including four daughters, living
in friendship with the natives, a teacher to
their children &c., of her many trials shared
with the saints, in their persecutions wander-
ings and drivings. She closes the prospectus
by saying the work shall have one merit, it
will be STRICTLY TRUE.
	MRS. A. J. DUNIWAY, one of the most ear-
nest and indefatigable workers in the cause of
Woman's Suffrage etc., delivered two lectures
during her recent stay in this city. The first
was designed particularly to benefit her sex,
and enlighten them on some particular essen-
tial points of which many women seem en-
tirely ignorant and unconcerned. It was en-
titled, "Personal Experiences which led to my
Mission." Mrs. Duniway is extremely practi-
cal and matter-of-fact in all her views, argu-
ments and reasonings. She lectured without
notes or references of any description, and
never hesitated for a word, or seemed in the
least embarrassed. Her second lecture was
upon railroads etc., and she evidently under-
stood her subject in all its multifarious bear-
ings. Her description of the North-western
part of the United States, Oregon and Wash-
ington was extremely interesting. She enu-
merated the many advantages which would
accrue to benefit the country at large if this
grand and wealthy region could be connected
by railway with San Francisco and the East,
making Salt Lake the central point. The tim-
bers, the coal mines, the fisheries which are so
abundant there, these were all descanted
upon in her most glowing descriptive terms.
	MONDAY MORNING Aug. 7th, the Normal
Institute opened its annual session for eleven
days, it is under the management of Dr. J. R.
Park. There was a full attendance. Most of
the teachers are residents of Salt Lake County,
Weber and Utah Counties are next best repre-
sented. The exercises so far have been very
interesting and highly instructive, and must
confer material benefit on those who are atten-
tively and assiduously applying themselves to
the department of teaching or the acquire-
ment of knowledge. Prof. Maeser's lectures
on history and natural science are remarkably
solid and well worthy contemplation. The in-
structions in geography by Mr. Charles
Wright, are very good, and the illustrations
are clear and practical. Prof. M. H. Hardie
on the subject of reading expresses his views
and ideas in such a manner as to awaken much
interest in this important subject; it is the
prevailing idea of most persons that to be able
to pronounce all the hard words and observe
the pauses and punctuation marks, constitutes
a good reader. Perfect reading, like music,
must be harmonious or it grates upon the
finer senses like a song badly sung, or an in-
strument out of tune. Mr. G. M. Ottinger's
illustrations of drawing are happily rendered
and calculated to make impressions favorable
to that useful and beautiful art.
	THE LADIES' Centennial Territorial Fair
still continues attractive to visitors. Several
additions in woman's work have been brought
since the first opening; among these, one which
deserves mentioning is a Scripture pic[-]ure in
needle-work, by Mrs. M. E. A. Wat[--]ough,
representing the child Samuel when he comes
to Eli, having heard the voice of the Lord
calling him. The scene is well represented,
and very natural for the kind of work, which
of course is not equal to a painting in likeness
to life. The tambour needle-work by Mrs.
Wm. Matthews is something exquisite in
beauty and rarity. The silk work, laces, veils,
fichu, and handkerchiefs; all entirely of home


production are the most wonderful. We have
heard several strangers remark upon their ex-
cellence, and were indeed surprised that the
energy and perseverance of the sisters had ac-
complished such beautiful and meritorious re-
sults. If those engaged in the manufacture of
silk are only as patient as the coral insects who
possess only instinct to prompt them to labor,
and from the smallest beginnings form im-
mense reefs; then indeed may we look for won-
derful results. We advise everybody to go
to the Fair, you will certainly find many
things to admire, and in no other way, can you
in so short a time, judge of the energy the
taste and skill of the women of Utah. Miss
F. R. Snow and the ladies engaged there
spare no pains to make it interesting and at-
tractive.
	THE SILK ASSOCIATION met Aug. 1st. at the
Fireman's Hall, Mrs. Zina D. Young presiding.
Mrs. Young cautioned those sending cocoons
to the society to be sure they were properly
dried, and floss them carefully. The cocoons
should not be moved off the rack or brush for
eight days, then they should be placed where
the sun would shine on them at least a week:
save the very best and finest cocoons to raise
seed from, and each one should raise more seed
than needed for his or her own use. What
was most needed now was the planting out of
mulberry trees; this branch of industry should
receive due attention and encouragement.
Mr. Paul A. Schettler said he felt sure the
raising and manufacture of silk here could be
made a success, and he was glad to see the
progress already made. Mrs. Dunyon gave
her experience in planting out mulberry cut-
tings, she advised sandy soil and to shade
them from the sun, at first to water them well
and for the first and second winter to lay
some straw over them. Mr. Groves said he
had good luck in raising silk worms and had a
fine grove of trees, the difficulty with him be-
ing he had not room enough. Miss E. R.
Snow said that to make a successful industry
of silk raising would require a vast amount of
perseverance and patience, and it could not be
accomplished without a united effort of the
people. She remembered hearing the Prophet
Joseph say the time would come when the peo-
ple of the world would come to Zion to buy
the finest of fabrics, said strangers had spoken
very highly of the samples of silk in the La-
dies' Fair. In Brigham City they thought of
making the raising of silk one branch of their
home industry another year. Mr. Smith, Mrs.
E. S. Barney, Mrs. E. Shipp, Mrs. M. A. White
and Mrs. Smith each gave a little of their ex-
perience in this industry and the result. The
next meeting of this association will be held
to-day, Aug 15th, at three o'clock at the same
place, such arrangements will then be made
as will be necessary for the advancement of
the society and a permanent organization ef-
fected.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.		45


NINETY-TWO.

	Miss Rhoda Richards celebrated her birthday
on Tuesday the 8th inst. She was ninety two
years old; and notwithstanding the heavy
rain fall, which commenced about mid-day,
two o'clock found a number of sisters assem-
bled at her humble dwelling, to offer congratu-
lations and kind wishes. A quiet little bless-
ing meeting was conducted, in the midst of
which the following pleasant note was receiv-
ed:
		Salt Lake City, August, 8th, 1876,
Sister Rhoda Richards,
					City;
My dear Cousin:
	The condition of the weather is such that I


do not think it wise for me to leave home. I
shall therefore be debarred the pleasure of call-
ing on you this afternoon and offering my con-
gratulations and blessings on this the anniver-
sary of your birthday. Permit me however,
by means of pen and ink to express my kind-
est feelings and regards, with the hope that
abundant blessings may continue to abide with
you to the latest days of your life upon the
earth, and increase with you throughout a
never-ending future of joy, happiness and ex-
altation. Such is the heartfelt prayer of your
Brother in the New and Everlasting Cove-
nant.
					BRIGHAM YOUNG.
	After choice blessings had been asked for and
pronounced upon the aged hostess by all pres-
ent, she said she did not wish to take all the
blessings to herself, but desired that her sisters
should also be blessed. Accordingly a bless-
ing was pronounced upon each in turn by the
others. At the close of this sacred and joyous
interview, the company sat down to a well-
filled table, and feasted bodily as well as spirit-
ually. When the repast was finished, Sister
Zina D. Young, in a sweet and beautiful pray-
er, returned thanks to the Giver of all good,
for the blessings of the day, and asked for a
continuance of God's mercies to His people.
Sister Rhoda gave an invitation for all to
be present at the next anniversary of her
birthday, and with affectionate adieus the com-
pany dispersed.


For the EXPONENT.
DEPENDENCE AND INDEPENDENCF.

	I often wonder why it is that women
will be so dependent. Why they think, or
at least a great many of them, that it is
their calling to be dependent upon man-
kind for all they have or are in this world,
imagining that it looks more womanly to
depend upon the sterner sex for a name and
standing in society, than to go to work and
earn it. Is it because that men as a gener-
al thing, have taught them that dependence
upon them is necessary; that a weak,
languishing woman with hardly life enough
to laugh a good hearty laugh, one, who if
left to depend upon herself would find her
staff of support very frail, is more to be ad-
mired, than one who has been taught to be
self-reliant, and who can, if necessary, earn
not only a livelihood: but a name which will
be honored by all. Bah! I get perfectly dis-
gusted with womankind when I think how
little independence there is among them.
There are so few who will think, or act for
themselves. It is my candid opinion that
this would be a happier world if there
were a few more independent women in it.
	JUNE.


THE LADIES Centennial Fair is still open
and seems to be a successful enterprise.
The variety of woman's work exhibited
there, speaks volumes for the women of
Utah, for their industry, energy and un-
flinching faith and perseverance. The
samples of straw goods are indeed very su-
perior in quality and taste. The cabinet of
curiosities and wonderful collection of min-
erals and specimens are worthy the atten-
tion and admiration of all. Varieties of
lace and silk work which have been created
and brought to perfection from the ele-
ments in Utah, are indeed beautiful and
worthy of commendation. Tidies, Anti-
macassars, curtains, bed-quilts, rugs, car-
pets of almost every variety, are there in
abundance.


This is positive proof that almost any-
thing can be accomplished by united efforts
and energy of purpose.


MAN'S DUTY TO WOMAN.

	Let him learn to be grateful to woman
for this undoubted achievement of her sex,
that it is she, she far more than he, and she,
too, in spite of him, who has kept Chris-
tendom from lapsing into barbarism—kept
mercy and truth from being utterly over-
borne by those two greedy monsters, mon-
ey and war. Let him be grateful for this,
that almost every great soul that has led
forward or lifted up the race, has been fur-
nished for each noble deed, and inspired
with each patriotic and holy aspiration, by
the retiring fortitude of some Spartan or
more than Spartan—some Christian mother.
	Moses, the deliverer of his people, drawn
out of the Nile by the King's daughter, some
one has hinted, is only a symbol of the way
that woman's better instinct always outwits
the tyrannical diplomacy of man. Let him
cheerfully remember that though the
sinewy sex achieves enterprises on public
theatres, it is the nerve and sensibility of
the other that arm the mind and inflame
the soul in secret. "A man discovered
America, but a woman equipped the voy-
age." So everywhere; man executes the
performance, but woman trains the man.
Every effectual person, leaving his mark on
the world, is but another Columbus, for
whose furnishing, some Isabella, in the form
of his mother, lays down her jewels, her
vanity, her comfort.—Rev. E. D. Hunting-
ton.—Ex.


THE FITNESS OF THINGS.

	The middle-aged lady who adorns herself
with diamonds on full dress occasions does
what is perfectly right and proper; but good
taste is outraged at sight of a girl of fifteen
wearing these jewels. It is no uncommon
thing to see school-girls, from the ages of
ten to eighteen, wearing diamonds on their
fingers and in the ears. A young girl does
not think her toilet complete without dia-
mond ear-rings and at least one diamond
ring. Then as she gets richer, come hair
ornaments, crosses, and lockets made of the
precious stones. In European countries it
is not until a woman has attained to years
of maturity that she wears diamonds, and
even then not unless she is very rich. In
this country no one is too young to wear
them, and scarcely too poor for many wo-
men who live in small houses up back
streets, appear at church or the opera glit-
tering in diamonds. One lady who is not
able to afford a servant, when she gets up
in the morning puts on a calico wrapper,
but that does not prevent her arraying her
self in diamond ear-rings, breast-pin, and
finger rings, with which she performs her
household tasks. Bad taste of this sort is
positively vulgar.—Ex.


THE PRINCESS Louise will again this year
it is said, have some work in the Royal
Academy. Her bust of the Queen stands
perpetually in the sculpture room, and a
very good bust it is. Most probably her
new work will be a picture. She has of
late been studying very hard at South Ken-
sington, and has enlivened many a class
there by her chattiness and love of fun.


46		WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
		CORRESPONDENCE.

	JOHNSONVILLE, WARREN Co., IND.
					        July 3, 1876.
EDITORS EXPONENT:
	DEAR LADIES:—The second number of
your excellent paper was received, and I
was well pleased with its high moral tone.
It was not just what I had expected to see,
coming from your city. I am more than
surprised at the interest manifested by
your women on the subject of reform, and
the up-building of the cause which you
have espoused, together with your co-opera-
tive system of doing business. I can but
admire the spirit evinced in the good
works.
	I have been reading some of your books:
"Voice of Warning," by Parley P. Pratt,
and two sermons by Orson Pratt, one of
which was highly instructive, and am now
perusing the Book of Mormon. I shall be
better posted by and by, particularly if I
could have the Elders to teach and explain
the Scriptures according to your views. I
might after a while be constrained to say—
"Almost thou persuadest me to be a Mor-
mon." The women of your community
cannot be much oppressed, when they have
the right of suffrage, and a paper entirely
devoted to their interests. That is more
than we can say, for we are not allowed to
vote even on questions directly concerning
us.
	In conclusion I will remark that, If you
carry out the doctrines taught and preached
by your Elders, you cannot be a bad people.
	Let me hear from you again, at least
through the columns of your paper, if you
cannot find the time to write.
	I shall be very happy to have your paper
for the rest of the year.
	Wishing you success in your enterprise
I remain				Very truly yours,
							M. L. Johnston.

				LOWELL, MASS.
						July 27, 1876
MY DEAR AUNT:
				The scenes I have had the
pleasure of witnessing and the people I have
seen, if aptly described, would perhaps in-
terest you and the other friends at home,
but I am not very good at descriptive letter
writing, and will leave you to the papers for
the Exhibition and the notables who attend
it. There are some things that don't often
get into the papers that interest me more,
and I love to write to you about, for your
appreciation of the work that absorbs the
attention and is the delight of missionaries
makes it a pleasure to write you. Of course
I have not done much preaching while trav-
eling from one great city to another, but oc-
casionally I have had opportunity to advo-
cate our doctrines to some of the people who
travel by my side in the cars, whom I meet
in the boarding houses and hotels, in the
art galleries and museums, viewing monu-
ments and parks, and in the various associ-
ations that are formed by travellers.
	Since I came to New England I have
been devoted to a few poor, humble British
Saints,' who, crowded in the back streets of
the manufacturing towns are trying to live
in their old English style at American
prices, and are consequently, with good
wages, getting in debt, and getting farther
from Zion than they were before they left
their Albion shores. Still a spark of that
fire, that fills the soul with light and the
hearts of the people with love, is found
glimmering in their breasts, and when you

[Column 2]
speak to them encouragingly, it revives,
and glows and sparkles in their eyes like
burning coals. But when we go away it
smoulders again, and the ashes of discontent
envelop and almost smother it out. Again
and again has this fanning and cooling pro-
cess been experienced by them, and will
continue I suppose until the last. I wish the
Saints in the world could have a foretaste
of the experience that is necessary for them
to pass through when they are gathered.
I think it would be easier for them to com-
prehend the reality of their religion more
than they do at the present. The Lord has
queer material with which to work, but I
suppose it is the best He can get. Only the
poor and lowly have time to entertain the
homely truths of the old fashioned gospel of
Jesus. It is enough for the rich and learn-
ed to meditate upon the glittering bubble,
conjured up in the minds of enthusiasts, and
surmounted by the golden cross and brazen
image, which the truth-denying, self-loving,
prophet-denying, mis-called Christian priests
present to them as the gospel of peace,
and effigy of salvation.
	The Lord's economy in taking the very
lowliest of all people with which to perform
His work, is of course the best, for if they
were not already humble, He would have
to bring them down; and often material is
worn out before a vessel of either beauty or
utility can be formed of it, for the reason
that there is so much of the brittle, refuse
matter mixed with it, that the process of
expunging it wastes away the rest.
	I think that the Centennial year of our
nation's independence presents to the
Saints a condition of society throughout
the world, peculiarly interesting, in view
of the pointed, prophecies of which it is the
realization and fulfilment. The civilized
nations, particularly our own FREE country,
and the savages of our country, are des-
cribed better than most writers of the pres-
ent can describe them, and yet the scene we
witness was foretold over a thousand
years ago. There is no more satisfactory
nor interesting reading than the prophets
of the East and West, particularly the lat-
ter. The changes that are to transpire in
the immediate future, we see from these
writings before their actual advent, and are
prepared for them; and with assurance of
safety for ourselves, we take out our pencils
and books and proceed to chronicle the ful-
fillment of inspired words of other ages. I
think those who are interested, will find
matter in this Centennial year to occupy
their leisure moments in narrating for the
benefit of future generations.
	Excuse so long and rambling a letter.
	Give my love to all the folks.
	May the Lord bless you and guide you in
all your labors by the inspiration of His
Spirit, is my desire.


[COLUMN 1]

Yours affectionately,
	JUNIUS F. WELLS.

PROVIDENCE, CACHE Co.
	July 26, 1876.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	Our Relief Society was organized April
27, 1868, under the counsel of Bishop W.
Budge. From that time up to the present
it has progressed very favorably. We have
endeavored to do all in our power to comfort
and bless the poor, the widow, the father-
less and the sick. We hold regular month-
ly meetings beside special ones which are
called on certain occasions. The sisters
manifest a kind and affectionate spirit to-
wards each other, willing to listen to and

[COLUMN 2]

practice the instructions that are given to
them. We have a kind, motherly, kind-
hearted sister for President, (Margaret
Matthews) who is always on hand to render
all the assistance she can, both temporal
and spiritual; to wait upon the sick, and
comfort those that mourn; this can be said
of all its members, for we desire to be one
with all our sisters in the labor of love; to
be useful in our day. We need not think
that we have nothing to do, it is not so; it
is true our brethren are called to go on mis-
sions to the nations of the earth to preach
the gospel, and to bring the Saints to Zion,
and we are permitted to remain at home to
take care of our families and to receive the
Saints that may come into our midst, to
teach and instruct them in the duties of
life. We see the necessity to encourage
HOME MANUFACTURE; we have quite a
number of young ladies learning to braid
straw. They have been quite busy during
the past season making hats and straw
trimmings, which is leading in the right
direction, for we sisters in the country are
obliged to use economy, for we are not like
our sisters in the large cities whose hus-
bands are merchants or clerks, etc., that
receive very large salaries, from $150 to
$300 per month, and mechanics that receive
from four to five dollars a day, whereas our
husbands that follow farming raise a little
wheat, potatoes, &c. &c., and if we did not
use economy we should never be able to
sustain our families. But you may say—
when we visit our sisters in the country
it seems by all appearances that they are
getting rich and able to build comfortable
houses to live in, and make other improv-
ments, while we in the cities (with all the
large salaries) can hardly live. Let us for
a moment look into this, and compare notes.
In the first place, who are these that go to
the theatre with their families, that go to
the grand balls in the winter season, that
dress up in the highest style, some of whom
pay from fifteen to twenty dollars to have a
dress made up for their wives and daugh-
ters? Who are they that send their sons
and daughters abroad to be educated? Do
they belong to the Saints that live in the
rural districts of this Territory? Oh no.
They belong to those persons who are in
prosperous business, or receive large sal-
aries. Is it true when they say they hard-
ly know how to live? Yes. They live so
extravagantly they are not able to pay their
tithing and their offerings to the Lord that
are required of them as Saints. When I
reflect upon the circumstances we are in as
Saints, surrounded with the blessings of
the Lord, and we make such foolish and
wicked use of them, and how ungrateful
we are for them, it seems strange that the
Lord is so good to us; how merciful He is
that He does not permit our enemies to
come upon us and drive us from our homes,
(which has been done frequently) how much
longer will the Lord bear with us as a peo-
ple? We are certainly as a people follow-
ing after the foolish fashions of Babylon.
	Our Society receives good advice and as-
sistance from our worthy Bishop, who is al-
ways ready to encourage us in our labors.
Our means are limited, but the sisters do-
nate all that is required to sustain the poor,
helping towards the missionaries and the
temple. We have shares in the Co-op. store
and have a first class knitting machine, with
which we intend to do all the knitting in
the place. The sisters made a quilt for the
Centennial, now we have sent it to the Fair
in Salt Lake City.
	At a recent meeting of the brethren some
expressed a desire that we should celebrate
the 24th of July. The Bishop proposed that


the sisters of the Relief society, assisted;
so the sisters met together and got up a
Programme, which was composed of speech-
es, songs and toasts; a sumptuous dinner
was given; about 300 persons including
children sat down; and in the evening a
dance, which finished the proceedings of the
day. All went off pleasantly, joy and glad-
ness beamed upon every countenance.
throughout the day. There were quite a
number of emigrants just arrived, and they
were cordially invited, with strangers from
other settlements. Among the speeches of
our brethren there were two missionaries
who had just returned from Switzerland
who spoke. They remarked they had just
returned from a mission and looked upon
their brethren and sisters in Zion, and com-
pared them, and their circumstances with
the condition of the Saints, there it was a
vast contrast; the Saints here could enjoy
life and be happy and comfortable, there
was nothing here to prevent them from
living their religion, but in the world it was
different; the Saints are poor and have to
work hard from morning till night, and get
hardly anything; and if those that are all
the time complaining and finding fault with
everybody and everything could be taken
back there it would do them good.
	The EXPONENT is very much appreciated
by those that take it here, and if more
would subscribe for it, it would be a great
benefit to them, because it contains articles
that are calculated in themselves to cause
us to reflect and meditate upon our conditions
as sisters and as mothers in Zion. There is
many an article that brings joy and peace to
those that read it, and I consider for myself
that it is one of the very best papers that
we sisters can subscribe for, it is very good
to have other papers in our houses (I mean
those that are good, or are edited by our
brethren) but for me, in my leisure moments
of reflection, give me the EXPONENT; there
I can find something to peruse suited to my
condition; some sister's letter wherein she
gives her experience of life in this church,
or her testimony on some certain principle,
which give new impulses to do good.
	Hoping that you will receive encourage-
ment in your labors in editing a paper so
useful among the Latter-day Saints,
				I remain Your Sister, &c.
					EMILY MADISON.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
				VISITING.

	There are three essentials which must be
understood and acted on before visits and
visiting will ever fulfill the idea of the
pleasure they ought to be and to give.
First, nothing must be done for parade or
show. No matter how rich a man may
be, if he sets on his dinner table one thing,
either to be eaten or looked at simply for
show, it is a challenge and offense. Of
course, nobody is so absurd as to maintain
that the rich must make dinners of herbs
because the poor cannot afford stalled oxen.
A very feast of good things, both for the
eye and the palate, can be set out without
any attempt at, or suggestion of mere par-
ade, mere display. There are plenty of
ways in which a man may advertise his
wealth if he likes, without doing it in his
hospitality. To do it there, or even seem
to do it there, is the very essence of vul-
garity, and real inhospitality. To avoid by
every possible device and by the utmost
tact, the bare suggestion that the guest is
the recipient of and not conferrer of favors,
is the first principle of entertainment. To
ask a man to your house and treat him as if
he were inspector of wines or tax gatherer

[Column 2]
on silver, is a piece of very coarse stupid-
ity; and to make a very great difference
between the way you live when you are
alone and the way you live when you have
friends under your roof, is surely a very di-
rect way of injuring one's self-respect, of
casting an unpleasant reflection on one's
own personal standard of taste.
	The second essential of the ideal visit
and visiting is, that the host not the guest,
set the limit of the visit. There is so false
a notion in this country on this point that
it almost seems as if it must spring out of
a national lack of directness and sincerity.
Everybody knows when he asks a friend to
his house, how long he wants him to stay.
There may be fifty reasons combining to
make him very glad of a visit three days
long, and very much inconvenienced by
one longer. Honest people ought to find no
difficulty in saying this and honest people
take no offense at hearing it. This does
away with all possibility of misconstruc-
tion on either side, with all uncertainties
as to welcome, with all fears of intrusion.
	A third essential of ideal visits and visit-
ing is that there be on the part of the host
no strained effort to entertain or amuse the
guest, on the part of the guest, no expecta-
tion to be amused or entertained. Simply
to meet for the interchange of good will
and cordiality, the usual current of life go-
ing on undisturbed, the habits of the fami-
ly remaining unchanged—that is the true
social visit. The man who knows how to
"drop in" of an evening draw his chair up
to your hearth as if it were his own, and
fall into the usual evening routine of the
household as if he were a member of it—
how welcome he always is! The man who
comes to stay under your roof for a season,
and who without being intrusive or famil-
iar, makes you feel that he is at "home"
with you, and is content in his usual fash-
ion of occupation—how delightful a guest
he is! And the houses—ah, how few of
them!—into which one can go for a day or a
week, and feel sure that the family rou-
tine is in no wise altered, the family com-
fort in no wise lessened, but on the contra-
ry increased by his presence—what joy it is
to cross their threshold! What good har-
bors of refuge they are to weary wanderers!
	When the world mends its ways in these
regards, when these essentials are fulfilled,
the lost art of conversation will revive;
hospitality will be worthy of its name; and
householding and housekeeping become, as
they ought to be, the greatest pleasure a
man can have.—H. H. in the Independant.

has no feet and yet wears shoes?—Dust
	THE woman of work sweeps everything
before her, while the woman of fashion
may be said to sweep everything behind
her.

wer.) Trying to lead a five pound exist-
ence on a two pound salary.
	BOGGS recently invested in a box of cigars.
He calls them tickets to a course of lectures
to be given by his wife.
	BROWN PERPLEXED.—Mrs. Brown tells
her husband not to sit in his shirt sleeves
or he will catch cold. How can a man sit
in his shirt sleeves?
	MRS. PARTINGTON expresses her appre-
hension that the people of the gold regions
will bleed to death, as the papers are con-
stantly announcing the opening of another
vein.


PATIENT, anxiously to doctors after con-
sultation:—"Tell me the worst gentlemen;
am I going to die?" Doctor: "We are divi-
ded on that question, but there is a majori-
ty of one in favor of your living."
	ONE GRAND object accomplished by the
visit of the Prince of Wales to India will be
the substitution of the Sanscrit, Arabian
and Persian languages for the Latin and
Greek classics at Oxford for the benefit of
Hindoo students and candidates for the In-
dian Civil Service.


HOUSEHOLD HINTS.

	Omelet:—To four well beaten eggs add one tea-
spoonful corn-starch dissolved in half teacup of
milk, one tablespoonful sugar, a little salt and
pepper; fry as an omelet.
	Lemon Cream Pie:—One cup sugar, one raw
potato grated, one cup water, one lemon grated,
and juice added, baked in pastry top and bottom.
This quantity is sufficient for one pie.
	To Extract Ink Stains:—Saturate the spots with
turpentine, letting it remain several hours; then
rub it between the hands, and the ink will crum-
ble away without injuring either the color or the
texture of the article.
	Furniture Polish:—One of the simplest and
best; get a bottle and fill with equal parts of boiled
linseed oil, and kerosene oil; mix and apply with a
flannel and rub dry with a second flannel. It
will remove all scratches and marks by bruising.
Destroy the rags after using, because they might
ignite spontaneously. An experienced house-
keeper recommends this polish, having used it her-
self for years.
	Cook and Housekeeper:—A good cook is not
the one who uses the most and and richest ingre-
dients, regardless of the expense; but she who
studies economy, and is able to concoct a delic-
ious meal from scanty materials. To be a success-
ful housekeeper, a woman requires as much sustem
and tact as she would to carry on any other busi-
ness.


HYGIENIC NOTES.

	Lightning Strokes:—Dr. Fothergill, says per-
sons struck by lightening are not always dead
when they appear to be so. There are few re-
coveries from it, because no means are tried to re-
store the sufferer. He believes that cold effusions
to the body has a decided action in such cases.
The free use of cold water under these circum-
stances cannot harm any one, and if only an oc-
casional recovery took place it would be well
worth the pains bestowed.
	Artificial Cooling of Roons:—M. Martin, in
the "Herald of Health" suggests a plan for the
cooling of rooms, which, it is affirmed, works ad-
mirably, especially for sick persons confined to
their bed or room. Open the windows wide and
hang wet cloths before them. The water as it
vaporizes absorbs the calorie, and lowers the tem-
perature of the apartment by several degrees,
while the humidity which is diffused renders the
heat much more supportable. By adopting this
plan even in the heighth of summer, you may re-
alize a freshened atmosphere, "analogous to that
which prevails after a storm."
	Liver Complaints:—The usual remedies are
iron, quinine, liquors, etc., but do not these do
more harm than good? One important item in
successful treatment of liver complaints should be
to find out its cause; there are a great variety of
causes, but among them all, perhaps our unwise
eating, sleeping and working habits have as much
or more to do with it than anything else, then the
best methods of cure, would be those which all
persons that are afflicted could apply for them-
selves, such as would b[-] eminently health-giving.

WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


WOMAN'S RECORD.

	MRS. CARABELLO, who died recently at
Canelones, Banda Oriental, had lived to the
extraordinary age of 120 years.
	MRS. JULIA N. Gott, of Ann Arbor, died
July 7, aged sixty years. She established
the first Young Ladies' Seminary in Ann
Arbor in 1840, and was herself a teacher in
the institution three years.
	MISS EMELINE Shepherd has on exhibi-
tion at Philadelphia a cambric handkerchief
the work of two years. It is embroidered
with a "fac simile" of Independence Hall,
a liberty cap and other national emblems,
valued at $500.00.
	SENORITA DONA Rosario Acunary Ville-
nueva has written and given to the public
in Madrid, a drama, founded upon "Rienzl
the Tribune, which has been very success-
fully played." The young lady is yet in
her twentieth year.
	MRS. ELIZA Esdaile, recently deceased,
was the only surviving daughter and child
of the poet Percy Bysshe Shelley by his ill-
fated wife Harriet Westbrook. She was
born in 1813, and could have no recollection
of her father; having been one of the three
children taken away from him by Lord El-
don, which circumstance called forth the
celebrated "Curse" to the Lord Chancellor.
	HON. MRS. Talbot of London, England,
has attained considerable celebrity in Wo-
mon's Work, by devising and practically
starting the "Parochial Mission Women."
She began this enterprise with three wo-
man, whose great and untiring labor in
ministering both to the physical and spirit-
ual wants of the poor in their own houses
were sufficient guarantees of the good faith
and earnestness with which Mrs. Talbot
forwarded the scheme. There are at present
183 of these co-workers, sincere and devot-
ed to the cause of carrying comfort to the
poor and unfortunate, and instructing them
concerning health and the management of
homes and children. "Mrs. Talbot is an in-
stance of the way in which all the great
questions of social politics are being met
and worked out by women, at the present
day, in England."


WOMAN'S VOICE.

	We the sisters of the Relief Society have
been having good times in my estimation
of late. We seem to be very united and
full of love for each other and willing to do
all we can for the cause and the kingdom.
We love our President and her counsel-
ors, and feel to be one with them in
all things. We are trying to strengthen
and aid each other, and we want to enlarge
on the right hand and on the left with all
the facilities we can bring to our aid. We
meet with our sisters in the south part of
the district once in two weeks and we en-
joy ourselves exceedingly. We had a most
enjoyable time yesterday the 24th of July.
We had everything arranged according to
our own ideas. The day was beautiful af-
ter lovely showers the night before, so we
had very little dust. The procession was all
that could be wished for. The twenty-four
old ladies looked splendid in their cos-
tumes, and I wish you could have seen our
girls, especially the Retrenchment Associa-
tion, they looked lovely, no long trains but
straw hats on their heads and white dresses,
plain and neat, and very becoming to
the "Daughters of Zion." It was all good
to me.
						E. MCKAY.
	Ogden, July 25, 1875.


A LADY asked her husband if her new
dress was not as sweet as a spring rose. He
said it was, even to the minor attraction of
having a little "due" on it.
	TRADE TOA[-]TS: A painter was at dinner
where several artists, amateurs and literary
men were convened. A poet, by way of
being facetious, proposed as a toast "The
Painters and Glaziers." The toast was
drunk, and the painter, after returning
many thanks for it, proposed "Success to
the Paper Stainers," and called on the poet
to respond.

WOMAN'S EXPONENT.

VOL. 6.	SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, SEPTEMBER 1, 1876.	No. 7.


For the EXPONENT.
	LINES.

BY A RETRENCHMENT GIRL.

Why are we to the things of earth
	So carefully endeared,
Forgetful of "The Kingdom" oft,
	Which must to God be reared?
Why do we vain and foolish thoughts
	Upon a home bestow,
Which winds or floods or flaming fire
	Might in an hour lay low?

A safe, bright home awaits us,
	A heavenly mansion fair;
The loved ones we have mourned as dead,
	Now live and worship there.
They'l welcome us with out-stretched arms,
	At the Eternal Gate—
Oh! happy, joyous, meeting—
	Sweet friends, how can we wait?

But ah! the richest boon of all,
	When this life's work is done;
Will be to know those Blessed Ones-
	the FATHER and the SON!
To look upon their faces kind,
	To press their gracious hands,
And hear their own lips teach the law,
	And utter wise commands.

How can we, and why mnst we wait!
	Yet, patience, O my soul!
Perform thy mission faithfully,
	All anxiuos haste control.
'Tis not for you to question why
	The way is long or rough,
But to be ready when the call
	Shall come, "It is enough."


[COLUMN 1]

For the EXPONENT.
MRS. DUNIWAY'S LECTURE.

	I suppose, dear girls, you will all think,
"Well, if I were going to write about any-
thing, I would write it in the season thereof,
and not wait till the circumstance was a
month old, and had been forgotten by every
one except myself!" But I'll tell you how
it is with me. When I am washing, iron-
ing, sweeping, cooking, sewing or running
after a mischievous baby I cannot be writ-
ing; and these former things occupy so
much of my time that I only find now and
then a spare moment for anything else.
	I went to hear Mrs. D's. Sunday evening
lecture, because—my mother said I might.
—I liked it very much too, except one or
two small insinuations which I considered
uncalled for, and which served to weaken
rather than to give vim to the otherwise
excellent speech. She told her audience
that she had milked cows and made butter
in abundance, in earlier days; she had
washed, cooked, plucked geese, taught
school, kept a milliner's shop and raised a
family of boys; she advised the girls when-
ever they considered themselves old enough,
and strong enough, and wise enough to take
upon themselves the responsibilities of
wifehood, to get married as she did. I
liked all that. "But," she added, "do not
imagine that I would have you take FRAC-
TIONAL husbands, as many of the women of
Utah seem perfectly willing to do." This
was one of the weak points which she pre-
sented, and I did not like it.
	What right, let me ask, has such a wo-
man to advise the girls of Utah upon a
subject of which she knows, practically,
nothing, ontradistinction to the teach-

[COLUMN 2]

ings of their own noble mothers, many of
whom have lived in polygamy for ten-
twenty and thirty years? Mrs. Duniway
appears to be a thoroughly practical woman.
I think she should let polygamy entirely
alone until she actually knows something
about it.
	The speaker gave a highly interesting ac-
count of her own personal experience.
she spoke with energy nnd earnestness in
favor of equality of the sexes, in the right
to control property and make laws concern-
ing it. She truthfully illustrated the lit-
tleness and insipidity of the souls of wo-
men who are perfectly content to let the
world move on in its own way, with no
thought for others because all is well with
themselves. This was only in reference to
property and woman's right to vote.
	She did not say as I would if I were a
lecturer, that every good woman has a right
to be married to a good man; and that, as
there are so many more worthy women than
men, it is noble and good for more than
one woman to accept the same man as a
husband. Of course I did not expect her to
say anything of the kind, but I felt I should
like to say it for her.
	I did not go to hear her Rail Road lecture,
though I wanted to very much. I had
been washing that day and was tired; then
I must have taken a long walk in order to
attend the lecture. Still I would have gone,
but that my mother said, "I think you had
better not my dear. I should like to have
you go and learn all you might, but you
have worked hard, and it is not best to
over-tax nature."
	I have learned, girls, that to listen to the
counsel of a good mother, pays better than
anything else.
	A FARMER'S DAUGHTER.


NOTES AND NEWS.

	THERE WILL be a Republican Convention
held at Worcester, Mass., Tues., Sep. 5th.
	A FREE HOSPITAL is about to be erecte
at the Begum of Bhopal, as a memorial of
the visit of the Prince of Wales to India.
The cost will probably be 80,000 rupees.
	MAURICE SAND, son of the French au-
thoress, warns all persons possessed of
manuscript copies of letters from his moth-
er, not to make them public without special
authorization from him.
	A STATE Convention is to be held in Bos-
ton on Tuesday, Sept. 12, 1876, at 11 o'clock
a. m., to consider their political duties in
regard to the fall elections; also to make
nominations for state officers if it is con-
sidered advisable. Men and women are in-
vited who are interested in Woman suf-
frage.
	A SCHOOL is to be founded to the mem-
ory of Lady Augusta Stanly, in the neigh-
borhood of Westminister Abbey. The
school is to be a Home and Training School
of Nurses. To the poor this lady was a con-
stant and tender friend, and always carried
with her the warmth and energy of true
sympathy in the hour of adversity or sick-
ness.
	HARPER'S WEEKLY says the truly inde-


pendent voters support Hayes, not because
they do not severely condemn much for
which the Republican party in any intelli-
gible system of party government must be
held responsible, but because, upon a sensi-
ble and practical survey of the situation
and the parties, they have decided that the
better element of the Republican party is
more likely to prevail for good results than
that of the Democratic.
	AT THE Exposition, near to Machinery
Hall, is situated the Turkish Cafe, a pretty
cheerful resort. A Turkish gentleman
from Constantinople presides over it, and
the attendants are in the picturesque cos-
tume of that locality. If you wish coffee,
it is made at once in little brass coffee pots,
each holding a cup, over a little charcoal
fire in a small furnace in one corner of the
room. The berry is ground as fine as meal,
and the coffee is poured into a tiny cup
which rests on a stand of silver filigree, the
liquor is as thick as cream; it soon settles,
and the beverage sweetened with rock
candy and served without milk is sure to
be delicious to lovers of good coffee. To
strangers this glimpse of Oriental life, its
people and customs is peculiarly interest-
ing. They will also serve you with Turk-
ish pipes and tobacco in their own peculiar
style.


FOURTH EPISTLE OF HAL TO
			THE "OLD MAID".

	I looked for you at the Normal Institute,
for I was there—were you? If you an-
swer me that you were really present at
those very instructive meetings, I believe
I may pronounce myself satisfied that I
know your name now, and should recognize
you if good fortune should ever chance to
bring us face to face.
	Please tell me if you were not greatly de-
lighted with Mr. Ottinger's lectures. I
thought them remarkable for clearness, and
besides drawing is a subject in which I am
much interested. I thought your face gave
evidence of interest during all the lectures,
but especially when something particularly
amusing was going on. I feel sure that you
were there, and that I made no mistake in
the one I singled out for you.
	You have not read Nasby's "Hannah
Jane?" You will find it in Hill's Manual,
which is an excellent book for young teach-
ers.
	You would like to see me, "just for fun"
would you? I am sorry to say that I am
not at all funny. But as funny people and
sedate people generally agree well together,
I hope to hear from you again very soon;
and
	If human forms were formed to soar,
		Like human fancy light and free,
	Here would I calmly wait no more,
		On hope's bright wings I'd fly to thee.
	But since we're doomed to dwell apart,
		And 'tis the common fate of men—
			(And women, too,)
	Thank heaven for this goodly art,
		Communication with the pen.—
			Fair maid, adieu.

ties—Tellus.

R. S. REPORTS.

	The General Retrenchment meeting held
in the 14th Ward, on Saturday, August 19,
was well attended. Counselor Mrs. B. W.
Smith presided. The speakers were spir-
ited and earnest, and seemed to be es-
pecially led to dwell upon the subject of
building Temples. Counselor Mrs. Sarah
M. Kimball alluded to the time when the
Kirtland Temple was being built, when the
Saints all considered that what they pos-
sessed belonged to the work of the Lord;
when the women would churn and cheer-
fully send their butter to the workmen on
the Temple and eat without any on their
own tables; then they were all hopeful and
joyous; she had never seen happier days in
her life that she knew of. Sister Mercy M.
Thompson spoke beautifully concerning the
giving of donations for the building of
Temples. She considered that the poor
saint's dollar, which was given with a
grateful hand out of a meagre pittance,
would go farther towards purchasing eter-
nal blessings in the kingdom of God, than
the dollar of a person whom the Lord has
blessed with abundance, so that the small
sum given would never be missed. A num-
ber of sisters bore strong testimonies to the
truth of the Gospel. At the close of the
meeting, Counselor Mrs. Phebe Woodruff
asked the sisters to remember in their pray-
ers—Sister Horne—the beloved President of 
the Associationwho has been so long unable
to attend.

HYRUM, CACHE Co., Aug. 17, 1876.
Editor Exponent:
	The members of our society are still alive
to the interests of the kingdom and al-
ways ready to lend a helping hand to the
poor and the needy, and to give a word of
comfort to the downcast. We held our 8th
Annual meeting on the 7th inst. There
were present on the stand several of the
brethren of this place and Sister Maughan of
Logan. Our report was laid before the
meeting which showed we had done many
good deeds in assisting the poor, donating
to the emigration, missionaries and Temple.
The brethren gave very good instructions
and encouraged us to faithfulness and per-
severance.
	Sister Maughan addressed the meeting a
short time, said our balance sheet was a
great credit to us; exhorted us to continue
in well doing. President Christina Liljen-
quist felt thankful for the good instructions
we had received, and asked the blessings
of the Lord upon the Saints. We had a
good time and the Spirit of the Lord was
with us; all seemed to feel to renew their
exertios to do good and to sustain our
President and those in authority. We
hope to build a house of our own next sea-
son and feel sure the Lord will bless us in
our endeavors. All is peace and quiet in
our little town and the Lord has blest us
with a bountiful harvest which the breth-
ren are very busy gathering in.
	Wishing success to your valuable paper
I remain your Sister,
	NANCY M. NEILSON, Sec'y.


[Column 1]
		SOME OF MY THOUGHTS.

	ADDRESSED TO THE RELIEF SOCIETY
				OF NEPHI.

	Owing to a diffidence which always at-
tends me whenever I attempt to speak be-
fore my sisters in our meetings, I thought
I would write a few lines which I surely

[Column 2]
could command courage enough to read.
When a vote of thanks was given me for
the faithful performance of my duty as
secretary my heart overflowed with grati-
tude and thankfulness to my heavenly
Father for the good feeling manifested by
my sisters, but I could not express my
thanks then, which I now beg you to ac-
cept.
	When a meeting was appointed for the
organization of a Relief Society, I thought
I would attend, partly from curiosity to see
how it would be conducted not expecting
I should be called on to occupy a place in
this society, but that I would, if permitted,
at some future day become a member. But
when I entered the house, before I took my
seat our Bishop called my name for secre-
tary. I thought it impossible for me to fill
that position, and therefore begged to be
excused on the grounds that I already had
as many duties as I could perform, and that
there were many others who could fill the
place much better than I could, not having
had the advantages of an education which
would qualify me for such a position. But
my excuses were not accepted and I was
elected. I knew my inability; and the
thought came, the Lord must help me or I
shall fail; I felt humble and asked His aid,
and am now willing to give Him thanks for
the honor that was bestowed upon me at
our last meeting. I have often noticed how
well our sisters in these societies fill
their positions, and I know it is because
they are humble and willing to try with the
help of the Lord to do what is required of
them. We are not all called to fill the same
places, but every one that does her duty in
the office assigned to her to fill, be it ever
so humble a one, is just as honorable and
deserving of praise as the one who occupies
the highest position; and I think I can say
in truth that our President and Counselors'
are just as willing to award praise where
they see one attending to her duties faith-
fully, whether in attending her meetings
or in staying at home to look after the fam-
ily that God has given her; for He will re-
quire her to guard well the jewels He has
entrusted to her care. And here let me
say how often has my heart been pained to 
witness the neglect that we as mothers are
guilty of in regard to our children. When
I see so many of our girls and boys run-
ning wild, disgracing themselves and their 
parents, and sinning against God, I think
we shall not all escape punishment. Moth-
ers, let me exhort you, with myself, to gird
on the armour of watchfulness, and never,
no never lay it off again until we have
rooted out this arch enemy (recklessness)
which has been the cause of so much evil
among our young people. What a stigma
on virtue and religion, what a disgrace to
our settlement, and above all what a sin be-
fore God! Arouse sisters and let us unite
in this great work that lies before us, and
let us call on our young sisters to assist for
the responsibility also rests upon them. I
feel that we have already put this matter off
too long and that God will hold us account-
able for the same. I presume that many
mothers feel that their children are young
and want to enjoy themselves, but we
must remember that because they are
young and have not the wisdom and experi-
ence of older persons, they are not pre-
pared to shun temptation, therefore they
need our watch-care and counsel. Better,
far better to curtail their pleasures or offend
them now, than to allow them to go unre-
strained and unprotected.
	If we take the right course with our
children when they are quite young, talk
kindly to them and tell them the conse-

[Column 3]
quence of an incorrect course, and point
out the path of virtue, and portray its
blessings and advantages, there will be but
very few who will not have confidence
enough in their mothers to heed what they
say, and in after years will bless them for
their strict requirements of obedience to
their wishes, and for their guidance and
care.
	When I commenced to write, I had no
intention of touching on this subject; but it
was forcibly presented to my mind and I
felt it my duty to pen some of my thoughts
in regard to it.
	Hoping that God will help us to be dili-
gent and faithful in all the duties of life,
and fill honorably the station assigned—to
each of us, I will add no more.
							A. L. B.


FLORAL HINTS.

	FOR RAISING FERNS:—Make a bed of
deep rich soil from the woods; in this bed
plant the native ferns; they will bear
transplanting at any time. This bed for
ferns should be on the north side of the
house or fence.
	THE rose requires high culture, and
should be planted in good, well-drained
soil, it can scarcely be too rich. The in-
sects which are most injurious to the rose
may be easily destroyed by syringing the
plants in tobacco water. Care should be ta-
ken in winter to protect them from severe
cold and frosts; cover them with leaves or
straw, or remove them to a cool cellar. A
nice way to plant rose-bushes is in a circle,
with some tall growing shrub in the cen-
tre; in this way they can better be pro-
tected in winter by throwing some straw or
light brush over them.
	CARE OF PLANTS:—Almost all house
plants require constant care and attention,
plenty of sunshine and fresh air, they will
not thrive without it. Windows which face
the south are the best for most plants; am-
monia water is one of the best fertilizers,
making the leaves fresh and green, also
liquid manure, but very weak. For some
delicate plants the Floral Cabinet recom-
mends glue-water; get the most common
dark glue, put it in a pitcher or jar, pour
boiling water over it, then when it is cold
dilute it and water the plants with it about
once a week; glue being made of bones and
refuse of various kinds is a very good stim-
ulant and makes the plants look healthy and
fresh. Sprinkle and wash plants frequent-
ly, and as much as possible keep them free
from dust.


CHINESE GARDEN:—The largest garden
in the world is in China; it covers an area
of fifty thousand square miles, round
Shanghai; it has been in a high state of cul-
tivation for hundreds of years. Three crops
a year are harvested from it; it is all mea-
dow land, raised a few feet above the wa-
ter, and with a complete net work of water
communication.


THE term "conservative," so says an
English writer of note, was first used in
the "Quarterly Review," for January, 1830,
by John Wilson Croker thus. "We despise
and abominate the details of partizan war-
fare, but we are now, as we always have
been, decidedly and conscientiously attached
to what is called the Tory, and which might
with more propriety be called the Conserva-
tive party."


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.		51


ISABELLA.

Oh, Woman! Genius, power, kingdoms,
Thrones, with all their charms and prestige—
Sceptres, subjects, ministering spirits—round,
Hover round thy footsteps, to watch thine eye,
To catch thy lightest word and that obey;
To stand in highest altitude, erect and firm,
And ne'er turn dizzy with the regal hight—
Who still is WOMAN, still her nature holds,
Pure, unsullied, as a queen should be,
Who has an ear for Truth, for Genius,
In whatever form it comes, though stript
Of worldly grandeur—all the world calls great;
And Standing like the far-famed obelisks
Of far-famed Egypt, alone, unread,
With naught to herald it but that intense,
That persevering Genius God bestows
On those He singles out to do His work;
His work on earth where man his agent is,
And woman too, his partner and co-mate
In all the grand, essential moves upon
The mighty chess-board of the game of life
Such was Isabella, Sovereign of Castile
And Queen of Ferdinand and Spain.
To Ferdinand—himself a king—she brought
Her throne, her kingdom, subjects, and herself,
And laid them at the feet of him to whom
More, more than all, she gave her loving heart—
A gem of which a monarch might yet be proud;
And he—most rarely found—proved worthy
of all the wealth she showered on his head.
They lived, and loved, and reigned conjointly;
Hand and heart united in their regal work,
When lo! a man in humble guise presents
Himself, and with them craves an audience.
He is admitted. In person, regal
As the pair to whom he is presented;
She has an eye at once to read the man;
A heart to feel he bears upon his form
The duplicate of HIM who gave the mission,
And sustained throughout, in ev'ry need the man
Ferdinand gives attention—Isabella
Enthusiasm. She felt the man of God
A messenger direct, and hence prepared
To learn his mission and obey his voice.
Columbus spoke! and then at once a queen
Stood forth as tho apostle of his cause;
A queen, a woman, a proselyte of truth,
A truth that did enrich his day and age,
Was first received by woman's gentle heart;
Isabella was that woman; and she
Never waver'd, but until death was true.
Before her court's indifference, before
His enemies, or his reverses, she
Believed him true, and was upon the throne
His proselyte, and to the grave his friend.

Ferdinand, in this, was worldly-minded;
Isabella saw, with spirit eyes, he
Was a man of destiny! a man of God!
And told the man appointed to investigate
The claims Columbus advocated,
That they were haggling with God the price of
Empire, and of souls, whom to idolatry
Their infidelity would leave. The king
Had not the faith that he could meet expense
So mighty as a naval outfit for
The man, who, in returning, promised
An Empire that should astound the world
And ages yet unborn.

Isabella listened to the colloquy, then with a
Burst of heavenly enthusiasm cried,
"I will undertake the enterprise alone
For my crown of Castile; I will pawn
My diamonds and my jewels, in MY eyes
Most worthless, compared with what is offered
By this great, good man, whom God has sent to us."
This noble burst of woman's feeling triumphed
The king, chagrined, consented to inspect
His treasury and see what could be done!
"Disinterestedness is the true wisdom
Of great politicians!" Columbus
Was re-called. He bow'd at the feet of her
Who'd nobly won for him the victory;
His soul was full of sweet emotions,
And she wept tears upon his noble head;
Ferdinand was moved and ratified the deed,
And into unknown seas the explorer passed.
	HANNAH T. KING.


TEMPLE FUNDS.

	The Countess of Huntington, who lived
about one hundred years ago, was a religious
reformer. We think she was one of the
noblest women of whom we ever read.
She gave her time and talents, her exten-


sive influence and her entire fortune to the
service of the Lord. So great was her de-
sire to advance the good work which she
had espoused that she allowed herself only
one new dress a year. Her means were
employed in building and supporting chap-
els in which converted souls might wor-
ship undisturbed by the show of pride and
vanity which characterizes common church-
goers.
	In contemplating the earnest zeal with
which that excellent lady worked for the
benefit and salvation of mankind, as far as
she understood, the thought arises, what
a vast amount of good might be done by
our sisters, if they were really as earnest
and as zealous in the cause of truth and
righteousness as she was. True she was
a lady of wealth and influence. She sold
her jewelry and used the means thus pro-
cured in the erection of a college for the
benefit of young ministers. No one of us
could do anything like that. But suppose
that several hundred or a few thousand of
us should put together all our best jewelry
—reserving, perhaps, some sacred tokens,
such as "the ring MY MOTHER wore," or a
wedding ring—would not the money thus
obtained do something towards building a
temple to our God?
	We have been informed that in some of
the Retrenchment Associations in this city,
five cent Temple Funds have been estab-
lished. In these funds, each young lady or
little girl may deposit a nickle once a week,
or as often as she can get one, instead of
spending it for a piece of ribbon or lace or
a bit of candy; an excellent plan, we think.
And not only should the officers of these
associations encourage the younger mem-
bers, more by example than precept, to
sustain and enlarge funds of this kind, but
mothers should find great satisfaction in as-
sisting to make such things items of spec-
ial interest.
						A MOTHER.


WOMAN’S RECORD.

	CHARLOTTE Bremer, the only sister of
the celebrated Frederika Bremer, and sharer
in her literary labors, died at Stockholm,
July 8th. She was born in 1799.
	THERE were more than two thousand
years ago women missionaries, one the
daughter of an emperor who, at her own
expense went to Ceylon and introduced
Buddhism into that island.
	MADAME Christine Neilson has recently
given over one thousand pounds to the
Committee of Diseases of the Throat. This
is the second gift from her generous hand
to the same institution, and was the pro-
ceeds of a concert given in May.
	MRS. Thomas, widow of a Baptist mis-
sionary, has been made superintendent of a
diocese in Burmah, which includes thirty-
nine native Karen pastors besides other
evangelists, schools, etc. The annual re-
port of the Baptist Missionary Union, speaks
of her new plans and labors, and her emi-
nent fitness for the position.
   MRS. L. H. Green, M. D. writes from
the Women’s Dispensary, Barielly, India:
"People of all castes come to the Dispensa-
ry. Ladies of the higher classes spend so
much time in retirement that they are glad
to go out under any pretext that may seem
to their friends respectable.
	MISS Jane Bay, of Baltimore, who died
recently, bequeathed over $200,000 to the
"Jane Bay Home for Boys." We agree in
the sentiments expressed by the Woman's
Journal. "How much better if she had re-
membered the still more destitute orphans
of her own sex, also." This institution
was incorporated by the last Legislature for
educating and training orphan and poor
boys.
	THE widow of Frederic VII of Denmark,
the Countess Danner, left her property val-
ued at $4,000,000, for the maintenance of an
institution for orphan and deserted girls of
Denmark. The central building of the in-
stitution will be the castle of Jagersprls, in
North Zealand, and it is expected will ac-
commodate [-]00, or 800 children.


PROFESSOR RIGGS ATTENDS THE
		EDUCATIONAL CONGRESS.

	At the three days session of the National
educational association, in the Academy of
Music, Baltimore, twenty-six states and
two territories, viz, Arizona and Utah were
represented. The papers read and the sub-
jects discussed, made the association one of
the most interesting and instructive char-
acter to the educator. At the close of the
association, the international congress as-
sembled for a one day's session in the same
place. The countries represented there
were: Of America—The United States,
Canada, Brazil and the Argentine Repub-
lic; of Europe—England, Lapland, Norway
and Sweden; of Asia—China and Japan;
the Sandwich Islands and Australia. The
speeches made by all those representatives
could be understood, except the Laplander's,
which was interpreted by Dr. Meyerberg of
Stockholm.
	After the adjournment, the teachers of
the public schools of Baltimore, tendered
the convention an excursion to Fair Haven
on the steamer Matilda. Fair Haven is
about fifty miles down the Chesapeake bay,
and was reached about 6 o'clock, where two
hours were spent and a collation of Mary-
land delicacies enjoyed. On the return
passage a business meeting of the conven-
tion was held in the cabin of the steamer,
at which the following resolutions, with
others, was adopted;
	Resolved, That, next to liberty, educa-
tion has been the great cause of the mar-
vellous prosperity of the republic in the


first century of its history, and it is the
sure and only hope of its future. The high-
est concern and the grandest duty of the
new century now opened is the right edu-
cation of every child born into American
liberty.				O. H. RIGGS,
			Ter. Supt. Dis. Schools.


KIND WORDS DO NOT COST MUCH:—They
do not blister the tongue or lips, and no one
ever heard of any mental trouble arising
from this quarter; though they do not cost
much, yet they accomplish a great deal;
they help one's own good nature and will.
Soft words soften our own soul; angry words
are fuel to the flame of wrath, and make it
blaze more fiercely; kind words make other
people good-natured; cold words freeze peo-
ple and hot words scorch them, sarcastic
words irritate them, and bitter words make
them wrathful. There is such a rush of
all other kinds of words that it seems de-
sirable to give kind words a chance among
them. Vain words, silly words, hasty
words, empty words, profane words, bois-
terous words, and a great variety of naughty
words. Kind words produce their own im-
age on men's souls, the image of beauty.
They soothe, and quiet and comfort; they
shame away sour, morose and unkind feel-
ings; kind words should be used in abund-
ance.
