SALT LAKE CITY, SEPT. 1, 1876.


[COLUMN 1]

TAKE CARE.

	Autumn has come again. September with
its fair cool mornings, its mild and pleasant
days and chilly evenings is with us once more.
Now is the time when thoughtful florists
and gardeners begin to shelter their tender
plants; cautiously watching for the approach
of early frosts, making preparations to ward off
the same, and prevent all injurious effects.
	And now, oh! anxious mothers, is one of the
times when special care should be exercised
over the bodies and health of the little human
plants which God has placed in our charge.
Do not wait until freezing winter weather
comes before you think of the childrens' wool-
en dresses and stockings. Already they should
be in readiness to supply the places of light
summer clothing.
	It does seem as though enough has been said
and written to revolutionize the world in this
respect. And still there is astonishing evi-
dences of much ignorance and want of thought
on the subject. A woman comes into a meet-
ing house where religious services are being
conducted, and as she enters, the damp, chilly
atmosphere fairly makes her shiver although
she is wrapped in a warm woolen shawl; yet in
her arms she carries a bright, fat baby, dressed
in white jaconet, with bare arms and neck,
blue with cold, and thin cotton stockings
which do not come to the knees! Such in-
consistencies are difficult to imagine, yet as
day by day they are brought to notice, the
stern reality of their existence, and even of
their prevalence, cannot be denied. When will
the people learn, and when will they put in
practice the good they know? Sometimes
there seems to be so much on hand, so many
things crowding at once, that it is almost an
impossibility to bestow the requisite care up-
on the babies. But mothers should always
keep in mind the fact, that it does not require
half the time or half the trouble to take care
of a well child, that it does to nurse a sick one.
If all things cannot be accomplished as a wo-
man may desire, let something go undone.
Let her can or dry less fruit than she intended,
and give the surplus, which would otherwise
be wasted, to her poor neighbors. Let beds go
unmade or kitchen work undone if necessary,
let anything else be neglected rather than the
children. And if by close care and attention
they can be kept in good health and spirits,
let every one carry a cheerful face and a thank-
ful heart, and let no one fret about what has
not been done. or cannot be done.
	Never regard symptoms of disease in a child
as nothing serious, but study how to take wise
precautions against dangerous illness. And
when in the case of a child being slightly in-
disposed, anything ought to be done, let it be

[COLUMN 2]

done at once and not put off for a more conve-
nient time when other matters have been at-
tended to. By saying "take care," we do not
mean to be over anxious and uneasy and full
of undue solicitude all the while concerning
the little ones. Wise care prevents cause for
anxiety, saves trouble and expense, and often
averts sorrow.
	The autumn will come again; beautiful
flowers, delicious fruit and rich, golden grain
will blossom, mature and ripen. But if one
of the children is taken from us, we can never.
have it back while we remain in mortality.
Yet when care and watchfulness have failed to
retain the little being here, there is still con-
solation in the reflection that neglect or im-
prudence had nothing to do with its suffering
or its departure.
	Be mindful of your own health mothers, for
your own sakes, but more particularly for the
sake of your babies. You will lose less time in
taking care of yourselves if you do so, than in
being unable to work if you do not.


[Column 1]
		A GLANCE AT THE FUTURE.

	There is in the nature of all intelligent per-
sons a definite or undefined longing to look for-
ward into the future and to discern by the pres-
ent transpiring events, or judge from the past,
what the indications are of coming prosperity or
evil. When any great change is on the tapis
this is more especially the case; people cannot
but question and wonder, what the great move
will bring about, and whether it will promote
and foster an influence for good or ill.
	In America perhaps more than in any other
country in the world, the majority of the peo-
ple interest themselves understandingly in the
subjects of rulers and governments; because
each person considers himself a citizen of free
and independent principles, and a part of
the great body politic.
	Then the educational system of this grand
country has been established upon such a
broad scale as to reach all classes and therefore
they are taken as a whole, better enlightened
on all practical subjects and interests. It
would seem to one not knowing anything of
the real wickedness and corruption that exists
in the United States, and possessing a knowl-
edge of the laws and constitution, and being
familiar with the geography of the country,
its great internal resources, and the enlighten-
ed condition of its inhabitants, that of all peo-
ple in the world the people of America should
be the most prosperous; but how far from this
is this their present condition? Underlying
the surface which is not in itself altogether
free from turmoil and ripples, is an element of
contention seething and boiling and foaming,
ready to burst its bounds at any moment if not
quelled and kept in subjection to the powers
that be.
	The people at the present time seem to
think, that whatever change occurs, matters
really cannot be made worse, and naturally
hopeful, expect that the next presidential era
will assuredly settle some of the many diffi-
culties now pending. The corruption of men
in power has become the theme of common
conversation, and there is scarcely a child old
enough to read that does not seem somewhat
informed in regard to crime in high places;
with such men at the helm, what can be ex-
pected of the state of public society?
	It is said and justly too, that happy homes
and well-disciplined families are the true ele-
ments of great institutions, and social and
and general government; but there can
scarcely be any permanent, thorough home-

[Column 2]
happiness when the sovereign power of the
nation is vested in men who have no genu-
ine interest in the homes of the people.
	Of course it is humiliating for a nation who
have boasted so much of freedom and liberty
to acknowledge that they are wrong, that they
have allowed unrestrained license to those in
power, instead of the liberty of action, which
the rights of the CONSTITUTION guarantee; but
the wrong has become so undeniable [-]o out-
rageously self-evident that every intelligent
person must perforce perceive that some posi-
tive change is necessary.
	Liberty implies responsibility, and that is
not liberty which resolves itself into selfish-
ness and indifference to another's rights; this
odious attitude of public matters totally and
hopelessly demolishes all real society all true
general government. If there are any persons
so blind to the present state of national affairs
as not to be able to see the elements of total
wreck and dissolution, they have only to open
their eyes and ears to attain completely this
uncomfortable conviction. The great difficulty
in the matter is, how wisely and prudently,
to select and then apply a sale and speedy
remedy; and for such an issue at the present
crisis, with all the differences of opinion which
exist, is there any sure foundation or funda-
mental basis for believing such a change will
be effected?


HOME AFFAIRS.

	MESSRS. G. M. Ottinger and C. R. Savage
have started for Philadelphia where they pur-
pose remaining a few days as visitors to the
great Centennial Exposition.
	THANKS FOR the "Utah Musical Times,"
number six of the publication: it is the first
we have had the pleasure of perusing. A jour-
nal devoted to music, art and literature ought
certainly to be of great advantage in a com-
munity to encourage refining and elevating
influences. We are pleased with its contents
and wish it success.
	MRS. DR. Ellen Fergurson who is at present
residing in St. George, recently gave a lecture
in that city. Her subject was Rome. We had
the pleasure of seeing Mrs. Ferguson and her
husband when they were in Salt Lake on
their way to St. George. Mrs. Ferguson is a
woman of very pleasing manners and appear-
ance, has traveled extensively, and has had a
superior classic and a thorough medical educa-
tion, with considerable practice.
	HON. GEORGE Q. Cannon delegate to Con-
gress from Utah, with his wife and daughter,
has returned home, arriving Monday evening
Aug. 21st. He has given most complete satisfac-
tion by his labors the past years, to those most
interested in the future welfare of Utah, and
truly merited the good feelings of the people he
has served. We greet him welcome to Utah
and his mountain home.
	THE DESERET University opened as usual at
the University Buildings, Mo[-]day Morning,
Aug. 21st, Dr. J. R. Park, Pri[--]cipal. The
rules and regulations and the system of in-
struction are similar to that of pre[-]ious years.
The teachers substantially the same; the at-
tendance is as large as could be expected so
early in the season. Dr. Park and those en-
gaged with him spare no pains to make the
school in every respect equal to similar insti-
tutions in the East. Dr. Park himself is a
teacher of vast experience, and one who mani-
fests the truest interest in the development
and education of the young.


[Column 1]
	MISS M. E. Cook will open her Graded
School in the Social Hall on the first Monday
in this month, September 4th, assisted by her
former teachers. Miss Cook has taken great
pains and been untiring in her efforts to es-
tablish this system of school here; she has so
far succeeded as to draw forth encomiums of
praise and commendation from persons inter-
ested in the subject of schools and education in
Utah, and from strangers who have visited her
school and taken pains to go through her rooms
and hear recitations. Miss Cook has taught for
many years and is a thorough and energetic
worker in the cause of education in the school
room and elsewhere. Her terms for tuition
will be the same as usual.
	MRS. WELLS: Yesterday I had the pleasure
of an interview with Mrs. Porter; a Silk Rib-
bon weaver.
	Mrs. Porter has just arrived from England
and has brought harness, reeds, shuttles &c.
&c., with a model of her loom. This morning
she goes to Bountiful, Davis Co., where she
expects to locate, and, as early as practicable,
resume her accustomed occupation.
	It is to be hoped that through the assistance
of our energetic sisters in Bountiful and else-
where, we can trim our hats with Ribbons of
Home Manufacture.				E. R. S.
	S. L. City, Aug. 19.
	THE NORMAL Institute closed its regular
session Thursday, Aug. 17th. There has been
a great deal of instruction given during this
term which cannot fail to be an advantage to
those engaged in teaching, and indeed to all.
There was a report from the committee on res-
olutions tendering thanks to Prof. J. R. Park
and others who had assisted him, the rail-
road companies, and all who had favored the
Institute in any way. Prof. Park returned
his thanks and also in behalf of all interested,
and made some most excellent closing remarks
which it would be well for every person to re-
member and profit by. He exhorted the teach-
ers to remember always at all times and in all
places, in the schoolroom and out of the school
that their conduct should be most exemplary.
He told them how closely children watched
the conduct of teachers and those who were
placed over them; consequently how much
evil a bad example might work upon the
minds of those who were too young to discrim-
inate for themselves. He said the future hap-
piness and prosperity of a nation depended up-
on the training and education of the young;
and that teachers could not be too careful and
judicious in the course they pursued, them-
selves; and by example as well as precept in-
stil into the minds of pupils true principles.
	The Institute in its session this year number-
ed 158 members, including some from almost
every county in Utah. These reunions will
serve to give tone and elevation to the system
of education and by bringing together the best
talent of the country promote the advance-
ment of literature and science.
	HON. JOHN Taylor and Hon. J. F. Smith,
with the teachers of the territory, met in the
University Building Sat. Aug. 12th, and
Monday Aug. 14th, to discuss the question of
suitable school books for the territory. It is
well known that some of the superintendents
and teachers of schools have declined accept-
ing some of the schools books adopted by the
convention. The result of the present conven-
tion was the adoption of certain specified reso-
lutions which we give in full below.
	Resolved, That the following resolutions be
submitted by the secretary of this convention
to the honorable superintendent, O. H. Riggs,
of Utah, as the sentiments of the teachers of

[Column 2]
this territory, and that the same should be
published in the leading newspapers of said
territory.
	Whereas, The acts of the late convention
held by the superintendents of schools of the
territory April 6th, 7th and 8th, 1876, for the
purpose of adopting text books in accordance
with section 16 of the school law of Utah, fail-
ed to meet the approval of many of the citi-
zens of said territory, in consequence of which
said act failed from being carried into execu-
tion; and
	Whereas, Said failure has been attended
with much inconvenience to our schools, in
some instances even breaking up classes, thus
placing our teachers in a very embarrassing
position.
	Therefore, it is the voice of this convention
that the Hon. O. H. Riggs call a convention
for the purpose of unravelling the present
difficulty, and if possible bring about a change
before opening our winter schools.
	In offering this resolution this assembly does
not presume to criticize the acts of said con-
vention, but simply from necessity calls for re-
lief, and appeals to those whose right it is to
say what text books we shall have.
			CHARLES WRIGHT, Chairman.
	ARTHUR PARSONS, Sec:
	WE HAVE had the pleasure of a call from
Elders' M. B. Young and M. H. Hardy who
have been traveling for some months past,
most of the time through the settlements in
the interest of the Young Men's Mutual Im-
provement Associations. They visited most of
the settlements South as far as St. George, and
organized in all the towns where it was deem-
ed practicable; they express themselves as
having been treated with the utmost kind-
ness and hospitality by bishops and people,
and everything done as far as possible to ad-
vance the special mission in which they were
engaged. Br. Young while South made quite
a collection of minerals and curiosities to add
to his cabinet; among them were specimens of
petrified corn which must have lain at least a
hundred years, but were perfect in outline.
Upon the top of a very high mountain he
found a great number of petrified oyster shells,
perfect, and some of them closed together, nev-
er having been opened, proving beyond contra-
diction that the sea had once covered that por-
tion of the country.
	Bro. Hardy informed us they had, while
traveling, visited the day and Sunday Schools
and spoken to them whenever they could. At
Monroe they had been very much gratified
with the manner in which the school was con-
ducted by Mrs. Bean, a lady who seemed to
have a thorough understanding of her work.
She had a class in the Book of Mormon which
was very interesting; this Book as a history of
this country and its former inhabitants and
government, ought to be well understood by
the rising generation, and especially by the
children of the Latter-day Saints.
	On the 10th of July Elders' Young and
Hardy started North to visit that part of the
country on the same mission; they held their
first meeting at Brigham City, with the young
people there in a general assembly and had an
interesting time. They then proceeded through
Cache Valley visiting almost every town,
organizing associations at Hyrum, Smithfield,
and Wellsville; thence to Soda Springs and on 
through Bear Lake Valley holding meetings
and organizing Associat[-] societies in nearly all
of the towns and settlements. Br. Hardy in-
formed us that he had been distributing tracts
and pamphlets containing the first principles
of the Gospel, through the Sunday Schools and
associations in the settlements; it will no doubt

[Column 3]
have a tendency to facilitate the work of the
Sunday School—teachers very—considerably.
There are many of the children who are not
sufficiently informed of the fundamental prin-
ciples of the Gospel. Elder Hardy has been
for some years assistant to Dr. J. R. Park in
the Deseret University in Salt Lake City, he
has now gone to assist Br. Carl G. Maeser in
the Academy at Provo. We wish them the
success and patronage we are sure they emi-
nently deserve.


﻿WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


RUNNING IN DEBT.

	I dwell on this point, for I would deter
others from entering that place of torment.
Half the young men in this country, with
many old enough to know better, would go
into business—that is, into debt—to-mor-
row, if they could. Most poor men are so
ignorant as to envy the merchant or manu-
facturer, whose life is one incessant struggle
with pecuniary difficulties, who is driven
to constant "sinkning," and who, from
month to month, barely evades the insolv-
ency which sooner or later overtakes most
men in business; so that it has been compu-
ted that but one man in twenty of them
achieve a pecuniary success. For my own
part I would rather be a convict in the
State prison, a slave in a rice-swamp, than
to pass through life under the harrow of
debt. Let no young man misjudge him-
self unfortunate, or truly poor, so long as
he has the full use of his limbs and facul-
ties, and is substantially free from debt.
Hunger, cold, rags, hard work, contempt,
suspicion and unjust reproach are disagree-
able, but debt is infinitely worse than them
all. And if it had pleased God to spare
either or all my sons to be the support of
my declining years, the lesson which I
should most earnestly seek to impress up-
on them is, "never run in debt." Avoid
pecuniary obligations as you would pesti-
lence and famine. If you have but fifty
cents and can get no more for a week, buy
a peck of corn, parch it and live on it, ra-
ther than owe a dollar! Of course I know
that some men must do business that in-
volves a risk, and must give notes or other
obligations, and I do not consider him in
debt who can lay his hands directly on the
means of paying, at some little sacrifice all
he owes; I speak of real debt—that which
involves risk and sacrifice on one side, ob-
ligation and dependence on the other—and
I say from all such, let every youth humbly
pray God to preserve him evermore.—
Horace Greely.


WEIGHT OF LIGHT: Scientists have
now proved that light has weight, it seems
impossible to associate light with weight
so long have we looked at it in another sense,
that it seems incredible to associate the two
together, but from the fact that light exert-
ed a certain repellant force upon whatever
it fell, Mr. Crook, at the Royal Institution
in London, has lately demonstrated not
only this truth, but that this force or weight
could be measured. Of course this has all
been done on a very minute scale and with
a very finely constructed apparatus; yet it
suffices to give a standard, and from this
the calculation is that sunlight falls with a
weight of thirty-two grains to every square
foot, fifty-seven tons to the square mile, or,
3,000,000,000 tons on the whole earth, a
force that would be sufficient to drive our
globe from its orbit, and send it flying off
into space, but for the still more gigantic
influence of gravity."


54		WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
						For the EXPONENT.
		ACTION OR INDIFFERENCE.

			BY BLANCHE BEECHWOOD.

					[handwritten] E. B. Wells

		"Act! for in action are wisdom and glory;
		Fame, immortality—these are its crown;"

	Does not the very indication of the times
demand sober, earnest thought and prompt
and decisive action? And are not the ques-
tions now before the public, such as call for
the united influence of men and women?
What shall be said then in this day of
great social questions when modern society
seems to have arrived at a culminating
point which requires some immediate at-
tention, when crime has so steadily and
surely worked its way into popular and
prominent places and positions, that it is
almost countenanced if not actually sanc-
tioned; what shall be said I repeat, of those
possessing ability and influence who sit in
listlesness and indifference and do not lift
their vioce, or in anywise deprecate this
state of things, seemingly apathetic on the
score that it has nothing whatever to do
with them?
	Let the favored ones, the fair daughters
of the nation who look with such apathy
upon all the abstruse questions of public
and general interest, pause and consider;
they fare sumptuously, their lives are pleas-
ant ones, their homes are elegant, their ta-
bles luxuriously supplied, their husbands or
fathers are perhaps attentive to their every
wish, the world pays them homage, it mat-
ters not to them who wields the power of
state, who sits at the helm; they think not
of the many who must toil or starve, wives,
widows, mothers or sisters, who sue in vain
for some recognition of their equality with
their brother MAN; widows and orphans un-
justly deprived of their honest dues, whose
entreaties and intercessions pass unheeded;
are they not a part of the same great fam-
ily, one universal brotherhood; and if in-
justice is dealt out to some, should not all
feel interested? Think of this you who sit
carelessly with folded arms, while the daugh-
ters of the nation by hundreds and thou-
sands go down into the great whirlpool of
sin and wickedness, their names dishonor-
ed and their eternal future blighted, their
hopes of happiness forever wrested from
them; will you not even ask the cause?
Are you so unconcerned as to allow such
tragedies to be enacted under your eyes and
not even question the justice of such ine-
quality as visits shame and dishonor upon
the head of woman while man the offender
goes scatheless?
	Women, you who are surrounded with
peace and plenty, or competence, with lov-
ing hearts to cherish you and strong hands
to bear you up, think you, that you will be
held guiltless because it is not your daugh-
ter, sister or mother that is wronged?
Should you allow the cares or pleasures of
this world to so absorb your attention that
you cannot take any interest in those im-
portant questions which involve the gener-
al happiness or misery of the nation? Are
you quite sure, you who do not wish to get
mixed up in vexed questions, that you will
be free from blame? Who has absolved
you from duty to your fellows? Is it be-
cause you are so ignorant of the terrible
consequences that are involved by even one
person leading a life of sin? If so, where
is your excuse? Everywhere around you
are the means of enlightenment on these
points; and lectures, public meetings and
conventions where you can learn of these
things if they do not come under your im-
mediate observation; is not this very plea
of ignorance a sin in itself? Are you not

[Column 2]
interested in the welfare temporal and spir-
itual of all the great world of mankind?
Have you not then a desire to know by
what laws countries are governed, and
whether all women YOUR SISTERS, are free
to live their own lives, to follow out their
own convictions of right and wrong, and to
worship God according to the dictates of
their own consciences?
	"I leave all these subjects of discussion to
my husband," says one lady, "he attends to
all these things, it is out of a woman's
sphere, I detest women who mix up with
political affairs and talk like men, I would
not vote if I had ever so much freedom."
These are the sentiments of a large class,
usually happy wives, content to live only
in their husbands affections; as yet, their
homes have never been invaded by any
wolf in sheep's clothing; but the majority
of women have not such homes, are not so
happily situated. And death often takes
away the husband or father, the supporter
and defender, and those so carefully nurtur-
ed by tenderest care, have to struggle with
the world, face to face in the conflict, with
no strong arm for a shield and support, and
should injustice be meted out to them, they
may reflect they could better have informed
themselves in the days of prosperity before
the evil days came. Women shrink away
abashed at the sight of one of their own
sex who they know is openly living a de-
graded life, that of an outcast, and think
themselves modest, and that the presence
of such abandoned ones would pollute them;
they do not want to breathe the same at-
mosphere. Meantime let me ask where
are the men who have caused the downfall,
the ruin, the utter recklessness of these
poor creatures, whose garments must not
touch yours? The world does not frown
upon them, society receives them with open
arms, they are FAST, A LITTLE FAST, by and
by they will settle down into respectable
fathers of families. Yes, and what will
they expect their wives to be— women of
spotless purity. Will they not be unequal-
ly yoked? Why should not woman de-
mand the husband to bring a spotless repu-
tation to the marriage altar?

						"But not alone
	The girl is pure: the boy with beardless lip
	Will blush as fair as any girl, and shrink
	From all unchaste, or evil word or deed
	As virginal as she; and God designed
	No more that woman should be pure than man.
	Your bodies are the temple; know ye not,
	In which the Holy Ghost was made to dwell!"

	Boys should be held morally responsible
as well as girls, and little misdemeanors and
digressions from rectitude should not be
over-looked in them and license allowed
them in the matter of self-restraint, on the
plea that "boys will be boys." There is an
underlying cause and it is useless to at-
tempt the cure of an evil unless the cause
is ascertained; society tolerates certain
wrongs in men simply from long-continued
custom, while it visits with scathing ven-
geance an irreperable blight and disgrace
upon women for the same offences. Should
not those mutually guilty receive the same
censure, or punishment irrespective of sex?
	Men have the reins of power, they "hold
the fort," but in this age of progress, if wo-
men are awake to the great events which
must transpire while these grand evolutions
are going forward, and are found ready to
lend a helping hand in each great move up-
on the "chessboard of life," or within the
arena of action, if they stand firmly side
by side with man in any great social, polit-
ical or national quostion, then may they
work for the advancement and enfranchise-
ment of their sex, be it ever so quietly or

[Column 3]
unobtrusively. In the meantime be thank-
ful for small favors, although they may be
viewed as something doled out more as a
peace offering, than an inherent privilege
by right, yet accept such gladly as a prelim-
inary step, a firm footing gained in the lad-
der of advancement; make use of the rights
allowed, act as far as possible in unison with
each other, and never let one point gained
be so lightly h[-]ld that it will slip from you.
	No intelligent woman should consent to a
grievous wrong or injustice even by her si-
lence; but by all the eloquence and influ-
ence she can bring to bear, seek to instil in-
to the minds of all who come within her
sphere, such principles of honor, justice and
equity as cannot fail to make an impression
upon the side of right.
	Women who are happy wives, who are
surrounded with all that adds beauty and
attraction to life, who have personal and so-
cial influence with MAN, the ruling power,
could, were they fairly aroused, do most in
the cause of enfranchisement which would
materially benefit the condition of all, be-
cause their very position will command fa-
vor and power, and they would not be held
up to ridicule as seeking for notoriety, or
acting from a spirit of revenge; they would
not be caricatured as "hybrids." If such
women could be made to realize the terri-
ble condition of the working-girls whose
wages are not a compensation for labor, but
a mere pittance which will hardly suffice
to keep them from starvation, and that thus
they are the more ready prey of the seducer,
could they be so indifferent? In many in-
stances the net is spread for the girls by the
too wily employer, or those who are suffi-
ciently acquainted with the circumstances
to take advantage of their poverty, and in-
nocent and unsophisticated, they walk into
the snare without warning.
	Give them equality in all respects, and
let each work with the same chance of suc-
cess according to the labor performed, with-
out the consideration of sex, and there
would soon be a vast change which would
be of material benefit to the existing state
of society. But be not discouraged; small
beginnings are the most auspicious, and no
great achievement has been popular at the
outset, and so surely as "coming events cast
their shadows before" it is self-evident that
woman's cause is surely and steadily ad-
vancing; and men who possess the sound-
est principles morally and socially, if they
are really honest, will forward the move-
ment and understand the true policy of a
united effort. Woman does not seek for
any independent power, but only to become
a co-worker in the future development of
human progression, and mutual recognition
of right.
	And why not? There are plenty of in-
stances to prove that women have made
themselves famous in science, art and liter-
ature; and as rulers, have not the reigns of
queens been as successful and judicious as
those of kings?
	You may say they are only exceptional
cases where women become celebrated for
any particular genius, power or excellence
of talent or ability; but is it not equally so
with men in comparison? Woman has had
more difficulties to contend with, her ad-
vantages have been fewer, but let them
have the same opportunities for an educa-
tion, observation and experience in public
and private for a succession of years, and
then see if she is not equally endowed with
man and prepared to bear her part on all
general questions socially, politically, in-
dustrially and educationally as well as
spiritually.
	S. L. City, Aug. 21st, 1876.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[COLUMN 1]

AN INCOMPLETE CENTENNIAL.

	But the ceremonies were incomplete.
Some Frenchman,—if possible, a soldier of
the Republic, should have stood shoulder to
shoulder with orator and poet, bearing his
witness that the old charter is a living let-
ter at the century's close.
	And again, there was incompleteness, but
there was fitness, perhaps, in the omission.
The magnificent chorus that alone gave ex-
pression to the opening day, the chorus
which owes its existence to Philadelphia
women, was absent from the ceremonial of
the Fourth. Created by the energy and
musical taste of the Women's Committees,
there was something typical in the fact of
its absence. A hundred years ago, Abi-
gail Adams, the wife of one President and
mother of another, was writing to her hus-
band. "I long to hear that you have de-
clared an independency. And, by the way,
in the new code of laws which I suppose it
will be necessary for you to make, I desire
you would remember the ladies, and be more
generous and favorable to them than their
ancestors were. Do not put such unlimited
power into the hands of the husbands." And
again in another letter,—"I cannot say I
think you are very generous, for while you
are proclaiming peace and good-will to men,
emancipating all nations, you insist upon
retaining absolute power over wives."
	The women of the Centennial Commit-
tees have had three years of education.
Their services have been sought, their as-
sistance invited, the profits of their hard
work have been given to build up the en-
terprise, yet not unfrequently has the snub
been administered, reminding them that
the old century men, still claim "absolute
power" over them and their belongings.
They may have had feeble powers of reas-
oning, it is true, when they began to work,
but in the severe school of adverse decis-
ions and continual subordination, their per-
ceptive faculties have grown and widened.
They refuse any longer to accept palaver in
the place of justice. Resolutions of thanks
is one thing, recognition is another.
	If they apply the experience gained in
judging of some other matters, the school
of their training must shoulder the respon-
sibility. They fail to perceive why the
Michigan woman, who settles upon govern-
ment acres, preempts her little farm by cul-
tivating it, making a home for her child-
ren and her drunken husband, is denied
the privilege of ownership, which that hus-
band could have obtained at the govern-
ment's hands any day he was sober enough
to claim it. To make out her title to these
acres her own hand had planted and sown,
it was necessary for the United States Sen-
ator to tell her whole sad story to his fel-
lows, and by a special act of Congress ena-
ble her to own what she had paid for to the
United States. The beautiful discrimina-
tions of the law, which forces the hard-
working wife to wait for special legislation,
dragging the sad circumstances of her life
into public discussion, must be encouraging
to other married women, driven by hard
necessity to make homes for themselves
and children.
	Again, we fail to understand also, if it
was highest virtue, in Boston men, a cen-
tury ago, to throw into the harbor tea that
did not belong to them, because they would
pay no taxes without their own consent,
why should it be the best kind of a joke
when two ladies of Connecticut, who own
more property in their township than ten of
their neighbors, shall refuse any longer to
pay taxes, thereupon, unless granted some

[COLUMN 2]

power in spending, for the township's good,
the rates levied upon them, which their un-
taxed neighbors have full disposal of. We
fail to perceive the logic that makes the
tea-chest a heroic emblem, while the year-
ly sales by the sheriff, from the Glastonbury
farm are newspaper jokes from Maine to
Georgia.
	Again, Abigail Adams was happily mar-
ried, but she had the courage to speak for
other women less favored. She could look
out from her secure shelter by her hus-
band's side, and see how other mothers suf-
fered at the hands of the law. So long as
the law, now, as then, gives to the father
absolute power over his child, the power to
send it whithersoever he will, away from
its mother, standing faithfully at his side,
the power to will it with his dying breath,
into other care and guidance than hers, no
wonder that this far-sighted woman of the
Revolution told the men of the day, that
they were ungenerous and cruel. The wo-
men of '76 ponder these things in their
hearts. They rejoice with unfailing grati-
tude, at the victories won, the difficulties
conquered, and the power and freedom the
old century has brought, with its declara-
tion of rights to all men; they look wist-
fully into the new, requiring that it shall
give unto them also; some surer ground to
stand upon than the shifting sands of for-
tune, and the caprice or indulgence of the
law-making sex—New Century for Women.


HYGIENIC NOTES.

DEATH IN THE NURSING BOTTLES.

	The following we clip, by request, from an old
number of the Scientific American. The subject
thereof is of such vital interest to all mothers; that
we would advise them to read carefully and profit
by the same.
	"But there is a prolific source of infant
mortality, to which we wish now to direct
special attention, namely the patent nursing bot-
tle. It consists of a rubber tube, one end of
which is held in the child's mouth; the other end,
passing through a cork, is attached to a glass rod
which descends to the bottom of a bottle of so-
called milk. We might write a page on the dan-
gers that reside in the milk, unless special care has
been taken to obtain fresh, or by suitably diluting
pure condensed milk. But this danger is well
known, and our business at present is with the
bottle, or rather its dirty tube, which should nev-
er be used more than once, then thrown away and
a new one bought.
	Even when new, these white tubes, impregna-
ted as they are with oxide of zinc, are not unobject-
ionable: far worse are they when saturated with
sour milk, germs of putrefaction, decay and dis-
ease. Some of these child-murdering, Yankee
inventions have reached Berlin, and have called
forth the following from a practising physician of
that city:
	"The supposed advantage of these bottles con-
sists in this, that they can be placed beside the in-
fant in bed, while other bottles must be held in
the hand all the time. What sensible mother or
nurse would leave a child with a bottle without
watching it? The danger of the bottle consists
in this, that it is absolutely impossible to cleanse
it. When sucked on, little particles of milk be-
come attached to the tube and cork; these curdle
and soon turn sour. If some of this deposit be
placed under a microscope, we see innumerable
bacteria, organic beings which indicate decomposi-
tion and decay. At every meal the child draws
in thousands of these germs. The decomposing
process acts upon what it finds in the mouth,
æsophagus, and stomach, and the result is diar-
rhea, cholera infantum, etc. I will here express-


ly remark that the usual method of placing the
apparatus in water, or merely rinsing it out with a
stream of water, is in no way sufficient. Some
dealers sell a suitable little wire brush with the
bottle, but even this does not answer the purpose,
for the apparatus is not clean by a long way after
drawing the brush through it several times; and
who will take the trouble to clean it so thoroughly
eight or ten times a day? How much time it
would require! Another disadvantage is that the
bottle is air tight, and a partial vacuum is formed,
which renders sucking so difficult as to exhaust
the child, and it stops before its hunger is satis-
fied. Hence, parents, ye who are compelled to
feed your children with a bottle, throw away this
apparatus, which can only bring destruction upon
your children, and either select a bottle with glass
mouthpiece, which is filled from below, or take a
large rubber mouthpiece, which is perforated by
a small hole and can be drawn directly over the
neck of the glass bottle. This large mouthpiece or
nipple can readily be turned inside out and thor-
oughly cleaned and rubbed with dry salt."
	After reading the above, we sorrowfully ac-
knowledge that our own experience warrants us in
saying, that many infants, children of parents re-
siding in Salt Lake City, and Utah Territory,
have succumbed to cholera infantum, as a result of
using the Patent Nursing Bottles above referred
to, and the heading thereof is true that there is
"Death in the nursing bottle."			C. S.


HOUSEHOLD HINTS.

	Glue.—A teaspoonful of saltpetre added to a
large potful of glue will effectually prevent it from
smelling; besides, it causes it to dry faster and
harder than it would without it.
	A New Mucilage.—The Journal de Pharmacie
states that if, to a strong solution of gum arabic,
measuring eight and a half fluid ozs., a solution of
thirty grains sulphate of aluminum dissolved in
two thirds of an ounce of water should be added,
this makes a very strong mucilage, capable of fas-
tening wood together, or of mending porcelain or
glass.
	Apples Pickles:—Quarter and core the apples,
then steam them until a fork will go through
them readily. Make a syrup of sugar and vine-
gar and pour over them while it is hot. Stick a
clove or two into each quarter. Seven lbs. of ap-
ples prepared to three lbs. of sugar and one pint
of vinegar are good proportions.
	Drop Cakes:—One teacupful of cream; one of
sour milk, one teaspoonful soda, a pinch of salt,
two teacupsful of flour, two tablespoonsful of su-
gar with one egg stirred well together, and drop-
ped from the spoon into a dripper well buttered.


LATE MARRIAGES:—It is greatly to be
regretted that young people are reluctant
to marry because they cannot at once set
up house-keeping in an expensive or stylish
manner. Society as a whole is seriously
endangered by these absurd and false ideas
of social economy, and late marriages have
become largely characteristic of modern so-
cial life. Young love if true and godly
will make early struggles wholesome and
joyous, and let not the prudence of such a
question be pressed too hard; if a young
man has a position that will enable him to
marry with ordinary carefulness, and if he
has found a woman, who he believes will
make his home happy, and be a true com-
panion, let him marry and take her to a
humble home, let them practise such econ-
omy as is consistent with their circumstan-
ces. It will discipline them in the right
path and give them an experience they
could never have gained in affluence. Life
will be the better for it and fortune more
acceptable and more wisely disposed should
prosperity beam upon them and success at-
tend their efforts.


56		WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


MISCELLANEOUS.

	OF all the celebrated lovers of George
Sand, two only survive—Jules Sandeau,
the first love of that passionate, undiscip-
lined heart, and Prince Napoleon. Jules
Sandeau, who is the first novelist who ever
gained by reason of his novels alone a fau-
teuil at the Academy, is still a very fine-
looking man, bald-headed and stout it is
true, but still of a pleasant and comely pres-
ence, and of peculiarly graceful and win-
ning manners even for a Frenchman.
	DIFFERENT TYPES OF THE REINDEER:
—Mr. Lamont, an English sportsman and
member of Parliament, who has made
many summer yacht voyages to the Arctic
seas, says that the reindeer in Nova Zem-
bla is much larger and wilder than in Spitz-
bergen, and he is disposed to believe that
the smaller and tamer variety found in the
latter island is allied to the reindeer of Nor-
way and Lapland. As these animals are
noted for their wandering habits, Mr. La-
mont conjectures that they may have
made their way originally into both is-
lands from the continent, over seas not usu-
ally frozen, but which then happened to be
so. It is a curious fact, however, that al-
though Nova Zembla is much nearer the
continent of Europe than Spitzbergen is,
the reindeer there is of the wild and large
American type, while those of Spitzbergen
more nearly correspond to the European
variety.


WOMAN'S VOICE.

	A few words of exhortation especially to
the young, praying to the Lord to direct
my thoughts that I might through the me-
dium of the EXPONENT bear my testimony
and add my voice to that of my sisters and
co-workers in the great work of educating
the young and rising generation spiritu-
ally; we look forward to them, to take their
part in assisting to establish the kingdom of
God upon the earth, and my prayer is that
the Lord will bless them with wisdom and
power to overcome everything which is op-
posed to the kingdom of God, and that they
may carefully guard themselves against
the temptations of the adversary and be
prepared for the great changes that are
about to take place here on this earth, even
the coming of our Lord and Savior–Oh
that we may be prepared to behold him
with joy in the Temple of the Lord. Then
let us endeavor to become sufficiently pure,
that we may become saviors of our dead
friends who cannot be made perfect with-
out us, or we without them; let us consider
how anxiously they are waiting for us to
do our part.
	I pray mightily to the Lord to bless me
with wisdom to do my duty, that I may
have joy. And it is the desire of my
heart that I may know the duties I should
perform for those who are gone behind the
veil without having the opportunity which
has been granted to me.
	Sisters let us live our religion, be true to
our covenants, and faithful in observing
counsel.
					JEMIMA BROWN.
	20th Ward, S. L. City.


"BROTHER, why don't you ask the stran-
ger to pray?" "Because," reprovingly ob-
served the deacon, "this ain't no place for
practical jokes. That man's the president
of a gas company.".


SCIENTIFIC NOTES.

	STEEL which has received the hardest
temper takes the strongest magnetization
	A BOILING Lake is reported to have
been discovered in the Island of Dominica,
in a mountainous region, 2,500 feet above
the level of the sea.
	M. JAMIN, treating upon magnets, says
he has proved that a bundle of thin steel
plates is stronger than a single steel bar of
the same dimensions, the proportion in-
creasing indefinitely with the number of
plates.
	MR. Lamont, the Arctic sportsman, in
speaking of the transmission of sound by
water, says "I am perfectly sure that vibra-
tions of a boat striking against the ice-edge
are conveyed a distance of two or more
miles. Directly the boat comes in contact
with the ice every seal for miles around
raises his head and is on the alert.
	MULTIPLEX TELEGRAPHY: Recent ex-
periments have proved it is possible for two
messages to be simultaneously passed
through a single wire called "duplex tele-
graphy;" and experiments have also been
made in "quadruplex telegraphy;" in one
instance an experiment was successfully
carried out, whereby eight messages were
sent simultaneously from each end of a
wire two hundred miles in length.

WOMAN'S EXPONENT.
VOL. 5.	SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, SEPTEMBER 15, 1876.	No. 8.


GIVEN NOT LENT.

Friends of faces unknown and a land
	Unvisited over the sea,
Who tell me how lonely you stand,
With a single gold curl in the hand
	Held up to be looked at by me,—

While you ask me to ponder and say
	What a father and mother can do,
With the bright fellow locks put away
Out of reach, beyond kiss, in the clay
	Where the violets press nearer than you,

Shall I speak like a poet, or run
	Into weak, woman's tears for relief?
Oh, children!—I never lost one.—
Yet my arm's round my own little son,
	And Love knows the secret of Grief.

'God lent him and takes him,' you sigh;
	—Nay there let me break with your pain:
God's generous in giving, say I,—
And the thing which He gives, I deny
	That He ever can take back again.

He gives what He gives. I appeal
	To all who bear babes—in the hour
When the veil of the body we feel
Rent round us—while torments reveal
	The motherhood's advent in power,

And the babe cries!—has each of us known
	By apocalypse (God being there
Full in nature) the child is our own.
Life of life, love of love, moan of moan,
	Through all changes, all times, everywhere.

He's ours and forever. Believe,
	O father!—O mother, look back
To the first love's assurance. To give
Means with God not to tempt or deceive
	With a cup thrust in Benjamin's sack.

He lends not; but gives to the end,
	As he loves to the end. If it seem
That he draws back a gift, comprehend
'Tis to add to it rather—amend
	And finish it up to your dream.

So look up, friends! you, who indeed
	Have possessed in your house a sweet piece
Of the Heaven which men strive for, must need
Be more earnest than others are,—speed
	Where they loiter, persist where they cease.

You know how one angel smiles there,
	Then courage. 'Tis easy for you
To be drawn by a single gold hair
Of that curl, from earth's storm and despair
	To the safe place above us.  Adieu.
		—MRS. E. B. BROWNING.


[Column 1]
		ESSAY ON EDUCATION.

READ BEFORE THE TOOELE RETRENCHMENT
			ASSOCIATION,

	Having been appointed to write a com-
position I will endeavor to comply with
the request; although I am conscious that
my abilities for the task are very limited.
The subject that presents itself to my mind
is Education.
	Webster, in giving a definition of the
term, says: "Education comprehends all
that series of instruction and discipline
which is intended to enlighten the under-
standing, correct the temper and form the
habits of youth, and fit them for usefulness
in their future stations. To give children
a good education in manners, arts and scien-
ces is important. To give them a religious
education is indispensable. Our daily pur-
suits of life should be such that we could
gain information. Our language at home
such that there would be no offense taken
from it. Our manners should be courteous,
pleasant, and charming; so much so that

[Column 2]
those around would be kept in a good hu-
mor and a pleasant state of feeling. The
education of manners depends greatly upon
the kind of society in which we mingle;
almost unconsciously we will gain refine-
ment, from intercourse with the cultivated
and educated classes; while the contact with
low, vulgar minds, must, even insensibly,
affect our own manners. A certain young
lady could not comprehend the counsel of a
good father who advised her not to mingle
with certain parties; he picked up a cinder
from the hearth and presented it to her re-
questing her to take it into her hand; she
drew back thinking it would burn her; but
her father assured her it would not burn
for it was cold, she then took it into her
hand, and her father said; there, although
it has not burnt it has blackened your
hand; so if you desire to preserve pure and
refined manners, mingle not with the vul-
gar and low, lest you blacken your charac-
ter and pollute your manners. We should
strive as an association to converse with
those who are wiser than ourselves or
whose age and experience have given them
greater scope for observation than we have
had. We should always seek the assistance
of our God by prayer in passing through
the events and pursuits of life.
	In our minds this question may arise;
what kind of associates should we strive to
become acquainted with in order to edu-
cate ourselves? Now this is a question of
such great importance that we should not
pass it over without a glance. Says one.
Why should I be so very particular about
the company I keep? We should be partic-
ular in this matter, because if we form a
friendship with an ungodly, disobedient,
unwise, reckless and frivolous person, we
are liable to pattern after them; or at least,
to drink of the same spirit; hence, let our
friends be selected from among the God-
fearing, virtue-loving people, they are the
kind that God is pleased with; and His
promise to the meek and lowly is that "they
shall inherit the earth."
	Now my dear sisters, let us educate our-
selves in the ways of God, that we may be
able to do His will; and become instruments
in His hands in building up this church and
kingdom. This is the humble prayer of
your Sister in the New and Everlasting
Covenant.
						M. L. L.


HOW TO CHOOSE A WIFE.—That young
lady will make a good wife who does not
apologize when you find her at work in the
kitchen, but continues at her task until it is
finished. When you hear a young lady
say, "I shall attend church and wear
my old bonnet and waterproof cloak, for I
fear we shall have a rain storm," depend
upon it she will make a good wife. When
a daughter remarks, "Mother, I would not
hire help, for I can assist you to do all the
work in the kitchen," set it down that she
will make somebody a good wife. When
you hear a young lady say to her father,
"Don't you purchase a very expensive or
showy dress for me, but one that will wear
best," you may be sure that she will make
a good wife.


NOTES AND NEWS.

	THE ANNUAL Meeting of the Woman's
National Christian Temperance Union will
be held in New York City, October 25. The
delegates from each Congressional District
should be chosen before that time.
	A CORPORATION has been formed in the
District of Columbia, to be known as the
Woman's National University, for the pur-
pose of affording to women a thorough
knowledge of the sciences, divinity, medi-
cine and law, both in theory and practice.
	REPEATED EXPERIMENT has proved that
in schoolrooms lighted by windows on both
sides the children suffer more or less from
injured vision; and so important has the
subject been considered in Germany that a
law has been passed forbidding such win-
dows in schools.
	WE LEARN through the columns of the
"Woman's Journal," that in Massachusetts
at the present time they have a political
party, equally composed of men and women,
which makes one of its avowed objects the
establishment of "Woman's Suffrage."


OUT-DOOR EXERCISE FOR
MOTHERS.

	Consider it your religious duty to take
out-door exercise without fail each day.
Sweeping and trotting around the house
will not take its place; the exhilaration of
the open air and change of scene are abso-
lutely necessary. O, I know all about
'Lucy's gown that is not finished," and
"Tommy's jacket," and even "his" coat,
his buttonless coat, thrown in your lap, as
if to add the last ounce to the camel's back;
still I say, up and out! Is it not more im-
portant that your children in their tender
years should not be left motherless, and that
they should not be born to that feeble con-
stitution of body which will blight every
early blessing? Let buttons and strings go;
you will take hold of them with more vig-
or and patience when you do return bright
and refreshed; and if every stitch be not
finished at just such a moment (and it is
discouraging not to be able to systematize
in your labor, even with your best efforts),
still remember that "she who had done
what she could" is entitled to no mean
praise. Your husband is undoubtedly "the
best of men," though there are some ma-
licious people who might say that that is
not saying much for him. Still he would
never, to the end of time, dream what you
were dying of; so accept my advice, and
take the matter in hand yourself.


MR. F. G. HEATH in his useful and in-
teresting work "The Fern Paradise," tells
us what a fern is, and how it may be dis-
tinguished from other varieties of plants.
A fern is a flowerless plant, but although
flowerless not seedless, but its peculiarity
is that it acquires its seed without the in-
tervention of flowers. Ferns belong among
plants to the class "Cryptagamia," which
literally means "concealed fructification."
They are therefore flowerless seed-bearing
plants, and when they bear seek hide, it
away.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
		R. S. REPORTS.

		PROVO CITY, Aug. 30th, 1876.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	We notice in your valuable paper many
reports from various parts of our Territory,
of the exertions made for the improvement
of the youth in each community. We take
interest in the rise and progress of the Lit-
erary and Mutual Improvement Association
of this city, believing that we are doing an
important work. It was organized by coun-
sel of Sister Eiiza R. Snow, under the su-
perintendence of Mrs. M. T. Smoot, in
company with the four presidents of the
Relief Societies of this place, Nov. 7th, 1873,
with 27 members; the young ladies presided
alternately for a time, after which Mrs.
Jane Jones was elected to the presidency,
and was subsequently succeeded by Mrs.
Margaret A. Cluff July 20th, 1875.
	We now number one hundred and fifty-
seven members. The Association meets
every Thursday evening, and the exercises
consist of reading from church works and
other good books, with occasional addresses.
We also have a semi-monthly paper, enti-
tled the "Young Ladies' Diadem," with
motto "Virtue, Faith in God and Earnest
Endeavor," edited by the young ladies as
per appointment by the president; the con-
tents of which, consist of original essays,
poetry &c., by the members.
	We have made several quilts, which with
the proceeds of an entertainment given by
the Society in the "Brigham Young Acad-
emy" Aug. 18th, 1876, amounting to about
one hundred dollars, is to be appropriated
to the building of the Temple in S. L. City.
We take the EXPONENT and Juvenile,
besides we have a number of Church books
and other good works which are read in the
Association.
	Our meetings are generally well attended
the young ladies manifesting a great inter-
est in the work of improvement.
	Wishing prosperity to the Woman's pa-
per, and all other enterprises for the pros-
perity of Zion we subscribe ourselves,
		Your Sisters &c.,
			ZINA B. SMOOT, Sec'y.
			EMILY G. TILL, Assistant.

				SALEM, Aug. 27th, 1876.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	The Young Ladies' Retrenchment Asso-
ciation held their first Annual Meeting
July 17th, 1876. Opened by singing and
prayer; after which some excellent peices
were recited by members of the association.
Remarks were then made by Brothers' Levi
Jackman and Robert Davis President of
this place, Clous Baker, President of the
Young Men's Mutual Improvement Asso-
ciation, and Willford Crocket; also by Sis-
ters Lucinda Jackman and Josephine
Curtis, each encouraging the young ladies
to be faithful to the work they were en-
gaged in; said the time was shortly coming
when they would have to rouse from their
stupor and help to bear off the kingdom of
God, with many other excellent remarks.
I will send the remarks of Father Jackman
in full as they were his last in public, and
perhaps there are some who knew him
that would like to hear them. He said: I
am always in my element when I have the
privilege of encouraging what I call the
"HOPE OF ISRAEL." There are a great
many who are in the habit of addressing
public audiences that have their sermons
written down, some write on one subject
and some another, the subject to-day is to
the youth; how often I wish that I could

[Column 2]
picture to you your present condition; the
young have got to have a knowledge of
the truth for themselves, they cannot gain
it by hearing another's experience, they
must know it for themselves. I would say
to my young sisters who have been recit-
ing, you have done remarkably well, and
I feel to say to you, go ahead and God bless
you in your efforts; it is not time thrown
away, and you will gain confidence in your-
selves by so doing, so that you will not be
frightened to arise before a congregation
three times as large as this and speak or re-
cite. I would say to the parents encourage
your children to learn all that they can
while young, for the time will come when
they will not have the chances they
now have; they will grow up and become
mothers in Israel and will have the cares of
a family on their minds; then they will not
have the opportunities that are now present-
ed before them to improve their minds.
This is why I am so anxious for the young
to learn all they can now. There are those
in our midst who are doing all they can to
overthrow the kingdom of God, if they
find they cannot get the advantage of the
older ones, they will try the young; they
pretend to be Saints of God but they care
no more for God and His laws than they do
for the wind that blows, nor half so much,
they worship Mammon and care only for
SELF; but God is able to do His own work
and don't need any of their help. After
awhile the cry will be made "Behold the
Bridegroom cometh, go ye out to meet
Him." Have we got oil in our lamps, or
will we have to borrow of them that have?
What do you mean by having oil in our
lamps? It means to get the Spirit of God
and keep it burning in your bosoms. I
will say to those to whom we have been
listening, you have done honor to your-
selves, and those around you, I don't think
that there is one here that would say they
have not been greatly edified. I could talk
all day, but I will close now by asking God
to bless you all in your endeavors to do
right, Amen.
	Our Association is doing well under the
guidance of our beloved President Sister
Martha Curtis and her Counselors. The
young sisters take great interest in these
meetings, and are willing to respond to any
call that is made of them.
	May God bless all who are interested in
the kingdom of God, is the wish of your sis-
ter, &c.
					ALMIRA JACKMAN.

		SPANISH FORK Aug. 25, 1876.
EDITORS' EXPONENT:
	The Annual Meeting of the Relief So-
ciety of this place was held July 31st, '76.
The meeting was well attended and a good
spirit prevailed. The sisters bore their
testimonies, exhorting to faithfulness and
diligence in the building up of the kingdom
of God, knowing that the time is short and
much to be accomplished. Made mention
of the loving kindness of our Heavenly
Father; expressed their desire to aid by
their labors and means to build Temples, to
sustain HOME INDUSTRIES, and also to
bless the poor, the fatherless and the wid-
ows. They expressed their satisfaction at
the result of our year's labors as a society.
	Report of the year ending June 2nd, '76,
is as follows:

	The Young Ladies' Association is prosper-
ing under the presidency of Mrs. Mary
Morrison (formerly Miss Warren.) They
hold their meetings once in two weeks, and
the Relief Society once in two weeks, they
meet with us and we meet with them, so
we meet every week, one meeting for la-
bor and one for spiritual improvement.
	May God bless the humble efforts of all
who are laboring for the advancement of
His Kingdom.
	Your Sister in the Covenant,
						MARY FERGUSON.

		SPANISH FORK, Sept. 4th, 1876.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	We held our first Annual Meeting March
31st. There was about one hundred and
thirty-six assembled in the Social Hall at 2
p. m. The time passed very pleasantly,
and about 5 p. m. when we all dined at the
same table. Every one seemed filled with
a spirit of union and advancement and all
felt like moving on with renewed energy.
Bishop Snell and several of the brethren
were present and gave some excellent in-
struction. Bishop Snell said it filled his
heart with joy when he contemplated the
progress we had made in one year. We
have held our meetings every Monday un-
til lately, when we meet every alternate
Monday with the Relief Society. Since
our last report we have been called to
mourn the loss of Sister Mary Cornaby,
who died May 1st, 1876. She was a faith-
ful member of the Retrenchment Society
and was beloved and respected by all who
knew her.
		Yours Truly,
			LUCY H. SNELL, Sec'y.
		SARAH WARNER Ass't Sec'y
		TOOELE CITY, Aug. 25th, 1876.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	The Young Ladies' Retrenchment Asso-
ciation of Tooele meets every other Thurs-
day afternoon at two o'clock. We have
excellent times in assembling together.
Our older as well as our younger sisters
strive unitedly to make our meetings as in-
teresting as possible. We endeavor to main-
tain the principles of the Gospel as contain-
ed in the Bible, Book of Mormon, Doctrine
and Covenants, and other Church publica-
tions; likewise those precious counsels that
flow continually from the lips of the living
oracles of God. Your valuable little paper
is like a welcome messenger in our midst,
greeted with smiles of approbation from all
quarters; and when one of our sisters holds
it in her hand to read from its pages we
feel assured that something good is coming,
and we are filled with joy and gladness.
We endeavor to discipline ourselves in the
noble and exalting principles of truth: and
we hail gladly the tidings of the progress of
Retrenchment throughout the Territory as
communicated through the columns of the
EXPONENT.


BY Mohammedan law, all sculptures and
engravings of the human face are strictly
orbidden.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


COLUMBUS.

A moment let me view, as on the disk
Of mental vision—and by the aid of
Memory's mystic gift, the portrayed
History draw of this most patient man!
The majesty of man, in him, behold!—
His stature, tall, erect, and powerful,
Ever towering above his fellows!—
He wore a bland, yet theughtful countenanee,
The impress bearing of divinity.
Pale by study, brown'd by sun and sea;
No levity, or carelessness were trac'd
Upon his form or visage, every gesture,
Gravity and deliberation showed.
Among the potentates he stood a king,
Though poverty enwrapt nought could hide,
The majesty, that as a mantle cover'd him!
The prime of life he scarcely had attain'd,
Yet mental care had prematurely bleach'd
His auburn locks, and streaks of grey appeared.
His voice, deep, sonorous, and impressive,
Like one accustomed to profound reflection.
He wore a modest self-respect the aspect
Of a worshipper in audience of Deity.
Columbus!—Yes, 'tis he! whose name has rung
Throughout the circumnavigated globe!
Strange! that the Hemisphere exhumed by him,
And placed as "Beacon on a hill" before
The wond'ring world, bears not his noble name!
His great, his noble, mystic, thrilling name!
—Like imbecile ingratitude this seems!
No matter! he has yet a FUTURE—
And among the Harmonies of heaven,
Myriad, yet will tune their golden harps
In praise of him; endowed of God, and sent
To bring before the world a hidden land,
And fore-ordained to cradle gospel-fruit,
And be the Nursery of the First-born's Church.
Columbus! Blessed genius! Blessed name!
God's instrument for a work stupendous!
Isabella, with all her strong convictions,
Her warm and rational enthusiasm,
And all the whisperings within her heart,
E'en SHE dreamed not the glory of her work!
She saw Earthly Empires and present good,
And in a measure this was true and right,
But little did she dream GOD'S KINGDOM
Was to rise from out the Empire Columbus
Was impelled to seek and resurrect.
"God's Kingdom cometh not with observation."

So the work progressed, the day has dawned,
And light breathes in upon the mystic work
That Isabella and Columbus organized;
And their names shall be enthron'd forever,
And Ferdinand the TRIO shall complete.
Pioneers of untold glory were they!
God worked with them to impel them on,
Hence nought could daunt them.—On and on they
	went,
And HE, the instrument, was made to bear
Calumny, and scorn, and pinching poverty,
And the imputation "Thou art mad!"
Maniac. dreamer, visionary man!
And thus he lived, assuring them of truth,
Till youth was gone and much of manhood's prime
Until a WOMAN God inspired to hear
The message he for years had yearned to tell;
A regnant queen his helpmeet was to be,
And she his mission nobly did endorse,
And unto death remained his firm ally.

How strange an atheist is man! Show him
A NEW TRUTH, and he will spurn you from his door.
He rarely says, reason be THOU my guide,
I will INVESTIGATE, and then decide;
But no, it comes in contact with my views,
My theory, and my understanding;
The "canon's full," and I am also full,
Being fed on such a nutriment. I,
On such a canon, hence, will live and die—
Such was my father's creed, and therefore mine!
Such is the logic of the worldly-wise,
And such the "Daniels" in the judgment seat;
So TRUTH is veil'd till God sees fit to tear
The mummy wrapping, and HIS truth declare.
Then those who've crushed conviction rise to truth,
And worship her with all the warmth of youth;
And then she'll reign immortal and for aye—
And all her mandates they with joy obey.
	HANNAH T. KING.


"JOE," said Tom, "I heard a certain fel-
ler say to his girl, 'shall I have your utensils
engraved on that ring?' What do you
think of that?" "I think," answered Joe,
"he must be the same fellow who asked
the girl to let him ride in her phantom."


A MEANS OF HIGHER EDUCATION
FOR ENGLISH WOMEN.

	Among the many efforts which have been
made in different parts of England to aid
in giving women opportunities of continu-
ing their education after they have left
school, and of consolidating it even when
the thought of school days has been long
left behind, none have been more persistent
or more successful, says a London journal,
than those made at Bristol. During the
last eight years the Clifton Committee for
Promoting the Higher Education of Women
have organized and kept in action lectures
and classes. This year, however, they do
not propose to have classes or lectures of an
independent character, and the reason for
this determination is that Bristol is about
to have a college of its own—the University
College; and this college, which will be
opened in October, will admit women to its
lectures, and will also organize for them
separate classes in the various subjects treat-
ed by its professors and lecturers, and it is
believed that these classes and lectures at
the University will supply an education
more definite and systematic than that
which has been given hitherto by the class-
es for ladies. University College is the
first English institution which has recog-
nized the higher education of women as
one of its primary aims, and the committee
in their circular hope "that so unusual an
opportunity for mental training will be
turned to good account by those for whom
it is intended." For the first time men
and women students will sit in all the lec-
ture rooms of the college, a part of the lec-
ture room being appropriated to women.
The classes which will follow up the lec-
tures, and which will give more detailed
instruction, will, however, not be "mixed,"
but in them ladies will be taught alone.
The subjects which will be at first taught in
the college will be chemistry, modern his-
tory, modern literature, mathematics, ap-
plied mechanics, experimental physics, pol-
itical economy, and classical history and lit-
erature. There are also to be evening class-
es, open to students of both sexes, taught
in all the above named subjects by the same
professors and lecturers, and in such other
subjects as may seem to the council to be
required. Medical education is to be pro-
vided by the Bristol Medical School; but it
is not said that women are to be allowed to
attend this special branch of instruction.
Four scholarships for women will be offered
at the beginning of October, of the annual
value of $60 each, tenable for two years at
the University College, Bristol. These schol-
arships will cover the expenses of lectures,
classes, and registration. It is proposed to
make up two of these scholarships to the
value of $250 each, if the successful candi-
dates should need help to enable them to
continue their college course.


THE common sense of Americans has
long ago perceived the necessity for some
women to be self-supporting. Feminine
dignity and delicacy as sung by poets and
vaunted after-dinner orators, when the
last toast is drunk and the best gift to man
is gushingly if somewhat incoherently
cheered, is as pretty as painted china. Un-
til the State is prepared to furnish elegant
shelving for such porcelain, in other words,
shelter and maintenance befitting its ex-
treme fragility and preciousness, the finan-
cial crash, with its complicated problem of
wreck, constantly repeats itself; and the
question arises, what is to be done with
china, for which there is no place in the
world?—New Century for Women.


WOMAN'S RECORD.

	EX-QUEEN Isabella has informed King
Alfonso that she will take no part whatever
in the political affairs of the kingdom.
	AT the laying of the corner stone at the
new building for indigent women at St.
Louis, Mo., a lady officiated, Mrs. Shepherd
Wells. This is the first instance of the kind
of which we are aware.
	ANOTHER woman editor, Mrs. Signiago
of Granada, Miss., has assumed the edito-
rial duties of the "Grenada Sentinel," as
her husband's health would not permit his
retaining the position.
	MISS Willard, in an address delivered re-
cently in Boston, Mass., argues in this wise
upon woman's duty to vote: "She had long
hesitated, but now the ballot had been pros-
tituted to undermine the Sabbath, and rob
children of the influence of Bibles in
schools, she thought it time WOMAN, who
is truest to God and her country by instinct
and education, should have a voice at the
polls, when the Sabbath and the Bible are
now attacked by the infidel foreign popula-
tion of the country."
	MRS. Paulina Wright Davis died of con-
sumption in Providence, Rhode Island, the
last week in August. Mrs. Davis was an
earnest and practical worker in the cause of
the advancement of women. She was one
of the callers of the first Convention held
in Worcester twenty-six years age, and her-
self took an active part. She was for a
time editor of a weekly paper devoted to
the interests of women, styled "The Una."
She gave public lectures to women upon
Anatomy and Physiology: she began her
career of lecturing before the public ac-
knowledged woman's right to the platform,
and helped to open up this avenue of pub-
lic speaking for others, and to make their
way less difficult.
	MATILDA Fletcher of Iowa, is spoken of
in the public papers as doing an enormous
public literary work. It is af[--]rmed that
during the past year she has lectured one
hundred nights, traveled seven thousand
miles, written a book upon "Practical Eth-
ics," for shools and families, prepared three
new lectures, two of which have been
given, "The Heart of Man" and "What can
You Do?" The other one will be presented
to the public the coming season; it is styled
"The Ethics of Marriage." Besides this
work she has devised a plan of establishing
Industrial Expositions in the public schools,
which the lower house of legislature, acting
upon her suggestions, last winter enacted
into a law." She has also spent some of
her leisure hours in practical inventions.
She is certainly a most happy illustration
of the advancement and enlargement of
woman's sphere, and th[---] who need in-
spiration and courage to persevere can find
it in such real examples as this.


THE language of flowers, which is pecu-
liar to the Turkish harems, owes its celeb-
rity wholly to Lady Wortly Montague; she
introduced it into Europe. Persians per-
sonify the rose and make it mistress of the
nightengale, to whom he tells his love and
sorrows at each return of Spring. The
Hindoos dedicate flowers to their divinity,
and represent to his imagination their var-
ious ideas through this flower language.
But in Turkish harems this mysterious lan-
guage is turned to its fullest account.
There the language of flowers is the favor-
ed amusement of the fair secluded ones,
and a perfect knowledge of it can only be
obtained through the "slaves of the harem."
