SALT LAKE CITY, SEPT. 15, 1876.


NOTICE.

	We would say to our agents and subscrib-
ers that our terms to clubs are the same as
heretofore, no change having been made;
but we withdrew the article (Club Rates) as
our paper is small and we thought it suffi-
ciently understood; had any change been
made our agents and the public would have
been apprised of it.


[Column 1]
			HOME AFFAIRS.

	THE UNIVERSITY of Deseret has been re-
moved from the Council House Building to the
south west corner above Union Square in the
Seventeenth Ward. The building was former-
ly designated Union Academy, but will hereaf-
ter be styled University Building. The location
being a little way from the centre of town, it
will be more quiet and retired which is an ad-
vantage to study.
	DURING THE last month many. new mounds
have been made in the city grave-yard, mostly
little one's. We never watch the hearse mov-
ing solemnly along, bearing a little coffin, or
glance at the evening or morning paper which
contains accounts of the departure of little
friends, but our heart goes out in tender, yearn-
ing sympathy, and we pray God to comfort
the stricken and bereaved.
	MISS E. R. Snow, Mrs. M. I. Horne and
Mrs. E. Howard, have gone on a visit to
Tooele, and vicinity, in the interest of the
Relief Societies, Retrenchment Associations,
and Woman's Department of Home Indus-
tries. They will spend a few days with the
sisters in that locality, and after their return
Miss E. R. Snow, with some of the sisters, in-
tends going north, visiting settlements as far
as Franklin, in the interests of women gener-
ally, and with the view of arousing the siste[-]s
to renewed efforts in all the home institutions
established in Zion for the promotion of good,
and the higher elevation of the human family.
	WE HAVE been requested to publish an arti-
cle in regard to seri-culture; which will be in-
structive to those just entering upon this
branch of home-industry, who are too far re-
moved from Salt Lake City to attend the meet-
ings of Association:
	When the cocoons are ready to use, put them
in scalding water and they will soften, take
from an old broom eight or ten of the straws
that are worn and tie them together, and stir
the cocoons with them and the end will cling
to them so you can get hold of it and start to
wind it on a reel; it can be wound on a com-
mon reel, although there are reels on purpo[-]e
for silk. There is no mistaking what end of
the cocoon to begin at it, because there is
only one end outside and the other inside.
Such cocoons as are perforated and cannot
be r[--]ied, can be made available by boiling

[Column 2]
them well in strong soap-suds, they will soften
and then can be spun without reeling, so that
not one inch need be wasted.
			PLAIN CITY, Aug. 29th, 1876.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	We are certainly a blessed people in this
place. Everything we have sowed or planted
this year has produced abundantly and we
have great reason to be thankful that the Lord
has so rewarded our labors. The kingdom of God
is increasing fast in this little settlement. We
have, during the present month, had ten babes
born, all living and doing well; for those ten
babes there were only eight mothers; all are
bright beautiful children, and born in the new
and everiasting covenant. Success to them all,
and may the kingdom of God flourish and in-
crease as the bees in the hive, is the earnest de-
sire of a country woman.			A. C. H. *
	The Territorial Central Committee held a
meeting in the City Hall Friday evening, the
8th inst., to adopt such measures as should be
deemed practicable for the nomination of a
candidate for Delegate from Utah to the Forty-
Fifth Congress, Nov. 7, next ensuing. It was
decided that in order to awaken sufficient in-
terest in this important matter to elicit an ac-
tive vote, a People's Territorial Convention
should be held, composed of Delegates from all
the counties, at the City Hall in Salt Lake City
on Saturday, Oct. 7th, 1876, at 12 m. The
number of delegates to compose the Territo-
rial Convention has been fixed at seventy-eight,
and the allotment to the different counties
based upon their representation in the Legis-
lature. To secure a proper selection of these
county delegates, it was suggested that each
county hold a county convention composed of
delegates sent from primary meetings previ-
ously held in the several precincts. Each dele-
gate to the Territorial Convention should come
with credentials duly authorized and signed by
the President and Secretary of the convention
which appointed them. Each county conven-
tion should also appoint a County Central
Committee to attend to all election matters in
its county, and this committee, as soon as or-
ganized, should communicate with the Peo-
ple's Territorial Central Committee at Salt
Lake City, that there may be a concert of action
in this matter. It is for the future interest of the
people of this Territory to be prompt and ener-
getic as well as cautious and wise in these
matters, and thereby secure such measures as
shall be for the benefit of the entire commu-
nity.
	DIED AT Bountiful, Davis Co., Utah; on
Tuesday, Aug. 8th, 1876; after a lingering ill-
ness of cancer in the throat, Alvira Thomas,
beloved wife of Chancey Loveland, aged 68
years, 7 months and 3 days; her husband died
Aug. 6th, and during his funeral services she
quietly passed away. Deceased was born in
Glamorganshire, South Wales, where she em-
braced the Gospel on the 12th day of October,
1846, emigrated to Utah in 1856, crossed the
plains in Captain Bunker's Hand-Cart Com-
pany. In 1858 she removed to Bountiful
where she has resided ever since, and was
highly respe[--]ed. She was a lovi[-]g and duti-
ful wife and faithful saint. She w[-]s a lively
member of the Relief Society, and acted as
counselor to the Teachers Quorum, always
present at our meetings as long as her health
permitted. She rejoiced in the society of the
saints, and in the principles of the Gospel, and
was always ready to administer to the sick and
poor in word and deed, her actions spoke loud-
er than words; she was full of faith and integ-
rity in her religion. She was a firm believer
in the order of Celestial Marriage and lived in


[Column 1]
it for many years. Her example was worthy
of imitation, and she will reap the reward of
the righteous. She looked calm and lovely in
her rest, merciful death had smoothed many of
the care lines in her face. Great is the reward
she has won.
						M. J. CROSBY.
	Bountiful.
	DESERET SILK ASSOCIATION.—The Deseret
Silk Association met at the Fireman's Hall,
Wednesday, Sept. 6th, Mrs. Zina D. Young
presiding.
	Mrs. Young said we had met to transact im-
portant business, pertaining to this society
and to find out a way to pay for the coccoons
as they are brought in.
	Mr. P. A. Schettler read a letter he had re-
ceived from the Atwood Machine Company,
Gonn., stating the prices of different ma-
chinery, as follows—
	Winding frame complete, with
		swifts, 50 spindles			   $125
	Cleaning, 60 spindles				     100
	Doubling, 52 spindles				     150
	Spinning, 114, to 120 spindles $250 to 400
	Stretching, 					   20 to 100
	Reel,						     20 to 50
	Soft Silk Winder,				   24 to 100
	Spoolers for table, (2 machines)	     100
	Mr P. A. Schettler said he had sent for a
reel that would cost $25.
	Mr. A. C. Pyper suggested that every one
interested in this organization and in the com-
monwealth of Utah be solicited to donate to
the society and the amount given be left en-
tirely to the individuals donating; also that our
merchants, business men and others be solici-
ted for donations, the object of the donations,
being purposely to raise means to encourage
and remunerate those who had raised the
cocoons, for their labor; and not only that, but
to open another branch of home industry,
whereby the aged, the lame and the young
can find employment and be recompensed;
and it would also be a source of great wealth
to Utah.
	The question was asked, 'What price shall
we pay per pound for cocoons? Mr. A. C. Py-
per said he wished to see the raisers of cocoons
paid for their labor and stimulated to pursue
it, and he would be in favor of continuing to
give two dollars per pound for good cocoons.
	The following members donated as fol-
lows—
	Mr. A. C. Pyper $10, Mr. P. A. Schettler $10,
Mr. R. Simpson $10, Mr. Wm. Smith $5, Mrs.
Zina D. Young $10, Mrs. E. B. Wells $10, Mrs.
M. A. White $10 (cocoons), Mrs. A. K. Dun-
yon $10 (cocoons), Mrs. Ann Morgan $5 (co-
coons).
	Mr. P. A. Schettler suggested that the presi-
dents of all the Relief Societies throughout the
Territory be solicited to act as agents for this
Association, to solicit donations from the
brethren and sisters in their respective dis-
tricts; and as Miss Eliza R. Snow is chairman
of this committee, please forward all donations
to her address, Lion House. It was also sug-
gested that the following gentlemen be ap-
pointed to solicit donations in Salt Lake City
—Mr. A. C. Pyper, Mr. P. A. Schettler, Mr.
A. M. Musser, Mr. R. Simpson; and Mr. Wm.
Smith in Bountiful, Davis Co.
	These suggestions, were adopted by the meet-
ing, which then adjourned till the first
Wednesday in October, at 2 o'clock p. m ,
at this place.
				R. SIMPSON, Secretary.

residence and receive payment.	R. S.


NO CHARACTER.

	Picking up one of the latest magazines we
find allusion made to Pope's cynical reference
in his Moral Essays, of the character of wo-
men. He commences to a lady in this style;

	"Nothing so true as what you once let fall."
	"Most women have no characters at all."

	So women are indebted to one of their own
sex for this withering sarcasm, which has been
perpetuated to this day, and must even now
be brought up to prove that women are weak,
feeble and unstable in character. The writer
goes on to say, "They have no opinions they
can call their own, no preferences, no dislikes,
they are not strong enough to keep even a prej-
udice, they will passively agree to a long suc-
cession of opinions, no two of which could be
held by the same man at the same time."
	No wonder that women who are determined
to live down such accusations as these should
be designated "strong minded." Will it not
take some moral courage, some undaunted he-
roism to face such imputations of feebleness,
especially when coincided in by the majority
of women?
	It is women themselves in a great measure,
who are to blame for the estimate given of
their own character; but there will not always
be cause for such assertions, for such channels
of education and cultivation are opening from
time to time as will give to women all the supe-
rior advantages which they can possibly make
use of, and with use the faculties and capac-
ities will enlarge on general principles, and
man will find that woman is capable of strength
of character as well as man, and subsequently
when some standard writer, a second Pope,
portrays woman to the world, she will be rep-
resented in a different light. Instead of such
arguments as these hindering woman's pro-
gress it only gives an impetus to all their
movements to stimulate such as are earnest
in their endeavors to redouble their exertions
and maintain such firm purpose as shall in-
sure to them ultimate success.
	Women have seen the dire necessity for the
movement they are making, namely, Woman's


Suffrage," before they entered upon the "arena
of action." They knew from the openly-
avowed sentiments of men that they had noth-
ing to expect of them, taken as a class, but
ridicule and loss of "caste." Every woman
has an innate desire to be loved by the oppo-
site sex, God has implanted it in her nature,
consequently it is so much the harder for her
to have his opprobrium almost his disrespect,
for the sake of maintaining justice and equity
for those who are dearer to her than her own
life—her posterity.
	A very popular writer has just published
his ideas of the course which should be pur-
sued thus: "A woman with a mission is an
affliction that ought by every means within the
law to be proscribed as dangerous to public and
private peace."
	Then women are first ridiculed because they
have no opinions, and then without judge or
jury condemned as heretics because they have
opinions of their own. This subject will bear
descanting upon; we leave the field open to
those who have talents given them to use, and
our opinion is that SUCH MEN have not yet
got rid of Women with a Mission.
				[handwritten] E. B. Wells


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.		61


For the EXPONENT.
			VALEDICTORY

	OF THE LADIES' CEN. TER. BOARD.

	The Directors of the Ladies' Centennial
Territorial Fair which was opened on the
Fourth of July and closed on the 2nd of Sep-
tember, before relinquishing their responsibili-
ties, avail themselves of this opportunity to
express their sincere thanks to all those who
either furnished specimens for the Fair, or in
other ways assisted in the enterprise.
	The interest manifested by those who have
visited the Fair has been very gratifying, and
constitutes the most desirable PRESENT success.
We have taken pride in exhibiting from day
to day the beautiful articles of taste and util-
ity, (many of them produced entirely from the
elements of Utah,) manufactured by the skill
and industry of the people of our Territory,
and humbly trust that our united efforts in
our Centennial Exhibition will prove instru-
mental in stimulating to greater energies and
be productive of much good in encouraging
home-industries.
	Through the gratuity of Pres. Young and
the generosity of the City Fathers, we are en-
abled to meet our heavy expenses incurred
through the condition in which we found the
Fair Building, occasioned by the late terrible
explosion, but after satisfying all demands,
shall have a small surplus, which will be de-
voted to some charitable purpose.
	On our own part we have spared no pains,
and have cheerfully devoted our time, efforts,
and labors gratuitously, feeling satisfied that
they were in the right direction. The only
financial success we have had in view, from
the commencement, beyond meeting our ex-
penses, is in prospective, when through the ex-
tension and general establishment of Home-
Industries, we shall as a people, become self-
sustaining.
	In conclusion we wish to be understood that
all articles of exhibition which have not been
taken away, will be safely deposited in the
Lion House, where they will remain until
called for.
				ELIZA R. SNOW, Pres
				LOUISA A. ASHBY, Sec.
	S. L. City, Sep. 9th, 1876.


For the Exponent.
	SOME THINGS THAT I DESPISE.

	I despise to see a girl get up in the morn-
ing rather late, lounge around all day with
a novel in her hand, while her mother does
the work. Such a girl will sooner or later
come to some bad end.
	I despise to see a girl, who, when there
is any work to do, has the headache, back-
ache, or some other kind of an ache; but if
there is a ball or opera to attend, her ills
will all vanish like dew before the morning
sun. I pity the mother of such a girl.
	I despise a girl who will not learn to knit
her own stockings, and make her own cloth-
ing, who considers it no accomplishment to
know how to make good bread and butter,
I pity the man she marries, especially if he
happens to be poor.
	I despise a boy whose parents have work-
ed hard to give him an education and ad-
vantages for usefulness, when placed in cir-
cumstances to earn a name and an honorable
position, will waste his time and talents,
and outrage the laws of God and common
decency, by drinking, swearing, smoking,


and gambling. And I despise the girl
who will keep the company of such a boy
	I detest a woman who is always talking
of improving her mind, but who reads
nothing more instructive than dime novels.
Such a woman will, no doubt cause a great
reform some day.
	I despise a girl or boy who are not always
kind and gentle to their parents, kind to
the poor, the old and infirm, and who do
not do all the good they can in this world.
Such are of no use, and we would do better
without them.					JUNE.


A CENTENNARIAN.

	Mrs. Hannah Hawley, of Boston High-
lands, celebrated the anniversary of her
one hundred and third birthday, Aug. 26th,
1876. She was born on the 26th of August,
1773, at Stratham, New Hampshire. Her
father, Jonathan Rollins, held a lieuten-
ant's commission in the colonial army and
stood by Gen. Warren when he fell. Mrs.
Hawley married at the beginning of the
war of 1812; her husband was stationed at
Ft. Independence, and they had been mar-
ried scarcely nine months, when in over-
exerting himself moving a piece of ord-
nance he burst a blood vessel and survived
but a few hours after. Mrs. Hawley has
remained a widow ever since, true to her
first love. Her personal appearance is said
to be most remarkable; her health and
strength are excellent, and she walks with
ease and a buoyancy of step which surpasses
many very much younger. Her hair
is abundant but very white, undisturbed
by a single dark thread. She possesses a
cheerful mind, and a placid, serene coun-
tenance, her conversation is pleasant and at
times jocular; her hearing is good and she
has never worn glasses; she reads and does
light work about the house, and up to a
year ago was in the habit of sewing; her
appetite is excellent, and she sleeps remark-
ably well, she seldom retires before ten and
always rises at half-past five. She has a
happy disposition and delights in relating
anecdotes of the war of 1812. She is greatly
attached to a pet rabbit which she keeps in
the basement and always attends to it her-
self, going twice a day down two pairs of
stairs for that purpose. She says she dis-
tinctly remembers picking huckleberries on
Boston Common, where she recollects see-
ing Mrs. Gen. Hancock and other noted
ladies who pastured their cows there.
	In speaking of cooking she remarked
"why shouldn't I cook? I'm old enough."
She remembers very little about the Revo-
lution, being quite young when her father
was in the habit of telling stories about the
war. She now goes out but little, finding
her happiness all within the home of her
declining years. She has been connected
with the Baptist denomination ever since
she was seventeen; and has never attended
any public place of amusement or rode in a
steam car. On her recent birthday she re-
ceived numerous callers, with whom she
conversed freely and cheerfully, evincing
a remarkable degree of vitality and de-
claring she was not in the least wearied by
the attention she received.


﻿To the Centennial Exhibition went Darby
and Joan, whom a correspondent met in
the Egyptian department included in the
Khedive's contributions. "What's that?"
said she.  "That," said he, reading the la-
bel attached, "is from the floor of the tem-
ple of Minervy, at Babylon, two thousand
years old." "Du tell," said she; "well, it
looks durable don't it?"

HOUSEHOLD HINTS.

	Tomato Salad:—Take ripe tomatoes and cut
them in thin slices; sprinkle over them a small
quantity of finely chopped green onions, add salt,
pepper, vinegar, and oil if liked. Serve with
roast meat.
	Chow Chow:—Chop together very finely a head
of cabbage, six green peppers, six green tomatoes;
add two teaspoonfuls of mustard, one tablespoon-
ful of salt, a little cloves and allspice and vinegar
to wet it; this is ready for use and will keep for
a long time.
	For Jelly:—Take a preserving kettle, porcelain
is the best, have your fruit carefully picked over,
then put it over the fire and when thoroughly heat-
ed, take it off and let it cool, then strain it through
a jelly bag and measure the juice, return it to the
fire and let it boil twenty minutes briskly; add one
pound of loaf sugar to each pint of juice, stir it
until well dissolved; pour it into the glasses or
whatever vessels you use, and seal closely when
cold. The best method is to use the fruit just as soon
as it is ripe, not let it become what is called "dead
ripe."
	Dried Corn:—This is a very good way of keep-
ing corn and is much the surest method. Pick the
corn the night before and husk it in the morning,
then cut it from the cob and put it into pa[-]s and
set on the stove or in the oven, with the oven doors
open; stir it often until thoroughly scalded; spread
it on cloths in the sun, and take care of it at night
and it will soon dry. Put the corn in paper bags
and have it near the fire for some time after its
having been out in the sun, a sure way to pre-
vent its gathering dampness, and hurrying the dry-
ing prevents its souring. Good housekeepers as-
sert a batch of corn can be finished in twenty-four
hours. Soak it over night before cooking, then
simmer on the stove until thoroughly done; season
with cream or butter.
	Canning Corn:—To make corn keep well re-
quires more care than almost anything else, ow-
ing to its containing a large amount of gas in its
composition. The only recipe we can give is this;
gather the corn the night before, husk it in the
morning, cut it from the cob, put it in a jar or ves-
sel to scald, with a little salt; let it cook slowly,
so as to let all the gas that will, pass off; have
your bottles ready, and see that they are all per-
fectly air tight, and put the corn in while it is at
boiling heat, and close the jars up quickly, keep
them in a cool, dark place and never allow the jars
to touch the wall or each other, if you have a clos-
et that has no outside walls it is preferable, as
moisture exudes even from the driest walls in win-
ter and spring. It is necessary occasionally to
look at them and if there is any sign of fermenta-
tion boil the contents up again.


HYGIENIC NOTES.

	For Prickly Heat:—One oz. cream of tartar and
one oz. flour of sulphur, mixed in half a tumbler
of water; take a teaspoonful three times a day, use
vegetable diet and cold or tepid bathing.
	For Poison:—A safe and simple remedy, if one
has swallowed poison, is a heaping teaspoonful of
common salt and the same of ground mustard
stirred well together in a teacupful of water and
swallowed quickly. It will immediately cause
vomiting, and lest any remnants of the poison re-
main in the stomach after the vomiting, beat up
the white of an egg with sweet oil, or butter or
lard, several spoonfuls, and take it; these com-
com articles will counteract many virulent poisons.
	The Wet Sheet Pack:—The advantages of this
r[-]medy in sickness and ill-health and especially in
colds, is incalculable, and as there are perhaps
some of our readers who are unacquainted with its
use, we will give them the best method in a few
words: First put the blankets on the bed, then
take all clothing off the patient and wrap him or


her in a wet sheet, covering the body closely with
blankets; if the feet are very cold a warm brick
or bottle of hot water can be used; bathe the head
of the patient while in the pack with cold water
if there is fever or heat in it, and give them cold
water to drink or some warm herb tea if preferred;
the patient should remain in the pack from half an
hour to two hours, or until there is a sense of heat
or oppression. After the pack is over the patient
should be quickly washed off by sitting in a tub
while water, either cold or tepid, is poured over
the body, or if the patient is not strong, rub off
vigorously with a dry towel or with liquor. It is
better and safer than any kind of drugs or medi-
cine, and will very often prevent the need of send-
ing for a doctor.


A REFORM CAMPAIGN NEEDED.

	The greatest danger which menaces the
Republican institutions is the growing
apathy of the intelligent masses of our citi-
zens in regard to their political duties. In
Massachusetts the statistics of elections
show a steady decrease in the proportion of
votes cast to the whole number of voters.
This decrease has been going on for twenty
years, and in the absence of some new meth-
od of political activity, it bids fair to con-
tinue at an increasing ratio. More and
more—the leading classes of society stay
away from the primary meetings and the
polls. Just in proportion as they neglect
their political duties, they exaggerate the
corruption of others, and lose faith in the
principle of representative government.
Less than sixty per cent of our qualified
voters voted in the last Presidential elec-
tion. Less than fifty per cent. of our voters
voted at our last State election.
	The only radical remedy for this evil lies
in the extension of Suffrage to women,
thereby enlisting the social element in poli-
tics. But meanwhile, it is important to
overcome the existing apathy, in order to
secure a fairer expression of public opinion
in reference to this and all other public
questions. How can it be done?
	Most readily, as it seems to me, by reviv-
ing the good, old, western fashion of hold-
ing mass meetings of the whole people, and
addressed by speakers of both parties—in
other words—by public political debates.
	Let the National and State central com-
mittees, in behalf of our strongest Repub-
lican speakers, challenge the leading Demo-
cratic speakers in every State to discuss the
national issues before the whole people.
Let all citizens, men and women, irrespec-
tive of party, be invited to attend these
great mass meetings. The novelty of the
method and the interest of an intellectual
conflict will bring out our entire population,
and insure a full and fair expression of pub-
lic opinion next November.
	There is another and higher reason for
reforming existing political methods by the
introduction of these united political meet-
ings of both parties. It will compel a high-
er order of political discussion. Speakers
will be more guarded in their statements
when they are subject to criticism and re-
futation upon the spot. Their appeals be-
ing made to members of both parties, a tone
of comparative candor and moderation will
prevail. To hundreds of thousands of our
citizens such meetings would be a political
education. Especially in the South this is
the only way in which the bitterness of
party feeling can be mollified, and a fair
election insured. For such discussions
will bring together men of all parties to hear
both sides, and this will necessarily modify
prejudice, and awaken independent thought
and judgment. They would be worth more


to Southern Republicans than a standing
army.
	With the abolition of slavery and the
theoretical acceptance of manhood Suf-
frage, the ancient  barriers are broken
down. To-day both parties substantially
accept a common platform. In this Centen-
nial year why should not both parties con-
duct a reform canvass, upon reform princi-
ples, by inaugurating political meetings of
American citizens, to consider questions of
national interest, not as partisans but as pa-
triots?						H. B. B.
—Woman's Journal.


A CURE FOR HYDROPHOBIA.

	The Salut Public of Lyons says: Dr. Bus-
sion claims to have discovered a remedy for
this terrible disease. In attending a female
patient in the last stage of canine madness,
the doctor imprudently wiped his hand
with a handkerchief impregnated with her
saliva. There happened to be a slight
abrasion on the index finger of the left
hand; but, confident of his own curative
system, the doctor merely washed the
parts with water. He was fully aware of
the imprudence he had committed, and
says: "Believing that the malady would
not declare itself until the fortieth day, and
having various patients to visit, I put off
from day to day the application of my rem-
edy,—that is vapor-baths. The ninth day
being in my cabinet, I felt all at once a pain
in my eyes. My body felt so light that I
felt as if I could jump to a prodigious
height, or if thrown out of a window,
could suspend myself in the air. My hair
was so sensitive that I appeared to be able
to count each separately without looking at
it. Saliva kept forming in my mouth. Any
movement of air caused great pain to me,
and I was obliged to avoid the sight of
brilliant objects. I had a continual desire
to run and bite—not human beings, but ani-
mals and all that was near me. I drank
with difficulty, and I remarked that the
sight of water distressed me more than the
pain in my throat. I believe that by shut-
ting the eyes, any one suffering from hy-
drophobia can always drink. The fits came
on every five minutes, and I felt the pain
start from the index finger and run up the
nerves to the shoulder. In this state, think-
ing my case was preservative, not curative,
I took a vapor bath, not with the intention
of cure but of suffocating myself. When
the bath was 52 centigrade (93. 4. 5. fahren-
heit,) all the symptoms disappeared as if
by magic, and since then I never have felt
any more of them.
	I have attended more than eighty per-
sons bitten with mad animals and have not
lost a single one. When a person is bitten
by a mad dog, he must for seven successive
days take a vapor bath, a la Russe, of 57 to
62 degrees. This is the preventive remedy.
A vapor bath may be quickly made by put-
ting two or three red-hot bricks in a buck-
et of water for fifteen or twenty minutes.
When the disease is declared, it only re-
quires one vapor bath, rapidly increasing
to 37 centigrade, then slowly to 53, and the
patient must strictly confine himself to his
chamber until the cure is complete."
	For God's sake give this remedy a trial
should a case present itself.
				G. F. Colburn,
					Washington, D. C.

[Column 1]
			LORD BYRON.

	An American writer who met Lord By-
ron at Florence, has left some interesting
remarks concerning his lordship. He says,
"Lord Byron lived then in a street in the
rear of the church Santa Maria Annunziata.
A large garden at present intervenes be-
tween the house he occupied and the Palaz-
zo Zimenes. It is a pleasant and very re-
tired spot, with extensive and delightful
views towards the north. He received
me with great affability, and began chat-
tering on all sorts of subjects, asking twenty
questions in a breath. I was a good deal
surprised at the first sight of him—first, on
discovering that he was the person I had
seen on a former occasion, and whom, in
my fancy, I set down as a decided nobody—
certainly not for a poet; secondly on mark-
ing the total difference between the real
Byron and all the portraits of him I had
ever beheld. The likeness seemed to be
drawn from the 'Corsair,' 'Lara,' or 'Har-
old,' a frowning, supercilious, disdainful
thing; but here was an ordinary-looking
man, who, if he was not short and thick,
was at least shortish and thickish; and
whose countenance had good humor to rec-
ommend it, but which, in spite of a certain
regularity of features, I could not think re-
markably handsome. Of his dress I re-
member but little; only his shirt-collar was
not turned down, as in the portraits, and
his pantaloons were strapped close over the
feet—the lame one was drawn up a little
out of sight, which, I understand, was his
usual practice. He conversed with great
readiness, but not at all in a sermonizing,
bookish way. He skipped from one sub-
ject to another, broke off into digressions,
left things half said, was often incoherent,
sometimes ungrammatical, and now and
then, in spite of his readiness at an idea,
was at a loss for a word to express a very
simple thing. What he uttered was, in a
thousand instances, better said than if it
had been coolly elaborated by study, yet it
was no more than the prompting of the in-
stant. We talked in a rambling style for
some time, in the course of which he grew
more cheerful than he appeared to be at
first. His countenance struck me as sus-
ceptible of a great variety of impressions,
and his smile was in the highest degree
engaging; although the habitual expression
of his countenance when not under the ex-
citement of talk, was rather sombre than
lively."
	BYRON'S DEFENCE OF HIS PLAGIAR-
ISM.—He allowed, frankly, that he was in-
debted to the hints of others for some of
the most esteemed passages in his poetry.
"I never," said he, "considered myself in-
terdicted from helping myself to another
man's stray ideas. I have Pope to counter-
ance me in this. Pope was a great hunter
up of grains of wheat in bushels of chaff:
perhaps I have not been so laborious a
searcher, but I have been no more scrupul-
ous than he in making use of whatever fell
in my way. Mankind have been writing
books so long, that an author may be ex-
cused for offering no thoughts absolutely
new; we must select and call that inven-
tion. A writer at the present day has
hardly any other resource than to take the
thoughts of others, and cast them into new
forms of association and contrast. Plagiar-
ism, to be sure, is branded of old; but it is
never held criminal except when done in a
clumsy way, like stealing among the Spar-
tans. A good thought is often far better
expressed at second hand than at the first
utterance. If a rich material has fallen in-

[Column 2]
to incompetent hands, it would be the
height of injustice to debar a more skillful
artisan from taking possession of it, and
working it up. Commend me to a good
pilferer. You may laugh at it as a paradox,
but I assure you the most original writers
are the greatest thieves"—Byroniana.


LOST WOMEN.

	We commend the following thoughts to
the public;—"Has it ever occurred to you
what a commentary upon our civilization
are those lost women, and the attitude of
society towards them? A little child strays
away from her home enclosure, and the
whole community is on the alert to find the
wanderer and restore it to its mother's
arms. What rejoicing when it is found,
what tearful sympathy, what heartiness of
congratulations! There are no harsh com-
ments upon tired feet, be they ever so
so weary. No reprimand for the soiled and
torn garments, no lack of kisses for the
tear-stained face. But let the child be
grown into womanhood, let her be led from
it by the scourge of want; what happens?
Do Christain men and women go in quest
of her? Do they provide all possible help
for her return, or if she returns of her own
free will, do they receive her with such
kindness and delicacy as to secure her
against wandering? Far from it. At the
first step she-was denounced as lost—lost!
Echo, friends and relatives—we disown you;
don't ever come near us to disgrace us.
Lost says society, indifferently. How bad
the girls are! And lost—irretrievably lost
is the prompt verdict of conventional mor-
ality, while one and all unite in bolting
every door between her and morality. Ah!
will not those lost ones be required at our
hands?"—Ex.

SPLINTERS.

GENERAL.
	Georgia has forty cotton mills running
full time and paying handsome dividends
........Jersey Day at the Centennial was cele-
brated by twenty thousand citizens of New
Jersey........Fish of all kinds are scarce in
the East, and bring a very high price........
Large forest fires are reported as having
raged fiercely in several places in the States
.........The Boston Museum opened its 34th
regular dramatic season Monday evening,
Aug. 28th, with Victorian Sardou's play
entitled "Ferreol".......Boston Theatre, on
the same evening as the Museum, opened
with "The Streets of New York," Frank
Mayo personating Tom Badger........The
Globe has just opened with the drama
"Pique." This play run last year eight
consecutive months in New York at Daly's
Fifth Avenue Theatre.......The small pox has
been raging in Chili, attended with great
loss of life.......The standard of the prophet
was raised recently in Turkey; the emblem
is black, and is only displayed in times of
great peril........The World's Homeopathic
Convention, recently held in Philadelphia,
will assemble again for the same purpose in
1881........Normal schools have become ex-
ceedingly popular in Canada........Prepara-
tions are being made for building a hand-
some granite vault over the remains of
Wm. B. Astor........Musical promenades were
very popular in London the past summer
......They talk now of keeping the Centen-
nial Exposition open until December......
Gilmore's Band will begin to play in New
England in December......The Musical Fes-
tival at Philadelphia will begin Sept. 20.

[COLUMN 2]

PERSONAL.
	James Hamilton is employed painting a
large picture of San Francisco Bay at sun-
set......Queen Victoria has entered upon the
fortieth year of her reign......Mr. Henry
Vincent, the English orator, will make a
farewell lecture tour in this country this
winter, arriving in America in October......
John Bright informs the "Redpath Bureau"
that his visit to America is yet undeter-
mined; he will not leave home before 1878
...........Levy is preparing "Casta Diva" for
Gilmore's Band.......Minnie Palmer, the
New York beauty, is going to star in "Kiss-
es" the coming season........Mrs. James A.
Oates has compromised the difficulties with
her business manager by paying him $1000
......Senator Bayard received the honorary
degree of L. L. D., at the recent commence-
ment of Delaware State College......Mary
A. Livermore and Wendell Phillips were
two of the speakers at Prohibitory Rally,
in Faneuil Hall, Boston, Mass., Sept. 7th
.......Mr. Tilden and Mr. Hendricks, in their
letters of acceptance, said not a word about
the woman question.

Foreign.
	The King of Dahomey has threatened to
kill all the English in his kingdom........The
Prince Imperial of Austria will make a
visit to this country of two years' duration
.......Gen. Sickles, Ex-Minister to Spain, has
sold his establishment in Paris with a view
of returning to America......The Emperor
William of Germany will have seen seventy
years of military life next New Year's Day
......In France there are six women who are
Knights of the Grand Cross of the Legion
of Honor........Mr Duff of Edingburgh
thinks he could keep up all the "missions"
with the surplus silk worn by the ladies,
with which they sweep the streets......Earl
Russell called for an autumn session of Par-
liament.......The Bishop of Lincoln thinks
the Lord should be specially entreated in
behalf of Servia against the Turks......
Prince Milan wants peace..........Three Spanish
gunboats have been sent out to Chinese
waters at the request of the Spanish Rep-
resentative at Pekin.


PEN AND SCISSORS.

	"'TIS false," as the girl said when her
lover told her that she had beautiful hair.
	To write a good love letter you ought to
begin without knowing what you mean to
say, and finish without knowing what you
have written.—Rousseau.
	A COMPOSITOR, setting up a report of a
horse race, said the "fool-sellers were
busy," instead of the "pool-sellers." But
it did not alter the sense of the paragraph
much.
	IN the window of a shop, in the obscure
part of London is this announcement.
"Goods removed, messages taken, carpets
beaten and poetry composed on any sub
ject."
	A YOUNG lady in Georgia is six feet two
and a half inches high, and when her
young man sings, "Thou art so near and
yet so far," he can throw more feeling into
the song than any other man in the State.
	MARK Twain, speaking of a new mos-
quito netting, writes: 'The day is coming
when we shall sit under our nets in church
and slumber peacefully, while the discom-
fitted flies club together and take it out of
the minister.'

FLOWER'S.

	THE firmest friendships have been form-
ed in mutual adversity; as iron is the most
strongly united by the fiercest flames.
	KIND words judiciously used make us
feel all the better for saying them, and cre-
ate a glow of warm respect in our hearers
that will live forever in the heart, a moun-
tain of radiant cheer and good-will to us
and the One above.
	BY the prayers of those nearest and dear-
est to him, is a man often softly drawn away
from an immortal purpose. Where power
and strength might avail nothing, tears
and a little love avail much to shake the
soul's sense of some pitiless duty. An in-
fant's little hand may thus draw the just
man from justice, the righteous man from
righteousness; for justice and righteousness
are alike awful, while to stoop and kiss is
sweet. When a man's house is armed in
affection against him, when, instead of
help and a sword, he finds on his own
hearth only feebleness and a love that can
not understand, strong indeed must be his
purpose, supreme indeed must be his faith,
if he walks still onward and upward to the
terrible heights of God.—Robert Buchanan.


THE FIRESIDE.

	It is as the focus of home fellowship and
intercourse that we speak of the fireside—
as the spot consecrated to the freeest action
and utterance of family sympathies and af-
fections—where conjugal, parental, frater-
nal and filial anxieties, hopes, fears, joys,
sorrows, loves, resentments, confessions,
forgiveness are wont to be exchanged.
There is no other place in which can be re-
alized more thoroughly the weaving into
one of several lives, each imparting and re-
ceiving something from the rest. No oth-
er is so sacred to the memory of those who
have been summoned thence into the wide
world, who are, perhaps, afar off on the sea,
or doing their allotted work amongst stran-
gers, or removed to those more inaccessible
shores where "the wicked cease from troub-
ling and the weary are at rest." Elsewhere
the absent may be forgotten, but seldom,
for long together, at the fireside. It is
crowned with associations which touch the
heart at some point or other of its surface,
and make it thrill with affectionate emo-
tion, in which every member of the family
gathered aronnd the hearth can take an ap-
propriate share. No lesson leaves a more
abiding impression than those which gently
drop into the mind at the fireside. No fun
is more tickling, or leaves behind it less re-
gret. No history is purer, as a whole, than
fireside history, and none lingers longer or
more lovingly in remembrance. He who
cannot look forward with yearning desire to
fireside enjoyment, as the staple enjoyment
of life, is greatly to be pitied, and, if the
cause be himself, greatly to be blamed.


A GENTLEMAN.—When you have found
a man, you cannot have far to go to find a
gentleman. You cannot make a gold ring
out of brass. You cannot change a Cape
May, crystal to a diamond. You cannot
make a gentleman till you first find a man.
To be a gentleman it is not sufficient to
have had a grandfather once. To be a gen-
tleman does not depend upon the tailor or
the toilet. Blood will degenerate. Good
clothes are not good habits.
	A gentleman is just a gentleman; he is
no more, no less; a diamond polished that
was once a diamond in the rough. A gen-
tleman is gentle. A gentleman is modest.


A gentleman is courteous. A gentleman is
slow to take offence, as being one who never
gives it. A gentleman is slow to surmise
evil, as being one who never thinks it. A
gentleman subjects his appetites. A gen-
tleman subdues his feelings. A gentleman
refines his taste. A gentleman controls his
speech. A gentleman deems every other
better than himself.
	Sir Philip Sidney was never so much of
a gentleman—mirror though he was of
England's knighthood—as when, upon the
field of Zutphen, as he lay in his blood, he
waived the draught of cool spring water,
that was to quench his dying thirst, in fa-
vor of a dying soldier.—Waverly Maga-
zine.


"MAN," says Victor Hugo, "was the
conundrum of the eighteenth century, wo-
man is the conundrum of the nineteenth
century." An editor adds, "We can't guess
her, but we will never give her up. No, 
never."
	IT was told of a famous tragedian that,
at the close of an act in which he had been
the prominent character, a goose's head
was thrown upon the stage by some one
who had a spite against him. The trage-
dian picked it up, handed it to one of the
others to take away, and said, with perfect
nonchalance. "The gentleman who has
thrown his head upon the stage can get it
back at the close of the performance."
	PENCILS have lately appeared, the writ-
ing of which is capable of being copied
more or less perfectly in the press. They
are said to be made of graphite, kaolin and
aniline violet. The graphite is used in
the form of a thick paste, the koalin in a
finely pulverized state, and the violet in the
form of a very concentrated aqueous solu-
tion. The whole well mixed and molded
under the press with cylinders about four
inches long and of the required diameter.

WOMAN'S EXPONENT.
VOL. 5.		SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, OCTOBER 1, 1876.	NO. 9.


GREETING.

Spread, spread the tables wide—a feast prepare,
Of bounteous blessings, wholesome, sweet and rare.
Bring fruits and flowers, bring every beauteous thing,
And bread the staff of life, in plenty bring:
Bring ye of costlier tokens to impart,
The purest, richest treasures of the heart.
Bring joyous greetings, smiles and cheerful faces:
Bring tender words and kisses and embraces:
Bring friendship and its holy offspring love;
Bring all fair graces that are born above,
Bring truth to speak from every lip and eye—
Bring sounding praises to the Great Most High!
Sing to His name a grateful, happy lay,
Who hath to us returned this welcome day;
Our Parents still both living in the flesh,
Their spirits quickened, hearts and memories fresh.
Now forty years they've journeyed on together.
'Mid storms and sunshine and uncertain weather;
But what to them the sunshine or the shade,
They still press on, undaunted, undismayed.
They do not live for Mammon nor its cause,
The world's opinion, flatt'ry or applause,
Their aim is higher, their reward more sure,
Hence they afford life's crosses to endure.
And while they thus, the way of life are threading,
Custom would call this day their "Ruby Wedding!"
Rubies and gems we have not to bestow,
Nor aught of glittering brilliancy or show,
But bring we each a holier offering.
Father and mother see ! our souls we bring,
Laden with gratitude to God and you.
Who hath ordained and taught them to be true!
True to the work which will at length prepare
The faithful in Celestial wealth to share.
We'll all be there, your daughters and your sons,
Lovely and brave, with Mates and little ones.
Friends will your mansions crowd, this day at least,
They'l come in myriads to your glorious feast;
And who shall say but there, with all the rest,
Our Father God may be your honored guest?
Granting you blessings, while we sing His Praise,
And gladly celebrate this day of days.
	Ulala.
Aug. 29th, 1875.


[Column 1]
					For the EXPONENT.
	LOVE OF THE BEAUTIFUL IN
				NATURE.

	Ever since I was a wee little girl with
rosy cheeks and clusters of bright, brown,
wavy hair (that never would lie smooth,) I
have had a passionate love for the great
out-spread book of nature. I have wander-
ed dreamily for hours "in childhood's days"
through the grand old forests in the vicin-
ity of my New England home, never lonely,
sitting down here and there on the beds of
moss fashioned like cushioned seats upon
some fantastically shaped rock, or the gnarl-
ed trunk of fallen trees, listening to the
songs of birds, and the incessant monotony
of the woodpecker, gazing now and then
up through the leafy branches, catching a
glimpse of the azure above, watching the
rays of light as they shimmered and flashed
through the leaves, adding new brilliancy
to the variegated coloring, as they trem-
bled and swayed with the soft breezes, and
the boughs waving gracefully towards each
other as though embracing tenderly, and
whispering inarticulately in an under-tone,
seemingly a language of reciprocal affection.
	There were always volumes of unwritten
poetry for me in the woodlands and mead-
ows, and in the sparkling brooks with their
shining pebbly depths; and in haunts of
forest shades and falling waters, enthusias-
tically I have drank in, in rich, exhilarat-
ing, inspiring draughts nature's beauty and

[Column 2]
glory, and intuitively I seemed to under-
stand that God was in and over all.
	O how the pealing voices of memory
ring down into my heart, the recollection
of the weird land of the past, whenever I
wander forth among wild, romantic scenery,
or fresh, green pastures, babbling brooks,
deep, winding rivers, or any of the diversi-
ties of nature's grandeur and magnificence,
which insensibly strike the silent chord of
response in my bosom. There is something
inexplicably attractive in the association
with mountains, glens, canyons and forests,
and it calls forth the purest and happiest
emotions, elevating and stimulating the fac-
ulties of thought and reflection and irresist-
ibly leads the soul to the Fountain of all
beauty, of all intelligence. If would one
bring from the deepest recesses of the soul
the richest gems of thought, the most bril-
liant ideas, and clothe them in the choicest
language, let that person go out among the
wildest haunts of nature undisturbed; these
beauties have an inspiration and speak to
the soul in their own simple, unmistakable
language, breathing audibly the music of a
Creator's voice. The grandest parlors with
all the appliances of modern luxury may
lull the senses to rest and wrap the soul in
soft, dreamy raptures, but can never call
forth the intense emotion, the soul-thrilling
ecstasies of thought, that will shape them-
selves into language to touch the hearts of
thousands, opening up great avenues of
sympathetic feeling, like the association
with the wild haunts where the splashing
waterfall foams and dashes, and the snow-
crested mountains tower above, like the
giant kings of some palatial domain.
	It is good for us all at times to leave the
dust and din of city life behind us, and for-
getting the daily cares of every-day life,
which so tax the physical strength, and
harass the mind, go for a little season into
some quiet country nook, nestled among
the hills, where we can think, where we
can rest, where we will be free to act our-
selves, unnoticed by Madame Grundy. We
recruit, we add vigor to our life and
zest to our enjoyments by this means, and
if only for a day, we breathe another at-
mosphere, and it helps us to gather fresh-
ness and activity, quickens our pulses and
strengthens our nerves.
	My intense admiration for the beautiful
helps me perhaps to appreciate a ride, or a
walk where there is always something new
and attractive, and I beg pardon if I over-
estimate the advantages to be derived from
such resources. To me, they are ever an
inexhaustible fund of enjoyment, and I
never look upon them without contrasting
them with similar scenes of the "long ago."
Then I was wont to build castles in the air,
to indulge in all sorts of dreamy fancies,
and embody all sorts of childish ideas in
silly rhyme and rustic prose, and I am
afraid that in this one speciality I am a
child yet, although grown up people will
persist in calling me,
						AUNT EM.
	S. L. City, Sep. 19, 1876.


MISS Anna Dickinson has accumulated
quite a fortune by lecturing, and yet she
has been extremely generous in dispensing
to others.


NOTES AND NEWS.

	GENERAL NEWTON'S little girl, Mary,
not quite three years old, fired the mine
that blew Hallett's Point Reef to pieces. A
most remarkable incident certainly in the
life of a little child.
	THE AMERICAN Woman Suffrage Asso-
ciation will hold their Eighth, Annual
Meeting in Philadelphia, on Monday and
Tuesday, Oct. 2, and 3, morning, afternoon
and evening sessions. The public are in-
vited, a[-]d auxiliary State and local socie-
ties are considered entitled to send dele-
gates according to the respective delega-
tions of their States. President, Mary A.
Livermore, Secretary, Alice C. Fletcher.
	THE FOURTH Woman's Congress will be
held at Philadelphia Oct. 4, 5, and 6, in St.
George's Hall, daily sessions twice, also
evening sessions. In this Congress will be
assembled many of the most superior wo-
men of the present age, working with un-
tiring zeal and earnestness for reformation
in public and private. Maria Mitchell will
preside.
	IT HAS been proposed to flood the desert
of Sahara. Mr. McKenzie, the projector,
with an engineering party, is about to make
the necessary surveys for turning the wa-
ters of the Atlantic into the great desert.
He is confident that a canal eight or nine
miles long will accomplish the object, flood-
ing the low lands and bringing Timbuctoo
within navigable distance of the sea, open-
ing up a vast trade with the interior of Af-
rica which is the great inducement.
	THE MINISTER of Public Instruction, Mr.
Waddington, proposes an entire reorganiza-
tion of State Education in France. His
scheme embraces primary, secondary, and
university education, making the first com-
pulsory, and for that purpose he asks an ex-
tra grant of five million francs; and looks to
the departments, and communes for fifteen
million francs more. This is in direct op-
position to the scheme adopted by the
French Assembly last year, and of which
the Roman Catholic Church has so vigor-
ously availed itself. The plan is strongly
opposed by the Roman Catholic universities
and their party.


WOMAN'S RECORD.

	MADAME Louis Blanc, who died recently
in Paris, must have been very lovable, as it
is said tens of thousands followed her re-
mains to the cemetery.
	MISS Anna Oliver, the young preacher,
is filling with characteristic dignity, an en-
gagement at the First Place Methodist
Church in Brooklyn.
	MISS Tora Dutt, a young Hindoo lady,
has just published a book of translations of
French verse into English. Critics praise,
and esteem it as an item "in the history of
the progress of culture."
	"SHELLS FROM THE SANDS OF TIME,"
is the title of a book written by Lady Lyt-
ton, (widow of Lord Lytton the author) in
which she comments upon modern servants
"stuckapativeness."

[Column 1]
			R. S. REPORTS.

	The General Retrenchment meeting held
in the Fourteenth Ward Assembly Rooms,
Saturday Sept. 16, 1876, was presided over
by Mrs. W. East, but during the meeting
President Mrs. M. I. Horne, and Counselors
Miss E. R. Snow and Mrs. E. Howard ar-
rived from Tooele where they had been vis-
iting in the interests of the Societies. Af-
ter the minutes of the last meeting and
from the several wards represented had
been read, Mrs. W. East spoke upon obedi-
ence, fashion and the principles of Celestial
Marriage; referred to Bro. George Q. Can-
non's remarks in the Sunday School Union,
exhorting the teachers upon teaching that
subject.
	Counselor Miss E. R. Snow referred to
her absence from the meetings on account
of her duties at the Ladies Fair; said she
had enjoyed the spirit of God while en-
gaged there, spoke of home-industries and
the necessity of becoming self-sustaining as
a people. She spoke of her absence from
these meetings having been similar to a
long fast, but that the last week she had
been feasting. On Tuesday, of that week,
she met with the Society at Mill Creek,
Wednesday in the 20th Ward, Thursday
morning with Sisters Horne and Howard,
started for Tooele, where they held a gen-
eral meeting in the afternoon; Friday they
had another general meeting of brethren
and sisters. She had enjoyed the time
very much; exhorted the sisters to punctu-
ality and improvement; remarked the great
blessings that would attend the faithful.
Mrs. M. E. Watmough then spoke upon
the subject of elections, and the neglect of
the sisters on this point, also in regard to
teaching the children as they grew up
every pure principle and duty. Mrs. M. I.
Horne expressed her pleasure in being able
to meet with the sisters again, spoke upon
the principles of marriage, and the superi-
or blessings conferred by the higher law of
marriage which has been revealed in this
last day, also upon seri-culture, and elections,
and their visit to Tooele.
	Counselor Mrs. E. Howard impressed up-
on the sisters the necessity of using decor-
um, and teaching it to their children, and
advised them to remember this subject in
the coming Conference, it would add to the
good order and quiet of the meetings, in
large assemblies particularly.

	HYDE PARK, CACHE Co., Sep. 14, 1876.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	Our Relief Society met July 18th, for the
purpose of organizing a Retrenchment As-
sociation. Our Bishop was present and gave
us some excellent advice; after which a few
of the sisters made suitable remarks and
the young ladies were organized with the
following officers:
	Sarah James President,
	Ellen Woolf,		}
	Celia Woolf,		}
	Permelia Drury,	}	Counselors.
	Lizzie Thurston,	}
	Rose Hyde,		}
	Orrilla Woolf,	}
	Mary Burnham, Sec'y.,
	Kate Perks, Assistant Sec'y.,
	Rosanna Hurren, Treas.
	We have about forty members, have
made resolutions similar to many that have
been published. The girls all feel pleased
with the arrangement and are energetic in
the work. We hold our meetings once a
week, and should be so glad to have some of

[Column 2]
our sisters from Salt Lake come and talk to
us. We intend to try and live up to our
resolutions and improve day by day. Your
paper is read in our meetings, and we could
not well do without it. Praying God to
bless every effort made for its circulation,
we are
		Yours Truly,
			MARY S. BURNHAM Sec.
				KATE PERKS Ass't. sec.

		ST. CHARLES, Sept. 15th,, 1876.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	Report of the Annual Celebration of the
Young Ladies Retrenchment Association,
held in St. Charles, Sep. 6th, '76, just one
year from their organization. Proceedings
commenced at 10 a. m. with singing "Oh ye
mountains high," by the Retrenchment
choir, Opening prayer by Bishop Budge,
singing, "Hard Times come again no
more"—
	The Annual Report was read by the Sec-
retary; items as follows:
	Retrenchment Association of St. Charles,
organized Sept. 6th, 1875, with President,
eight Counselors, a Business Manager or
Superintendent, Secretary, and twenty-sev-
en members. Have held twenty-four meet-
ings and had one party, the proceeds of
which was forwarded to Miss E. R. Snow to
help defray the expenses of the Ladies Fair
at Salt Lake City.
	Then followed select reading and recita-
tions, also singing and music. Speeches
were then made by Pres't. Rich, Bishops
Budge and Hunt; they thought the young
ladies had made great improvement during
one year, said they would be pleased to
meet with them a year from that time, and
see if there would be such a marked im-
provement at the close of another year, as
there had been at this. Meeting was then
adjourned until 2 o'clock p. m. Closing
hymn, "We thank Thee O God for a Pro-
phet"—Benediction by Bishop Hunt.
	We met again at 2. p. m., and commenced
dancing, had songs and recitations at inter-
vals. At five the hall was cleared and ta-
bles arranged for supper. A committee of
six young ladies appointed waited on the
tables, dressed in costumes to represent a
hundred years ago; there were two tables
the length of the hall and about 50 persons
seated at each. Thanks were offered by
Pres't. C. C. Rich; the supper was a com-
plete success and all passed off pleasantly.
When the tables were removed, dancing re-
commenced. The committee danced in
their costumes the rest of the evening,
which created a great deal of mirth; about
8 o'clock in the evening Pres't. Rich made
a few remarks, expressing that it had been
altogether an enjoyable affair; then he with
Bishop Budge, bade the company good
night and started for their home in Paris.
The dance was closed at 11 o'clock in the
evening, all retired feeling-that the spirit
of God had been with them.
	We hold our meetings every two weeks,
and have in each select reading from the
EXPONENT.
			LIZZIE M. PUGMIRE, Sec'y.

	DEWEYVILLE, BOX ELDER Co.,
						Sept. 6th, 1876.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	The Annual Meeting of the Relief Socie-
ty of this place was held in the School-
House, Aug. 9th. There were present, Pres-
ident J. C. Dewey, of this place, and Pres-
idents' Harriet and Minnie Snow of Brig-
ham City. They spoke words of encour-

[Column 3]
agement to the sisters and exhorted them
to be united and live their religion. The
report of our last years' proceedings was
read; after which Pres. Mary Dewey made
a few remarks. The present officers are
as follows:
	Mary Dewey, President,
	Heneretta Call,	}	Counselors
	Althea Lewis,	}
	Eliza Howard, Secretary,
	Matilda Lish Treasurer.
	There is a small amount of means in the
treasury, and we hope to do more in the
next year than we have done in the past;
some of the sisters have united and are
saving the eggs their hens lay on Sundays
for the Temple. We meet once a week and
have good meetings, and those who attend
them regularly, feel well and are desirous
of doing all they can to help the work along.
We have been organized one year and we
know how to appreciate the blessings of a
Society. Your excellent paper is read in
our meetings with great interest.
	With best wishes for the advancement of
every good work, we remain your sisters,
&c.
			MARY DEWEY, Pres.
				ELIZA HOWARD, Sec.

		WELLSVILLE, CACHE CO.,
						Sep. 12, 1876.
EDITORS' EXPONENT:
	The Annual Meeting of the Relief So-
ciety of this place, was held July 11th '76.
Our financial matters for the past year are
as follows:
	Disbursements to the needy... $ 85 19
	To Mrs. Romania Pratt and .....
		Centennial Flag..................    15 00
	Towards building Society House. 59 49
	Sundries.....................................     40 92
				Total.................... $ 200 60
	In the Treasury.......................... $     5 45
	Temple clothes, and Sundries..     91 30
	Shares in Co-op Institutions.....   118 25
				Total....................... 215 00
	We had a good meeting, the Spirit of
God prevailed in a marked degree. Act-
ing Bishop Jardine who was present, en-
couraged us by his wise exhortations to
continue faithful in our honorable and
holy mission, in striving to alleviate suf-
fering among the children of our Great
Parent. Several of the Sisters then arose
and bore testimony to the truth of the Gos-
pel as taught by the Latter-day Saints.
We expect to finish our Relief Society
House this fall, it was commenced last
Spring and the outside work is now com-
pleted, thanks to the brethren and sisters
who generously donated for that purpose; it
is a splendid structure, being composed of
three rooms; a large one running east and
west, 30 by 15 ft., and a room at each side
14 by 12, the large room will be dedicated
to meetings and quiltings. One of the
small rooms is intended for a store, the
other for various purposes. This good piece
of work was the suggestion of our worthy
Bishop, Wm. H. Maughan ere he went on
his mission.
	Success to the EXPONENT and every en-
terprise that has for its object the good of
the human family.
	Yours in the Gospel of Peace,
			ELLEN WHITON, Pres.
				LUCY HA[-]LAM, Sec.

WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


For the Exponent.
THE MAGIC OF MUSICAL ECHOES. 

There's a strain of music floats on the breeze,
	And its echoes, the rhythm of fairies tell,
And mingling with notes of the sighing trees
	It enchants the soul with a weird-like spell.
And the sweetest visions of long ago
	Are afloat on the summer breeze again,
And we pause as we think of time's swift flow,
	And we listen to catch the sad refrain.
The thoughts that come with that musical strain
	Bring the past enwrapt in a mystic light—
And the halo of memory once again,
	Beams dim o'er our path on a moonlit night—
Away in the shadowy, hazy past,
	Ere clouds had darkened the azure skies;
Our pulses were quick, and our hearts beat fast,
	And earth in its beauty, seem'd Paradise.
The tender chords of the heart may be stirr'd
	By a sweet strain of music soft, and low,
Repeating the echoes that erst were heard
	From the depths of the years of long ago.
A lingering sweetness of cherish'd hours,
	Sweeps through our lives as we journey along,
As delicate perfume from fragrant flowers,
	Or the melody of some long lost song,
Which wake in our soul where remembrance stays,
	The joys which e'en time can never outlive;
And in hours of pleasure, or darkest days
A nameless charm does their influence give. 
	EMILE. 
S. L. City, Sept. 27, 1876.


[COLUMN 1]

	For the EXPONENT.
PIPSEY PAPERS.

	I want a little talk with our girls, and I do
not know of a better opportunity than
through the columns of the EXPONENT. I
love them all, bless their dear hearts, and
the boys too. I love to see them inno-
cent, intelligent and beautiful. They can
all be so, yes every one of them. No mat-
ter if nature has not endowed them with
faces that are most attractive to the casual
observer, they may cultivate sweet tempers
and pleasant dispositions, until they will ob-
tain the admiration and respect of all who
are associated with them, and to those who
know their true value, their faces will beam
with grace and beauty. Sons and daugh-
ters of Zion, you are destined, if you so
will it, to fill high places in the world's
honor and esteem. A life of usefulness is
before you. The foundation is laid where-
on you can rear the walls of a goodly edi-
fice, and your names may shine forth in the
annals of history, brilliant as the sun at
noonday. You may be classed with the no-
blest genius of the age, and your names
handed down to posterity emblazoned in
letters, brilliant as of gold; or, they may be
held up to your fellow countrymen as ex-
amples of degradation and shame. It lies
with yourselves to choose your path, and to
walk therein. Let truth, modesty and vir-
tue be your standard, let your lives be open
to investigation, that all your actions may
bear the strictest scrutiny without calling a
blush to your cheek. Then no stain or
blemish will rest upon your characters and
the good you strive for will not be thrown
back as a mockery. Every woman should
guard her virtue as she would her life, that
no tarnish may rest on her fair fame. It is
a duty she owes, not only to herself, but to
her friends and to unborn generations. How
often are parents hearts bowed down and
their lives shortened by the reckless and
unwise course of their children. I often
think, when I see some broken-hearted
mother, mourning over the disgraceful con-
duct of her children, how heartless and
hardened they must be to cause so much
sorrow. If they have no regard for their
own honor or welfare, they might, at least,
respect the parents who reared them, who
would suffer and lay down their lives for

[COLUMN 2]

them if need be, and for THEIR sakes keep
themselves pure and unblemished. For
the sisters who love them, and whose pros-
pects in life may be blighted through no in-
discretion of their own, but through the
recklessness and ill conduct of those who
should, and do profess to love them. They
not only injure themselves, but they do an
irreparable wrong to those who are bound
to them by the tenderest ties of relation-
ship. Then I would say to my young
friends, be watchful and prayerful lest in
some unguarded moment the enemy should
take advantage, and a rash act be commit-
ted that can never, never be recalled. No
tears, sorrow, or repentance, no remorse,
however deep, can give back to a woman
her lost chastity. No atonement can erase
the blemish on her fair fame. She brings
upon herself a lifetime of agonies, of suffer-
ing and repentance.
	She not only loses her own respect, but
she loses the respect, and outrages the feel-
ings of her friends and kindred, and be-
queathes to her children a legacy of shame.
How must a young man or woman of intel-
ligence and high principles feel to bear the
brand of illegitimacy? Who can imagine
the agony of outraged feelings, when the
disgraceful information comes to them as it
is apt to do sooner or later. Could they be
blamed if they turned with loathing from
the parents they had learned to love and
reverence? Thus do, misdeeds follow the
perpetrator as long as life continues.
	And what of the man that could cause
all this sorrow and despair? Can he pass
without spot or blemish? Not by any means.
He should be marked for execration, held
up as a thing too foul for the companionship
of modesty and virtue. Man who is strong
in his passions, in his love and affections,
should be strong also in honor and self-con-
trol. He should look on every woman as a
mother or a sister, and guard her honor as
he would her life, with his own if need be.
What man would not strike down the
wretch who by violence would harm a de-
fenseless woman. How much more should
he be struck down who would insinuate
himself into her kind feelings, win her af-
fections, and in an unguarded moment, steal
from her the honor that is more sacred to
her than her life. No words can describe
the execration we should feel for such a vil-
lain, his life is not too much to pay the for-
feit. But, says one, does not marriage make
reparation? Yes, a poor pitiful reparation,
but not restoration. It cannot erase the
blemish from her crown of virtue, nor re-
store to her the happiness and innocence of
her maidenhood. Such marriages are al-
ways incomplete, and often unhappy. Ev-
ery man prefers taking to his home a pure
and unsullied bride, and he will not give
honor to those of tarnished name even
though he himself is the cause of it. Neith-
er will he give the same respect and con-
fidence to a woman whose purity he knows
to be of such doubtful texture.
	Girls, guard your virtue as with a wall of
adamant, and do not even countenance the
man who does not hold virtue sacred, shun
him as you would the viper that is seeking
your heart's blood. Do not let love plead
his cause, for he is unworthy the name of
love, much less the affection of a pure and
honorable woman. Give heed to the coun-
sel of your friends, and let your language
and manners be modest and refined. Let
all your thoughts be pure and holy. Cast
aside all books and papers that are calcula-
ted to influence the emotions in a wrong di-
rection or contaminate the mind. Shun the
companionship of the vulgar and unprinci-

[COLUMN 3]

pled, allow no undue familiarity in any of
your communications, and you will never
have cause for repentance.
	MARY.


WOMAN'S VOICE.

		OGDEN CITY, Aug. 29th, 1876.
	I wish to say a few words through
your valuable paper; thoughts suggested by
reading an article in the "Sacramento
Daily Union;" it is headed "THE TWO PRO-
PHETS."
	I know that this Gospel is true, and so do
thousands of my sisters, and we wish the
world to understand that we know it is the
church and kingdom of God. In the above
named article the question is asked, "What
will be the situation in Utah on the death
of Brigham Young? The answer, by Joseph
Smith Jr., is this: "I THINK there will be
nothing to hold the people together on
Young's demise." I can answer this ques-
tion according to knowledge and not on a
mere think so. I know that this is the
kingdom of God, and that it will never be
thrown down or given to another people,
but will stand forever; it came not of man
or the will of man, but from God our Eter-
nal Father, and it is His cause and His
work. Mr. Smith's statement in regard to
our leaders and their principles, as well as
their actual lives, are altogether false; and
it is hard work to read, and not attempt to
refute such imputations.
	There are many thousands in comforta-
ble homes through the wise management of
Brigham Young, and the blessing of God
our Heavenly Father, and I am one of
those. It also says we are compelled to
pay the tithing. I answer I have always
paid tithing, but never been compelled to
do so, I have done it from my own free will
and choice, because I knew it to be a com-
mandment of God, and binding upon His
people. It also says that what we term
Celestial Marriage is a heresy. I say it is
a commandment of God to His people, and
the Bible will sanction me in this declara-
tion. Father Abraham practiced this doc-
trine, also Jacob, and God talked with them
face to face," and to Moses and many others.
To the pure all things are pure. Joseph
Smith Jr., calls it immoral and unclean and
says he has fought against it. I am sorry
to hear of any one fighting against it, for
those who will do so will become as dark as
midnight, and will lose the Spirit of God,
if they ever had it.
	We are witnesses for our Father's cause
and kingdom, whether the world believe
us or not, and our witness will stand against
them at the great day of accounts; it will
be a saviour of life unto life, and death un-
to death. Then O let us be valiant my dear
sisters in testimony and witness for God and
His kingdom; I am proud of the EXPONENT
for this purpose.
	I am your sister in the Gospel,
						ELLEN MCKAY.


The steel that has suffered most is the best steel.
It has been in the furnace again and again, it has
been on the anvil; it has been tight in the jaws of
the vice; it has felt the teeth of the rasp; it has
been ground by emery; it has been heated and
hammered and filed, until it does not know itself,
and it comes out a splendid knife. And, if men
only knew it what are called their "misfortunes"
are God's best blessings, for they are the molding
influences which give them shapeliness and edge,
and durability and power,
