SALT LAKE CITY, OCT. [-]1, 1876.


NOTICE.

	We would say to our agents and subscrib-
ers that our terms to clubs are the same as
heretofore, no change having been made;
but we withdrew the article (Club Rates) as
our paper is small and we thought it suffi-
ciently understood; had any change been
made our agents and the public would have
been apprised of it.


THE POWER OF MAMMON.

	That money is the ruling power of the age,
has become proverbial. No matter how supe-
rior one may be in morals, or attainments, in
innate generosity of mind, or chivalry of char-
acter, custom will not exempt him from bow-
ing on the altar of Mammon.
	In this age of professed culture and refine-
ment, when all the accumulated erudition of
the ages of the past has concentrated itself in-
to one grand avalanche of knowledge and
showered itself in rich profusion to grace the
crowning era of worldly wisdoms, strange that
men and women should not inherit and pos-
sess more strength of principle and of charac-
ter, than to yield to this fascinating, all-absorb-
ing influence of the power of gold. It is a 
baneful deterioration to society, leading to all
sorts of stratagems and expediences to keep up
appearances that are false and shallow.
	The majority of people have not the cour-
age to live within their actual resources, be-
cause if they do, those whose companionship
they are desirous of retaining will "cut" them.
And for such flimsy reasons they ignore all
true pride of character, and become virtually
slaves in bondage to custom, or to the tyrant
money. They must maintain their position
and keep up a certain style—and at whose ex-
pense? In many instances not their own.
	Where is the change to begin, the great re-
formation, how will it commence? Sim-
plicity in dress, in the style of living—that is,
of eating, drinking, visiting, giving entertain-
ments—in order to come down to some solid
basis is highly necessary, or sooner, or later
the final crash which all are trying to avoid by
shifting the burden from one's shoulder to an-
others will be inevitable. Otherwise it would
seem necessary for every person to come into
possession of a gold mine in order to live up to
the style of this fast age.
	To listen only for a few minutes to the con-
versation of what is termed business men, one
cannot but draw the conclusion that money
has actually become the motive power that de-
termines the genuine value of every thing on 
earth.


What capital can a man possess to ensure
him permanant standing in modern society
without money? Honesty is apparently at a 
discount; will brains or mind avail him?
Not unless he can make practical use of 
them which will yeild a heavy percentage in 
hard coin. One would almost doubt whether a 
colossal mind would even call forth admira-
tion unless backed by a colossal fortune.
						[handwritten] E. B. Wel


NOTICE.

	A meeting of all the officers of the Relief
Societies and as many others as wish to attend
will be held on Wednesday, Oct. 4th, at 2
o'clock, p. m., in the Council House.


HOME AFFAIRS.
	Thursday Evening, Sept. 21, a Bishop's
meeting was held in the Council House build-
ing, at which time the house was dedicated
for the use of the Priesthood. Elder John
Taylor offered the dedicatory prayer.

	Mr. James Dwyer has gone East to pur-
chase a new and liberal stock of books and
holiday goods; and to attend the great Book
Fair at New York on the 25th of Oct.
	THE SALT Lake Theatre will open on the 5th
inst., and remain open during conference.
The first play presented upon the boards will
be "Through by Daylight" and Mr. Jim Ward
and Miss Winnetta Montague will play the
prinicipal parts.
	THE AMATEUR Dramatic Company who
have been playing for the benefit of Mrs. Ro-
mania Pratt, have succeeded well in Utah
County, having given good satisfaction, and
been renumerated financially, quite equal to
their expectations.
	UTAH MUSICAL TIMES.—We have received
the seventh number of this new magazine.
The editorial "Vocal Music in Schools," we
would recommend specially to the notice of all
interested in the cultivation and education of
the young. It also contains two very sweet
pieces of original music, one, composed by
Prof. Careless and the other, entitled "Prayer,"
by his wife.
	As a home paper, which contains items of
general interest, we take pleasure in recom-
mending the Ogden Junction, which has now
entered upon its fifth volume: as a local paper,
the citizens of Ogden and vicinity, have every
reason to be proud of it. It contains a vast
amount of real humor, and in this feature it is
particularly characteristic of the editor.
	THE MEETINGS, conventions etc, which are
being held in the interest of the coming elec-
tion, have been so far well attended, and good
order and quiet have been the rule. The elec-
tion of a Delegate to Congress to represent the
interests of the people in Utah, is an import-
ant subject, and the people, MEN and WOMEN,
who hold the right of franchise should be
prompt in exercising it to secure such a candi-
date as shall be qualified in all respects to pro
mote the best interests of the community.
	PORTLAND, SALT Lake, and South Pass rail-
road company, reorganized from the Portland,
Dallas and Salt Lake railroad company, in-
tend commencing to grade the road as soon as
the location is completed in Malad city. There
is a large corps of engineers in the field survey-
ing and locating the line. They intend to
complete the portion of the road to Malad city
and have it in running order by the 1st of
January. Colonel H. I. Chapman has charge
of the company's business and is forwarding
the work with great dispatch.
	The Deseret Silk Association will not hold
any meeting until sometime during the com-
ing conference, and then due public notice will
be given. It is expected that there will be a
great many of the brethren and sisters from
the country in the city then, and that all
those who are interested in seri-culture will at-
tend this meeting and such arrangements be
made, and instructions given, as shall awaken
increased interest, and promote the develop-
ment of this branch of home-industry. 

THE FORTY-SIXTH Semi-Annual Conference
is now very near: this people have been blest
in a most wonderful manner in establishing
themselves here in these vallies. This present
year there has been a bountiful harvest in
many parts of the Territory, and assuredly
should the people with one accord give praise
and thanksgiving to God whose hand has
been stretched out continually in their behalf.
There will be in all probability one of the larg-
est conferences ever assembled together in
Utah; It adds very much to the enjoyment of
any meeting and more especially is it neces-
sary where there is a multitudinous assem-
blage, to observe the laws of order and decor-
um. People can scarcely be too particular in
this respect.
 INTEMPERANCE AND in what way best to
check the growth of it, is a subject that is be-
ing considerably agitated in this community
at the present time. It is certainly one of the
most important matters which can be treated
upon, and requires the utmost thought, cau-
tion and wisdom in passing ordinances or reg-
ulations. It is one of the greatest evils with
which the world is contaminated and leads to
more crime than anything else in the whole
catalogue of vices. Every person who has
eyes to see may be aware of the great change
in this city in a very few years, in the growth
of this terrible sin; and it is decidedly time
that some efficient measures were taken to
prevent further increase of and profit from the
degrading traffic in this community. It is
sincerely to be hoped for the best interests of
all, and especially the young and innocent,
whose future it is the duty of the people to
carefully consider, that such measures may be
adopted as shall successfully hinder the sale of
ardent spirits, except for medicinal purposes.
God speed the time when every saloon here,
or elsewhere shall be closed forever. This sub-
ject should be kept before the people until some
permanent movement can be unitedly deter-
mined upon.
 THE  SUNDAY School: Jubilee of Cache
County, held in the bowery at Logan, Sep. 24,
1876, was in its complete success most encour-
aging, not only to those who had been instru-
mental in bringing about the gratifying result,
but to all persons interested in the future wel-
fare and progress of the youth of the Territory.
The Sunday School is one of the most efficient
educators of children. The congregation, it is
believed, consisted of over 2,000 children, and
about two thirds, or three-fourths as many
adults. Elder George Goddard, Assistant Gen-
eral Superintendent of the Sunday School
Union, addressed the children on the power of
the Sunday School to inculcate and promote
the best and truest principles in the minds of
the young, at a time when they were suscepti-
ble of receiving lasting impressions. Bishop
Wm. B. Preston in his remarks alluded to the
pleasure he felt in being able to say that there
were no "grog shops" there to allure and pol-
lute the habits and morals of the children, and
felt that he would like to take that vast assem-


blage in his arms and care for, and carry them
through, pure and unspotted from the evils of
the world, and see the children grow up in vir-
tue and purity."
	On reading this we could but think what an
appropriate speech for a Bishop, the father of
a people. Would that there were many such
fathers who would carry out in their lives the
feeling portrayed so eloquently in this brief
sentence. President Brigham Young. Jr., in
his address, contrasted the multitude there as
sembled, to the few who were only a few years
since gathered in Salt Lake City. He alluded
in a pathetic manner to the history of Joseph
Smith, said when the children were answer
ing the questions, he could fancy he saw "the
boy Joseph Smith wending his way to the
hill Cumorah." Spoke of the joy he must
have felt in receiving the plates from the stone
box. He alluded briefly to the joys and sor-
rows that afterward followed, and said the rec-
ollection of these things brought to him both
pain and pleasure in meditating upon them.
	Sup't. Moses Thatcher read some selected
lines which were very appropriate to the occas-
ion.
	Towards the close of the Jubliee a standing
musical committee was formed. The Jubliee
was most thoroughly enjoyed by all present,
and the most perfect harmony and good order
prevailed.

MISTAKES CORRECTED.

	Mrs. Duniway, in an account of her recent
visit to this city, speaks of going through the
"great 'co-op' on Sunday." We are willing to
believe that the mistake thus published in her
paper, the "New North West," was merely a
typographical error. Still, it demands atten-
tion; as the erroneous idea might be created
thereby, that the "Mormons" have no more
respect for the Sabbath than to have their
stores open, and business transacted upon that
day. Mrs. Duniway's visit to the building re-
ferred to, occurred on Saturday instead of on
Sunday.
	She mentions "a Mormon lady who acted as
chaperon, and was both obliging and intelli-
gent." We bow acknowledgement to this deli-
cate compliment; and consequently we are
prepared to speak knowingly concerning the
circumstances of which Mrs. Duniway writes
during her visit to the store known as "The
Co-op." She speaks of a gentleman being ac-
costed by her chaperon, and relates the follow-
ing short conversation which took place be-
tween them.
	"'Have you been to see—lately?' evidently
meaning one of the wives of the man address-
ed.
	'No,' said he, 'not for some time. How are
they getting along?'
	"Nicely,' replied our guide; 'but you ought
to go and see her. She has a nice new baby.'
	'I know I ought to go, but I'm too busy,'
said the saint."
	Mrs. D. then comments as follows:
	"When the mothers of men are thus neglect-
ed, through viciousness and drunkeness, when
undergoing the fiery ordeal of unwelcome and
enforced maternity, men denounce the cruelty,
though failing to prevent it by law; but what
can we say of such inhumanity when prac-
ticed under the name of religion, and adhered
to as an ordinance of God, the law; meanwhile,
remaining powerless?"
	Was Mrs. Duniway watching for iniquity,
that she imagined she discovered a huge black
fact, where in truth, not the shadow of a dark
reality was visible?
	We take pleasure in correcting this mistake


also, which we consider of too much import
ance to pass unnoticed. The gentleman with
whom the above conversation was held, is the
father-in-law, not the husband of the lady
spoken of; which, we think, alters the appear-
ance of the case materially. We do not, and
would not knowingly hold conversation with
such an inhuman husband and father as Mrs.
Duniway must have considered our gentle-
manly and much esteemed friend. It is not
among the Mormons, we are happy to say,
that "the mothers of men," and their offspring
are "thus neglected." The present glory and
the anticipated exaltation of the Latter-day
Saint, reclines in his little kingdom begun on
earth.
	We hope the "New North West" will pub-
lish this explanation, which as an advocate of
truth and true intelligence, it should do in jus-
tice to its subscribers as well as to a people who
are often ignorantly maligned.


For the EXPONENT.
OUR PARENTS' LOVE.

	There is no love as true and faithful as
the love our parents give us, and for none
are we so ungrateful. We take this devo-
tion as something which is our right, or as
something we deserve, even though our
mothers toil and labor and deny them-
selves time after time for our gratification,
from our earliest infancy till our growing
to womanhood. We can never comprehend
the extent of her love and patience with
us; yet is there anything like it? Our lover
may desert us, our friends vanish, and in
our sorrow and distress leave us; but our
parents cling to us in all trials and troubles,
and we are dearer to them for any misfor-
tune or grief we may chance to have, or
meet with  in this life. Then let us do all
we can to make our parents hearts rejoice,
and that will add to their happiness in any
way, and show them by our actions that we
appreciate their kindness and love, and try
to lighten their troubles and labors; then,
when they shall leave us, we will have
nothing to reproach ourselves with on their
account; but to look forward eagerly to the
time when we shall meet them with joy
and gladness, and be assured that the meet-
ing will be a welcome one.
	M. J. R. R. HAMPTON.
S. L. City.


[Column 1]
				WOMAN.

	I do not know how I shall throw my ideas
into language, but my theme is Woman! I
fear I shall fail in doing justice to the sub-
ject, yet I will try; I am a woman's friend
in the fullest sense, for I will show how
much we owe to woman! Who gave me
life, naturally speaking? My mother! who
watched over me in my baby-hood, and
boyhood, in sickness and in health? Who
taught me to speak? Who taught me to
pray? My mother! who has watched over
me through the critical moments of youth-
ful life up to manhood? My Mother! Who
told me when I was wild, oh! so wild! to
obey the Priesthood? My Mother! Who
gave me strength to subdue my wild nature
by her kind teachings and wise suggestions?
My Mother! Who on earth are my two
best friends to-day? My wife, and my
Mother; I class them together now, because
I know no difference! Being so blest, should
not woman be my theme? And where can
I get a better subject to write upon? Ladies!
I ask you from 19 to 95—do you ever think
of your position on this earth? The influ-

[Column 2]
ence you throw around, either for good or
for evil? Think of it! Think of the power
you have for good, or its opposite! Do you
realize the life-giving faculty you possess,
like your Mother Earth? Do you try to un-
derstand your present, and your final des-
tiny? Oh! Women! Oh! Girls! if you would
only think, if you would only "take coun-
sel together," and say "come let us reason
together," if this be true, let us take heed and
obey it, what is wrong, let us refuse and
endeavor to appreciate and practice that
which is wise and good. Let me (although
a comparative stranger to many of you)
persuade you to think, think, think of your
position in life! You will say, perhaps, where
is my position? Well, I will try and give
you some of my ideas, but hold! I shall get
myself into a "fix," for I was going to pre-
sent my wife as a specimen! But perhaps
you would say, "oh! he's infatuated." Well
then to suit you I won't take her, but of
course you will let me take my Mother!
My own Mother! My Teacher in early
years, and up to the present time; No, I
hardly dare trust you yet! So I wont take
my Mother! If you all knew her, I would
not fear you censuring me for partiality—
but don't we all love our mothers? Are they
not literally our guardian angels upon the
earth? Yes—and we may add, "We knew it
not!" Now I must finish these desultory
thoughts and ideas, with a few more that
are in my mind. Burns, in speaking of the
works of Nature, says:

	"Her 'prentice Han', she tried on Man,
	And then she made the Lasses, O!"

I believe that God ordained man to be the
Leader and Woman to be his "Help-meet,"
that is, that she should walk foot to foot,
and shoulder to shoulder with her Brother
Man! Why, oh! why cannot you as Sisters,
as Wives, as Mothers, as Women as Girls,
follow in the footsteps of your Brother Man,
if worthy, in whatever capacity, or position
he may stand to you. Give me the woman
who is one with me through the journey of
life, my God, her God, my hope, her hope;
my people, her people. Do I ask too much?
Verily No! There have been many such
women upon the earth, though they are
among the few! but quite enough to re-
deem the sex. Then my sisters, awake
from your lethargy, your apathy, shake off
the listlessness that now enfolds so many
and say we will realize the Mission God has
given us, whether man helps us or not,
whether he owns it or not we WILL be a
Helpmeet for Man.
							IOTA,
	S. L. City, Sep., 1876.


HYGIENIC NOTES.

	For Erysipelas:—Take smartweed moisten with
water, and apply to the part affected. If the
herb can be obtained green it is preferable; a tea
of the same drank inwardly will assist in remov-
ing the disease from the interior of the body.
Erysipelas is caused by an injudicious living,
which creates a putrefaction of the blood. No
person would have this disease if sufficient out-
door exercise was taken accompanied by a natu-
ral and proper diet, and sponge-bath every even-
ing and morning to keep the pores of the skin
free, so that nature could throw off the impure
matter which accumulates. This disease is often
caused by medicine which has been previously ta-
ken, and may have been gathering in the system
for years, in which case there is no permanent
cure except to purify the blood.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
	YOUNG LADIES OF SOCIETY.

		BY BLANCHE BEECHWOOD.

							"I your glass,
		Will modestly discover to yourself,
		That of yourself, which you know not of."
	The term young lady as expressed in
"common parlance," means one that dress-
es stylishly; if she has an indulgent father,
one that can be coaxed and wheedled out of
money for a caress, a few kisses, or win-
ning smiles, or, perchance, if the occasion
is urgent, a few sobs and a shower of tears.
Her hair must be frizzed and crimped, and
arranged "a la mode," regardless of facial
or cranial suitability. She is harmonized
and toned down to the level of modern
small talk, and has no disagreeable hob-
bies or opinions to make her offensive
in the eyes of the opposite sex; she is so in-
nocent and unsophisticated that whatever
she does is beautiful, in their eyes at least,
and in whatever she says or does she mani-
fests that she knows the power of sweet
looks and simplicity.
	She never seeks to lighten one's burdens
not even those of her own father or mother;
she is too delicate for any occupation, save
the fancy work she toys with, and she has
no power to strengthen or make happy the
lives of others. Even her little brothers
and sisters are an annoyance to her, she
dislikes children and if she marries never
intends to have any. She expects every
one to accommodate her, or yield her supre-
macy, the best seats, and the first privileges
are hers by right, one would suppose from her
behavior. Her accomplishments are varied
but she excels in none; she sings a little
when it pleases her, or plays a little on the
piano, or guitar, she knows a few French
phrases and has read scores of novels. She
is well versed in small talk and knows how
to flirt to perfection. She can dance all
night, but cannot possibly assist her mother
in sweeping, dusting or any household em-
ployment because it is too fatiguing.
	She ridicules the idea of women taking
any prominent part in any public enter-
prise, or meeting, is disgusted with wo-
men who want to vote, she expects she will
be all right, let the condition of politics or
the national affairs be prosperous or other-
wise, the world is indebted to her, one
would imagine so sure is she of her rights.
	She feels sadly ashamed of the mother
who ventures to suggest the propriety of
being self-sustaining, and her mother would
not by any means, like her to know that
she really had addressed a public congre-
gation.
	She knows nothing of the real worth of
money only as a power to be used for her
comfort, and to procure her kid gloves, em-
broidery, laces and jewelry. She never
thinks of denying herself any pleasure that
it may administer solace to another. SELF,
is her prevailing impulse. Her whole
every-day life is one grand mistake from
beginning to end, and will culminate in a 
grand failure.
	The fashionable young lady estimates
men by their faultless attire, the whiteness
of their hands and teeth, the fashionable
cut of their hair, the peculiar curl of their
mustache, and their polished manners. This
is her "beau ideal" no matter if his mind
is as bare and as unfruitful as the desert of
Sahara, if he possess these qualifications and
added to them has money to board at a ho-
tel or travel about he's a "good catch."
	Is this a fitting example for the daughters
of Zion? and are some of them not trying
to ape these very things, living continually
for the indulgence of SELF, without any

[Column 2]
higher aim than to create and foster admir-
ation?
	When one contemplates the great work
which woman has assigned her upon this
earth, and the necessity of commencing it
early, fostering and inculcating those gen-
uine principles of virtue, chastity, self-de-
nial and true nobility and generosity that
will develop into the most sublime attri-
butes in the character of woman as a wife
and mother, how earnest one should be to
impress upon the minds and hearts of the
daughters of Zion the great importance of
improving time and opportunities.

	"I would not waste my spring of youth,
	In idle dalliance; I would plant rich seeds,
	To blossom in my womanhood, and bear fruit
						When I am old."

	S. L. City, Sept. 23rd, 1876.


[COLUMN 1]

WOMAN'S CONVENTION.

	A delegate convention of the Woman
Suffragists of Mass., was held in Meionaon,
Tremont Temple, Boston, Sept. 12, 1876, in
response to a call made upon them for that
purpose. Their motive was to consider po-
litical duties in regard to the fall elections
and to make nominations for State officers
should it then seem advisable.
	The Committee, or Credentials reported
that there were present 706 delegates, rep-
resenting 105 towns and wards. Mrs. Mary
A. Livermore was elected Chairman. The
meeting was one of special interest, many
subjects were discussed, all aiming at Wo-
man Suffrage.
	The Convention read the second time the
resolutions that had been adopted in 1872,
which were received with cheers.
	"Resolved, That we heartily approve of
the recognition of the Rights of Woman
contained in the National Republican plat-
form; that the Republican party of Massa-
chusetts, as the representative of Liberty
and Progress, is in favor of extending Suf-
frage on equal terms to all American citi-
zens irrespective of sex, and will hail the
day when the educated intellect and en-
lightened conscience of Woman finds di-
rect expression at the ballot box.
	Mr. Lothrop, for the Committee on Reso-
lutions, reported the following:

DECLARATION OF PRINCIPLES.
	The Woman Suffragists of Massachusetts
in Delegate Convention assembled, respect-
fully submit to the people of the Common-
wealth the reasons which compel them for
the first time to make independent nomina-
tions for State officers.
	For many years they have appealed to
both the great political parties of the State
to rectify the injustice which taxes and gov-
erns women without their consent, and
which subjects them to cruel legal disabili-
ties, as wives, mothers and widows. But
their appeals have been disregarded. The
Republican party of Massachusetts, after
repeatedly endorsing Woman Suffrage in
its platform and repudiating it in the Legis-
lature, has just refused to invite Republican
women to take part in the nomination of
their representatives, and has framed a
platform which it declares has a double
meaning on the Suffrage issue. The Demo-
cratic party of the State has refused to
take any action on the subject. The Pro-
hibitory party of the State, on the contrary,
has invited women to take part in its pri-
mary meetings, with an equal voice and
vote in the nomination of candidates and
the transaction of business. It has made
the establishment of Woman Suffrage one

[COLUMN 2]

of its avowed objects, and has nominated
candidates, all of whom are Suffragists.
	State governments have jurisdiction over
matters with which the National Govern-
ment has no concern. We maintain that
State officers should be elected upon State
issues, independent of National politics.
And, whereas, Woman Suffrage is purely a
question of State policy, it is the duty of
Woman Suffragists of Massachusetts so to
cast their ballots next November as to rep-
resent their principles in the choice of
State officers.
	Believing that "Governments are just
only when they rest on the consent of the
governed," and that the establishment of
a truly representative government is vastly
more important than the success of Rice or
Adams, we commend the nominees of this
Woman Suffrage State Convention to the
Suffrages of the people, irrespective of
party, as the candidates who represent Im-
partial Suffrage and Equal Rights for all."
	Mrs. Livermore's speech was spirited
and eloquent, containing many excellent
points; she referred to the temperance
movement resting with women and that
men felt it to be so; she believed there
were 30,000 votes in the state that could be
obtained for Woman Suffrage. She spoke
of the influence of women in making pub-
lic meetings quiet and orderly. She ex-
horted women not to be scared by the word
"unwomanly," which scares so many out of
their boots. The definition of the word
womanly, means "being a fool," to most
men." She said if consecration makes
men noble, it lifts women to the height of
a grand purpose."
	Mrs. Spaulding, of Cambridge, offered
the following resolution, which was passed
without discussion:
	"Resolved, That we pledge ourselves as
Suffragists to vote for no person for Sena-
tor or Representative to the General Court,
who is not fully committed to the Suffrage
movement, and that the State Central Com-
mittee be instructed to take measures to en-
able Suffragists to carry this resolution at
the polls."


R. S. REPORT.

		CEDAR VALLEY, Sep. 13, 1876.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	On the 21st of April, 1875, we had a pleas-
ant visit from Sister E. R. Snow, who or-
ganized among us a Retrenchment Associ-
ation Since that time we have met once a
week, and although we are but few in num-
ber, our meetings are generally interesting.
We read selections from the Exponent and
other good works, and original essays. We
have done no work in our meetings until
lately, now we meet every two weeks to
do work of different kinds. We desire to
improve ourselves and to do all we can to
forward the work in which we are engaged.
The officers who were selected are as fol-
lows:
	Miss Rebecca Rodeback, President,
	"	Polly D. W.,		 }
	"	Melissa Weeks,	 }
	"	Mary J. Glines,	 } Counselors.
	"	Phebe Wilcox,	 }
	Mrs. Harriet Walton,	 }
	"	Margaret Bennett, }
	Miss Mariett Cook, Secretary,
	"	Serena White, Assistant Secretary.
	Wishing your paper success, and the
blessing of God to attend you in your la-
bors, and hoping you will excuse this, my
first attempt, I remain your sister,
				SERENNA. WHITE, Sec'y.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


For the EXPONENT.
MISUSE OF POWER.

	God never gave to man nor woman,
brains, heart and muscular strength, with
the intention that any power pertaining to
the organization should remain dormant;
or that either should tryannize over and sub-
ject the others. Yet, in our mortal weak-
ness, we call it "human nature," that one
propensity over-balances the rest at times,
and often in spite of better judgment,
makes men and women speak and act like
dolts;
	To bring all the selfish and ignoble quali-
ties of our natures into subjection before
the better and higher ones, is the individ-
ual mission assigned to each one of us. But
how we over-step the bounds at times, and
wander strangely from the given path. Our
great desire to be magnanimous in helping
others to improve their ways, makes us for-
getful to reform our own.
	And thus it is, that whether we study
and practice self-control or not, we are quick
to show our wonderful appreciation of
right and order, by assaying to govern ac-
tions, tongues and tempers for those of
whom we receive the guardianship. If we
are parents, our children should be perfect,
or as nearly so as possible, notwithstanding
our own inconsistencies. If Statesmen,
rulers of a nation, the people for whose ben-
efit we labor, who feed and clothe, and find
us luxurious homes, must be respectfully
submissive to our prudent, wise decrees,
even though we, ourselves, are open viola-
ters of the country's laws.
	But such is not the pattern God has given
for those who wish to follow after Christ.
He requires none to be more holy than
Himself. To prove that Abraham would be
His faithful Friend, He asked no larger
boon than He was ready to bestow Him-
self on the ungrateful, unbelieving world.
	If this example were followed by God's
children on the earth, we should have no
"might opposing right," we should know
no wrongs created by the misuse of power.
	MABEL.


BEAUTIFUL EXTRACT.

	[The following beautiful tribute to Wo-
man was written many years ago. It occurs
in a tale of touching interest, entitled "The
Broken Heart"--its author, Dr. F. J. Strat-
ton:]—
	"Oh, the priceless value of the love of a
PURE woman! Gold cannot purchase a gem
so precious! Titles and honors confer upon
the heart no such serene happiness. In our
darkest moments, when disappointment
and ingratitude, with corroding care, gather
thick around, and even the gaunt form of
poverty menaces with his skeleton fingers,
it gleams around the soul with an angel's
smile. Time cannot mar its brilliancy; dis-
tance but strengthens its influence; bolts
and bars cannot limit its progress; it follows
the prisoner into his dark cell, and sweetens
the homely morsel that appeases his hunger,
and in the silence of midnight it plays
around his heart, and in his dreams he folds
to his bosom the form of her who loves on
still, though the world has turned coldly
from him. The couch made by the hand of
the loved one is soft to the weary limbs of
the sick sufferer, and the potion administered
by the same hand loses half its bitterness.
The pillow carefully adjusted by her brings
repose to the fevered brain, and her words
of kind encouragement revive the sinking
spirit. It would almost seem that God,


compassionating woman's first great frailty,
had planted this jewel in her breast, whose
heavenlike influence should cast into for-
getfulness man's remembrance of the Fall,
by building up in his heart another Eden,
where perennial flowers forever bloom, and
crystal waters gush from exhaustless foun-
tains."


FLORAL HINTS.

	AUTUMN LEAVES:—Gather as large a num-
ber and as great a variety of these leaves
as you wish, use old books for drying, or old
magazines, or newspapers; put as many
leaves on a page as you can, only not let
them touch each other, then turn five or
six pages and arrange similarly until you fill
your book; place in a cool dry place under
a heavy weight for twenty-four hours, then
remove the leaves to a dry book; change
them thus three times, when they will be
in beautiful condition and ready for use.
Get some cake wax, such as is used for
"fruit molding," put it in a vessel and set
that in a pan of water on the stove; when
melted, add a few drops of turpentine or
fir balsam, to render the wax pliable; when
the wax is the right temperature, dip the
leaves in one by one, or spray by spray and
hold each until cool, then place them upon
newspapers to harden completely: Thus
prepared they will present the natural ap-
pearance of the leaf, but to give them a
glossy surface give them a thin coat of De-
mar varnish with a camel's hair brush.
The best time to gather ferns for winter use
is September and October, also all kinds of
grasses and varieties of autumn berries.
Ladies of skill and ingenuity may arrange
these so artistically as to beautify and adorn
their homes with very little expense.


FASHIONABLE WOMEN.

	Fashion kills more women than toil and
sorrow. Obedience to fashion is a greater
transgression to the laws of woman's nature,
a greater injury to her physical and mental
constitution, than poverty and neglect.
The slave-woman at her tasks will live and
grow old, and will see two or three gener-
ations of her mistresses fade and pass away.
The washer-woman, with scarce a ray of
hope to cheer her in her toils, will live to
see her fashionable sisters die all around
her. The kitchen-maid is is hearty and strong,
when her lady has to be nursed like a sick
baby. It is a sad truth that fashion-pam-
pered women are almost worthless for all
the ends of human life. They have but
little force of character; they have still less
power of moral will, and quite as little phys-
ical energy. They live for no great purpose
in life: they accomplish no worthy ends.
They are only doll forms in the hands of
miliners and servants, to be dressed and fed
to order. They dress nobody, and save no-
body. They write no books; they set no
rich examples of virtue and womanly life.
If they rear children, servants do all save
to conceive and give them birth—and when
reared, what are they? What do they ever
amount to but weak scions of the old stock?
Who ever heard of a fashionable woman's
child exhibiting any virtue or power of
mind for which it became eminent? Read
the biographies of our great and good men
and women. Not one of them had a fash-
ionable mother. They nearly all sprang
from strong-minded women, who had about
as little to do with fashion as with the
changing clouds.—Ex.


SKELETON OR PHANTOM LEAVES:—The
collection of the various kinds of leaves
having been made, place them in an open
tub or pan, covering them several inches
above the leaves with rain water, then put
them in the open air in full sunlight. Put
a pane or two of glass or a light china plate
over them with a weight sufficient to keep
them well down in the water, the loss of
which by evaporation must be made up by
adding more when needed, to keep up the
quantity. After two weeks examine them
and if some are pulpy, take those out, let
those which have kept well remain, exam-
ine them once or twice a week, do not dis-
turb the water too much; after the leaf has
become slimy and pulpy remove them by
slipping a card under them, and allowing
them to float off into a basin of clear water.
The cleaning process is the next, for which
you will need a stiff camel's hair brush, a
stiff bristle brush and a tooth-brush. Slip
the leaf off upon a piece of smooth glass,
gently pass the brush over the surface, aid-
ing the brush with a stream of water poured
over it carefully. Then slip the leaf again
into the water, and let it float off on to the
glass on the opposite side cleaning that in
the same manner; the green surface must
be entirely removed until nothing but the
skeleton or fibrous veins remain; if the soft
brush will not do it, the stiffer one or the
tooth-brush must be applied; and for some
strong leaves gentle scrubbing is necessary;
it must not be a sweeping motion but a
downward tapping. As fast as the leaves
are cleaned they must be put in clean water
and left until the bleaching process, which
should be done as soon as possible. The
best method for bleaching is with a solution
of chloride of lime, and immersing them a
day or two in a covered dish, in a dark clos-
et, and covered with a folded towel.
To make the solution, take one table-spoon-
ful of chloride of lime in a quart of water,
adding a few grains of citric-acid, shaking
well until dissolved, then bottling the clear
liquid for use. Some experienced operators
prefer using chloride of soda. After bleach-
ing the leaves, put them in clear water for
twenty-four hours, float off on a card and
turn over on a soft napkin, and gently press
until all the moisture is absorbed, and then
curled gracefully or pressed between the
leaves of an old book, under pressure. They
will now bear handling and can be arrang-
ed to suit the taste, under a glass shade or
in a deep recess frame. Frames lined with
dark-colored velvet are very beautiful. A
black cross covered with them is very
pretty. Such leaves as contain tannin should
never be placed with others, such as Oak
and Hazel. A method sometimes used for
these leaves is to put with them a number
of the caddis worms which eat away all of
the soft green part of the leaf, leaving the
skeleton entire. The making up of these
phantom leaves requires tact and nicety,
but the effect is very ornamental.

day of her entrance at boarding school said: "The
first evening we had prayers and then singing, and
a passing round of bread which I did not take,
because not being confirmed, I thought I had no
right to take communion. Afterward I learned I
had lost my supper.

or s[-]t about too soon.

HOUSEHOLD HINTS.

	Icing for Plum Cake.—Take the white of an
egg, a quarter of a pound of loaf-sugar, and a tea-
spoonful of gum dragon, melted. Mix them into
a paste, and lay it on the cake.
	Carpet Moths.—We find that ground pepper,
plentifully sprinkled about the edges of carpets,
will prevent them, in a great measure at least,
from the ravages of this destructive creature.
	Night Light.—Put a bit of phosphorus into a
small phial, then fill it one-third with boiling olive
oil, and cork it close; whenever the stopper is ta-
ken out in the night, light will be evolved suffi-
cient to show the hour upon a watch.
	Making Vinegar.—To eight gallons of clear
rain water, add three quarts of molasses, put into
a good cask, shake well a few times, then add two
or three spoonfuls of good yeast cakes. If in sum-
mer, place the cask in the sun; if in winter, near
the stove, where it may be warm. In ten or fif-
teen days, add to the liquid a sheet of brown pa-
per, torn in strips, dipped in molasses; and good
vinegar will be produced. The paper will, in this
way, form what is called the "mother" or life of
vinegar.


Catarrh Snuff:—Powdered elecompane root,
half an ounce; powdered myrrh, a quarter of an
ounce; gum arabic a quarter of an ounce; blood-
root, half a drachm. Mix altogether and use
twice a day. It must be continued to effect a per-
manent cure.
	For Dyspepsia:—One ounce of double tanzy,
dry, one ounce of boneset, one ounce of flour of
hops, two ounces of spearmint, one ounce of car-
away seed, half a pint of Holland gin, half a
pint of cold, soft water, a teacup half full of
loaf-sugar, a quarter of a pound of glauber salt.
Add all together, then strain, and take a desert
spoonful before each meal; and on going to bed.
	Benefit of Laughter:—A hearty laugh is a vi-
talizer to the whole body. The blood moves quickly
and as its chemical electricity conveys a different
impression to all the organs of the body it car-
ries on its mystic journey new life, and electric
force. There is no doubt a good laugh promotes
happiness, and brings joy and gladness to the
heart. It is better than all the pills and electric
shocks in Christendom:—C. A. Von Cort, M. D.


MISCELLANEOUS.

	WHAT species of love is that which is
never reciprocated?—A neuralgic affection.
	An undoubted truth.—The people
want the gas companies not only to make
their dwellings light, but their bills light-
er.
	England has waged forty-nine wars since
1300; France, thirty-eight; Russia, twenty-
two; Austria, twelve, and Prussia, eight.
	The stone bridge across the river Taff, in
England, was built in 1746. It has a span
of one hundred and seventy feet, and is as
firm to-day as when built.
	CONSIDERATE.—"Now Spriggins my
dear," said Mrs. Spriggins at the theatre
after the first act, "you needn't go out to
get any coffee for I've got a pound in my
pocket."


AT the request of the king of Sweden,
Mme. Nilsson consented to appear as Mar-
guerite in the Royal Theatre at Stockholm.
She has been enthusiastically received in
her native land, and feted and honored as
few but queens ever are.


A curious calculation has been made in
the printing office of a Paris newspaper.
Assuming that a good compositor handles
24,000 letters a day, and that his hand moves
one foot with each letter in taking and set-
ting the type, it will be seen that his hand
travels 43,000 feet, or nearly ten miles, a
day, and in the course of a year, excluding
Sundays, travels fully the distance from
Paris to New York.
	MAKING A FOOL OF ONESELF.—Making a
fool of oneself is with many people a part
of the educational experience of life. Once
brought to the experimental realization of
this fact, one is apt to be wiser ever after.
A man generally makes a fool of himself by
wasting his money; or going into some mad
speculation; or believing in mines and com-
panies that are but traps for the unwary; or
by losing his temper at a critical moment.
A woman manages to do the same thing
through the means of her tongue or her
heart. She talks herself into tribulation:
or she gives her love to some worthless
creature who becomes the bane of her life.
It is over his empty purse that a man gen-
erally stands and clenches his hands, and
mutters, "I've made a fool of myself." It
is over her empty heart that a woman usual-
ly wails forth the same words.
	Let those who would attain real greatness keep
themselves true to their conscience and to their
Creator; let them order their lives with wise sim-
plicity; be frugal in all the substances of life; and
set the example so greatly needed in modern times,
of wholesome moderation. Show by what you
do and by what you are, that you esteem character
above all else.

WOMAN'S EXPONENT
VOL. 5.	SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, OCTOBER 15, 1876.	No.10


NEVER GIVE UP.

SELECTED.

Never give up! It is wiser and better
	Always to hope than once to despair;
Fling off the load of doubt's cankering fetter,
	And break off the spell of tyrannical care.
Never give up! Or the burden may sink you;
	Providence kindly has mingled the cup:
And in all trials or troubles bethink you,
	The watchword of life must be "Never give up."

Never give up! There are chances and changes
	Helping the hopeful a hundred to one:
And, through the chaos, high wisdom arranges
	Ever success if you'll ever hope on.
Never give up! for the wisest is boldest,
	Knowing that Providence mingles the cup;
And of all maxims, the best, as the oldest,
	Is the true watchword of "Never give up!"

Never give up, though the grape-shot may rattle,
	Or the thunder-cloud over you burst:
Stand like a rock, and the storm or the battle
	Little shall harm you, though doing their worst.
Never give up! if adversity presses,
	Providence wisely has mingled the cup;
And the best counsel in all your distresses,
	Is the stout watchword of "Never give up.


[COLUMN 1]

OUR HOME HERE AND HEREAFTER.

	Let us imagine 'tis Spring! and every-
thing is bright and beautiful: all living
things are filled with new life and vigor.
Man, the noblest work of God, is light-
hearted and happy, ready to commence
another year of labor. The birds are
singing, bees are humming, and the
innumerable voices of life are listened
to with thankfulness to our Maker. Homes
of every description are being built by the
various inhabitants of the earth; the sky is
a deep, clear blue, free from all clouds, and
the sun that has been hidden for so long
now shines forth in all his glory, casting his
warm rays upon the damp earth, causing
vegetation to spring forth; the trees that
have been bare for so long are undergoing
a change preparatory to regaining their
rich foliage and verdure; the brooks and
rivers are filled and gushing over with clear
sparkling water, and all nature seems fresh
and new. Time rolls on. Summer takes
the place of spring, all nature is in its ma-
tured state, and every thing at its height,
and we experience all the pleasure and
buoyancy which summer brings to the ad-
mirer of nature's perfection; this passes by
and gives way to Autumn, the busiest of
all seasons; the fresh green verdure which
looks so cool and pleasant has undergone a
change; bright, brilliant hues have taken
the place of the green foliage and shrubbery,
and when looking at them we know it is
the brightening up before death, that it is
near the time of decay for them; rich, lus-
cious fruit weighs down the branches of the
trees, the grasses are withering and the
leaves are falling; it is at this season we
have our glorious sunsets, when it seems as
if the whole heavens were illuminated, one
blaze of living light, and it occurs to us
that all inanimate nature is giving one last
heroic struggle before death. The last of
the successive seasons is winter; black
clouds are heaped up in the sky, the winds
are whistling and the frost is biting, but is
this winter really cheerless and cold to us?
No, if we have warm, comfortable homes, are

[COLUMN 2]

surrounded by friends, and above all if we
have warm hearts; it is then that friends
and families have most opportunity of con-
versational intercourse with each other, ex-
changing visits, and enjoying each other's
society at home and around the fireside; it
is then that we should use every effort to
make our homes pleasant and happy, so
that everything will be bright and cheerful
within, no matter how cold and cheerless
without; it is then we can most fully enjoy
the profits of our former labor; this is the
earthly home our Father gives temporarily,
in order to protect ourselves from the tem-
pest; we build structures or houses, and
consequently shut out the beauties of nature
it is therefore necessary that we should
beautify the interior of our dwellings; there
are a variety of ways in which we can do it
and benefit ourselves. Do not our lives
compare with that of nature? The life of
mankind upon the earth compares well with
the variety of nature's seasons. We are
sent upon earth, to do a certain work;
why should we not do that work uncom-
plainingly and grow up good, strong and
wise men, and women so that our Father
would be proud of us? The farmer who
plants his trees is proud of those that with-
stand all the tests of the elements, and
grow up to be large and stalwart, and speaks
of those that wither and become dwarfed
and ugly as not of much worth, perhaps we
are judged likewise.
	If we live faithfully through the Spring,
Summer and Fall of our lives, according to
the dictation of our heavenly Father, the end
will be as our home in winter and warm
within we will be ready to meet our Father
with a clear conscience and unblushing faces,
and we will know we are worthy of holding a
high position in the kingdom of God, for
He will be pleased with us, and we will be
rewarded according to our works.
	M. M.


BOOKS FOR A HOME.

	Books are not made for furniture, but
there is nothing else that so beautifully
furnishes a house. The plainest row of
books is more significant of refinement
than the most elaborately carved side-board.
Give us a home furnished with books rather
than furniture. Both, if you can, but books at
any rate. To spend several days at a
friend's house and hunger for something to
read, while you are treading on costly car-
pets and sitting on luxurious chairs and
sleeping upon down, is as if one were brib-
ing your body for the sake of cheating your
mind. Books are the windows through
which the soul looks out. A house without
them is like a room without windows. No
man has a right to bring up his children
without surrounding them with books, if
he has the means to buy them. It is a wrong
to his family. Children learn to read through
being in the presence of books. The love
of knowledge comes with reading and
grows upon it. And the love of knowledge
in a young mind is almost a warrant against
the inferior excitements of passion and
vice.


NOTES AND NEWS.

	ANNA DICKINSON will soon appear in
Utica in her new play, and if she isn't suc-
cessful there will be a real tragedy.
	ALBERT WELLES of the Society Library
N. Y., is about to publish "The Pedigree
and History of the Washington family.
This will be authentic.
	MR. CHARLES Francis Adams, it is said,
is as persistent in work as was ever his fa-
ther or grandfather. Though he has a car-
riage, he seldom rides, and always walks
five miles or more every day, winter or
summer.
	THE BOSTON POST considers Mrs. Lucy
Stone a very wise woman in selecting the
Democratic party to represent the interests
of Woman Suffrage, the champion in the
cause being the Hon. Richard Fothering-
ham, and agreeing all petitions to Congress
be presented by him.
	PRINCE LOUIS Lucien Bonaparte has for
many years been engaged in superintending
the translation of portions of the English
version of the Scriptures into the various
dialects spoken in England and Scotland,
and travels about Britain collecting informa-
tion about these dialects.
	THE OREGON State Woman Suffrage As-
sociation held its second special session at
Salem, Oregon, opening on Tuesday, Sep-
tember 26, closing September 29. There
were morning, afternoon and evening ses-
sions. President Mrs. H. A. Loughary.
Judging from the minutes of the conven-
tion we have read, they transacted their
business in a very parliamentary like man-
ner, and the speeches of the woman work-
ers in the cause of temperance and the bal-
lot for women were earnest, spirited and
eloquent. The speech of the President Mrs.
Loughary we would gladly publish in full
if we had space; it contains some excellent
items, full of pith and calculated to arouse
dormant energies.
	THE EIGHTH annual meeting of the Amer-
ican Woman Suffrage Association com-
menced on Monday evening Oct. 2, at Han-
del and Hadyn Hall, Philadelphia. The
Hall was filled; Mrs. Mary A. Livermore
presided and made the opening address.
She dwelt earnestly upon the Temperance
question, the benefit it would be to that
cause to have the vote of women; the many
reasons that there were, why women should
have the ballot, and the many flimsy objec-
tions that men made to prevent it. Twen-
ty-five States were represented by delegates
some of them from long distances. There
was entire harmony of feeling according
to the accounts of the proceedings, and a
spirit of encouragement in regard to Wo-
man'a future representation in the govern-
ment. The meeting was a success pecuni-
arily, and every way. Committees of dif-
ferent kinds were appointed to act in vari-
ous capacities such as are identified with
the interests of the representations of such
conventions.


To be blest with happiness we must be
beautified with holiness.

R. S. REPORTS.

TOOELE CITY, Sep. 25th, 1876.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	On Thursday, the 14th of this month, we
had the pleasure of a visit from Miss E. R.
Snow, Mrs. M. I. Horne, and Mrs. E. How-
ard. Meetings were held two days, and
some very valuable instruction was given;
the sisters had gathered from the different
settlements in Tooele County to hear the
words of life, and they were not disap-
pointed; much that was said related to cul-
tivating ourselves; that we should cultivate
our spirits as well as our bodies, that we
may become thoroughly pure and noble in
disposition and actions, so that our whole
lives may be spent in doing good. Consid-
erable was said about home manufacture
and the culture of silk. A committee was ap-
pointed to attend to the silk industry, and
the Saints were forcibly shown the neces-
sity of moving forward in all things both
temporal and spiritual.
	That heaven's choicest blessings may
rest continually upon those who labor so
faithfully for Zion, is the prayer of your
Sister,
	 EMILY WARBURTON.
		Secretary.

SANTAQUIN, Sep. 25th, 1876.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	Our 8th Annual Meeting took place Sept.
14th, 1876. Our School House was decora-
ted for the occasion, with the HOME INDUS-
TRIES of the sisters, consisting of bed
quilts, rugs, embroidery, and crotchet work
of every description, fancy quilting, mil-
lenery, and even wooden-bottomed shoes;
but the most conspicuous of all was the
coverlets, shawls and flannel made by the
sisters in Santaquin.
	On the stand was our President, Mrs.
Elizabeth Stickney, Pres't. Jane Simonds
of Payson, with her first Counselor and
Secretary, also Presidents Mary Johnson of
Goshen, and Elizabeth Broadbent of Spring
Lake District, and Bishop George Halla-
day. Many other visitors from Payson and
Goshen were present.
	After the usual exercises of reading the
minutes, etc., the Annual report was read
by the Secretary, showing we had received
during the year.........................	$184,87
	Disbursed do....................	  125 84
	The Young Ladies' Improvement Asso-
ciation, held their 3rd Annual meeting at
the same time; their report, and also the re-
port from Spring Lake District was very
favorable.
	The meeting was addressed by Presidents
Stickney, Simonds, and Johnson; Mrs.
Douglas, Bishop Halladay, and Bro. Coombs
of Payson. All the remarks were encour-
aging, and expressive of their pleasure in
hearing our reports, and in beholding the
products of the talents of our sisters.
	Our President is a good financier, as well
as a great economist. Nothing is wasted
that can be turned to account for the benefit
of the poor. She is herself, humble, kind,
and loving, respected by all who know her,
and we wish her long to be our president.
	Wishing great success to your valuable
paper, we beg to remain yours in the new and
everlasting covenant,
	ELIZABETH J. STICKNEY, Prest.
		ELIZABETH CARTER, Sec.

EDITORS' EXPONENT:
	The Annual Meeting of the Young La-
dies Retrenchment Association of the

[COLUMN 2]

Twentieth Ward was held in the Relief
Society Hall, Sep. 13th, 1876. The leading
officers of the Senior and Junior Co-opera-
tive Association being present, also Elder
C. W. Staynes. President Mrs. Sarah How-
ard made a few opening remarks appropri-
ate to the occasion. Miss E. R. Snow then
addressed the meeting explaining the ob-
ject of these organizations etc.; said she
was present at the meeting when God,
through His Prophet commanded the intro-
duction of these organizations; said she
well remembered the occasion of the or-
ganization of this Association, and thought
we had improved our time well. She gave
good counsel and exhorted the mothers to
encourage their daughters to attend these
meetings, as great good would result there-
from. Mrs. M. I. Horne expressed her
pleasure of again having the privilege of
meeting with the sisters of the Twentieth
Ward, remembered Sister Snow making
the remark at their first meeting, that al-
though it was the last organized it would
not be the least, said she could see that
assertion had proved correct. Mrs. B.
Smith, Mrs. Persendia Kimball, Mrs Sarah
Kimball and Mrs. E. S. Barney gave ex-
cellent instructions, exhorting the young
ladies to be energetic in the pursuit of use-
ful knowledge. Elder C. W. Stayner then
spoke, explaining the responsibility that
will rest upon the young in filling import-
ant positions in preparing themselves to
guide the good ship Zion into its haven of
rest. He spoke lengthy and glowingly on
the upbuilding and future glory of Zion.
All present were greatly edified and a good
spirit prevailed.
	Report for the year ending Sept. 13th,
1876, of the Young Ladies Retrenchment
Association of the Twentieth Ward; this
association was organized Sep. 3rd., 1875,
by Miss Eliza R. Snow, with the following
ladies as officers: Mrs. Sarah Howard, Pres.,
Mrs. E. Stayner and Mrs. Isabella Glade,
Connselors, Mrs. Jane Fowler Superintend-
ent, Miss Rebecca Manning Sec. and forty-
one members. At a following meeting, Mrs.
Henrietta Simons, Mrs. Alice Simons, Miss
M. A. Sanson and Miss Janette Sharp were
elected as counselors, the two last named
ladies were also elected assistant secretar-
ies; but through unavoidable circumstances,
Miss M. A. Sanson has since resigned her
office as secretary, and Mrs. E. J. Mathews
has been appointed to fill the vacancy. We
have held regular meetings weekly and
with but few exceptions, have generally
had a good attendance. The meetings have
been very interesting and instructive, the
exercises consisting of essays, select read-
ing from church works and other good
books; and to add in bearing testimony to
the fulfillment of Sister Snows remarks,
made at our organization, there has been a
marked improvement. The society num-
bers at the present time, upwards of
150 members. A fund has been estab-
lished for the purpose of emigration, help-
ing the poor, or assisting in any good en-
terprise. Donations received for the fund,
and by a party given by the association,
amounting to $54,20, has been disbursed
for various benevolent purposes.
	President Mrs. Sarah Howard proposed
that a five cent temple fund be established
which met with a hearty approval, amount
received for said fund $5,05. It was also
proposed that we make a quilt for the ben-
efit of the fund. Received for purchasing
material for quilt $3,75.
	Total amount received........	$63 00
	Disbursements....................	  57 95

[COLUMN 3]

	In Treasury, the quilt and cash
amounting to...........................	5 05
		REBECCA WILLIAMS Sec.
	ELIZABETH MATHEWS, Ass't.

MORGAN CITY, Sept. 25, 1876.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	Allow me through your columns, to add
my mite to the already large stock of news
and interesting reports you deal out to us
semi-monthly.
	Yours is a noble mission indeed, to carry
the expressions of love and gratitude from
thousands of hearts to each other, all over
Utah.
	Our little city was honored, on the 19th
inst., by a visit from two "bright stars"
from the Relief Society Constellation: Sis-
ters E. S. Barney and E. Howard, who
spoke words of instruction and comfort.
The Lord poured His Spirit on them in
such abundance that they fed our hungry
souls with the food they most needed. Their
texts were: Education, watch-care of our
girls, plural marriage, care of the sick,
word of wisdom, womanly dignity, the im-
portance of availing ourselves of the right
of the ballot, folly and extravagance of the
fashions of the day. Showing to us that
the mission of the Relief Society is not only
to relieve the poor, but to introduce and
cultivate everything that tends to the ex-
altation of woman and the saving of souls.
Seri-culture, straw-braiding, and all kinds of
home industries were highly recommended.
	We feel to bless them for their kind and
cheering words; and I say God bless our
dear Sister E. R. Snow, who presides over
these institutions.
	Your Sister, etc,
		ESTHER E. E. FRANCIS,
			Sec.


WOMAN'S RECORD.

	MRS. D. W. Rogers is superintendent of
schools in Danby, Conn., and is the first
woman that ever held public office in that
town.
	MISS Helen Potter, the reader, is doing an
excellent business professionally, her in-
come for the season it is said will be about
$15,000.
	MRS. A. J. Duniway, of Portland, Oregon,
and editor of the "New North West," de-
livered an earnest and characteristic speech
at the Woman Suffrage Convention at Phil-
adelphia on taxation without representation
and other subjects.
	MISS Abbie G. Ketchum of Marshall,
Michigan, sailed from San Francisco Oct. 1,
for China, to commence missionary work in
that country. She had the congratulations
and earnest blessings of t[-]ousands to follow
her as encouragement in her labors of de-
votion and self-sacrifice.
	MISS Lydia Becker, the London advocate
of Woman suffrage, in a recent speech at
the excursion of the British Association,
gave a happy description of the visible
change and marked improvement in regard
to the position of women at society gather-
ings. On the last occasion when the British
Association had visited Glasgow, the gen-
tlemen had been invited to luncheon and
the ladies left outside, the gentlemen had
pretended to have more pleasure in toasting
the ladies in their absence, but it was real-
ly more satisfactory to respond to these
toasts in person, and contributed more to
the enjoyment of the c[-]mpany.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.		75


THE WIFE TO HER HUSBAND.

		It seems to me that should I die,
And this poor body cold and lifeless lie.
And tho[-] shouldst touch my lips with thy warm breath,
The life-blood quicken'd in each sep'rate vein,
Would wildly, madly rushing back again;
Bring the glad spirit from the isle of death,
		It seems to me that were I dead,
And thou in sympathy shouldst o'er me shed
Some tears of sorrow, or of sad regret,
That every pearly drop that fell in grief,
Would bud, or blossom, bursting into leaf,
To prove immortal love could not forget.
		I do believe that round my grave,
When the cool, fragrant, evening zephyrs wave,
Shouldst thou in friendship linger near the spot,
And breathe some tender words in memory,
That this poor heart in grateful constancy,
Would softly whisper back some loving thought.
		I do believe that should I pass
Into the unknown land of happiness,
And thou shouldst wish to see my face once more,
That in my earnest longing after thee,
I would come forth in joyful ecstacy,
And once again gaze on thee as before.
		I do believe my faith in thee,
Stronger than life, an anchor firm to be,
Planted in thy integrity and worth,
A perfect trust, implicit and secure;
That will all trials and all grief endure,
And bless and comfort me while here on earth.
		I do believe who love hath known,
Or sublime friendship's purest, highest tone,
Hath tasted of the cup of ripest bliss,
And drank the choicest wine life hath to give,
Hath known the truest joy it is to live.
What blessing rich or great compared to thi[-]?
		I do believe true love to be,
An element that in its tendency,
Is elevating to the human mind,
An intuition which we recognize
As foretaste of immortal Paradise,
Through which the soul will be refined.
											EMILE.
	SALT LAKE CITY, Nov. 17, 1876


For the EXPONENT.
		"THE BACK BONE OF THE
			KINGDOM OF GOD."

	At a recent Fast Meeting, I was much
pleased with the testimony of a humble
brother. He said he noticed, wherever she
went, that the sisters seemed to be the
main strength of the Church. In the old
country, when he would be preaching,
perhaps out in the open air, it was the sis-
ters who gathered around him and strength-
ened him with their faith and prayers.
And here, in the Ward meetings or tho
Tabernacle, everywhere, it was the sisters
much more than the brethren, who would
turn out to perform these religious duties.
"The sisters" he said, "form, substantially
the BACK BONE of the Kingdom of God!"
	Similar remarks are frequently made by
other Elders, which helps to prove that in a
general way at least, the women of the Lat-
ter-day Saints are appreciated.
	I do like the idea of the sisters forming
the "back bone" of the Kingdom of God.
It seems to show me exactly where we (sis-
ters) stand, what our calling is and how
great our responsibilities are, more than any
small sentence I ever heard. I have al-
ways realized, since I became old enough
to reflect, that the woman could not right-
fully claim the leadership, or profess to be
the head. But I have also felt that, some-
how, her position was quite as essential as
that of her—generally acknowledged head
—the man. And now I believe I see this
little matter as it is.
	Of what use would a head be without a
backbone to assist in keeping it in its prop-
er position?  It would be worth just about
the same as a kingdom of men without wo-
men.


I am thankful that I am numbered with
a people whose men accord to woman's
worth so fair an estimate. I would rather
claim such a man as husband, in connection
with many other wives, than to hold any
position women may possibly gain outside
the Church and Kingdom of God, where
none even profess to be guided and direct-
ed by the power and authority of the Holy
Priesthood.						INEZ.


[Column 1]
			CORRESPONDENCE.

		HEBER CITY; WASATCH CO.,
						October 3d, 1876.
EDITORS EXPONENT:
	We have a Relief Society here that meets
once a month to bring in our offerings for
the poor and bear testimony of the things
pertaining to the kingdom of God. Our
President, Margaret Muir has been talking
to the sisters here about learning to braid
straw and making their own hats, and also
to have shares in the co-operative store and
to trade among ourselves.
	On Wednesday and Thursday, September
27 and 28, we held a special meeting, Mrs.
E. S. Barney and Mrs. E. Howard were
present from Salt Lake City visiting in the
interest of the Societies and Deseret Silk
Association. Mrs. E. S. Barney in her address
to the sisters said they had visited already
ninteene meetings; spoke of the great work
which had been done in building up towns
and settlements and from what small be-
ginnings of Joseph Smith's mission to es-
tablish the Latter-day work; and the many
temples that must be built in the north and
south and all over the land of America, and
referred to the prophecy that the people
should "fly as doves to their windows, and
come on swift beasts;" she spoke consider-
ably upon the principles of Celestial Mar-
riage, and the follies and fashions of the
world and how they were creeping in among
this people.
	She dwelt most pointedly upon the de-
velopment of home industries, and the spe-
cialty of sericulture, its different depart-
ments from the planting out of the mul-
berry trees to its manufacture into the most
beautiful lace and ribbons, she touched up-
on the necessity of our living our religion
so we need not be scourged with grasshop-
pers and plagues of that nature; the train-
ing and educating our children in the
things pertaining to the kingdom of God,
the great benefits resulting from the organ-
izartions that had been established among
the women, and the privileges they enjoyed
in voting and closed with remarking that
she had been left an orphan and was self-
educated in nearly all respects.
	At the meeting on the following day, af-
ter the opening exercises Mrs. E. Howard
addressed the congregation first upon punc-
tuality; said it was the first meeting where
the people had come together at the appoint-
ed time. She exhorted the sisters to be
faithful in their societies and have a good
testimony of them enrolled upon the books;
she referred to the neglect of duties and
the ultimate result of such a course, that we
should be kind to our children and reason
with them and seek to preserve them from
temptation. She spoke of her astonish-
ment to see such a beautiful little city here
and thought it would be a good place in
which to carry out the United Order. She
dwelt considerably upon the useless waste
of time among the young, and their frivo-
lous ideas of love and marriage; she spoke

[Column 2]
of intemperence as being one of the great-
est curses to this nation or to any people
who would allow it to make inroads in a
community. She dwelt upon the Word of
Wisdom and the blessings attached to its
observance; the great detriment outside
trade was to this Territory, taking away the
means that would help to sustain and build
up our own institutions and manufacturies.
She said in Brigham City they had forty
home industries. She urged upon the sis-
ters the necessity of using economy in re-
gard to the smallest matters, also to teach
it to their little children, and that even in
the gathering up and saving of rags it was 
beneficial, that in saving rags they might
take the "Juvenile" and "Exponent" for
the benefit of their families.
	The instructions and counsel of these sis-
ters was very impressive, and will no doubt
be an incentive to action in the societies
they visit.
	We donated in Heber City for the Deser-
et Silk Association $16.20.
				ELLEN LEE, Secretary.


HOPE AND CUSTOM.—In our estimate of
the ills of life, we never sufficiently take
into our consideration the wonderful elas-
ticity of our moral frame, the unlooked-for,
the startling facility with which the human
mind accommodates itself to all change of
circumstance, making an object, and even a
joy from the hardest and seemingly the
least redeemed conditions of fate. The
man who watched the spider in his cell,
may have taken at least as much interest
in the watch as when engaged in the most
ardent and ambitious objects of his former
life; and he was but a type of [-]is brethren;
all in similar circumstances would have
fonnd some similar occupation. Let any
man look over his past life, let him recall,
not moments, not hours of agony, for to
them Custom lends not her blessed magic;
but let him single out some lengthened per-
iod of physical or moral endurance; in has-
tily reverting to it, it may seem at first, I
grant, altogether wretched; a series of days
marked with the black stone—the clouds
without a star; but let him look more close-
ly, it was not so during the time of suffer-
ing; a thousand little things in the bustle of
life, dormant and unheeded, then started
forth into notice, and became to him objects
of interest or diversion: the dreary present,
once familiar, glided away from him, not less
than if it had been all happiness; his mind
dwelt not on the dull intervals, but the step-
ping-stone it had created and placed at
each; and, by the moral dreaming which
for ever goes on within man's secret heart,
he lived a little within the immediate
world before him, as in the most sanguine
period of his youth, or the most scheming
of his maturity. So wonderful in equaliz-
ing all states and all times in the varying
tide of life, are these two rulers yet level-
ers of mankind, hope and custom, that the
very idea of an eternal punishment includes
that of an utter alteration of the whole
mechanism of the soul in its human state,
and no effort of an imagination, assisted by
past expecience. can conceive a state of
torture which custom can never blunt, and
from which the chainless and immaterial
spirit can never be beguiled into even a mo-
mentary escape.—Bulwe[-].

production of paper of all kinds in the
world amounts to 1,800,000,000 lbs. Half
of this is used for printing purposes.
