SALT LAKE CITY, OCT. 15, 1876.


[Column 1]
		SISTERS BE IN EARNEST.

	At the suggestion of President Brigham
Young we would call the attention of the
women of this Territory to the subject of sav-
ing grain. It is one important item which
President Young has labored diligently to im-
press upon this people ever since their residence
in these valleys. His advice has ever been to
the brethren to cultivate the soil and let the
mines, and mining speculations alone, for the
grain was of more consequence than gold or
silver. And these people are witnesses to that
fact; practically having experienced the scar-
city of provisions and especially of breadstuffs,
in the earlier settlement of this country.
	The Lord has blessed this people abundant-
ly in the few years past in their fields and in
their stores of grain and provisions; and as in al-
most every other case when there is a profusion
of blessings, the people who receive them be-
come indifferent and do not appreciate them as
they ought. The people have been careless in
regard to taking care of their grain and many
other things. In many instances they neglect
to pay their tithing, and dispose of their pro-
ducts in such a manner as, were it to continue,
the Lord would be sorely displeased with them.
This year grain is unusually low, yet the grass-
hoppers have been visiting different parts of the
Territory, and, it is feared by many, have laid
their eggs; therefore it is more than ever essen-
tial that such measures be taken as will secure
the grain now in market. The Relief Societies
and Co-operative organizations in which some
of the women of this Territory are interested,
have funds at their disposal from time to time,
which can be made available to carry out Presi-
dent Young's wishes in regard to b[-]ying and
taking care of the grain.
	It is no doubt the best investment of means
that could possibly be made, for if the prophe-
cies of the Scriptures, both ancient and modern,
and of Joseph Smith are to be fulfilled, which the
Latter-day Saints profess to believe, there will
be a desolating famine in the land; and if the
women of this Territory will be wise, and
hearken to the counsel given to them now,
there is no doubt that the result will be an emi-
nent success. If the means now on hand in the
societies and women's associations can be turn-
ed into grain, and such measures taken to store
it and preserve it from damp and all other
things that would damage, or destroy it, as can
be made practicable according to circumstances
and advantages, then one point will be gained
towards the temporal salvation of this people in
case of emergency.
	In whatever women undertake they are gen-
erally earnest, and our advice would be im-
mediately without any delay (except such as
is unavoidable) to commence to carry out Presi-
dent Young's counsel in this matter. Where
there is a will there is (almost invariably) a
way, and in this matter there should be a per-

[Column 2]
sonal and individual as well as a general in[-]er-
est. In order to satisfactorily accomplish any
scheme, one great object is to be in earnest; on
this earnestness depends much of the success of
whatever enterprise is undertaken. If you
determine to do any thing of whatever name or
nature, first be sure it is the proper thing to do,
and then without losing time, bring your ener-
gies into immediate requisition and you are
almost sure to succeed.
	The women of this Territory are capable of
carrying out President Young's counsel in this
matter; and if they do not they will be left with-
out excuse; he has called upon the brethren
until he has become wearied, and now he ap-
peals to the sisters. Is not this a proof of his
confidence in their desire to listen to counsel?
The sisters will have ample opportunity now to
prove their ability in financiering; and it is
greatly to be hoped that they will manifest
such earnestness in this matter as cannot fail to
accomplish the desired object.
	President Young further advises the sisters,
that they enter into such arrangements as are
applicable to their circumstances in their va-
rious districts and settlements, to build store-
houses for the storing of their grain in the sec-
tions of country as they shall divide off, or ap-
portion to certain localities; also that they so-
licit donations from their husbands and the
brethren to assist them in carrying out this en:
terprise, both for the building of the store-
houses and also in obtaining grain to fill them
and preserve it for future need.
	We sincerely hope that what has been said
will be sufficient to arouse a spirit of determin-
ation in the minds of the women of Utah, to
devise and to put in practice, such measures as
shall be economical and wise, and for the gen-
eral good of all concerned.
			[handwritten] E. B. Wells


THE FORTY-SEVENTH SEMI-
			ANNUAL CONFERENCE.

	The interest manifested by the people call-
ing themselves Latter-day Saints, at the late
general Conference was great. Each day large
congregations assembled, to listen to the words
of instruction, relative to their temporal and
eternal salvation; and the servants of the Lord
were truly blest in their endeavors to minister
to the wants of the people. The first discourse
of the Conference was delivered by President
Daniel H. Wells, the last by President Brig-
ham Young; the intermediate spaces being
principally occupied by different members of
the quorum of the Twelve Apostles. President
Young was present at and presided over all the
meetings. As a text upon which to reflect and
preach, he gave to the people the question,
when shall we begin to live according to the
pattern chosen when we embraced the Gospel
of Christ? or in other words, when will the
people become one and learn to be saints?
	This is a wonderfully significant text. Surely
the answer arose in the hearts of the many
thousands "Now!" And although this senti-
ment in the souls of individuals formed no ut-
terance, certainly the majority of the people
will set to with renewed diligence and deter-
minations to assist each other in the great
work of uniting their interests as one. The
Conference just past over was one never to be
lost sight of by those who attended it. It was
adjourned till the 6th of April, 1877, to meet
in the Temple at St. George.


[Column 1]
			HOME AFFAIRS.

	ON FRIDAY and Saturday evenings, Oct. 6
and 7th, select entertainments were given in
the 10th Ward School-house, consisting of
songs, recitations, dialogues, instrumental per-

[Column 2]
formances etc. The most striking and pleas
ing feature of the entertainment was the reci-
tation of "Beautiful Snow," by Miss Mattie
Paul. In character of expression, in voice a[-]d
gesture, her rendering of this pathetic poem
was equal to that of a cultivated artiste.
	OGDEN RELIEF SOCIETY.—On Saturday eve-
ning, Sept. 20, an entertainment was given for
the benefit of this society in the Ogden Thea-
tre. Misses Carrie Cogswell, Susie Marden and
Edith Clawson, and Messrs. Forster and Clarke
of this city, Mr. Alfred Thorne, and members
of the Ogden Dramatic Company, produced
the play of "Camille," which was followed by
an "Olio," and concluded with the "Tower
Scene" in the play of "King John," in which
Miss Clawson gave her clever rendition of
"Prince Arthur." There was a "packed"
house, and the general opinion given was that
it was an excellent dramatic entertainment.
	President F. D. Richards thanked the per-
formers, musi[-]ians, etc., all of whom had gen-
erously given their service gratuitously, and
the audience, on behalf of the Relief Society.
	Credit is due Sisters Richards and S. Herrick,
the instigators and managers of the entertain-
ment, from the proceeds of which the Relief
Society will be benefitted.—Ex.
	DESERET SILK ASSOCIATION.—The Deseret
Silk Associotion held a special meeting at Fire-
men's Hall, on Saturday afternoon, Oct. 7th,
Mrs. Zina D. Young presiding.
	Mrs. Young reported that she expected a reel
would be here in a few days. She had received
a large quantity of cocoons. This organiza-
tion was not for any personal benefit, but for
the benefit of the community of Utah. We
ought to be looking after our trees and cut-
tings, as it was very near time to plant them
out.
	Mr. Robbins said it was best to plant them
in the fall. He had had fifty years' experience
and had always found it best.
	Mrs. Barney reported that, while in the
north, she had collected $105. She could not
raise the worms, but felt a desire to see the
movement a success.
	There being quite a number present from the
various settlements, the remainder of the time
was taken up in asking questions and answer-
the same.
	The following donations were received:
Cynthia Clyde, $1; Mary S. Clark, $1; Martha
Thomas. 50c.
	Meeting adjourned till the first Wednesday
in November, at the same place.
					R. SIMPSON, Secretary.

	THE PEOPLE'S Territorial Convention for the
nomination of a Delegate to Congress, was
held in the Co[-]rt House in Salt Lake City,
Saturday, Oct. 7th, at half-past twelve, p. m.
David O. Calder, acting chairman, called the
convention to order; Judge Elias Smith, on
motion of Mr. Calder was chosen temporary
chairman. John T. Caine, on motion of Col. J.
R. Winder was elected temporary Secretary.
The business was all arranged and carried for-
ward in a quiet and methodical manner, and
no confusion whatever occurred. The com-
mittee, which had been appointed in the usual
form, reported the following officers f[--] a per-
manent organization. President D. H. Wells;
Vice Presidents, according to their districts,
A. O. Smoot and A. Hatch; E. Snow and J. N.
Smith; J. F. Smith and Brigham Young. Jr,
Secretaries, John T. Caine, Moses Thatcher,
and R. S. Horne; Chaplain, F. D. Richards;
Sergeant at Arms, Joel Grover. This report
was unanimously carried. Hon. W. H. Hoop-
er Chairman of the committee on resolutions,


reported the following subjects in form. State-
hood, Education, Irrigation, Silver Currency,
Stumpage, General. The convention sustained
the present people's territorial central com-
mittee. Hon. George Q. Cannon was formally
nominated and several speeches made on the
subject. The nomination was received with
universal applause.
	SUNDAY SCHOOL MOVEMENT.—A general
meeting of the Sunday school superintendents
and teachers of the Territory was held at the
Fourteenth Ward Assembly Rooms, Friday
evening Oct. 6th. Of the Twelve Apostles
there were present Elders John Taylor, Wil-
ford Woodruff, F. D. Richards, and George Q.
Cannon. There were representatives from
nearly all the counties and the time was occu-
pied mostly by hearing from the superintend-
ents; the reports of the progress of the move-
ment being very encouraging, and indicated
that the Bishops and other leading men were
taking a more lively interest in it than hereto-
fore.
	THE SUNDAY School is one of the greatest
educators of the young that has ever been
instituted; and upon the youth of any na-
tion, or people depends the future strength
and power for good or evil. It is the influ-
ence of Sunday Schools in a very great de-
gree that gives sublimity of tone to the
aspirations and ambitions of the young.
The subject is one which involves the highest
and holiest interests of a community. Here
they are, or should be taught to reverence
every thing that is sacred and pure, to imitate
the blessed Savior in humility and meek-
ness which were the striking characteristics of
His life and death.
	Let every mother be awakened to renewed
interest in this direction, and inspire, if possi-
ble, all within her circle with the same zeal
and earnestness. To the children of the poor,
and to the orphan the Sunday School is a bless-
ing which it is impossible to estimate. God
bless the Sunday School; it is one of the most
potent workers of the redemption of Israel.
	DR. ELLEN B. Ferguson to whom reference
has been made in this paper, has returned from
St. George and taken up her residence in Pro-
vo, where she will continue the practice of
medicine. Mrs. Ferguson is a regularly grad-
uated physician, has studied her profession
in England and America, and spent four years
of her life in the hospitals of London and
Paris; has had twelve years experience in an
extensive practice in the United States. We
would recommend her to the sisters in
Provo and vicinity who need the services of a
skillful and experienced physician. She is al-
so a fine musician and vocalist and will give
lessons in vocal and instrumental music at her
residence. Office and residence in A. F. Mac-
donald's house, Provo, Utah Co. Mrs. Dr.
Ferguson has traveled and lectured extensively
in different countries; she is a lady of most
pleasing and attractive manners, her voice is
clear and full of melody, and in notices we
have seen from the Eastern press, she has been
spoken of as a woman possessing "rare intel-
lectual ability and literary culture; a thorough
knowledge of all that belongs to the human
system, and the pathology of disease;" Mrs.
Dr. Ferguson is also spoken of as "one having
in a large degree those qualities of hert and
mind that must always be the crowning glory
of true and enlightened womanhood."
	Mrs. Ferguson's course of lectures on Gyne-
cology, delivered in the East, treat of the Con-
stitution, Idiosyncrasies and Diseases of Wo-
man, together with the Laws of the Social and
Physical relations of the Sexes.
	A Woman's Convention.—On Wednesday,
Oct. 4th, at 2 o'clock p. m., the officers and
members of the Relief Society from all the
different wards of the city and vicinity con-
vened in the Council Hose by previous ap-
pointment. President Miss E. R. Snow arose
and explained to the sisters that President B.
Young would not be present, as had been an-
ticipated, but would send his private Secretary
with a communication. She thenn nominated
Mrs. M. I. Horne to preside over the meeting,
and Mrs. E. B. Wells to act as Secretary,
which motions were unanimously carried.
	After the opening exercises, the assemblage
being too large to be accommodated in the
Council House, the meeting adjourned to the
Old Tabernacle. Elder George Reynolds, hav-
ing arrived with President Young's communi-
cation, arose and addressed the sisters on the
object of the meeting and read the following
letter from President Young:
			"Salt Lake City, Oct. 4th, 1876.
		"To the President, and Members of the Re-
lief Societies, in meeting assembled at the
Council House this afternoon.
	"Dear Sisters.—It would be very gratifying
to us if you could form an association to start
business in the capacity of disposing of home-
made articles, such as are manufactured among
ourselves, for the benefit of the Latter-day
Saints; also to make a selection from among
the sisters, members of this association, to take
charge and carry on this business on commis-
sion or by purchase, and to transact all the
business pertaining to this institution. If you
cannot be satisfied with the selection of sisters
from among yourselves to take charge, we will
render you assistance by furnishing a compe-
tent man for the transaction of the financial
matters of this establishment.
		"Your brother in the Gospel,
					"Brigham Young."
	After reading this letter, Brother Reynolds
made some very suggestive and appropriate re-
marks on home industries and the great desire
President Young felt to have the people push
forward in the development of home manufac-
ture.
	An organization was formed by electing the
following persons as officers of the association.
Miss E. R. Snow, President; Mrs. Priscilla
Stains, Vice President; Mrs. Elizabeth Davis,
Secretary; Mrs. Bathsheba Smith, Treasurer;
Mrs. Sarah M. Kimball, Mrs. Lydia Ann
Wells, Mrs. Phebe Woodruff, Mrs. Presendia
Kimball, Directors.
	Many excellent remarks were made by the
officers and such suggestions by Miss E. R.
Snow and others, as were adapted to the occa-
sion and organization of the association. After
all the business had been transacted which
seemed requisite at the time, the officers of the
board were requested to meet at the Old Con-
stitution building at 6 o'clock the same eve-
ning, and after singing and prayer the Conven-
tion adjourned sine die.
	The Relief Society Woman's Mercan-
			tile Association.
Has opened a store for the sale of home man-
ufactures in the building lately occupied by
the Woman's Centennial Territorial Fair,
known as the Old Constitution Building, op-
posite Z. C. M. I. store.
	We are now ready to receive home made use-
ful and ornamental articles of all kinds, which
we will sell on commission.
	Many of our citizens have felt the want of a
place devoted exclusively to Utah productions,
and now that we are, in accordance with the
wishes of President Young, uniting our hum-
ble efforts in this direction, we confidently
trust that we shall not only have the support
of all branches of the Relief Society through-
out the valleys of the mountains, but also of
our brethren and all others who feel an inter-
est in the future welfare and prosperity of
Utah.
	Commencing as we do without capital we
wish it distinctly understood that at present
we can only sell on commission.
				Eliza R. Snow, President.
				Priscilla M. Stains,
						Vice President.
				Elizabeth Davis,
						Secretary.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


NOTICE.

	The Relief Society Woman's Co-operative
Association or Woman's Commission Store is
now open at the Old Constitution Buildings
opposite Z. C. M. I. The Superintendent and
those interested with the management of the
organization are prepared to do business on
commission in all departments of dry goods
home productions. They have for sale many
useful and ornamental articles, and their
supply is rapidly increasing. They have near-
ly all the varieties of church books, including
"Book of Mormon" and "Doctrine and Coven-
ants" new edition, and Mr. E. W. Tullidge's
new book "The Life of Brigham Young, or
Utah and Her Founders." This enterprise is a
most laudable one and the Association labor
under many inconveniences in starting out in
this new direction. We sincerely hope the
Latter-day Saints will interest themselves
practically in this praiseworthy undertaking,
and by their patronage thus promote the es-
tablishment of more successful and extended
home-industries, and thus advance the inter-
ests of economy in devising and practising
such measures as shall result in a thorough
system of self-support throughout this com-
munity.


OBITUARY.

	DIED IN Cedar City, Iron Co., July 28th, '76,
Mrs. Rachel Whittaker, aged 68 years. She
received and obeyed the Gospel in Bolton,
Lancashire, England, in 1850, and came to
Utah in 1851, with her husband and four child-
ren, arriving in Salt Lake City, Sept. 27th, of
the same year. She was one of the first set-
tlers in Cedar City and remained there till her
death. She was President of the Relief So-
ciety of that place since its first organization
until within a year of her death, when she re-
signed on account of failing health. She had
the confidence and love of all the sisters, was
ever found at the post of duty at the bedside of
the sick, or ministering to the poor; wherever
sorrow or trouble was she was always ready to
console and administer relief, and was indeed
a mother in Israel. She was one of the most
patient women in suffering, never complain-
ing or repining, and at the last she fell asleep
without a struggle, and with abundant hope
of a glorious resurrection. She will be greatly
missed as a counselor and benefactor in the
vicinity of Cedar City where she was so much
revered and beloved.

chine.

above who do not enter by the narrow gate
below.


78		WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


ADDRESS.

	READ BEFORE THE RELIEF SOCIETY
		OF SANTAQUIN, COMPOSED BY
			THE SECRETARY.

	Dear brethren and sisters, I do not know
what may be your feelings on this occasion,
but mine are full of intense gratitude to
the author of every good and perfect gift.
	While contemplating the display of the
sisters' home-made work, so gracefully dec-
corating these walls, I cannot help feeling
grateful to God, that he has blest our sisters
with such talents, and so inspired the hearts
of worthy men and women, to introduce
into our church such a heavenly society as
the Relief Society is. There is nothing
but the Relief Society that can so effectu-
ally carry out the one great principle ever
taught by our Saviour while upon the earth.
Except ye have charity, nothing will avail
you.
	If our society cannot make the display
that other settlements have done before us,
it is not for the want of talent or energy,
but for the want of time and means. We
as a society are composed of poor sisters,
who have to labor with our husbands and
sons, and whose means are but limited. We
have a number of poor families to care for,
and our disbursements many times exceed
our income. We have a President who is
ever alive to the wants of the poor and the
needy, and who well knows how to dis-
pose of the means intrusted to her care, for
their relief, likewise when sickness and
death are in our midst you will always find
Prest. Elizabeth Stickney among the first
to visit and administer to the afflicted ones.
We have wise counselors, faithful teachers,
and a good treasurer; we also have about
one hundred or more faithful sisters who
can stand up at our meetings and nobly
bear their testimonies and who love to give
their mites for the up-building of the King-
dom of God, and who relieve the wants of
the poor widows, orphans, aged and dis-
tressed, and who say they never miss what
they give to the Relief Society. We like-
wise have a kind father in our Bishop, who
knows how to advise us in any difficulty,
and who cares for our welfare in every par-
ticular.
	That our Society, although poor, is blest
with faithfulness, unity and love, is a fact
not to be denied; therefore it is for this that
I feel full of gratitude to God at this time,
who has blest us so richly ever since this
society was organized here, by our beloved
but departed brother, Pres. D. H. Holladay,
who said that the society should be blest,
and that it should never fall.
	May God bless us, one and all, and save
us in His celestial kingdom, is my prayer in
the name of Jesus Christ, amen.
								E. C.


[Column 1]
				EXTRACTS,

	FROM A RECENT BOOK BY DR. GARTH
		WILKINSON, ENTITLED, "HUMAN
			SCIENCE AND DIVINE
				REVELATION.

	Speaking of the new freedom of man-
kind he says:
	"This is seen very especially in the per-
ception looming everywhere of the rights
of women. Not to mention other branches
of the subject, political rights, and the
rights of education may be adduced in
point. No reason has yet been given, why
Woman should be denied her experiment;

[Column 2]
why any real sphere of life should be clos-
ed against her; and her public free will be
abated or wholly or chiefly managed by the
masculine free will. The despotism over
her cannot last; her incessant cause will
wear it out, will tire it to death. When
she has been enfranchised, and barrier af-
ter barrier has been removed, when no stat-
ute not obviously sexual contains either
"he" or "she" distinctively, and all the ways
of life are open to her, and property is no
longer masculine but human, what will she
do with her freedom? There is no possibil-
ity of forecasting that future. It is adduced
only to show the new strain upon responsi-
bility which is coming; and how much God
and prayer will be needed to enable her to
bear the strain and to receive the light
which is required. Clearly many rights
will drop when the power to exercise them
is given. Clearly a new education for femi-
nine wisdom will accrue from her earnest
desire and inquiry to know what fields she
may occupy. It is obvious that freedom
and experiment alone can qualify her for
knowing her qualifications; that she will
then learn from failures and successes, as men
have done, where she is, and become a
teacher to herself. In the meantime, even
now, with Parliament against her she is
inwardly in the tension of this new free-
dom, and is not sure whether she is to be
as a man, or whether there is a whole new
womanly side to affairs and the state.

	Let male law be lifted off as a burden and
a shame, and woman be left to her proper
self determination; to be as much like a
man, or as little like a man as she pleases;
to vote, propose marriage, administer prop-
erty and the like, and her quality will
right itself, because her experimental in-
struction about her place can then begin."
	"In her thoughts of education also, the
same strain is felt. Is she to be as a young
man and a collegian here, and to be made
into a man-woman by exactly that education
which it is supposed has produced the pres-
ent masculine race with its dominant pow-
ers? It is not perhaps any person's busi-
ness to answer that question, but rather it
must be referred to experiment, of which
her new mental freedom is the condition.
The world must await the trial, and succor
the stress which it involves.
	It is evident that woman has the same
mental powers as men, but differently sex-
ed and centered. She can enter in her
way into all arts and sciences; she can sit
on thrones, command and lead armies, ex-
ert eloquent speech, make excellent litera-
ture, practice medicine and perform sur-
gery, preach in pulpits, and receive divine
communications. In short, the scale of her
powers is co-equal with his in all things. She
can do in every department what she has
an adequate affection for doing. But what
perhaps she cannot profitably do in the long
run is, to lay hold of things on the purely
intellectual side and enter upon them thus.
Her heart predominates, as the head pre-
dominates in the man. Now this being the
case, to give her equal chances with man,
a complete rehabilitation of knowledge in
her interests also is necessary, that she may
learn a corresponding heart-truth in every
detail for the man's head truth. Man has
the advantage of all his own intellectual
experience; gained through his own effort
by his own sexual process of thought. Wo-
man has no present advantage of the kind
before her; and must be content to enter
upon the faculties of man and their acqui-
sitions, at second hand. This is a grave
consideration for the advocates of the higher
education of women; indeed for their gen-

[Column 3]
eral education of all; that the intellect of
the heart that beautiful intellect, should
have no knowledge of nature and things,
and no learning at first hand, but only what
supplants its freedom, and is compelled up-
on it by the intellect of the head. It ar-
gues the throwing away of a marriageable
quality, the abeyance of the woman's in-
tellect before the man's.
	Is she to rewrite history to reconstruct
science, to reproduce metaphysics, to rein-
vent the arts, from her own womanly intel-
lect so as to have a world of her own, and
introduce it into harmonious and conjugal
relationship, as of the heart-mind with the
head-mind with the existing intellectual
fabrics, and art fabrics which is so prepon-
derantly of masculine growth? That can
hardly be intended; because what has been
done hitherto belongs to the past, and no
copy is possible; nay, no new originals that
follow the old lines. Moreover it is to be
hoped that the man has done, and is doing
the coarse work of thought, invention and
practice; and that his partner, in the days
of time, is to be saved that labor. Never-
theless it may be thought that Woman's
mind has a sphere of its own in everything;
and that all things through her faculties,
now that they are being filled with the new
free-will which is creative, will reappear in
their new heart-forms.

	Now the thought that c[--]es out of a true
sympathy is full of the finest natural truths.
The new Woman will therefore be present
by heart truths all through the common-
wealth, and will be prime minister of the
new man in his permitted administration
of the Godwealth. The coin of regenera-
tion will pass through her fingers in its frag-
ments and to its ends.—Woman's Journal.


﻿MISCELLANEOUS.

	Flowers seem intended for the solace of
ordinary humanity. Children love them:
quiet, tender, contended, ordinary people
love them as they grow; luxurious and dis-
orderly people rejoice in them gathered.
They are the cottager's treasure, and in the
crowded town, mark as with a little broken
fragment of rainbow, the windows of the
workers in whose hearts rests the covenant
of peace. To the child and the girl, to the
peasant and manufacturing operative to
the woman of the world and the nun, the
lover and the monk, they are precious al-
ways.
	Until we can read the heart of another
without doubt and know all the circumstan-
ces of his or her life, all the many links in
the chain of accident that has surrounded
the erring one, we should not blame too
much or be too wrathful towards such a
one. What one has done may be wrong,
but we cannot tell why it was done, or how
the doing of it may have been repented. A
moment of strong feeling, an hour of wrath,
may turn the feet upon the downward road
so hard to retrace. We see one lying at
the foot of a precipice; but we do not know
how he or she fell over—what cruel hand
may have smote!—what lie betrayed—nor
how the wounded one may have clung
vainly to the brink, praying in vain for
some helping hand.


VOLTAIRE, having lampooned a noble-
man, was caned by him for his wit, when
on applying to the Duke of Orleans, then
Regent, and begging him to do him justice,
the duke replied with a smile, "Sir, it has
been done already."


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


A VINDICATION OF WOMEN.

	With all the boasted chivalrous feeling of
the United States it is astonishing to see
how many of them are attributing to wo-
men the condition of things that has led to
the crimes and the disgrace of the late Sec-
retary of War. There have been innumer-
able sermons printed, in newspapers con-
ducted by men, on such texts as woman's
extravagance, woman's ambition, woman's
love of admiration, woman's fast living,
and woman's want of principle. All these
assumed characteristics of the sex in Ameri-
ca are illustrated by anecdotes or by scandal-
ous insinuations furnished by the wretched
creatures in men's clothing, who make their
living by the retail of personal gossip, mis-
named "society-news," for papers that cater
for the vulgar tastes of the people that fre-
quent the lowest kinds of balls, clubs and
bar-rooms, and the still lower resorts where
virtue in either sex is unknown and impos-
sible. If the women of American soci-
ety were really composed of such beings as
put themselves at the mercy of these crea-
tures, and allow them, or pay them to ad-
vertise their style of beauty, their wit,
their accomplishments, their dinners, their
receptions, their dress, their jewels, their
visiting lists, their followers of each sex,
and their utterly heartless, heathenish
worldliness, then one might well despair of
the country.					But happily
where one woman, or half a dozen women,
can be found to commit a disgrace that is
felt by the whole nation, there are millions
of women that are the very vital essence of
the goodness of the American people.
	Without these millions of good women
there would be no good men, or very few.
				Each one of these mil-
lions of good women, be she matron or
maid, is exercising a good influence, accord-
ing to her physical and her mental strength,
that tells upon the general condition of so-
ciety quite as much as does the influence of
each one of the small number of famously
bad, or silly, or extravagant women, one of
whom has lately been so ruthlessly held up
as a typical American woman by ignorant
or cowardly men.				The
old history of the Garden of Eden is re-
peating itself frequently. But in all recent
repetitions of it, especially in political af-
fairs, the Adam is the worst sinner. In
ninety-nine cases out of a hundred he puts
himself in the way of temptation, and in
most of these he is saved from a fatal fall
by his Eve. If his is the controlling spirit,
and he leads her to join him in sins of his
own devising, he is the party to be dis-
graced and punished. His Eve ought to be
treated at least with a little of the mercy
given so abundantly by a Divine being, in a
later dispensation, to a woman who had al-
ready sinned but was really repentant.
There was the one grand case of chivalry
that was more than knightly—THAT was di-
vine.—Ex,


POSITIVE PEOPLE.

	Of all the trials appointed by way of dis-
cipline in this sublunary sphere, is not the
greatest that of being forced into the per-
petual companionship of a positive person?
One who is so eminently and distressingly re-
spectable that he never did a wrong act in
his life, yet who constantly clashes upon all
your little harmonies and ideal dreams of
life, who shadows your rose-colored atmos-
phere, dispels your fancies at a glance, and
transforms every chord of your life to a dis-
cord. To be able to discover a flaw in the


character of such an individual would be
to your mind more refreshing than summer
rain to the parched earth. But no; from
his immaculate and appaling respectability
there is no appeal. Defenseless and silent
must you listen to his oracular opinions on
all conceivable subjects. His ground is
staked out at right angles, around which
your feet, long accustomed to wander at
their own sweet will, must walk circum-
spectly.
	There is no subject, from the Heaven
above to the Earth beneath, on which his
mind is not established firmly as the ever-
lasting hills. Who are you to presume on
questioning his opinions?
	Oh, for the grace of adaptability? Like
charity, it covers a multitude of sins.
Though your friend be more unlettered
than a Patagonian, more unconformable in
temperament than the Furies of old, yet
the grace of adaptability to times and moods
shall transfigure him in your sight, and his
coming shall ever bring you pleasure, and
his leaving pain!
	It is a great virtue in machinery that it
is easily adjustable to different kinds of
work and different operators. It is no less
a virtue in life. The gauges of living must
meet various and widely different demands.
Unforseen exigencies are continually aris-
ing. Opinions, duly labelled and stowed
away in one's mind, are not available for
the common needs of humanity. The best
of all things is a susceptibility to progress-
ion. People who pride themselves upon
being always the same, yesterday, to-day
and forever, are not only the most tiresome,
but the most detrimental to the true inter-
ests of society. Dr. Holmes remarks some-
where: "Don't be consistent but be simply
true."
	If your opinion has changed, you are,
doubtless, wiser than you were before. It
is not probable that wisdom has found a
termination in a life of any one individual,
and it becomes us to feel some regard for
the opinions of every one. A sweet and
gracious adaptability—a mental pliability,
so to speak—which, if not analyzed, is al-
ways realized. It is the slight and unassim-
ilated culture that repels. The genuine ar-
ticle attracts, and, unconsciously, perhaps,
to itself, draws all around it upward. It is
the "life of beauty" which invests all
around it with the "beauty of its thought."
It is the "little learning," the little culture,
the little thought, that is "a dangerous
thing," not the deep, sure foundations
widening every day. Positive people do not
change, therefore they do not progress.
At twenty years of age they may be shin-
ing lights in the community, but see if at
fifty they are not hard, unyielding and un-
comfortable.—Lady's Journal.


HOUSEHOLD HINTS.

	Ginger Cake.—One cup sour cream, one of mo-
lases of any kind, one of sugar, one teaspoonful of
salt, one of soda and two of ginger, Mix stiff
and bake quickly.
	Packing Eggs for Winter use.—Strong lime
water is the best of anything, but in the absence
of lime many pack their eggs small end down in
common salt. Some grease the shells with melt-
ed lard and pack in bran. A very good way too,
is to fill a calendar full of eggs and pour over
them a teakettle of boiling water, then, when
cool, pack in salt. The hot water slightly cooks
the outside of the egg, making it air-tight.
	Citron Preserves.—Pare the citron, take out
the seeds, and cut in small pieces; weigh it and
put it in cold water and boil until tender; throw in


a few peach leaves to give it a green color; also a
small lump of alum to harden it. Make a syrup
of three-quarters of a pound of sugar for each
pound of citron, and for every seven pounds use
four lemons and two ounces of green ginger-root.
Slice the lemons and ginger-root thin, scald them
in a little clea[-] water, after which add the sugar.
Boil the syrup down until thick, then add the cit-
ron and let it boil five minutes. Take the whole
up in jars and seal up close, and keep in a dark,
dry, cool place.


HYGIENIC NOTES.

	Sure Cure for Inflamed Eyes.—Take half an
ounce of Golden Seal (you will find it at the drug
stores), pour one-half pint boiling water upon it
and let it cool. Bathe the eyes with a linen rag
dipped in this, each night on going to bed, and
you will soon effect a cure.
	How to Grow Fat.—Take soup every day,
also milk-coffee or chocolate well sweetened, 2
Let your food be chiefly farinaceous and vegeta-
ble; bread with butter or milk; milk-mush, pota-
toes, cabbage, turnips etc., prepared with butter,
sweet puddings. 3 Eat meat only once a day;
the fatter kinds are the most suitable. Take
neither acids nor alkalies, and avoid everything
that disagrees. Milk, butter and sugar are very
fattening, but everybody cannot take them with
impunity, and to grow fat it is essential that di-
gestion be almost perfect. Sleep all you want, and
take exercise in moderation.
	Action of Cod-Liver Oil in Disease.—Buch-
heim finds that cod-liver oil has an acid reaction,
and contains, in addition to the fatty glycerides,
free fatty acids—oleic, stearic, and pahnitic—the
quantities of which vary in different kinds of oil,
but amount in the clear variety to about five per
cent.; and no biliary matters are present in the
oil. Its value, according to Buchheim, depends on
the presence of the free fatty acids, since the ab-
sorbtion of fats is preceeded, to a certain extent
at least, by their disintegration into glycerine and 
fatty acids; and as the latter are introduced in 
cod-liver oil in their free condition, and thus easily
unite with the alkalies of the intestinal juices to
form soluble and easily absorbed soaps and com-
binations, a part of the digestive work is spared,
which is of importance for weak persons with de-
ficient power of producing the gastric juices.
	Barley Water Preferable to Milk.—A paper en-
titled "Observations on the Digestibility of Milk,"
read a short time since in London, by Dr. Carter,
has attracted considerable attention. In this pa-
per, Dr. Carter, as reported, discussed various
methods which had been generally used with a
view of promoting the digestibility of milk, point-
ing out that their efficiency was essentially due to
the dilution of the caseine, thus causing the pre-
cipitation, on its introduction into the stomach,
in a granular form, of what would otherwise be
firm, bulky, and compact. He further showed by
experiment, that simple dilution with water was
insufficient for this purpose, and that the object is
far better attained by adding a mixture of alka-
line or starchy waters with the mix. Dr. Carter
summed up by giving a decided practical prefer-
ence to barley water, and this conclusion he illus-
trated and supported by various experiments.


The following are some of the meanings
attached to precious stones: Diamond, in-
nocence; ruby, forgetfulness of vexations
caused by friendship and love; emerald,
success in love; sapphire, repentance; ame-
thyst, sincerity; garnet, coustancy; topaz
fidelity; turquoise, prosperity; opal, hope;
bloodstone, courage; agate, health and long
life; cornelian, contentment; sardonyx, con-
jugal fidelity; chrysolite, antidote against
madness; malachite is not a precious stone
but its color green, signifies hope.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


THE NEAR FUTURE.

	A San Francisco, California, prophet gives
us items of his predictions in regard to
events just about to transpire. We know
this to be an age fraught with wonders
great and marvellous; the Scriptures, Book
of Mormon, the prophecies of Joseph Smith,
all warn us of thrilling scenes, national revo-
lutions and fearful convulsions of the ele-
ments of nature's realm. Some of our
readers may like to hear what one of the
world's seers says, and for their benefit we
copy the prediction in full:
	"A dark cloud is visible over a great
part of the United States, between October
and the 10th of November. This denotes
ill feeling, stringency in money matters,
political disturbances, and want of harmony
in nearly all particulars. Somewhere about
October 20 or 25th, a great catastrophe will
occur in one of the large cities in the Uni-
ted States, involving much loss of life. A
dark cloud in the political horizon denotes
the near approach of the death of a politi-
cal leader of note, also to a renowned capi-
talist in the East. A convulsion of nature
is threatened upon this coast in October,
and great storms in the East. A very
prominent man upon this coast will be the
mark for many tongues in regard to finan-
cial integrity. After much trouble he will
probably save himself from utter ruin.
Disturbances of various kinds will trouble
the peace and quietness of the people in a
thickly populated part of this city, and
many alarming rumors will be prevalent in
October and November. In mining stocks
an unusual market is denoted in October,
and many sensational reports will be used
to check the growing bonanza, which stock
at the present time is but little thought of.
	In Europe, a nation now at peace, will
make warlike preparations, which will
probably end in war, but not during this
year. A great convulsion of nature is
threatened in a country situated to the
south. A country now at war in Europe
will by changes of administration settle all
difficulties and suspend hostilities during
the last of the year.
	In our own government a stupendous
fraud will be discovered near the seat of
government which will bring disgrace to
several very prominent officials. Figura-
tively a cloud visible, just rising in the
West, and the form it takes is that of a
man whose advent will overtake all exist-
ing forms of government. Many people
will flock to his standard during the early
part of '77, and the outlook of that year in-
dicates that it will be the most noted one of
the century."—Ex.


A VISION.

	I had a vision in which I was transport-
ed to the city of Paris. There I saw noth-
ing, however, which as a traveler, I should
have sought. I was conducted by an un-
known guide through crowded streets, into
the heart of the city—into a house, and by
a winding passage into the centre of the
house. That room was small, without win-
dows, and brilliant with gas. The occu-
pant was a man, and we found him violent-
ly at work turning a crank, which seemed
to communicate its motion to machinery
outside of the room. His neck and arms
were bare, and he was red, and puffing with
the violence of his exercise. His express-
ion was evil, and I began to retreat; but
my guide held me, and said, "Ask him
what he is about." So I said as graciously
as I could:


"What manufacture may you be engaged
in, sir?"
	"I turn out the fashions," said he. "My
master keeps a hand at this machine day
and night, throwing off the fashions which
you may see in magazine plates all over
Christendom; now a new style of bonnets,
now of sacks, of dresses, of jewelry, trim-
mings, every thing which goes to make up
feminine attire. By means of this machine
a perfect metamorphosis in woman's dress
is accomplished at least every two months.
One fashion is only adopted before another
is ready to displace it."
	"What is the use?" said I.
	"Well, master is fond of power," he re-
plied, and he rules the world, as you may
say, in this way. This is his grand device
to keep women from thinking. She has no
time to think; she has only time to change
her dress. I turn the wheel, and she must
keep step. She is like a dazed bird—charm-
ed with changing colors—and my master
keeps easy possession of her."
	Then I saw that the adversary of souls
contrived this great system of fashions to
keep woman in a vain show all her life; and
when I went into the street, I saw in every
line of the "latest fashion" his deep design.
	R.
—The Gem.


DIVERSIONS.

	WHAT word will read backwards and for-
wards the same?—Level.
	WHAT flower is emblematic of a fop ad-
dicted to fighting?—The dandy-lion.
	WHY are we sure to have justice done us
after dinner?—Because we get our dessert.
	WHAT word in the English tanguage be-
comes shorter by having a syllable added?—
Short.
	WHAT word of one syllable becomes a
word of two, by having two letters taken
away?—Plague.
	IT was said of a certain judge that he
was so reserved in his manners that one
would never suspect he had any.
	WHEN Richard III. was "himself again"
of course we knew who he was; but who
was he when he was not himself?
	WHAT word is it, the first two letters of
which are male, the three first female, the
first four a brave man, and the whole word
a brave woman?—Heroine.
	DEFINITION of a baby:—A palpitating
tiny bunch of nothing rolled up in flannel,
with the one faculty of almost automatic suc-
tion.

WOMAN'S EXPONENT.
VOL. 5		SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, NOVEMBER 1, 1876.	NO. 11.


TWILIGHT REVERIES.

The shadows rest upon the mountains high,
	And blue and purple [-]aze enwraps them now,
And clouds, fantastical, pil'd 'gainst the sky,
	Wreathe quaint, odd garlands round the mountain's
		brow.

How gently twine shadows and clouds so gray,
	Changing to dullness things but late so bright,
Yet from the beauteous west the closing day
	Casts glim'ring glances of its fading light.

Oft have I watch'd the phantoms twilight made,
	Dissolving day and night in one another,
And in similitude my fancies play'd
	With light and shade, and blended them together.

The soft breeze gently whispers in the leaves,
	In tones so musical, and sweet, and low,
The echoes thrilling me—'til mem'ry weaves
	The reveries of the hour with "long ago."

The visions of the past fill all my soul,
	And through the vista of the years grown grim
I trace the outlines, as upon a scroll,
	Of forms and scenes familiar, though so dim,

I see the forests near my childhood's home
	And hear again the voices in the breeze,
And troop on troop the floods of mem'ries come,
	And I seem wand'ring 'mid those ancient trees

And all that then was mine, of youthful hope,
	And sweet affection, with her myriad powers:
All these, I gather in the shadow'd scope
	Of fancy, as I muse in twilight hours.

Sweet tones of love fall lightly on my ear,
	Again I feel the clasp of hands now cold,
And shudd'ring pause, 'twixt happiness and fear.
	As the vast panorama is unrolled.

'Tis pleasant thus to view the path we've trod,
	And mark where here and there our feet have stray'd,
Where sometime fell a heavy, chast'ning rod
	And soft the whisper, be not thou afraid!

Sooth'd all the wound—and cheered us ever on,
	And list'ning close the still small voice we hear,
Now plainer grown than when life first begun,
	Helping and guiding as the goal we near.

If on our way some shadows in the years,
	Darken'd our path, and hid the light from view,
Now looking back through mists of bitter tears;
	We know WHOSE HAND guided us safely through
											EMILE.
	SALT LAKE CITY, Oct. 30, 1876,


[Column 1]
		THE GRAIN QUESTION.

	During the past few weeks, I have heard
many questions and remarks, similar to the
following: Is it true that President Young
advises the sisters to buy and store up the
grain? But what can they be expected to
accomplish in a matter like this? It has
always been considered a woman's sphere
to cook the food; has it now become her
place to look after and provide it also? If
the women are to engage in such business,
what, indeed, will be left for the brethren
to do? How do we know but that, if we
undertake such measures we shall [--]come
speculators, as men are? Buy up large
quantities of grain at low figures, and as
the current fluctuates, turn round and sell
it at high prices, just as our brethren have
done.
	I am happy to find that in an editorial of
the last EXPONENT, nearly, if not quite all
these questions were carefully answered.
I hope, my sisters, you have all found the
same. If any of you have read that arti-
cle, entitled "SISTERS BE IN EARNEST!"
and have failed to drink its full meaning,
or to receive the spirit by which it was
given, please read it again. I did so, with

[Column 2]
profit; and I feel that I may also answer
some of my sisters' inquiries here, as one
deeply, vitally interested.
	Are our good, faithful brethren likely to
be left without plenty of employment, if
the sisters do all they can to assist in the
great Latter-day work? I think not, in as
much as temples are to be built, many of
them, in different parts of the land, the
gospel preached, and the plan of Salvation
carried on in its various ramifications. No
one need be troubled with the fear of hav-
ing nothing to do. There is abundance of
work for both men and women, in forward-
ing the progress of the Kingdom of God.
When we are told what to do, our whole
duty is to receive the message with meek-
ness and cheerfulness; and then go to work
"in earnest," as we have been told, and,
never wavering, honorably discharge the
obligation with which we are entrusted.
	The idea of women becoming speculators
is nothing to be dreaded. A woman has as
good a right to engage in honest speculation
as a man has; but there is no danger of the
Latter-day Saint sisters going into any
speculation with grain at this time, on ac-
count of the privilege opened to them, ex-
cept to buy up and store away all they can.
If any one doubts this assertion, let her
look abo[-]t her, behold the myriads of little
children in the land, and recollect that the
women who are now called upon to save 
grain, are their MOTHERS.
	O! my sisters, even now while I write, a
little chubby hand pulls my sleeve, two soft,
dark eyes look smilingly up into mine and
a pair of cherry lips ask me for some sugar.
Could I imagine those pure, baby-lips, which
with all a mother's tenderness I stoop to
kiss, asking for BREAD when I had none to
give, and yet sell one kernel of grain that
might be preserved at home! I tell you, I
have no fear of the sisters turning this
grain movement into a speculation. All we
want and all we need, is to be firmly united
in this as in every other effort; and with
the help of the Lord and His Spirit to guide
us, we will be wise as was Joseph in Egypt
and as successful. God grant that not one
honest heart may falter; and all the honor
and glory be ascribed to him forever.
							MABEL.


[Column 1]
		NOTES AND NEWS.

	IN THE country where the Eastern war is
carrying on its dreadful devastation, there
are said to be many thousands of men, wo-
men and children almost naked and liter-
ally starving.
	MRS. S. Westman of Indianapolis, a lady
lecturer, has drawn forth the highest enco-
miums of praise for her eloquent and logi-
cal method of dealing with the subject of
Finance.
	THE INTRODUCTION of women clerks
into the goods offices of the London and
Northwestern Railway at Birmingham, has
proved so successful that the Directors have
been induced to try the experiment in oth-
er large centers of traffic. So say some of
our exchanges.
	MRS. LUCRETIA Mott, the venerable
quakeress and staunch supporter and beloved

[Column 2]
advocate for woman in her most true and
use[--]l sphere, was upon the platform at the
recent Woman's Congress held in Philadel-
phia, and in her speech before the house,
traced the development of woman's educa-
tion for the last thirty years.
	"MUSIC, AND Woman as a Musician,"
one of the papers read at the Woman's Con-
gress, is certainly an article of real merit.
It is decidedly the best article of the kind
we have ever had the pleasure of perusing,
and contains so many ideas of superior ex-
cellence that we feel would be appreciated
that we intend publishing a part of it in
our next number.
	IN THE Woman's Journal, Oct. 7th, there is a
short article headed "Put Yourself In Her
Place." This article refers to Lucy Stone
at Faneuil Hall, speaking of the absurdity
of refusing Suffrage to George Elliot, one of
the most popular English woman authors
at the present age. The Boston Sunday
Herald makes its comments upon this, and
considers that for such a great woman and
writer, it would be a waste of time. The
writer in the Journal carries the argument
further and asks "What should we think
of this logic?" by substantiating the name
of George Elliot for the name of William
Cullen Bryant, James Russell Lowell or of
some other literary man, equally gifted
and popular. "Would discussing politics
be a waste of time for them?"	"NOTES FROM the Lecture Field," pub-
lished in the "Woman's Journal" of Oct 21,
in alluding to the many reiterations of the
assertions "Woman Suffrage is unpopular;"
"Public opinion is against it;" Women are
its most bitter opponents;" advances some
excellent ideas. The writer, Miss Matilda
Hindman, a very staunch supporter of Wo-
man Suffrage and an eminent lecturer, says
"these things are so often repeated that
even many friends of the cause believe
them true;" whereas Miss Hindman asserts
"it is not unpopular among the most intelli-
gent classes." The press she says has gen-
erally been unfavorable, and consequently
"the cause has suffered from adverse state-
ments." Miss Hindman speaks of having
traveled thousands of miles through some
of the States, holding meetings by hun-
dreds, conversing with the leading men and
women in these various localities, and she
believes she has had ample opportunity to
judge the sentiments of the people on this
subject. She adds "men who stand the
highest intellectually, morally and socially
in their own States are the best friends of
Woman Suffrage."


MONEY is not all one has to give. The
poorest in purse may bestow real sympathy
and comfort, may minister to the sorrowing
and rejoice with the joyful, weep with
those that weep and add largely to the sum
of human happiness.
	THE every-day cares and duties which
men call drudgery, are the weights and
counterpoises of the clock of time, giving
its pendulum a true vibration, and its
hands a regular motion, and when they
cease to hang upon the wheels, the pendu-
lum no longer swings, the hands no longer
move, the clock stands still.—Longfellow.

[Column 1]
			R. S. REPORTS.

	The meeting of the Senior and Junior
Retrenchment Association was held in the
Fourteenth Ward Assembly Rooms, Satur-
day, Oct. 14. President Mrs: M. I. Horne
was present and after the reading of min-
utes from different wards addressed the
meeting on the various subjects now under
consideration. She exhorted the sisters to
encourage all home institutions, and above
all to live their religion in an acceptable
manner; she spoke of the sorrow mani-
fested by the people now that they could no
longer have the privilege of officiating for
their dead, and advised all those present to
beware of procrastination lest the Bride-
groom should come and find them unpre-
pared. Mrs. E. S. Barney gave some ac-
count of the recent visit she had made to
some of the settlements, and the success
which had attended their efforts. Mrs. B.
W. Smith, Mrs. P. Woodruff, and Mrs.
Margaret P. Young all spoke [-]pon the prin-
ciples of the Gospel and the testimony of
the Holy Spirit. The meeting was an in-
teresting one and the Spirit of the gospel
seemed to abound in the hearts of the sis-
ters.

	SELINA, SEVIER COUNTY, Oct 7th, 1876,
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	The following is is a brief report of our
societies. The sisters are alive to their du-
ties and are trying to serve God in a right
and acceptable manner. Our Relief So-
ciety was organized Dec. 25, 1873, and Sis-
ter Margaret Rasmasen was chooson for our
president, but being called to move away,
Sister Margaret Anderson was chosen to fill
her place. We find her a good, kind presi-
dent and one who is trying to live her re-
ligion and help the sisters, and especially
the poor in all their needs for comfort or as-
sistance of any sort. A number of the
Saints here have been baptized for the re-
newal of their covenants, enjoying the
Spirit of the Lord, and He has blessed them
with an abundance of harvest this season.
The brethren have built a store-house to
store away the grain, and are very busy.
We meet every Fast day and every second
Thursday and converse upon the principles
of our religion. The teachers visit the dif-
ferent wards once a month and find out the
candition of the saints and where there
are any sick or poor that are in need we
give comfort and attend to their wants im-
mediately. We also have a Young Ladies
Society which was organized last Fall with
the following officers. Mrs. Mary Ann
Steele, President; Mrs. Hannah J. Spencer,
and Mrs. Sarah Casto, Counselors, Miss
Catharine Martin, Secretary and Mrs.
Martha Fenn, Treasurer. There are but
very few young ladies in our small place,
but there is a large num[-]er of small girls
from nine to twelve years of age, who love
to attend meeting, and we hope at some
future day to have a large and intelligent
Society.
	Wishing you success, I remain your Sis-
ter in the Gospel.
				HANNAH J. SPENCER

			MILL CREEK, Oct. 17, 1876.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	Our Relief Society meetings are held
every second Thursday in each month.
Last month we had the pleasure of a visit
from some of the sisters from the city.
Miss E. R. Snow, Mrs. Zina D, Young,
Mrs. E. Davis, Mrs. P. Staines, and Mrs. E.
Howard.

[Column 2]
	Miss E. R. Snow spoke to us about home
industry, and of making substantial cloth-
ing such as we can manufacture ourselves;
she also spoke of the necessity of meeting
together often.
	Mrs. Zina Young spoke upon seri-culture,
showing how very easy we could raise the
materials, and that it would be for our good.
We had a good meeting and if we do not
do right it is not for the want of instruc-
tions.
	I will thank my sisters for their kindly
visit and good counsel, and may God bless
them in their labor.
	We are doing as well as we can in our
scattered condition. We have a good room
well carpeted and furnished. We take care
of the sick, and help the needy. The
Young Ladies Retrenchment Association
is doing well under the presidency of Mrs.
Isabella Carlisle. They take the EXPONENT
and read selections from it in their meet-
ings.
	With best wishes for the advancement of
every good work and home manufacture,
we remain the co-workers of those who la-
bor for the advancement of the Kingdom of
God,
			MRS. MARY MCALLISTER,
							Pres't.
		MRS. ELIZABETH CARLISLE,
							Sec'y.

	VIRGIN CITY, KANE CO., UTAH,
					October 16th, 1876.
EDITORS EXPONENT:
	When we last wrote to you, in May, our
Retrenchment Association numbered thirty-
two; since that time it has increased to for-
ty members, and more are joining almost
every meeting. We hold our meetings
once in two weeks; we employ our time in
speaking, reading from the EXPONENT and
other good publications, and in working at
different kinds of work. On Friday, Oct.
6th, the Association met at the school house
for the purpose of quilting a quilt, the first
that had been completed in the Association;
the young ladies worked very diligently
till 12 o'clock, when all brought in their
picnic, and a table was set the length of the
hall, and at one p. m. about forty (including
a few of our mothers who had been invited
in) were seated at a very good dinner.. Af-
ter dinner the tables were removed, and
the quilt finished. Afterwards the Asso-
ciation held a meeting, to which the Young
Men's Association had been invited, and the
girls had been requested to invite their
mothers. After singing and prayer, Sisters
Maria Spendlove and Alice Isom delivered
lectures, which were listened to with great
interest, and which were truly encouraging
and edifying to the young. At six p. m. all
met at the hall to enjoy themselves in the
recreation of a dance, which closed at ten,
when all expressed themselves well pleased
with the evening entertainment. A good
spirit prevailed throughout the entire day.
	Hoping for the success of your valuable
paper, we remain as ever your sisters
	LUCINDA STRATTON,	}	Secretaries.
	ELEANOR JEPSON,	}

debt of £780,000,000. The United States,
£470,000,000; Italy, £390,000000; Spain,
£375,000,000; Austria, £350,000,000. The 
debt of Russia amounts to £340,000,000.
The German Empire £200,000,000; Tur-
key, £135,000,000; India, £130,000,000; 
Brazil £82,000,000 Holland £80,000,000.


THE GREAT JAPANESE "ALTAR
			OF DAILBUTZ."

	Dailbutz, meaning the "Great Buddha,"
a bronze idol is situated in the midst of a
plain, once the site of a large city, now only
surrounded by a thin grove of trees which
have outlived the more perishable temples
and shrines once included in these sacred
grounds, and of the city only a straggling
village remains, it is called Kamakura. Dail-
butz, meaning the "Great Buddha," is about
seventy feet in height, entirely of bronze,
and represents the god in his usual state of
profound repose, sitting cross-legged, the
eyes closed, and hands clasped, with thumb-
nails meeting. It rests upon a stone found-
ation, and in front of it, upon a stone altar,
are two large bronze vases, each containing
blossoms and leaves of the lotus, a sacred
plant of the Buddhists, perfectly carved in
bronze, and in front of both is the inevita-
ble cash-box. Climbing up the folds of the
god's garment, which form safe footholds
for some distance, we sat down on his im-
mense thumbs, and some idea may be
formed of the size of this statue by the
fact that three grown persons can sit com-
fortably on one thumb, between its end and
the second joint. This majestic idol, perfect
in its proportions, and sublime in its ex-
pression of an eternity of repose, was
placed in its present position many
centuries ago—so many that the exact
date of its erection is unknown. Two
wire-guarded windows are cut in the
back near the shoulders, and a door cut on
one side admits visitors. Inside is a good-
sized room, with images of various saints
on two rough altars, and incense burning
before them. Cut in the woodwork of the
altars, and written in Japanese ink, any-
where and everywhere about this interior
of the statue are innumerable names and
addresses of visitors.


Mothers have many serious cares: This
assertion is undeniable; the anxiety in-
volved in maternity is well understood by
all who have experienced its pains and an-
guish. The feebleness of the new born in-
fant, unable to express its wants or its pains,
continually beset by dangers to its frail life
from the very first, constantly appeal to the
mother's nature, when her own strength is
just slowly returning. The restless nights,
the attacks of sickness now and then, the
anxious watching and waiting, all tell upon
the mother. And when the baby can ex-
press its wants and its feelings how much
waywardness to be controlled and checked,
what ill tendencies to be subdued. And it
is seldom for one alone these energies and
sympathies are called into action, there is
usually a group, a little community, an em-
bryo kingdom, over which the mother has
sway, keeping, as far as possible, each in its
place, and giving to each the right which
is due. The variety of temperament is o[-]e
item which cannot be disregarded in the
government of the mother: due allowance
is necessary in order to guide and restrain
without repressing, and thus perhaps blunt-
ing some strong attribute which, carefully
guided, would constitute strength of char-
acter. Thus is the sphere of womanhood
equivalent to that of a queen, and she
should exercise her best and highest facul-
ties to sway such a sceptre as shall make
her a "queen without being a despot."


IN this simple fact that we cannot accu-
rately forsee the future, lies a refuge from
despair.—Lewes.

THE GOLDEN SIDE.

There is many a rest on the road of life,
	If we only would stop to take it;
And many a tone from the better land,
	If the querulous heart would wake it.
To the sunny soul that is full of hope.
	And whose beautiful trust ne'er faileth,
The grass is green and the flowers are bright,
	Though the wintry storm prevaileth.

Better to hope, though the clouds hang low,
	And to keep the eyes still lifted;
For the sweet blue sky will soon peep through,
	When the ominous clouds are rifted.
There was never a night without a day,
	Nor an evening without a morning;
And the darkest hour, the proverb goes,
	Is an hour before the dawning.

There is many a gem in the path of life,
	Which we pass in our idle pleasure,
That is richer far than the jeweled crown,
	Or the miser's hoarded treasure;
It may be the love of a little child,
	Or a mother's prayer to Heaven,
Or only a beggar's grateful thanks,
	For a cup of water given.

Better to weave in the web of life
	A bright and golden filling,
And to do God's will with a ready heart,
	And hands that are swift and willing,
Than to snap the delicate silver threads
	Of our curious lives asunder,
And then Heav'n blame for the tangled ends,
	And sit to grieve and wonder.
									—Ex.


For the EXPONENT.
			SPEECH.

	This is a theme we should do well to
study, for when we consider the millions of
words we utter that are vain and frivolous,
sometimes even wicked, it makes us trem-
ble when we remember that the Lord says,
"out of our mouth shall we be judged;" and
how large a number of this people have
covenanted in HOLY PLACES, not to use
vain, light or wicked language; words of
good sense ought ever to flow from the lips
of the saints. Not that we should always
speak in a serious or sombre manner, for
the best instruction may be conveyed in a
cheerful, easy and often mirthful conversa-
tion. For the young, I would recommend
this motto, "Be merry and wise." These
little words contain an epitome of wisdom.
	Speech, is one of the greatest gifts to
man and woman; it is the mark of Divin-
ity; no other creature has the gift of a audi-
ble speech. All creation speaks, but it is
with a silent eloquence; true they have
their tones, like a musical instrument,
when the chords are struck sound most beau-
tiful and harmonious, as the feathered
tribe, some deep and sonorous, as the lion,
the blood-hound, and cattle of inferior
kind, and in a less pleasing degree: but all
natural sounds are harmonious, and Scrip-
ture says, "There are many voices and
none are without signification." We can
fancy trees and flowers have a voice, but it
is the language of a book; and on their
leaves are the words,—God! Heaven! And
by their sweet perfume they make them-
selves tangible to us; who that has walked
down the rich and gay parterre, does not
thrill with gratitude to Our Father in heav-
en for the beautiful and delicious things
He has prepared and organized for His
children upon earth. The taste, the smell,
the eye, the ear is fed "with heaven's pe-
culiar manna." Yes! The spirit of our God
fills the atmosphere in such a spot, and the
mind is often soothed and comforted by the
feeling that heaven is around us; and we
know that these keep a celestial law unbro-
ken. But to turn more particularly to
speech as the grand medium of intelligence
amid the human family. There are mo-
ments when we crave silence in ourselves
and others and realize that solitude is best,
but in a general way, speech is the life of
society, and a delightful means of convey-
ing happiness and instruction. A silent
person has no charm for me, and I am sus-
picious of them, as I have often discovered
that such an one is taking items of the
words and actions of others, and hereafter
can freely criticize, perhaps satirize the
speakers of the company. The best feel-
ing is soon produced in a party where all
contribute, and I think there is a propriety
and good sense in the following lines which
a friend wrote on the fly-leaf of my album:
	"You may look in this album but learn [-]re you look
	That all are requested to add to the book.
	You may quiz as you please, but the penalty is,
	That you also leave something for others to quiz."
	It is astonishing how mind acts upon
mind, and spirit upon spirit, and I have
seen some that the moment they enter an
assembly, and begin to speak, the whole
company seem to arise to a higher degree
of intelligence and happiness.
	By the aid of speech we have the power
to convey instruction, amusement, comfort,
and consolation, and do a world of good;
if we pervert this God-like gift we can ex-
cite anger, disgust, revenge, sorrow, de-
spair and misery. When we meet in social
parties, it should be the duty of every one
to consider for a moment WHY we meet,
and what good will arise from the meeting,
and determine that we will try and say
something that will benefit some one. All
have talents, all possess knowledge on some
points that perhaps others have not acquir-
ed. Foolish jesti[-]g and frivolous talking is
not becoming to any woman or young girl
in company.
	We should be in our best spirits, our
brightest intellects, and the vehicle of these
should call forth our best manners; our cares
should be left out of the circle always. The
social party is for reunion, recreation, and a
a reciprocation of thoughts and sentiments,
and an interchange of talents, abilities and
the sweet amenities of life, and we retire
from such a circle refreshed and invigora-
ted in body and mind.
	When the Head of a House is the Con-
stellation of that Orbit we find order and
intelligence throughout, and all are made
happy for the time being, the cue being
naturally given and taken from such a
source, and spreads like a subtile perfume
throughout the assembly, and it is no hack-
neyed phrase or unmeaning assertion
when we say, yes, from our heart, it is
good for us to be here. Dryden says:
"Speech is the moving of the Soul, it
spreads its beauteous images abroad which
else lie furled in the clouded mind."
					HANNAH T. KING.


WOMAN'S RECORD.

	Ex-Queens Christina and Isabella are
"lobbying" for the payment of heavy claims
on the national treasury.—Ex.
	A WORK of the late Madame George
Sands entitled "Dialogues Philosophiques"
is about to be published shortly, as a review
by "M. Renan."
	MISS Emily Faithful, in speaking of wo-
men asking men for work, remarks, that
"if they do they will get what they have
always got—the [-]ind men do not want to
do themselves." Miss Faithful is one of
the most earnest and sincere advocates of
Woman's Rights, and is undeniably practi-
cal in all her ideas of the woman question.
	MISS Alcott, the very famous writer for


little folks, has just published a new volume
"Silver Pitchers," and "Independence," a
Centennial love story. The volume con-
tains nine short stories, and is calling forth
much comment and considerable criticism
from the literary world. "Silver Pitcher" is
a temperance tale and contains a moral.
Miss Alcott's writings possess the intrinsic
merit of imparting a contagious happiness.
And in this respect are valuable for they
contain no objectionable arguments or doc-
trine. She looks upon life as wholesomely
as a child, and leaves no morbid impression
upon the youthful mind.
	GEORGE Eliott's last new story, which
has just been concluded in Harper's Month-
ly, "Daniel Deronda," is said to have been
the event of the month in literary circles.
No story for years has called forth as much
discussion and attention among educated
and cultivated people. The book has just
been issued in a complete volume. The
public are likely to know something more
of this celebrated author from a new vol-
ume just published by Messrs E. Moxon &
Co. It contains the biography and autobi-
ography of Elizabeth Evans, the alleged
original of Dinah Morris in "Adam Bede."
The book is entitled "George Eliott in Der-
byshire. It has upon the title page the
names of Guy Roslyn and George Bennett
Smith, and contains a letter from George
Elliot concerning her characters in "Adam
Bede."

GRACE and glory are inseparably joined—
he that gets the one may be certain of the
other.
	THE best estimate of the population of
the American nation at the beginning of
its first century was 2,750,000. It has
now 44,675,000. The area of county has been
extended from 800,000, to 603,844 square
miles. Manufactures have advanced in val-
ue from 20,000,000, to 4,200,000,000. There
are only three nations which surpass the
United States in extent of surface; the
British, Chinese and Russian Empires.
