SALT LAKE CITY, NOV. 1, 1876.


[Column 1]
		BE WISE AND HEARKEN TO
				COUNSEL.

	To the women of this Territory we make this
appeal in all sincerity, and after most serious
thought, on storing away grain while it is with-
in their reach. We wish if it were possible,
the subject might be agitated in public and
private until every mother and every sister
should feel the necessity of immediate action.
	Is it not presumptiom in the Latter-day
Saints to say they believe in the inspiration of
the work of the last days now commenced,
and that this people receive divine revelation
for guidance, and then pay no attention what-
ever to the instruction given through the ser-
vants of God who hold the keys of power upon
the earth?
	Why are the people so slow in acting, so heed-
less of those things which are the most essen-
tial? Why do they slumber and sleep when they
are continually invoked to arise and shake off
their lethargy and prepare themselves for the
great and eventful future? And will they not
be left to mourn and lament if they neglect
the things they have been told to do?
	A great and wise prophet in ancient times
said, "Hath the Lord as great delight in burnt-
offerings and sacrifices as in obeying the voice
of the Lord? Behold to obey is better than
sacrifice, and to hearken than the fat of rams."
This mighty truth is as much a truth to-day as
when it was first uttered.
	What is there that will so effectually appeal
to human nature as want—want of bread?
Surely, if there is a spark of true fire in the
hearts of the women of this Territory, after
the request President Young has made of them
reposing such confidence in their ability and
exertions, they will arouse all their dormant
energies to earnest, decisive and immediate ac-
tion. Should the time come (and it will) when
there will be a scarcity of bread, what will wo-
men care then for a little sarcasm? It seems
too trivial to call forth the slightest notice in
comparison with the momentous subject in
contemplation. It would seem that this peo-
ple's privations in times past would have so
thoroughly taught them the necessity of econ-
omy and of wisdom in obeying counsel, that
it would only be necessary to ask them to do a
thing which is so palpable, and they would un-
derstand it. The hundredth part of the suf-
fering among, even this small remnant of peo-
ple, here in these valleys in the times of
scarcity, has never yet been told; it is some-
thing too deep for empty words; then only to
picture a scarcity all over the land as has been
predicted, and the scene is too horrible to
bear looking upon. Let every mother be awak-
ened to a realization of the necessity of heark-

[Column 2]
ening before the time is past. Wh[--] i[-] [---]
that can feel these things as deeply as a moth-
er can; think what it would be to hear your 
little one cry for bread, and worst of all to know
you had trifled away the day of grace given
you, had thought lightly, or not at all upon the
subject. That men, as a class, are very tena-
cious of their rights and privileges, is "most
true;" but let them carefully weigh this mat-
ter and they will assuredly grant to woman
the right of self-preservation, for herself and
her posterity. It is argued by men that an-
cient history proves woman's inferiority and
servile state, in the days when God had His
prophets upon the earth; but do not the Scrip-
tures certify woman to have been not only
queen and prophetess, but Judge in Israel, as
witness Deborah in the fourth chapter of Judg-
es. Is there then anything so inconsistent in
women building storehouses and filling them
with grain?
	It has been remarked by some that Presi-
dent Young thought that by doing this, the
wives and daughters of this people would cease
to ask their fathers and husbands to dispose of
their grain for finery, or unnecessary articles
for personal or household ornament. But in
our opinion, men themselves in a general way,
dispose of as much means for tobacco; and such
products as tend to degeneration and degrada-
tion, as women do for vanity.
	Of what use would all the finest fabrics ever
woven be, or jewels and diamonds if there was
a lack of bread? How little in comparison
would these things seem, how insignificant
and worthless. We distinctly remember (for
these things are not easily forgotten) that in
our exit from Nauvoo, when we were about to
launch forth into an uncultivated wilderness,
the thoughts of the people were concentrated
upon food for subsistence, and they were glad
to dispose of such things as they could possi-
bly spare; carpets, shawls, silk dresses, gold
watches, chains, rings, and many other valua-
ble things were sent down into Missouri to ex-
change for wheat, corn meal, or any of the
staple products which sustain life, as well as
for oxen and cows with which to pursue the
journey.
	To those who have experienced the priva-
tions which the saints have passed through at
different periods since the organization of this
church, the words of President Young must
carry a double force, for they can not only see
the future in prospect but the past in retro-
spect.
	The Lord has brought this people from among
their kindred and friends, and has made this
desolate land a fruitful and desirable one, and
plentiful harvests have rewarded the husband-
man for his toil. Have they been prompt in
the fulfillment of those laws and principles
which have been in existence ever since time
began, whenever the Lord had a church upon
the earth? The law of tithes and offerings. If
women have been remiss in the past, if they
have been dilatory in doing the "Master's
work" slow in hearkening to counsel, and have
sought after vain and foolish things, let them
seek to make what reparation they can while
there is opportunity. This subject is not the
embodiment of an idle tale or romance, but a
stern and positive reality which must be met
and acted upon. God grant our sisters wis-
dom to carry out President Young's counsel,
and we trust that ere another issue of the EX-
PONENT we may have reports from different lo-
calities, representing the movements which
will, ere then have been made. Surely the
Lord will bless His daughters and give them
strength and fortitude in such a noble enter-
prise. [handwritten] E. B. Wells


A WORD ABOUT WOMEN.

	One can scarcely take up a book, magazine,
or newspaper nowadays without being aware
that women are "creating a sensation," they
are certainly getting themselves talked about;
are men beginning to fear and tremble, (I fear
some are) and is there "a woman at the bottom
of the mischief?" That is a familiar saying of
theirs. We are told that "in the last days men's
hearts would fail them," &c., but we had no
idea it would be attributable to women, yet it
is even so from the pulpit down; this is idol-
breaking by women, and though men speak of
her as silly, vain, and frivolous; they will per-
haps find ere long that woman is terribly in
earnest; that she is no longer willing to be
trammelled by narrow conventionalities, but
to step forward on a broader platform, undaunt-
ed by the sarcasm or even foul aspersions cast
upon her by those who have not the inspira-
tion of a potent power urging them to a noble
purpose. She can endure all the calumny and
reproach of those who are ready to attribute
to her the motives of revenge, spite or disap-
pointed love, knowing that the end will more
than justify the means, and be ample atone-
ment for any personal sacrifice.

	"Through slander—meanest spawn from hell—
	And woman's slander is the worst—
	And you whom once I loved so well,
	Through you my life has been accurst."

	I will not agree that woman's slander is the
worst, Tennyson to the contrary, notwithstand-
ing, and I venture to dispute the long estab-
lished dogma, of total depravity in women;
tradition has handed it down from the days of
Adam and Eve. I take the ground that slan-
der has no sex; the "poison of asps" is no more
under the lips of woman than of man, and the
unruly member that stirs up a great fire belongs
no more to one than to the other; I am not one
who thinks men and women are made of differ-
ent flesh and blood, not even that men are made
of dust and women are angels, but that wo-
men from their very infancy are guarded from
all outward contamination, so that the very
elements of the deadliest slanders are wanting.
	Woman untaught, in a vast majority, for
countless centuries in all arts save such as per-
tain to domestic life or "the wiles that charm,"
at best subordinate or inferior, to come and go
as she was bid, has been termed silly, vain,
weak and cowardly, but give her justice and
she is no coward, give her cause and she will
face fearful odds, as is abundantly proved at
the present time in her putting forward a claim
for the right of the ballot; we have as much to
gain or lose by good laws, or bad laws as men;
we should be well versed in law and under-
stand all that pertains to marriage, to divorce,
and to property; we have as much to risk by
law as men. Does it detract from their dignity
because women prove themselves capacitated
for the same positions as men, is this any usurp-
ation of power or of rights? If they are really
superior to us let them move on, "there is room
higher up." They have not reached the pin-
nacle of greatness yet, to which it is possible
to attain, let them look to it that they advance
if they would not have women too close up-
on their track, for there is advancement among
women; thought is growing, a[-]d m[--] should
turn their attention and bend their energies
to things of more weighty consideration than
woman's faults and a jealousy of precedence,
should look with pride instead of fear upon wo-
men who are seeking to elevate, inspire and
ennoble the sex, for they are the molders of
men, it is conceded; then as women advance,
so does it more surely perfect the future race of
men. Woman bears the impress of the seal of
immortality. 						EMILE.
		[handwritten] E. B. Wells


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.		85


NOTICE.

	The Woman's Commission House, recently establish-
ed under the auspices of the Relief Society Woman's
Mercantile Association, is now transacting business on
commission at the Old Constitution Buildings, opposite
Z. C. M. I. Their stock consists entirely of home-
manufactures. They are well supplied with plaid, lin-
seys and waterproofs for winter dresses, wraps etc; and
shawls large and small, stockings and socks of all sizes
knit with machine or by hand, baby's socks, crochet
cuffs or wristers large and small. A dressmaker is
there, ready to make up material to order. There is a
good supply of men's and boys wear, of all the differ-
ent varieties manufactured here; an excellent tailor to
do all kinds of work in that line. There is also a full
stock of hats, straw and felt, and a miliner to attend to
that part of the work. They have also various other
articles to wear and use. All the standard church
books. Homemade soap, Jamaica ginger, ink, black-
ing and vinegar: also butter, cheese, and eggs. Among
the things noticeable are the lace and silk productions,
some of which are exquisite. The patronage of the
people is solicited for this institution, that Utah may
sustain by united effort the industry which is the pros-
pective wealth of her people. The women of this Ter-
ritory are making this almost unprecedented movement
to concentrate their exertions in one grand enterprise to
become self-sustaining, that they may free themselves
as a people, as soon as possible, from the shackles of 
Babylon!


[Column 1]
				HOME AFFAIRS.

	SEVERAL OF the missionaries appointed at the last
Semi-Annual Conference, started to-day on their jour-
ney. Some had left previous and others will start on
the sixth!
	THE DISTRICT Court seems unable to dispose of
some of the cases on hand, as there are so many crooks
and turns taken that these cases are likely to drag on,
until they shall be ruled out of Court, or assume the
character of cases in chancery.
	THE UTAH Musical Times, No. 8, now issued con-
tains many excellent things. The editorial "Singing in
Schools" deserves to be widely read, and all interested
in the advancement of the young are sure to read it
with interest. The hints given on the subject are adapt-
ed to the situation of the people. The music is always
of a high order and tends to refine the taste. We hope
this journal will be well sustained. It has our best
wishes, especially as we believe it to be one step towards
the progression of Utah in musical art.
	THE MUSICAL and dramatic entertainment tendered
to Mr. O. F. Whitney, and given in the Fourteenth
Ward Assembly Rooms, Wednesday evening Oct. 25th,
was very well attended. The whole performance was
creditable, and there were several parts which were exe-
cuted in such a tasteful and artistic manner that they
deserve notice made of t[-]em. These amateurs ought to
be congratulated for their efforts to please their friends
and especially for lending their efforts in a good cause.
Mr. O. F. Whitney tenders his sincere thanks to Prof.
Thomas, the Salt Lake Orchestra, the Union Glee Club,
also the young ladies and gentlemen who so kindly took
part in the performance.

EDITOR EXPONENT:
	Through your paper I wish to announce to
the sisters who in days past have been associa-
ted with Sister Lovina Walker, the sad news of
her death which occurred on the 8th of Oct.
at half-past one, p. m., soon after having giv-
en birth to a fine son.

[Column 2]
	She fell asleep without a struggle, and per-
fectly conscious that her departure was near:
she seemed as calm as a summer evening, man-
ifesting neither anxiety nor regret. She has
left a husband and eleven children, with whom
we deeply and sincerely mourn the loss of a
kind and affectionate wife, and a fond and de-
voted mother. But for her there is no cause for
grief, for she had "fought the good fight and
kept the faith." Her work was done and she
has gone to her parents and friends.
	Here are very many who have known sister
Lovina from the days of her girlhood when
her kind and loving father, our beloved mar-
tyred prophet, brother Hyrum Smith, was
with us. She is his eldest child. It has been
my happy lot, which I consider no ordinary
privilege to spend many interesting and profit-
able seasons in listening to her descriptions of
scenes and circumstances connected with her
father's life.
	I first became acquainted with Sister Walker
in May, 1859; I with my two little children
and an elderly sister who was living with me,
had come from Philadelphia with the Emigra-
tion on my way to the Valley. I had expend-
ed my last means and had no provisions left—
we arrived in Florence at 9 o'clock p. m., it
was dark and raining, the brethren helped us
from the wagons into a vacant house and we
went to rest as soon as possible.
	Early next morning we we e awakened by
some one knocking at the door, the sister with
me opened it, there stood a little girl with a
cup of milk in her hand, she asked if "there
was a little woman there with two little child-
ren," the sister told her "yes" and asked her to
come in, she did so and when she saw me said,
"If you please, my ma wants to see you,
she has sent this milk to your little girls." I
asked her ma's name, thinking it was some one
I knew, but when she told me I knew no one
by that name—I accordingly went to her
mother's house; she met me at the door with
both hands stretched towards me in welcome.
"Good morning Sister Elizabeth," said she. I
told her she had the advantage of me as I did
not remember ever seeing her before. She said
"No and I never saw you before; I am Hyrum
Smith's daughter, my father came to me three
times last night and told me that you were a
child of God, that you was without money,
provisions or friends and that I must help you.
The first time he came to me, I awoke my hus-
band and told him, not liking to be disturbed,
he told me I was dreaming, that it was only
fancy. My father came to me again and told
me your name, he also told me to send my lit-
tle girl with some milk and prove to my hus-
band that it was not fancy." She requested
me to go for my children and the sister that
was with me to come into breakfast, and after-
wards, I learned that the last food she had in
the house was on the table that morning; after
breakfast she went to Bro. Johnson, told him
the circumstance and asked him to help her
provide for me while I was in Florence, which
he did directly, as I have since learned. When
I left Florence I did not see her again for thir-
teen years; then Bro. Hyrum came to me in a
dream and told me to go to Farmington and
help Lovina as she was sick; being unable to
go I sent my daughter to see her, who wrote
me to come directly as my dream was too
true.
	From that time to this we have not been
separated long, and I thank God our Father,
for the friendship that existed between us, and
hope to live the rest of my life so that when I
go hence there will be one happy greeting, one
dear friend ready to welcome me where sorrow
will be known no more.

[Column 3]
	And now, sisters let us not forget the depart-
ed one, but let us watch over her little mother-
less children and extend to them a loving,
helping hand to cheer them through the rough
journey of life, for our Saviour said, "Inas-
much as ye have done it unto one of these lit-
tle ones ye do it unto me."
					ELIZABETH DAVIS.
	17th Ward, S. L. City.


For the EXPONENT.
			SENSITIVE PEOPLE.

	By this, I mean those who are sensitive to
an extreme. All those who can lay claim to
this unenviable distinction are certainly ob-
jects of pity; for of all the throng that
moves in society, there are none who have
so much cause for misery. Hold, my criti-
cal friend, ere you arch your brow in scorn.
I do not mean that sensitive people have
more real trouble to bear than others, but I do
mean that their cause for trouble is greater,
yes, tenfold, because it is imaginary, and
imagination calls forth more misery, wretch-
edness and despair than all real troubles
that ever existed.
	A sensitive nature—some claim that it is
a mark of superiority—Hannah More has
said
	"Where glow exalted sense and taste refined,
	There keener anguish rankles in the mind,
	There feeling is diffused through every part,
	Thrills in each nerve and lives in all the heart."

	This may be so, but who is anxious for
the distinction? Who, for the sake of be-
ing thought a little more refined, would
sacrifice his peace of mind by the change,
if he could have it at will?
	The aim of life is happiness, but this class
of sensitive people destroy not only their
own share, but detract seriously from that
of others who surround them. A hasty
word, a careless expression of half digested
thought, or even a harmless witticism is
sufficient to cast them into the depths of
gloom and despondency.
	Happy are they who can live above such
trifles—who can move on just as gay and
light-hearted as ever whether the sun shines
or not. Who heed not the looks of the en-
vious, the tongues of the slanderous, and
the frowns of the haughty and proud. Who
can take the world as they find it and live
through it without repining—this is the
class to be admired. You sensitive, melan-
choly people do not think that you are bet-
ter than your fellows, because your feelings
are more easily hurt. You are in error. It
is a species of madness which afflicts you
and which, if you encourage, will be the
bane of your lives.
	Shake it off! Be good-natured and cheer-
ful, fight your natures and win happiness
as the reward.
							STOIC.


THE thing which an active mind most
needs is a purpose and direction worthy of
its activity.
	TEAR from a woman's life all else that
she holds dear and leave her love, and she
is rich; load her with jewels, clothe her in
costly stuff, let slaves wait upon her and
courtiers bow, and she is but a pauper, if no
one loves her.
	POETS know, and statesmen ought to
know, it is by sentiment when well direct-
ed—as by sorrow when well used—great na-
tions live. When sentiment dies out and
mere prosaic calculations of loss and profit
take its place, then comes a Byzantine
epoch, a Chinese epoch, decrepitude and
slow decay.—Kingsley.


86		WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
		CORRESPONDENCE.

		SALT LAKE CITY, Oct. 13, 1876.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	I will tell you of a short chapter from
the lives of two of the women of Utah.
	Early Monday morning 18th of Sept. last,
Mrs. E. Howard and your correspondent
met at the Salt Lake depot, with satchels in
hand and preparation for journeying. In
the course of a couple of hours we reached
Ogden, where amid a great many people,
I thought certainly we had arrived at the
Metrapolis of the West. As we past the
hotels, we saw waiters hurrying about pre-
paring meals in such haste, as though ac-
tion was the only principle by which they
were allowed to live. We paid passage on
the Eastern bound train, and presently we
were seated in the cars, we heard something
said about an accident that had just hap-
pened, which we afterward read, of a man
dropping a double-barrel gun, and the dis-
charging of both barrels into a man and
woman who were walking along, and it was
thought the wounds would prove fatal, we
a few minutes previous had passed over the
same ground.
	At Peterson Station we were met by Bro.
Jesse Haven and wife, whom we accompa-
nied to their home at Enterprise, a small
place which had suffered, in times past,
from grasshoppers and frost, and considera-
ble destruction of cattle by the cars, but by
the energies of the people they have suc-
ceeded in establishing homes and have a
pleasant town-site, with a small white
school-house which seemed to over-look
everything else.
	In the afternoon we crossed Weber River
to Peterson settlement; Bishop Peterson's
son was lying sick with the typhoid fever,
but he had all faith that he should recover
in time to go to conference.
	We met in the evening with the sisters,
and I told them the object of our visit, that
we were traveling in the interest of the
Silk Association, and as Agents for the
WOMANS' EXPONENT and other sundry
items.
	Bishop Peterson related to us his success
in making a splendid article of cheese
which was done upon a co-operative princi-
ple; in two months and ten days, they had
made six thousand lbs. I told him I could
not see the policy of our importing cheese
when that of a superior quality could be
furnished at home.
	Five miles further at 10 a. m., on the
19th, we met with the sisters at Milton.
One particular feature of this place was a
nice house built and owned by the High
Priests. Here the people were all very ac-
tive in taking care of what crops the frost
had left them. We learned that all the
settlements through Morgan, Summit and
Wasatch Counties had been visited with a
cutting frost on the 9th of August, which
had naturally affected their wheat crops,
the altitude is such that their seasons are
very short, having late springs and early
falls; they were just threshing their grain
in many places where we visited. Half a
mile further, was Littleton. Bishop W. G.
Smith accompanied us on to South Morgan,
two miles ride brought us to Bro. Eding-
ton's. At four o'clock we met with a full
house and after meeting, Bishop Smith in-
vited the people to accompany him
to North Morgan; in the evening at 7
o'clock, we found another full house,
we returned to Bro. Eddington's and enjoy-
ed their hospitality for the night. In the
morning of the 20th, Bishop Smith came
along and accompanied us up Canyon Creek

[Column 2]
three miles to Richville, where we met the
people of the settlement in a meeting house
and about a million wasps. Nearly all the
meeting houses that we entered, as we
continued up the valley were inhabited
with them, also some dwelling houses.
	Two miles further, at West Porterville,
we held a meeting at two o'clock p. m.
Bishop Smith invited the people to accom-
pany us to East Porterville and attend a
meeting at 7 o'clock. The meeting was
held at Mrs A. Porter's the President of the
Relief Society. From this place, Bishop
Smith was obliged to return home; he told
us he felt our labors were effecting a great
deal of good among the people. There was
a crowded house both men and women and
we were listened to with attention and in-
terest.
	The morning of the 21st we came down
the Creek to Morgan City again. Went up
the Weber about a mile and a half and
came to little sunny "Round Valley" appar-
ently large enough to make half a dozen
good farms. Here we solicited and urged
the raising of the mulberry trees which to
all appearance could be raised successfully.
The sisters here, as in other country places
seemed to be endeared to the sunbonnet,
we endeavored to show them the utility of
making and wearing home-made straw hats.
Here we met Bishop Edward Hunter's son,
he had a nicely located piece of land, and
a foundation for what I should call a New
England farm.
	We continued up the Weber ten miles
over a very rough road to Croydon; on this
road is the "Devil's Slide," it is on the side
hill about half a mile long with a couple of
perpendicular rock walls about twenty feet
high leaving a space of a few feet be-
tween. We passed the tunnel which seem-
ed at the distance to be no more no less
than a black hole in the mountains
where some wild animal had his den.
From the bushes, as we traveled along,
we gathered choke-cherries, haws, and au-
tumn leaves. We arrived at Croydon feel-
ing very tired. There was a meeting at
four o'clock; after a refreshing repast of
tender beefsteak etc. at the house of Prest.
Ellen Thackeray (which I wish by the by
that half our young girls knew how to cook
as well,) and somewhat rested, our spirits
were more buoyant, and we hurriedly
improved our time; the sisters expressed a
regret for the brevity of our visit. At sun-
set we left for Henneferville, three miles
further, stayed all night at Bishop Rich-
en's. 
	We met with the Relief Society at 10 a.
m. The Prest. Anna Bunot had bur-
ried her infant twins; she seemed beloved
by all; and her spirit appeared as if it was
almost too feeble for this mundane sphere.
After meeting, the Bishop took us through
Echo to Coalville. We arrived later than
expected, and the meeting was nearly over;
but there was one appointed for the next
day at 10 a. m., at which time we assem-
bled with the Saints and enjoyed a refresh-
ing time, attended to business &c. We
then went three miles to Unionville and
met with the sisters at 2 p. m. The Presi-
dent, Ann Lowe, is sixty-five years of age,
is full of spirit and hope, the inhabitants
are scattered and she was obliged to walk
three miles to attend meeting, she also
walked six miles to attend a meeting at
Coalsville. The same evening we arrived
at Sister Susan S. Richards, at Wanship.
There we went to the Sabbath meeting, but
Sister Howard was quite ill.
	Monday 25th, at 10 a. m., we met with
the sisters at meeting, also visited a sick
sister. On entering one sisters house, we

[Column 3]
were interested with an evergreen vine
that was trained from a couple of windows
into the four corners of the room and from
thence forming a complete net work on the
ceiling. Three and a half miles further, at
Lockport, we met with the sisters at 2 p. m.
The girls had decorated the house with
lovely autumn leaves. Everything spoke
of order and neatness. The President,
Mrs. Bryant, is a sister to Mrs. Wheeler,
President of the Society of the 2nd Ward,
S. L. City. She was full of zeal and en-
ergy.
	We passed on to Peoa, four miles further,
and at 7 o'clock met the sisters in another
well filled house. We met in several pla-
ces where the floors were wet from recent
scrubbing. How unwise for delicate wo-
men and girls to assemble in halls, under
any circumstance, on wet floors, it causes
much lamentable sickness and distress.
Bishop Marchant, a jolly, good man,
seemed quite elated over our meeting; said
if we had been told the condition of the
settlement, we could not have made more
appropriate remarks. This shows that the
Lord is with His handmaidens to help them
in their weakness.
	On the 26th at 10 a. m., we were in Masell,
where we were met by the Bishop and held
a meeting in a private house. Went on to
Kamas, where the people assembled in a
large fine hall, but not yet properly seated.
The appearance of the place bespeaks that
the people have more energy, or their fac-
ulties are far in advance of some of the
settlements we have visited. The Bishop's
wife seems well adapted to lead out, is gen-
tle and kind, and co-operates with the en-
ergetic sisters she presides over. We as-
sembled with the sisters in their Society
House, at 10 a. m. on the 27th. The house
is made of logs nicely whitewashed, and
the floor covered with a carpet of their own
make. The sisters expressed themselves
well paid for meeting. We journeyed
seventeen miles further to Heber City, with
Bishop Samuel Atwood. The roads were
good, but as we went down many hundred
feet from the summit we could realize a
great change in the atmosphere. As we
traveled along our eyes wandered over the
picturesque scenery that lay on our way.
It seemed to me that the many thousand
miles over which I had traveled, I never be-
fore saw all nature clothed with such beauty;
every color of the rainbow could be seen
among the autumn leaves, and foliage that
covered the hills in groups, and clusters; it
appeared as if the God of Nature had been
forming bouquets to decorate the hills. Al-
though the air seemed soft and balmy as a
summer's morning, I found from the cold I
had taken from the wet floors that I had
quite lost my voice, and was obliged to talk
in a whisper. We reached Heber, which is
quite a city, with many well finished public
buildings, painted and grained, stores, etc;
great contrast to the farming towns. We
arrived at Bishop Hatch's. Relying upon
a higher power, and through the prayers of-
fered in my behalf, I was supported with
sufficient voice to address an audience one
hour and a half, in a large beautiful hall.
Sisters Howard also bore a strong testimony.
The Bishop appointed a meeting at 10 a.
m., on the 28th, for Sister Howard and my-
self to speak more particularly to the sisters,
after which we went to Midway about five
miles directly west of Heber, and a couple
of miles northwest of this place we visited
some great curiosities of Nature's works.
There were warm springs and many mounds
that had formed from the lime in the water,
as it boiled up over their pots. One large
hill was sending forth lime water over its


edge and down its side and still continu-
ing its work of building. At Midway, the
people were about one half Germans. This
place was particularly noticeable for some
very fine brick houses, the corners of which
were laid up of white lime stone rock and
then whitewashed which added very much
to the beauty of the buildings. Our even-
ing meeting was well attended, some peo-
ple having come from Charleston. Bro.
Watkins of Midway, has a quantity of canary
seed which he said could be raised here as
well as paying an expensive price for the
imported; he took us to Charleston and Bro.
Nymphas Murdock took us to Wallsburg,
where Sister Wall gathered her little flock
together at three p. m., and we talked to
them. At seven in the evening we met in
a beautiful, well-furnished meeting house,
built and kept expressly for worship, with
easy stationary seats. This was the last of
23 meetings which we held in twelve days
	Saturday we commenced our journey
down Provo Canyon, the roads were very
rough, but the waterfalls and the scenery
was altogether magnificent. We arrived
at home Sunday morning, Oct. 1st,
feeling we had accomplished some lit-
tle good.
						E. S. B.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.		87


FROM HAWAII.

	EXTRACTS FROM SEVERAL LETTERS BY
SISTER JANE E. MOLEN TO HER FRIENDS.

			LAIE, OAHU, June 19, 1876.
	We left Ogden May 10th; had a pleasant
ride on the cars, lasting two days and nights;
spent a day and a half in San Francisco.
We sailed on the Steamer Zealandia on the
24th; had a very successful voyage; arrived
at Honolulu June 1st where we met Bro.
A. L. Smith, waiting with conveyances to
bring us to this place, the distance being
thirty-two miles. We found a hearty wel-
come waiting us, both from our folks and
the natives.
	The natives seem well pleased with their
new President, Bro. Pack, and show great
love for Bro. Smith, who has done a great
deal towards bringing them together and
uniting them.
	I am well pleased with this place, also
with the people. The children show a great
desire to learn English.
	Sister Mildred Randal has started a nice
little school. I hope that I may be able to
continue the work and be an instrument in
the hands of the Lord in doing good.
	Sisters Woolley and Lambert wish kind
remembrance. They are good Latter-day
Saints, and feel to exercise patience and
faith.
	I think this a very healthy place. There
is a continuous breeze, from the sea, and one
does not feel the heat so much as at home.
If this place were our home, and our dear
friends were all here, I should think it beau-
tiful. We have a nice large school room,
larger than our school room at Hyde Park.
It is the meeting honse.
	I like the pupils; they are like our chil-
dren at home—some more studious than
others.
	The natives grow very pretty flowers.
The geranium grows wild here, pink, scar-
let, sweet-scented. Many of our choice
plants grow here without water except the
rain. There are but few of the natives, as
far as I have become acquainted, who care
enough for anything like refinement to grow
flowers. They are, mostly, the most de-
praved people that I ever heard of. I have


come to the conclusion that if a virtuous
people ever grows out of the people here, it
will be through their gathering to Utah.
There are a considerable number wanting to
go.
	I sometimes think how pleasant it would
be to meet with all my dear friends at home;
but I desire and pray that I may be patient,
and fulfill my mission honorably. It would
seem very lonesome when my husband is
away, but the Lord strengthens me and I
enjoy a peaceful spirit.
	This is a strange people. They are well
educated in their language. The little boys
and girls will read the newspapers as read-
ily as their parents can. Children twelve
years of age have good educations. The
Government compels them to go to school.
			To be continued.


SPELLING IN OLD TIMES.

	In the reign of Henry V. good spelling
and clean shirts were equally rare luxuries.
Leicester, says Disraeli, spelled his own
name in eight different methods, while the
family appellation of Millers, in deeds and
documents relating to the house, is spelled
in at least a dozen. Mainwaring passed
through 131 orthographical permutations,
and is even now, if spelling have aught to
do with pronunciation, spelled incorrectly
at last. The immortal bard himself, not to
speak of what others did for him, changed
his own mind some thirty times, according
to Halliwell, as to the letters and the se-
quence of the letters composing his illustri-
ous patronymic. Elizabeth wrote sovereign
in as many ways, as she knew languages—
that is, seven. The young Pretender, fol-
lowing his own sweet will, and entirely
free from any s[-]rvile bondage to the letter,
writes of his father as a certain Jems or
Gems. In those palmy days, when every
man was his own speller, when military ex-
aminations were not, little attonishment
would have been raised by such arbitrary
orthography as lately adorned the paper of
a candidate for one of her Majesty's appoint-
ments in the line. That candidate spelled
elegy leg, and ingeniously evolved pash-
shinger out of passenger. Much ingenui-
ty, nay imagination, inspired another, who
framed Indian eras out of engineers.
	But what are such trifling irregularities
as these to the caprice of—say her Grace
the Duchess of Norfolk? The Duchess was
one of the most accomplished ladies of the
sixteenth century, the friend of scholars,
the patron of literature. She wrote to the
Earl of Essex, thus:—"My ffary gode lord,
—her I sand you in tokyn hoff the neweyer
a glasse hoff Setyl set in Sellfer gyld. I
prayou tak hit An hy wer hable het showlde
be batar," &c. The patron of literature
has ingeniously contrived to spell I and it
each in two different ways in as many lines.
What this friend of scholars intended the
Earl to understand by Setyl is very obscure.
There is a Scotch word something like it,
signifying "a disease affecting sheep in the
side," but this the most accomplished lady
can scarcely have intended.—Cornhill Maga-
zine.


DAVID A. WELLS ON COMMERCIAL
			DEPRESSION.

	The following extract from the address
of David A. Wells, the well-known statis-
tician; delivered by him before the conven-
tion of social science, of which he is presi-
dent, and which recently met at Saratoga,
will, we think, be of value at this time in


furnishing some solution for the oft-repeat-
ed question "Why are commercial interests
so depressed?"
	It is evident from examination, that the
present condition of the country is not due
to natural causes. It is due to artificial
causes, and, as such, can be removed. The
first point that strikes attention is the uni-
versality of the present depression in busi-
ness. It is world-wide, though slight in
particular nations, as in France. It is the
greatest in the United States. This does
not teach however that the depression is
due entirely to causes acting universally,
but investigation will show that the general
result we recognize has been due rather to
the current action of very many causes,
originally, local, and which, if nations had
been isolated to the extent they were a
hundred years ago, would have been ex-
ceedingly limited, or endemic, in their in-
fluences, but which, under the close rela-
tionship into which nations have been
brought by improved means of intercom-
mnnication, have become all-pervading or
epidemic, in their influences. Of such gen
eral causes originating in one country, but
coming into action and producing disturb-
ances throughout the whole circle of civiliz-
ed states at nearly the same time, I can
think of only one that is entitled to be thus
classified, and that is the steady and exten-
sive introductoon and use of labor-saving
machinery and processes. A more import-
ant influence, however, is the aggregate
loss of capital which for ten or fifteen years
the world has been experiencing. These
losses are due to bad investments in railroad
bonds, the colossal war expenditures of the
world (the national debts of the world have
increased ten billion dollars since 1861,
making their aggregate now twenty-five
billion dollars,) the panics and mercantile
failures of the world, the brnkruptcy of
governments, and the enormous increase
of taxation, particularly in the United
States.
	Another agency, which has operated con-
jointly and even more powerfully than war
expenditures and an excessive irredeema-
ble currency to impair the aggregate of na-
tional abundance, by diverting labor from
productive to unproductive or less produc-
tive employments, and which is even now
in full continuance, has been, and is the
wholesale recognition and practical appli-
cation since 1860, of the doctrine of ex-
treme protection as a part of our national
fiscal policy. And the loss of capital and
the waste of labor which we have experi-
enced, and are now experiencing in conse-
quence of this policy, have contributed
more than any one thing, in my opinion, to
make us poor, and it will continue to keep
us so, so long as it is continued.—Eclectic
Magazine.


FLOWERS.

	THE best friends are those who stimulate
each other [--] do good.
	GREAT works are performed, not by
strength, but by perseverance.
	THE man who contents himself to-day
with that which he has will content himself
to-morrow with that which he may have.
	People who suppose that a good prayer is
preferred to a good act, doubtless imagine
that God has more hearing than eyesight.
The end, we fear, will show that they rea-
soned from false premises. The poor are
oftener prayed for than helped. The rea-
son is, we believe that air is cheaper than
bullion.


88		WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


WOMAN'S HEART.

	It is my opinion there is a period in the
early life of every true woman when moral
and intellectual growth seem, for a time to
cease. The vacant heart seeks for an occu-
pant. The intellect, having appropriated
aliment requisite to the growth of the un-
crowned feminine natures, feels the neces-
sity of more intimate companionship with
the masculine mind, to start it on its second
period of development. Here, at this point,
some stand for years, without making a
step in advance.
	Others marry, and astonish, in a few
brief years, by the development of higher
and holier emotions, their sweet tempers,
their new physical and mental beauty,
their high accomplishments, and their noble
womanhood, those whose blindness led
them to suppose women devoid of such lov-
able traits of character.
					MRS. DR. RHUGO.
S. L. City, Oct. 14, 1876.


HYGIENIC NOTES.

	Lime-water and milk is said to be a most useful
compound for sick or delicate infants, and also for
persons at a later period in life, when the functions
of digestion have been seriously impaired. A
tumbler of milk and four tablespoonfuls of lime-
water is about the requisite proportion for a grown
person, and will afford proper nourishment when
almost everything else fails.
	It is asserted upon the authority of persons of
great experience in sickness and disease, that in
many instances men and women who have suf-
fered from nervousness until their hands shake
like leaves on a tree in a windy day, and others
who have become so affected as to cause irregular-
ity of the pulsations of the heart which produces
undue excitement, have been effectually cured by
the daily use of celery eaten at meal times.
	A medical lady of some repute tells us that
thousands of people create lung disease from their
own willfulness and ignorance, from the effects of
a pressure on the lungs through the habit of sit-
ting, standing and walking with the head drawn
forward and stooping; this prevents the lungs from
filling with air naturally, as they otherwise would.
Stand erect and throw your shoulders back if you
would preserve your health and life.
	An excellent cough powder may be prepared
from skunk cabbage root, comfrey root and white
sugar. Take equal quantities of each, pulverized,
and mix together, and use one-half dram for a
dose in a little water three times a day, or morn-
ing and evening. The skunk cabbage is a peren-
nial plant growing in boggy swamps. The root
has anti-spasmodic properties and is very useful in
chronic colds, coughs and asthma, and should be
prepared as a powder.


HOUSEHOLD HINTS.

	To prepare a counterfeit cream for tea and cof-
fee, take one egg, one half cup of sugar, one half
cup of sweet milk, one half cup of cornstarch, beat
well together.
	White Fruit Cake.—Take one cup of butter, two
of sugar, one of sour milk, the whites of three
eggs, the whole of one egg, one teaspoonful of soda;
two of cream tartar, and one and a half cups of
raisins stoned and floured.
	Cookies.—Two cups of sugar, one of butter,
one egg, one half cup of sweet milk, two teaspoon-
fuls of cream tartar and one of soda, add nutmeg.
Take sufficient flour to mix, and roll out. Sprin-
kle sugar over the top and roll it in before cutting
them out. Put them on a paper to cool before
piling them up to store away.


Old-Fashioned Fried Cakes.—Take 1/2 cup sugar,
1/2 cup shortening, one pint sour milk, one teaspoon-
ful saleratus, season with allspice or nutmeg; mix
with sufficient flour, but not too hard. Have your
lard hot enough to make the cakes a darkish
brown. These cakes can be made very quickly,
and will keep a long time in cool weather and do
not get stale or dry.
	Home Prepared Citron for Cakes and Pud-
dings.—Take the citron, cut and prepare it in the
ordinary way except to boil the syrup until it is
quite thick, but instead of closing up the jars,
leave them open for the air to enter, taking, of
course, every precaution against dust or damp.
The preserve will then dry gradually, and you
have an excellent substitute for the imported arti-
cle, which will answ r the same purposes. Now
is the time of year, and there are plenty of citrons
for sale very cheap. Another year perhaps many
housekeepers knowing this recipe may raise their
own citrons at home.

WOMAN'S EXPONENT.
VOL. 5		SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, NOVEMBER 15, 1876		NO. 12.


For the Exponent.
				OBEDIENCE.

	How holy the joys from obedience springing,
	Eternity's treasures unfolding to view,
	New songs of salvation God's people are singing,
	Renewing their vows to be faithful and true.

	Yes; those who are truly God's kingdom adorning,
	 Will part in the First Resurrection obtain,
	Arising immortal on that happy morning,
	Like Joseph the prophet, with Jesus to reign.

	Love, virtue and peace in these homes are united,
	Angels rejoice when they minister there,
	Come let us press on since all are invited,
	And strive by obedience these blessings to share.

	Welcome to us, then the trials which heaven,
	In wisdom dispensing our pathways surround,
	Faithful and true till the promise is given,
	Which the righteous may claim, may we ever be
		found.
							ELLEN H. WALLACE.
Salt Lake City.


[COLUMN 1]

	For the EXPONENT.
WOMEN AS MANAGERS OF
BUSINESS.

BY BLANCHE BEECHWOOD.

	Order, thou eye of action! wanting thee,
	Wisdom works hoodwink'd in perplexity;
	Entangled reason trips at every pace,
	And truth, bespotted, puts on error's face.
		AARON HILL. 
	Many inferences are drawn in relation to
women as practical and methodical mana-
gers in business affairs. It seems the world
are only just becoming conscious that wo-
men possess the faculties to qualify them to
adjust such matters as relate in a general
way to the commonplace transactions, by
which trade and commerce are carried on.
The idea is a novel one, to some who have
not been cognizant of the growing status of
woman in relation to finance, and when any
phenomena in illustration of the fact of wo-
man's practicability clearly elucidates itself,
men open their eyes in wonder, and imme-
diately set their wits to work to solve the
problem in some exceptional way, instead
of attributing it to the real genius or inher-
ent qualities of woman's competency. It
seems perfectly consistent that women
should be able to arrange and carry out sys-
tematically and effectively plans of action
which would operate successfully in business
affairs. The many phases of woman's house-
hold work require order, method, prompt-
ness and executive ability; if women did
not understand, by habit and intuition, the
easiest, quickest and best modes of dispatch,
they could never bring quiet, peace and
harmony to the domestic habitation. How
much careful study, thought and practice
are brought into requisition by the compli-
cation of circumstances which every house-
hold develops. All these different entan-
glements by being unraveled, smoothed
and harmonized teach useful lessons, which
to the woman who knows how to apply the
knowledge she gains, so as to make it a
means to an end, works out or solves the
great problem of commercial interests by
the application of her own private expe-
rience in domestic economy. Who is there
that has to practise more thorough disci-
pline, or be more accurate in management
than the woman who is supervisor of her

[COLUMN 2]

own household affairs? The work which
devolves upon the mother and wife requires
the most perfect method in arranging so as
to ensure any degree of home-comfort, and
it will bring to its aid tact, energy and
skill, in order to evoke an organization com-
plete in the fulfilment.
	Then through this very process do women
become initiated into the very channels
which are the most available for the trans-
action of business; their capacities expand
with their facilities for action, and the very
elements which bring to the home circle the
genius of an inspiration for making every-
thing adaptable are those which will best
assist women in the transaction of affairs
which will eventually help them to estab-
lish industries and manufacturies for the
general interest of communities.
	The arrangement of family and house-
hold affairs requires as nice order and meth-
od, as does the management of a store, or
an office. In this respect there would no
doubt be as much difference in women, as
there is in men, and probably no more.
The woman who is not prompt and ener-
getic, is not the one who will succeed in
business, and in any and all of the private
walks in life will always cause more or less
confusion, dissatisfaction and dislikes. She
consumes the time of others and gives them
reason for thinking that she looks upon them
as not of sufficient consequence to make her
punctual; method is the soul of business;
and order in every and all departments of
business, or of life, is one of the most im-
portant matters to be impressed upon man
or woman. Pope says:
		"Order is heaven's first law; and this confess'd,
		Some are, and must be, greater than the rest."
	We often hear it said, that "to the busi-
ness man, time is money," and to the busi-
ness woman time is money, and so is
method.
	That there are women who look upon
their time as of very little consequence there
is no doubt, but are there not thousands of
men who waste their own time and that of
others in a more shameful and wicked man-
ner than women do? One need not go far
to prove this assertion; here on the corners
of the streets, in the stores, the saloons, the
hotels, on fences, steps, everywhere that
there is room enough for a knot of men to
collect, they are in the habit of gathering
to talk, smoke and so forth. There are
some who never seem to do anything else.
Men are very fond of saying women get to-
gether (even in their meetings) to gossip
and backbite, but in the name of common
sense, what do so many men huddle togeth-
er in little caucauses at the corners of streets,
etc., for? To discuss politics and the state
of the government, and a variety of other
interesting topics, so they say; but in my
opinion they would be doing the world a
great favor by going to work; and if they
cannot find any other employment, they
could help their wives, sisters or mothers
to cook their food and tend the babies, for
women not only never have any idle time,
but are often worked beyond their strength.
	There are a few individuals who summer
and winter may be seen loitering around
corners; wherever the sun shines in winter,
and where there is a shade in summer; how

[COLUMN 3]

they get their living is a mystery. Such
men are not capacitated for business of any
sort, any more than women who have not
been disciplined in habits of industry, tact,
accuracy and punctuality; yet they are men,
and they can vote, and discuss politics, and
carry the world on their shoulders, figura-
tively speaking, that is one might imagine
so to hear them talk.


NOTES AND NEWS.

	ABOUT ONE hundred specimens of an-
cient pottery, which were dug up in the
State of Missouri have been recently presen-
ted to the Museum of Yale College. It is sup-
posed they are about 2,000 years old. In
our opinion this is another testimony to
substantiate the validity of the Book of
Mormon.
	THE EMPEROR of Brazil is about to pub-
lish a book of travels. The first volume,
now finished, contains an account of his tour
through the United States. It will be pub-
lished in December in Paris. Although
written in Portugese, it has been translated
into English, French and German.
	THE SALE of the Old South Church in
Boston, which has been so much talked of
by the women of that city, has at last been
formally completed. The purchaser, Mr.
B. M. Pulsifer, gave a bond to the commit-
tee of ladies which if they raise the money
within a reasonable time will entitle them
to a deed of the property.
	THERE WAS a reception given to the Wo-
man's Congress which recently assembled
in Philadelphia by the Woman's Centennial
Committee. The affair was to all intents
and purposes a brilliant one of its kind,
and yet without any ostentation or display,
even in the matter of dress. Everything
being arranged with simplicity and ele-
gance. It is much to the credit of all wo-
men that this extraordinary occasion, for
an example, furnishes one incident notice-
able, and which, it is greatly to be hoped,
will be extensively imitated elsewhere; it
is this, there was no wine upon the table.
	AN OLD CUSTOM.—This season, the cere-
mony of opening the Nile, near Cairo,
Egypt, took place in the presence of the
principal ministers and officials and several
thousand persons. The ceremony is held
when the river attains a certain height.
An opening is cut to admit its fertilizing
waters to the numerous canals spread all
over lower Egypt. The effigy, of a woman
in gorgeous apparel, and bedecked with
jewelry, is floated down the stream, and
money is distributed to the natives present,
in token of the riches and abundance that
will result wherever the Nile flows.


LEAVES seem light, and useless, and idle,
and wavering, and changeable—they even
dance; yet God has made them part of the
oak. In so doing, he has given us a lesson,
not to deny the stout-heartedness within,
because we see the lightsomeness without.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT


R. S. REPORTS.

	The General Retrenchment Meeting, held
in the Fourteenth Ward Assembly Rooms,
Saturday, Oct. 28, was well attended. The
minutes read on that occasion from the dif-
ferent societies deserve praise; they were
full of wise and choice remarks from the
young members of the different or-
ganizations. The blessing, which have al-
ready accrued from these institutions can-
not be estimated. President Mrs. M. I
Horne spoke upon the grain question princi-
pally, suggesting to the sisters the propriety
of entering into some understanding that
would facilitate the accomplishment of the
object. Counselors E. R. Snow and Sarah
M. Kimball both spoke on this subject and
other general ones—the principles of eter-
nal life, the increase and multiplicity of
cares and burdens; said that those who were
not living in the line of their duty would
scarcely be able to endure all that was laid
upon them to bear. Counselor E. Howard
spoke in reference to her recent journey
among the Relief Societies, and the great
desire they manifested to hear instruction,
and the courtesy shown by Bishops and pre-
siding men in the places she visited. Coun-
selor B. W. Smith spoke of the woman's
store, of the great good to be derived from
an institution of home manufactures; if
carried out upon a systematic plan for the 
public weal.

WALLSBURG, WASATCH CO., Oct 31st.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	On Friday, Sept. 29, we had a pleasant
visit from Sisters E. Howard and E. S. Bar-
ney, who, by their teachings and instruc-
tion, greatly encouraged the sisters of this
little burg. Our Relief Society is in a
flourishing condition, and a majority of the
sisters are trying to live their religion.
	Wishing success to every enterprise for
the good of Zion, and the blessings of God
to attend you in your labors, as well as all
who are working for the spread of truth,
we remain your sisters in the Gospel,
	NANCY WALL, President,
		SARAH GLENN, Secretary.


[COLUMN 1]

BE CONSISTENT.
	The young are very apt to think that
mothers and aunts want to lecture them;
somehow even the boys are more on the
look-out for these prosy little side thrusts in
the form of advice from elderly women,
than from the fathers, uncles, or elder bro-
thers. One would naturally imbibe the
idea from these grounds that it was woman's
province to check, and restrain, and tone
down, through this lecturing process, the
extremes into which the young are apt to
be led. I love dearly to give little im-
promptu lectures to the young whenever I
get an opportunity, somehow it seems nat-
ural, although it may not be always pleas-
ant for the young folks. Many of them
run away from long sermons, and they never
read long articles on any of the subjecis
which are essential for them to understand,
so it is absolutely necessary for some one to
take them in hand and tell them in some
inadvertent manner, of their inconsisten-
cies.
	I do not know whether to address girls or
boys in this article (I'll try and make it
brief); they both need training, they get so
many false ideas of life, its duties and re-
sponsibilities. There are so many of them
that get the impression that when they are

[COLUMN 2]

older, and especially if they marry, they'll
find a balm for every wound, and that, like
the hero or heroine of a romance, the
troubles are all ended, and there will be not
more need of exertion to make onesself hap-
py, for bliss supreme will be the crowning
portion of married life.
	Now we would not for the world shatter
your little castle of fairy splendor, nor dim
its lustrous brilliancy, but we would only
ask you to be thoroughly consistent with
yourself and see whether you have so disci-
plined yourself in body and mind as to fit
you to fill a sphere more advanced than the
one in which you are now graded. Every
step we take higher requires careful prepa-
ration, and attention to its higher laws, just
as surely as does preferment in a school.
Our whole life is symbolical of a school; it
may well be termed one. True marriage is
a rank above single life, and is indeed to be
desired, but do not think for one moment
that you will not need to exercise all your
faculties to render you competent to fill
this higher position; just so surely as you
enter it without careful consideration of all
it involves, and a correct understanding of
its being another prolific source of manifold
developments, so surely will you have to
feel the sting of disappointment.
	If you have heretofore been nursed in
the lap of indulgence, and had every desire
of your childish heart gratified, then be-
fore you take into your keeping the affection
and welfare of either a husband, or wife,
pause—for you must be prepared to exer-
cise self-denial—school yourself well in this,
if you would not shed many bitter tears of
sorrow, if not remorse, when you find the
one to whom you have given so much (your
dear untutored self) requires you to be con-
sistent.
	My dear girls, don't think I am overdraw-
ing the picture. There is a way to be hap-
py, certainly, but that girl who marries to
become more happy, who does not know
how to create happiness in her father's house
is but illy prepared to make it in her new
and untried sphere. Cultivate all those
graces of mind and character that are en-
durable, not the "arts and wiles that charm"
but the solid and sterling qualities which
enhance the value of every good and true
woman, and are a more precious dower to
bring to the marriage altar than the wealth
of Croesus.
	I do not say men or women are to blame,
but that many erroneous ideas are entertained
on this subject; girls are very apt to think,
and they are not in fault, custom has taught
them, that their husbands must overlook all
their imperfections and weaknesses, that the
very fact of maternity is sufficient to entitle
them to all compassion and sympathy; but
how is it? Women find themselves totally
unqualified to enter upon these arduous du-
ties, which come perhaps almost unawares
upon them, and then they are ready enough
to come to mother, or aunt, or some good
friend they have proved, for advice and
comfort. Thank God for the advancement
of knowledge and wisdom and light upon
these subjects; that mothers have at last
realized the necessity of training their
daughters to comprehend the high and holy
offices of wife and motherhood.
	The idea that marriage of itself is to lift a
woman all at once above the necessity of
exertion, and that through this means one
obtains ease and happiness, cannot too soon
be dispelled, for such a delusion is sure to
result in unhappiness, domestic discomfort
and wretchedness, if not divorce. These
feelings are entirely incompatible with or-
dinary marriage relations. It is doubtless
owing to the false notions of complete and

[COLUMN 3]

perfect wedded bliss that there has been of
late years so many suits for divorce, and so
much dissatisfaction in the married state;
and much of this is owing to the excess of
novel reading, sensational and extravagant,
diametrically opposed to common sense
ideas. This generation profess great wis-
dom and enlightenment, and science is
making rapid strides, and one fancies that
mankind are approaching very near to per-
fection in many things, but there is still a
great deal to be guessed at in regard to the
assimilation of human nature in man and
woman. And no doubt while we live on
the earth we shall always have need of char-
ity for each other's shortcomings and incon-
sistencies. But if we can save young peo-
ple from any excess or error, which our ex-
perience has taught us to avoid, if they can
be induced to steer clear of breakers which
have wrecked those who had not been pru-
dently and carefully trained, let us do so
in all earnestness. And to the young peo-
ple we would say, look life straight in the
face and do not expect too much, but bear
in mind that every person is the architect
of the structure which surrounds life with
beauty, gladness and peace, or with misery
and squalor. If one wishes to build well,
let him carefully observe the architecture of
others, and cautiously lay a firm foundation
first, afterwards ornamenting and adorning,
and remember that it requires patience,
will and energy to complete any arduous
undertaking. In every part or portion of
life one essential item is strength, which
gives a firm support. However beautiful any
edifice may be, however charming any idea
or principle, if it lacks strength it is liable
to fall. Then, my dear young friends,
strengthen your lives by the development
of all the substantial qualities of head and
heart; and, girls, do not forget that mother
has had an experience which qualifies her
to give you just that counsel that will help
you to govern and control your ofttime
restlessness, and moderate your inconsis-
tencies.
	AUNT EM.


REFINEMENT.

	Refinement is not fastidiousness. It is
not luxury. It is nothing of this kind. It
is far removed from excess or waste. A
person truly refined will not squander or
needlessly consume anything. Refinement,
on the contrary, is always allied to simplic-
ity and a judicious and tasteful employ-
ment of the means of good and happiness
which is at command. It seeks to divest
itself of superfluities and aspires continu-
ally to the utmost purity. Refinement
leads to personal cleanliness and elegant
neatness, good taste and simplicity in dress.
All "loudness" or "flashiness" is repugnant
to its spirit. In its home and surroundings
—whether palatial, affluent, or humble—
the same chasteness and natural grace are
maintained. The abode of genuine refine-
ment and a mere pretender to it, are very
different. In the former you will find no
excess, gaudiness or false glittering; but the
latter abounds in it. In personal manner,
refinement is most conspicuous. A man of
refinement is always polite without flourish,
gentle without effeminacy, and considerate
without stiffness. Display and ceremony
are not identical with refinement and are
poor substitutes for it. There is, of course,
no refinement like that of the heart, which
impels its possessor to show on all occasions
a thoughtful and kindly regard for the feel-
ings of others. No adherence to etiquette
can compare with it for the spontaneous ob-
servation of true and gratifying politeness.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


For the Exponent
AARON'S ROD.

Hist! Jehovah speaks! Moses, his servant,
With his brother Aaron, bend to listen
To that voice, from which love had cast out fear—
They, waiting to receive, and then obey
The mandates from the voice of God:
"Lo! a miracle will Pharaoh ask of thee—
Then say to Aaron, take thy rod, and cast
It down before King Pharaoh's feet, and lo!
It shall become a serpent, 'neath his gazing eye."

Enthroned sits Pharaoh, servants waiting round,
When lo! the servants of a higher King,
A greater Potentate—but drop at once
All vain comparison, where none can be!
The servants of the God of earth and heaven,
The King of kings and Lord of Lords, present
Themselves before proud Egypt's king,
Bearing an edict from that God he brav'd,
But which he cannot with impunity resist.
No pompous forms, or courtly etiquette
Precede these men of God! but prompt to do,
surely and simply, the bidding of their King.
With brow serene, and calm, majestic mien
The brother of Moses, Aaron, lifts his arm,
Nerv'd with the Spirit of the living God!
And hurls his rod before King Pharaoh's eyes;
E'en that proud, stubborn spirit quails before
The mighty reptile springing into life
From out the mystic rod that left his hand;
To spring to life, alone its mission seemed,
And there it lay before king Pharaoh's eye,
And the eyes of Pharaoh's servants, alive:
A living, panting, breathing, monster thing,
Waiting the mission it was sent to fill
Then rose the passionate, wicked, daring king,
And called his sorcerers and his wisest men,
Magicians of proud Egypt's magic realm,
And bade them use their cabalistic charms;
In like manner, they with their enchantments,
Cast down, every man to earth, his rod;
When lo! they sprang to life, and serpents they
	became.
Marvelous power of infernal art!
The mind can scarcely grasp the horror
Such a scene could throw around, and cause
The stoutest heart and eye to shake and quail.
Pharaoh exults, and feels his art equals
The God of Moses, and of Aaron too.
But wait! not long has he for exultation
Before that serpent God has sent, by him
Chosen and ordained, and fitted by Himself
To show His wonders to the world of men,
The mighty reptile opens wide his jaws
And swallows up the puny worms around!
That God still lives! His servants live to serve
His bidding, and to work His will; and He
Again has spoken, in these latter times,
And in another dispensation—Yes,
Pharaohs still are on the earth, and Moses
And his brother Aaron also have their types
Upon this earth of God! Yes, blessed truth!
Holy writ abounds in types and parables—
And in my simple mind a thought springs up,
Which takes a form embodied, palpable—
I see with Daniel's eye—or rather,
Reading him, I drink into his spirit,
And clearly see that in the Latter days
A kingdom shall arise, and stand forever!
And that it shall consume and break in pieces
All other kingdoms, as the rod of Aaron
Which became a living thing that swallow'd up
The counterparts and counterfeits of Satan.
So shall the last days' work gather up
All things in One, and that One shall be Eternal,
	HANNAH T. KING.
S. L. City.


[COLUMN 1]

WOMEN IN LITERATURE.

	The above heading is that of an article
in "The Woman's Journal" of Oct. 28th,
read at the recent Woman's Congress. We
have copied a few scattering paragraphs
from it as illustrative of our own ideas on
the subject. The article in its complete
form is an excellent one, is highly interest-
ing and contains many excellent sugges-
tions, and plain and truthful statements in
relation to the subject.

	Through all the ages, exceptional women
have been poets, historians and novelists.
But if we look back only for thirty years,
we will realize that it is only in our own

[COLUMN 2]

generation that women have obtained a rec-
ognized and a thoroughly respected posi-
tion in literature. The term "blue stock-
ing" applied to all women who used the
pen, did not lose its sting until a very re-
cent period. And it was more than hinted
that such women not only had ink-blacked
fingers, uncombed hair, and slipshod feet,
but that they must necessarily be neglect-
ful of all recognized womanly duties.
	Thirty years ago there were two or three
woman editors in the world. To-day there
are scores of them; while reporters and spec-
ial correspondents of the same sex are like
daisies of the field for multitude.
	The field of literature is conquered for
women. There are no longer bars or ob-
structions of any sort in the way. A wo-
man who has anything to say, is privileged
to say it; and if it is worth hearing, the
world will lend an attentive ear.
	Women also tell us that because they are
wives and mothers they have neither time
nor opportunity, What folly! Have not our
best women-writers been wives and moth-
ers? And are not our men-writers husbands
and fathers? If they are not they should be, if
they would gain the clearest, truest percep-
tion of human affairs, so that they may be
qualified to speak words of wisdom to the
world.
	First of all it will be well to disabuse wo-
men in general, of their erroneous ideas
concerning a literary career. A woman
must not expect to find any play when she
adopts literature for a profession. A prac-
tical writer leads a life of BONA FIDE drud-
gery. She may, now and then, find her pet
poems, or finely written stories or essays, ac-
cepted and paid for; but if she would make
an actual living of the business she mnst
devote herself to it, just as she would to 
dress-making or dish-washing.  The profes-
sional writer must compel the inspiration,
or write without it.  A woman who writes
for a living must hold herself in readiness
at any time and at all times. She must be
ready to write upon any subject, and in any
style. She must send a poem by return
mail, if required; or write up a whole geo-
graphical division, without previous knowl-
edge of it, and with nothing but a guide-
book and a dictionary of dates for referen-
ces; and yet have her article interesting,
and read as if unlimited information was
withheld solely for want of space. If she
be a reporter, she must be able to write on
the wing, and in the midst of crowds and
confusion, which would bewilder the intel-
ligence of either the ordinary man or wo-
man. If she be a correspondent she must
be at her pen, headache or no headache, when
her more favored companions are resting
from the fatigues of journeying or sight-
seeing. She cannot afford to be either
wearied or ill until after the inevitable let-
ter has been written and posted.
	Next to be considered is the kind of
training a woman needs. She must have
had experience in life. Without it she will
have nothing of value to offer. We never
learn from the experience of others as we
do from our own.	There-
fore until we shall have had a moral, emo-
tional or intellectual history, we can have
little to say worth hearing.

	There is no trade or profession which re-
quires so long practice before it becomes re-
munerative as that of literature. A wo-
man would have time to learn any manual
occupation twice, while she is preparing
herself for a frequently doubtful, and al-
ways precarious career in literature; I am
speaking absolute truth; and I wish every

[COLUMN 3]

woman who has founded false hopes on her
pen, might hear and heed me. There
would be fewer disappointed women in con-
sequence.
	After all this is surmounted, comes the
next difficulty. Has the woman anything
to say? Has she any fresh earnest thoughts
of her own, that the world will care to
hear? Or has she the happy faculty of ab-
sorbing, assimilating, combining and popu-
larizing the abstract and abstruse thoughts
of others? If she can say yes to any and all
of these questions then God speed her.
	Granted that a woman possesses the in-
tellectual culture, the practical experience
with the pen, and ideas; now comes the last
question; does she feel an irrepressible de-
sire to write? Can fancied want of time
prevent her? Do difficulties of any sort
hinder her? Can discouragement daunt
her? If so the crown of authorship was
never destined to rest upon her brow. If
she is inclined to fritter away her time in
trifles, let her fritter it away—her vocation
is not writing. If she is not actuated by a
powerful overwhelming impulse to write—
an impulse which will not be denied, and
which causes her thoughts to rest upon her
like undesirable burdens, which can only
be shaken off by the aid of the pen—and
which compels her to commit them to pa-
per in spite of every obstacle—she has not
the strength of character, and the fervor of
inspiration, which indicate that writing is
her especial undoubted vocation. The writ-
er will write and must write. Denial, ob-
stacles, difficulties, hindrances, only devel-
op more strongly the power which accumu-
lated behind them,
	Those are the men and the women to
whom the angel says "write" and when
they have written, the world will gladly
read what they have to say.
	E. B. DUFFEY.


Among the anecdotes of George Sands
with which the Paris papers have been re-
cently filled, the following is quite piquant.
"In the modest dwelling at Nohant, where
she died, her hospitality was not more deli-
cate than ample, and it not unfrequently
happened that a guest arrived and made
himself at home without having any claim
upon the author of "Mauprat" stronger
than the friendly commendation of an ac-
quaintance. One day such a visitor arrived
—call him Bakor—and from the moment he
crossed the threshold comported himself
with altogether Egyptian ease. He was
passionately addicted to cabbage, and in-
structed the cook to prepare for dinner on
the day of his arrival an immense dish of
that vegetable, which he conscientiously
devoured. Next day there was cabbage in
the soups and cabbage in the stew, and by
the third day the whole culinary depart-
ment, was conducted on the basis of cab-
bage. The mistress of the household, not a
little surprised, made inquiries, and dis-
covered that M. Bakor was responsible for
the revolution. After a week's stay, the
guest announced his intention of departing,
and just before starting waited upon his
hostess to present his respects. He found
her in the garden—the kitchen garden.
'Madame,' he said, 'may I not hope that,
after having been the guest of so distin-
guished an entertainer, I shall not be suf-
fered to depart without a souvenir of the
visit?' Madame Sands looked at him with
surprise; and then, inspired with a wicked
idea, called the gardener—'Baptiste!' The
gardener hastened to her. 'With pleasure,'
she said to M. Bakor; adding to the gar-
dener, 'Baptiste, one of your finest cab-
bages, for this gentleman.
