SALT LAKE CITY, NOV. 15, 1876.


TEMPORAL SALVATION.

	The above phrase has been quite a hobby
with many practical people in this church and
kingdom. Now the attention of, at least the
women of this people, is called to the subject
of a temporal salvation in a real and tangible
form: the buying and saving of grain. Do not
neglect the opportunity of commencing in this
enterprise if you would preserve yourselves
and families from want. Whatever the future
has in store the contemplation of it will be
more satisfactory to every prudent, far-seeing
person, if there is a good supply of the needful
on hand in the form of breadstuff.
	Whatever lack there may be, there is none
to equal the scarcity of the food which strength-
ens and imparts life. If history repeats itself,
which wise men aver, and the masses of man-
kind believe, then somewhere in the future, as
in the past, will famine with all her train of
attendant miseries and consequences, spread
blight and desolation in her path.
	This people were driven out beyond the
boundaries of civilization by the hands of their
enemies, but God works in a mysterious man,
ner, and who knows, but even as Joseph was
sold into Egypt by wicked and false brethren,
yet eventually it proved their salvation, and
he their temporal savior, to preserve them and
all their father's household from starvation by
famine; even so, perhaps, may the Lord prove
His watchcare over this people, by giving to
them the opportunity of heaping coals of fire
on the heads of those who sought to betray
them into the power of savages and wild
beasts.
	It has been prophesied, scores of times, to
different ones of the Latter-day Saints, that
their relatives and friends who cast them out
and scorned them, should yet come begging
for bread; then be wise, and prepare yourselves
with bread in abundance to feed the hungry.
It is something that will always be marketable
and there is little danger of its becoming a
drug in the market even should there be plen-
tiful harvests in the future for seven years to
come; surely there is as much faith and confi-
dence in the word of the Lord now, as there
was in the days of the Egyptians.
	The sisters have had the opportunity given
them to lead out in this noble work; let it not
be said they lack ability or ingenuity to devise
methods of action. [handwritten] E. B. WELLS


STORE UP GRAIN.

	AT THE regular General Retrenchment
meeting, held in the Fourteenth Ward Assem-
bly Rooms, Saturday Nov. 11, the subject of
arrangements in regard to the Grain Question
now being agitated, was brought before the


meeting. Counselor E. R. Snow, who was
presiding over the meeting in the absence of
President Mrs. M. I. Horne, proposed that
the officers of the several Relief Socities act
in the capacity of a committee to collect means
to buy and store up grain; that besides these
committees of separate organizations in order
to facilitate the work, there should also be a
Central Committee for counsel and reference
and to take cognizance of all these grain mat-
ters, that there might be perfect order and a
united movement for the universal good of all
interested. She read a communication from
Bishop R. W. Burton in respect to storing the
grain in the General Tithing Office. The dif-
ferent wards that have not suitable storehouses
can store their grain there, but will be expect-
ed to attend to the arrangement themselves, as
the hands employed there have no spare time.
	Miss E. R. Snow proposed to the assembly,
Mrs. Emmeline B. Wells, as chairman of the
Central Committee; no objection being made,
the motion was seconded and carried unani-
mously. It was then moved that the chair-
man choose a committee of two to act with her
in this capacity constituting the Central Com-
mittee; accordingly Miss Eliza R. Snow and
Mrs. Bathsheba W. Smith were selected and
the unanimous vote of the assembly given to
sustain them.
	It is not possible for too much to be said on
this important subject to inspire zeal and ear-
nest efforts in the matter; and let every exer-
tion be made to secure grain immediately. The
business demands careful thought and consid-
eration, for it is one that involves great conse-
quences. That God will impart to his daugh-
ters great wisdom to aid them in this vast and
noble enterprise is our earnest and humble
prayer.
	The Central Committee will expect to be
kept posted in regard to the proceedings of the
different ward committees, as also of the ar-
rangements in the country places, that there
may be a concert of action, which will give
power and strength to this unparalleled move-
ment, and a complete system of management.
				[handwritten] E. B. Wells


WOMAN'S COMMISSION HOUSE.

	The Relief Society Mercantile Association
have established a house for transacting busi-
ness in all home products and manufactures
on altogether a different basis to any other
here, or of which we have any knowledge. It
is considered by all practical persons, some-
thing requiring wisdom and experience, as well
as capital, to enter upon any mercantile busi-
ness transaction and make it successful; but to
do this without capital and without any ap-
propriation being made, to take hold of it
earnestly and without any expectation of re-
muneration, is something that would seem
entirely inconsistent to any one except a Lat-
ter-day Saint; and that women are found ready
to step forward, and for the sake of establish-
ing a principle which they believe will develop
into the ultimate good of the community, give
their time and ability to this work and keep
themselves, seems no more nor less than a mir-
acle. At any rate, it proves the devotion and
self-sacrifice of women to a very great degree.
When men go into business of almost any sort,
they anticipate, at least, a certain percentage
for their time and labor; but this store is be-
ing established to create a fund to sustain
itself: meantime, those who work for its wel-
fare are working for the Institution without
money and without price. This is certainly
laboring for the building up of Zion. The sis-
ters have commenced by selling goods on com-
mission, and it is to be hoped this people will
sustain the movement of this united effort,
both by their custom and influence.


"OUR OWN."

	Inasmuch as our kind readers were informed
concerning the coming of our little daughter,
it seems appropriate that they should also be
made acquainted with the event of her depart-
ure.
	Mary Greene Richards, our "Household
Treasure," left her earthly home for a safer
and better one, on Monday, the 6th inst., about
half past 10 p. m.. a brief illness with scarlet
fever being the occasion. She was two years,
four months and eleven days old.
	It is not our object in making this announce-
ment, to excite the sympathy of friends.
Rather would we offer words of consolation to
those who mourn and grieve.
	A short time ago, for parents to bury their
little ones, seemed to us among the most try-
ing and terrible things that commonly occur.
But in passing through the ordeal, a new gleam
of the true Gospel light seems to have beamed
upon our understanding. The Lord in great
mercy has sent to us, the Holy Ghost, the
Comforter. It now appears to us, that the
Latter-day Saints, with the knowledge they
have, and may have by seeking it, should
cease to think and speak of these things as
great misfortunes and irreparable losses; and
rather regard them as the dealings of an al-
wise God, Whose hand must be confessed in
all things. We have not lost our little girl.
She is safe in the best possible keeping. And
if we remain faithful and worthy of her pure
remembrance, we know she will sweetly and
patiently await our coming. We shall receive
her again, not only in spirit but, in the morn-
ing of the First Resurrection, the same beloved
and lovely little form which now rests in the
quiet grave, will come forth, filled with glory
and immortality and eternal life.
	Oh priceless hope! Oh sweetest, best assur-
ance! Mothers, our pure, bright gifts from
God, our precious babies, our treasures, our
darlings—although they slip away from our
clinging embraces and leave our arms all
empty for a time—are still OUR OWN.


HOME AFFAIRS.

	PRESIDENT BRIGHAM YOUNG and party left
Salt Lake City Wednesday morning, Nov. 1,
enroute for St. George, and arrived at their des-
tination Friday evening, Nov. 10, all well and
having had a pleasant journey. The President
designs spending the winter in St. George.
	NO POSITIVE conclusion has been arrived
at as yet in regard to the presidential election.
It is one of the closest contests known. It is
impossible now to guess how the scale will turn.
As Utah has no voice in the matter she will
not in this crisis be able to render the country
any service, but should Utah become a State
her bearing would be a noticeable one, for her
people are united and they would by their con-
centrated efforts be a strong power for the side
they espoused.
	THE WOMEN of Utah are about to publish
a book representing them truthfully in the
past and present; this book will contain
many interesting incidents connected with the


[Column 1]
lives of different individuals who have been
extensively engaged in establishing and as-
sisting in building up, the church and king-
dom of God, and carrying out the sacred prin-
ciples and ordinances given by divine revela-
tion. The book will be written by Mr. E. W.
Tullidge author of the "Life of Brigham
Young, or Utah and Her Founders;" Mr. Tul-
lidge is a brilliant writer, a man naturally gift-
ed with such qualities as make an author en-
tertaining, and has a very clear and happy style
of portraying his subjects. It is expected the
book will be published almost immediately.
Some chapters have been already written.
The women of Utah should all have an interest
in this book, as it will probably throw alto-
gether a different light upon their lives and
motives of actions.
	A MOST DISTRESSING accident occurred in
this city, Friday morning, Nov. 10, to a little
boy about four years of age; son of R. V. Mor-
ris; nineteenth ward. He, with his brother, a
little older than himself was crossing the street
from the corner of the Deseret News Building,
to the opposite one, and were trying to avoid
being run over by a wagon just then passing the
car track, when the car came along just catch-
ing the child and throwing him under the
wheels; one wheel passed over his legs just be-
low the knee; one leg was almost severed and
the other very badly cut. The broken leg was
amputated almost directly by Dr. Benedict, to
whose office the child was immediately con-
veyed. All the assistance possible was ren-
dered momentarily, and the sad news carried
to the afflicted parents. It is expected the
child will recover, as he is to all appearance as
comfortable as possible under the circumstan-
ces. We extend to the agonized parents our
heartfelt sympathies and trust our Heavenly
Father will soften the sudden blow, and re-
store their dear one to health.
	It is said by those who were witnesses of this
sad accident, no blame could be rightfully at-
tached to the driver of the car, who is over-
whelmed with sorrow that such a fearful catas-
trophe should have occurred.
	ON MONDAY evening Nov. 6th, the regular
monthly meeting of the Sunday School Union
was held in the Council House. The meeting
was crowded and great interest was manifested
in the subjects discussed. One item brought
before the meeting was the raising of means
for the Floral Committee, to be used to decor-
ate the Tabernacle for the yearly Sunday
School Jubilee. One scheme proposed was to
raise the money by means of a party, but as
this seemed impracticable in consideration of
various circumstances, it was decided to raise
it by donations of five dollars each, from the 
several ward Sunday Schools; and as most of
these number about one hundred pupils, it
would only amount to about five cents apiece.
The subject of suitable singing books, and ap-
propriate books for pupils was also dilated up-
on, but no definite conclusion arrived at. This
subject is worthy the attention of all who are
interested in the advancement of the kingdom
of God. The music of any people is an inspir-
ation, and the children of Zion should receive
the highest cultivation in this inspirational
art. There is a pathos and melody imparted
by the songs of little children which often-
times strikes a chord in the human soul, where
all the eloquence of oratory has failed to find
access. Then this people of all the nations of
[-]he earth should have songs, hymns, and an-
thems adapted to the ideas and principles
which they hold so sacred.
	SERICULTURE.—The Deseret Silk Associa-

[Column 2]
tion met at the Firemen's Hall, Wednesday,
Nov. 1st. Mrs. Zina D. Young presiding.
	Mrs. young said that the reel had arrived
and was now being worked in Logan, by an
Italian lady, who, previous to coming to Utah,
had worked at the business from early youth,
and was a first-class reeler; also that Miss Mary
A. Rockwood had gone from this city to take
lessons, so as to be able to reel silk here and
teach others, and thus extend it through all
the settlements in the Territory. Mrs. Young
was in hopes that she would have some speci-
mens of silk at our next meeting. Now was
the time to plant out our trees or cuttings.
She urged those who were doing so to put
out a few more than they needed for them-
selves, as there might be others that would
want some, and thus keep extending this
branch of home industry.
	Mrs. Barney said that we had got a good
foundation laid, and hoped that we would keep
on building until it became a mighty struct-
ure. She felt satisfied with what had already
been done; said it was reported that the so-
ciety were only giving one dollar per pound
for cocoons, which was a mistake; they are
giving two dollars per pound.
	Mrs. E. Baker said that she was used to
working among silk, and could teach seven
branches from the cocoon.	The question was asked, where can we get
our trees and cuttings?
	Mrs. Zina D. Young said that she had been
informed that Mr. John Reading, of this city,
could furnish them at very reasonable prices.
	Meeting adjourned until the first Wednesday
in December, at this place.
					R. SIMPSON, Secretary.
		DEATH OF COL. JACKSON.
	A dispatch from St. George announces the
death there, Monday morning Nov. 1st, of
Col. Alden A. M. Jackson. On the 15th
of last February he was first struck with ap-
poplexy, which affiiction has now proved fatal.
Col. Jackson was a Colonel in the regular army
under Gen. Scott. and subsequently located in
Southern California, where he practiced law as
an attorney. In 1860 or [-]61 he removed to St.
George, where he resided up to the time of his
death. He was an intelligent, highly respect-
ed citizen, whose death will be mourned by
many warm friends. He was sixty nine years
of age.—Ex.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.		93


NOTICE.

	The Woman's Commission House, recently establish-
ed under the auspices of the Relief Society Woman's
Mercantile Association, is now transacting business on
commission at the Old Constitution Buildings, opposite
Z. C. M. I. Their stock consists entirely of home-
manufactures. They are well supplied with plaid, lin-
seys and waterproofs for winter dresses, wraps etc; and
shawls large and small, stockings and socks of all sizes
knit with machine or by hand, baby's socks, crochet
cuffs or wristers large and small. A dressmaker is
there, ready to make up material to order. There is a
good supply of men's and boys wear, of all the differ-
ent varieties manufactured here; an excellent tailor to
do all kinds of work in that line. There is also a full
stock of hats, straw and felt, and a millinerto attend to
that part of the work. They have also various other
articles of wear and use. All the standard church
books. Homemade soap, Jamaica ginger, ink, black-
ing and vinegar, also butter, cheese, and eggs. Among
the things noticeable are the lace and silk productions,
some of which are exquisite. The patronage of the
people is solicited for this institution, that Utah may


sustain by united effort the industry which is the pros-
pective wealth of her people. The women of this Ter-
ritory are making this almost unprecedented movement
to concentrate their exertions in one grand enterprise to
become self-sustaining, that they may free themselves
as a people, as soon as possible, from the shackles of
Babylon.


MIXED EDUCATION.

	Among the good things which will assur-
edly grow out of the "Woman's Rights"
clamor will be an enlargement of the sphere
of female education, giving the girls equal
opportunities with their brothers to acquire
knowledge and achieve scholastic honors;
and beyond this to attend the same schools,
and thus receive and confer the blessings
which must always attend upon intelligent
and prudent intercourse of the sexes. If
there is one idea as to education more falla-
cious than all others, it is that, to preserve
purity and healthful intellectual progress,
the sexes must be separated. As well
might one argue that there should be no
"mixed" families—when the commonest
observation shows that the most symmetri-
cally developed men and women are those
whose lives have been rounded and toned
by early and familiar contact with the op-
posite sex. A boy who has no sister to love
or care for is quite sure to go to one of two
extremes—he will either, in his yearning
for such companionship, wholly overrate its
beneficence or become inflated with an un-
due sense of his superiority. Nothing is
better for a self-contained coxcomb than to
have his vanity taken out of him by a
smart girl—and there are so many of them
who can do it! A noble, whole-souled boy
is proud of an intelligent sister; and, unless
his good sense is tampered with by that
most unjust but powerful agent, ridicule, he
will never dream that it is beneath him to
counsel on even terms with her, or to ac-
cept and act upon her advice. And what
is true of families is equally true of schools.
There is no one good reason why the sexes
should not be educated together; while in
support of the mixed system reasons are as
plenty as black berries.—Selected.


"PARADISE LOST."

	M. Edmond Scherer, a French writer,
gives the following criticism of Milton's
"Paradise Lost" in a recent work:—"'Para-
dise Lost' is a false, grotesque, tiresome
poem; not one reader in a hundred can go
without smiling through the ninth and
tenth books, or without yawning through
the eleventh and twelfth; it does not hold to-
gether; it is a pyramid balancing on its
point—the most frightful of problems re-
solved by the most puerile of means. And
yet, 'Paradise Lost' is immortal. It lives
in virtue of some episodes which will re-
main forever famous. In opposition to
Dante, whom we must read altogether; if
we wish really to possess his beauties, we
must read Milton only in fragments. But
these fragments are part of the poetic pat-
rimony of the English race."


WHAT ARE TAXES?

	THE axle on which the wheels of the
State coach turn.
	Periodical bleeding, prescribed by Gov-
ernment.
	Feathers plucked from all birds to line
the nests of a few.
	The Nation's little account for board and
lodging.


94		WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
				EXTRACTS.

	MUSIC AND WOMAN AS A MUSICIAN.

	(READ AT THE WOMAN'S CONGRESS.)

	BY MADAME FANNY RAYMOND RITTER.

	Notwithstanding the eulogiums of the
Greeks, music, as we understand it scarcely
had an existence prior to the establishment
of Christianity; its feeble germ, nursed in the
rocks and caverns where the early Chris-
tians took refuge, fanned by the sighs and
watered by the tears of the persecuted, de-
veloped into existence as the Ambrosian
and Gregorian-chants, and spread through
storm and transition into the life of the
people, as the troubadour, minstrel, and
folk-songs, and dance music, until it gradu-
ally became what it now is, the reigning art
of the present day, the most consummate
flower of modern civilization and artistic
culture.
	It is the most transcendental of all arts,
for it is a purely metaphysical outward man-
ifestation of the inward soul; it is the most
complex of all arts—vague, indefinite and
yet infinitely precise; it is the most ideal of
all arts, for—almost a religion in itself—it
is the exquisite embodiment of unshaken
faith in continual progress towards perfec-
tion. In the evanescent, intangible form of
music, from material the smallest, from pos-
sibilities the most vast, the human heart
and mind has gradually evoked a language,
an art, a science, compared with which the
miraculous, fabulous creations of the ma-
gicians of antiquity appear pale, cold,
empty, and meaningless.
	Woman's practical career as a musician
only began with the invention of the opera,
about 1600. It was not until her superior-
ity as an actress and singer had been unde-
niably and triumphantly established on the
stage, that she was allowed to resume her
musical participation in church services.
	And what a position as an artiste, wo-
man has established for herself during the
past two hundred years! The very names
of those songstresses whose fame still echoes
through the halls of musical history, are in
themselves romances, recalling, as they do,
the charms, gifts, caprices, accomplishments,
charities, errors, virtues, and adventures of
their possessors.
	One of the first of these ladies mentioned
in history, is Vittoria Archilei, a musician
at the court of Florence in 1600, who took
part in the first opera that was represented
in public. Who has not heard of Fanstina
Bordoni, born in 1700, a songstress in whose
name medals were struck, while societies
were founded in honor of her! Her rival,
Regina Mingotti, whose portrait, painted
by Rosalba, now stands in the Dresden gal-
lery, astonished Dr. Budney by her fresh-
ness of voice at an advanced age, as well as
by her power of conversing equally well in
five languages. Madame Mara, the favor-
ite singer of Frederick the Great, and Marie
Antoinette, delighted Europe for nearly fif-
teen years; Caterina Gabrielli, the pupil of
Metastasio, excited her audiences to alter-
nate frenzies of admiration and anger by
her voice, beauty, and caprice. It was she,
who, when Catherine of Russia complained
that her salary was higher than that of the
Field Marshals of the Empire, replied
"Let your Majesty try to make the Field
Marshals sing in my place, then!" Mad-
ame Catalani, born in 1779, possessed a
trumpet like power of voice. In London,
she received twelve hundred dollars for
singing the solo in "God Save the King,"
and twelve thousand dollars for assisting

[Column 2]
at a single musical festival. Mrs. Billing-
ton was accused by the superstitious Neapol-
itans, of causing, by her wonderful voice;
the eruption of Mount Vesuvius in 1794;
M. Thiers has translated her autobiography
into French. Mrs. Sheridan, wife of the
dramatist, charmed by her poetic gifts and
personal graces, almost as much as with her
thrilling voice.
	Though we find little trace of Woman in
the annals of composition, that songstresses
existed, capable of interpreting the works of
composers in an almost independent man-
ner, the scores of old operas prove. In
many of these, the melody, is reduced to a
mere thread, in order to give the songstress
perfect liberty in varying the theme accord-
ing to the exigencies of the action, and the
passion of the poetry she had to interpret.
And what may she not have accomplished
in a secondary character, as friend, consoler,
inspirer of the composer? Would Goethe
have accomplished all that he did, if, in his
Weimar circle, he had not found such wo-
men as the Duchess Amalie, Mdme. von
Stein, Bettina, and the singer Corona
Schraeter? Who does nor recall the exalted
friendships of Vittoria Colonna and Mich-
ael Angelo, of the Countess of Albany and
Alfieri, and many other interesting exam-
ples? So, among the friends of the compo-
sers, Mdme. von Breuning was a sort of in-
tellectual mother to Beethoven, in her kind-
ness to and care for him; Mdlle Boselli was
Haydn's inspirer; Mozarts wife, whose
name, Constance, he gave to the heroine
of his opera, "The elopement from the se-
raglio," was his best friend; the closest in-
tellectual comradeship existed between
Mendelssohn and his sister, Mdme. Hen-
sel; and the list of women who have made
a beneficent and inspiring use of their in-
fluence over composers, is a long one, through
less so than that of the lovers and friends
of the poets and painters.
	It is, nevertheless, difficult for the most
ardent advocate of Woman's progress, to
point to such a galaxy of celebrities among
feminine composers, as may be placed, with-
out losing their brilliancy, beside the names
that add lustre to womanhood in other
branches of art, and in literature. In com-
position we cannot boast stars of such pre-
eminent distinction as Sappho, Aspasia; Hel-
oise, Vittoria Colonna, Madame de Stael,
Dora d'Istria, Mrs. Siddons,, Fernan Cabal-
lero, Miss Cushman, Miss Bronte, Rosa
Bonheur, Miss Thompson, Mrs. Browning,
Mrs. Lewes, and the rest. The list of fem-
inine composers is a brief one, and most of
its members are now living. There was
Leopoldine Blahetka, daughter of a profes-
sor of mathematics in Vienna, a famous pi-
aniste who published more than seventy
songs and pianoforte pieces, some of which
were greatly admired by Beethoven; Jos-
ephine Lang, the friend of Mendelsshon;
who composed many charming songs; Mad-
ame Hensel, the sister of Mendelsshon;
Louise Puget, whose vocal romances en-
joyed an enormous popularity in France;
Elise Polko, who, carefully trained as a
singer, lost her voice prematurely, and
wrote many pretty novelettes, and now ap-
pears before the world as a song composer;
Madame Dolby and Virginia Gabriel, the
English ballad writer. Madame Schurman
and Madame Garcia have both composed
some fine works, though few in number.
But women have not hitherto realized what
long years of severe mental discipline and
scientific training are necessary in order to
master the art of composition. This is not
much to the discredit of their patience and
courage, for very few among musical stu-
dents of the other sex are, in America; will-

[Column 3]
ing to devote themselves to such a self-sac-
rificing study; too many, when they begin
to understand the amount of labor required,
become discouraged and abandon it; and
none among them yet, have acquired such
thorough early training as will insure per-
fect development to their talent for compo-
sition, and lasting fame to its results.
Mathematics, acoustics, psychology, foreign
languages and literature, the theory and
practice of many instruments, as well as
the science of music itself, must all be mas-
tered by the composer, and gradually,
through the application and assimilation of
long years of study and practice, become
the "second nature" of his mind.
	Genius, talent, irrespective of sex too,
receives the highest emoluments and hon-
ors in this art; then let Woman, whose suc-
cess has been so signal as an exponent of
vocal art,—the art of passion, persuasion;
emotion—make a further effort, now that all
opportunities are open to her, and endeavor
to compose for immortality. But, to do this,
she must abandon the idea that composi-
tion is an affair of instinct; the highest nat-
ural gifts demand the highest cultivation,
and without it they are unproductive, in-
significant, useless. It may be some en-
couragement to the aspiring student to
know that the grandest original idea of a
Handel or a Beethoven demanded more per-
fect working out, more patient polishing,
than the smallest fancy that issues from
the brain of the ballad writer demands.
	And let not women overlook one of the
most beautiful qualities of music; it pene-
trates not only to the intelligence, but to
the heart also, thus widening and vivifying
the faculties, and rendering them better
prepared, by sympathy, to receive humane
and elevated impressions. For this reason
we shall find music one of the most import-
ant factors in the coming era of universal
civilization, brotherhood, and equality.
Music possesses a lofty ethical significance;
the very heart of humanity beats in its
rhythm; and heart speaks to heart even
more completely than brain to brain. Music,
too, is based on the social sentiment of
mankind; it is the annihilator of egotism,
the most complete expositor of the life of
mankind in unison, the art whose truest
mission is to express the warmest, noblest,
most generous of human feelings,—love,
faith, patriotism; the art of order, unity,
harmony; the art that is destined to become
in some far distant but glorious day, the
universal language of humanity.


LEAP YEAR.

	Remember, O, maiden fair! two months
more and leap year is gone!—for you, per-
chance, forever-gone! How inexpressibly
sad—how inconceivably heart-touching—
the thought! But within two months,
brief though the period be, much may be
accomplished. Pick out judiciously, and
with an especial eye to his total abstinence
from those patent air-brakes to the aspiring
youth, beer and tobacco, the lordly leal and
liquor-contemning young man upon whom
you would lavish your gushing affections.
Where is the girl, whether fluttering in the
brocade of a princess or wearing the calico
polonaise of the laborer's daughter, who
would be told that for her three months are
not time enough for the wooing and the
winning of as good a husband as may be
wooed and won by any other girl with no
better faculties for the business? It is Shaks-
peare who somewhere says—and it is un-
questionably Shakspeare who knows—that,
"that man who hath a tongue, I say, is no
man, if with that tongue he cannot win a


woman." This is scarcely less true, perhaps,
than gospel. If it be true that any man
who hath a tongue can win a woman, it
would seem to follow, from a purity of reas-
oning, as the cheap disputants of some of
the religious newspapers have it, that the
woman who hath a tongue of her own—and
there be few of the sweet, delicious sex to
whom this gift is denied—must be very 
nearly akin to no woman if she cannot win
a man—and win him in less than two
months, too, and be ready with the assur-
ance in the end that she won him and
didn't half try. But enough.
	"Canst thou love me gentle stranger?"
		Blushing like a rose she stood:
	And the youth at once acknowledged
		That he rather thought he could.

PROPHECY OR VISION.

	The following prophecy, handed us for
publication, may be a sort of testimony that
there are others who are looking forward
to the fulfillment of ancient prophecy be-
sides the Latter-day Saints.
	WHAT MAY HAPPEN IN LESS THAN A
		HUNDRED YEARS HENCE.
	First.—About the year A. D. 1848, or '49,
there will be in Europe a revolution, either
in France, Italy, or Austria, and then com-
mences the "beginning of the end" spoken
of by the Prophets. New governments
will arise on the earth, and we shall have
wars and rumors of wars.
	Second.—Scarcely has the turmoil in Eu-
rope expired before our own America will
have taken up the tumult, and about A. D.
1860 or 1861 there will be a division in the
United States. The South will go to her-
self, and then war, famine and pestilence
will stalk abroad over the land—murders,
arson, incest, theft, and all other crimes
will be rife in the land. Hope will depart
from every human breast. Governments
that had been deemed impregnable will
topple and fall; but the end is not yet.
	Third.—Thirty years from the "begin-
ning of this end" will pass away, and upon
the broad earth there will exist no Govern-
ment such as now exists, or which may ex-
ist up to that time—all will be overturned
—all will be destroyed. Those then on
earth will return to the Government of the
Patriarchs, and the sire of many descend-
ants will rule and govern as in the days of
Abram, Isaac and Jacob, and servants and
slaves likewise will exist as in those days.
	Fourth.—Forty-five years of this Patri-
archial rule. and then comes the glorious
day—the Millennium—and the return of
Jesus Christ to the earth, here to reign as
temporal Prince three hundred and sixty-
five thousand years. Happy is he who
may be on earth, or who may have arisen
from the earth, fitted as a subject to live
under the mild government of such a Prince
and King.
							AMINADAB.
	Delaware River, 3rd mo., A. D. 1780.
	P. S. The above item, originally published
in the "Pioche Record, by one John Smith, he
avers to be an old American M. S. S. of the Re-
volutionary period.


"GOOD ENGLISH."

	A flippant phrase, which is most fre-
quently used by those who know least of
its practical significance. It is so easy to
say of a person whose sentiments do not
please us, or who is getting more of the
world's attention than we think becoming—


or, at least, more than has fallen to our own
lot—that he or she "does not write good
English." There is such a sweeping de-
nunciation in the charge, and it means so
much that its exact meaning may safely be
left to each one's own inference—each one,
of course, accepting without protest the
proffered office of umpire, and being fully
persuaded in his own mind that what he
does not know about "good English" is
hardly worth the knowing.
	It is scarcely possible that literary criti-
cism will ever become an exact science, or
that the individual tests which are so freely
applied will ever be more reliable or just
than at present; and while this is so—leav-
ing the final judgment to individual taste
and individual feeling—would it not be
best to accept as a fair test of "good Eng-
lish," that use of our vernacular which
shall convey most readily and surely to oth-
ers the very shade of thought which
prompts expression. It is a fact no less re-
markable than demonstrable that the most
frequent complaints against a lack of purity
in English composition are leveled at that
class of writing which is the most positive
and clear in its statements, and the least
likely to be misunderstood or misapplied.
Inelegant it may be, and unclassical; but the
English tongue is neither elegant nor classi-
cal, and is always best and purest when most
simple and expressive.—Ex.


WOMEN'S FACES.

	Somehow or other the women whose life
and grace of face are remarkable—so re-
markable as instantly to attract and fascin-
ate—almost never approach the ancient or
modern types of beauty. If you were to
take one of the women out of the pages of
Le Follet, and give her twenty times the
genius of George Elliot, she would still look
a fool. No power of brain could conquer
the simpering stolidity of the perfectly
regular face. Yet if pressed for an answer
as to what they consider the perfect type of
modern beauty, these men would think of
one of these women in a book of fashions.
There are the clear outlines of nose, mouth
and chin; the smooth forehead, small ear,
rounded cheek and accurately-placed eyes.
A man suddenly confronted with such a
face admires it; he is not moved by any in-
stantaneous sympathy toward it. Perfectly
beautiful women (there are not many, even
if we accept the low type mentioned) are
much caressed by society. They adorn din-
ner tables, are magnificent at balls, and
make good matches. (Regular lucifers,
sometimes[--].—Ed.) But they do not break
hearts; and the memory of their face, tor-
tured with pouting or glowing with the
quick joy of meeting, does not haunt a
man's life. A plain woman with a cultiva-
ted brain and good taste, ought always to
hold her own against merely pretty women.
—Ex.


IDLENESS is the nursery of crime. It is
that prolific germ of which all rank and
poisonous vices are the fruits. It is the
source of temptation. It is the field where
"the enemy sows tares while men sleep."
Could we trace the history of a large class
of vices, we would find that they originate
from the want of employment, and are
brought in to supply its place.


A POPULAR writer, speaking of the ocean
telegraph, wonders whether the news trans-
ported through the salt water will be fresh.


HOUSEHOLD HINTS.

	A good method for cooking rice, is to first put
it in cold water with a little salt, put on the fire
and let it boil five minutes, then drain and put
on a dish in a steamer and let it steam one hour.
	To clean glasses, bottles and decanters brightly,
break a few raw egg shells in the article to be
cleaned, with a little cold water,—if greasy, warm
water, and a little soda; shake well, rinse with
plenty of cold water, and the effect is perfect.
This is a common practice in France.
	In preserving, it may not be generally known
that to boil the fruit some time before adding the
sugar is very economical, because the bulk ot the
scum arising from the fruit is free from sugar;
boiling without a cover allows the water to evap-
orate, and so the fruit is firmer and better flavor-
ed.
	Veal Cutlets.—Cut the veal in good, fair sli-
ces, salt and pepper them and dip each slice in a
well-beaten egg, then in rolled crackers or bread
crumbs. fry in a good supply of hot butter or lard
until they are a delicate brown. Some prefer
them partly fried first and then covered with the
bread crumbs.
	Cement that will render india-rubber adherent
to glass or metal: pulverize some shellac and then
soften it in ten times its weight of strong am-
monia, this will become a transparent mass, and
after keeping it some little time it will become a
fluid without the aid of hot water. In three or
four weeks the mixture is perfectly liquid and
when applied will soften the rubber; for cement-
ing the rubber sheet, or the material in any shape,
to metal, glass or other surfaces, this cement is
strongly recommended.
	Pressed Beef.—Pickle the best piece of a thick
flank of beef in one pound of salt, and one ounce
of salt-petre; let it remain one week turning it
every day, after that add one half ounce of cloves,
the same of allspice, black pepper and coarse su-
gar; then let it remain a fortnight. Boil it with
onions, or whatever seasoning is preferred; until
it is tender; then place between flat dishes with
a heavy weight upon it, and let remain a day or
so; cut it when ready for use, the size of the dish
you wish to use, and pour over it jelly of what-
ever kind preferred, cover it over, and when served
it will present a very tempting appearance.
	Veal Pie.—Take a breast of veal, stew with
just enough water to cover it and season with pep-
per, salt and celery. When the veal is done, slip
the bones from the meat and remove the meat,
leaving the bones in the liquor the veal was stew-
ed in, so as to make a strong jelly. Take your
pie-dish, lay in it slices of veal, and between the
veal at intervals, the yolks of hard-boiled eggs,
and balls made of sausage meat, sprinkled over
with pepper, salt, and chopped parsley, pour the
gravy in the dish and ornament the top with
leaves cut out of pastry, brush it over with the
yolks of a raw egg and let it bake for one hour, or
untilth e gravy boils, and the crust is firm on the
top. Some prefer ham mixed with the veal.


THE LIVING HUMOR OF MADNESS"—A
gentleman visiting a lunatic asylum went
up to a distinguished-looking man sitting
moodily alone, and said to him—"How do
you do? I think I have seen you before'
may I ask your name?" "My name!" re-
turned the other fiercely. "I am Alexan-
der the Great!" "Why" said the visitor,
who suddenly remembered having already
had a discussion with the man, "the last
time I was here you were St. Paul!" Yes,
of course rejoined the man quickly, "but
that was by my first wife."

HYGIENIC NOTES.

	Cream of Tartar for Small-pox:—Take one
ounce of cream of tarter to one pint of boiling
water, let it stand after mixing and give it to the
patient in broken doses of a teaspoonful at a time.
It is said by those who have used it, to be one
of the best medicines ever used.
	The celebrated Dr. Greene in his comments on
the problem of health, in reference to laughter,
says: "There is not the remotest corner, or inlet
of the minute blood vessels of the human body
that does not feel some wavelet from the convul.
sion occasioned by good hearty laughter. The life
principle is shaken to its remotest depths, and
sends new tides of life and strength to its surface.
The blood moves more rapidly and conveys a dif-
ferent impression to all the organs of the body, as
it visits them on that particular mystic journey,
when the person is laughing, from what it does at
other times. Every good hearty laugh, tends to
lengthen life, conveying new and distinct stimulus
to the vital forces." The influence of the mind
upon the vital forces of the body can scarcely be
over-estimated. One writer in commenting upon
Dr. Greene's theory in regard to laughter, alludes
to an old school teacher who said "when child-
ren were laughing their minds were expanding.


DIVERSIONS.

	WHY are troubles like babies?—Because
they get bigger by nursing.
	THE saying, "Excuse haste and a bad
pen," has been attributed to a pig who ran
away from home.
	SOME one who believes that "brevity is
the soul of wit" writes, "Don't eat stale
Q-cumbers. They'll W-up." Other speci-
mens. "4tograph," (photograph.) "11-
worth;" (Leavenworth.) '10-a-C," (Tennes-
see.)   "Y-&." (Wyandotte.)
	WHAT a strain that is!" said Mrs. Part-
ington, as she heard an air from "Lucia"
sung in the highest style by a young lady
where she was visiting. "Yes," was the re-
sponse; "it is operatic!" "Upper attic?"
questioned she. "I should think it was
high enough to be on the top of the house."
	"WELL Sare," said a Philadelphia Cen-
tennial restaurant keeper, shrugging his
shoulders, when a customer, from Arkan-
sas demurred to paying a dollar for a cup of
coffee and a roll, "ze price he may luke
large—zat I admete, but zen, sare, reflect
zat you nafer have anozzer chance like zese
for one huntret years."
	SELF DISCIPLINE.—Costly apparatus and
splendid cabinets have no magical power to
make scholars. In all circumstances, a man
is, under God, the master of his own mind.
The Creator has so constituted the human
intellect that it can only grow by its own
action; and by its own action and free will,
it will certainly and necessarily grow.
Every man must, therefore, educate him-
self. His book and teacher are but helps;
the work is his. A man is not educated un-
til he has the ability to summons, in an
emergency, all his mental powers in vigor-
ous exercise to effect its proposed object.
It is not the man who has seen most who
can do this; such a one is in danger of being
borne down like a beast of burden, by an
overloaded mass of other men's thoughts.
Nor is it the man who can boast of native
vigor and capacity. The greatest of all
warriors in the seige of Troy had not the
pre-eminence because nature had given him
strength and he carried the longest bow,
but because self-discipline had taught him
to bend it.

WOMAN'S EXPONENT.
VOL. 5.	SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, DECEMBER 1, 1876.	No. 13.


THE CHILDREN.

If the earth would be dreary without them,
	The world all a desolate waste,
What wonder bright angels about them,
	Call home to the Father in haste?
Oh mothers! your treasures most holy,
	Which seem not a virtue to lack,
Almost like "The Lamb" meek and lowly—
	What wonder God wishes them back?

What wonder he chooses the purest,
	The happiest, dearest, most blest?
In His home all things must be surest—
	He wants but the truest and best.
He plans for us immortal pleasures,
	As emblems our babies are giv'n
He kindly secures us our treasures—
	He knows we should want them in Heaven.

And wanting them, how we should murmur,
	Grieving ever as here for a day!
It is well He is wiser and firmer,
	Whose mandates we can but obey.
It is well he can bear with our weakness,
	And courage and comfort restore,
And teach us through patience and meekness,
	To find our loved darlings once more.

Think; mothers, when with them you've parted
	For a few short hour's time on the earth,
How at meeting they made you glad-hearted
	With their welcoming kisses and mirth;
And oh think! in the blest Resurrection,
	What joy to embrace them again.
How could such a scene be perfection
	Were the actors all women and men?

Oh! the children! our light and our blessings,
	Without them the world would be nought:
Through Nature's rich chambers and dressings
	Would sweetness all vainly be sought:
Our hopes and delights were all riven,
	We should know only doubt and despair:—
who would ask for a portion in Heaven,
	If children were not to be there?
		LULA.


AUTUMN WINDS.

		Air—Autumn Leaves.

Autumn winds, autumn winds,
	That sweep so sadly,
Autumn winds, autumn winds,
	How hollowly ye sigh!
		Your ghostly, mournful music rolls
			Across the stubble lea,
		Like solemn notes the death-bell tolls
			When grand lives cease to be.

Autumn winds, autumn winds,
	Why must ye always mourn,
Autumn winds, autumn winds,
	In wailing billows borne?
	
Autumn winds, autumn winds,
	My spirit with you grieves;
Autumn winds, autumn winds
	Your tale my soul believes—
		This green-robed earth must naked be,
	And life of hopes be shorn,
		And broken hearts as lone as ye
	Thus bitterly must mourn;
Autumn winds, autumn winds,
	Like you of heavenly birth,
Autumn winds, autumn winds,
	They find no home on earth.
								LU DALTON.


[COLUMN 1]

OUR MODERN DORCAS

	There are, in our day, women whose
deeds compare favorably with Dorcas, who
lived in the days of Jesus' Apostles, who
was so beloved of the people; and whose name
has been perpetuated as synonymous with
benevolent associations down to the pres-

[COLUMN 2]

ent time. Writers of the day refer to this good
woman (Dorcas,) as a pattern of humility;
"doing what her hands found to do with all
her might;" which is no doubt a just con-
clusion, but to say she had no aspirations of
a higher nature, no thought of what her
work was accomplishing, that her brain was
not exercised as well as her heart and
hands, seems simply absurd. Women in
those times were endowed with intellect
and wisdom, and no doubt Dorcas under-
stood her vocation and worked with a pur-
pose, as the good sisters work in our day.
	This is perhaps merely an assertion, but
it has as much to sustain it, as the conclusion
drawn by the writers, who bring Dorcas for-
ward for such women to imitate, as have a
desire to vote, to legislate or "orate." She,
they affirm, had no ambitions for any of
these things. Now how do they know?
They were not there, and all they know of
her is a few words recorded in the Bible
from which they draw their own inference;
but being a woman and understanding a
woman's feelings, I should say she worked
for a purpose, no doubt that purpose was a
great one, and she was governed and guid-
ed by the circumstances of the time in
which she lived, and her devotion to the
cause she had espoused. The Savior when
upon the earth; taught the Gospel in its ful-
ness, and set the pattern for all to follow; in
many instances recorded in the New Testa-
ment, He manifested His great reverence for
women, and upon every occasion spoken of,
women were treated with affection and ten-
derness. This is particularly noticeable in
all His life and teachings. I am very will-
ing to believe that Dorcas was full of pious
zeal, that she worked with her hands and
made coats and garments for the poor, that
she comforted and blessed with good deeds
and acts of real kindness and loving words,
the widows and orphans. How beautiful is
such a life; how Christ-like the memorial of
such charity. "And all the widows stood
by him weeping and showing the coats and
garments which Dorcas made while she
was with them."
	How blessed is the work of such as live
to minister to others, forgetful of self, live
for God and His Kingdom. Surely the an-
gels have joy in watching over these prec-
ious, chosen ones, whose lives are devoted
to the noble and holy work of elevating,
purifying and refining the human family.
	In the Scriptures written by the Apos-
tles they recorded the things which
seemed most essential, but the acts and
charaties of women were almost left out,
although here and there they are mention-
ed in a casual manner, showing that their
good deeds were of common occurrence,
otherwise when these things were mention-
ed, the facts would be much more explicit.
	From the reading of the paragraph in re-
lation to Dorcas, one might infer that she
had filled some important position. There
is no doubt Dorcas had charge of some or-
ganisation for the poor, the widows and the
orphans, that her work was a great one, and
that when she died, they felt there was no
one to fill her place, and accomplish so much
good as she could have done. She was so
beloved by the people because she was ex-
tensively known for good works; as one that
ministered to the needy and desolate. Mod-

[COLUMN 3]

ern writers want us to believe that Dorcas
whom they hold up as a pattern for emula-
tion, stayed at home and stitched away for
the love of Jesus without any ambitious
ideas of what she would accomplish; "she
knew how to sew" which I suppose is inten-
ded as a hit for the Dorcas of modern
times, who does not sit and sew all day
and night too, and wear out her life, si-
lently and unobtrusively. I believe that
women should know how to sew, but I do not
believe in incessant stitching, and perhaps,
after all Dorcas may not have put in all those
stitches herself personally. We say Solo-
mon built the Temple, but we do not mean
by that assertion that he did it with his
own hands, but that he had charge of its
construction.
	There are those like Dorcas whose works
are manifest among us now, who are devot-
ing time, talents and means to the noblest
ends. Women whose names will be held in
remembrance, not only now, but hereafter.
Women whose deeds will shame those who
have had more abundant opportunities and
have not improved them. Is it not consist-
ent to honor these women whom we have
in our midst, who labor diligently with
head, heart and hands to promote the high-
est interests of the whole human family?
I believe it to be not only consistent but a
duty. Then while we esteem the Dorcas who
dwelt upon the earth so many hundred
years ago, let us also reverence those who
live in our day, who are giving their all
towards the work of benefitting and bless-
ing and preparing the earth for the millen-
nium.
	It is the custom of the whole world, to
extol the names of the dead and enumerate
their virtues, and let the living go unre-
warded; forgetful that they too are deserv-
ing of honor, and that a word of kindness
or appreciation, an acknowledgement of
their manifold labors would be far more ac-
ceptable to them while living than any trib-
ute to their memory after they are dead.
	It is recorded of Dorcas that she was
known for her "good works and alms'
deeds." Blessed record to be handed down
to her future generations; but there is a
duty to the living, and it would be well to
bear in mind that there are those in our
midst who have devoted themselves exclu-
sively to good deeds, and shall we wait un-
til they are dead to do them reverence?
Let it not be said of us who profess so much,
that we immortalize the names of the an-
cient Dorcas and yet we lack in doing our
duty and showing proper reverence to those
who are with us; if not, then what better
are we than the world? "Do not even the
publicans, the same?" We are said to be a
peculiar people, let us prove it by cherish-
ing those who devote their lives to the
work of the kingdom of God.
	THYRA.
S. L. City, Nov. 21, 1876.


No true artist ever worked yet for ambi-
tion. He does the thing that is in him to
do by a force far stronger than himself. The
first fruits of a man's genius are always free
from greed.


98		WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
		R. S. REPORTS.

	The meeting of the Senior and Junior
Retrenchment Association in the Four-
teenth Ward Assembly Rooms, Nov. 11,
was one which significantly illustrated in
its movements not only the "signs of the
times," but the implicit confidence the GOOD
SISTERS place in the teachings of their be-
loved Prophet and President. In the ab-
sence of the President of the Association,
Mrs. M. I. Horne, Counselor E. R. Snow
presided over the meeting, and after the
reading of minutes from the different wards,
and other formula connected with a meet-
ing of this kind, Miss Snow called the at-
tention of the sisters to the proposition of
President Young to them as to the buying
and storing of grain. Miss Snow thought
it necessary to have a Central Committee,
who were chosen then and there and sus-
tained by the vote of the assembly. Miss
Snow made many appropriate remarks up-
on the subject, referring to the prophecies
of ancient and modern prophets, and to the
coming of the grasshoppers during the pres-
ent year. Her words were precious, and
would they were engraven on every heart
that more care and economy might be mani-
fest in the daily lives of the Latter-day
Saints. Counselor Zina D. Young spoke
upon this subject, also upon the persecutions
the Saints had endured in Missouri and
other places; said she wanted to see the sis-
ters become a mighty phalanx, giving
strength and support to the kingdom of
God. Counselor B. W. Smith, and Mrs. R.
R. Grant, President of the Thirteenth Ward
Relief Society, expressed themselves in
reference to the great labors now being
performed by the sisters, and advanced
many good ideas in regard to the grain
movement. The subject of the Woman's
Book was laid before the meeting by Miss
Snow, and the sisters' aid solicited in be-
half of the publishing of it. The sisters
were also requested to hand in to Miss Snow,
or the author, Mr. E. W. Tullidge, any spe-
cial items in their lives, or spiritual mani-
festations which had been given them
which they considered strong testimonies
upon the principles of the gospel.
	These meetings become more and more
interesting and all who can make it conve-
nient to attend them will be able to form a
very good idea of the great work being done
by the women of Utah, and will be enabled
to receive such teaching and encourage-
ment as will direct their minds in a safe
channel, and help them to withstand temp-
tation.

			CENTERVILLE, DAVIS Co.,
					Nov. 17, 1876.
EDITORS EXPONENT:
	On the 15th our Relief Society was fav-
ored with a visit from Sisters E. R. Snow
and E. S. Barney; they fed us with the
words of life, pointed out many duties that
rested on the women of the Latter-day
Saints to arouse a feeling of higher and ho-
lier purpose above the cares of every day
life; said that we had gathered out of Baby-
lon and we should leave Babylon behind,
and unite in the general good of Zion, and
be helpmeets to man in very deed; they
laid before us many items of business—lay-
ing up grain against a day of need, to assist
in publishing the Woman's book, to sus-
tain the Woman's store, silk-culture and all
home industries, anything that helps build
up the kingdom of God, all of which we in-
tend to do to the best of our ability.
	They were inspired with the Holy Spirit

[Column 2]
and infused it into the hearts of the audi-
ence; it truly seemed like angels, visits and
we trust the fire kindled will continue to
burn in our hearts till we are enabled to
perform all that is required of us. Our
Bishop was present, and bore his testimony
to the truth of their remarks; was pleased
that the sisters had come, and wished the
Society to prosper.
	At 2 o'clock p. m., the same day, we had
another meeting; Sister Snow organized a
Young Ladies' Society with twenty-five
members, including officers. We have se-
cured a few bushels of grain as a nucleus
to gather to. We have quilts, carpet, stock-
ings, socks, and some cash we intend to
donate to the temple; we attend to the
wants of the poor though we have but few
in our midst. We wish to bear our testi-
mony to the sisters and to the world that
the Latter-day work is true, including co-
operation and plural marriage; that it will
bring happiness to all who will live up to
its requirements.
	Wishing your valuable paper a circula-
tion in every home, we remain,
	Your sisters in the Gospel,
		MARGARET RANDALL, President,
		REBECCA A. PORTER,	} Counselors.
		EMELINE SMITH.		}

		MALAD CITY, ONEIDA Co.
					Nov. 1st, 1876.
EDITORS' EXPONENT:
	Our Society is in a prosperous condition,
the sisters wish to do right so as to keep
the spirit of the Lord with them. We feel
the need of teachings from the Fountain
Head, as there are not many here that have
been in a society before, and we are but
young in experience. We will write again
soon.
	From your sisters in the Gospel,
					JANE PRICE, Prest.
				ELIZABETH JENKINS, Secy.

	CENTERVILLE, DAVIS Co., Nov. 20, 1876.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	Last Wednesday, the 15 inst., Sisters Eliza
R. Snow and E. Barney visited our settle-
ment and organized a "Young Ladies' Re-
trenchment Association." They met with
the ladies of Centerville at 2 o'clock, p. m.,
and appointed the following officers for the
association:
	Mrs. Kate Dalrymple, President,
	Mrs. Frankie Randall,	}
	Miss S. L. Smith,		} Counselors
	"	Hester Ford,		}
	"	M. E. Smith,		}
	"	Kate M. Chase, Secretary,
	"	Mary Parish, Assistant Secretary,
	"	Addie Woolley, Treasurer.
	Sister Wadoup was appointed Superin-
tendent.
	Our first meeting under the new organ-
ization was held at 2 o'clock on Saturday,
the 18th. There were present at the meet-
ing, beside the members, Mrs. M. Randall,
President of the Relief Society, and her
Counselors. Excellent and appropriate re-
marks were made by the President, Super-
intendent, Counselors and others. A lively
interest was manifested by all in the so-
ciety, and it promises to mature into a pleas-
ant and profitable institution.
	Forty members answered to roll call,
which is a fair indication of the desire felt
by all to assist in the progress of all such
enterprises as tend to instruct us, and facil-
tate the advancement of divine intelligence
among us.
			Yours Respectfully,
				KATE M. CHASE, Sec'y.

[Column 3]
				MANTI, Nov. 12, 1876.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	In accordance with your expressed wish-
es, I take the present opportunity of inform-
ing you that the Relief Society of this place
has responded to the call in relation to stor-
ing grain.
	We have about fifty bushels on hand for
that purpose, and we intend to continue in
well-doing.
	We have a small store, organized last
April, with a capital of $200,000, which is in
a flourishing condition, notwithstanding
hard times, opposition, and other adverse
circumstances. We paid a dividend of 34
per cent for our first six months, ending
Oct. 21st. We have increased our capital
considerably by taking new shares, and hope
by wisdom, economy and the blessing of our
Heavenly Father to continue to prosper. We
also have ample means on hand to assist the
needy and destitute whenever we are call-
ed upon; and all things considered, our so-
ciety was never in a more prosperous con-
dition than at present.
	A good spirit prevails in our meetings,
and we feel that we are greatly blessed in
our endeavors to do good.
	Wishing you success in your laudable en-
deavors to arouse the sisters to a realizing
sense of the great responsibilities devolv-
ing upon them, I remain,
				Very Respectfully,
					A. L. Cox, Sec'y.

EDITOR EXPONENT:
	The 19th Ward Relief Society held their
annual meeting Sept. 26. Sisters E. R.
Snow, M. I. Horne, Zina D. Young, B. W.
Smith, Presendia Kimball, Mercy Thomp-
son and Bishop A. H. Raleigh addressed
the meeting. Their remarks were replete
with words of instruction and encourage-
ment, and the counsel given, would, if fol-
lowed, aid us in bringing up our children in
a righteous manner, and help us to live
more faithful to our religion and to each
other. We were cautioned as mothers in
Israel never to speak lightly of the holy
and eternal principles of Plural Marriage,
for by that we are to gain our exaltation in
the Celestial Kingdom of our Father and
God. Our duty at the polls, the opening of
a store for the sale of home-industries, and
seri-culture, were also spoken of as matters
claiming our present and earnest attention.
	The yearly report of the society was read,
also the amount of donations and disburse-
ments of the society since the organization
of the society in 1869, which was as follows:
	Donations.......................... $ 2,138 61
	Disbursements..................    1,747 71½
	Balance...............................       390 90 ½
	For loan of Temple clothes        96 55
	There were others present, that we were
sorry time would not permit us to hear
from. At the close of the meeting, all pres-
ent were invited to partake of a picnic pre-
pared by the sisters of the Ward, and they
accordingly adjourned to the tables spread
with the bounties of the earth.
	With best wishes for the success of your
valuable paper—"OURS," as we consider
it,
	We remain yours, etc.
				A. A. PRATT, Pres't.
					A. E. NEAL, Sec'y.


MISS Elizabeth Stuart Phelps, has been
giving a series of lectures before the College
of Liberal Arts of Boston University, upon
"Representative Modern Fiction." The
Woman's Journal says George Eliot has been
discussed in four lectures."


﻿WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


For the EXPONENT.
AUTUMNAL THOUGHTS.

	The sad days come
	When to the tomb
Are borne the sweetest flowers,
	Which when in bloom,
	Threw rich perfume
Around this home of ours.

	It seems they're given
	As types of Heaven,
Those fragrant, beauteous flowers!—
	My soul's delight
	Is the cheering sight
They shed round summer hours.

	They have a voice,
	A silent voice,
That whispers to my heart,
	And to my brain
	In sweetest strain,
From which I sigh to part.

	How much we meet
	Sublimely sweet,
That's never told or written!
	But folds us round
	In bliss profound,
By heavenly breezes smitten!

	Now birds and bees
	Have ceased their glees,
All nature seems reposing;
	A moment, I
	Reflect and sigh
On the young eyes I've seen closing.

	And one stands chief,
	In bold relief,
Departing with the flowers!
	We laid her down
	In her floral crown
And returned to forsaken bowers.

	But spring will again
	Bring life in her train,
And the flowers their beauties resume,
	And the lov'd will arise
	With their glorious eyes,
And go forth in eternal bloom!

	When the resurrection
	Restores perfection—
Oh! day of consummate beauty!
	For it we can wait
	Yes, at any gate
With pleasure—and not "on duty."

	HANNAH T. KING.
S. L. City.


[Column 1]
	GENERAL MEETING OF CENTRAL
		AND WARD COMMITTEES,

		ON THE GRAIN MOVEMENT.

	The meeting was held in the Social Hall
on the 17th of November 1876. Called to
order, and opened with prayer by Mrs. E.
Howard. President of Central Committee,
Mrs. E. B. Wells, remarked as follows: "It
is very generally understood that this meet-
ing is called to discuss the GRAIN QUESTION,
It would be well to let our thoughts and
words be concentrated on the subject; we
want to make the best use of our time and
use all dispatch, that the sisters may get
home in good season. We would like the
Presidents of Relief Societies to let us
know what their ideas are on the subject.
	Miss E. R. Snow said: It is a very im-
portant occasion that has called us together,
probably such as never occurred on the
earth before. The Lord, through his pro-
phet, has called on the mothers in Israel to
prepare for a famine, which makes the sub-
ject we are called on to discuss a grave one.
We are well assured that the time is fast
approaching when the Lord will pour out
His indignation on this country, and al-
though we should escape, we will feel the
effects of it in a National capacity. Our
hearts must be hard indeed, if we will not
feel for those who may come to us for help.
The Lord showed his servant Joseph that
such things would come, and it was of such
a nature, and so distressing and revolting

[Column 2]
to humanity to witness, that he asked the
Lord to close it up. We can see, from what
has passed, that the predictions of the Ser-
vant of God, have been fulfilled to the let-
ter. He has now called on the women of
Zion to purchase and take care of wheat,
and I hope we may act with wisdom, that
the blessing of God may rest upon our la-
bors. We want to decide how to procure
the grain and take care of it; and whether
we will act in Wards or in a general capac-
ity; we have been told that there is room
in the Tithing Office to store it. But per-
haps some of the Wards have a good place
for storing; if so, and they prefer it, they
can do so.
	Mrs. E. B. Wells said: Bishop Hunter told
me we could store the wheat in the Tithing
Office, and that the wards could also if they
wished, as there was plenty of room unoc-
cupied now; but whatever we do there, we
will have to employ help, as the brethren
who labor there are all fully employed.
	Mrs. S. M. Kimball said: We have ap-
pointed a committee to visit in our ward.
They report that the feelings of the people
have been liberal and good. All we want
to know is what steps we are to take in or-
der to push the work forward. I feel that
the Lord will bless us in our undertaking.
	Mrs. E. B. Wells asked if any of the
sisters could inform the meeting where
wheat could be purchased.
	Mrs. W. East: I expect Mrs. Horne can
tell us where we can buy wheat, when
she returns from the South. I have been
told that the brethren residing in the coun-
try places, are awakening up, and see the
utilily of saving grain, and it may not be
a very easy matter for the sisters to procure
it. We held a meeting in the 14th Ward a
short time ago exclusively for this ques-
tion. The sisters feel the importance of the
mision and will do all in their power to ac-
complish it. We feel that the Lord will
open up the way for us to get wheat; and
that he will bless us in our efforts.
	Mrs. Elizabeth Goddard: I have heard
that wheat can be bought for a dollar a
bushel; and I think it would be well to take
flour also, it can be kept good in sacks for a
long time.
	Mrs. E. S. Barney: I can report from
three counties where they have no grain
for sale; Morgan, Summit and Wasatch,
were distressed with an early frost, which
destroyed nearly all their wheat; but feel
sure that we can make this business a suc-
cess.
	Mrs. R. Grant: The 13th Ward intend
saving grain. We have had a large bin of-
fered us for that purpose, which we will ac-
cept. It has long been preached that the
brethren should prepare for a day of famine;
it now seems as if the women were to have
a hand in it, and I hope the Lord will give
us strength to perform our work nobly.
	Mrs. Sarah Howard: I feel it is a priv-
ilege the Lord has given us, and we will try
and be united in it. For my part I will try
and do all I can, and I feel that the Lord
will open a way whereby we can obtain
grain, although it is late in the season.
Our Retrenchment Association has com-
menced a fund for that purpose, we will
work in concert with the 20th Ward Relief
Society.
	Mrs. Savage said: In the 20th Ward,
they intend doing the best they can to help
in this work, but just at the present time
there are no funds in the Treasury.
	Mrs. M. M. Hyde, 17th Ward, said:
We intend to build a hall for the use of our
Society; when we heard of this grain busi-
ness, we talked the matter over, and think
of making the hall smaller, and putting up

[Column 3]
a bin for wheat in part of it. I would just
suggest, that in the States, wheat some-
times spoils, through dampness, but our at
mosphere being so dry, I expect it would
keep good for some time."
	Mrs. E. B. Wells: President Young said
the sisters were to solicit help from their
husbands and the brethren, for this purpose;
by that means we would be able to get
funds to purchase the wheat with.
	Mrs. Reid, of the 16th Ward, said: The
sisters of our Ward are willing to do all
they can to carry out counsel.
	Mrs. L. G. Richards: I think that if
we unite our means, and have one energetic
sister to buy up the wheat; and by hav-
ing one, two or three hundred dollars, quite
an amount could be procured, and I think
would be better than being mixed up in
wards.
	Miss E. R. Snow said: What I have to
give, I intend putting it with the bulk. I
know no separate interest. I hope energet-
ic sisters will be appointed to attend to it;
one or two in each ward say, or a commit-
tee be formed, as may seem necessary.
	Mrs. E. B. Wells remarked: I presume
all the boards of the Relief Society will co-
operate in this work, and I think it will be
well for us to unite.
	Mrs. Chase of the 1st Ward: I have
talked on this subject in our meeting, and
we intend to unite with the rest in what-
ever plan they may suggest. I have a large
bin in which a great amount of wheat
could be stored; perhaps all we could get
this year. We have quite a number of
poor, but will be able to help some in all
good calls. At one time my husband put up
a good deal of wheat but after some time
the weevil spoiled it. But I hope we can
plan some way to take care of that which
the Lord will bless us with.
	It was then moved and seconded that a
general committee be appointed to purchase,
but that will not hinder wards from hav-
ing ward committees.
	The following ladies were elected as a
purchasing committee: Mrs. E. S. Barney,
Mrs. E. Howard, and Mrs. S. M. Kimball.
	Mrs. E. B. Wells said: Let every sister
secure and store away every bushel of
wheat that it is possible for her to obtain.
She also said it will be necessary to have a
Treasurer in some central place. Mrs. P.
M. Staines was sustained as Treasurer.
	It was proposed that a record be kept of
the proceedings in each ward, every name
and donation put down in a distinct man-
ner.
	It was suggested that we commence at
once and have no delay about it. In Manti
they have put up 60 or 70 bushels, and we
can't be too prompt in this matter. Many
of the brethren seem to wish to help us.
	Mrs. Mercy Thompson [---]: Flour that
is well sacked and kept in a dry place would
keep as well, if not better, than wheat. I
have some flour now using, that has been
sacked four years, and it is as good, if not
better than new flour. I know that the Lord
will bless us in our undertaking.
	Considerable business was transacted in
regard to the Woman's Book about to be
published; and the propriety of the sisters
making a carpet for the Historian's Office
was taken into consideration and the sisters
donated liberally for that purpose.
	Meeting adjourned until Nov. 24th, at 3
o'clock p. m. Sung "The Spirit of God like
a fire is burning." Benediction by Mrs.
Rachel Grant.


IN, what ship has the greatest number of
people been wrecked?—Courtship.
