SALT LAKE CITY, DEC. 1, 1876.


[COLUMN 1]

THE NATIONAL PUZZLE.

	First, who is elected President? And second-
ly, how is the fact to be demonstrated? Third-
ly, is it to be determined by the civil powers,
or by military force? No wonder people are
becoming confused on the subject, for confus-
ion reigns supreme, and if dissolution is not the
result of haranguing for reformation and
amendment, constitutionally why then Amer-
ica is surely exceptionally lucky. If only
some good fairy would whisper in the anxious
listening ear of some popular editor, whose
nerves are strained to the highest tension, or
touch, with magic hand the Electric Telegraph
and send the news throughout the length and
breadth of the country, now anxiously waiting
the grand national denouement, it would be a
most commendable and praiseworthy action.
Alas! the people of this highly enlightened
nineteenth century are too matter-of-fact to
believe in fairies or magic, and too self-confi-
dent to put their trust in an over-ruling power
who the Scriptures affirm, holds the nations in
His hand, and Who has declared he will turn
and overturn until the kingdoms of this
world become the kingdoms of God. So peo-
ple will go on wondering and puzzling and
querying what is to be the result of this pecu-
liar crisis in national affairs.
	It is not strange that people get muddled in
trying to solve the mystery, for there never
was a more vexatious riddle propounded to the
American nation.
	All the reports which have been received
from the different states since the Tilden party
were so positive of his election have been con-
tradictory ones. On every side arises difficulty
in determining the facts, or arriving at a just
decision, and the only way out of it, is to de-
clare the law, pertaining to presidential elec-
tors incomplete.
	This is the only policy left them to pursue
that will not implicate the governing powers.
One party may of course cry out against the
dishonest proceedings of the other, but when
both know themselves to be equally in fault,
what policy so clear as to lay the fault of the
evil issues upon their predecessors who formed
for them a government and constitution of
laws, surpassing all other existing forms, and
yet, (for their wicked purposes,) inefficient.
	And so the great American nation has made
such gigantic strides in advancement (corup-
tion) that they need another amendment to
meet the emergencies of this case. All these
things to those whose eyes are open to "the
signs of the times" are direct indications of
the approaching crisis, which will eventually
result in the final overthrow of all govern-
ments. Some powerful agency which the
world cannot understand is now manifesting

[COLUMN 2]

itself and producing sensations of fear
among the masses of the people, and some
have been so strangely impressed with fore-
bodings of evil, they have recalled the fire-
side tales told them in their childhood, and
shrink with trembling at the anticipation of
another political revolution. The wisest and
shrewdest of political men express themselves,
that when the truth is ascertained about the
election then something may be foretold in
regard to the work which will come be-
fore the House of Congress the ensuing season;
but when the scale will turn or what may yet
transpire, with all the party feeling that now
pervades the public mind still remains inexplic-
able. One thing is apparent to those whose
minds are opened to the light of truth, and
that is that the adversary is striving for mas-
tery in the hearts of the children of men, that
instead of peace, he will, if possible bring war,
and as a natural consequence of war, famine,
pestilence and plague. If this nation would
receive the testimony of the servants of God
many of the difficulties might be avoided; but
they are wise in their own conceit and harden-
ed in their hearts, and as kings and rulers of na-
tions refused to listen in ancient times, so do
they now reject that which would bring them
prosperity and happiness. But Babylon must
fall, and everything seems conspiring to bring
about the fulfillment of the prophecies quickly.
Already are nations of the greatest power and
strength preparing for war and ultimately a
war in which all nations will become engaged
will be the result. In contemplation of these
fearful occurrences the saints should be wise
and seek more than ever for the spirit of God
to direct them, that they may indeed become
not only saviors of individuals, but assist in the
protection of the rights and privileges of na-
tions.
	It may seem to our readers that we have
wandered from the subject, but a question so
momentous in its issues may admit of elabor-
ate discussing.
	It has been advised by those who are anxious
to assist in arriving at the adjudication of the
great question now pending, that caution and
quiet would be "the better part of valor" that
both parties are weakening their own side by
making charges of dishonest dealings; that
positive proofs are the only real specific. If
these wise men can succeed in lulling the great
political tumult and clamor, we hope it may
not prove the precursor of a tremendous na-
tional thunder-bolt that will split asunder the
two opposing parties, so effectually that it will
be impossible to cement them together again,
without, as brother Heber C. Kimball used to
say, "throwing the clay all back into the hopper
and grinding it over.
	E. B. Wells


[Column 1]
			HOME AFFAIRS.

	C. W. [-]ENROSE, editor of the Ogden Junc-
tion, and S. W. Sears, produce and grain mer-
chant, are gone on a business trip to San Fran-
cisco.
	WE HAVE recently received, through the
courtesy of the editor who was in Salt Lake
City a short time ago, as an exchange, "The
Watchman," published in Boston, Mass. It is
the second oldest newspaper in the State.
	HON. GEORGE Q. Cannon, the people's Del-
egate to Congress, left this city for Washing-
ton, D. C., Tuesday morning, Nov. 27th. We
wish him a pleasant trip and success in his
efforts to maintain the national rights of the
people of the Territory of Utah.
	CORRECTION: IN THE EXPONENT OF NOV. 1,

[Column 2]
we published a report from Mill Creek Ward,
Salt Lake County, in which two mistakes oc-
curred accidentally: every second Thursday,
should read Tuesday; and the name of the pres-
ident of the association, instead of Mrs. Isabel-
la Carlisle, should read Mrs. Isabella Walters.
	THE UTAH Musical Times, No. 9, which we
have received, contains some choice and inter-
esting articles. "Write Your Thoughts," is one
which expresses clearly the true system of
writing for the press. The music is of home
production and speaks well for Utah's musical
talent. It gives many late dramatic notes
of interest. Utah may be justly proud of this
periodical.
	WE HAVE received a specimen copy of the
"Daily Hotel Gazette," a new publication in
San Francisco, California. It seems from its
variety of advertisements of business &c., to
be valuable for reference to travelers, etc.
They are using Miller and Richard type which
they strongly recommend. This manufactory is
a Branch of the Edinburgh Type Foundry,
and has on hand, beside Hard Metal Scotch
type, presses, and printing materials. Office,
No., 521 Sacramento St., San Francisco.
	AT THE General Retrenchment Meeting,
Fourteenth Ward Assembly Rooms, Sat., Nov.
25, Elder Junius F. Wells, recently returned
from a mission to the United States, addressed
the assembly briefly. He expressed great joy
at the work in progress among the sisters, and
contrasted their ideas, feelings and knowledge
of the future, with the women of the world.
He manifested great interest in the Woman's
Book, believing it would open the eyes of an
enlightened class of society to the facts in re-
gard to the motives by which the sisters were
actuated.
	THE WOMEN of Utah have taken meaaures
to secure the publication of a BOOK which will
combine in one volume, the lives and testimo-
nies of the sisters as far as possible, from the
time of the rise of the church in Kirtland up
to the present day. Such arrangements have
been made as will ensure to every sister ample
returns for all money invested either in her
own name, or in connection with a Relief So-
ciety. It is expected this book will reach
many places, homes and individuals where
the doctrines of the Latter-day Saints, in what-
ever form presented, would not be tolerated;
for that reason it will do the most efficient
preaching to a certain class of people. Some
enthusiasm upon this subject would not be
amiss to assist in forwarding this laudable
work. The women of Utah certainly should be
able to publish their own lives. We hope to see
the sisters come forward and subscribe for this
immediately, that the work may be speedily
published and circulated.
	WE HAVE received from the author, a new
publication entitled "Overcome." The book
is published by Lovell printing and publish-
ing Co., New York. The writer is an Ameri-
can lady of prepossesing appearance and pecu-
liarly gifted in conversation. We had the
pleasure of meeting her here, when on her way
to San Francisco; she ran down to Salt Lake
for a day or two to see the "City of the Saints."
We return her our thanks for the book, and the
favorable notice given us in the "Chicago
Tribune." Her book is a gem in its way,
drawn from real life. It is a true tale, calcula-
ted to touch the heart, told in a charming and
pathetic style, and bears a similarity to many
incidents occurring in households all over the
country. If mothers and sisters can be in-
duced (and this is the author's noble design)
through earnest entreaty and the influence of


[COLUMN 1]

women of talent and their example, to discour-
age the drinking of wine, beer, or liquor in any
form at dinner-parties, social gatherings and
New Year's calls, a great deal might be accom-
plished for the cause of Temperance; for many
a young man, as the writer says, can trace his
downfall from all that was good and pure, to
the first taking of the social-glass. Mothers
and sisters, especially women of the Latter-
day Saints, let us entreat you to use all your
influence to do away with this foolish Gentile
custom, lest your hearts shall some day ache
as sorely as many hearts do now, throughout
the whole world from the effects of intemper-
ance.
	AT A MEETING of the Central and Ward
Committees, on the Grain Movement, held in
the Social Hall, Friday, Nov. 24th, many im-
portant ideas were advanced in a clear and for-
cible manner by those who spoke upon the sub-
ject. Reports from different wards were given
in regard to the movement and such sugges
tions as seemed applicable to the time an
place, from many presidents of Relief Socie-
ties from the several wards. It seemed that in
several of the wards they had good granaries
to store in separately, which will of course; be
an advantage in some respects, for there wil
be a better opportunity of preserving it from
damp, or mice, or weevils, or any of the an-
noyances that attend the storing of grain in
large quantities together.
	Miss E. R. Snow, of the Central Committee,
in her remarks, referred to having recently
visited Brigham City, and that her brother,
who is president there (Lorenzo D. Snow,)
thought this movement of the sisters a step in
the right direction. Br. F. D. Richards, of
Ogden had said to her, he thought what the
sisters did this year in regard to buying grain
must be done immediately as there could be
nothing done after Christmas. There are many
of the brethren in authority, Bishops and oth-
ers, who have spoken kindly and favorably to
the sisters, and given the movement their
hearty endorsement. A spirit of energy and
enterprise is manifest among the sisters to in-
vest their means in such a manner as shall be
approved by heaven. Not all the laces and
silks in the world could compare with bread
stuff in the time of scarcity.
	Mrs. W. East of the 14th ward, when she
rose to speak, said she was sure that the Lord
had called upon the sisters to act in this ca-
pacity. Spoke of a brother who had a speci-
men of wheat he had kept for twenty five
years, and that it was perfectly good. Those
who are not of us, scoff and sneer, but the sis-
ters should be independent of all outside influ-
ences, and take hold of the subject with that
fortitude and courage that will warrant suc-
cess.
	FARMINGTON, DAVIS Co., Nov. 24th, 1876.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	By request of our President, Mrs. S. I.
Holmes, I send you a report of what we are do-
ing in regard to the grain question. At our
meeting two weeks ago last Wednesday, she
read the editorial containing President Young's
counsel and suggestions and some other pieces
from the Exponent, explaining to the sisters
what is required of them; said she felt like
commencing right off, so that we should not
be behind in this great work. At first it was
thought best to build a granary that we might
have a suitable place to put the grain collected;
but afterwards, through advice and the kind-
ness of the brethren, one was found already
built which we can have the use of, therefore
saving the means which it would take to build
to buy grain.

[COLUMN 2]

	At the meeting it was suggested that a com-
mittee be appointed to visit the men in the
ward to see what they would do to help us;
the suggestion was carried and a good report
brought in. The majority of the brethren en-
tered into the spirit of the mission, and either
donated means or labor.
	Some of the brethren felt quite humbled to
think the women should undertake what the
men had failed to do; but I feel to offer a word
of encouragement to all such. The words of
Prest. Heber C. Kimball, which come to my
recollection, I think should suffice, wherein he
prophesied that the time would come when
the sisters would store up the grain and pota-
toes. I do not think that any good woman
would take the praise to herself, or think she is
better than her husband, on account of this
move that has been made. I feel that we
should work together, for the man is not with-
out the woman in the Lord, nor the woman
without the man; which is according to Scrip-
ture.
	The Society of Farmington have some grain
on hand and means to buy more, and do not
think of slackening up their energies in the fu-
ture.
	I wish to speak a word in favor of the Young
Ladies Retrenchment Association in this place.
They have a faithful president in Mrs. Lucy A.
Clark, and hold their meetings once a week;
they also have a paper of their own composi-
tion, called the "Index" which is both instruc-
tive and interesting. They also read selected
pieces in their meetings. The younger portion
of the members are quite zealous, so much so,
that they can hardly be induced to stay away
from meeting under any consideration.
	Excuse me for being so lengthy.
	Yours &c.
		A. S. ROGERS, Sec'y.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.		101


WOMAN'S VOICE.

	The new movement in regard to grain is one
which should receive the hearty commenda-
tion of the Latter-day Saints. It is part of our
religion to practice consistently the laws of
life and health. If we do not do this we are
not sustaining the laws of God. for He has
made all laws devolving upon each other so
perceptibly, that if we transgress one, we are
sure to infringe upon others. We could not
possibly carry out the design of our Creator
unles we preserve our bodies, for the first great
commandment given to man after the creation
was to "multiply and replenish the earth."
In order to do this we must have well preserv-
ed faculties and physique, otherwise we are de-
generating and falling away from the pattern
given us to follow. "And God created man in
His own image." It seems to me that to be
made in the likeness of God is something
which every individual should hold in rever-
ence, and it ought to be an incentive to purity
in the preservation of life, and thus give us op-
portunity for such knowledge as shall help us
to obtain a part in that higher life which will
be the ultimate result of our being upon the
earth.
	The Lord caused the earth to bring forth of
her fruits for the sustenance of the beings
which He placed upon it, and it has been ful-
filling the laws of its creation for many thou-
sand years. But we who believe the Scriptures
and the prophets of our own day know that
great changes are to take place, before the rev-
olution of all things which will usher in the
millennium. One of the most distressing of
those changes will be famine, and we have
been warned and forewarned of its coming,
yet the people, as in the days of Noah and oth-


ers, reject the testimony of the Servants of
God, and still pursue their every-day life, care-
less of the future, and regardless of the teach-
ings and testimonies vouchsafed to them for
safety.
	It is sufficiently apparent to every sensible
woman that if the famine were right upon us,
we are not prepared to sustain our own people
here in this Territory, and that we should feel
it as severely as others. But if we heed the
call made upon us by the servant of God, ap-
pointed to dispense His word unto the people
in due season, then we shall act wisely while
we have a day of grace given us; but let us not
dally or trifle away time and opportunities,
but seek with all diligence to store up an
abundance of grain for ourselves and our friends
who have jeered and ridiculed us and our re-
ligion. Let us exercise that charity that will
not only feed the hungry, but make the need-
ful preparations beforehand, that we may min-
ister to them in a substantial and proper man-
ner.
	[handwritten] E. B. Wells
								EMILE.
S. L. City, Nov. 24, 1876.


THE LAW OF NEWSPAPERS.

	1. Subscribers who do not give express no-
tice to the contrary are considered as wishing
to continue their subscriptions.
	2. If any subscribers order the discontinuance
of their newspapers, the publisher may con-
tinue to send them until all arrearages are paid.
	3. If subscribers neglect or refuse to take
their newspapers from the offices to which
they are directed, the law holds them responsi-
ble until they have settled the bills, and order-
ed them discontinued.
	4. If subscribers remove to other places with-
out informing the publisher, and the newspa-
pers are sent to the former direction, they are
held responsible.
	5. The courts have decided that refusing to
take newspapers from the office, or removing
and leaving them uncalled for, is "prima facie"
evidence of intentional fraud.
	6. The postmaster who neglects to give the
legal notice of the neglect of a person to take
from the office the newspapers addressed to
him, is liable to the publisher for the subscrip-
tion price.


NOTES AND NEWS.

	IN SOME of the places where women are
not permitted the right of the ballot, they
have made public demonstrations of their
sentiments in political matters by "flinging
to the breeze" flags bearing the names of
the candidates for the presidency, of the
party they wished to sustain. In one or
two places, they held political meetings and
discussed the subject and the probability of
its success.
	THE GREAT CYCLONE.—This terrific
wind storm that recently devastated a large
tract of country in the East Indies, com-
pletely submerging three large and four or
five small islands, is one of the most fearful
calamities ever known; and should be an
effectual warning to the living. The loss of
life is estimated by some at about a quarter
of a million. The destruction of property
is incalculable, but added to all this, and
the most heart-sickening, is the famine
which inevitably follows such unlooked-for
occurrences. The atmosphere, too, near
where the dead bodies are, is so polluted
that pestilence is almost sure to result.


102		WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


DESCRIPTION OF CHRIST

	LETTER OF PUBLIUS SENTILUS TO THE
			SENATE OF ROME

	There is a man among us who is called
Jesus Christ. He is accepted of the people
as a prophet. His own disciples say he is
the Son of God. He heals the sick, restores
sight to the blind, hearing to the deaf, and
raises the dead. In conversation he is mild
and amiable; his carriage is graceful and
modest; in reproving he is terrible, yet gen-
tle and forgiving. In person he is tall and
comely; his nose and mouth are beautifully
formed; his chin a model of Grecian Sculp-
ture underlaying a heavy beard; his eyes
like diamonds, indicating great wisdom
and penetration, comprehending everything
at a glance; bespeaking great compassion for
the poor and unfortunate. His hair waves
in flowing curls over his shoulders in orien-
tal style after the manner of the Nazarites.
He has not been known to laugh, but fre-
quently weeps; in short his whole form and
carriage are without blemish and his exte-
rior beauty surpassing all the sons of men.


FROM HAWAII

EXTRACTS FROM LETTERS BY MRS.
JANE E. MOLEN.

[CONCLUDED]
	It is Saturday, but I shall teach, to make
up for last Monday, which was the "Twenty-
Fourth." Our good brethren celebrated
it in honor of the arrival of the Pioneers
at "Monopohoko"—(Salt Lake.)
	I often look down the pasture, and think
how good it would seem to see a white face
approaching; that is a sight seldom seen
here. But I am quite busy trying to learn
the language, and have no time to get lone-
some. I shall not have so good a chance
to learn when Simpson goes away, he is so
patient in teaching me. He will soon start
for another island, and will be gone until
conference time.
	The natives are thick around me, so that
I can scarcely write. They are gathering
to make arrangements for a feast; our house
is their place of gathering for such affairs,
and it is generally very lively with their
noise.
	We have eight rooms, three verandas, a
nice large cook house, and no mud. It may
rain for hours and as soon as the sun comes
out, you may walk out without soiling your
feet. Grass grows even on the rocks. You
may spade your garden, and in a few days
grass springs up and looks green. I never
saw so clean a place. The water is as soft
as rain, very warm, but I do not notice it
now. I am getting used to everything ex-
cept doing without vegetables. Sorry we
did not bring seeds with us. The brethren
have not tried to raise a garden. Simpson
has been trying to get a few seeds here and
there, and has a garden started. I have not
seen a melon, or anything but sweet pota-
toes in the vegetable line since we came.

	It is seventeen years to day since I took
the marriage vow, and promised to be a
true and faithful wife. And as the mission-
aries are to start again tomorrow, we have
had a dinner, the first good meal I have
had the honor of cooking. There were of
us, brothers Lambert and wife, Woolley and
wife, Pack, Stringam, and Molen, myself
and little Millie. For dinner, I made a good
loaf of bread, a nice cake sweetened with
very brown sugar, four apple pies, (from ap-
ples that my dear mother dried,) stewed


two chickens which I bought of a native
woman, and made a nice gravy. Then we
had salt beef, sweet potatoes with butter
and pepper, and "poi."
	I imagine you laughing at my dinner,
but you need not. I saved things for over
two weeks, preparing for to-day. Brother
Wooley made me a present of some nice
pine apples, cocoanuts and a paper of figs.


[COLUMN 1]

	For the Exponent.
MATTER AND SPIRIT.

	In a late number of the EXPONENT, I no-
ticed a finely written article upon the beau-
ties of Nature. I am greatly in sympathy
with the writer of that effusion. From a
child I have been much given to contrast-
ing the works of man, with the works of
His Maker. By bringing even the tiniest
specimens of heaven's designing hand un-
der the microscope, and noting how precisely
according to the exact rule of its formation,
the smallest flower, for instance was organ-
ized. How certain its progress too, spring-
ing into life, blooming into its delicate
beauty for awhile, then fading, dying and
vanishing from sight. Yet in its disinte-
gration there falls from its minute but nu-
merous chalices an almost infinite number
of atomic germs of life—of flower life eter-
nal. Going out and upward from these
small thoughts, my eyes were opened upon
the fact, that in all the works of Deity, the
law of composition and of decomposition
is always the same under the same circum-
stances, that though He
	"Forms the shining worlds,"—or
	"Makes the cricket sing,"
still the fact of law of order of all atoms
and all masses, is an inevitable conclusion.
Then I said to myself, this being so, that an
inviolable law that cannot be changed, rules
all matter, that everywhere, like must
cleave unto like; that the chemical affinity
of substances are resting on the condition
and properties of each and every ultimate
atom of matter that enters into the struct-
ure of the vast Universe of the great Crea-
tor. That inert atoms must construct them-
selves by their own self-possessed forces and
characters into such masses as they were
made to be associated with, and cannot
combine with those elements which are not
of their kind, or endowed with such cor-
responding forces as shall bind them togeth-
er. If all these be so exactly held by the
laws of their creation, what then shall we
say of the immortal, or quickening element
of life in vegetable, animal, or man. Leav-
ing the two first we are most concerned in
the high powers and endowments given to
that being which, we are told, was formed
in the likeness of God—male and female,
man and woman—and who was first quick-
ened into life by the breath of the Al-
mighty. Even so, and more also shall not
God know the beings He has so created.
Has He not made, and will he not contro
those children of His by a law as just, as de-
terminate as the law by which the worlds
were poised in such position as should cer-
tainly induce in them movement, which
those orbs must make, in order to follow
the orbit marked out for them when He
drew the circle in the heavens, and calcu-
lated the just proportion of every force and
power which should keep them in the track
which He had assigned to them.
	Not to follow this subject further at this
time, for I never have much leisure, my
conclusion has been this. In view of all
the visible acts of God, I must conclude—
first, that every law of God is just, His acts

[COLUMN 2]

are laid in great understanding, that His
decrees are inevitable, that which He has
ordained must surely come to pass, that
though the heavens and earth fail, not one
jot or tittle of His word shall fail, that He
is Omnipotent as well as Al-wise, and hav-
ing said "the just shall live by faith, and
that not of themselves—it is the gift
of God." That no one can enter into
His kingdom who is not both just and faith-
ful.
	M. I. CORAY.
Coray's Ranch, York Station, Nov. 14, 76.


THAT OTHER WILLIE.

BY BELLE FRENCH.

	"Willie, why don't you go and play with
the boys and not be forever stuck at my
feet?"
	Such was Mrs. Gray's impatient question,
one day, when her little son came and seat-
ed himself in the parlor when his mother
was conversing with a visitor.
	"I would rather be with you than with
the boys," he answered timidly.
	"Oh, I never saw such a baby!"
	"Is it wrong to wish to be near you, moth-
er?" said the child, and his nether lip trem-
bled as he spoke.
	"Wrong? of course not. But you are old
enough to have some manliness about you.
See, yonder are Will and John Gowdy on
the ice. Run and keep them company. I
want to talk to Mrs. Brown."
	"Isn't he a queer child," she asked.
	The other raised her sad eyes and fixed
them with such a painful expression on the
mother's face, that for a moment Mrs. Gray
almost felt offended. She was a sorrowful
looking woman this Mrs. Brown.
	"I had a son once, but he's gone," she
said at last, and there were tears in her
eyes.
	Mrs. Gray gazed at her wonderingly.
She had not known this before.
	"It is a bitter thing to tear open partially
healed wounds," Mrs. Brown continued,
"but I will tell you my story:
	"Several years ago I was about to give a
party; a grand affair it was to be, and my
head was almost turned in making prepara-
tions. My Willie, (his name was Willie
too,) was about 16 years old. He had never
been to school, I had educated him myself.
At home, he was all a mother's heart could
desire; but he was shy, and when I forced
him into company he appeared so awkward,
that I often felt ashamed of him. This was
one reason of my deciding to give a party.
If he was obliged to act the part of host he
would overcome his bashfulness, I thought.
But Willie never approved of it.
	"I shall be so glad when the party is
over," he said one day; "for since you have
got it into your head, I have lost my moth-
er."
	"Poor little baby!" I responded, slightly
provoked at his lack of interest. "I won-
der how many more years I shall have you
tied to my apron string?"
	I spoke sneeringly, and a proud flush in-
stantly overspread his face.
	"I will be tied there no longer," he re-
turned, "I will seek other company in the
future."
	I was frightened at the result of my
words. Still I made no response. My son
putting on his coat and hat, went out. It
was the first time in his life he had gone
out without informing me where he was
going.
	In good time the party came off. It was
a gay affair, and none were gayer than Wil-


[COLUMN 1]

lie. He was a sort of extremist, and took
no medium stand. After that his books and
work were neglected, and his days, as well
as evenings, were spent abroad. Fast young
men became his constant companions. I
was left alone to mourn over the change I
had wrought. At first he made it a rule to
be in at night by ten o'clock, but after a
time he began to stay out later; and day-
break sometimes found him from home.
I tried to expostulate, tried to win him
back to his old habits, but my efforts were
unavailing. He had got a taste of a new
life and it held him by a charm. Well do
I remember the first night he came home
in a state of intoxication. It was his seven-
teenth birthday, just a year from the time
I gave the party. I had seen him under
the influence of wine once or twice before,
but this night he drank so deeply that some
of his companions had to help him home.
	The hours of that night were dreadful
hours of self reproach and agony. I was so
glad when morning came to dispel the
gloom—so glad when reason returned to my
erring child. He was very much ashamed.
He said again and again he would do bet-
ter; but his resolves were worthless. Two
nights later, he was again brought home in-
toxicated. After that it was a common oc-
curence. He fell lower and lower, squand-
ered all my ready money and, when I re-
fused to mortgage my property, that he
might have more, he left me with an oath.
	That night a large firm was robbed and
it was discovered that Willie was one of
the perpetrators of the deed. The next
morning the town was alive with excite-
ment, and I almost crazed with anxiety,
for my boy had fled. The news passed from
mouth to mouth; my house was searched,
and my son called a villain, but I had no
power to prevent either. No one gave me
a word of sympathy.
	"You have only yourself to blame," said
a blunt old woman, who called during the
day. "The boy was happy at home but you
drove him into bad company."
	That night at the hour of twelve, as I sat
alone, a window was opened softly and Wil-
lie stepped into the room. With a glad cry
I sprang toward him, but he pushed me
rudely away.
	"Can you hide me anywhere," he said,
"The bloodhounds are after me. Had you
given me the money yesterday, this would
not have been."
	"Oh, Willie!" I cried.
	"Yes, mother," he said sternly, "you
have made me a criminal. I want to tell
you I have secretly married Kate Hastings.
God knows what will become of her."
	Kate was a pretty little creature, only 16
years old, innocent as the violets which
grew around her home—My bleeding heart
gave a quick painful throb as he continued:
	"The world will not believe we are mar-
ried. She will be scorned by all. Hark!
they are coming. Mother, I am too young,
too wicked to die, but I must die. Fare-
well!"
	I saw his purpose now, for his hand
clutched a revolver and springing to my
feet I threw my arms about him to shield
him from himself. But he shook me off.
The next moment the loud report of his
pistol echoed through the house. One glance
showed me his lifeless form stretched on
the floor. Then existence was a blank to
me.
	"When I awoke to consciousness the
morning sun was shining and the house
was filled with people. But even justice
was satisfied, and I was left alone with the
dead. All day, tearless and motionless, I
sat beside that mangled corpse. Some peo-

[COLUMN 2]

ple kinder than the rest, came in to make
preparations for the funeral; and passed si-
lently out, but I did not heed them.
	Kate Hastings came just after dark. She
was dressed in deep mourning, and her face
was so ghastly that it startled me.
	"You, too, have come to reproach me," I
said.
	"No, mother. You suffer enough with-
out my reproaches. I have come to watch
with the dead."
	"I wish to watch alone," I said.
	"It is I who will watch alone," she re-
tuened. "It is my right, I am his wife."
	How calm she was! There was not even
a tremor of the voice to tell how she suffer-
ed.
	"Yes, it is your right, my poor child!" I
said. It gives me another pang to give
him up even to you my daughter; still I do
it.
	She looked up quickly.
	"He has told you?"
	"Yes."
	"Yet you speak kindly to me and do not
condemn us."
	A sad beautiful smile for a moment light-
ed up her features. She raised one of my
hands and kissed it reverently.
	"Thank you," she said. "Some time
you will be glad for having shown this
kindness to one so much in need of it.
Now, mother, leave me."
	I left the apartment but did not retire.
All night I sat on the floor outside the door,
hoping that Katie would bid me enter; but
no such summons came. Daylight returned,
and the busy world again moved, still I
head no movement in the chamber of
death. At last my anxiety became so great
that I opened the door and looked in. The
girl knelt by the side of the corpse, appar-
ently asleep. Softly I stole forward and
raised the drooping head. But no sad eyes
met my gaze, nothing but the white face
and staring orbs of a corpse. Katie had
died by her own hand, as a bottle which
she clutched proved.
	The next day they buried the pair; my
erring son and his child wife, in one grave;
and as the clods fell on the coffin, the
brightness of my life went out forever.
	Mrs. Brown could say no more, for sobs
choked her utterance. Her listener, too,
was deeply affected, as her pale face and
tearful eye showed.
	Leaving the bereaved mother for a mo-
ment, Mrs Gray stole softly to the door and
called—
	"Willie!"
	The child heard her and came quickly to
her side.
	"What is it, mamma?"
	"It is so lonesome without you, darling,"
she said, drawing him to her.
	A smile lit up his face.
	"Then, do you love me, mamma?"
	"Love you? Oh, Willie!"
	Her arms were about him now and she
was sobbing on his shoulder.
	"Did somebody tell you about those bad
boys?" he asked wonderingly. "They have
got a flask of whiskey, mamma."
	"Thank God! You are saved, my dar-
ling, she cried, hysterically.
	She drew him closer to her, she clung to
him, she showered kisses on his wondering
face. But never, until he was a man, with
a son of his own, did she tell him the story
of that other Willie, whose childhood and
his had been so much alike, and how, by
the knowledge of that other Willie's un-
fortunate career, he had been saved by her
perhaps from a like fate.—Peterson's Maga-
zine.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


WOMAN’S RECORD.

	Miss Susan Fletcher Smith, an American
lady, has just made a will, assigning her
body, after death, to the Royal College of
Surgeons, on the ground that it is the duty
of every member of the human family to
devote the body after death, to scientific
examination, until the principles of physi-
ological science are thoroughly established.
It seems to us this woman possesses the ele-
ments of true benevolence and heroism.
	Mrs. M. E. Marshall, of Brooklyn, N.
Y., has opened an institution in that city,
for the benefit of working women, which is
designed to be self-supporting, and inde-
pendent of any donomination or sect. She
has opened an evening school for girls who
cannot attend the day school, where trades
will be taught, in addition to English
branches and music. A fee of fifty cents
per week is charged. The amount received
above expenses will be devoted to provid-
ing a home for homeless children, and girls
who are out of employment. A select
boarding agency has also been connected
with the institution for the benefit of the
pupils and inmates." Such an enterprise
as the above mentioned, thoroughly syste-
matic and business-like, yet in its nature
truly consistent with womanly sympathy
and generosity, is a positive demonstration
of the ability and method which women
are capable of exercising.
	"MRS. Martha Bremington, of Trenton,
Mass., recently deceased, accumulated an
independent property, in a way truly re-
markable. Although she was in business
but a few years, and at that almost a girl,
and without assistance, she left an estate
which will reach nearly 40,000. Her in-
vestments were shrewdly made and were
often exceedingly remunerative, As a
shrewd buyer of goods she had no superior
in the market. Her energy and applica-
tion to business was tireless. The deceased
made a will before her death, and is said to
have left all, or nearly all her estate to her
present husband, Mr. William S. Breming-
ton."


HOUSEHOLD HINTS.

	To make Cream Cake.—Take one pint of boil-
ing water and add to it one tablespoonful of lard;
while this is at boiling heat stir into it a little salt
and sufficient flour to form a stiff dough, and then
when cool beat into the dough five eggs. Butter
a dripping-pan and drop in the dough in separate
quantities about the size of a walnut, it will puff
up and rise and be hollow inside; bake in a very
quick oven. Prepare the cream thus: take one
quart of milk and let it boil, while boiling take
sufficient cold milk to stir in two table spoonfuls
of flour and three eggs, add this to the milk while
boiling, as you would make gravy, put in half or
two thirds of a teacupful of sugar and season with
lemon, then when the cakes are hot open them
and fill them with the cream.
	Egg Blanc Mange.—Two thirds of a box of
gelatine, one quart of milk, one cup of sugar, six
eggs, salt and flavoring to taste. Put the gela-
tine into the milk set it on the back of the stove
until it is dissolved, and then add the sugar, beat
the yolks of the eggs until they are very light, add
a gill of cold milk when the milk on the stove is
scalding hot; put the eggs in; stir constantly until
it is done which will be in a few minutes. If al-
lowed to remain too long on the fire it will curdle.
Take from the fire and continue to stir it gently
until it is quite cold, then put the mixture into a
mold and set it on ice, if the weather is warm.
This is to be eaten with cream.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


HYGIENIC NOTES.

	About Sleep.—How much sleep is necessary
to health and activity of body and mind it is hard to
determine except by individual experience; medi-
cal men and women and scientists have come to
this conclusion, after a variety of experiences and
experiments. The difference in individuals is
greater than inexperienced persens would imag-
ine. It is very generally conceded that children
should be indulged to the extent of their inclina-
tions.
	In the "Journal of Commerce" we find the fol-
lowing interesting items on this subject. Dr. Cooch
mentions a man who sleeps only fifteen minutes a
day and enjoys good health. Blaine mentions a
a man who during an active campaign, never, for
more than one year, slept more than one hour in
twenty-four; and the same is related of Charles
XII of Sweden, during his wonderful career.
Jeremy Taylor, during a part of his life, slept
only three or four hours out of the twenty-four;
and Napoleon during his military career slept only
four or five hours out of the twenty four; but he
was able to fall asleep any time in the midst of
his work when he felt drowsy. John Winter,
the great surgeon, slept four hours at night, and
one after dinner. Sir John Sinclair gives the his-
tory of a man who had reached the age of ninety-
one, and all his life had slept but four hours in
the twenty four. Sir Walter Scott said he was
not entirely himself unless he passed seven hours
in total unconsciousness. Southey required ten
hours, going to bed at ten and rising at eight,
Sir William Jones laid down the rule of life for
students in the couplet—
	Seven hours to books, to soothing slumber seven—
	Ten to the world's a[-]lot, and all to heaven.


COLERIDGE was once out on a particular
ride. The horse and the get up of the rider
were alike open to vulgar criticism. A low
wag appreciating the general effect, asked
the poet if he had met a tailor just like
himself. "Yes," was the reply; "and he
said he had lost his goose." After this
lowly triumph, his road brought him with-
in hail of a more distinguished party of
horsemen, one of whom, a sporting M. P.,
known to have been bribed by the Minis-
try, stopped him to enquire the price of
horse and rider. "The horse," was the
answer, "is a hundred guineas; as for the
rider, as he is not in Parliament yet, I have
not fixed his price."


IMPROMPTU EFFORTS.—Although pains-
taking writers produce the best work, as a
rule; it cannot be denied that some of the
most popular things in literature, especially
hymns and short poems, with now and
then a story, have been written almost
without forethought, and printed with but
little alteration. As, for instance, the mis-
sionary hymn, "From Greenland's Icy
Mountains," which Heber wrote at a few
moment's notice. And "Sheridan's ride"
has a history as felicitous. It is said of it
that its author, Reed, had Murdock the ac-
tor, for a guest, and while at breakfast, saw
in the morning journal an account of Sheri-
dan's exploit. He withdrew to his own
room, and in about an hour came out with
that poem, which Murdock read at the ben-
efit the same evening, and which in the
course of a few weeks appeared in half the
newspapers in the land.

WOMAN'S EXPONENT.

VOL. 5.		SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, DECEMBER 15, 1876.		No. 14.


THE CHILDREN'S CHRISTMAS.

	Don't forget the children, "Santa,"
		Come with baskets round and large,
	With no thought of feeling scanty
		O'er the gifts you keep in charge:
	Picture-books and dolls and dishes.
		Quiet horse and gentle dove,
	Tops and knives and dogs and fishes,
		You know what the children love.

	Don't yo[-] talk of wages meager,
		Not a dollar or a dime—
	You were once a child as eager
		For the coming Christmas time;
	Dear old Christmas rule! don't break it—
		You are not obliged to buy!
	Think of something, earn or make it,
		You can do so if you try.

	When I was a tiny maiden,
		Don't I know how glad I was,
	When I found my stockings laden
		With the gifts of "Santa Claus?"
	Then some hints of hard times taught us
		Never to complain a word,
	For we knew he always brought us
		Just the best he could afford.

	Once a home-made ball for brother,
		Buckskin cover, neat and strong;
	("Santa" knew how much our mother
		Deemed to slight the children wrong.")
	Candy birds from beet molasses.
		Ah! we thought them extra nice:—
	Fairy childhood! how it passes,
		Once forever, never twice.

	Youth is but a narrow isthmns,
		Linking infancy with age—
	Let the young folks have their Christmas,
		Bright as any pictured page.
	Older folks can reason better,
		If they crosses have to bear:
	Never, never be a debtor
		To the child that's in your care!

	Ere another year has found us,
		When such tokens may be giv'n,
	Thousands of the darlings' round us,
		May have joined the hosts in Heav'n;
	Thousands more will then be with us,
		Entering life's uncertain track;
	But their presence cannot give us,
		One departed darling back.

	Do without the roasted turkey,
		Things more costly or less dear;
	Though the sky be fair or murky,
		Keep the children's spirits clear:
	Fill up every little stocking
		Let it be a merry day;
	With your "lambs" about you f[---]king,
		Bless them, "Santa," while you may.
										LULA.
S. L. City, Dec; 13, 1876.


[Column 1]
						For the Exponent.
		DESULTORY THOUGHTS.

	Ambition is certainly a laudable passion,
when rightly cultivated and controlled, and
little is accomplished without it. Many
persons mistake, or misunderstand those
who possess it, and call them proud. Many
persons pass for being very humble when
in fact true humility does not not belong to
them; so much are we apt to misjudge our
fellow creatures. The TRULY ambitious
are alone the truly great, and such are ever
the truly humble. We see this beautifully
illustrated in the character of Esther. She
was ambitious that her kindred shonld not
become the outcasts of Israel through the
mean jealousy of a bad man, and set herself
to work to bring about their deliverance;
she fasted and prayed and commanded all

[Column 2]
her people to do the same. In order to at-
tain her aim, she had to break an impera-
tive law of the king, her husband; kings
were absolute monarchs in those days, their
whims and their words were alone the law.
Read her history, and see how beautiful
and touching is her humility, which em-
bodies strong faith in the God of her fath-
ers; her ambition and humility are both
conspicuous and harmoniously blended; and
her devotion to her cause is that of a mar-
tyr. After I have done all I can "If I per-
ish I perish!"
	It is in vain to look for a really noble char-
acter, without a fruitful and cultivated mind,
which probably "the schools" have nothing,
or very little, to do with. The foundation
must be laid in early life—yes indeed even
in the anti-natal stage of our existence, by
her who bears to us the noble name of
MOTHER, and all this is done in such mi-
nute processes that no language could por-
tray, or pen delineate; truly it is the un-
written philosophy of a life! Like the an-
cient Jewish code it is oral, or voice without
sound! O woman! thou little dreamest of
thy power for good, or for evil! Thou main
spring of a world mental, moral and corpo-
real! Molded, and fashioned, and endowed
by thy Father—God! A veil has been over
thy mental vision, and the glory that be-
longs to thee has been hid from thine eyes!
Awake from the weak and frivolous dreams
of many centuries! Awake to the glorious
realities of the present! When the gospel
light is beaming on those who have obeyed
and live to its precepts; and when the rev-
elations of heaven are for us and for our
children!
	Sacred and profane history show us noble
women; from Palestine, from Rome, Greece,
yes, every country has had its model, its
representative women, and they stand as
exemplars to all who live on the earth to-
day! What constitutes greatness? Virtue
and righteousness with all the refinement
they ever bring. Did not the ancient no-
ble women give to their conntry a race of
noble sons? Did they not rear and nurture
them to fill high and commanding stations?
Was not their ambition shown and exem-
plified in those living oracles? Did they
not exhibit their own cultivation by their
self-controlling power, often the perfect ab-
negation of self—and demonstrated they
were worthy and understood the art of
training children for Time and Eternity?
Their pride was shown when like Cornelia
they presented their children before the
vain and frivolous devotee of fashion, hung
in chains of gold and studded with bril-
liants, and in the brief but full-meaning lan-
guage of truth from a mother's heart said
"These are my jewels!" Such a woman
was fit to be the "mother" of the Gracchi,"
the Scipios, the Catos, and others of like
notoriety! I think I may assert without
fear of contradiction, that few men have
ever been truly great unless they had a
mother who sowed the germs of greatness
in their infant minds. Many great men
have borne noble testimony to their moth-
er's training—set her on the highest pinna-
cle they could place her; Napoleon, Lamar-
tine, Bulwer, and a host of such like men
has each exalted his mother as a peeress
among women.
	I do not want to see woman step out of
her sphere—that would be ridiculous—her
sphere is so large she has no need to tran-
scend or get beyond its bounds. Let her not
fear the sarcasms of those who have never
studied the matter, but let her illustrate
Miss Edgeworth's beautiful and expressive
Epitome of a Woman's Mind—or rather
what it should exhibit—"That the tide of
learning had passed over it by its general
fertility." Now let me sing a little, I have
no time for precision.

	Oh! Woman! woman "know thyself,"
		And know thy mission, too—
	Thou camest from thy Maker's hand
		A mighty work to do!

	A ministering angel, thou
		Wert sent to man to be,
	As wife, as sister, and as friend,
		A work devolves on thee!

	The mother of his children be,
		The co-mate of his care,
	In sorrow cheer, in danger rouse
		And save him from despair.

	Thou canst uphold and comfort him,
		In virtue urge him on;
	Thy love should be his rich reward,
		Thy faithfulness, his song.

	In prosperous days his greatest wealth,
		In triumph's joyous hour
	His fairest laurel thou shalt be,
		By Love's almighty power.

	Oh! may our Father, thine and mine,
		Send wisdom from above,
	That we in honor may fulfill
		The mission of His love!
						HANNAH T. KING.
S. L. City, Dec., 1876.


FEMALE DELICACY.

	Above all other features which adorn the
female character, delicacy stands foremost,
within the provisions of good taste. Not
that delicacy which is always in quest of
something to be ashamed of; which makes
merit blush, and simpers at the false con-
struction her ingenuity has placed upon an
innocent remark—this spurious kind of
delicacy is far from good sense—but that
high minded delicacy which maintains its
undeviating walk among women and the
society of men; which shrinks from no nec-
essary duty, and can speak, when required,
with seriousness and kindness of things on
which it would be ashamed to smile or
blush; that delicacy which knows how to
confer a benefit without wounding the feel-
ings of another—which gives alms withont
assumption, and pains not the most suscep-
tible being in creation.


Z. C. M. I. has a most elaborate display of
fancy goods and toys, groceries and confec-
tionary. They have quantities of all these
articles sufficient to supply the demands of
the country merchants. There is every
size and quality of doll, dressed and un-
dressed, from the queen doll—an elegant
wax figure with real golden hair—down to
the tiniest specimen possible. The newest
and rarest articles are John Gilpin on his
horse in his wonderful ride to London, cap-
able of twenty-four different changes, and a
petite gymnast performing on the horizon-
tal bar, going through all the different atti-
tudes and positions when wound up with a
crank.


106		WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
			R. S. REPORTS.

	The General Retrenchment Meeting held
in the Fourteenth Ward Assembly Rooms
Saturday, Nov. 25, was one of special in-
terest to all who are interested in the ad-
vancment and progression of WOMAN.
Counselor E. R. Snow presided, and after
the general exercises she addressed the au-
dience in her able and earnest manner.
She contrasted the position of the sisters
now, with what it was ten years ago, and
expressed her gratitude at the change which
had been effected, and gave much excellent
counsel; her words were "as apples of gold
set in pictures of silver." Counselors Zina
D. Young and Bathsheba W. Smith, and
Mrs. E. B. Wells each expressed their feel-
ings in regard to the labors to be performed
by the sisters, and exhorted all present to
diligence. They also gave their views upon
the Grain Movement and the Woman's
Book. Some other sisters bore similar tes-
timonies. Elder Junius F. Wells then ad-
dressed the sisters for a few minutes. He
related some incidents that had come under
his observation while upon his mission, and
congratulated the sisters upon the superi-
ority of their knowledge, and their favor-
able condition viewed through the under-
standing they have of the Gospel. He bore
a strong testimony to the truth of the Gospel
revealed in this "the last dispensation of
the fulness of times."

	MORONI, SANPETE Co., Nov. 29, 1876.

EDITORS' EXPONENT:

	We held our annual meeting on the 20th
of this month. A good spirit prevailed
among the sisters and all of us are willing
to carry out the counsel of President Young
in regard to storing up grain. We have
commenced already to purchase the staff of
life while we can get it at a low figure.
Bishop Bradley has kindly lent us a gran-
ary to keep the grain in until our good
brethren can build us one. He has also ta-
ken  vote of the brethren that the pro-
ceeds of all the parties during the winter
shall be appropriated for the benefit of our
society; it was unanimously carried. We
as a society desire by the help of our Fath-
er in heaven to exert all our energies to se-
cure the precious grain that will be a tem-
poral salvation to us and our little ones.
Our Savior has said that nation shall rise
against nation, and kingdom against king-
dom; and there shall be famine and pesti-
lence in the last days; also those holding
the priesth[--]d of God in our day, declare
that it is even at our doors: and the time is
near when those who have persecuted us
for righteousness sake will come bending to
our feet, a[-]king us to give them bread that
will preserve life lest they starve with hun-
ger.
	Dear sisters let us be faithful stewards
over what the Lord has put in our hands, and
not give our "children's bread to dogs;"
and if we are "faithful over a few things,
the Lord will make us ruler over many."
	Ever feeling desirous for the welfare of
Zion, and the promotion of truth, I remain
	Your sister in the Gospel,
						JANE BAILEY.

				HILLSDALE, Nov. 15, 1876.

EDITOR EXPONENT:

	Perhaps there would be no harm in tell-
ing you how we are getting along in this
little village as a branch of the Lord's
church, and a few of my thoughts. We
have no Relief Society here yet, but the

[Column 2]
young folks' Mutual Improvement Associ-
ation is alive, and there seems to be a de-
sire for improvement in our midst, and to
march along with the kingdom; yet in my
experience I have seen it necessary that
every individual, before doing any good in
this kingdom, must humble themselves be-
fore their Maker, acknowledge their un-
worthiness without His guidance and help,
and it seems to me that unless we have the
Spirit of Christ to guide us in our thoughts,
words and actions, we are of no more use
than the moth or worm. But if we seek to
imitate His life we will know of His doc-
trine. And may we as sisters seek to be
guided by Him that we may help to bring
about peace, union and love is the prayer
of your Sister &c.,
					JULIA D. WILSON.


WOMAN'S VOICE.

				SALINA, Nov. 24th, 1876.

EDITOR EXPONENT:

	I take the privilege of conveying a few
lines to you. It seems presumption in me
to assume to write to a paper, especially the
EXPONENT with its sea of intelligence. I
cannot help but notice the growth of the
sisters intellectually, from reading that pa-
per; it does one good to read it, and I re-
flect, who would do without it, were it pos-
sible to be obtained, or who would deny its
privileges? I feel, this morning, to greatly
rejoice, yea! in the spirit of testimony; this
is what compels me to write. I can bear
testimony that we have a man at the head
of this church (even President Young) who
is inspired by the Holy Ghost and is watch-
ing over this church for good, and gives us
line upon line in the due season thereof.
Now the word has come to the sisters from
our living Head to save the grain, I know
for myself he is dictated by the spirit of
God. He has called upon the Elders for
many years to lay up grain. Have they
obeyed this counsel? They can answer for
themselves I shall not condemn them.
Then sisters, mothers in Israel shall we look
up on this thing lightly and count it but a
puff of wind? O no! we will go at this
work with all our might, mind and strength,
praying God to prosper our labors. There
is a grand speculation in this, which will re-
sult in blessings upon us, our children, and
friends, even those that may be afar off.
We remember our respected Brother Kim-
ball, he said the time would come when a
barrel of flour would be worth a barrel of
gold, and I believe it, and many other
prophecies made by him.
	Now sisters, I believe that the time is
nigh at hand, when the Lord will visit His
people, some in blessings, some in scourg-
ings; those who have been wise virgins
and have oil in their lamps will be prepared
to meet the emergencies. Now is the time
to arouse ourselves and awake and trim our
lamps and keep them burning. There is
plenty of oil within our reach, it is flowing
through the land in abundance. If we are
the children of Joseph through the loins of
Ephraim, shan't we then, like our father do
the same work? He laid up grain to save
all Egypt and his father's house.
	The sisters of Selina have already com-
menced the work, and our Bishop has very
kindly offered us a place in the United Or-
der Store-house to store away our grain;
which we have gladly accepted. I see no
need of being discouraged in this matter.
If we are but the despised women of Utah,
we will yet show to the world that we live
in the Gospel light, and will protect our-


selves and not be caught unawares. Where
do the queens and great women of the
world receive revelation from, to prepare
them for the calamities that are coming up-
on the earth?
	Sisters let us be humble, prayerful and
thankful for the things we do enjoy; for
prophets and apostles, and for the privilege
given us, to lay up grain for our temporal
salvation.
		I remain your loving sister,
			CATHERINE FAHY MARTIN.


HOUSEHOLD HINTS.

	Sauce for Dressing.—Two eggs, one half cup
of sugar, butter the size of an egg, four table-
spoonfuls of boiling water. Flavor with wine or
brandy.
	English Pudding.—One cup of chopped suet,
one cup of chopped raisins, one cup of molasses,
one cup of sweet milk, five cups of flour, one tea-
spoonful of soda. Steam three hours.
	Celery Slaw.—One-half head of cabbage, the
tender part of one bunch of celery, two hard
boiled eggs, all chopped fine, mix with it two tea-
spoonfuls of sugar, two of mustard, one half tea-
spoonful of salt and pepper. Moisten with vine-
gar. 
	To Cook Celery.—The outside leaf stalks of
celery may be utilized, and a very savory dish
made by cutting them in small pieces, and boiling
in salted water until tender. Then turn off the
water and add milk, butter, and flour enough to
form a thick gravy. Serve hot.—Mrs. Rustic. 
	Horse-Radish Sauce for Roast Beef.—Mix
four table spoonfuls of grated horse-radish with
one teaspoonful of sugar, one teaspoonful of salt,
one-half teaspoonful of pepper, two teaspoonfuls of
made mustard. Mix thoroughly. Then, moisten
with sufficient vinegar to give it the consistency
of cream. Add four tablespoonful of thick
cream. Heat (not boil) it, for hot roast beef, and
for cold meat serve the sauce without heating.
	Piccalilli.—In answer to request of "Young
Housekeeper," I send the following recipe: For
one gallon of sliced, green tomatoes, (salted and
drained) take one quart of cider vinegar, one
pound of sugar, one tablespoonful each of mustard,
cloves, and sweet fennel seed. Place all in a por-
celain kettle over the fire, until the tomatoes are
cooked tender; then drain them out, and boil the
syrup down till just enough remains to cover them,
they do not need sealing. Onions may be added
if liked.—Mc.
	Bread Making.—At night take luke-warm wa-
ter, according to the quantity of bread required,
and stir in flour until it is about right for pan-
cakes, then stir in a tea-cup of lively yeast, cover
it and set in a moderately warm place to rise. In
the morning it will be light. Then mix up hard,
kneading it well. Return the bread to the pan
and let it rise, when light mold it down, repeat-
ing this three times. Then make into loaves and
when sufficiently light, bake carefully. Good
dough may be spoiled in the oven, by too hot or
too slack a fire. The molding-down process may
seem like too much trouble, but it is not in prac-
tice. The addition of some warm milk, when the
bread is mixed up hard, makes it whiter and
more tender, but it also dries up sooner. The
season makes some difference about the trouble of
making bread, as in winter it is harder to keep the
sponge of the right temperature. One of the
best bread makers I know, uses a small tin pail
to mix her sponge in and then hangs the pail
from a wire over the stove, which keeps it warm
and makes the rising speedy. In very cold
weather it is best to mix sponge in the morning,
unless one has a house that keeps moderately
warm through the night.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.		107


HIGHER.

Higher—'tis lisped by the little one
Whose earthly journey is just begun,
While his eager hands are stretched above,
And bright eyes plead with looks of love,

To mamma or to sister dear
To lift him to the window near,
That out his restless eye may look
On bird and sky and running brook.

He's burst the cradle's love-linked chain,
And gained the floor—the baby's plain;
Explores the same, then mounts on high,
And fain would reach the azure sky.

Higher—'tis now the schoolboy's cry,
And comrades are all passed swiftly by;
How wide and fair is the prospect now,
As he gains the mountain's lofty brow.

He can see where the brook flows on, away,
That he loved when a little child at play.
He has climbed far up towards the azure sky,
Where he watched the white clouds sailing by,

And he feels as he stands on this airy height,
And drinks at the fountain sparkling bright,
A joy so pure his soul within,
The monarch would give his crown to win.

Higher—'tis breathed in the midnight hour,
By one of thought from the student's bower;
He would grasp all that science and art can give,
He would win for himself a name to live.

Wisdom and knowledge must yield their store,
And the soul still pants for more, still more;
For higher to soar in the realms of thought,
The loftiest prize most eagerly sought.

Thus spirits immortal can ne'er be at rest,
'Till they hold in their grasp the purest and best,
Until highest they stand in that ransomed band,
And join in the joys of our Fatherland.

Then higher—be ever our watchword and aim,
God and the right let us ever maintain;
Shrink not, nor falter, be fearless and bold,
And press on—it will lead to the City of Gold.

										HOPE.


[Column 1]
			CORRESPONDENCE.

EDITORS EXPONENT:

	I am hardly vain enough to think that
any one else but myself, and perhaps your-
self has noticed how long it is since any of
my scribbles have found their way to your
valuable little paper. But I should most
assuredly have written long enough ago
had not the destroying angel cast his dark
shadow across our happy threshold. But
thank God, through the administrations of
the priesthood, his hand has been lessened
and we are almost free from his presence.
Every one of [--] our little family has felt
his power, and my babe is still quite weak
and low. Excuse me for taking up so much
space in talking of my own affairs; but I felt
it my duty to explain my seeming neglect
of promise.
	In accordance with the promise in my
last letter I will endeavor to give your read-
ers a faint idea of the grandeur and beauty
of the greatest curiosity in U. T., viz., the
Temple in this city.
	Perhaps it would be well to give some
idea of St. George, its surrounding, and its
principal buildings. We will fancy that in
traveling here, we reach Washington about
5 o'clock p. m., where we stop a few mo-
ments to water the thirsty animals and take
a look at the Factory. Then onward we
proceed, riding due west; passing through
Middletown, going over rough, rocky dug-
ways, and sandy bottoms, until about half a
mile of the city; when suddenly wheeling,
we ride directly south for another quarter
of a mile. All this while we have not had
a glimpse of the city, although we have
seen for some moments past the green fields
at the southern end of the valley, with the
Rio Virgin lazily wending its way from
east to west in the center of the fields.

[Column 2]
Thumping over rocks, dragging through
the sand—we see just ahead, a little hill
aronnd which the road winds, then swing
round the little bluff facing directly west
once more, when, Presto! Here you are,
within a few rods of the much talked
of city of St. George. Stop the horses
a moment, and give us time to look at
this beautiful little city; remember we
are at the north-eastern extremity of the
valley, with the settlement directly facing
us.  (In the north is a line of red-sand hills,
terminating at the north-western extremity
of the valley by a picturesque, perpendicu-
lar cliff. There is a pass here, through
which we go to Santa Clara, Pine Valley,
and other adjoining settlements. On the
west is a line of black hills, the duplicate
of which comprises the eastern boundary;
while on the south, the eye is relieved by a
glimpse of blue, old fashioned mountains.
This is quite a small valley, being but two
miles square. The attention is then at-
tracted from these uninteresting hills and
mountains to the fair loveliness of the pure
white Temple, contrasted as it is with the
green foliage of the city, the red sand of
the valley, and the deep blue of the even-
ing sky. One pearly cloud is sailing over-
head, while the evening sun is casting its
farewell beams upon the white dome; we
might fancy, that in the stream of mellow,
golden light, we see angel forms flitting to
and fro from their heavenly dwelling-place,
to this, their Master's House on earth; and
we are almost sure, that, in the calm, still,
evening air, we hear angel voices chanting
praises to Almighty God for His infinite
goodness and mercy.
	We are reminded by the sun dropping
out of sight, behind the black ridge, it is
time to drive into town. We can but no-
tice however, as we pass along, that St.
George is cozily nestled right up against
the red hills, forming the valley boundary
on the north, in fact some houses are creep-
ing up the hills. We are quite tired now,
and fully appreciate the hearty welcome we
are sure to receive from the hospitable
brethren and sisters of this goodly city.
	Next morning, anxious to get a nearer
view of the noble edifice that stirs such
indescribable feelings within the breast.
We are rather surprised to see so substantial
and fine looking buildings "way down in
Dixie." However we spend little time in
speculating on it, but wend our way to the
Temple Block. Riding south on the prin-
cipal street, we pass on the right, the St.
George Hall, Telegraph Office, Co-op Store,
Bakery and Tithing Office; on the corner of
the next block we see the Tabernacle,
which, permit me to say, with the excep-
tion of the Temple, is the finest building
in the Territory. This building together
with the rest of the public buildings in this
little city, are built of red sand-stone and
present a very beautiful appearance. The
tabernacle faces east, and at that end has a
square tower on which rests a tall, graceful
spire; as we look down the street going
west, the sign of one of our old friends,
swinging lazily in the wind, announces that
Dr. A. B. Dunford is following his profess-
ion of Dentistry. On the street looking east,
is the Post Office. Going a little west of
south, there is nothing worthy of special
mention, except perhaps, that the people, as
an almost universal rule, build story and a
half houses, instead of raising them a lit-
tle higher and making them habitable,
which would cost but a trifle more.
	About ten minutes ride finds us on the
open space surrounding the Temple. If we
were charmed with its beauty at night, we
are awed by its grandeur in the day. Un

[Column 3]
der the shadow of its massive walls we feel
a[-] though we are in the presence of some-
thing so holy and sacred that our tongue
represses its utterance. We are incapa-
ble almost of thinking. We can only feel
ourself an atom in the greatness of the
presence that overshadows these walls.
	The main entrance faces east. There are
two, one on each side of the tower. The
steps are made of red sand-stone, each step
being one massive rock. They are wide
and sweeping, and give the building a free,
noble look. The buttresses between each
window, the parapets on top, and the large
tower at the eastern end, add more to the
picturesqueness and grandeur of the build-
ing than can well be imagined. It is built
of red sand stone, (the foundation of black
rock) and is plastered over with a clear
white. It stands on a rising swell in the
center of the valley. The tower is sur-
mounted with an octagon dome. The win-
dows are 12 ft. high on the first floor, above
these are circular windows about 3[-] ft.
square. Still above these are another row
of long windows, the same heighth as the
lower ones, and crowning these, comes an-
other row of circular windows. There are
two main halls 27 ft. high and 99 x 78 ft.
across. On each side of the arching roof
above the main rooms are eight rooms on
each side, 32 rooms in all. The main stair-
ways are in the north-east and south-east
towers and are circular, also several private
stairways connecting the different floors.
There is a cistern under the main tower
which will contain when full, 12,550 gal-
lons of water. There are three square rooms
in the main tower, which are perhaps in-
tended for vestry rooms. The rooms are all
beautifully finished off with plaster of paris
cornices and centre-pieces, (some of the fin-
est work in the western country.) The
whole of the wood work is painted a spot-
less white. The doors in the outer courts,
leading into the separate rooms are chaste
and elegant; no flashy carving, but all fin-
ished off with the nicest skill. Altogether
it has a simple yet grand, quiet but impos-
ing appearance, that is quite in keeping
with the purpose for which it has been de-
signed. Passing out we can but reflect that
it looks like a fair pure bride, almost ready
to receive the crowning presence of her
Lord.
	The feelings that have been awakened in
the heart are too profound for expression;
and we go from the edifice with the holiest
emotions, determined to live a purer, better
life.
	In another letter I will try to give a de-
scription of the famous Johnson Gardens,
and other principal objects of interest here.
	Remembering myself to my friends in
Salt Lake, I will close; ever praying for the
success of the EXPONE[-]T. I am,
			Yours respectfully,
							S. Y. D.


A GENERIC DIFFERENCE.—School girls
out for a walk: 1st schoolgirl—"I am so
tired of walking along by two and two in
this way; it's as bad as the animals that
went into the Ark." 2nd school girl—"It's
worse, for half of them were masculines."
	FOR DOCTORS.—"Massa, can you tell me
de fust ting a doctor does when he goes to
see a sick man?"
	"Why he looks at his tongue, of course."
	"And can a doctor tell what's de matter
wid de man by looking at his tongue?"
	"Of course he can."
	"Den I want a doctor."
	"Are you unwell, sir?"
	"No, but I have got a wagon dat got
someting de matter wid it, and de wagon
is got a tongue.
