SALT LAKE CITY. DEC. 15, 1876.


UNITED WE STAND, DIVIDED
				WE FALL.

	If this saying, so oft repeated and so often
verified in many instances which come under
general observation, is applicable to the condi-
tion of the government of the United States at
the present time, then surely it is bound to
fall. It is apparently so equally divided that
the majority cannot be determined upon with-
out introducing some new experimental meas-
ures. Experiments in many cases are in-
jurious and injudicious. In testing govern-
ments, it seems to us they would be peculiarly
so. A government that cannot be maintained
in an emergency in and of itself will scarcely
be improved by new measures.
	The President's Message seems most indiffer-
ently and unsatisfactorily worded, and the re-
ports from different sources, though conflicting,
still lead to one strong conclusion in the minds
of the people, that is, that this stupendous
fabric, the glorious constitution, is about to be
crushed in the great collision of the two politi-
cal parties. Telegrams say South Carolina has
two houses and two governors. Is the United
States to have two houses and two Presidents?
If they could only look consistently at the
final result of such immoderate language as
they are using towards each other, they could
see how it would stir up strife, and strife wag-
eth war.
	"The fathers have eaten sour grapes and the
children's teeth are set on edge;" this applies
admirably to the present national condition;
they have gone on corrupting public offices,
trespassing upon the peoples rights, commit-
ting governmental frauds, until it is no longer
bearable, and each party wants to implicate
the other, and as in thousands of other cases so
in this, one story is good till the other is told,
and when you hear both sides you wonder how
Congress and the House of Representatives
have sustained their position at all on such
hypocritical and unsubstantial BASING. In
contemplation of the present and future it be-
hooves the Latter-day Saints to look well to
themselves, and put their trust in the arm
that is powerful to save, and if in any possible
way they can avoid being party to any of the
schemes for ruining and overthrowing the
government, then are they favored of the Lord,
for surely the Lord has said judgments will
come, but wo unto him by whom they come.
"Let him laugh who wins," the saints can af-
ford to wait, and in the intervals of peace and
plenty prepare wisely for the day of trouble
and scarcity. Seek diligently unto the Lord
to be taught in His ways lest any be led astray
and partake of the plagues and scourges pro-
nounced upon the ungodly.
		[handwritten] E. B. Wells.


WOMEN'S COMMISSION HOUSE.

	This house established recently upon a self-
sustaining principle, without capital, for the
sale of home products and manufactures, thus
giving encouragement and further promoting
facilities for home industries, ought to receive
the patronage of every person who has the
best interests of Zion at heart. It is one of
the noblest schemes ever devised and those who
are laboring so diligently to establish it should
receive the most hearty commendation. It is
an enterprise which will be the nucleus of a
great culminating system of financiering with-
in the Territory. Many hands make labor
light, and union is strength. Then let the wo-
men of Utah prove to the world by their unit-
ed co-operation in this and other matters that
they are equal to any enterprise they may un-
dertake.
	The Woman's Store recently received a beau-
tiful assortment of artificial flowers suitable
for hats, bonnets, head dresses caps etc. The
patronage of the ladies of Utah and their in-
fluence is solicited for the Woman's Commis-
sion House. Ladies please call and examine,
see what Utah is capable of producing at home.


HOME AFFAIRS.

	CHRISTMAS IS coming, and in anticipation of
its arrival the indefatigable Mr. James Dwyer,
whose efforts to cater to the tastes of the citi-
zens and children of Salt Lake City, have
hitherto been so eminently successful, has open-
ed his holiday stock of goods making the most
brilliant and elaborate display of his style of
goods which has ever been made in this city,
in honor probably of the Centennial Christ-
mas of America. On the evening of the open-
ing Dec. 11, the store and side-walk in front of
it were crowded so that it was almost impossi-
ble to obtain egrees. He has a full stock of all
the standard poets, novelists and historians,
and works upon science and art. He has also
many choice works upon all subjects, and a
full supply of the school books now in use;
every variety of paper pencils etc. Russian
leather goods of all descriptions and beautiful
designs. Perforated card board with or with-
out stamping. Ladies' fine cutlery, elegant
card cases, writing desks and work-boxes of
nearly every variety from the most elegant,
down to the plainest workmanship. In short,
he has got in his assortment any and every
article of fancy goods suitable for Holiday
gifts. Go and examine for yourself and you
will be sure to find something suited to your
taste and resources.


OBITUARY.

	The many friends of Sister CAROLINE
AUGUSTA JACKSON, of St. George, will be
pained to hear of her death, which occurred at
half past seven o'clock on the morning of the
7th inst., in that city, of pneumonia.
	Sister Jackson was born at Hampton, N. H,
and was aged 50 years, 4 months, and 23 days.
She embraced the gospel of Jesus Christ at the
age of 17 years, leaving her father's house as
the cost, and in February 1864, embarked upon
the ship Brooklyn, with her husband and in-
fant daughter, arriving in California six
months later, to endure the terrors of war and
famine for many months. Enriched by the
gold discovery she gave liberally to the poor,
and kept for twenty years open house for the
traveling Elders of the Church. She collected
sixty children made orphans by cholera, and
bore their entire expenses for six months,


meanwhile exerting her influence successfully
toward the foundation of the still existing
Protestant Orphan Asylum in San Francisco,
and was one of the first board of managers."
	During Buchanan's administration, Col.
(then), Thomas Kane passed through San Ber-
nardino, Gal., and was searched for by a mob
who divined him and his mission; she render-
ed him such service that he ever after held her
in devoted remembrance, and when, four win-
ters ago he met herself and husband in St.
George, his tears, rather than his words, ex-
pressed his emotions.
	The evening before he left St. George, he
called late to bid her good-bye. She went with
him to the door; the night was dark, windy,
and raining. She gave him her shawl, which
he wrapped around him: Extending his hand,
he said, "this night is like the political horizon.
Stay you in camp, eat roast beef and rest; I
will go in the storm, and stand on picket guard
for you" Good-bye, thrice repeated, and he
was gone into the dark night.
	After her arrival in Utah she settled in St.
George, and was the first Treasurer of the St.
George Relief Society, and afterward appoint-
ed Secretary. She was an agent for the EX-
PONENT in that city.
	Her name had been mentioned as one of the
sisters to labor in that Temple, and she had
extended the offer of a home to certain of the
brethren called to labor in the Temple. Her
last work was the writing of her testimony for
The Woman's Book; after which she went to
the gate with a friend, receiving a cold which
ended her life one week after, passing away in
sleep, and without pain.—COM.


TOOELE CITY, NOV. 10, 1876.
EDITOR EXPONENT:

	We the sisters of Tooele would like to repre-
sent ourselves in the WOMAN'S EXPONENT. I
can say that peace health and prosperity pre-
vail, for which we are thankful to our heaven-
ly Father. We meet in the new meeting
house every two weeks, where we enjoy the
society of one another and get our spiritual
strength renewed. We hope soon to have our
new hall finished, it is progressing slowly; we
want to get it covered in as soon as possible so
that we can give parties in it this winter in or-
der to raise means to help defray the expenses.
Our President, Sister Micklejohn is most un-
tiring and energetic in her labors and has the
full confidence and love of all the sisters. The
spirit and blessing of our heavenly Father is
with us. Our new hall is for a Library and
Reading Room and one room is expressly for
the Relief Society. Sister Delamare and my-
self raised a few silk worms this summer for a
beginning in Tooele, and hope that this will not
be the last of it, as there are plenty of mulberry
trees here and plenty of little girls that could
lend a helping hand. We had a very pleasant
visit from Sister Howard two weeks ago at the
Young Ladies Retrenchment Meeting. She
gave some very valuable instructions to the
young ladies; but was very sorry to see so few
in attendance. We highly prize the EXPO-
NENT for it is full of the purest gems of light
and love; we get a feast when it comes. I am
thankful that I am numbered among the Lat-
ter-day Saints, and it is my humble prayer that
we may all endure faithful to the end.
	I remain your sister, etc,
							ANN TATE.


SERICULTURE.

	Minutes of a special meeting of the D. B. As-
sociation, held in the Fireman's Hall, Dec. 7,
Mrs. Barney presiding. Mr. P. A. Schettler


felt proud of our success the past season. He
exhibited some fine specimens of silk that had
been reeled in Logan, which went to show
that those interested in this branch of home-
industry are making rapid progress. He stat-
ed that this season from the small expense of
forty-five dollars for the raising of the cocoons,
he received a profit of eighty dollars, receiving
two dollars per pound for cocoons; and that
amount of cocoons when reeled into raw silk
would bring a net profit of one hundred and
twenty dollars, paying at the rate of three dol-
lars a pound for reeling. Some samples of silk
had been sent to Patterson, New Jersey, and
other places, and an answer received that it
was worth twelve dollars per pound, which
was very encouraging to the society and ought
to give us more courage another year; those
wishing to secure mulberry cuttings should get
them now and keep them in the cellar until
the frost is out of the ground next spring, and
then set them out.
	Mrs. Barney said that the prospects of our
home industries were brightening and espec-
ially that of silk, and this association hoped
that all those who might have felt discouraged
by not finding a market for their cocoons,
would take new courage as we could now reel
our silk at home, and get a remunerative price
for it. All of our farmers [-]hould set out mul-
berry trees next spring. By doing so they
would be helping one of the home industries
that ere long will rank among the best, and be
enriching themselves and the community,
by keeping hundreds of dollars at home which
is now sent out of the Territory every year.
	The meeting was addressed by others and
all felt encouraged.
	Meeting adjourned till the first Wednesday
in January, 1877, at the Firemen's Hall.
				R. SIMPSON, SECRETARY.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.		109


[Column 1]
	NOTES FROM A CORRESPONDENT.

	You asked me in your last, to give you all
the political information I could. Now the
political matters of this nation are one stupend-
ous fraud from beginning to end; but as this
does not preclude the possibility of telling the
truth about it, I will endeavor to respond to
the requirement as far as my natural abil-
ities will permit. But, to come to the point,
there is one thing which has come under my
notice while here, whether it will bear a polit-
ical construction or not I leave you to judge.
One thing certain though, political and legis-
lative tyranny is the cause. The matter to
which I allude is the hard times now so preva-
lent throughout the country, and which, poor
people are deluded into the belief will change.
Aunt Em, the "good time coming" to this na-
tion as a body, is a myth and delusion which
the rich and proud are in the habit of using to
beguile the minds of the poor and hard-work-
ing classes while they bind their oppressive
chains the tighter.
	The state of affairs in this region, at the
present time is the state of affairs throughout
the United States. Corruption and tyranny
are the characteristics of those who are in
power, poverty and misery those of the work-
ing classes.
	The branches of industry, the more remuner-
ative ones, are in the hands of, and con-
trolled by a few rich men who beside this are
the members of the legislature; and they in-
stead of working for the good of the people at
large are banded together for self and use the
power placed in their hands, for the purpose of
robbing the poor and enriching themselves.
It might be said that the whole state itself,

[Column 2]
is owned by these few; for not only can
they control the capital of the country, but
even the lives liberties and consciences of her
citizens.
	As an instance. Among the branches of in-
dustry here, coal mining is the most im-
portant; especially in the place where I now
am is this the case, and the companies who
work the mines also own or control the rail-
roads.
	In the adjoining large cities, large store
houses are built and constantly supplied with
coal, as the demand increases or diminishes.
While the demand is equal to the supply all
goes well, the rich man pockets his dividend,
and the poor miner can support his family.
But should the state of the market change,
and the coal on hand begin to accumulate
while the owners dividend decreases corres-
pondingly, the order is given, the owners shut
down, discharge their hands who depend on
the employment for their daily bread, and the
coal houses and yards, or rather the superin-
tendents advance their prices till they pay the
same dividend, 10 per cent. for coal is always
in demand to some degree; thus you see,
while the rich man loses nothing by the
change, the poor man is in danger of starva-
cion. And not only this, but when they can-
not keep their own increase up to the estab-
lished line, they lower the wages of their em-
ployees, or let them lay idle half the time.
All this time provisions are as high as ever,
wages may lower, but provisions are the same
or higher. And the worst is this, that it is the
poor man's fault. They dare not vote for other
men than those who employ them, they would
be discharged, and thus this system of coer-
tion is the means of placing these tyrants in
power, while the laboring classes, not being
united, forge the very chains they are groan-
ing under. If the superintendent of a mine
votes for Hayes and Wheeler, or vice versa,
his employees dare not do otherwise; their
bread and butter are at stake and they submit
to having their liberties stripped from them,
their consciences violated by being obliged to
uphold men whom they fear and despise, and
their rights trampled on by the laws of a coun-
try which boasts of freedom and equal rights
to all.
	Who can view this picture without disgust-
Does it not foreshadow something terrible?
We can but come to this conclusion, the hand
of Almighty God is on this nation, and the
day of their destruction, which has been pre-
dicted by the holy prophets is close at hand.
They are nearly ripe in iniquity, and the
wrath of the Almighty is but staid, in mercy,
to give them a chance to repent.
	Woe betide those who harden their hearts
against the truth, for the edict has gone forth
and the Lord has spoken.
	Good honest men as rulers make "good
times." This country cannot boast of either
at this time.


THE BLESSINGS OF RETRENCH-
		MENT ASSOCIATIONS.

	In the first place we never realized our
positions or mission on the earth until these
institutions were brought forth by the in-
spiration of heaven. Can we imagine what
would have been the condition of the
Daughters of Zion to-day had not the Lord
in His mercy conferred this blessing upon
on us. It certainly could not have been as
favorable as it is. Many can look back to
their first associations in these societies as
to the dawning of a new and happier life,


because of having become acquainted with
their heavenly Father, and the principles
of the holy gospel which bringeth joy and
and peace that the world can neither give
nor take away. A continual growth in the
things of God can be observed in those who
have been faithful to their duties in these
societies from the first. They have learned
to govern self, and have to a great degree
gained power over the evil propensities of
their nature, and temptations of daily life,
have learned to seek the good of their fel-
low creatures, and have proven that their
greatest happiness consists in ministering
to the comforts of others. We have learn-
ed that heaven must be begun at home, that
if we ever truly enjoy a heaven, we must
exert all our powers to assist in making it
ourselves, and help establish the principles
of truth and righteousness upon the earth,
that there may be a people prepared for the
second coming of the Lord and Savior
when He comes to take vengeance upon
the wicked and all who know him not.
We can also comprehend something o[-]
that charity of which Paul speaks, "which
suffereth long and is kind, thinketh no evil,
is not easily provoked, hopeth all things,
endureth all things," to make the allowance
for our fellowbeings that we desire they
and the Lord should make for us; consid-
ering that when we have striven to our ut-
most we are yet unprofitable servants. We
are taught to keep the Spirit of God
continually with us, to purify our every
thought and feeling, that we may have
claim upon the blessings of the Lord. In
fine, the instructions we receive include
everything that is pure, noble and exalting,
and are calculated to make those who ac-
cept them and carry them out in their lives
great and mighty women in the kingdom
of God, and finally exalt them in His pres-
ence. Then should we not be grateful for
this boon? Yes, we should give thanks daily
to our Father, and prove our appreciation
of this blessing by being faithful to all the
covenants we have made, carrying out in
our lives every commandment that is given
through the servants of God, being willing
that ourselves and all we possess shall be
used for the [-]urtherance of His righteous
purposes, "For what doth it profit a man if
a gift is bestowed upon him and he receiv-
eth not the gift, Behold he rejoiceth not in
that which is given him, neither rejoiceth
in Him who is the Giver of the gift." [Doc-
trine and Covenants, p. 99.] Dear sisters,
let us receive and rejoice in the opportuni-
ties given us through the Retrenchment
Associations, which will, if improved, bring
u[-] knowledge and happiness both here and
hereafter.
							MARY A. E. [handwritten] [---]
	S. L. City, Oct. 18, 1876.


Spanish Cream.—One half box of gelatine, one
quart of milk, four eggs, beaten separately, one
cup of sugar. Take a small quantity of the milk
to soak the gelatine, put on the back of the stove.
Let the remainder of the milk boil. Mix the
yolks and sugar together and stir quickly into the
scalding milk, let boil about a minute, then take off
and stir in the soaked gelatine, put aside for fif-
teen or twenty minutes, then stir in the whites of
the eggs, by the spoonful, slowly and thoroughly,
and flavor to taste with vanilla. Dip your molds
in cold water and pour in the cream to cool.


Lemon Jelly.—Take one package of gelatine,
add one pint of cold water, juice of six lemons;
let them stand one hour, then add one pint of boil-
ing water, two lbs. of white crust sugar, strain and
cool.


110		WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
	BELIEF AND UNBELIEF IN GOD.

	READ BEFORE THE WASATCH LIT-
			ERARY ASSOCIATION.

	This may seem a peculiar theme for one
of so little experience as myself to com-
ment upon, yet in consideration of my in-
terest upon the subject it is not at all inap-
propriate.
	I find there are many whose minds ought
to be firmly established on this matter, who
waver, and doubt, and query and wonder
and do everything almost, except humble
themselves and ask help from a divine
source; even this it will be said requires
some faith in God to do, and this belief it
will be averred, is not strong enough in the
heart to warrant it. Then there is something
serious the matter; for every intelligent
person is born with inherent principles of
reverence for a Supreme Being, unless they
are heathen who worship idols, or perhaps
in rare instances the children of ungodly
parents.
	In this kingdom we are all taught from our
earliest childhood to believe in God, and it is
something which we should cultivate and
retain after we arrive at years of discretion,
instead of encouraging doubt and uncer-
tainty upon the subject, and arguing to find
out whether we really are believers or not.
	The great end and aim of life (very gen-
erally conceded) is the pursuit of happiness;
the whole world will tell you there is neith-
er perfect rest nor happiness upon this earth
in its present condition.
	Then shall we admit we are pursuing a
phantom which eludes us when seemingly
within our grasp, or shall we so harmonize
ourselves with our individual being and
surroundings as to be able to bear all things
philosophically, looking forward to that hap-
piness in the eternal, which shall more than
compensate us for endurance of trials here.
	The Author of our existence would not
leave His children without a law, when He
has so firmly fixed the laws and order of all
inanimate things, so that they fulfill the
laws of their creation and bring forth in
due season and keep their appointed times.
Judging from nature as we term it, we may
draw this conclusion, that a God who would
so carefully devise and plan the workings
of the most minute specimen of that which
possesses not an immortal gem, would by
His care for that which must exist forever,
devise the most perfect laws by which they
may through their own agency attain the
highest degree of glory and everlasting
happiness. Perhaps you will say I have
taken the ground that there is a God with-
out proving it. I grant you so much, for
there is a testimony of His existence dwell-
ing within me which it shall be my first
duty to retain. This very witness, or faith
which I have in His existence, aids me in
contemplating His purposes, in understand-
ing some little of His laws and the princi-
ples laid down for those to follow, who
would be like unto Him and arrive to that
perfection which should, in my opinion, be
our highest emulation. I can see no beauty,
no happiness, no merit of any sort in unbe-
lief; consequently, to my mind the very
idea tends downward.
	There are so many ills and crosses in life,
so many disappointments, so much that is
disagreeable and unpleasant to be borne,
that one needs all the stimulus of the no-
blest qualities and highest aims to pass
through life unscathed. There are beset-
ments on over hand to draw aside the un-
wary, and tempt them from the paths of
rectitude and honor; and human nature is
weak and prone to err; therefore there is

[Column 2]
need of some strong support and where
does it lie? Can we find balm in Gilead?
Is there perfect trust in any source except
our faith in God? I think not. Death may
snatch from us the nearest and dearest treas-
ures, those in whom we place the most im-
plicit confidence often prove false to us in
our greatest need, our hearts misgive us,
feeling our weakness and insufficiency, and
unless we have faith in God we are liable to
fall at any moment.
	Therefore my friends and companions,
you whose welfare here and hereafter is
dear to me (and I trust, to one another,) let
me entreat you not to allow yourselves to
get into darkness and unbelief, lest in an evil
hour the Holy Spirit which has been given
to all who have obeyed the Gospel, should
be grieved away and you be left to wrestle
with temptations, alone, unaided by Him
who has said "His spirit will not always
strive with man."
	The pathway of duty is the safest road
to happiness, and we owe a duty to our
parents which we can never repay if we
grieve them through unbelief. This should
make us cautious in acccepting any other
principles unless we could feel assured with-
in ourselves that we should through those
principles become capable of doing a great-
er amount of real good.
	I know it is customary to talk of being
led blindly, and having no knowledge for
one's self, but what sound reasoning is there
for the sceptic? Is not that all uncertainty
and doubt? And supposing even one actu-
ally reasons one's self out of the belief in
God what comfort or satisfaction is afforded
by it or what benefit is it to mankind?
	On the other hand the consciousness of
the hope one has in the justice and mercy
of an alwise Father and His constant watch-
care and guidance which will not allow
even a sparrow to fall to the ground with-
out His notice, is always under, all circum-
stances of however painful, or distressing a
nature, a solace and panacea which affords
more real consolation than all other things
without it.
	Too much controversy stupefies us, and
we may be overwhelmed with a confused
jumble of sophisms and truths and be so
perplexed thereby as to distrust everybody,
which would be the most dangerous state of
the human mind. We who live in the
days of Gospel light, whose parents have
sought so faithfully to train us up in the
nurture and and admonition of the Lord,
cannot afford to wander after the doctrines
and theories of men and forsake the Lord
and His ways. Therefore we should seek
to retain the Holy Spirit, the Comforter,
whom Jesus said He would send to guide
us into all truth.
							[-] M.
		[handwritten] Emma Wells
	Salt Lake City, Dec. 6, 1876.


For the EXPONENT.
				WOMAN.

	How few there are who realize that every
thought, word or deed go to make up their
general appearance and true character, and
leave their inevitable mark upon their face
as well as help clothe the soul in light
which is from God, or darkness which only
comes from a depraved condition of the
heart.
	How few there are that can conceive the 
good or evil that may come from even se-
cret thoughts the influence of which are as
lasting as time, and may be as boundless as
eternity. She that thinks, talks and acts
nobly, she that strives to live up to her

[Column 2]
high privilege, she who is truly what God
made her, a Woman, is one of the greatest
works of the Creator, a blessing to all both
high and low; her influence for good is as
boundless as the sun's light, and her power
is something wonderful. Look at a true
woman as wife, mother, or sister, and say
who or what can bound her influence, or
who can weigh her power, for though she
rules with love, which is God-like, yet her
love is more powerful than the sword or all
the engines of death combined. When in
her presence we feel her hallowed influence,
and though we may but look upon her, we
go away feeling truly blessed that we have
been permitted to receive of that wealth of
loveliness which can only come from a pure
fount, and we in a measure impart to oth-
ers the sweet influence which lingers with
us, blessing those with whom we associate.
Why woman should be called "the weaker
sex" I know not, unless it be her deep and
pure love which gives her the lasting faith
(of which men so frequently take the ad-
vantage) for she being pure thinketh no
evii, until her eyes are opened to see that
she like "Another Eve" has been deceived
by Satan appearing in form of man instead
of a serpent, and when once deceived she is
often left like the vine torn by ruthless
hands, dragged from its lofty place upon the
branches of the towering oak where it has
climbed to bask in the warm sunlight, to
the ground from whence no one cau[-]re-
place it, or even tries to lift it up, and is left
to be trampled under foot and die[---] Thus is
woman when betrayed and left with no one
but God to pity or see that a soul is lost or
saved, and though she lies far beneath the
sphere for which she was made, like the
flower that in being crushed imparts its
rich perfume even under the foot of its de-
stroyer, her soul ofttimes goes out in earnest
prayer in behalf of him who has brought
her low—he whom once she loved as only
woman can love.
	But what is woman to man? To some
she is only a toy to be put aside when tired
of; to others she is a gay butterfly whose
colors they admire, to another she is but an
object or convenience to keep his home in
order and attend to his every care and
want; to some she is a pretty thing as is a
favorite horse or dog, for her sweet face or
form which others admire, he is proud to
say she is his wife, and to the man who can
appreciate her, woman is what vegetation
is to the earth, what the sun is to the flow-
er, what the rose is to the thorny bush,
what the diamond is to the gold in which it
is set, or what the sight is to the eye. She
is what God intended her to be, a part of
his very life, that abundantly enters into all
that is good in him, bearing an impression
for both time and eternity.
	The chords of her pure love twine around
his heart and bring out his noble manhood,
raising his aspirations to things that are
great and good, and when he is about to
faint or fall, she in her rich wealth of good-
ness imparts to him of her strength of soul
by her helping hand, encouraging words,
good counsel, or by but a look from the
love-lit eyes, (which appear to him the win-
dows of heaven) and he is encouraged to
make a greater struggle, strengthened by
the aid of her noble nature, and perchance
attains the goal which may be his salva-
tion.
	A man's name, often goes forth as one of
tne great and honorable men of his age, by
the help given him through woman's influ-
ence; or if he stands among men as does the
giant of the forest among its smaller neigh-
bors, and by storms of adversity is felled to
the ground, [---] like the v[--]e which still


clings to the trunk of the great tree, bud-
ding and putting forth its bright foliage,
making it if possible, more beautiful than
when standing, honors him by placing
around his name the laurels he has won.
	The picture is not too highly colored,
and though all may not be able to attain
greatness here, yet their good works are
none the less important.
	How many there are whose whole life
seems to be composed of beautiful thoughts,
words and deeds; whose souls so expand
with charity that they take  in the whole
world with their love for God and His crea-
tures, forgetful of self are ever able to help
in some way all who are brought within
their godlike influence, until their whole
being is one great song of praise to the
Creator and blessings to their fellow man,
and it may truly be said of such a woman
"a thing of beauty is a joy forever."
	R.
S. L. City, Dec. 5th, 1876.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
	SOMETHING ABOUT HOLIDAYS.

	Little children always expect something
new and pleasant at Christmas time. I rec-
ollect perfectly when I was a little girl how
I used to tease my mother and big sisters
and everybody else, even grandpa, to tell
me stories and sing me songs about fai-
ries and all sorts of funny things, including
Santa Claus and his wonderful maneuver-
ing. I always like to interest myself in the
children. I know that the time we bestow
upon them is not wasted time, if we are in-
culcating in their young and tender minds
such germs as shall expand and grow into
principles of truth, and knowledge of right
and wrong. Mothers do not grudge the
time you bestow in preparing surprises for
the little folks. How beautifully nature has
ornamented the city for the advent of our
yearly holiday. Can any lover of the pic-
turesque fail to admire the tasteful and
graceful ornamenting of the trees, shrub-
bery, and crystal glass? All these furnish the
most delightful winter landscape in the val-
ley, and looking afar to the tops of the ma-
jestic mountains, how sublimely grand their
hoary tops tower above us like the "ever-
lasting hills." It seems as though the king
and queen of fairyland had been decorating
the city and all its surroundings for the me-
morial of the birth of the blessed Redeem-
er, leaving only the work of preparing our
own dwellings and a sanctuary in our hearts
fit to cherish His love and His life. If we
would imitate Him whose birth we Christ-
mas-day commemorate, we must be full of
charity and loving kindness, doing good
deeds, be they in ever so simple a way.
	Let no frowns be upon the little faces,
but smiles and joyous words, songs full of 
praise and rejoicing for all the manifold
blessings given us here. Cheer every one
around you with the gladness of your happy
childhood; if yon have not the very gift
you longed for, be grateful for that which
you have, and think how many little chil-
dren there are in the world who would be
glad to have even a comfortable home and
warm fireside, and if you know any who
are not as well provided for as you are,
any little orphans, or lonely; sorrowful ones,
let it be your first errand to share with
them your own gifts, and see if it does not
prove the truth of the Savior's words, "It
is more blessed to give than to receive."
When I was a little girl we used to deco-
rate our homes with evergreens and all the
varieties of scarlet berries, which abound
profusely in the New England woods, but
in this new country we have not yet all

[Column 2]
the varieties of winter vines and shrubs,
but all in good time; we have many things
we can beautify our homes with, and every
year prosperity increases in these vaileys,
and if we can teach our little children to
be grateful to the Giver of all blessings the
time will come when no good thing will be
withheld. The city in its frosty adorning
reminds us of the land of enchantment; and
is most peculiarly adaptable to the season of
holiday enjoyment.
	There are many pleasant games for indoors
suitable for the children which we hope
they will appreciate with merry laughter,
glad voices and happy hearts. Think of all
your sweetest songs to sing on the anniver-
sary of that blessed morning when Jesus
was born in Bethlehem, and let the merry
Christmas welcome spring from the depths
of your joyous hearts, to usher in the first
glad morning of the winter holidays. Do
not let one frown on your face darken the
light of your home, but laugh, and shout, and
sing merrily. And do not forget the poor,
nor the sick, nor even the dumb animals,
feed and pet them—your dogs and cats, or
your little canaries, and throw out plenty
of crumbs for the little snowbirds, and love
every creature God has made, for He pro-
nounced them all good. Remember "A
Merry Christmas" to all, and if you like to
hear from me, you shall have another letter
for the glad New Year.
	Perhaps some of my little readers who
are very fond of humorous and funny things,
will think my style too dull and tame for
such a jolly time of year, but I will tell
them that while I was hurriedly jotting
these few items, I was busy performing an-
other part in a drama, so new that even the
import of it is scarcely discernible to those
who have been chosen to take the most
prominent parts. Shall I tell my young
friends that one of the good sisters, who la-
bors for Zion, and myself were looking after
the hauling and storing away of grain, and
while we waited around the platform and
walked up and down, back and forth, to at-
tend to that business, I was writing this ar-
ticle, so you see while I was writing for you
little folks; I was laying up grain in case of
future emergency, lest any dear little ones
like you should ever suffer for bread.
	Hang up your stockings and see what
"Santa Claus" will bring this Centennial
Christmas; of course he is wild with delight
like all the great people, and he will show
it by his extravagance, else he would not
be a fair specimen of the type of his class—
the fun-loving, holiday-making, alms-giv-
ing, jolly-eating, precious good fellows, who
shower down their treasures through the
chimney, or jump in through the cracks and
crevices as soon as every-body sleeps.
							AUNT EM.
	S. L. City, Dec. 13, 1876.


[COLUMN 1]

SURPLUS WOMEN.

AND WHAT THEY ARE TO DO.

	Bessie Bramble, in the "Woman's Jour-
nal," discourses on the "Surplus Woman"
question after the following fashion, taking
for her text the announcement in that jour-
nal that there are in Great Britain 900 000
women in excess of men:
	900,000! Gracious goodness! Just im-
agine 900,000 slim, stiff, staid, starched old
maids, and no fault of theirs; 900,000 maid-
en aunts more or less "fussy;" 900,000 wo-
men, who by a mysterious dispensation of
Providence are unprovided with husbands
to "support them;" 900,000 ivies without
any oaks to twine on; 900,000 "lone roses

[COLUMN 2]

to pine on the stem"; 900,000 Juliets with
"nary" a Romeo; 900,000 "weaker vessels"
and nobody to lean on; 900,000 "social fail-
ures," as Sir Henry James calls them; 900,
000 women out of their heaven ordained
sphere; not from choice, perhaps, but be-
cause they cannot get in; 900,000 women
who cannot ask their husbands at home,
when they want to know anything, as St.
Paul teaches; 900,000 women who must
"hoe their own row;" 900,000 women who
stand no chance of being harassed and wor-
ried out of their lives by good-for-nothing,
mean men, and finally, to be brief, 900,000
women in Great Britain, alone, left "out in
the cold," although the wise men tell us
that God and Nature have ordained that
woman's "true sphere" is home and house-
work with a husband to "support her."
With this the most of women are, doubt-
less, disposed to agree, but what can be
done about 900,000 exceptions to the rule?
	Carl Schurz tells us that women should
be educated for wives and mothers, and all
education for girls should tend to husbands
and housekeeping. This would be all very
well, perhaps, if the supply of husbands
would "hold out," but statistics tell us there
are over 900,000 surplus women in Great
Britain, and over 30,000 in the State of
Massachusetts alone, and heaven only knows
how many elsewhere.
	Now in view of this tremendous fact, we
call upon the philosophers and learned men,
who profess to know all about the chief
end of women, to explain what is to be done
with these surplus women who are fixed, by
fate, out of their true sphere?	Oh, but the education of women should
tend to "husbands and housekeeping," says
Mr. Schurz; but he doesn't tell where the
husbands are to come from, or where wo-
men are to get the houses to keep or the
money to keep them, and housekeeping is
not a business that pays, as every woman
knows. Now if Parliament, in lieu of hus-
bands, would furnish each of these 900,000
surplus women with a house to keep and an
adequate income, we have no doubt the
most of them would be content—even with-
out a husband—to go on keeping house to
the end of the chapter, and would show no
disposition whatever to crowd their way in-
to the businesses that pay. But as long as
these women have to make their own
money or starve, they will reach out, just as
men do for whatever will pay best.
	A woman who has discovered that she
can make three thousand a year and keep a
carriage, as a doctor, is not to be scared
back into the kitchen for board and clothes;
if she should find, by dint of pluck and per-
severance, that soft seats were not reserved
for men alone, it is not likely she will dig
and delve in the depths of drudgery, for
five dollars a week; if she should discover
that fat fees followed fine ability and full
knowledge of the law and gospel, without
regard to sex, it is hardly probable she will
sew shirts for a shilling a piece or darn
stockings for nothing. The philosophers
and wise men may talk till all is blue about
woman's true sphere, but as long as hun-
dreds of thousands of women are com-
pelled to shift for themselves, they will do
what they best can for the best pay, sphere
or no sphere. The time is gone by when
they can be hooted away from the profess-
ions or the trades, because to enter these
will, as men say, unsex them.
	Mrs. Swisshelm says, "The philosophers
seem to know that hard work never unsex-
es a woman, only wages can do that." A
woman may toil like a beast of burden, as
in Germany; she may work in the harvest
field all day and do the house work at


night, as in Pennsylvania; she may work
eighteen hours out of the twenty-four any-
where, but there is no flurry about her be-
ing unsexed until she reaches for the same
wages as man in the capacity of a doctor, a
lawyer, a preacher or a legislator. Ay,
there's the rub
	That's where the howl comes in! But
women who have to take care of them-
selves are learning to disregard howling.
Experience teaches them, as it does men,
that they must strike out for themselves or
go to the bottom. They soon find that
while it is very pretty and poetical to con-
sider woman an angel and man as her pro-
tector, yet when the question comes down
in plain prose, to the "bottom facts," of
bread and butter, they must look out for
"number one." And when a brave, true-
hearted, high-spirited woman makes up her
mind to take the bull of public opinion by
the horns, ten chances to one she wins.—
The Golden Dawn.
	We do not believe there are any surplus
women; there is not one too many in all
the world. It is only the consequence of
man-made laws and misconstructed gov-
ernments. The writer calls upon philoso-
phers and learned men to explain what is to
be done with these surplus women, who are
fixed by fate out of their true sphere. We
will give her some ideas upon the subject in
our next issue of the EXPONENT, as it was
crowded out of this.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


Headache From Eye strain.—Dr. Weir Mitch
ell, in the American Journal of Medical Sciences
observes that few but opthalmic surgeons are
aware of the need of interrogating the eye for an-
swers to some of the hard questions which are put
to us by certain head symptoms; and he wishes to
impress upon the profession: (1.) That there are
many headaches which are due indirectly to disor-
ders of the refractive or accommodative apparatus-
of the eyes. (2.) That in these instances the
brain symptom is often the most, and sometimes
the sole, prominent symptom of the eye trouble,
so while there may be no pain or sense of fatigue
in the eye, the strain with which it is used may
be interpreted solely by occipital or frontal head-
ache. (3.) That the long continuance of eye trouble
may be the unsuspected source of insomnia, verti-
go, nausea, and general failure of health. (4.)
That in many cases the eye trouble becomes sud-
denly mischievous, owing to some failure of the
general health, or to increased sensitiveness of the
brain from moral or mental causes. Several illus-
trative cases are given, and the moral is: Be
careful not to strain your eyes.


On the evening of Dec. 5, the most tragi-
cal event ever known to transpire in any
theatre took place in Brooklyn, at the Brook-
lyn Theatre. The last act in the play of
"The Two Orphans" was being enacted,
when an alarm of fire was given from be-
hind the stage. The wildest confusion pre-
vailed immediately, and nearly three hun-
dred persons were burned and crushed to
death.


Fannie—"Why, Susie, what a queer
way of doing your hair—just like a snail."
"Well, my dear, I don't think any one can
call that a 'fast' headdress."

WOMAN'S EXPONENT.
VOL. 5	SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, JANUARY 1, 1877	No. 15


For the EXPONENT.
THE NEW ERA.

	Now, with the dawning of the glad new year,
Comes thoughts of other days and mem'ries dear
How thrillingly the finer senses glow
As retrospective visions come and go:
And lo! we grasp the shadow's quiv'ring ray,
And blend it with the REAL of to-day.

And once again, we wander as of old
'Mid scenes which radiant fancies now unfold,
Where loving friends around the cottage hearth
With song and jest pour forth their joy and mirth:
We summon to our thoughts the friends we knew
And linger fondly o'er the good and true.

Oh! hush the throbbings of the pent up heart!
Why will the tears of long-gone sorrows start?
Banish the griefs—put miseries aside—
Let joy replete in merry measure glide—
Yet, pause! methinks I hear the people say,
Another era has begun to-day,

And those who see a few more years go by
Will know Messiah's coming draweth nigh.
E'en now the signs of judgments in the land
Should cause the wise to wake and understand;
That this proud nation's cup is full and more,
With rank corruption, which is running o'er.

Ye great and high ones! for the nation's sake,
Repent, reform, and restitution make,
Lest those whose happiness should be your care,
Are driven to the depths of dark despair
By war and famine's desolating hand,
Marring the beauty of this goodly land.

Woman, awake! as mother, daughter, friend,
Thine energies and earnest efforts lend,
To help thy country in her hour of need—
Prove thy integrity by word and deed.
For woman's star is lighting up the dawn,
And rosy gleams presage the coming morn.

Yes, woman hath a mission to perform,
Embodying the germs of true reform.
For her a nobler era, broader sphere;
Then banish shadows, doubts, and ev'ry fear;
The inspiration of a clearer light,
Will strengthen thee, and nerve thee for the right.

S. L. City, Jan. 1, 1877.	E. B. WELLS


CLOSE OF THE CENTENNIAL
YEAR.

	It came and it has gone! The year so
lately new, so long looked forward to and
labored for, anticipated, dreamed and prated
of, is now but simply like its fellow
years, a monument of history, of memory,
of the past. We look about us, and with
startled breath, pronounce the old year
dead! and wonder that its days have passed
so soon. But wherefore marvel? Days and
years, like men are born to die. And men
must stamp alike their times and names,
by works of good or ill, with light or
shade.
	Now comes the query, what events will
mark the period just closed? Have indus-
try and true ambition had free exercise, for
good and noble deeds? Have men in high
positions in this land made manifest an in-
nate sense of honor? Has virtue's progress
been their generous aim? Has God been
in their councils? Has his voice approved
their course? Has His justice character-
ized their movements? The present aspect
of our national affairs, answers too plainly,
too profoundly NO! The hundredth birth-
day of American Independence is shame-
fully disgraced by shameless men. The
grand Centennial year is darkly shaded; no
wonder 'twas in haste to fill its time.


The glory of America is surely gone.
Political intrigues, official cheats, bold, law-
less demagogues with vile intent, hypocri-
sy and tyranny and crime, have rendered
her high courts corrupt as Hades and as in-
famous! Where justice once with Godlike
wisdom reigned, now dire confusion rages
frantically. There is no purity, no reason
left.
	The fierce and heartless cry of "Crucify!"
again seems rending the perverted air.
"Polygamy!" "Polygamy!" is the note
that's sounded from the very head of gov-
ernment. "The Mormons must be punish-
ed for their sins." But mark! These men
so zealous in the cause of forcing others to
their form of right, will yet concede they
have enough to do among themselves, to
quarrel with and tantalize each other.
Their dreadful work of preparation for the
land to be laid waste and desolate, moves
rapidly along. And soon will sound abroad
the mournful wail, the sad and terrible and
wild lament—America, the great, the proud,
the FREE, is fallen! fallen! fallen!
	But will this seal the closing scene of her
great destiny? No! when iniquity is swept
away, then will commence the glorious
reign of peace. The good and true from
every nation will continue to be gathered,
to build, to people and inhabit here. This
choice, promised land will be o'erspread
with cities of the Saints, and dotted with
superb and lofty Temples, built to the name
of Israel's Most High God. Another hun-
dred years will, doubtless, bring a brighter
day for fair beloved America, than ever
yet has dawned.				INEZ.
	S. L. City, Dec. 31, 1876.


NOTES AND NEWS.

	AN EXCHANGE says that "Dr. Forbes
Winslow records the startling fact that over
10,000 persons of unsound mind are confin-
ed in the lunatic asylums of the United
States, driven mad from over excitement
on the subject of spiritualism, and that in-
sanity from the same cause is prevalent in
England and increasing every day."
	AN INTERNATIONAL Retrospective Ex-
hibition of Curiosities of all kinds, from in-
struments of prehistoric times to objects of
art up to to the year 1800, will be specially
held at the Paris Exposition of 1878. An
Archaeological Committee has been institut-
ed for that purpose, under the direction of
M. de Longperier, of the Institute. The
collectors of the world are invited to parti-
cipate in the interesting enterprise.
	THERE is in London a "Women's Print-
ing Society," which, it is said, affords more
facilities for girls to become proficient in
typesetting and other branches of printing
which are practicable for them than ordina-
ry workshops of this sort. They go on
trial for one month, after which they enter
upon a three year's apprenticeship, receiv-
ing small weekly wages which are increased
in equitable proportion. It has been pro-
posed that when the women employed by
the Society shall be competent workers
they receive the ordinary trade rate of re-
muneration, so as no unfair competition
with men's work can result from it.


PETITION OF SUNDAY SCHOOL
CHILDREN,

	We publish the following touching peti-
tion in full, which we consider indicative of
the sentiments and expressions of the
children of Zion, "the hope of Israel."

Salt Lake City, Dec. 1, 1876.
	To the Honorable the Mayor and City
Council:
	Gentlemen:—We, the undersigned, Teach-
and Scholars of Sunday Schools of Salt
Lake City, respectfully, yet earnestly, peti-
tion—That your honorable body will use to
the fullest extent the powers with which
the law invests you, to control, restrain and
reduce the traffic in intoxicating liquors,
which at the present time is growing to
such alarming proportions in the midst of
our highly favored city.
	We do this, because we know that drain-
shops, with their kindred resorts, where
liquor is sold, are too often the cause of our
youth taking the first step to moral degra-
dation, infamy and destruction; because we
know their open doors are an ever present
invitation to the unguarded to sin, and a
detriment to those who are seeking to re-
form; because we realize that in these sink-
holes of iniquity not only are habits of in-
temperance formed, but also others equally
degrading and repulsive; that not only are
they the schools of the drunkard, but also
of the gambler, the thief, and often the
murderer; that they are the pest houses
that corrupt society; that they, more than
any other cause of evil, fill the prisons of
our country with felons and its almshouses
with paupers, and they even rob the tem-
perate and sober of their earnings by in-
creasing the taxes of every citizen.
	We most heartily desire to see these evils
averted from ourselves: Therefore, we, as
the youth of Salt Lake City, with those so
much interested in our temporal and eter-
nal welfare, sincerely trust that you will aid
us all in your power and, as we have been
ever taught to pray to our Heavenly Father
to "leave us not in temptation," so do we
now ask of you, the Fathers of this City, to
remove, so far as your power and authority
extend, these causes of temptation and
thus aid us in our efforts to live lives of
temperance, virtue and sobriety and to be-
come law abiding and worthy citizens of
our much loved Utah.


PEN AND SCISSORS.

	WHAT goes first in at the church door?
The key.
	WHAT is that which is often found where
it is not? Fault.
	THE longest periods in a boy's life.—
Those between meals.
	"Borrow as you go" is popular, but "bor-
row all you can and then go" is more so.
	AN old author quaintly remarks, "Avoid
argument with the ladies. In spinning
yarns among silks and satins a man is sure
to be worsted and twisted, and when worst-
ed and twisted he may consider himself
wound up."


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
			R. S. REPORTS.

			ALPINE CITY, Dec. 19th, 1876.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	Desiring to be one with our sisters, I
write a few lines to the EXPONENT, that
you may know the intentions of our Relief
Society in regard to the saving and storing
of grain. The sisters are responding to the
call by donating liberally of the small
amount of means at their command. It is
the intention of the sisters, as fast as means
can be obtained for this purpose in the So-
ciety, to use the same for buying grain.
The sisters are interested, and desire to
make the business a successful one.
	We remain your sisters in the covenant,
	Margaret McCullough, Prest., Ann Wal-
ton, Angela Vance, Counselors.

			LAKEVIEW, Dec. 12, 1870.
EDITORS EXPONENT:
	The members of the Relief and Retrench-
ment Societies are generally all alive to
their duties, we each have our meetings
every two weeks; we have made a com-
mencement towards the laying up of grain.
The sisters of our ward held a social party
last Friday evening, nearly all the members
of our ward attended. We all partook of
supper at four o'clock which had been pre-
pared for the occasion; after the tables were
cleared away, we enjoyed ourselves in danc-
ing, singing and also had some very ap-
propriate speeches from our invited guests;
among whom were Sister Mary Micklejohn,
President of the Relief Society of Tooele,
Bros. Robert Micklejohn and Abel Parker,
who are acting presidents of Tooele in place
of our much respected Bishop who has gone
on a mission; also Bro. Hugh Gowans who
is acting as Bishop, and Bro. Delamare.
We all enjoyed ourselves very much, and
the party closed at 11 o'clock. We had no
round dances, never have had any since our
Retrenchment Association was organized
by Sisters E. R. Snow, M. I. Horne and
Elizabeth Howard, nearly three years ago.
						I. G. M. 

		BEAVER CITY, UTAH, Dec. 12, 1876.
EDITORS EXPONENT:
	We enjoyed a rare treat during the recent
visit of Mrs. M. I. Horne. She held three
meetings in this place which were well at-
tended by both young and old of both sex-
es. Her instructions, principally, were to
the ladies of the Relief Societies and Re-
trenchment Associations, but were of a
character to benefit all who heard them.
	The subjects most spoken upon were the
necessity of laying up grain, sustaining the
EXPONENT, assisting in the publication of
the Woman's Book, and patronizing home-
manufacture. She advised the sisters to in-
terest themselves in silk-culture, and gave
good counsel on many subjects pertaining
to our every-day duties.
	Mrs. Caroline Shipp, also from Salt Lake
City, was present and made a few excellent
remarks.
	There was a change in the organization
of our Relief Society last June, occasioned
by the resignation of the President, Mrs.
Lucinda Houd. The organization now
stands as follows:
	Mrs. R. W. Tyler, President.
	"	Eliza W. Hawkins,	} Counselors.

	Miss Harriet S. Shepherd Ass't Sec'y.
	Mrs. Almira H. Murdock, Treasurer.

			14 WARD, S. L. CITY,
					Dec. 22, 1876.
EDITORS' EXPONENT:
	Our esteemed Pres. Mrs. M. I. Horne has
requested that I send you a brief report of
our ninth annual meeting which was held
at the Society Hall, Dec. 13, 1876.
	More than the usual number of sisters
were in attendance, and we were also hon-
ored with the presence of Bishop Thomas
Taylor, and Counselor G. H. Taylor; after
singing prayer, and roll call, the Secretary
read the Annual Report as follows:

									———
	Total.......................................... 233 78 1/2
	Balance on hand.......................	36 08 1/2
	We have donated to the Temple in quilts,
	stockings, shirts, clothing etc...... $	77 25
	To Woman's Commission Store..	 7 65
	We are also making preparations to in-
vest seventy-five dollars in the purchase of
wheat.
	After the Report had been unanimously
accepted, Prest. Mrs. Horne addressed the
meeting in her usual earnest and energetic
manner, upon storing our grain, the Wo-
man's Book, and other subjects of business.
	Bishop Taylor being called upon, ex-
pressed himself pleased to meet with us, al-
so pleased with our reports and method of
doing business. Thought the storing of
grain a very wise movement, and proffered
room in the basement of the Ward Hall for
our bins. Said we had his best feelings and
confidence and that he would assist us all in
his power. Counseled us to be temperate
in all our movements, even in our sympathy
for the poor, but to help and bless all who
are really needy.
	Elder G. H. Taylor and Sister W. East
also made some timely and interesting re-
marks.
	The same spirit of unity and good feel-
ing prevailed which has always character-
ized the meetings of our society.
	That the blessing of God our Heavenly
Father may be upon you to assist you in
your high and holy calling, is the prayer of
your sister &c.
				ELMINA S. TAYLOR,
								Sec'y.


"A MAN should always live within his in-
come," says an American, "even if he has
to borrow money to do it."


NOTES FROM MARY CLEMMER'S 
LETTER.

	Some time when I am old and the glory
of the past shall come back to me, to light
up the gray shadows of many years, I shall
see this picture of the Centennial. Not its
booths and bazars, its noisy Turks, its peace-
ful Chinese, its endless throngs, and illimit-
able displays; but this: the sun sending his
lengthened rays across a wilderness of
bloom and shooting myriad arrows of fire
through the lofty oaks, elms and chestnuts
clustering above the Gothic roof of Horti-
cultural Hall. I shall recall the glory of the
grounds when the marvels of the miles of
show-cases have faded out of sight. Lands-
downe Valley, with its glancing rivnlets,
its profuse vines, touched with the first vi-
vid scarlets of autumn, its native giants of
oak and elm, maple and sycamore, is a mar-
vel of beauty that seems cut straight out of
Naure's heart, while a whole letter could
not paint the glory of the gardens. When
I came here the scarlet and rose geraniums,
verbenas, hydrangeas were at their bright-
est. Wandering through the broad inter-
esting avenues, far as the eye could reach,
on and on, the emerald sod was shot with
these broad bands of flaming color; while
beside them—"like moonlight unto sun-
light"—were the softly-shaded beds of
pale yellow, green and white, the pampas
grasses nodding their silvery plumes, the
pale English ivies dappled and white-edged.
Then the tamarisk stretched forth its bright
green wands, and the weigelia waved its
white and scarlet blossoms, and the homely
flowers of home were everywhere beside
our feet. The ragged robin, marigold,
Joseph's coat, larkspur, fox glove, spider
plant, snapdragon, the morning glory, the
clematis, old-man, love-lies-bleeding, the
mourning bride, bergamot, petunias, and
dahlias—all were here, with their memor-
ies of home, childhood, and youth. How
gay they looked one evening, how glad, as
I sat down on a seat by the way, just before
the gates closed. It was then the sun
spread out his rays gathering all this beauty
into his splendor and made my picture for
all time. I came back in the morning, and
"the brightness of their smile was gone
from upland, glade, and glen." In the night
"the frost fell from the clear cold heavens,"
and suddenly the fervor of color was
quenched. The flowers were dead. The
dahlias hung to their blackened stalks. The
long ramparts of bloom that stood so brave
and glowing yesterday, were shriveled and
fallen now. The meek sunlight of these
Indian Summer days draws the subtle
sweetness of the waning year out of these
sepulchers of the flowers. The air is odor-
ous with their dying fragrance. Here and
there a late, late rose still dares to bloom in
the face of the menacing frost. The wax
balls hang amid their crisp leaves. Here
and there a cluster of scarlet berries show
ruddy against a gray back-ground; but the
amber skies are crossed by leafless boughs.
The gay umbrage of the park is sodden un-
der our feet. "The year smiles as it draws
near its death" and it has almost "had its
day"—the great Exhibition.

and give Aunt Julia a kiss, and say good-
night." Gerty: "No, no! If I kiss her,
she'll box my ears like she did papa's last

WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


THE GOD WE WORSHIP.

	[From the San Francisco Chronicle]

We choose all substance: what remains
The mystic, blind sectarian gains:
All that each claims may each possess,
Nor grudge each other's happiness.

An immaterial God they choose,
An immaterial Heaven and Hell;
For such a God we have no use;
In such a Heaven we cannot dwell.

We claim the earth, the air and sky,
And all the starry worlds on high;
Gold, silver, pearls and precious stones,
And bodies made of flesh and bones.

Our God like us can hear and see,
Taste, smell and feel eternally:
Immortal brain He has to think,
Organs to speak, and eat, and drink.

With man on earth or heaven He dines;
His heart is cheered with fruits of vines;
Of Sara's bread and Abram's calf
He ate and drank, 'till Sara laughed.

The foremost in all branches He
Of useful art or industry;
To plant, to build, or dress the field,
Or make old Eden's garden yield.

A farmer, architect and scribe,
A tailer and all else beside;
He taught old Abram how to farm,
And make him coats to keep him warm.

Noah He taught to build a ship,
And Moses how man's record keep;
He introduced the heavenly plan,
Of architecture unto man;

By which stupendous worlds were reared,
And courts on earth for Him prepared,
That when from heaven He chose to come,
He found on earth a heavenly home.

Such is our God, our Heaven, our all.
When once redeemed from Adam's fall
All things are ours, and we shall be
The Lord's to all eternity!


[COLUMN 1]

CORRESPONDENCE.

LANCASTER CITY AND CO., PA.,
	Dec. 16, 1876.
EDITOR EXPONENT:

	This mission has fixed in my mind a con-
clusion which to your readers may be both
new and novel. I allude directly to the
immense amount of good the Orthodox
ladies of Utah could do abroad with their
husbands or father's, in allaying the deep-
seated prejudice there is against our—WILL
BE—most popular institution, viz., "Celes-
tial Marriage." Throughout this country
the people have been treated with periodi-
cal doses of "pen dente lite," "Tell it all,"
"Fifteen years among the Mormons," and
numerous other trashy, lying, sensational
effusions, gotten up by designing men for
money-making purposes. They have not
seen or heard a "Mormon" Elder for a great
many years in this, and no doubt most other
counties of the State, and I am sure that a
hopeful, thorough Elder, with the presence
of a faithful, devoted wife, or daughter,
would accomplish as much or more good
than two Elders traveling together. I feel
pretty certain that the feeling of confidence
in the ability and integrity of the ladies of
Utah is rapidly obtaining in Zion, hence the
confident appeals to them to treasure up
wheat, nurse home industries, such as the
manufacture and sale of home products, and
the vast amount of good they are doing
through the Relief Societies. That God
may speed them success is my humble
prayer. I would suggest in this connection
that the Elders had better look to their
"laurels," or they may lose the credit of
being Saviors on Mount Zion. A popular
writer referring to a universal prejudice

[COLUMN 2]

says: "Such has been the crushing effect of
public sentiment that women almost feel
like apologizing for being on the earth at
all." I witnessed a striking illustration of
the greater susceptibility of women over
men in religious matters a few evenings
ago, in Strasburg, in this County, while at-
tending a distracted Methodist meeting.
Out of the goodly number that knelt at
the "anxious seat" not a single man was
present, and yet there were numbers in theroom that might have gone up to that man-
ner of chaff and husks with profit to them-
selves and families.
	I need not inform you that everybody
and his neighbor is anxious to learn all
about the "peculiar institution," and that
questions on that subject are always first in
order. But I will tell you that I have had
but little trouble in removing much of that
prejudice at all times when a fair opportu-
nity is given.

	I expect Bro. O. F. Whitney to join me
here in a day or two; will try and retain
him with me, as I much need his help.
	That the Lord will always bless your ef-
forts in disseminating the truth is the
prayer of your
	Brother in the Gospel,
		A. MILTON MUSSER.

SALT LAKE CITY, Dec. 18, 1876.
EDITOR EXPONENT.
	In company with my husband and daugh-
ter, I left home on the 11th of Nov. last to
visit my children who reside in the coun-
try, and also to meet with the Relief Socie-
ties in different settlements through which
I might pass, and give them such instruc-
tion and encouragement on the various sub-
jects that are now being brought before the
sisters, as I was capable of doing with the
aid and assistance of my heavenly Father
upon whom I humbly and implicitly rely
for strength. The subjects most dwelt upon
by me in talking to the sisters, were, the
laying up of wheat, the raising and manu-
facturing of silk, sustaining the Woman's
Store and all other home-industries, also to
subscribe more liberally for the Woman's
Exponent, and invited the sisters to take
shares in the publishing of the Woman's
Book. During my journey I met with the
Relief Societies of Beaver, Fillmore, Scipio,
Gunnison, Nephi and Santaquin. In Beaver
the Relief Society has a full lot of land and
a good substantial brick building on it. I
advised them to set out mulberry trees over
the ground, and thus commence the silk-
culture. In all our meetings a good spirit
prevailed, and the people received us every-
where with marked cordiality. The Bish-
ops of the several settlements met with the
Relief Societies in the meetings, imparting
good counsel and encouragment and wish-
ing the sisters success in their united ef-
forts. The sisters everywhere were very
earnest and active in the storing up of
wheat. It is a subject which seems to have
taken hold of the hearts of the women and
they are determined to carry out this coun-
sel of the servant of God. I sincerely hope
that some good may result from my visit to
these settlements; they have not the same
advantages and opportunities that some of
us have, and are anxious to be co-workers
with those who labor for Zion.
	Trusting the seed sown may produce good
fruit in the hearts of those who desire to
keep the commandments of God, and pray-
ing always for the welfare of the Saints at
home and abroad, desiring to live to pro-
mote knowledge among the daughters of
Zion, and to be useful in the work of the

[COLUMN 3]

last dispensation, I will now close this let-
ter to my sisters.
	Truly Yours,
		M. I. HORNE.


ATTEND OUR MEETINGS.

	To all the Saints I say, let us attend our
meetings. There is no privilege or higher
duty resting upon us than this; we call it a
privilege because it is productive of great
benefit, and again it is a duty the neglect of
which will involve as much loss, as the
observance of it will gain. Meetings are
fraught with great blessings and tend to
build up the faithful Saints, or strengthen
their desire to serve the Lord and to en-
large their understanding of the doctrines
of our most holy faith; it is simply absurd
for persons who have embraced the truth, and
having begun to taste the fruits thereof, to
vainly imagine that after having conformed
with the first principles of the Gospel, they
are sure of the possession of all those
heavenly enjoyments which are due to the
faithful and diligent alone; for the posses-
sion of those promised gifts, men and wo-
men, after having found the way of salva-
tion, and commmenced to walk therein,
should continue in the line of duty and
seek to acquire sufficient knowledge of the
grand principles of the gospel which must
necessarily be understood and carried out
in our daily lives. To neglect our meetings
is ignoring our lessons in the various
branches of the gospel; it is there we are
taught not only from the lips of God's ser-
vants, but by the Holy spirit which influen-
ces and dictates the minds of those who
meet together in His name; the Savior has
said, "where two or three are gathered to-
gether in my name, there am I in the midst
of them." No matter how few the number
may be of those who meet together, the
Lord has promised to be there, and as we
are commanded to cleave unto the Lord,
and as Paul says, that we might walk wor-
thy of Him, being faithful in every good
work. Thus increasing in the knowledge
of God, our attendance to worship and per-
formance of all duties pertaining to the
"new and everlasting covenant," should be
constant and faithful, so far as possible; the
individuals who do not attend their meet-
ings will fall asleep spiritually; neglecting
this one duty leads to further and greater
negligence.
	Step by step, men and women have
fallen into darkness, and it has often been
preceded by the neglect of their meetings;
the man or woman who walks up to every
obligation resting upon him or her can nev-
er retrograde in the knowledge of the gos-
pel; such persons are all the time on the
march of improvement, and are filled with
joy and satisfaction and hope of eternal life;
they are learning, while those who neglect
their meetings are losing ground and are
looked upon as dull and idle scholars or la-
borers in the Lord's vineyard.
	I therefore say again to all the Saints, at-
tend your meetings and be humble and the
Holy spirit will be with you and manifest to
you the gifts of the gospel; you will pro-
gress with the truth and be prepared for
greater responsibilities, consequently a
greater reward will be yours and happiness
in this life and the life hereafter.
					FRANCES B. HART.
	Alma, Weber Co.
