COMPLIMENTS.

The following came by Deseret Telegraph:
	"ST. GEORGE, Dec. 24, 1876.
"MY FAMILY AND FRIENDS AT SALT LAKE
AND ELSEWHERE:
	"The compliments of the season; wishing
you all a 'Merry Christmas' and 'Happy New
Year.' May you live to accomplish the work
given you to do of our Heavenly Father, and I
pray you may do it faithfully, that you may be
prepared to dwell eternally in the heavens.
May God grant it. Amen.
	"BRIGHAM YOUNG."


[Column 1]
		THE PAST AND PRESENT.

	Some wiseacres say "live in the present,"
yet it is natural to look backward at certain
times, and wonder too, what the future will
be, and draw comparisons between that which
was, and that which is, and form from these
certain significant, though perhaps unreliable
conclusions.
	Another year has told its tale, delivered up
its record and sealed it in the archives of the
past until the great day of reckoning: and be
the record good or evil in the balance, it must
stand—it is gone beyond recall. Every thought-
ful person must have serious reflections, as he
or she enters upon each successive year, per-
chance like this, have the deeds of the past
been such as may be disclosed without the
blush of shame? How is it with this nation,
the glorious land of liberty and freedom?
	What has brought disunion and discord into
the halls of the Capitol? Have the sins of the
people gone before them to judgment. and is
the Lord pouring out upon the nation the vi-
als of His wrath? Verily it seemeth so.
	From the east and west, north and south,
the cry of dishonesty, injustice, oppression and
all the evil results of wrong-doing are echoed
forth until "the ear is pained" and the soul
sickened with the recital of wrongs. Inevita-
ble are the consequences which result from the
breaking of God's commandments and reject-
ing the Gospel of His Son. In the unchange-
able and immutable laws of nature, causes
produce effects, and this nation have sewed
the wind and must reap the whirlwind.
	There are times when national matters, as
well as many others of a less grave nature, seem
to have reached a climax, where they require
some direct and powerful force or agency to act
upon them, to bring them into subjection or
even to rend them asunder. Wrongs and mis-
takes seem to have been accumulating in the
affairs of this nation until the wit and wis-
dom of the most profound statesman and di-
plomatist seems incompatible with the resist-

[Column 2]
ance brought to bear against right. What pow-
er is there left that can be made to react and
avert the terrible calamities that seem about to
overtake the nation. The past year has been
one of unusual note in many respects. The
Centennial year of American Independence
has been marked by many strange incidents
and fearful dilemmas in different parts of the
earth; therefore the contemplation of it as it
passes away and takes its rank among its pre-
decessors, gives thinking people questions to
solve, that puzzle the stoutest brain and the
quickest intellect. They are amazed at the
events which are transpiring and wonder what
the future will bring about. Naturally enough
some try to believe that a change of politics
might quell many disturbances and allay some
of the grievances here in the United States;
others that an empire or kingdom would be
more suited to the condition of the various peo-
ples who have gathered here under the protec-
tion of a government that seemed to possess
all the elements and strength necessary to
maintain justice and equal rights for all, Jew
or Jentile, black or white.
	The words of the Savior in the New Testa-
ment show plainly that in the last days men
should hasten to destruction, that the time
would be so short that great events would fol-
low each other quickly; this seems to have
been particularly the case during the year now
past. "Men hurrying to and fro," is not this
scripture literally fulfilled? To what the great
and universal excitement is tending the world
do not discern, but the times are ominous, if
not prophetic, even to them. Many who do
not comprehend clearly the "signs of the
times" are looking to this year and the new
administration as an augury of asupicious ris-
ing, but "Alas for my poor country," as has
been sung in other lands, unless some all pow-
erful hand is reached forth in mercy to save,
soon will this sentiment so pathetically signifi-
cant re-echo through America.


A SINGULAR DREAM

	We have been requested by sister Diantha
Billings, of Provo, an aged and venerable lady
to publish the following dream; the lady who
had this singular dream is the daughter of the
late Elder Isaac Morley.
	MANTI, SANPETE CO.
DEAR AUNT DIANTHA:

	I have had quite a curious dream and I
should like to tell it you in person, but as I
cannot do that I will write it; I have had a
strange feeling ever since, I do not know what
it can mean. I will try and describe to you
what I saw in the heavens. I thought it was
evening and I was out looking at the stars, all
at once I saw something moving in the sky,
I watched it until it formed a circle, and in
this circle was inscribed, "God and 1888, or 7 or
5," I cannot tell which, but it was one of these
three. I called my mother, she came and we
watched together, and outside this circle other
circles formed until there was a large complete
diagram as it were, interlined regularly at in-
tervals. It seemed to cover a place larger than
our house. I started to call my husband and
others to look, when my mother said hush!
the Lord is coming and we have no oil in the
lamp, and I said, oh mother what have I done
that I feel so bad, and my mother said again
hush! the Lord is coming, and this means
worlds without end; and as we stood there,
reading seemed to form all over the circles
formed in this diagram, in red and brown col-
ors. And then I dreamed I went into the
house and woke the folks up, and felt so bad
because there was no oil, and the sky all seem-
ed to become a dark brown with a border of
red, and to come closer and closer to the earth
or to us, and it covered the whole wide canopy
of the skies with reading all over it. Then I
heard the sweetest singing I ever heard in all
my life, and mother saying "The end is near,
the Lord cometh worlds without end;" and I
awoke. At first I could have told what all the
figures were, but I cannot remember them
now. I would like to know if it is a warning,
or what it means.

	MARY L. M. HANSEN


HOME AFFAIRS.

	CORRECTION.—In a former issue the report
of the Manti Relief Society investing two
hundred thousand dollars in starting a small
store, should have read two hundred dollars.
	WE HAVE many articles contributed to our
columns that we had not room to publish in
the present number. We cordially thank our
contributors for their favors and hope to merit
their future efforts.
	WE ANTICIPATED furnishing an article for
this issue giving our ideas on Surplus Women,
but had not space, so must defer it until Jan 15.
It is a subject which has many bearings and
ought to be fully descanted upon: We would
be glad to hear from some of our correspond-
ents on this subject.
	A HAPPY NEW YEAR to our patrons and
friends and especially the little folks. The
Centennial Christmas has come and gone, and
the people have feasted, and rejoiced in the
song and dance, and in the various amuse-
ments which characterize the "Winter Holi-
days." For this people there seems to have
been an abundance of good things and to spare.
Surely those upon whom prosperity has lav-
ished her bounties have bestowed liberally and
made glad the hearts of the poor. A univer-
sal song and prayer of gratitude should arise
from all hearts to the Most High, who has
caused the earth to yield rich harvests, yea,
"the desert to blossom as the rose." If the
saints forget the Giver in the day of prosperity,
He will not lend a listening ear in the day of
adversity.


DESTITUTION IN SCRANTON, PA.

 	        HUNGER AND DISTRESS AMONG
			THE UNEMPLOYED. 

	     FEARS OF FAMINE ENTERTAINED. 

	The condition of affairs among the poor peo-
ple of the coal regions is assuming an alarm-
ing aspect. Thousands, who have neither work
nor bread, nor the prospect of obtaining any
for themselves and families, are on the verge
of starvation and do not know what to do or
which way to turn. The destitution is wide-
spread and distressing and is not confined to
the laboring classes alone, as there are num-
bers of skilled mechanics who have been idle
for many months and whose little store has
long since been exhausted. There are hun-
dreds of families in and about Scranton, and
not in this city alone, but in every settlement
throughout the coal regions, who do not know
where to get to-day's dinner, and who would
be shivering by cheerless firesides were it not
for the heaps of coal dust that lie scattered
along the valley and from which they draw
their supply of fuel, such as it is. So many
poor people, or such intense downright despair-
ing distress has not been seen in this section
of the country for many years. It is actually
painful to witness the want and sorrow extant.


[COLUMN 1]

Unlike the great cities, toward which the
wretched of all classes tend and in which pov-
erty is seen at all periods, the towns of the in-
terior are generally free from any considerable
share of needy people in prosperous times, but
a change has come over the land, and men who
a year ago would deem it a disgrace to ask for
assistance under any circumstances, are abso-
lutely famishing for the ordinary necessities of
life. Mechanics, too, men in receipt of wages
varying from $2.50 to $3.50 per day at the open-
ing of the Centennial year, are now reduced to
the verge of pauperism, anxious to earn fifty
cents a day if they can get anything to do.
		MEETING OF THE DISTRESSED.
	They talk easily of their distress and no
longer make any effort to hide their poverty,
and at a meeting of their number held the oth-
er night, asking the city authorities to furnish
them with work and bread, some sad stories
were related. The pitiable pictures presented
at their homes are touching, and in some in-
stances would wring tears from the most cal-
lous heart. It is a sad spectacle to see a bro-
ken spirited husband in the midst of his little,
feeble family, for which he is unable to pro-
vide bread, even though their appeals are like
so many daggers to his heart. The patient
mothers invariably seek to stifle their own
grief, and whenever I have called they have
endeavored to hide the poignant features of
their sorrows. But the wan face, and prema-
turely old and haggard looks have told a story
that the eloquence of the sweetest voice could
not gainsay. It is remarkable how patient in
most cases the women have shown themselves
wherever I have been. In the same homes
where despairing men raved incoherently and
cursed corporations and the government, as
well as their own bitter fate there was an air of
quiet resignation and meek submission on the
faces of the women, that was truly heroic. It
is the fact that women endure more in any
great ordeal and endure it better. Where a
burly man will go howling about with a big
sorrow, showing it to his neighbors, a little
woman will set her face against fate and hide
it in her breast. The sorrows that kill men
only bring out the sweetest, noblest natures of
women.
		INDUSTRIES IDLE.
	Various industries of the city are idle, and
will continue so for many months to come.
The extensive iron and steel works of the
Lackawanna Iron and Coal Company, upon
which the poor of Shanty Hill, a thickly pop-
ulated portion of the city, are dependent for
the means of support, have suspended opera-
tion until the 1st of March, and, as a conse-
quence their employees, who at the best of
times found it a difficult matter to sustain
themselves; are fairly dismayed and disheart-
ened at the gloomy prospect. Pinching, pallid
poverty sits by many a fireside to-day, and the
indications are that the coming Christmas
will be the saddest ever experienced in the an-
thracite coal regions of Pennsylvania.
		THE BITTER COLD.
	The recent heavy fall of snow and accom-
panying bitter cold have made the situation
felt more keenly, and the scantily clad and
starving people are reduced to the lowest
depths of despair with not a single ray of hope
in the immediate future to light their footsteps
along the dismal and weary way. The coal
industry, too, has recently been falling off,
and in many of our settlements mining has
been suspended altogether. Merchants and
small dealers who were wont to encourage the
miners and laborers from time to time with
credit, are compelled to refuse them now for

[COLUMN 2]

their self-protection, and this superadds to
their sorrow and intensifies the bitterness of
their cheerless and unhappy lot.—Sunday
Free Press.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.		117


[Column 1]
		A WIDOW'S PARTY.

			S.L. CITY, July, 1876.
						16th Ward.
	Opened by singing, "The Spirit of God
like a fire is burning." Prayer, by Bro.
Riser. Singing, "We thank thee, O God
for a prophet." Bishop Kesler then made
remarks, expressing his pleasure in meet-
ing with a company of this kind. All pres-
ent are widows with but one or two excep-
tions, and yet not over half of those who
reside in the ward are here. He had never
met on an occasion of this kind before and
it brought a great many reflections to his
mind. Many of the partners of these
sisters had stood by his side and labored for
the cause of Zion. The Lord has a great
work to do in this dispensation; He called
Joseph Smith to do a mighty work. These
sisters husbands, when living, spent a por-
tion of their lives in this work. God has
set up His kingdom as Daniel saw, and the
honest in heart are being gathered from all
portions of the earth, and great is the work
to be performed in connection with our
dead. The wicked will be wasted away
and cut off from the earth, and some of
them are our relatives by blood, and they
cannot be brought out of prison until they
have "paid the uttermost farthing." To
look at this in one way it seems hard, yet
if we view it in another light, it is all right.
In Noah's day the people would not accept
the gospel and were destroyed, but Jesus
went and preached to them in prison, show-
ing that there is an opportunity for pardon
after death, providing the unpardonable sin
has not been committed. We can also be
baptized for our relatives as far as we can
trace their geneaology, and there will be a
great work during the Millennium. And
my sisters your husbands have gone to per-
form a portion of this work. Bro. Brigham
has said when the Temple is built, angels
will come to it and bring the names of those
who wish us to be baptized for them; then
we shall know who are willing to obey the
gospel and we shall in reality be "Saviors
on Mount Zion. What a blessing it is that
we have the privilege to gain celestial glory.
All will not attain it, because they will not
accept the terms; but all will get a reward
according to their works.
	I rejoice that the day has come for the
Lamanites to obey the gospel; and he hoped
the day would soon come when our ene-
mies would have no power over the saints.
	We have met together to-day to have a
social party and to be instructed. We all
carry an influence with us wherever we go
either for good or evil, and we should set
an example in everything as Saints of God
and bind up the broken-hearted and com-
fort those who mourn; it is our duty to do it;
and not seek to pull each other down, and
then trample upon the weak and fallen. I
can realize our surroundings to some extent;
the evil spirits are trying to lead us from
the straight and narrow path, and decoy us
from our duties and they will continue to
try until we are tried as Abraham was; he
was tried as close as was possible for mor-
tal to endure; have any of us been tried
equal to that? The Savior was tried, He
went through the most trying ordeals He
was a wanderer on the earth, and he passed
through these trials in poverty; but as Paul
said "if in this life only we have hope, we
are of all men most miserable." We have

[Column 2]
been cast out from among the world but the
Lord has been with us, and will continue to
be if we are faithful. Many of you sisters
will soon pass through the veil and meet
your husbands, what a joyful meeting that
will be.
	The Lord is very merciful to us, and looks
over many of our weaknesses; and the best
way is for us to be prayerful and do our duty
in all things, both to the living and the
dead. The judgments of God are being
poured out upon the nations; you have only
to read the news to be aware of it; there
will be many calamities this year, and they
will still grow worse; but the Saints should
rejoice for the work of God is progressing,
and the righteous will go onward and build
a Temple unto the Lord, and His glory will
rest upon it for He has promised it.
	You widows may ask what can we do?
Why you can prepare to meet your hus-
bands behind the veil, and let your wills be
swallowed up in the will of God; we have
got to do it before we can come into His
presence. Pray as the Savior prayed, he
prayed and wept too. Call your families to-
gether to pray, and observe the Sabbath
day and not speak evil of your neighbors.
We all have failings, but there is a balm in
the gospel for every ill. Keep the Word
of Wisdom, and observe the duties of to-
day, and not let the sun go down before
we have faithfully fullfilled our daily du-
ties, thus we will be saved each day.
	I exhort you to do these things, so that
you may meet your husbands and Father in
heaven. This is my best wish for you in
the name of Jesus.
	Brother Riser said: I am pleased to meet
with you on this occasion. It is our privi-
lege to meet together often, for we need to
be reminded of things that we would for-
get otherwise; our vision will be extended
if we live in the light of the spirit of the
Gospel. There is a crisis near at hand, and
already men's hearts begin to fail them, the
world has gone to the extreme with wick-
edness, and unless we are faithful we will
not be prepared for what is coming. He re-
lated considerable of his experience both
before and after embracing the gospel, and
bore a faithful testimony to its truth, and
the healing power which he had seen mani-
fested. Said he never regretted the step
he had taken in obeying the gospel. The
prize of eternal life is before the Saints and
if we pursue the path our Bishop has
marked out, we shall be able to meet our
faithful friends who have gone before us.
	I ask God to bless you all in the name of
Jesus, amen.
	Benediction by Brother Riser.
	All present then partook of refreshments
which had been bountifully prepared for
this special occasion, and the season spent
together was one of peaceful enjoyment.
	The company then adjourned to meet 
again.		SARAH CUMBERLAND
								Sec'y.


TO THE LACE MAKERS:—Those who
wish to purchase bobbins for lace making,
can obtain them by sending orders to Mrs.
E. Davis, "Woman's Commission House,"
S. L. City. Bro. Crompton, a bobbin maker
from England, will be ready to fill all or-
ders at a low figure.


ANNA Dickenson seems to have been
warmly applauded in her recent appearance
upon the stage in St. Louis, yet she is se-
verely criticized by newspaper writers and
journalists; they have an idea that she is
too much herself, and does not give suffi-
cient dramatic effect.

[Column 1]
	MORE EXTRACTS FROM LETTERS
			FROM HAWAII.

			LAIE. Oahu, Sept. 30, 1876.
	I have been standing on the porch to-
night looking toward the loved home of my
many, many dear friends and associates and
thinking of the great water lying between
us, and the many changes which may per-
haps take place before we meet again. We
are well and feeling in excellent spirits,
with good feelings toward all mankind. I
am teaching the Laie school, have thirty
odd pupils who seem to be learning very
fast; I am also a pupil learning their lan-
guage, and I fear not a very apt one, "aka
hoas mau" (but I continue to try). Millie
talks very good for the time she has been
here, is quite contented and appears very
much like a native. I like the natives
much better than I imagined I should; they
are very kind to me; and take great pains
in teaching me their language.
	I have had quite an experience since I
come here. I was eye-witness at a death-
scene which took place shortly after our ar-
rival. I had called to take a sick woman
some rice, thinking something nourishing
like that would be relished. I saw she was
struck with death, and told her husband as
much. I had not been in the house more
than half an hour when she breathed her
last, as quietly as if going to sleep; I closed
her eyes and tied her chin, and looked to
see what her parents were doing when a na-
tive sister came up to me with a shroud
which they had been busy preparing against
her death. I assisted in putting it on, and
spread a beautiful "kapa" to lay her on (her
husband never leaving her side;) when all
was ready for her to be seen by her friends,
as many were gathered, I stood looking at
the corpse, when I was almost raised from
floor to ceiling by the most dreadful wailing
I ever heard. I gave one glance about the
room and saw how fast it was filling with
mourners, and will leave you to imagine
how long I was getting home, which is
about a quarter of a mile. They keep their
wailing and feasting up all night. I had
forgotten to tell you that they always pre-
pare a great feast in honor of the dead.
	I have also been a guest at a grand wed-
ding, had the pleasure of eating with the
bride and groom. The food consisted of
"poi" pork and beef, which all ate with a
relish, using fingers instead of knives and
forks.
	I enjoy myself in many ways here, but
nothing compares with our mountain home.
					Sunday, Oct. 1st.
	We have been on the Island four months
to-day. We arrived on the 1st of June, after
seven and a half days at sea. We enjoyed
ourselves a great deal in the same way as
many of our friends before us, by being very
liberal to the sharks. I was very sick the
first three days, in fact I was quite misera-
ble until we landed. We were well provid-
ed with everything for our comfort and one
of the best stewardesses; she was like a
mother to me. I have been to meeting the
most of the day; it is our fast day; we hold
it the first Sunday in each month.
	I want to tell you of our ride in the hills
yesterday, thinking it will show you a lit-
tle how we feel. Brothers Wooley, Molen,
and wives with children, on horse back, took
picnic and went up in the canon to get a
breath of fresh air. We rode native style,
(now don't laugh) as it was safer; riding
over rocks and brush, over hills that looked
impossible for horses to travel, but we found
a nice cool place and better than all a cool

[Column 2]
spring of water. We took with us papers
from home, and read, visited, ate, and some
of us slept, but not your correspondent.
	We are living at the Plantation house. I
am chief cook and house cleaner; I am kept
very busy teaching and keeping house, and
find very few idle moments. My health is
much better here than at home. I believe
I could eat my rations of Irish potatoes, and
pork, beans and cabbage, had I the chance.
Not wishing you to think I suffer at all, for
we live good enough, and what we have we
relish, I can eat quite a meal of meat and
"poi"; we try to have flour on hand all the
time. I will have quite a yarn to spin when
I get home.
	I begin to think we as a people do not
know how to appreciate comforts when we
have them.
						Oct. 7th.
	This is the second day of Conference. I
thought as the mail would leave in the
morning I would finish, I will not be able
to give you the news of to-morrow. I do
not know where Simpson's lot will be cast
in this missionary labor. Bros. Lambert and
Stringam have not been able to get here
for Conference on account of high winds,
which has caused quite a disappointment.
The natives are turning out well and seem
to enjoy the spirit of the Lord. Their fa-
ces glow when they sit and hear our breth-
ren talk. I think this mission has a com-
pany of as good men as you generally come
across. I do think the young men here
have been faithful and performed a good
long mission, and when they get their re-
lease they will not feel to complain.
	Please remember me with much love to
all my friends and acquaintances, not for-
getting Sister Wells and family. Tell Un-
cle Charles I want his prayers now, more
than ever. I am very tired now; there are
eight of our good folks stopping with us,
beside our own family. Bro. Richards
boards with us when at Laie.
	Remember me to all. I will re[---]e as it is
after ten o'clock. Good night, a [-]pleasant
dreams.				JANE E. MOLEN.


HOUSEHOLD HINTS.

	Baked Omelet.—Boil half a pint of cream, or
rich milk; beat six eggs thoroughly—they will be
nicer if the whites and yelks are beaten separate-
ly; have a deep dish hot and buttered; stir the
beaten eggs, with a little salt, into the cream; put
all quickly into the dish, and bake from five to ten
minutes, depending upon the condition of the oven.
It should be lightly browned, and taken directly
to the table in the dish.
	Scalloped Oysters.—Have ready an earthen
baking-dish well buttered, sprinkle in a layer of
rolled crackers, then a layer of good fat oysters, a
little pepper, salt, and bits of butter, another
layer of crackers, another of oysters, then sea-
soning as before, and so on until the dish is filled;
have the top covered with crackers; turn over it a
cup of the oyster liquor. Set it in the oven for
thirty or forty minutes.
	Suet Pudding.—To two coffee cups of flour with
a heaping teaspoonful of baking powder stirred
thoroughly through it, add one coffee cup of finely
chopped suet, one teaspoonful of salt; one well-
beaten egg; one pint of sweet milk. Grease a
tin pail, pour the mixture into it, cover, set it in a
kettle of boiling water, and let it boil constantly
two hours. Renew the water as it evaporates with
boiling water from the teakettle. Send to the ta-
ble hot. Serve with wine sauce, or lemon sauce.
	Children's Pudding.—Fill and earthen baking-
dish with finely chopped apples. Season with
sugar and nutmeg; add a little water. Set it on
the back of the range until the apples are tender.
Then make a crust of one teacup of sweet milk,
one tablespoonful of butter, a little salt, one tea-
spoonful of baking powder, flour enough to roll
out. Lay the crust on top of the apples and
bake. To be eaten hot with sweet sauce, flavored
with lemon or vanilla. Other kinds of fruit may
be used in the same manner.


HYGIENIC NOTES.

	For Wakefulness at Night.—Take about 20
grains of carbonate of soda in half a glass of wa-
ter before going to bed. The best remedies are
hygiene diet, cold water, exercise, brisk walking
and gymnastic exercise for half an hour before go-
ing to bed.
	To Cure a Felon.—As soon as the part begins
to swell, wrap the part with a cloth thoroughly
saturated with tincture of lobelia, and the felon is
dead. It is said this never fails to cure if applied
in season.
	Another: The following simple remedy is said
by an exchange to be a sure cure. Take common
rock salt, dry it in an oven, then pound it fine and
mix it with spirits of turpentine in equal parts.
Put it on a rag and wrap it round the finger, and
as it gets dry put on some more, and in twenty-
four hours we are assured the felon will be dead.
	And Another: Take half a gill of strong vine-
gar, dissolve in it a teaspoonful or more of salera-
tus; heat it as hot as the flesh can bear; soak the
felon in it as long as desirable. Repeat the ap-
plication as often as the pain returns, and a cure is
certain. This remedy must be applied in the first
stages, as it is of no avail after the finger is greatly
swollen.
	Insanity.—The principal causes of insanity are
the result of an inordinate mode of living, eating
and drinking those articles which coagulate the
blood, and consume the natural forces; and also
the effect of medicines, which have for the last
half century been taken into the human system


under all circumstances. Study the emanations of
physical laws and adhere to the requirements of
the circulating medium, which moves the wheel of
life and carries in its force of action the finer ele-
ments thrown upon the tissues of the brain. If
this circulation becomes impeded, especially through
the brain, it creates an obstruction which destroys
the equillibrium, and insanity must ensue in a
greater cr less degree. Opiates and other concen-
trated medicines, which are now so freely used
are often the cause of insanity. It is possible for
insanity to be brought on by suffering, but unless
the brain had previously become diseased from
some other cause, I am inclined to think physical
force would succumb to suffering before the intel-
lect. Live in accordance with the finer elements
of life and health, and then nature will sway the
sceptre of her utmost power.
						Mrs. C. Van Cort.


PARAGRAPH FROM A GENTILE
LADY'S LETTER.

TO MRS. M. A. BOISE, ONEIDA CO.,
IDAHO.

	I get the WOMAN'S EXPONENT regularly,
and tongue can not express the thanks I
have to offer you for sending me such a val-
uable paper. You cannot know what solid
comfort I take in reading it. I see the util-
ity of your good Relief Societies there in
the Mormon Church. Where is there an-
other church where the women work for
each other's benefit like the women of
Utah? This paper is, in a great measure,
removing the prejudice from some who
thought that there was no good among the
Mormons.
	HARRIET M. PURVINES.
Petersburg, III., Dec. 15, 1876.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.		110


[Column 1]
			WOMAN'S VOICE.

						Dec. 14, 1876.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	According to your request I write to in-
form you of the feeling existing in our set-
tlement in regard to the Grain Question.
We feel with the rest of our sisters to obey
counsel; we have met together and talked
upon the subject, we intend to take meas-
ures to secure some grain, if not much; we
have not a great deal in our settlement; we
were visited by early frost which cut our
crops short. We feel to make some united
effort, and do all we possibly can, then leave
the result to the Lord. I will write to you
again after we have ascertained or decided
what we can do under the circumstances.
	I remain your sister in the Gospel,
					ELIZABETH CLARKE.
	Weston, Oneida Co., Idaho.

			SALEM, UTAH, Dec. 17, 1876.
EDITORS EXPONENT:
	The EXPONENT becomes more interesting
as it grows older, particularly the last num-
ber excels all the rest. I do not wish to
miss one number as I have taken it from
the first. It makes my heart feel thankful
to God when I read the doings of the sis-
ters, and may God bless them in their
glorious undertaking, and when gaunt hun-
ger stalks abroad may their bins be over-
flowing that none may call in vain on them
for assistance.
	Success to the EXPONENT, and all con-
nected with it is the constant prayer of
your sister in the Gospel,
				MATILDA. P. KILLIAN.

EDITOR EXPONENT:
	Amid the cogitations of my mind I have
dwelt with amazement on the title which
has been placed upon the societies of wo-
men. The word Relief fills my soul with
reflections such as how shall I with dignity
fill such a responsible calling; viz., to make
my home comfortable, to make those going
from home happy and contented, relieving
the wants of a large family circle in sick-
ness or trouble; responding to the require-
ments of my husband in his every day busi-
ness, and instructing the household that
they be economical and discharge their du-
ties faithfully, so as to relieve the father
and husband of unecessary costs etc; and if
I think I will while away an hour or two in
gossip, and rest my mind a little, just then
my attention is called to one who is in need
of relief, perhaps sick, or in trouble or des-
titute of the comforts of life, that perhaps
I may relieve a little in word or deed; then
I must omit my anticipated gossiping, con-
sequently I fear I shall not be posted in my
neighbors business, and the interesting
chit chat, which the Apostle James tells
us about in giving his description of the
tongue, will have to be put off. Then
comes a paper, perhaps the EXPONENT, of
course I am anxious to read that, here I find
reports from the several branches of the Re-
lief Society which are organized and set
apart to relieve the wants of the afflicted,
and those who are unfortunate and down-
cast. And as I have a responsible calling
in this Relief Association, I ask myself,
how shall I honor this calling? I must
cease to portray and represent the weak-
nesses of others, I will not assist to bind
down the broken hearted, I must not be
hasty in giving my decision on hearing a
rumor, as they often arise from misunder-
standings only, while a hasty reply is the
cause of many difficulties; nor will I say

[Column 2]
that the commandment of saving grain,
given by our honorable president is a spec-
ulative one, but rather will I say I have
been a participant of hard times in the past
and will exert my energies with those of
my sisters who are trying to make better
the future. In this life-giving counsel, we
have a chance to exhibit relief beyond our
present comprehension. I am happy to say
that our honorable bishop and others of the
brethren are willing to assist us in procur-
ing and storing away grain, and we as a lit-
tle band of sisters are united in this sub-
lime matter; and let us chime in together
with the poem: "Hard times, Hard times
come again no more."
	With congratulation, I am
					Yours Truly,
							M. A. BOICE.
	Oxford, Dec. 2, 1876.

HUMILITY.

	Humility is the subject before the meet-
ing, and it is very important that we study
and reflect so that we may comprehend it
in its true light. We should all live hum-
ble and prayerful that the spirit of God
may dwell in our bosoms, and so that there
be nothing to grieve it from us. The Lord
says "if ye are not as humble as little
children ye can in no wise enter the king-
dom of heaven." Then let us examine our-
selves and see if we are sufficiently humble
and that we remember His words, for the
Lord means just what He says. He is the
same yesterday, to-day and forever. How
shall we then keep this spirit of humility
with us? Will it be by neglecting our du-
ties and by forgetting God and letting our
thoughts be carried away with worldly
things? No! I think not. We must seek
the Lord often in earnest prayer, and ask
Him to endow us with that spirit of meek-
ness that will bring to our hearts true joy
and lasting happiness. We know that God
is no respecter of persons, but acknowledges
every honest heart, no matter whether the
possessor dwells in a hut or a palace. Our
Father in heaven knows our motives and
desires, and is not pleased with outward
display.
	We are striving to assist in advancing
the kingdom of God on the earth, and we
cannot do this except we cherish this spirit
of humility. Then my sisters let it be our
aim to serve the Lord with all our might,
mind and strength if we expect to be num-
bered with the chosen. We cannot serve
the Lord with one hand and Satan with the
other. We must be saints in very deed.
Our whole souls must be in the work, other-
wise we can accomplish but very little good.
It will not do to say we will accept this and
reject that. True saints must embrace
every principle pertaining to salvation, ad-
vanced by the servants of God, and try to


live in accordance with the same. There
is nothing too hard for us to do in order
that we may obtain our exaltation. God re-
quires nothing of His children that they
are not able to perform, but we must be
determined, and pray for strength and it
will be given us. The Lord is very merci-
ful; He is always more willing to bestow
than we are to receive and obey. Our re-
ligion brings to us sweet and holy feelings
and we will obtain eternal life through its
teachings if we seek for it truly, and is it
not worth working for, and enduring all the
little trials of this life?
	Let us be obedient to those who are
placed over us, and the Lord will bless us;
and especially let us carry out the wishes of
our president, and do all we can to encour-
age her, and sustain her with our faith and
prayers. We have a glorious work to ac-
complish here below. We each have a
mission to perfect ourselves, and prepare
for the great hereafter.
	Sisters let us then be united, for in unity
there is strength. Let us sustain each oth-
er and devote all our time and talents to
the building up of the kingdom of God, so
that we may have His spirit at all times.
					JOSEPHINE MYER.
	11th Ward Relief Association, Sept., 1876.


VERDI thus describes the fashion in
which he composes: "I first study thor-
oughly the characters of the dramatis per-
sonae. Then I commit the libretto to mem-
ory, and, girding myself for the task, work
away for eight or nine months, and more
if necessary, until the opera is completed.
Meanwhile I only live in my imagination;
hence my irritability, ill-humor, and inciv-
ility; for, though I am a bear by nature, at
such times I become more of a bear than
ever." Verdi rarely attends the theatre,
especially the opera. It is said that he nev-
er witnessed the representation of his
"Ballo in Maschera" until ten years after
its first appearance upon the stage. A man
of culture, he is a connoisseur in art, is
thoroughly versed in Italian, French and
Spanish literature, and is familiar with the
best German and English authors. He has
frequent recourse to the original source of
his inspiration, the Bible. Though he styles
himself a "bear," and often alludes with a
spice of pleasantry to his peasant birth, he
is courteous though simple in his manners,
easy and fluent in his conversation, and per-
sonally a great favorite among his more in-
timate friends. A Senator of the Kingdom,
he betrays no ambition to shine as a parlia-
mentary leader.


When Professor Aytoun was making pro-
posals of marriage to his first wife, a daugh-
ter of the celebrated Professor Wilson, the
lady reminded him that it would be neces-
sary to ask the approval of her sire.
	"Certainly," said Aytoun; "but as I am a
little diffident in speaking to him on this
subject, you must just go and tell him my
proposals yourself."
	The lady proceeded to the library, and
taking her father affectionately by the
hand, mentioned that Professor Aytoun had
asked her to become his wife. She added,
"Shall I accept his offer papa? He says he
is too diffident to name the subject to you-
himself."
	"Then" said old Christopher, "I had bet-
ter write my reply and pin it to your back."
He did so, and the lady returned to the
drawing-room. There the anxious suitor
read the answer to his message, which was
in these words: "With the author's compli-
ments."


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


PEN AND SCISSORS.

	"I THINK I will have Autumn leaves on
my new hat," said she. "I am glad of it,"
said he, "for Autumn leaves are falling."
	A would-be gentleman, the other day,
called at the post-office, and displayed his
ignorance of natural history or the French
language, or both, by requesting to be sup-
plied with a stamped antelope!
	UNEXPECTED HARMONY.—He was carv-
ing at dinner, and thought he must talk to
the aesthetic-looking angel on his right.
"How do you like Beethoven?" asked he,
at a venture. "Well done," said she
promptly, interested in the business at
hand.
	It was sometimes the custom of the poet
Wordsworth to ramble about the country in
his neighborhood composing poems aloud.
The estimate placed on that sort of thing
by those of the vicinity may appear by the
answer of a poor man who was breaking
stones on the Rydal road. A gentleman
who knew both him and Wordsworth found
him at work one day, and greeted him
with, "Good morning, John. What news
have you this morning?"
	"Why, nothing very particular, only old
Wordsworth's broken loose again."
	And when the poet died, a sympathizing
neighbor suggested, as an alleviation for
the affliction, that "the mistress is a clever-
ish sort of body, I reckon she can carry on
the business."


LANGUAGE OF FINGER-RINGS—In case
of a gentleman wishing to marry—literally
in the market with his heart—he wears a
plain or chased gold ring upon the first fin-
ger of the left or heart hand. When suc-
cess attends his suit, and he is actually en-
gaged, the ring passes to the second finger.
After marriage it passes to the third finger.
If, however, the gentleman desires to tell
the fair ones that he not only is not "in the
market," but does not design to marry at
all; he wears the signet upon his little fin-
ger, and all ladies may understand that he
is out of their reach. With the fair sex
the "laws of the ring" are: A plain or chased
gold ring on the little finger of the right
hand implies "not engaged," or in plainer
words, "ready for proposals, sealed or other-
wise." When engaged, the ring passes to
the third finger of the right hand. When
married, the third finger of the left hand
receives it. If the fair one proposes to defy
all seige to her heart, she passes the rings
on her first and fourth fingers—one on each
—like two charms to keep away the tempt-
er. It is somewhat singular that this dis-
position of rings is rare,


THOMAS C. JONES, of Salt Lake City,
brush maker, requests the people of Utah
who are killing pigs this winter to save the
bristles, and when they come to the city be
so kind as to bring them and leave them
with David Hilton, butcher, at the Tithing
Office. Bro. Jones can't give pay for them
as the work of preparing them will not af-
ford it, and so asks it as a favor.

WOMAN'S EXPONENT.
VOL. 5	SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, JANUARY 15, 1877.	No. 16.


HOROSCOPE.

My baby sleeps: his precious head is laid
Upon my arm close to my happy heart.
In all the beauty of sweet infancy,
And heavenly innocence, and undefiled,
Angelic purity, he nestles close
Within my sheltering arms; and, gazing now
Upon thy fair, unconscious face, my son,
And feeling deep within my love-rapt heart
The countless worth of one pure infant soul,
My spirit kindles with the living glow
Of prophecy; to-night I could withdraw
The close-drawn vail between the fleeing now,
And that bright realm of hope futurity,
And say, dear child, what life will bring to thee.

My mother-heart, with all its love and pride,
Would crown thee king in heart and mind and soul,
Without one mortal frailty strong enough to chain
Thy heavenward aspiring soul to earth.
It pictures thee so strong that thou canst lend
A helping hand to all the weak and frail;—
So brave that courage shall ignite anew
The failing fires of hope in many a heart
Whose lamp of faith is flickering dim and low,
And from whose failing arm the strength is gone;—
So great the meanest never fear to claim
Their little due, their modest consequence;
So pure that gold, though seven fold refined
Is base alloy beside thy spotless worth,
And Christ himself, though Lamb of Innocence,
Might well delight to share with thee His crown.
Oh, joy! I will the mystic vail unclose
And feast my soul upon this vision bright,
And bask in light thus shining from afar,
And bless my motherhood thus glorified!

But stay! a shadow gloomy, strange and chill,
Falls o'er the glowing presage fancy wrought,
And bids me, sorely 'gainst my willingness,
See, what might be the bitter opposite—
My noble giant weak, and frail, and vile;
Speed like mad charges to the gulf of death;
This lily hand, which I so fondly kiss,
Imbued in some weak brother's welling blood;
This seraph brow black with the brand of Cain.
This incense breath hot with the fumes of wine;
And, reeking with the foul narcotic weed,
These censer lips pollute with blasphemy;
Woe! shame! My brain grows sick and wild
My tortured fancy shrinks aghast—appalled
I dare not look! Oh, draw the curtain close,
Lest I be seared in heart, and brain, and eye.
Thou God of love, and could it then be so,
That this white soul could grow so black and vile?
Oh! rather take him to thy heart of love,
mind leave me on the earth without a child.

I strain my cherub to my bursting heart,
And shower tender kisses, fervent prayers,
And tears of grief and love upon his head.
Love is so strong, and yet so powerless
To hedge the loved one in from danger dire!
I can but pray; 'tis he—this child—alone
Who must the fearful conflict win or lose.
I can but feebly arm the unskilled hand,
Almighty God, but thou canst make it strong.—
The waves of thought sweep swiftly on and on
Till, through my love-born, deep anxiety,
Unto my troubled heart the "still small voice"
Breathes low and sweetly, "Peace, be still!"

Though earth be overrun with craft and guile,
And men defile their bodies and their souls,
And say God sleeps, and neither hears nor sees
Their deep iniquities, I am awake!
I have reserved unto this fearful day,
Unto myself a chosen noble few.
To come forth now—to shine on earth
A living light that all who will may see.
Let thine be one: and do thou watch and pray,
And pour into his soul my living word
To be his spirit's light 'mid deepest dark,
His sure defense whatever foes assail;
His arm of strength to raise my fallen ones;
His heart of grace the weakest ne'er to scorn;
His rock of faith which shall forever stand;—
Do this, O mother, and be strong of heart,
For all who trust in Me shall surely find
My word returns not void; nor one true soul
Will I forsake:—Remember I am God."
	LU DALTON.


[Column 1]
				LETTER.

						Dec. 28th, 1876.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	A sense of gratitude impels this attempt
to address you, and a species of gratitude
that only the "absent one" can feel for
aught which speaks of home and its beloved
associations. I do not wish you to think
your little paper is unappreciated; it is a
regular and truly welcome visitor, and its
contents I peruse with the deepest interest,
especially the accounts of the noble efforts
of our sisters to carry out the counsels of
our President. How nobly they are work-
ing: and truly the results of past endeav-
ors have proven what the united efforts of
faithful women can accomplish. Who can
but recognize the important role played by
WOMAN in every community. No organiz-
ation is complete without her influence, and
whree is her influence more potent and
more freely acknowledged than among the
Latter-day Saints. The world regard Po-
lygamy as degrading and lowering, and as
the great circumscriber of the intellectual
faculties of woman, but how well do we
know the contrary to be the truth, and un-
derstand the great advantages of a family
organized upon this elevated plan. Every
wife is benefitted both temporally and spir-
itually, and her sphere becomes broader,
grander and holier. Not only can we per-
form the sacred duties of wife and mother,
so dear to every true woman's heart, but
our usefulness can extend to other great
and glorious fields, and we can be as co-
workers with our brethren in rolling forththe great work of the Latter days.
	In addition to the counsel to organize in-
to Relief and Retrenchment Societies—the
great blessing of which all Israel can but
acknowledge—we have had many other im-
portant counsels, which the majority of our
sisters have faithfully endeavored to put
into execution, and the results have gener-
ally proven satisfactory, because our Father
is the great instigator of all.
	Cultivate the mulberry and manufacture
silk; make your own hats and bonnets, and
"let your adornments be the workmanship
of your own hands." In short the idea is,
sustain home manufacture in every sense of
the word, by your faith, your prayers, your
patronage, and by conducting and sustain-
ing an institution wherein home products
may be bought and sold.
	And now the word is, "Sisters, go thou to
and save the grain." What a mission! Do
we feel sufficiently the responsibility? Truly
it should be regarded as the "word of the
Lord," and we should understand that all
assist in this enterprise by retrenching
from every superfluity and practising wise
economy.
	Then let us all be gleaners, that the great
Reaper may not be forced to chastise us for
extravagance, or indolence, but seeing our
faithfulness and diligence, will, as in that
harvest time of old, leave for us "a few
bundles by the wayside."

	Then, sisters, work with woman's will,
	And all the bins and gran'ries fill;
	Yes, every mother, sister, wife
	Save grain for "bread—the staff of life."
	That when the time so long declared

[Column 2]
	Shall come, we'll not be unprepared;
	Save every penny, nickel, dime—
	Lay by your frills and ribbons fine,
	Retrench from all that will not give
	Results to teach us how to live
	According to the counsels given
	By noble men inspired of Heaven.

	Praying for the success of the EXPONENT
and all other efforts of our sisters to obey
counsel, I remain
	Your sister in the Gospel,
							I. V. H.


NOTES AND NEWS.

	CONGRESS RECEIVED on Tuesday, Jan. 2;
the staues of Adams and Winthrop, as
the gift of the State to the Pantheon in the
old Hall of Representatives at Washington.
	"HISTORY AND Description of the Cen-
tennial Exhibition.". In a very attractive
volume, Edward C. Bruce condenses a his-
tory of the late Centennial Exhibition; and
by the aid of pen and picture brings the
whole event before the reader in a living
panorama. It will be of great interest to
those who visited the Exposition.
	AS HAS been mentioned, the next Inter-
national Exhibition will be at Paris; con-
tracts for the buildings and other works are
already given out, and it is announced that
the opening will be on the 1st of May, 1878.
The Prince of Wales has authorized the
British amdassador at Paris to declare, in
his name, that he will send for exhibition
the fine collection of interesting and pre-
cious objects that he brought with him from
the East.
	TEMPORARY STARS are occasionally dis-
covered in the heavens which suddenly
flare up with intense brightness, remain
shining a while, and then dwindle down to
very small stars or disappear entirely. On
November 24 a brightly radiating star was
seen in the constellation of Cygnus by an
astronomer at the observatory in Athens,
Greece. On December 20 this new star was
found and examined at the Litchfield Ob-
servatory of Hamilton College. It was
then greatly reduced in size from what it
was when first seen. The most remarkable
temporary star on record is the Nova of
Tycho Brahe, that appeared in 1572 in Cas-
siopeia. It became bright as Venus, and
was even visible in the daytime. It could
be seen with the naked eye for seventeen
months.
	REFERENCE WAS made some time ago to
a plan for the fertilization of Africa, by the
submerging of the desert of Sahara through
a canal which would let in the waters of the
Mediterranean. This seems to have been
abandoned, as it was feared that very great
meteorological changes might be produced
thereby, which might be prejudicial. It is
now proposed to submerge a part of North
Africa by a canal from the Gulf of Gabes,
letting in the waters of the Mediterranean
westwards, over the lake region of Djerid;
and this scheme is said to be not only prac-
ticable, but likely to prove remunerative;
the area being comparatively small, and the
meteorological changes likely to be slight,
quite local, and beneficial.


122		WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]

R. S. REPORTS

	The General Retrenchment meeting, held
in the Fourteenth Ward Assembly Rooms,
Saturday, Dec. 23rd, 1876, was presided
over by Mrs. M. I. Horne, who had been for
some time absent, and who expressed her-
self gratified to be again at home with the
sisters, who were co-workers with each
other in the great work of the improvement
of the spiritual and temporal condition of
the saints. She called the attention of the
sisters to the many duties to be performed,
and the necessity of diligence and faithful-
ness; she referred to the preparation the
sisters had been called upon to make in re-
spect to the future, when famine, pestilence
and destruction would visit the nations.
She exhorted the sisters to pray for wis-
dom, and listen to the still small voice bear-
ing witness in their hearts to the words of
eternal life. She referred the sisters to the
subject of the closing year, and entreated
them to consider well their opportunities
and blessings, and desired that each one of
those who had taken upon them the name
of Saint should honor it and improve
every good gift or talent, and devote them-
selves to the great work of reformation and
redemption. She told the sisters God had
revealed His relationship to the children of
men, and it devolved upon all to seek
mightily to carry out the Savior's golden
rule, "To do unto others as we would they
should do unto us," and overcome the self-
ishness which has become so general all
over the world, and penetrated into the
church and kingdom of God.
	Counselor Miss E. R. Snow then address-
ed the meeting upon the subject of punctu-
ality; said it was not only the key to busi-
ness but to the confidence of the people; it
is one of the essential traits of character to
cultivate, and should be carefully observed.
Counselor Zina D. Young and herself had
been North and held meetings in Harris-
ville, Mariot, and met with the brethren
and sisters in Kaysville. They found the
people waking up considerably to the inter-
ests and welfare of Zion. She spoke con-
cerning the Lamanites, and some manifest-
ation s that had been given to the sisters,
and gave much valuable instruction.
	Counselors Zina D. Young and Bathsheba
W. Smith and several other sisters spoke
upon subjects which are applicable to the
present time.

				TOOELE, Jan. 5, 1877.
EDITORS EXPONENT:
	We wish to say a few words to our sisters
through your columns, to let them know
how we are prospering, and if possible to
give some poor encouragement to others
who are trying to walk in the same narrow
path. We opened our new Hall on the 22d
of December, to help defray some of the
building expenses, and I can say our party
was quite a success. We partook of supper
prepared by the several sisters in the school-
house opposite the new Hall; about seventy
sat down to the tables which were luxuri-
antly supplied with the good things of the
earth. A good spirit and feeling prevailed
throughout the entertainment; those who
participated in the dance enjoyed it ex-
ceedingly, and harmony and union charac-
terized the assembly. The sisters in every
ward donated liberally in the way of provi-
sions and delicacies, and there was an abun-
dance left to distribute among the poor and
also to the poor Indians, who have not yet
learned the art of cultivating the soil, and
supplying themselves with the comforts
they see us enjoying. Nothing was lost or

[Column 2]

wasted, and a great deal of good must be
the result of such gatherings.
	On the 25th another party was given for
the same purpose, viz., to receive means to
help defray expenses of the building of
the Hall. The sisters of the Relief Society
are alive to their duties; we have some-
thing near one hundred bushels of wheat
laid up for a future day, for those emergen-
cies which have been predicted. We feel
thankful for the knowledge given us through
inspiration, that we may prepare ourselves
and thus escape some of the many calam-
ities which will transpire in the last dispen-
sation. We meet every two weeks in the
new meeting house, when we enjoy the
Spirit of the Lord, and those who attend
regularly feel desirous to do all in their
power to help roll on the work of redemp-
tion. Our President, Sister Micklejohn, is
earnest in exhorting the sisters to become
united and live their religion to its truest
sense and spirit. Your excellent paper is
read with great interest, and the best
wishes are expressed for the advancement
of every good work.
	We remain your sisters &c.,
		EMILY WARBURTON, Sec'y.
			ANN YATE, Ass't Sec'y.

	EPHRAIM, SANPETE Co., Dec. 13, 1876.
	By request of our President, Mrs. S. A.
Petersen, I send you a report of what the
sisters in Ephraim are doing about the
"storing of Grain." The Sisters of the re-
lief Society went out in the harvest field
one day each, to glean; they gleaned fifty
bushels. They have also made cheese and
and jeans which they have sold; and pieced
up patch-work quilts and obtained one hun-
dred and seventeen bushels more, making
one hundred and sixty-seven bushels be-
longing to the Society.
	In addition to this our President has pro-
posed a plan, that as many sisters as choose
to co-operate together and take shares, one
bushel of wheat being a share, the wheat to
be untouched until a time of famine. This
wheat to be earned by the sisters them-
selves not troubling their husbands about
it at all; and strive to increase the amount
of their shares until famine puts a stop to
their exertions in that line. Several sisters
have taken shares, some one, some two,
some five, according to their ability. The
shares already taken amount to one hun-
dred and thirty-three bushels; so we have
altogether, three hundred bushels. The
Bishop has promised us a bin in the new
store-house when it is finished. The sis-
ters are alive to their duties in the Gospel,
and in all the labors pertaining to the
kingdom.
	I will close with best wishes for your
happiness.
	AGNES ARMSTRONG.

	PAYSON, Jan. 4, 1877.
Editor Exponent:
	At a meeting held by the Relief Society
Dec. 6, 1876, B. J. Simons was chosen
President of the Purchasing Committe,
Mrs. Martha Simons, Mrs. Susan Fair-
banks and Mrs. Clarissa Moore to solicit do-
nations and purchase grain, Mrs. Ann Mat-
tison and Mrs. Sarah Ann Reece Assistants,
Mrs. Agnes Douglas Treasurer, Mrs. M. A.
Hardy Secretary.
	We have on hand nearly one hundred
bushels of wheat with a prospect of more;
the sisters feel with our President and
Counsel that the move is a grand one; and

[Column 3]

they all desire to be one with their sisters
in Salt Lake City and throughout Utah in
aiding, with all their might and strength,
to roll on the great work in which we are
all engaged; they join with me in love,
wishing you a happy New Year.
	I deeply sympathize with our dear sister
Mrs. L. Greene Richards in the death of
her little girl, having buried five children
myself. How sweet were the words that
flowed from her pen in the Exponent of
Nov. 15th; may our Heavenly Father bless
and comfort her
	Your sister &c.,
		M. A. HARDY.


WOMAN'S VOICE.

			PLAIN CITY, Jan. 6, 1877.
EDITORS EXPONENT:
	We have just had our first meeting, to
talk about the call there was made on us to
store up grain as helpmeets in the kingdom
of God. We are all willing to do the best
we can with the little means we have; our
President says we can afford to buy fifty
bushels at ninety cents per bushel. We
have got a good bin to store it in, and hope,
by the help of God and of our brethren,
we will still add to it an abundant supply
in the future. Thare is no sickness in our
place, nor starving poor.
	God bless you and all who try to do right
is the prayer of your sister in the Gospel,
				A. C. HEDVIG HANSEN.

				SANTAQUIN, Jan. 3, 1877.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	In accordance with your wishes, I write
to inform you that the Relief Society of
this place has responded to the call in rela-
tion to storing grain. A good spirit pre-
vails among the sisters, and all feel willing
to carry out the counsel of President Young
in regard to storing up grain.
	Bro. August Tietgen has kindly given
us the use of one of his wheat bins, until
our good brothers can build us a granary.
The sisters have nobly and liberally given
according to their circumstances, and have
donated at present sixty-five bushels, and
the stream still continues. We feel willing,
with the help of our Father in heaven, to
do all we can in storing up grain for our
temporal salvation; and, dear sisters, when
we have secured the grain, let us be faith-
fnl stewards over the same, and, putting
our trust in God, leave the event with Him.
	I remain your sister in the Gospel,
				ELIZABETH J. STICKNEY.

			BEAVER CITY, Jan. 3, 1877.
EDITORS EXPONENT:
	I wish to add to the testimony of my sis-
ters a few thoughts and suggestions in re-
gard to the interests in which the women of
Utah are engaged. I feel a great interest
in the EXPONENT; I would rather miss a
meal of victuals daily than be without it.
Times are dull and money scarce, but I know
the feelings of the greater number are that
the EXPONENT should be sustained as well
as to attend to every other call made upon
us now—the laying up of grain, assisting
msssionaries, caring for our own poor, the
Woman's Book, and sundry other duties
and responsibilities. We have built a gran-
ary sufficiently large to hold four or five
hundred bushels of grain, and have placed
a small quantity of grain in it, and we in-
tend to add to it as fast as it is in our power,
and also to build a larger building when
needed for our increased supplies.
	Your sister &c.,
					RUTH W. TYLER.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.		123


For the EXPONENT.
A VOICE FROM AN ABSENT ONE.

The wintry day, now drawing to its close,
Invites all wearied nature to repose,
And shades of night are falling dense and fast,
Like sable curtains closing o'er the past.
Pale, through the gloom, the newly fallen snow
Lays like a shroud upon the earth below;
As though, in mercy, God had spread the p[-]ll—
A symbol of forgiveness to us all.
The flood of thought, which struggles to be free,
Now opens wide the gates of memory;
Love lights the way, and, guided by its beam,
My soul floats on the surface of the stream.
I cannot go to rest, but linger still
In meditation at the window sill,
While, like the twinkling stars in heaven's dome,
Come, one by one, sweet memories of home.
And wouldst thou ask me where my fancy roves
To reproduce the happy scenes it loves?
Where hope and memory together dwell,
And paint the pictured beauties that I tell?
Far, far away, among the Western hills
And gardens watered by the mountain rills:
Where giant peaks uprear their heads so high
The cloud capped-summits seem to pierce the sky;
Where smiling valleys, from the desert torn,
Redeemed, are waving with their golden corn;
Where virtue, truth and innocence, allied,
Are held in veneration—far and wide;
Where sweet religion in its purity
Invites all men to its security:
There is my home—the spot I love so well—
Whose modest worth and beauty few can tell—
Away beyond the prairies of the West,
Where exiled saints, in solitude, were blessed;
Where industry the seal of wealth has set
Amit the peaceful vales of Deseret.
Unheeding still, the fiercest blasts that blow—
With tops encrusted by eternal snow—
The peaks, with bases set on rock-bound sod,
Stand, types of freedom, reared by nature's God.
The winter snows melt, part, in summer's beams,
And from the canyons rush the crystal streams—
Our God protects the church of His first born—
And winds are tempered to the lamb that's shorn—
Divinity the bounteous means supplies
A desert to reclaim and fertilize.
Throughout this place, for nine and twenty years,—
Since, first, the little band of pioneers
Explored the waste, and opened up the way
For those who followed at a later day—
Have lived a people, honest, firm and g[-]o[-];
Who, by their mighty faith in God, withstood
The wrath of man and nature, in a cause
Which comprehended His eternal laws.
The wilderness that naught, before, could yield,
Is now become a fertile, fruitful field:
Where roamed at will the savage Indian bands,
In pride and wealth a peaceful city stands.
All men who seek the truth are welcome there;
All followers of Christ their bounty share;
And all, who cast base prejudice away
And let impartial judgment bear the sway,
Will find a virtuous people, falsified
To whom a freeman's just right is denied. 'I
Sweet Mountain Home! The refuge of the world—
Though persecution's rage is at thee hurled,
Thou shalt stand firm, for God in thy defense,
Will waste thy foes with war and pestilence.
This is His promise—will He break it? No,
We've proved Him oft, and know it must be so.
Then be we brave as lions—meek as doves—
For such our Father, in His wisdom, loves.
Heed not the slander of the evil tongue,
Nor doubt, the hand of God is o'er us hung.
Has He not said—"The weak of earth I take
The power of the worldly wise to shake?"
And has he not, in many scenes now past,
Proved that he will be with us to the last?
Then let each simple daily action prove
That we are striving to deserve His love.
"Feed and clothe the poor, comfort those who mourn"
And heal the wounded heart by sorrow torn,
Keep His commandments—to all men be true,
"Do unto others as we'd have them do;"
And let our faith and trust unaltered to
In God—through time and all eternity.
									O. F. W


"AM you familiar wid de Talmud?" asked
a literary African of a sable brother; to
which the latter responded, "De tall mud?"
"Yes, sah, to de tallest kind ob mud. I'm
a native of New York and to de mud and
manner born."


[Column 1]
		NOTES ON TEMPERANCE.

	TO THE SUNDAY SCHOOLS OF AMERICA.

	We are sometimes told that total absti-
nence is not temperance. What is tem-
perance? Let me give you a short reply.
Temperance is the lawful gratification of a
natural appetite. Is the appetite for intox-
icating liquors a natural appetite? No.
Therefore temperance is total abstinence
from intoxicating liquors as a beverage.
	This principle is a sensible principle.
When you are as old as I am, you will re-
gret many things you have learned in the
past; but you will never regret that you
did not learn to use intoxicating liquor. I
have never met a person, nor do I believe
you can find the person in the world who
would say, "I am fifty years of age, and
never drank a glass of liquor in my life;
and I regret that I did not learn to drink it
when I was young." No. When you meet
with one who has never drank, he tells you,
"I am glad of it," or, "I am proud of it."
A man once called on me, and said; "I am
not a reformer. I care little for reforms, or
missions, or Sunday schools. They are all
very well in their way, but they are not in
my line. I have been an actor since I was
eighteen, and I am now forty-three, and I
never drank a glass of ale, wine or spirits in
my life. What do you think of that?" I
am pooud of it. He had little care for re-
form, but he never drank liquor in his life,
and was proud of it.
	Dear children, you would be shocked if
you could read some of the letters that lie
in my desk, and that have been written to
me by persons of all grades of society—
young men, old men, lawyers, physicians,
ministers of the Gospel, teachers, mechan-
ics, clerks, and some ladies—who have ac-
quired the appetite for strong drink. One
says, "Dives in hell never longed for a drop
of water, as with all the power there is in
me I long for drink." Another says, "Is
there any hope for me this side of the
grave?" Another says, "God knows how
near I have been to self destruction through
drink," and so on.
	One poor man, actually holding my feet,
cried out, "O! help me out of this hell,
Drink is my curse." Yes, dear children,
the cry comes from the inmates of the lu-
natic asylums, "Drink is my curse;" from
the state prisons, "Drink is my curse;"
from innocent victims—wives, mothers,
children, "Drink is my curse;" from the
burning lips of the dying drunkard comes
the despairing cry, "Drink is my curse."
There are broken hearts, blighted hopes,
blackened characters, crushed intellects,
and lost souls as the results of strong drink,
and not a single individual but rejoices in
his escape from it.
	Is not the total abstinence principle sen-
sible? "But all who drink do not become
drunkards." I know that; but if fifty
young men begin to drink, some will assur-
edly be rnined by it. Then there is a risk.
Now we all desire safety and security.
Suppose you desire to travel from New
York to Chicago, and there were two lines
of road—one on which there were accidents
constantly occurring; on every train some
disaster, passengers killed and wounded; in
short, a very risky road;—and on the other,
never since its opening had there been the
slightest accident. Which road would you
take? If you are sensible, and regard your
own welfare, you would take the safe one.
Suppose some one should tempt you to take
the risky road, by telling you how much
more beautiful the cars were, and what a

[Column 2]
jolly company you would find on board the
train. You would say, "I care not so much
for the gaudy cars and jolly company as for
my safety. I want to be safe." Now it is
your safety we seek, when we urge you to
abstain entirely from strong drink. There
is no certainty that you will become vic-
tims if you begin to indulge, but there is a
risk.
	Some say, "I can govern myself; I have a
mind of my own." What would you think
of a captain of a steam vessel who would
put on a full head of steam, and then knock
down the man at the wheel? Or, of a con-
ductor on a railroad, who would let on the
steam and then disable the engineer? You
would say he was a reckless man. The
steamer or the engine might get through
without accident, but it might go crashing
to destruction. So when a person, using
that which weakens the power of his will,
depends on his weakened will to serve him,
—using that which warps his judgment,
and then depends on his warped judge-
ment to guide him,—using that which af-
fects his self-control, and then trusts his de-
ranged self-control to keep him from dan-
ger—he is reckless. Simply because some
men drink and do not become drunkards,
can you? I once saw a man stand on
a small platform outside the spire of a
church, and looked down on the pavement
150 feet below. Because he did it, can you?
Think of these things before you run the
risk, ank remember what the risk is.
	You have, under God, the future in your
power; your destiny in your own control.
Remember there is no one evil in the world
before which so many fall, as the evil of in-
temperance.
	I most earnestly desire that the youth of
our country, especially our Sabbath-school
scholars, shall give all their influence
against this great evil. May God help you
to avoid the perils in life's journey, and the
traps that are set for your feet, so that from
your place of safety you may reach out
your hand to help the tempted who are
sturggling in their chains, and may by self-
denial be enabled to "fulfill the law of 
Christ," by helping some poor, burdened
souls into the higher life of purity and
freedom!—Messenger of Peace.


A POPULAR "SAVING'S BANK."

	An exchange says:
	"A saving woman at the head of a family
is the very best savings bank yet establish-
ed."	Possibly, but there is small incentive for
a woman to establish these banks, for after
a long life of rigid economy and careful
painstaking the entire accumulations are
beyond her reach and belong solely in law
as well as in name to her husband. The
use of one-third during her lifetime, and
the "bank" building rent free for one year
to live in after the death of the husband is
by men's laws and enactments deemed suf-
ficient compensaaion to the woman to whom
by common consent is accorded the credit
of saving the whole. Women who have to
"pray the court for relief" in the shape of a
monthly stipend for the support of them-
selves and children and find this doled out
to them from their own "savings bank,"
according to a stranger's judgment of their
needs, understand this matter. While
present customs obtain, we don't advise
any woman to deny herself anything that
the finances of the firm will purchase, in
order to build up a future over which she
has no control.—Ex.

SALT LAKE CITY, JAN. 15, 1877.


[Column 1]
		WORK FOR A PURPOSE.

	There has just arisen for man and woman-
kind another year; with each successive year it
is customary to take a g[-]ance of retrospection
over the past one and contrast it with what
one had previously thought to accomplish.
There seems to be allotted to all persons a cer-
tain round of duties which some seem bound
to perform faithfully, and others dally over or
neglect. To the diligent it is always a source
of unpleasantness not to be able to do all that
seems necessary. If men or women resolve to 
approach a certain standard of excellence and
catch up all the little tangled threads dropped
inadvertently as they travel on the great high-
way of life, they must exercise all the energies
and fortitude they possess and even then it 
will be no easy sask. One great advantage the
diligent and constant worker has, is a freedom
from melancholy; regular work cheers the 
spirits and enlivens many otherwise lonely
hours. It is an antidote for sadness and de-
spondency. People live longer and enjoy life
more completely who have a settled object in 
view.	There is a great work to be done in this gen-
eration and those who are interested in the 
welfare of posterity should be willing and anx-
ious to take up their share of duties and respon-
sibilities and become co-workers with those
who are laboring-assiduously for a great pur-
pose.
	Was there ever a time when there were
more noble incentives to prompt men and wo-
men to action than now? The great vineyard
of the Lord needs more laborers; the bv-ways
and highways are thronged with idlers who 
hinder the workers from accomplishing their
duties and tasks, and offer all sorts of reasoning
and excuses for neglects, and sneer at and r[-]d-
icule the patient, earnest ones and reach out to 
them inducements to falter and linger and
neglect the field of labar. "There is no need
of me" is echoed here and there, "I can pur-
sue my own course and it will be quite as re-
munerative and more congenial, why should I 
trouble myself about the general welfare of
others. I can lead my own life." Yes a pur-
poseless one so far as eternity is concerned,
but pause, all mankind owe the world the best
solutions drawn from varied experiences and
phases of life. All these incidents help to an-
alyze the mysterious problem of the truest
aims and surest success in life. We do not
mean to be understood as alluding to success in 
monetary interests, but to the wisest most judic-
ious and safe control and guidance of all the 
gifts of God to mankind. He has placed His
children here to learn certain lessons, to gain
knowledge of high and exalted principles, and
work out such problems as shall best subserve

[Column 2]
His truest interests. The human body is in all
probability the finest workmanship of the great
Master Architect; and to understand the uses
and applications of all functions and faculties
God has given to man and woman, and make a
proper and just use of time, opportunities and
talents, is something greatly to be desired.
With all the eloquence each one possesses, let
him or her, if they aspire to eternal life
and exaltation, exhort and entreat in all hu-
mility, neighbors or friends, to be more awak-
ened to the special purposes of their creation
and mission upon earth. What need the true
and upright care for scorn or even censure from
those who have not the same noble aims to 
stimulate them? Work for a purpose and let it
be worthy all the sacrifice and endurance mor-
tals are capable of in this mundane sphere.
