			HOME AFFAIRS.

	WE HAVE received for the first time as an
exchange The Utah County Enquirer, full of
County and other news, and seemingly well
adapted to its time and place.
	TENTH WARD.—The pupils of the 10 Ward
Sabbath School had a good time on Sunday.
A hundred and sixty prizes, embracing a wide
variety of articles, were distributed among
them. The teachers of the school also present-
ed a testimonial of respect to the superintend-
ent, Brother Jas. Woods.
	WE ACKNOWLEDGE the receipt [-]rom Wash-
ington of the "Congessional Record" of Dec.
30, 1876, containing the speech of Hon. H. W.
Blair in the House of Representatives. on the
manufacture and sale of intoxicating liquors.
We would be glad to know that there were
many members of Congress as well as all other
public offices and positions, who would lend
their united restraining influence to abolish
this great curse of the country from the United
States. The speech is well worth reading and
gives many statements of facts and figures up-
on this important subject.
	"COMPILED LAWS of Utah."—This volume,
a work of about 900 pages is now completed.
It contains all the general statutes of Utah
now in force, and also the Declaration of Inde-
pendence, the Constitution of the United
States, the Organic Act of Utah, and all the
laws of Congress especially applicable to this
Territory: The work is arranged in regular
legal form, the c'assification of the acts, with
titles and chapters, head and marginal notes, a
complete, clear, and comprehensive index,
show that the legislative committee on compi-
lation, Honorables A O Smoot, Silas S. Smith
and R. T. Burtan, have been most careful and
exact in the performance of their labors.
	The volume is printed in clear, bold type, on
an excellent quality of law paper, and is well
and substantially bound in law calf. The work
was printed and bound at the Deseret News
Office.
	CHRISTMAS FESTIVAL—The 10th Ward
Young People's Christmas Festival, was a most
pleasing affair. At 2 p. m, the gentlemen of
the Deacons' Quorum, and young girls of the
Retrenchment Association assembled at the
new meeting house with their baskets laden
with good things. The hall was beautifully
decorated with festoons made of evergreens,
which the boys procured in the canyons, inter-
spersed with bright-hued artificial flowers
made by the young ladies—the "workmanship
of their own hands;" and those who have a
"love for the beautiful," must surely have felt
a pleasing influence on that bright Christmas
day, as they entered the hall which looked

[Column 2]
truly lovely in its holiday dress. After par-
taking the refreshments luxuriously supplied,
lively games and music were indulged in,
which added hearty enjoyment to the festive
scene, and peal after peal of merry laughter burst
forth from the joyous throng, which proved
very beneficial to the digestive organs, and
created a fund of good feeling in the participa-
tion. At half-past six o'clock the quadrille
band arrived, and the remainder of the even-
ing was past pleasantly in the dance which
closed at a reasonable hour. Thus ended our
Christmos day, and we feel to acknowledge
that God has abundantly blessed us.
									M. P.
	SERICULTURE.—The Deseret Silk Associa-
tion held a special meeting Thursday, Jan. 4,
at the Fireman's Hall, Mrs. Zina D. Young
presiding.
	Mrs. Young said the object of calling the
meeting was to adopt measures to instruct the
people how to raise silk worms successfully,
and in order to do so she thought it best to get
the experience of those who had been success-
ful, from the hatching out of the worm until
it had spun its cocoon; especially the mode of
feeding, and then have the information print-
ed in pamphlet form. She also stated that the
Association had eight pounds eleven ounces of
reeled silk that had been reeled by Mrs Cardon
of Logan.
	Mr. Paul A. Schettler stated that Mr. Joseph
Newman, silk raiser of San Francisco, had
paid us a visit on the 29th of December on his
way home from Philadelphia, where he had
been exhibiting silk culture in all its branches.
He gave us a good deal of useful information,
and said that though our reeled silk was not as
perfect as his, he thought we had made a good
start and ought to feel encouraged. He said
the principal object was to get our farmers to
plant out mulberry trees, millions of them.
	Meeting adjourned till the first Wednesday
in February, at two o'clock p. m.
					R. SIMPSON, Sec'y.

		NOTICE TO SERICULTURISTS
And all persons who have raised silkworms in
Utah. The Association would like them to
send their experience as to the best method to
pursue to raise the worms successfully.
				Address,
					Mrs. Zina D. Young,
							Salt Lake City.
		R. SIMPSON, Secretary.


OBITUARIES.

	SARAH SMITH Griffin, daughter of Hyrum
and Gerusha Smith, died Nov. 6th, in Ogden
City, leaving an infant three weeks old and
four other children with her husband to mourn
her loss. She was born Oct. 2d. 1837, in Kirt-
land, Ohio. Her mother died when she was
only eleven days old, her father being at the
same time absent from home, in Missou[-]i at-
tending to business pertaining to the Church.
She removed with the rest of the family to
Missouri in 1838, and was driven with the
Saints back to Illinois early in 1840; shared in
the exodus from Nauvoo in the dead o[-] winter,
in 1846, and removed to Winter Q[-]arters.
She came to Utah in 1848.
	In Jan. 1854, she was married to Charles G.
Griffin; and removed to St. Thomas, on the
Muddy in its first settlement, where she endur-
ed much suffering from privations and hard-
ships, which she bore with great fortitude and
patience. On their return from that place the
family settled first in Coalville, from thence
they removed to Ogden City in 1873. She was


the mother of ten children, five had passed
away before, ready to meet her when her hap-
py spirit took its flight to the regions of the
blessed. As a wife she was kind and affection-
ate, a helpmeet in the fullest sense of the word;
as a mother, indulgent almost to the extreme;
as a friend she was true as steel. She was
truly benevolent beyond her means, often im-
parting to others that which she really needed
for herself, not wishing to let her left hand
know what her right hand did. She was natu-
rally of a retiring habit, humble and unassum-
ing so that but very few knew her real worth;
"those who knew her best, loved her most."
	We would not recall her to a suffering world
like this, but let her enjoy the society of her
mother, her martyred father and uncle, with
an almost incalculaole number of loving and
beloved frinds. Truly the memory of the just
is blessed.
								COM.

	DIED, in Santaquin, Nov. 18, 1876; Eva,
eldest daughter of Peter N., and Charity An-
derson, aged seventeen years, six months and
twenty days. She was an active memder of
the Young Ladies Retrenchment Association,
and held the office of treasurer until her de-
mise. She was obedient and dutiful to her par-
ents, lived her religion truly and consistently,
and was beloved by all who knew her and her
absence is great[-]y felt by all her friends.
						HANSINA SANBURG.

	DIED, at Clifton, Oneida Co., Idaho, Nov.
29th, 1878, after a lingering illness of liver
complaint, Jason Elihu Howell, aged 39 years,
2 months and 28 days. Deceased was a faith-
ful member of the Church of Jesus Christ of
Lotter-day Saints and well respected and be-
loved by all who knew him. He leaves a large
family and circle of friends to monrn his loss.

[Column 1]
			PRACTICAL CHARITY.

		"In faith and hope the world will disagree,
		But all mankind's concerned in charity."

										POPE.

	There are persons who cannot reconcile prac-
tical and helpful charity with the ideas they
entertain of unselfishness and disinterested-
ness. Charity they believe to be exclusively
an emanation of the heart independent of the
head. "Let not thy left hand know what thy
right hand doeth" is often quoted in condem-
nation of practical organizations for charitable
purposes. I do not propose to bring forward
or expound Scripture to illustrate the opinions
or the ground I have taken, but I honestly be-
lieve the Savior's words in their truest sense,
would confirm my sentiments upon this sub-
ject. Charity loses none of its sweetness or
grace by being wisely directed, and how can
we be entirely and judiciously wise in bestow-
ing charity without thorough knowledge of the
circumstances of any given case unless we as-
certain facts in relation to it. Thus you may
perceive the necessity of investigation in all
matters which require action of the nature of
charity; and the process of investigating and
rendering assistance should certainly be sub-
mitted to an authorized committee, and the de-
cision should be free from prejudice, as it must
invariably be a strong point, and perhaps one
involving reputation, unredeemable faults, and
in many instances entailing misery and woe,
if not the loss of life itself. True charity is
compatible with all the gentleness of the sym-
pathetic heart, and the ablest executive ability
of the head, the combined strength of the head
and heart are es[-]ential to the most complete

[Column 2]
system of self-helpfulness which is one of the
leading characteristics of the charity which
loves its neighbor as itself.
	There should be of course, some living links
that connect the people with such associations
as are formed for the amelioration of the con-
dition of those who are subjects for charity in
any of the ordinary significations of the term;
and these persons should familiarize them-
selves with details in whatever manner pos-
sible; if it were in the power of those who
lead out in these enterprises to give to those
who visit, some special training for their work,
such instruction would be most serviceable;
but here we might say with some considerable
degree of consistency, how can these persons
holding positions, teach that which they them-
selves are not versed in? One qualification all
visitors of this kind should possess, in a large
degree is tact, want of it will make him or her
more offensive than the want of help would
be unpleasant; one admirable qualification of
individual life is adaptability. Enter into the
subjects which those understand, with whom
you have to deal in these grave matters, let
them feel you can be interested in those things
which they estimate as their best and truest
helps in life.
	I wonder if those who give these subjects
time, thought and means, consider how much
real and permanent good might be effected by
the influence of the casual association between
the helper and the helped. There are many
young ladies who languish and pine of "ennui"
who might thus render good service to their
fellow-beings; and their very gentleness and
refinement, even their gaiety and beauty com-
ing with true grace and respectful demeanor,
into the humble dwellings of the less fortu-
nate, would be like the genial rays of sun-
shine after the clouds had darkened the sky, or
the soft rain or refreshing dew to the parched
earth. Smooth the pillow of the sick with
your soft, loving hands; read to the aged, or to
the working woman while she knits or sews;
talk to them as if you understood and recog-
nized their temporal as well as their spiritual
needs. Show them that you have a heart that
can feel for them, and if you will do some of
these things you will be laying up treasures
in heaven, and doing similar works, in a hum-
ble capacity to those of the beloved Savior.
Give a little of your leisure, a little of your
strength, a few cheering words and smiles, a
tender and wise sympathy for trouble or sor-
row, and it will be a real blessing to the receiver.
There are many who need these things who do
not want your money or your gifts, they want
sympathy, they want love, they want knowl-
edge how to live, and resolution to put good
and sensible ideas into every day practice.
The Relief Societies are organized on a thor-
ough and true system, but the minor points
are left to the genius and tact of those who
minister in these matters. It is thought to be
an inferior position to be a teacher (or visitor)
but if those teachers would fully weigh the
matter they would certainly infer that as they
minister in all humility and wisdom, upon
them is the very foundation resting of the best
development of this holy institution.
								THYRA.
	S. L. City, Jan. 10, 1877.


LOVE is won by love, or not at all. There
is no money or pride that a true heart
knows. Its exchanges are not low equiva-
lents. They are gifts, or they are nothing.
They are unalloyed attachments of love to
love, of heroism to heroism, of enthusiasm
to enthusiasm. Love is a celestial attrac-
tion of souls.


THE OLD AGE OF SAMUEL ROGERS.

	Lady Morgan once told this story, which
has just been put in print: That when
Samuel Rogers was past ninety, and in an
almost unconscious state, she called at his
house with Miss——, to whom he had of-
fered marriage, in order to inquire after his
health, and leave cards. "His carriage was
at the door, and he was about to be lifted
into it for an airing. His faithful valet sug-
gested that we should accompany him on
his ride, adding that he would surely recog-
nize me, and that he would take it kindly
of me. Miss——objected slightly at first,
but we both got into the carriage; after a
time I took the old man by the hand, and
said, 'You don't know me, Mr. Rogers, do
you?' He looked at me with lack-luster
eyes for a while, but gradually a little gleam
of intelligence appeared in them, and he
said very slowly, 'Yes, I think I know you,
but I'm not quite sure. Is it Lady Mor-
gan?' I told him he was right. 'Ah, Lady
Morgan,' he replied, 'it is very kind of you
to come and see me.' He then relapsed
into unconsciousness, and so remained for
about ten minutes, when I again took his
hand, and said, 'And as you know me Mr.
Rogers, perhaps you know this lady also?'
Miss——sat on the seat opposite, and Mr.
Rogers, who had not previously observed
her, looked at her attentively; and after an
effort, as if he were recalling some fast dis-
appearing train of thought, said slowly,
'Yes, I know her; she has come to marry
me.' My companion afterward told me
that she felt as if she could have sunk
through the floor of the carraige; but Mr.
Rogers relapsed into utter unconsciousnes,
closed his eyes, and never said anotners
word, or bestowed a look upon either of us,
until we deposited him safely at his door."


FLOWERS.

	To have a respect for ourselves guides
our morals; and to have a deference for
others governs our manners.
	IN the affairs of life activity is to be pre-
ferred to dignity, and practical energy and
dispatch to premeditated composure and
reserve.
	MORALITY without religion is only a kind
of dead reckoning—an endeavor to navigate
a cloudy sea by measuring the distance we
have to run, but without observation of the
heavenly bodies.
	WE are told by those who know, that
there are stars so for distant that it takes
years for their light to reach the earth; if
they should be suddenly extinguished, we
should be unaware of the fact for as many
years as might be required for the last rays
to reach us. We might look up nightly
and see the same old radiance, as if nothing
had happened. It is just so with great and
holy lives. Long after they have gone out
in the darkness of death they continue to
exert an influence, and leave behind them a
beauty and power which make it impossible
to conceive of them as extinguished.


"How many genders are there?" asked a
school-master.—"Three, sir," promptly re-
plied a little blue-eyed girl; "masculine
feminine and neuter." Pray give me an
example of each," said the master.—"Why
you are masculine, because yon are a man,
and I am feminine because I am a girl."
"Very well; proceed."—"I don't know,"
said the little girl: "but I fancy Mr. Jenkins
is neuter, as he is an old bachelor."

[Column 1]
	NOTES OF CORRESPONDENCE.

	STRASBURG, LANCASTER CO., PA.
					DEC. 25, 1876.
MY DEAR AUNT EM:

	It is evening, Christmas 1876 will soon
be counted in the past—transformed by
time from an anticipation into a memory.
What kind of a Christmas have you had?
In the midst of friends and at home, an ex-
treme contrast to mine.
	My impression, heretofore of Pennsylva-
nia, has been taken from Luzerne Co., but
I am happy to say Lancaster is of a some-
what different character; there we were in
the midst of coal mines, iron works, pov-
erty and ruffianism; here among the farm-
ers and quakers in a delightful little town
eight miles from Lancaster city, the coun-
try is rolling, small hills, but no mountains,
and woods crown every summit nearly,
while rich farms lie between. The pros-
pect for converts is not any more encourag-
ing, though we will make a start at the
Town Hall on Sunday. Have made the ac-
quaintance of one prominent gentleman,
and Bro. Musser has nearly—well if not
converted, at least made an impression—or
as we term it, "wounded" him.
	The condition of the religious world is
deplorable in the extreme. All is mystery
and darkness to them—the devout portion
don't know what they worship, while the
others are lost in an apathy, a lethargy and
indifference which is alarming. It reminds
me of my own case while at home. How
careless I was in regard to my salvation—I
tell you Aunt Em, there is a reformation
needed among the boys and girls who
claim fellowship with God's people, I shud-
der to think of it. How many of us are
prepared for that great "day of the Lord
which cometh like a thief in the night,"
How many will be caught unawares and
lost because they would not reach out their
hands for the prize when it was near their
grasp. We must not think that the word
"Mormon" or the professing of Latter-day
Saint is going to save us. We will all be
judged by the deeds done here, and each
individual is responsible for himself. I
would to heaven I could impress the impor-
tance of this upon my dear friends and fel-
low associates at home—but no, they would
say "oh, he's got religious" all missionaries
talk that way, he'll change," etc. Yes,
this is partly so, but what makes all miss-
ionaries talk and think so? It is because
they live nearer to God, and are compelled
to rely on Him; they see what it really is
to be a saint, while the rest are blind, and
if they (missionaries) ever change after, as
is predicted, it is because they are again in
darkness, a true saint should always feel the
same whether at home or abroad, and be
ever ready to sacrifice anything and every-
thing for the faith.
	I don't care how much I am ridiculed and
sneered at, if I know I am on the Lord's
side, He holds the destinies of all men in
the hollow of His hand and could crush
them to atoms if He chose. I hope I never
will be as I have been again, in some things.
My ambition is to be a follower of Christ,
and to be a good and useful citizen in the
kingdom of God. It was a hard struggle
between my pride and duty—duty tri-
umphed for once at least. I have seen
enough of the world to become disgusted
with its corruption. It is ripe in wicked-
ness and were it not for the indifference of
His chosen people God would hurl his ven-
geance now—the final scene depends on
two conditions—that of the saints and that

[Column 2]
of the sinners. The latter are wicked
enough to be destroyed, but the former are
not good enough to be saved. How much
longer will we tempt God? How cool and
lukewarm we are! Many of us will be
caught as were the antedeluvians and the
Sodomites—not believing that the end is at
hand—But as God lives, it will come, and
then the woe, misery and remorse; "if I
only had" and "it might have been," will
be the echo of the unprepared, and it will
be a fearful scene. No one can realize but
under actual experience what the "beau
monde" is; I had an idea that all was splen-
dor outside, I thought to work my way up
the ladder of life and become famous and
wealthy, become a favorite everywhere,
and lead a life of gaiety and pleasure—a
change has "come o'er the spirit of my
dream"—It's all hollow, hollow, hollow!
"The kingdom of God, or nothing" shall
be my motto. I want to remain with the
Lord's people and am willing to make any
sacrifice for them and the great Latter-day
Cause.
	My friends will laugh at me perhaps, but
I am used to worse than that already, and
it will have no effect; oh, how I wish they
could all see these things. I have made
light of such myself, but I was wrong.
Paul was wrong when he persecuted the
apostles, and he became one himself after-
ward. It is never beneath any one to re-
pent of a fault.
	It is pride that does it all, that "never
failing vice of fools." The "Mormons"
like the true saints in all ages, are unpopu-
lar—it is not fashionable to serve God—
thats the whole thing condensed.
	I hope God will ever keep me faithful. I
can conceive nothing more terrible than the
doom of the apostate. "It had been better
ye had never received the faith, than to
turn from the holy commandments once
given" as Peter says in such effect—I don't
quote verbatim. This world is but a pre-
face to the great book of eternity. We
must lay up our treasures in heaven, where
moths do not corrupt nor thieves break
through and steal." How many of us will
meet each other there?
	I am too sleepy to write any more to-
night—Good night till morning.

						Dec. 26th.

	Aurora ushers in another day: a cloudy
one too, like the heads of many who awoke
this morning after the effects of Christmas
imbibations. This is an apt simile but not
a pleasant one, therefore a truce to the sub-
ject.
	What the programme for the day is, I do
not know yet. I suppose we will call on
Bro. Musser's relatives. One of them is a
Memnonite—this sect have for a basis the
command of Christ "if they smite ye on
one cheek turn the other, etc. If attacked
they would not feel justified in defending
themselves, but when Bro. Muss[-]r asked
this gentleman if he would su[-]mit to the
assassin or robber without any effort, he
said I don't think I would. What good
does it do to have a theory, and practice in
direct contradiction to it. They are all as
blind as bats. They take one principle of
the primitive gospel as a nucleus and sur-
round it and their votaries in a cloak of
mystery and supposition, hope and doubt.
And yet they boast of the spread of Chris-
tianity. There is a cord around the huge
neck of every so-called Christian denomina-
tion and Satan has a firm hold on the other
end and is leading them through mystery
further from the light of truth, deeper in-
to the darkness of perdition. The time

[Column 3]
foretold by Isaiah, Nephi, and other pro-
phets has come, the fulfillment is literal, and
it is a marvel that people who pretend to
believe in the Scriptures can not see it.
"Darkness shall cover the earth, and gross
darkness the minds of the people." From
the xiv to xiviii chapter of Revelations is a
subject, or reading matter, which all should
look over. On the 104th page, Chapt. 12,
Second, Nephi, Book of Mormon, is depict-
ed the exact state of the religious world of
to-day, in a prophecy uttered by Nephi to
his people. "For it shall come to pass in
that day that the churches which are built
up, and not unto the Lord, when the one
shall say unto the other, Behold! I am the
Lords; and the other will say, I am the
Lords. And they shall contend one with
another, and their priests shall contend one
with another, and they shall teach with
their learning, and deny the Holy Ghost,
which giveth utterance." etc etc.
	When I think of these things my heart is
filled with unspeakable gratitude to the
great good God who has granted me the
privilege of living in this age, and being
on the right road. The time is close at
hand when Zion will be the only safe place
where in to stand. Babylon is doomed and
all who leave and go out into her except on
God's errands will be buried with her in
her great fall.
	The curse of the Almighty is upon her
and is only withheld for a season. "Come
out of her my people, that ye be not par-
takers of her sins, and that ye receive not
of her plagues. For her sins have reached
unto heaven, and God hath remembered
iniquities." She must be thrown down
with violence even as a millstone cast into
the sea; this is why I have given up the
idea of leaving my home to become an in-
mate of the outside world. "Come out of
her" is the command, and I will not risk
my future salvation for the chance of a
name in the world.
	I may never return as it is,—No one can
tell how soon the cloud will burst and del-
uge all both saint and sinner. God has
said, though, "he who loseth his life for
my sake shall find it," and if it be His will
I am satisfiid. We must sacrifice every-
thing for Him who gave it all, and he who
holds back any let him beware and remem-
ber the fate of Annanias and Saphira.
	The prospect of another war is imminent.
It will not be as before, two great sides bat-
tling against one another, but it will be an
internecine conflict of family against fam-
ily, city against city, state against state.
The condition of the time shows plainly
how easy it would be for the Almighty to
fulfill all He has said. A crisis is impend-
ing. The world thinks it the result of nat-
ural events, and so it is, God works in that
way, He permits men to destroy them-
selves, and they are too blind to perceive
that they are fulfilling predictions to the
letter.
	As already said, the wicked are ready,
but the good are not, we must all reform.
Jesus says "Except ye be as this little
child, ye can not enter the kingdom of
heaven," how many of us are as meek and
pure as little children? Are we willing to
obey, are our minds as untainted as a little
child's? Without God's help it is nearly
impossible, but if we do our best God will
help us; He knows our weaknesses better
than we do and is ever ready to forgive.
	That we may ever bear these things in
mind and be prepared for the coming of
Jesus, is my prayer.
			Yours Affectionately,
							O. F. W.

[Column 1]
			AN ALLEGORY.

	In traveling through Hindostan some
years ago, the writer accidentally came
across a curiosity of which very little ap-
pears to be known. Having occasion to
remain for a few days at a village which
lay a short distance from a large body of
water—perhaps of sufficient extent to enti-
tle it to be called a sea—my attention was
directed to the ruins of what had once been
a grand and beautiful temple, an object, as
I was informed, that would well repay a
visit. This temple was situated immedi-
ately on the border of the sea, and from a
variety of marks it was evident that its
destruction was greatly owing to the re-
peated and continued beating of the waves
against the structure. The remains of pil-
lars of stone, placed at regular intervals,
particularly attracted the attention. In-
scriptions, that had originally been cut very
deep, were still visible on many of these
pillars, and on one the word "Hope" was as
plain as though it had been chiseled yester-
day; on another, but not so clear, the word
"Mercy" could be seen; and in fact each pil-
lar bore an inscription of some meaning.
The hand of Time, however, had pressed so
heavily on some as to render them very in-
distinct, while the waves had worn others
so much that to decipher them was impossi-
ble.
	While gazing on these ruins, and wonder-
ing what motives could have actuated the
erection of this temple, I saw a rather
strange looking person approaching the spot
where I stood When he had arrived with-
in speaking distance, I asked him if there
was not a history connected with this tem-
ple, upon which he related the following
story, which I believe contains a moral of
worth. Here is the story as he spoke it:
	"Long ages ago a great and good being
called God, caused this temple, the ruins of
which you now behold, to be erected, and
named it the 'Temple of the Soul.' The
dome of the structure, on which the name
of the temple was inscribed, was support-
ed by a number of pillars, which were
placed so close together as to render them
impenetrable. On each of these pillars was
cut, or engraven, the name of some attri-
bute, among which were Truth, Friendship,
Hope, Purity, Love, Faith, Conscientious-
ness, Pity, Reason, Charity, Wisdom, Be-
nevolence, and others, many of which have
been forgotten in the transmission of the
history from generation to generation. The
names lost could never be recovered, be-
cause many of them were effaced, while
some of the pillars have gradually mould-
ered away until there are now left only a
letter or two, and others are entirely de-
stroyed. This temple God left in the charge
of his children, to whom he gave pointed
and unmistakable instructions that it should
be carefully preserved.
	It was erected so close to this sea, which
is called the 'Sea of Evil Influence,' that
even in the fairest weather the water laves
the sand within a few rods of the temple;
so that, whenever the tide ran up the beach
an unusual distance, or a storm arose, the
waves dashed furiously against the edifice.
Had the children of God paid that respect
which was due to His instructions, they
could have prevented the encroachments
of the 'Seas of Evil Influence' upon the
'Temple of the Soul;' but they deluded
themselves into the belief that, as the close-
ness of the pillars rendered the temple im-
pregnable, the building was perfectly se-
cure, forgetting that with every dash of the
waves against the temple, the structure was
weakened.

[Column 2]
	"You see," said he, "that stump which
extends further towards the sea than the
rest? That pillar was called 'Truth,' but as
time wore on, and being more exposed to
the water than the other, the letters are en-
tirely erased, nay, there is hardly any of
the pillar left.
	When the children of God discovered
that unless some action was taken the Sea
of Evil Influence would ultimately ruin the
'Temple of the Soul,' they determined that
they would endeavor to avert the probable
danger. But as they consoled themselves
with the thought that no great damage had
yet been done, and as there appeared to be
no pressing necessity for immediate action,
they concerned themselves very little about
the matter. Meantime the Pillar of Truth
was slowly but surely succumbing to the
continued action of the waves, till finally a
portion of it was washed away. The charm
was lost. That which had been an impene-
trable barrier was now broken and the wa-
ters of the Sea of Evil Influence rushed tu-
multuously into she Temple of the Soul, to
which all the pillars were now exposed.
The next pillar that fell was that of Faith,
which was immediately at the left of the
pillar of Truth, and on which still remains
the faint outlines of the letters f and a.
The work of destruction was now thorough-
ly begun, and all the pillars of the temple
were at the mercy of the waves. After
the pillar of Faith had fallen the next that
gave way was Reason; then Purity, and
Love, Charity, Conscientiousnecs, Mercy,
Wisdom, and lastly Friendship followed;
and now the only pillar remaining uninjured
is that of Hope. For ages that pillar alone
has withstood the action of the waters to
which it was exposed, nor, strange as it
may seem, does it bear any visible marks
either of the hand of Time or the influence
of the sea, while the word Hope is still as
clear as it was originally. Thus, through
disregarding the commandments God gave
his children, the result is the ruin of the
magnificent 'Temple of The Soul.'"
	The stranger then left me to meditate on
the history he had just related, and draw a
moral therefrom.
									S.


[Column 1]
			WOMEN IN ITALY.

	Italy first, towards the close of the Mid
dle Ages, permitted to Woman the free ex-
ercise of distinguished talents. Olympia
Morata lectured publicly in Ferrara, the
universities of Padua and Bologna gave
chairs to female professors, and the study
of medicine, the higher mathematics, and
classic literature was open to the sex.
Shakespeare's "Portia" is less than a full
type of the accomplished Italian woman of
that day. At this moment, three or four
hundred years later, Germany, France, and
even England are not m[-]ch in advance of
the public sentiment which then accepted
such a recognition of Woman's capacity:
while no modern university has ventured
to go so far. Already, in the present gen-
eral development of Italy, the women are
preparing to claim their ancient privileges.
A new intellectual activity is stirring the
sex; not only in literature and art, also in
politics and sociology, we are beginning to
hear softer voice. The popular sympathy
with the martyred Bulgarians, which ex-
pressed itself through immense mass meet-
ings in Rome and Naples, no less than in
London and Glasgow, has given the Italian
women an opportunity which some of
them have instantly seized. It is true, in-
deed, that the Italian poetess, Signora Fau-

[Column 2]
Fusinato, has frequently made brief ad-
dresses before societies; but we cannot re-
call any previous instance of a woman ad-
dressing a great assemblage of the people.
At the meeting held at Rome in the Apollo
Theatre, on the 3d ult., the Countess Chioc-
ci appeared upon the platform, and present-
ed a written address, which she requested
to be published with the proceeding of the
meeting. It was accepted, and the address
would have been willingly heard by the au-
dience, had the Countess not lost her cour-
rge.
	A week afterward, an immense multi-
tude assembled in the atrium of the monas-
tery of Santa Maria la Nova, In Naples.
Thousands upon thousands of all classes of
people were present. The third speaker
was Miss Matilda Caselli, who displayed
such remarkable spirit and eloquence,
rousing the vast audience to such uncon-
trollable enthusiasm, that the remaining
orations in the programme were instantly
suppressed, and the resolutions adopted by
acclamation. From the report of her
speech we find that Miss Caselli was no less
daring than eloquent. In Naples, the so
long Bourbonized Naples, she cried to the
new people which is rising out of the for-
mer lazzaroni: "The Cross, the emblem of
humanity, can have no brotherhood with
the Crescent, the emblem of barbarism!
We repudiate the Jesuit policy which now
favors the Crescent, as we reject his author-
ity who usurps the name of Vicar of
Christ!" To appreciate the force of this ut-
terance, it must be remembered that the
policy which emanates from the Vatican
has been hitherto unfriendly [-]o the Orient-
al Christians, and therefore, so far as it
may affect diplomacy. favorable to the
Turkish side of the question. But the free-
dom of speech of Miss Caselli is hardly
more remarkable than the readiness with
which it was accorded to her, and the man-
ner in which it was received by the au-
dience. There are still many turbid ingre-
dients in the life of Italy, and if Italian
women are as wise and patient as they are
able and eloquent, they may render noble
service to their country.—N. Y. Tribune.


THOSE WHO suffer from sleeplessness—and
the number is not small—might do well to
notice their position at night. A German
physiologist states that the heating of the
earth by the sun causes magnetic currents
from the equator to each pole. We should
lie, therefore, in the direction of these cur-
rents; if we lie across these, the result is
unpleasant, and want of sleep is the conse-
quence. The head to the north is the best
position—to the west the worst. Physicians
who have charge of hospitals all attend to
the directions in which the beds are placed,
and with the best results.


"IT will do!"—"It will do!" is the com-
mon phrase of those who neglect little
things. "It will do!" has blighted many a
character, blasted many a fortune, sunk
many a ship, burned down many a house,
and ruined thousands of hopeful projects
for human good. It always means stopping
short of the right thing. It is a makeshift.
It is a failure and defeat. Not what "will
do," but what is the best possible thing to
do, is the point to be aimed at. "Let the
man once adopt the maxim of "It will do!"
and he is given over to the enemy; he is
on the side of incompetency and defeat;
and we give him up as a hopeless subject."
—Smiles.

PEN AND SCISSORS.

	"I DON'T believe in fashionable church-
es," said a lady, recently; "but after all,
considering that we can all go to the same
heaven, perhaps it is better to keep up the
social distinctions as long as we can."
	A DARKEY'S EXPLANATION OF TELE-
GRAPHING.—"Now you see Sam, s'pose
da was a dog, and dat dog's head was in
Hoboken, and his tail in Brooklyn." "Go
'way, da ain't no such dog." "Well, s'pose
there was." "Well s'pose da was." "Well,
den, de telegram is jest like dat dog. If I
pinch dat dog's tail in Brooklyn, what he
do?" "Dunno." "Why, if I pinch dat
dog's tail in Brooklyn, he go bark in Hobo-
ken. Dat's the science of it."—Harper's
Drawer.
	"You voted for Tilden?" queried a Bos-
tonian, andressing a New Yorker. "Well,
yes, all the fellers of our club voted for
him," replied Fifth Avenue. "Well, what
kind of a man is he, any way?" asked Bos-
ton. "Well," said F. A., wiping a speck of
dust from his eye-glass, "I don't know
much about him. One feller said the other
night that Tilden might do for President,
but he shouldn't want to have him own
fifty-one per cent of the stock of a corpora-
tion of which he and his friends held the
other forty-nine per cent. I don't think it
is complimentary—do you?"—Boston Jour-
nal.
	"IN what way is your husband profli-
gate?" asked a Californian judge. "What
do you mean?" the woman asked. "In
what does his profligacy consist? what
does he do when he is profligate?" "Oh, I un-
derstand. Well he's profligate with horses."
The judge's countenance underwent a
variety of expressions, in which astonish-
ment and perplexity predominated, as he
put the next interrogatory: "Profligate
with horses! What—what do you mean by
that?" "Well he went not long ago to
haul twenty dozen of eggs two miles to sell
them, and he hadn't got more than half
way when the horses shied, tipped the wag-
on, and broke thirteen dozen, and eggs
fifty cents a dozen at that. If that ain't
being profligate with horses, then I don't
know what is."
	A SHARP Yankee went into a country
store down East, and thus accosted the pro-
prietor; "Squire, do you trade?" "Consid-
erable," was the reply. "I mean do you
dicker?" "Some; what ye got fer dicker?"
"Guess I'd like a darnin' needle" The re-
quired darning needle was dickered for the
egg, and the Yankee was going away when
he turned and said, "Squire do you treat?"
"Well, I don't mind if I do," replied the
good-natured store-keeper. They repaired
to an adjacent tavern, and the usual bour-
bon was produced. "Hold on," cried the
Yankee, "my chist's weak, and I never
take whiskey without an egg in it." The
generous shopkeeper handed him the dick-
ered egg, but without asking him for his
needle again. The Yankee broke the shell
on the edge of the glass, when he ex-
claimed. "Geewillikins! this egg's got two
yolks! Guess you'd better give me anoth-
er darnin needle."

WOMAN'S EXPONENT.
VOL. 5		SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, FEBRUARY 1, 1877.		No. 17.


PREEXISTENCE.

	Beloved friends! I knew ye well:
	Our spirits did together dwell
	In regions where all pure and bright
	Immortal beings live in light.
	We saw the nations joys and tears,
	Upon this earth thousands of years;
	And yet with full consent we come
	To earth and left a happier home,
	That we in trembling, love and fear,
	Might work out our salvation here;
	Might share in sorrows, grief and woe;
	And all the contrast fully know.
	And thus receive wisdom to gain
	Eternal lives, and power to reign
	With Jesus, in that blessed day,
	When sin from earth is done away.
	We knew the TRUTH must be brought forth
	In Latter-days upon the earth,
	And that we might this glory share,
	And with the noblest ones prepare,
	Temples and towers, wherein might shine
	The glorious rays of light divine,
	When God His purpose should fulfill,
	Subjecting all things to His will.
	We in the Gospel light rejoice
	And listen to the "still small voice,"
	That we a crown of life may win
	Regenerated from all sin.
								S. E. PRATT.
S. L. CITY, Sept. 19, 1876.


[Column 1]
						For the EXPONENT.
			BACKBITING.

	Of all pernicious habits to which the
weakness of the human race permits us to
become addicted, there is none more prev-
alent, contemptible, treacherous and crim-
inal than what is familiarly known as
"backbiting." Prevalent, because it is
practiced unknowingly; contemptible, be-
cause it is ever linked with hypocrisy;
treacherous because it not only calumniates
its victims but debases its votaries; and
criminal because it violates a direct com-
mand of God. It is double dealing and dis-
honorable; for, in addition to causing the
contempt of our associates, it forces us to
despise ourselves. Like a two-edged sword
in the hands of a child, or a subtle poison,
envenoming all it touches. It lives by
associating with its sister Gossip, and
alighting on a half-ruined character and
mangling it when it thinks no danger is
near; like a vulture too cowardly to attack
its prey openly, when some other hand has
struck the fatal blow, greedily pounces up-
on the dead and dying; how many of us
there are who yield to and practice a form
of this vice, and yet do not realize that we
do so!
	We are sitting in our room, alone, when
a friend drops in to chat a few minutes; the
conversation flags for a moment, when either
one or the other happens to remember what
such a one said about so and so, or what
public opinion is respecting Mr. A.; again
it is confided to us, in the st.ictest secrecy,
that Miss B. really did this and that, or
that it is a great pity, a matter of regret
that Mr C. is of such a nature or character,
etc.; and ere we are aware, we find our-
selves exchanging critical opinions and
commentaries, and dealing out this social
scandal as though it were an incumbent
duty.
	Everywhere it is the same—at home,
abroad, on the street or in the parlor and

[Column 2]
ballroom—we feel that we connot be enter-
taining; and making this end justify the
means, adopt this careless and ruinous mode
of accomplishing it. If we knew the con-
tempt this habit inspires in the minds of
our listeners—even though they practice it
—it might influence us to suppress it. Let
us ask ourselves if we are pleased to hear
others abused? No, we are not. It is in
opposition to Christian philanthropy; and
what assurance have we to the contrary,
that it is only our presence which secures
us from similar treatment? None whatever,
the scandal-monger and backbiter are com-
mon enemies to mankind.
	These, however, are generally of the
more innocent kind, that draw on the re-
source for conversational material. There
is another class, though it is hard to draw
the line of demarcation—actuated by envy
and personal malice, and use such unfair,
mean ways to avenge their wrongs and pro-
mote their private interests. These are
slanderers. It is difficult to determine,
though, which of the two kinds produce
the worst results, but we are led to believe,
strange as it may appear to some, that the
first may lay claim to the distinction; for,
on the same principle that a false friend
may work more injury than the bitterest
enemy, so can the apparently friendly,
semi-condoling gossip create more trouble
than the professional slanderer. Why?
Because we will listen to and heed the one,
while we always distrust the other.
	But every word has its weight, and
though a good character is the best proof
against abuse, yet even as continual drops
of water wear away the stone, so if there
be a pregnable or half-weakened spot in an
otherwise good name, evil words, canker-
like, gnaw their way through. But, asks,
one, will not truth arrest itself? Yes, if
we will allow it a fair hearing, but alas! we
often deny it the privilege of a trial, too
readily trample it into the dust; and though
"truth crushed to the earth will rise again"
yet it is frequently discouraged by relent-
less persecution, and falls to rise no more,
until after-history vindicates the rights con-
temporaries denied.
	If we would but stop a moment and cal-
culate the result of every disparaging word
we feel tempted to utter, few of us could
be induced to proceed. If we knew the
unhappiness and misery that flies through
the world on the wings of slander, we
would pause at length before adding our
quota to the general curse; for as hesitation
loses much, so does it gain much, and the
still small voice of conscience is an arbiter
which is ever ready to condemn a wicked
deed, or applaud a good one, if we will al-
low it to assume its office as the dictator of
our actions. Professional slanderers, thank
heaven, are the exception and not the rule,
but how careful we ought to be that we do
not progress in the downward path until
we swell their numbers and merit their
punishment—ostracism from good society
and the favor of onr God. The only way
to escape is to repudiate all tendencies in
that direction, to discountenance by exam-
ple and precept, the vicious propensity of
"backbiting." By doing this we follow
the teachings of our Savior "speak evil of

[Column 3]
no one." We are all frail creatures and
continually liable to go astray. We ask
God to forgive our weaknesses; should we
not, then, forgive the shortcomings of our
fellow men? and unless we do, is it not
hollow mockery to pray? The apostle Paul
says: "Though I speak with the tongue of
an angel, though I have faith to remove
mountains and understand all myster-
ies and all knowledge, and have not char-
ity, I am nothing." Let us then be charit-
able and forgiving, and when a fellow crea-
ture says or does wrong, or is not just as
we think he should be, don't tear his name
to pieces, or disparage him by unkind
words, but show him his error and encour-
age him in his efforts to reform. Remem-
ber the words of our Savior. "Judge not
lest ye be judged," and, "let him that is
guiltless cast the first stone."
							REFORM.


CORRESPONDENCE.

			CANTON, FULTON CO., ILL.,
							Jan. 10, 1877.
TO ELIZA R. SNOW.
	Dear sister:—As you are President of the
Relief Society, I deem it proper to report
progress to you, and the good sisters asso-
ciated with you in your labors of love and
charity among the Saints in Zion. Although
charity begins at home with the ladies of
Utah, their power is felt for good all over
the land; not only in words, but by noble
deeds of sacrifice in the establishment of
right. I feel proud of my sisters in Zion,
and am one with them in the glorious work
of assisting to establish righteousness in the
kingdom of God. Although my calling is
to preach the Word of life and salvation
abroad in this dark, benighted nation, dis-
tracted and demoralized by corruption and
misrule, until anarchy has taken the place
of law and order all over the land. The
fact is they have fallen upon the "little
stone in the mountains" and are being brok-
en to pieces. And thus it may become the
Head of the Corner, but that is their busi-
ness. Our labor is to build up the kingdom
of God, and warn the honest in heart to
flee from the impending judgments about to
fall on this nation.
	On the 5th of last March, Junius F.
Wells and myself organized a Branch of the
Church in this place with seven members;
now it numbers fifty souls, all rejoicing in
the truth. On Monday, Dec. 31st, I organ-
ized a Branch of the Relief Society in this
place, to be called the Fulton Co. Branch
Relief Society, with Sister Sarah Williams
President, Elizabeth Emanuel and Emile
Williams Counselors; Emile Williams Sec-
retary, and Ellen Vaughan Treasurer.
	We instructed them to send a report of
their proceedings to the Editor of the EX-
PONENT for publication, as they desire to
be one with their sisters in Zion. They
had a temporary organization and were
awaiting my return to organize them offi-
cially.
	Remember me kindly to your co-workers
in the cause of truth, and believe me as
ever one with you, and all the faithful in
the kingdom of God.
						D. M. STUART.


130		WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
			R. S. REPORTS.

	The General Retrenchment meeting, held
in the Fourteenth Ward Assembly Rooms,
Saturday, Jan. 6th, 1877, was the first of
the year 1877, and the sisters who spoke
were most earnest in their expressions of
renewed interest on the subject of advance-
ment in all good works and home improve-
ments. President Mrs. M. I. Horne pre-
sided, and the instruction and testimony
given through those who spoke were worthy
of the time and institution, and we sin,
cerely trust may be made applicable in the
lives of all who love virtue and righteous-
ness and are seeking after eternal treasures.

	The General Retrenchment meeting held
in the Fourteentn Ward Assembly Rooms
Saturday, Jan. 20, was crowded almost to
overflowing; there was not a vacant seat in
the room. Counselor Zina D. Young pre-
sided over the meeting, (President Mrs.
M. I. Horne being too ill to attend,) and
gave some excellent instruction. Counsel-
ors E. R. Snow, B. W. Smith, and E. How-
ard, Mrs. W. East, Mrs. E. B. Wells, Mrs.
E. Tuft, Mrs. M. E. Watmough and others
all spoke upon those things which are al-
ways inspiring and encouraging to the Lat-
ter-day Saints.

			HARRISVILLE, Jan. 11, 1877.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	Our Relief Society has been re-organized,
and held their first meeting Thursday, the
4th, of this month.
	The former president resigned her office,
and we have started anew with a determi-
nation to do all in our power to be one with
all the other branches of this great and
grand institution, which the Lord has
deemed requisite to establish upon the earth,
to assist in carrying out His wise and holy
purposes, for the re-generation of the hu-
man family. The following officers were
appointed.
	Mrs. Ellen C. Fuller, President.
	"	Elizabeth Laney,	} Counselors.
	"	Ann Newton,	}
	"	Hattie E. Laney, Secretary.
	"	Amanda Daly, Treasurer.

				DRAPER, Jan. 14, 1877.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	We, a band of young sisters, were organ-
ized Nov. 30th, 1875, into a Retrenchment
Association, for the purpose of co-working
together for the building up of the kingdom
of God, which has been established in these
last days "no more to be thrown down or
given to another people." We hold our
meetings once a week, and those of the
members who attend seem to take a deep
interest in the Society, and are willing to
do all they can to make the meetings profit-
able and regular. We have considerable
reading in our meetings from the EXPO-
NENT, the Book of Mormon, and Doctrine
and Covenants, and such other books as are
calculated to benefit us. We realize that
we have been greatly benefitted by meet-
ing together and talking to each other on
the principles pertaining to life and salva-
tion. We should all pay the strictest atten-
tion to our religion and not let the follies
and fashions of Babylon decoy us from our
duties, for we cannot take any of these
with us when we leave this earth, but we
can treasure up in our minds the works of
God, and that will prepare us for each and
every duty that God requires at our hands.
And as this is the commencement of a new

[Column 2]
year, I trust that we will all commence
with it and strive to reform ourselves a lit-
tle more, and try each day that we live to
do a little better than we did the day pre-
vious. And if we do this in humility and
meekness before our Father in heaven we
shall not be left to wander in darkness from
the pathway which will lead at length to
life eternal.
	May each and every one of us strive at
all times to live our religion, that we may
gain that exaltation which we are in pur-
suit of, which will bring us back into the
presence of God, and yield us that happi-
ness and joy which the world cannot give.
				ANNIE BURNHAM,
							Sec'y.

		HYRUM, CACHE CO, Jan. 15, 1877.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	By request of our worthy president, Mrs.
C. Liljenquist, I address a line to you to in-
form you that we are still trying to honor
our calling as a branch of the Rellef Society,
and to do all in our power to assist the
needy, and comfort the afflicted either by
word or deed. We are one with our sisters
in Utah to follow out the counsel of our
honored President with regard to storing
up our wheat. Bro. Ira Allen was kind
enough to proffer us a room in his granary,
a committe was appointed who have already
bought between one hundred and one hun-
dred and fifty bushels of wheat and are still
busy. They are going to visit the brethren
and solicit from them according to counsel.
I am happy to say that our Bisbop and
many of the brethren are willing to assist
us in procuring and storing our grain, and
the sisters as a majority are united in this
important movement. We are also called
upon to donate means towards assisting in
building the Temple; and it is our desire
to be always ready to respond to the differ-
ent calls made by the servants of the Lord.
In conclusion I will say that the EXPO-
NENT is a very welcome visitor. It is ap-
preciated by both old and young, and may
sucess attend the noble women that spend
their time and talents in contributing to
make it interesting and instructive, is the
prayer of your sister in the Gospel,
				NANCY M. NEILSON,
								Sec'y.


[Column 1]
		WOMAN'S VOICE.

	PROVIDENCE, CACHE Co., Jan. 14, 1877.
EDITORS EXPONENT:
	At our regular Relief Society meeting
which is held on the first Thursday of
every month, our worthy Bishop and a few
of the brethren met with us, a good spirit
prevailed, much good instruction was im-
parted to us by the Bishop. He said it was
his desire that we, as sisters, should take
hold and carry out the counsel given by
President Young in regard to storing up
grain.
	The President, Sister Margaret Mathews,
urged upon us the importance of taking
heed to this counsel, and in faith and with
energy do all we can whereby we might
sustain ourselves and our little ones in the
day of famine and distress; she also coun-
seled the sisters to sustain the EXPONENT
by subscribing for it, showing to us the im-
portance, as wives and mothers, to sustain
to the best of our endeavors everything
calculated in its nature to benefit the saints.
We have commenced collecting grain, our
means are very limited, but it is the inten-
tion of the sisters to do all they can. When

[Column 2]
we heard of the arrival of Sister E. R.
Snow in Logan and that it was her inten-
tion to visit this settlement, the sisters here
were expecting a treat of good things by
listening to her instruction, when lo! the
next report that came, was that she had re-
turned to Salt Lake City, and no one could
tell the cause of her sudden departure, and
many conjectures were circulated among
the sisters but the most feasible idea that
we could arrive at was, that the weather
was too cold for her to travel, and that when
it got warmer she, with others, would visit
us.
	We hail with delight the prospect of
having a Temple erected in Cache Co., to
the name of the Lord, whereby we can of-
ficiate for those that have died without the
knowledge of the Gospel.
	The Lord has blessed us with health, we
have not been afflicted with sickness as
some settlements have, and hope and pray
that the Lord in His goodness and mercy
may continue to look over our imperfect-
ness, that our lives may be spared to do good
among the Saints, and while certain individ-
uals that once partook of the good spirit,
but through transgression have denied the
faith and are striving to bring trouble upon
us, we as saints will renew our diligence,
and become more united in our actions and
feelings, so that we may enjoy a greater
portion of the Holy Spirit and become
more perfect; this is the desire of my
heart.
						E. E. M.

			MORONI, Jan. 15, 1877.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	Agreeable to your request I write you our
proceedings relative to the storing of wheat.
We have in our possession between one
and two hundred bushels of wheat, well se-
cured in a good granary; and we still feel
to go ahead and do all we can to gather in
the staff of life. And by so doing we obey
the counsel of our beloved president, Brig-
ham Young, and ever feeling it our duty
and desire to do our part in all things per-
taining to the kingdom of God, standing
firm and unshaken in the cause of truth,
that we may hold out faithful to the end is
my prayer.
						D. P. IRONS.
	P. S. The reason that there are so few
that take the EXPONENT here is that two-
thirds of them are Danish and Swedish and
cannot read the English language.
							D. P. I.


NOTES AND NEWS.

	ELDERS A. M. MUSSER and O. F. Whit-
ney are creating something of a sensation
in Eastern Pennsylvania where they are
seeking to promulgate the principles of the
Gospel as believed in by the Church of Je-
sus Christ of Latter-day Saints. Persecu-
tion followed the Savior and His Apostles,
and those who undertake to proclaim the
same Gospel taught by them will receive
similar persecution. Jesus said "If they
persecute me, will they not also persecute
you?" "The Daily Examiner and Express"
of Jan. 13, published in Lancaster, Penn.,
contains some items concerning these Eld-
ers, and makes reference to the vile reports
which have been circulated concerning El-
der Musser, which they forbear publishing
"as he is a native of Lancaster Co. and very
respectably connected." The same paper
published a letter from Elder Musser giving
the people some ideas of Mormonism, and
statistics of Utah's progress.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.		131


THE GOOD GIRL'S RESOLVE.
			[TUNE, "AUNT SALLY."]

I'll not give heed to vanity, to fashion or to pride
Nor any of those silly ways God's servants have decried,
Appearing ever neat and clean, avoiding all excess,
Empolying my own handiwork in whatsoe'er I dress.

					CHORUS.

	Good girls rally, sustain the cause of truth,
	Remember your Creator in the days of early youth.

I'll never condescend to be a coquette or a flirt,
Allow my skirts when walking out to trail in filth and 
	dirt.
To chastity, and modesty I'll ever be inclined
Avoiding rude loud laughter which denotes the vacant
	mind.

I will not use the powder puff, nor daub my face with
	paint,
And never deign to eat or drink what would my system
	taint;
I never will be found among young men who smoke or
	drink;
Avoiding as associates rude girls who leer and wink.

The cause of God I will sustain with undivided heart,
All evil passions will restrain, from sinful ways depart:
At all times good examples set whate'er my lot may be,
Whene'er my pathway is beset, from all temptation flee
									WM. WILLIS
	Dedicated to the Young Ladies' Retrenchment Socie-
ties, Dec. 13th, 1876;


THE COMMON LOT OF ALL.

	There is no heart but hath its inner anguish,
		There is no eye but hath by tears been wet,
	There is no soul but hath been known to languish
		O'er hours of darkness it can ne'er forget.

	There is no cheek, however bright its roses,
		But faded buds beneath its hue are hid;
	No eye that in its dewy light reposes,
		But broken starbeams tremble 'neath its lid.

	There is no voice with laughter ringing,
		However light and gay its words may be,
	But hath in hours of agony been seeking
		For power to solve its life's deep mystery.

	We are sojourners in this land of dreaming,
		Hands meet, hearts join, and eye to eye replies;
	Nor deem we that below a brow all beaming
		A hidden grief all unrevealed lies.
											E. R.
S. L. City, Jan. 5, 1876.


[Column 1]
		CORRESPONDENCE.

				ST. GEORGE, Jan. 2, 1877.
EDITORS EXPONENT:
	Yesterday the Temple in this city, or a
portion of it, was dedicated. At 10 o'clock
the people began to wend their way to the
Temple block. It was announced on the
day previous, at meeting, that the doors
would be opened at 12 o'clock m. and closed
at a quarter to one. Everybody seemed
anxious to be there too early rather than too
late. Riding down a little before twelve,
one met people going by ones and twos;
wagons loaded with men and women; and
some few carriages occupied by happy-look-
ing people; and on almost every face was
seen  thoughtful but joyous expression, in
keeping with the occasion. The building
was surrounded by a throng of people when
we arrived. Presently the carriage con-
taining the President drove up at the south
side entrance to the basement. Simultane-
ously the several doors were opened, and
the people crowded into the two large rooms
—running north and south—of the base-
ment story. President Young: together
with those of the Twelve Apostles who
were present, and some of the leading
brethren, occupied the font-room. The
large double doors connecting the large halls
with the font room were thrown open to en-
able all to hear. At the time appointed the

[Column 2]
outer doors were closed and the services
commenced by the congregation singing
the hymn, "The Spirit of God like a fire is
burning." Bros. Woodruff and Snow then
gave some instructions to the people, as to
how they should conduct themselves while
the prayer was being offered up. That
every heart should be uplifted to God, and
every head bowed, and every eye closed.
Apostle Woodruff then solemnly ascended
the steps leading to the font, and in an im-
pressive manner appealed to Almighty God
to accept of His people's offering. My own
heart and mind were too interested in the
prayer to notice anything. But nothing
was forgotten in it. The blessing of Heav-
en was sought on all the Relief Societies
throughout the world. Also upon all the
sisters who had contributed in any way to
the building of the House. Of course I
could but remember this, as I was included
among those sisters. At the close of the
prayer, the choir, under the leadership of
Bro. McFarlan, sang a beautiful hymn com-
posed for the occasion by C. L. Walker.
The congregation then proceeded to the
main hall on the next floor. After complete
order was obtained, another hymn was sung
and Bro. Erastus Snow delivered the dedi-
catory prayer for that room and its sur-
roundings. President Young with some of the
leading brethren then ascended to the seal-
ing room to dedicate that. The voices of
the brethren united in a hymn floated down
to us, like the distant strains of a heavenly
choir. After the brethren returned to the
lower room, the people were told that work
was to be commenced in the Temple imme-
diately.
	Then that which the people had so longed
for, and still hoped for, transpired; Presi-
dent Young arose in the power of the Holy
Ghost and addressed the multitude. His
exhortations were powerful and impressive.
The house seemed filled with a heavenly
host, and the President's face fairly shone
with the light of the Holy Ghost. His re-
marks were reported in full and will short-
ly be published.
	To-day he feels much better than could
be expected; seems cheerful and comforta-
ble; was out in his carriage this afternoon.
	It was estimated that there were over
1,200 people present, yesterday, at the
Temple.
	The weather here is simply delightful.
Imagine yourself enjoying an ideal spring.
A deep blue sky, a few white fleecy clouds
a warm, brilliant sun, with rather cold
nights, and you have a picture of the state
of the weather day after day. Not even
rain enough for a little mud. Warm and
pleasant, except at night.
							S. Y. D


[Column 1]
						For the EXPONENT.
		NO SURPLUS WOMEN.

	But though I loved you well I woo'd you not,
	And yet good faith, I wished myself a man;
	Or that we women had men's privilege
				Of speaking first.

									SHAKESPEARE.
	The very fact that God is in all His
works consistent that He never created
something for nothing, is a positive demon-
stration that there are no "surplus women,"
and that according to the first command-
ment given to man, (and we believe just as
binding upon woman) namely to "multiply
and replenish the earth," and from which
law there has never been an appeal, proves
that every man and woman who desires to
obey the laws of God should marry. There
is no doubt as many men as women born

[Column 2]
into the world but nature's laws are pervert-
ed, the means she has provided are not
properly adjusted, because sin has come into
the world, and an antagonism has developed
itself which has to be met and dealt with;
it has created a disturbance and confusion
and disarranged the eternal laws and prin-
ciples which were they carried out in their
perfection would reconcile and harmonize
all animate nature.
	God has given to those whom he created
in His own image, an agency and the power
to use that as an influence for good or evil,
and through the errors and darkness of
"the blind leaders of the blind," instead of
trusting in all humility and meekness upon
God, men have wandered further and fur-
ther from the right and become so wrapt
in traditions that it is almost impossible to
make them comprehend the will of Jeho-
vah. Misconstructed governments which
contain in their codes the elements of dis-
solution, are the causes of the degenerating
consequences which are becoming more
than ever noticeable. A few years back
there was less talk of the great majority of
old maids, and also of divorces. What are
the causes of these results? Philosophers
tell us there is no effect without a cause,
and if, as some writers affirm, there are not
women enough in certain localities for each
man to have a wife, why do not men marry
those who are marriageable; probably the
fact is, that women choose to remain old
maids, rather than marry useless, profigate,
or intemperate men. We still persist in
saying there are more good women than
men and if they cannot marry honorable
husbands they choose to remain single, un-
til they fully comprehend the laws of com-
plete progression, and then they will not
unite themselves wtth wicked men, for of
what avail would it be? They would only
lead them into misery and degradation and
entail upon them an everlasting curse instead
of blessing.
	It is no mysterious dispensation of provi-
dence that has left them "lone roses to pine
on the stem," or unprovided with husbands
to support, protect and comfort them.
	Sir Henry James (we are informed) calls
unmarried women "social failures," by
what name or phrase would he christen
bachelors? They not only fail socially but
morally, physically and spiritually; and on
them instead of woman, no doubt will fall
the sin of not fulfilling the first command-
ment, because woman by tradition, custom
and long-established laws of society, does
not assert a right to choose for herself, one
who shall be her companion and the father
of her children. Woe be unto her if she
dare so outrage the man-made positive laws
of social and domestic life. Such a woman
is made the jest and by-word of her sisters
even, and the scoff and scorn of those who
call themselves "the protectors and support-
ers of the weaker sex." Occasionally you
will hear the remark that woman has as
much right to her choice as man, but she
must not speak out, or show her preference
except by seeking to attract with womanly
grace and sweetness, modestly, quietly and
unobtrusively. In my estimation it is far
more honorable to openly avow one's senti-
ments than to practice all the wiles and arts
of modern or ancient invention for the sake
of procuring the husband of one's choice,
or failing in that, to profane the holy ordi-
nance of marriage before high heaven at
the sacred altar, with one who does not
form with you that perfect bond of love and
confidence which is essential to the ele-
ments which should enter into that new
life which is to be the fruit of your union.

			TO BE CONTINUED.
