SALT LAKE CITY, FEB. 1, 1877.


GOVERNMENTAL POWERS.

	Throughout the world, the wisest and the
greatest of mankind have sought to establish
firm governments, for a safe protection to all
classes of society. People have talked and
written about it, devised laws and statutes,
legislated and presented memorials; spent time,
talents, money and the best energies of life, in
pursuit of such knowledge as would secure the
best and most perfect justice under all the dif-
ferential circumstances which might or could
occur to individuals or peoples in all the phases
of life. In all ages wars have been waged,
nation against nation, to maintain certain
principles of power, lest they should lose any
part of the foundation of strength which up-
held and sustained the dignity and authority
of government. Are mortal men capable of
maintaining a government which will stand
all the hard pressure of general and special con-
tingencies? It would indeed seem otherwise
from the evidence given through long periods
of time, of the breaking up and destruction of
all the various forms of government which
have been most firmly and securely establish-
ed even by the strongest forces which could be
exercised. In consideration of all these inci-
dents and occurrences, in the past history of
the world, the conclusion must inevitably be
drawn, that the government of God is the only
perfect pattern.


[Column 1]
	WINTER EVENING REVERIES.

	"Now stir the fire, and close the shutters fast,
	Let fall the curtain, wheel the sofa round;
	So let us welcome peaceful evening in."
								COWPER.

	In the long winter evenings there are hours
which the great Creator of seasons and times
seems to have allotted to mortals for enjoyment
and self-examination. It is customary at this
season, among all classes of people, to draw
around the hearth, cheerful or dull, and partici-
pate more or less in some simple home pastime
—such as stories of a marvellous nature, or old
legends of fairies, ghosts, hobgoblins, or any
remniscence of reality or fiction that presents
itself first upon the tablet of the mind. Sub-
jects chosen a[-] this time are usually character-
istic of the person or the place, and partake in
a noticeable degree of the atmosphere which
predominates.
	Sitting by a blazing fire of coal, all aglow,
sending forth animation and warmth, one sus-
ceptible of outward impressions feels imbued
with pleasant thoughts and bright pictures or
representations of life; something appropriate
to the cheerful aspect of the room, and the ra-
diant faces lit up by the glow and warmth of
the burning coal, which suggest happy

[Column 2]
thoughts and pleasing ideas on the past, prese[-]t
and future.

		"Ere the evening lamps are lighted,
			And, like phantoms, grim and tall,
		Shadows from the fitful fireside
			Dance upon the parlor wall."

	At such moments, when the busy toil of the
day is over, and one sits down to muse for a
respite, ere the night fairly closes in, with all
its ceremony of silent potency, this verse of
Longfellow's may be most fully realized. Who
has not sat and watched fitful shadows cast
upon the wall by the reflection of the dull fire-
light, and drawn comparisons suited to the con-
dition of mind and the adaptibility of such
shading and outlining to the musings of the
fancies within?
	"We belong to our time," some one has said;
is it because people partake in a large degree
of the influence of surroundings and associa-
tions?
	It is extremely difficult to realize the cold-
ness, apathy and hard selfishness of human
nature taken as a whole, when the heart is full
of generous warmth, and overflowing with
blessings, which, though small perhaps in
themselves, are great, according to the spirit of
appreciation in which they are received.
	The actual busy "work-a-day world," with all
its shifting, noises, sights and struggles, is shut
out from the circle by the fireside, as though it
were a thing of the past. There may be news-
papers telling of some friend that has passed
away, or some one who has failed in business,
or of the general obligations of mankind—but
their interest is transient and fails to fix the
attention but for a few moments only.
	It is pleasant and customary in some homes
to have tea or chocolate with rosy apples or
dough-nuts, to while away the long evenings
which succeed the short days; but we were
upon the subject of reveries, we were not think
ing of newspapers, letters, recreations, tea or
refreshments just now, "a truce to all these,"
(nevertheless "one cannot feed upon air") but
our cogitations sitting here by the glowing:
grate were of old legends, past experiences,
dreamy fancies, that have long since died in
embryo—sentiments choked to death in the
expression, memories of childhood and school-
days, and by-gone anticipations, which are now
perhaps the more pleasant from lack of fulfill-
ment, thinking of these and conjuring up com-
parisons, shadings and imitations to life,
watching the fitful blaze and the shadowy re-
flections find it most delightful amusement for
a few hours.
	Musings like these are not unprofitable, they
lead one to look into the labyrinths of the
mind, and ascertain whether there are still
rich, fertile, uncultivated, or unexplored fields
for life's uses, with fresh bubbling springs of
thought ready to bring into real actual service
the reserved forces, which time and opportuni-
ties may yet develop into special phases for the
good of mankind. These reveries are not only
attractive in themselves, but ofttimes a posi-
tive relief, especially to the over-burdened
mind; the scientific thinker in any and all of the
professions, the pursuits and avocations of daily
labor, and some of these comprise real drudg-
ery, mental or physical, such persons should
allow themselves part at least of these long,
winter evenings to recruit mental and physi-
cal forces. If all vitality is drained the person
becomes stale, dry and unfruitful, then the
special mission assigned them upon the earth
will fail in its complete accomplishment.
	These are hours given more especially for
contemplation and reflection, and charity for
one's self and all the universal brotherhood.
Cherish then in these hours of firelight reve-

[Column 3]
ries "love of our neighbors" and a desire to
recognize the affections which centre in home-
life, and become better acquainted not only
with one's self, but with each others finer and
more lovable attributes. The winter evenings
should contribute to that tenderness, devotion
and regard for each other's happiness, which
are the most pleasing features of home inter-
course. These are times when men and women
may enter with zeal and true appreciation into
those charming little courtesies that endear
and beautify home-life, purifying the best ele-
ments of character, and refining the tone of
every-day association. Were men and women
to devote a little more time and thought to
practising virtues and endearments at home,
there would be less excuse for seeking these at-
tractions and enjoyments abroad.
					[handwritten] E. B. Wells


HOME AFFAIRS.

	A MOST perfect surprise was tendered to Miss
E. R. Snow on Monday, Jan. 22, the day fol-
lowing her birthday. All unexpectedly she
was invited to the residence of Mrs. Bathshe-
ba W. Smith, where she found assembled as
many of the sisters as the house could hold, to
congratulate her on the anniversary of her
natal-day, and wish her many returns of the
same with increased joy, prosperity and happi-
ness. Prest. Daniel H. Wells and Elder Orson
Pratt, sen., were present on the occasion; each
of them, in a happy manner, made some ex-
cellent remarks appropriate to the time and
place. Mrs. E. A. Whitney chanted forth in
praise a "song of Zion." A sumptuous dinner
had been shrewdly and quietly arranged, and
everything went "merry as a marriage bell."
Miss Snow was the recipient of some elegant
and valuable presents from her "dear five hun-
dred friends," but the choicest gift of all is the
love of the people.
	ANOTHER GOOD TIME.—The Fourth Annual
Meeting of the Young Ladies' Association of
the 10th Ward was held Jan. 10, 1877, at the
ward meeting house. There were in attendance
several ladies of the General Retrenchment
Association, Mrs. E. B. Wells of the Woman's
Exponent, the officers of the Ward Relief So-
ciety, and others. After Prest. Mrs. M. J.
Pierce and several of the young girls had made
suitable remarks, Sisters Bathsheba W. Smith,
Margaret Young and Wilmirth East spoke
beautifully on the principles of the Gospel, our
duties as mothers in Israel, daughters of Zion,
and their good instructions and kind counsel
will long be remembered and cherished by
those who heard them. We have had a true
spiritual feast, which will cause many to live
purer and better lives.
	Mrs. E. B. Wells spoke on the Grain Ques-
tion, Woman's Book, and Woman's Exponent.
Never before has it been known where women
have saved grain for a famine; this is a privi-
lege which God has assigned to us. The publi-
cation of the Woman's Book should be pushed
forward by the sisters, for it will preach the
Gospel in many places where the elders would
not be listened to, and will remove much of the
prejudice in regard to the Mormon Women.
The EXPONENT should also be sustained, for it
also has a voice in proclaiming to the world,
the rights and motives of the Women of Utah.
	Other remarks were made, after which we
adjourned until our next regular meeting.
	M. P.


EDITOR EXPONENT:
	In response to my request Sister Jenson of
Brigham City forwarded the following statisti-


cal items of Mantua; not the Italian Mantua,
celebrated as the birthplace of Virgil the poet,
but a small, thriving town, one hour's drive
north from Brigham City, in "Little Valley,"
Box Elder County.			E. R. SNOW.
	"This little town was first settled in the
Spring of 1863, by eight humble and poor fam-
ilies, over whom my husband, H. P. Jensen,
presided as Bishop. They have since increased
mostly from the Old World, of the same hum-
ble class, to the number of two hundred and
eighty-three.
	They now constitute a well-ordered and hap-
py society of Latter-day Saints. They have
good meetings for worship, with a good choir.
The fathers are organized in Quorums of the
Priesthood—the mothers organized in a Relief
Society, work together in love and unity, hav-
ing a capital of $300 (three hundred dollars)—
they are striving to honor every requisition—
looking after the wants of the poor, storing up
grain against a day of famine, and living up to
the spirit of the times. The daughters are or-
ganized in a Young Ladies' Retrenchment As-
sociation—their wearing apparel being as much
as possible, "the workmanship of their own
hands." They make their own hats, artificial
flowers, &c. They meet together once in two
weeks, and have interesting meetings, and, as
daughters, are truly an honor to their parents.
	The sons are organized in a Young Men's
Mutual Improvement Society—have very in-
teresting meetings, and to their credit it may
be said, and truly, not one of those young men
indulge either in strong drink or in the use of
tobacco in any form.
	The sexes of those associations meet together
once a month—the young gentlemen and ladies
presiding alternately. This has proved a great
benefit in their improvement in the develop-
ment of their talents.
	A good day school is taught by Miss C. An-
derson. The Sunday School is well attended.
Dancing parties and other amusements are
strictly controlled according to the published
circular and counsel of the Priesthood, and
neither liquor nor round dancing is ever intro-
duced.
	Here the people have manifested their faith
by their works. In the past year, 1876, they
have donated
	For the Temple......................... $708.20
	Fast-day offering......................   136.35
	For emigrating the poor..........     78.40
		Arizona mission................   238.70
		Brigham City Tabernacle..  300.00
							     ————
			Total............................1461 65
	As this little town lies in the rude moun-
tains, eight hundred feet above Brigham City,
the people have had to contend with many dif-
ficulties in the form of grasshoppers, crickets
and frost: the former and latter were so des-
tructive last season that their wheat crop was
reduced eight bushels per head, but their econ-
omy is admirable and they are not discouraged.
They have a store in the United Order, where
the account shows that in two months' time
they sold butter and eggs to the amount of six
hundred dollars.
	So much for the little tree planted thirteen
years ago, in a cold corner of the Lord's vine-
yard."
								S. J. J.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


MORMON ELDERS IN THE EAST.

	A debate was held on Thursday and Friday
evenings, Dec. 28 and 29, between Elder A. M.
Musser of this city, and the Rev. Dr. Martin
of Ohio, in Massasoit's Hall, Strasburg, Lan-


caster Co., Penn. The following were the
heads discussed:
	1. We believe that the atonement of our Sa-
viour covered only the sins entailed upon man
by Adam's fall, and not the individual, personal
sins of men.
	2. We believe that men must work out their
own salvation from their individual sins by
their good works through faith, repentance,
baptism—by immersion, and for the remission
of sins, and the laying on of hands for the re-
ception of the Holy Ghost.
	3. We believe that the gifts of the Spirit as
enjoyed by the Saints anciently, are absolutely
necessary to the true and proper understanding
and interpretation of the Holy Scriptures,
which is a Divine record, and that the Holy
Scripture is as a sealed book to all who do not
enjoy the Holy Ghost as enjoyed by those who
wrote it.
	4. We believe that the celestial order of Mar-
riage for time and eternity, commonly called
"Polygamy," as taught by us, is strictly a
Scriptural doctrine.
	5. King James' English translation of the
Bible is our standard.
	Signed
		A. MILTON NUSSER,
		ORSON F. WHITNEY,
	Elders of the Church of Jesus Christ of Lat-
ter-day Saints.
	I agree to impugn the above doctrines of
Mormonism, maintaining the doctrines of the
Presbyterian Church, including the immediate
inspiration of the Holy Scriptures as written
in the original language of the inspired pen-
man, and I believe that King James' transla-
tion is generally very good.
	Signed,
		JOHN WYNNE MARTIN.	Each speaker was limited to twenty minutes,
Elder Musser taking the affirmative, and Dr.
Martin having the concluding speech upon
each subject. Each party chose an Umpire
and those two chose a third who acted as mod-
erator.
	Passage after passage of Scripture was quoted;
and the whole evenings were spent in this
manner. Some thought the Rev. Dr. was not
as competent as he should have been, being a
little too nervous, &c. No doubt the questions
were ably dealt with by Elder Musser, but as
the adage goes,
		"Convince a man against his will,
		He's of the same opinion still."
	The world do not want the truth, they want
popularity, position and money.


STORING GRAIN.

	In an article upon storing grain, published
in the Deseret News of Jan. 17, written by
O. B. Shaw, we find some items in regard
to the subject which as we are seriously in-
terested in the matter, we desire to lay be-
fore our readers.
	"There are three great agencies operat-
ing in all nature in the destruction as well
as in the organization of all vegetable
and animal life forms, viz. heat, moisture
and light. To the operation of these subtle
forces is due nearly all the great changes
that recur from season to season. Modify
or control these forces and you have the
key of the whole subject. Construct gran-
aries impenetrable to moisture and unaf-
fected by the heat of the sun or surround-
ing air, cool, dry, and dark, and no in-
sect life can flourish in them, no parasitic
life will grow, and the conditions thus se-


cured, together with the conditions of dry
soil for the land where the various grains
grew and mixed copiously, completes the
natural law or conditions for their preserva-
tion through long periods of time. This
soil mixing among the grain does no injury,
as it all escapes in the milling process.
	Dig deeply into the alluvials of the earth
the world over, and expose the soils from
those depths to heat, moisture, and light,
and in nearly all cases, a vegetation essen-
tially different from that found on the sur-
face springs np.
	Joseph in Egypt, directed the storing of
grain with the dry soils of the lands, thus
showing himself to be well acquainted
with the natural facts before mentioned.
	Granaries constructed of good concrete of
lime and gravel are perhaps as good mate-
rial as any; they must be cool, dry, and
dark, and care be taken that the building
and contents, wheat and soil, are as dry as
possible.


OBITUARY.

	Died, in Ogden City, Jan. 14th, 1877, Mary
W. Brown. She was born in Stockport, Ches-
shire, Eng., 30th of March, 1814. Embraced
the Gospel in 1850, and came to the valley in
1854. In 1855 she was married to Capt. James
Brown of Ogden. She was an exemplary wo-
man. She adopted two motherless children
and bestowed on them the care, anxiety and
love of a fond mother; and during her sickness
the thought of them was always uppermost
and she would frequently say, "What will be-
come of my poor boys?" She was a subscriber
to the EXPONENT, and a member of the Relief
Society. As a faithful Latter-day Saint she
loved her religion and lived it truly and con-
sistently, and has died with a glorious hope
of the resurrection, respected and esteemed by
all who knew her. "Blessed are the dead that
die in the Lord," for they shall come forth in
the morning of the resurrection crowned with
eternal glory."					A. M. F.
	Ogden, Jan. 24th, 1877.


AN INTERESTING discovery of a part of
the old Roman wall has been lately made at
London in the heart of the city. Some
buildings were demolished in Camomile
Street, near its junction with Bishopsgate
Street, and in the excavation some thirty-
six feet of the old wall were revealed, be-
sides a bastion. The wall was fully nine
feet thick; the bastion projects on the north-
ern side, is more than semi-circle, solid; and
about sixteen feet across; the heights va-
ried from two to four feet and a half, and
the whole was about ten feet below the lev-
el of the modern street. The wall had had
faces of roughly wrought stone, with a
double band of the usual bright tiles on thecity side; but on the outside much dark
iron stone had been used. Much Kentisli
rag stone and green stone had also been em-
ployed, and this seems to point to an epoch
for the construction, when the Romans had
a knowledge of the country and ability to
transport materials to considerable distance.

[Column 1]
	WOMAN IN THE CHURCHES.

	Rev. Isaac M. See, of the Wickliffe
Church, who is upon trial for permitting
women to preach from his pulpit, occupied
the entire session of the Newark (New Jer-
sey) Presbytery on Thursday, in making
his defense, speaking thereon for three and
a half hours. The matters contained in
the charge, he said, were such as related
to his relation as a Christian minister and
to the Lord Jesus Christ, the king and head
of all things in the Church. The women
who had preached in his church were dele-
gates to a National Convention which was
being held at Newark, and he had no
thought that any of the delegates proposed
speaking in his chapel until the day pre-
vious to their appearing there. He had
been glad to have them there, for he was
pleased to see that the Lord had opened up
a field for his daughters in a sphere where
men had so signally failed; but in permit-
ting this occupancy of his pulpit he had no
thought of going counter to the wishes of
the Presbytery. It was only after prayer-
ful consideration that this action had been
taken, and it was not until meeting with
Dr. Craven on the succeeding Wednesday
that he had the least intimation that he
had done aught at which any of his breth-
ren could take offense. He believed that
women had as much power in the preach-
ing of the Holy Gospel as he had himself.
His experience in the company of blessed
baptized women had been for the past
twelve years in attendance on what were
called "Tuesday meetings" at the house of
Dr. Palmer, in the city of New York. Mrs.
Palmer he regarded as a woman of God,
baptized of the Holy Ghost for her work.
A more noble life than hers he did not
think he ever saw, nor a person of more
personal power. He would scarcely be
willing to stand and preach when he could
not invite such a person as Mrs. Palmer to
stand and preach and teach the Holy Gos-
pel. In those Palmer meetings he had
heard the grandest eloquence from female
lips.
	In relation to the Bible teaching, he
would call the attention of the Presbytery
to the passage, "We are neither Greek nor
Jew, bond nor free, male nor female," and
in his opinion, Woman was the most vital
portion of the house of God. It was evi-
dent that she was coming into a wider lib-
erty than ever before; the events of the
past few years would prove this to every
unprejudiced mind—not to a Presbyterian
mind, perhaps, but to the mind that looks
outside a narrow sectarian view. He would
not urge her right to ordination, but he did
claim that she had a right to stand, as any
layman might stand, and preach either in
pulpit or pew, for the pulpit in his opinion,
was no more sacred than a barrel-head or a
sidewalk." With regard to the commands
of Paul that women should be silent in the
churches, Mr. See said that in those days
in Corinth there were babbling women, and
it was probably such women that Paul had
reference to. There were also babbling wo-
men in the present day, as any one might
find, if they tried to get up a church fair or
festival. The women of Corinth were of
the most degraded type, which was a suffi-
cient reason for Paul's mandate to them.
The act for which he stood charged was not
forbidden either in the book of discipline
or in the confession of faith, and why should
he promise not to do it any more?
	A Brother—Because you are bound to re-
gard the peace of the Church. You are
bound to keep it.

[Column 2]
	Mr. See—Jesus said he came not to bring
peace, but a sword.
	Mr. See next alluded to the deliverance
of the General Assembly of 1874 to the ef-
fect that the ministers and elders of each
church must decide for themselves as to
the propriety of women teaching; "and so,"
said he, "on the one side is the Apostolic
injunction to silence, and on the other the
General Assembly says: 'Do as you please.'
Dr. Cuyler's people have done as they
pleased, and my people have done as they
pleased. The whole matter is that the
teaching of Paul was correct and fitting for
his day, but his ministry ended with his
death. The Church of Christ lived through
the ages, and each age must be governed by
its customs. The woman of to-day is diff-
erent in every respect from the woman of
previous days, and the woman of the future
has a yet more glorious destiny before her.
For myself, I had rather die in ignominy
to-night than to yield this point in the least.
My fear is not for myself, but for you, lest
you should cling to the letter that killeth
rather than to the spirit which giveth light.
May God bless you and guide you aright."
	Oliver Johnson, editor of the Orange (N.
J.) Journal makes the following comment:

			BREAKING LOOSE.

	What is coming now, we wonder? A few
years since, the Rev. Dr. Cuyler, of Brook-
lyn was overhauled by his Presbytery upon
complaint of Dr. Vandyke, for allowing Miss
Smiley, the sweet-hearted Quakeress, to
call sinners to the Saviour from his pulpit.
The case, we believe, was taken to the Gen-
eral Assembly, whose utterance upon the
subject was not satisfactory to the com-
plainant. And now behold! here is anoth-
er rebellious minister, the Rev. Mr. See, of
Newark, who, on Sunday, Oct. 29 last, (we
love to preface when such a crime is report-
ed) "did, both in the morning and evening,
introduce females whom he permitted and
encouraged then and there publicly to
preach and teach, being in violation of the
Book of Discipline." The women here re-
ferred to were members of the late Wo-
man's Temperance Convention, whom Mr.
See permitted to occupy his pulpit on that
occasion. They were Mrs. Robinson, of
Indiana, and Mrs. C. S. Whitney, of Hart-
ford, Conn.
	The Presbytery met in Newark on
Wednesday, for the trial of Mr. See. After
some preliminary skirmishing, the com-
plaint was taken up, and Mr. See admitted
the specifications but denied the charge.
The Rev. Dr. Craven, the complainant, then
took the floor and in a two hours speech
presented his side of the question. His
mouth was full of compliments for woman.
Nobody appreciated her noble qualities, her
delicacy, her refinement, her purity and de-
votion more than himself; but inasmuch as
Paul had commanded the ignorant and
newly converted Greek women to be silent
in the churches, when if they had spoken
they would only have brought reproach up-
on the cause, he insisted that women in all
ages however intelligent and well qualified
to present the claims of the gospel and how-
ever wide the demand for their services
and the willingness to hear them might be,
should hold their peace. He felt kindly to-
ward Mr. See, but believed it to be his duty
as a minister of this Presbyterian Church
to resist this innovation of woman's preach-
ing as contrary both to Scripture and the
Standards.
	On Thursday Mr. See defended himself
with power and eloquence. He did not
flinch, but met the question squarely, de-

[Column 3]
claring his belief that God is in this new
departure for Christian women as teachers
and preachers.
	The Presbytery adjourned till Wednes-
day, when it will meet to render its ver-
dict. Meanwhile we have a "little story,"
which we commend to Dr Craven and
those who may be inclined to agree with
him.
	Nearly forty years ago, two Quaker wo-
men, of high Southern birth (Sarah and An-
gelina Grimke), having emancipated their
own slaves, came to the North to plead the
cause of their enslaved sisters before those
of their own sex. They went by invitation
to Massachusetts, where in such vestries,
halls and private houses as in that day could
be procured for such a purpose, they depict-
ed the wrongs and cruelties of the slave
system, as they had witnessed them from
childhood. They produced a powerful im-
pression wherever they went, and at length
the Legislature of the State invited one of
them to speak in their Hall, and attended
the lecture in a body. Those clergymen of
the State who stood guard over slavery
were in a terrible state of dismay in view
of these proceedings, and when the general
Association of Congressional ministers came
together in annual meeting, a Protestant
"bull," framed by the man who afterwards
earned the soubriquet of "South-side
Adams," was promulgated for the purpose
of arresting the evil. This "bull" is the
subject of one of Whittier's stirring lyrics.
	But the nub of our little story is a pen
caricature, drawn at that day by a Quaker's
skillful hand. The picture represents a
crowd of women enclosed by a circular
wall, so high that they could not scale it.
Here and there was a breach, through
which a few were trying to escape. March-
ing around this enclosure was a guard of
men in clerical garb, with guns upon their
shoulders, some of them with the point of
the bayonet, driving back the women who
sought to escape. In the background stood
his Satanic Majesty in his well known uni-
form, and from his mouth escaping this le-
gend: "That's right, my helpers—don't
let 'em out; if you do my cake will be all
dough."
	We commend this bit of history to the
Rev. Dr. Craven and the Newark Presby-
tery.—Woman's Journal.


SAYING HATEFUL THINGS.—What a
strange disposition is that which leads peo-
ple to say "hateful" things for the mere
pleasure of saying them! You are never
safe with such a person. When you have
done your best to please, and are feeling
very kindly and pleasantly, out will come
some underhand stab, which you alone will
comprehend, a sneer which is masked but
which is too well aimed to be misunder-
stood. It may be at your person, your
mental feelings, your foolish habits of
thought, or some little secret opinion con-
fessed in a moment of genuine confidence.
It matters not how sacred it may be to you,
he will have his fling at it; and since the
wish is to make you suffer, he is all the
happier the nearer he touches your heart.
Just have a dozen words, only for the pleas-
ure of feeling a cheek flush, and an eye
lose its brightness; only spoken because he
is afraid you are too happy or too conceited!
Yet they are worse than so many blows.
How many sleepless nights have such mean
attacks caused tender-hearted mortals! How
after them one awakes with aching eyes
and head, to remember that speech before
everything—that bright, sharp, well-aimed
needle of a speech, that probed the very
center of his soul.—Ex.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


THE CURSE OF THE HOUR.

	There is too much lying. On every hand
we meet with exaggeration, equivocation,
deception. We call it all lying, and every
one who intentionally varies one iota from
the strictest fact and truth, is indeed a liar.
	The expressman agrees to deliver a trunk
for you at a certain place by a certain hour.
He delivers it the day after. The grocer
promises to send you the best tea in the
market. He takes the first he lays his
hands upon without any care for the quality,
and dispatches it to you without a twinge.
The printer promises to do your work
cheaper than it can be done elsewhere in
town. He forgets his promise and charges
what he pleases. The tailor agrees to de-
liver a suit of clothes without fail by six in
the evening. You get them in the morn-
ing. The dentist pledges his word that
your teeth as filled by him will be all right
for a dozen years. The fillings come out
in six months. The man over the way is
in need of a temporary loan. You lend him
a small sum which he promises to return at
given time. He keeps it a month over the
time. An auctioneer tells you that a cer-
tain picture is by a master artist. He lies
and is not worthy of trust. A salesman
lies about his goods. A watch maker about
your watch. The gossiper at the dinner
table tells exaggerated stories to astonish
the ladies. The florist assures you that his
flowers were picked in the morning, when
they are two days old. The book publisher
advertises that his book is selling by the
tens of thousands, when he has not sold a
thousand.
	Everywhere we hear lying, lying, lying.
Men and women, who would knock you
down if you called them liars. Deception
is the rule rather than exception. Canvass-
ers lie about companies. Brokers about
stock. Editors lie about politics. Exagger-
ation and misrepresentation rule the hour,
and are its curse.
	Gentlemen— ladies — why can not the
truth be told always and ever? Why all
this deception and lying? Why so much
falsifying and cheating? In the name of
all that is true and good, we beg of you to
do as you agree!—The Alpha.


CAUSES OF ILL-HEALTH.

	In looking over the Journal of Nov. 4th,
I was saddened by the perusal of an article
on the failing health of Western women;
not believing in Dr. Draper's assertion in
his Physiology, that "Woman is mentally
and physically imperfect," but merely con-
sidering the doctor a second edition of that
impious King Alfonzo of Spain, who said
that if the Almighty had consulted him
when he made the world, he could have
given him some useful hints! It seems to
me there are two idols worshipped by wo-
men, which are the cause of so much mar-
red existence. The first, Fashion that great
Diana of the Parisians, before whom the
world lies prostrate and Woman disabled;
upon her ring of changes trade is kept brisk.
She is remorseless and untiring in her de-
mand, slaying her thousands, while she fills
the coffers of the merchant princes.
	The masterly silence of the physicians
are spoken of, this I think will often be
found a necessity, to cover the uncertainty
and short coming of the profession. This
is the second idol; women at large learn al-
most everything but that which is of the
most vital importance to them, viz: the
knowledge of the "House they live in,"
which is really a captivating study; then,


if from irregular manner of living, insuffi-
cient sleep, lack of needful exercise, er-
rors in clothing, and mechanical restraints
of dress on the free movement of the body,
women are lost to all healthy enjoyment,
they consign themselves, it may be, to the
hands of a strange physician, without in-
quiry even of his antecedents and surround-
ings. Then often it is masterly arrogance
on one side, and ignorant submission on the
other.
	The American Journal of Medical Science
says: "Every thoughtful man who wishes
well to this department, Gynoecology, (dis-
eases of women) must view with concern
the unwise haste with which young practic-
ioners, who have neither time nor oppartu-
nity to acquire experience in general medi-
cine, strive to devote themselves to it."
Perhaps not more than one-tenth of those
who take the two years' courses of lec-
tures in our great rival schools, ever have the
opportunity of practical knowledge in hospi-
tal wards. Huxley's address before the
managers of the John Hopkins University,
it is to be hoped will have a happy influ-
ence in this direction.
	General culture is the remedy for this
gaeat evil. Human Physiology should be
taught in every girl's school, so that moth-
ers may no longer cripple their children
from the cradle.
	Dr. B. W. Richardson, in that admirable
book, "Diseases of Modern Life," says "It
is as if the fashion willed that to be fashion-
able is to be physically disabled."
	In conclusion, shall I offer a quotation
from another physician, Dr. Samuel Bard,
to wit: "I confess, not without severe regret,
that toward the end of thirty years' prac-
tice, I found much less occassion for the
use of instruments than I did in the begin-
ning, and I believe we may certainly con-
clude that the person who, in proportion to
the extent of his practice, meets with the
most frequent occasion for their use, knows
least of the powers of Nature, and that he
who boasts of his skill and success in their
application is a very dangerous man.”
	THEMISTA.
PHILADELPHIA, Pa.—Ex.


SCIENTIFIC NOTES.

	A magnificently beautiful meteoric dis-
play was recently witnessed from Jackson-
ville, Ill. Kansas, Missouri, Iowa, Illinois
and Indiana were favored with a sight of
this meteor, which is said by observers to
have surpassed in grandeur anything of the
kind which has ever been seen in this coun-
try. The meteor lighted up the whole
earth and heavens brilliantly, and imparted
a subdued green coloring to the objects on
which the light rested. One singular fea-
ture of the occurrence was that, instead of
coming towards the earth, it passed in a
horizontal direction from west to east. It
was seen first in the west, when it resem-
bled a blazing, burning ball, nearly as large
as the full moon. As it swept along with
its fiery tail it seemed some twenty de-
grees in length, and some ten to twenty
fragments following it. When it reached a
point considerably east of north it burst in-
to from ten to twenty fragments, not unlike
the bursting of a rocket, and these frag-
ments seemed to disappear in a bank of
clouds. From the time the meteor was
first seen in the west till lost sight of in the
east full twenty seconds had elapsed.
	Recently the "San Francisco Chronicle"
described a ball of fire visible in that city,
which fell into the sea. It is stated that
between twelve and one, just after mid-


night, there was visible a peculiar light on
the sand and sea around, and looking up an
immense ball of fire was observed descend-
ing to the earth. Its course was rapid and
the mass fell into the sea about half a mile
from shore; it made a loud hissing noise,
followed by a sharp explosion. The com-
bustion occasioned by the immense velocity
with which the object traveled through the
atmosphere remained visible for about two
minutes. From the brilliancy and area of
fire surrounding the splash by the sudden
immersion in the water, it is conjectured
the aerolite must have been of immense
size. The number of meteors lately visible
have called the attention of scientific per-
sons to the cause; there is much diversity
of opinion regarding the nature and origin
of these aerolites. The two most widely
accepted is that they are bodies of the
planetary system that have come near
enough to be acted upon by the orbits. A
great many aerolites have been found and
many are in the different scientific institu-
tions in the world. The largest of which
there is any authentic record was found by
a Swedish Arctic Expedition in 1870, on
the west coast of Greenland. It weighs 25
tons, and is now at the Royal Academy at
Stockholm. The opinion most generally
conceded for the great number of meteors
which have appeared of late is that the
earth is passing through a belt of aerolite.


WORDSWORTH SNUBS CHARLES
				MACKAY.

	Wordsworth gave Charles Mackay a cu-
rious interview. "He suddenly said, 'I
am told that you write poetry. I never
read a line of your poems, and don't in-
tend.' I suppose I looked surprised at the
apparent rudeness of this," says Mackay,
"for he went on to say—'You must not be
offended with me; the truth is, I never read
anybody's poetry but my own.' Again I
suppose that my face must have expressed
what I certainly felt—a slight degree of
wonder at a declaration which I thought so
very gratuitous. 'You must not be sur-
prised,' he added, 'for it is not vanity that
makes me say this. I am an old man, and
little time is left me in the world. I use
that little as well as I may, to revise all my
poems carefully, and make them as perfect
as I can before I take my final departure.'
It was qnite evident from the frankness of
the explanation that the old gentleman did
not mean to wound my self-love while ex-
plaining and vindicating his own; and I
could bnt take in good part the confidence
he had reposed in me. Desiring to turn
the conversation, I stopped a moment in
our walk to admire the outline of the pic-
turesque mountain across the lake, and
pointing to it, asked him its name. 'Dear
me!' he replied, 'that's Nab Scaur. Have
you never read my poems?' It was on the
tip of my tongue to retort that I never read
anybody's poems but my own; bnt I reflect-
ed that he was old enough to be my grand-
father, and not only that, but how untrue
the statement would have been. So I re-
frained, and listened attentively as he spoke—
'I have described Nab Scaur more than
once in my poems. Don't you remember
the following?" And here he recited; in a
deep bass voice, a passage of twenty or
thirty lines which was entirely new to me,
though I did not like to tell him so."

HYGIENIC NOTES.

	Professor C. Voit, in a lecture at the "Public,"
held at Munich, remarked the weight of the body
has often been assumed as an infallible proof of
the maintenance of the conditions of the body, or
of a deposition of tissue, and the food which
keeps up a man's weight as, on that account, suf-
ficiently nutritious. But the weight of the body
is no criterion of the value of the food taken, be-
cause while the weight remains constant, or even
increases, water may increase in the tissues and
albumen and fat diminish; or there may be an in-
increase of weight and doposition of fat, while
there is also at the same time a diminution of the
albumen of the body. Badly nourished people
are usually not lighter than others, but their bodies
contain more water and less albumen and fat than
those who are well nourished.
	Digestion.—Food is most thoroughly digested
when taken early in the day; ere the nervous and
secretive forces are exhausted by toil. Rapid di-
gestion in the early part of the day contributes to
the immediate demands of motion and enervation.
Food for the repair of the continuous wear and
tear of the tissues is in less immediate request,
and it is best taken towards the evening, when an
opportunity is afforded for its leisurely absorption
during sleep. Rest before meals makes digestion
more complete. Exertion immediately before
meals retards digestion, and exertion immediately
after deranges it. Sleep retards digestion but
makes it more complete. Alchohol renders diges-
tion slow and incomplete. Earnest pre-occupation
of mind retards digestion and may even annul it.
Water quickens digestion and encourages the ab-
sorption of fatty and saccharine matters; but its
effect on the complete solution of albumen is doubt-
ful.


HOUSEHOLD HINTS.

	Cheap Pudding.—One quarter of a pound each
of currants, flour and suet, two ounces of sugar,
two spoonfuls of molasses, one teacupful of milk,
season with lemon or other flavoring, boil it in a
basin for three hours.
	Veal Salad.—Take a fine leg of veal, and put it
in water enough to simmer until it is perfectly ten-
der; remove the bones, chop it small, season it as
the chicken salad with parsley celery and so on.
Make some gravy with milk, take two tablespoon-
fuls of corn starch to blend with the milk, add a
raw egg well beaten, pour this over the pudding
and stir it well.
	Potted or Spiced Meat.—Remove all gristle,
hard pieces and fat from cold roast or boiled beef,
or remnants of tongue or ham; mince it very fine
and pound it in a mortar with a little butter or
gravy and a spoonful of good Worcester sauce;
season it during the pounding process with ground
cloves, or cinnamon, or allspice, mace, or grated
nutmeg, salt and pepper, a little cayenne; put it
into jars and press it close down for some time.
	Bread Pudding.—Take about two ounces of
bread, half a pint of milk, one egg, half a cup of
sugar; heat the milk boiling hot, pour it over the
bread, let it stand until well soaked, then stir in
the egg and sugar well beaten, with grated nut-
meg or other flavoring, and bake or steam it.

WOMAN'S EXPONENT.

VOL. 5.		SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, FEBRUARY 15, 1877.		No. 18


TEMPLE SONG.

	Hark, hark! angelic minstrels sing
		A sweet, melodious strain;
	Heav'n's high, celestial arches ring
		With joyful news again,
	Lo! now another key is turned:
		'Tis God’s divine behest;
	And those for whom our hearts have yearn'd,
		Our dead, again are blest.

CHORUS.

From the valleys of Ephraim hosannas arise,
And new hallelujahs descend from the skies,
Glad shouts of redemption from bondage resound
From the shades where the spirits in prison are bound.

	In eighteen hundred seventy seven,
		Let holy records tell,
	A Temple's finished—bolts are riven
		In twain where spirits dwell
	We've been baptiz'd for them, and now,
		As agents, in their stead,
	We're wash'd and we're anointed too—
		The living for the dead.

CHORUS—From the valleys, &c.

	Within a Temple's sacred court,
		Beneath its royal tower,
	Let humble, faithful saints resort
		To weild salvation's power.
	Salvation's work! O, glorious theme!
		Too high for mortal tongues;
	Seraphic hosts its grace proclaim
		In everlasting songs,

CHORUS—From the valleys. &c.

	A great, momentous time's at hand,
		Portending signs appear;
	The wise will see and understand
		The day of God is near.
	Ye heav'nly gates, no more ajar—
		Henceforth stand open wide;
	The Bridegroom's voice is heard afar,
		Prepare, prepare the Bride.

CHORUS—From the valleys, &c.

	E. R. SNOW.


[Column 1]

	For the EXPONENT.
MY PURPOSE.

	I am not ashamed to own that I am work-
ing for a purpose in this kingdom, and that
purpose is to secure an eternal salvation in
the kingdom of God. When I left my
home in England and came here, it was to
learn more fully to keep our Father's com-
mandments, and it was with a pure motive
that I became practically acquainted with
the principles of plural marriage. During
twenty years' experience I have never re-
gretted entering into that holy order of
matrimony; I know it to be a righteous
principle, and it is calculated to be a bless-
ing to all who are trying to improve them-
selves and their posterity both mentally and
physically. I know, too, that the blessing
of the Lord attends it. I have long wished
to bear this testimony to all who will read
it, and though perhaps I have not always
improved all my opportunities of qualify-
ing myself for the position I hold as a
Mother in Israel, yet I am trying to learn
every day how best to live to accomplish
my "purpose" I am only a humble home-
worker. Circumstances and family duties
prevent me from much public service, and
I know there are many of my sisters more
capable and more at liberty to search out
the distressed, comfort the mourner, guide
the weak and erring, lead the young and
strengthen the aged by efficient and appro-
priate help, and scatter words of counsel

[Column 2]

and encouragement to the many through
the EXPONENT. For all this I am thank-
ful, and I know that every one will be re-
warded for all the good they do.
	But to return to my "purpose." I wish
to live so that I may be worthy to receive
from my heavenly Father, as my own
through all eternity, these children which
He has lent me. If I am faithful to my
trust, and can with the Lord's blessing do
my part towards instilling and implanting
in their minds the principles of this gospel
while they are young, then I think I shall
gain "my purpose." "As the twig is bent
the tree's inclined," and the comforting,
cheering assurance is given us by the wise
man, that if we "train up a child in the
way he should go, when he is old he will
never depart from it." If the Lord spares
these boys to me, and I can so teach coun-
sel and guide them that they grow up to
manhood honest and true, and become good,
faithful and efficient Elders in this Church,
then I think I shall not have lived in vain.
This is the problem for me to solve, this is
the purpose before me. While they are in
infancy we can watch them, and check lit-
tle tendencies to evil and cultivate the good;
but as they grow older they are away at
school, or in the field at work, or separated
from us for weeks and months through un-
controllable circumstances, but if they have
been taught to confide to us, their mothers,
their thoughts and ideas, to tell us of their
difficulties, and above all never to do any-
thing they cannot ask the blessing of God
upon, they will not go far wrong. When
the father is absent they should take part
in praying in the family circle, and in ask-
ing blessings; this would give them confi-
dence, and will accustom them to pray in
their own innocent, child-like way, and I
think no prayer is more acceptable with
God than that of an earnest, innocent child.
Not more than a year since, a boy of four-
teen years, with whom I am acquainted,
was riding alone across the desert from
Dugway mountain, fifty miles west of this
place; the weather was very cold, it was
near night, and he had rode hard all day,
had neither bedding nor provisions, and
was many miles from shelter. He alighted
and led his horse to a spring to drink, when
it broke from him and run off; the poor
boy ran till he was almost exhausted, but
failed to catch the animal. Realizing his
danger he knelt down and prayed earnestly
to the Lord for help. Very soon, quite un-
expectedly a young man rode up, to whom
he told his trouble; he rode after the horse,
caught him and sent him safely on his way.
With tears the boy told his mother of the
circumstance and that the Lord had an-
swered his prayer, adding that he would
always remember it.
	We cannot only have a watch-care over
our own, but every thoughtful, observing
mother will see many opportunities of
dropping a word of advice, counsel or en-
couragement, or to implant a good idea in
some young mind; in short, if we cannot
engage in much public enterprise, let us
always be ready to lend our influence to the
right, and never by a word encourage any-
thing that is contrary to, or that would
bring reproach upon any principle of this
Gospel. Let us always strive to imbue the

[Column 3]

minds of our children with the love of OR-
DER, and of UNITY, and TRUTH; teach
them to respect their parents, then they
will respect and be obedient to the Priest-
hood; and also instruct them in the princi-
ples of the United Order and of Celestial
Marriage, that they may feel like the boy
who, when asked by his mother, if with the
understanding he had, he regretted that his
mother was his father's plural wife, an-
swered her, "No, mother, I am proud of 
it."
	ESTHER A. BENNION.
Vernon, Jan. 26, 1877.


NOTES AND NEWS.

	THE convention of Miss Anthony, Mrs.
Stanton, and their co-workers, in behalf of
a Sixteenth Amendment to the Federal
Constitution, prohibiting political disabili-
ties on account of sex, was well attended.
The addresses of these ladies, and of Mrs.
Blake, Mrs. Spencer, Mrs. Swisshelm and
others, were well received. The Hutchin-
sons sang, and the meeting continued two
days.
	At a hearing subsequently had before the
House Judiciary Committee, action was
urgently requested, affirming Woman's
right to practice law before the Supreme
Court, and it is hoped that a favorable re-
sult may be obtained.
	Petitions from twenty-three States, sign-
ed by several thousand citizens, were pre-
sented to Congress and referred to the Ju-
diciary Committee.—Ex.


The "Journal of Chemistry" gives utter-
ance to the following sentiments, and there
is so much truth in them that parents would
do well to take heed to their daughters who
are bringing home such piles of school-
books: "It is indeed a depressing sight, the
groups of girls released at night from our
public schools, struggling homeward laden
with six or eight text-books, which they
are to pore over with intense anxiety dur-
ing the long evening hours—hours which
should be devoted to healthful recreation.
An anxious school-girl is a pitiable object,
as with that anxiety comes cough, sleep-
lessness, loss of appetite, and a long train of
formidable evils. We are almost willing to
say that girls ought to be prohibited by
statute law from studying out of school
hours. A law of this nature would seem
to be as much needed as that which prohib-
its manufacturing establishments from em-
ploying youth under a certain age."


"Home" is the chief school of human
virtue. Its responsibilities, joys, sorrows,
smiles, tears, hopes and solicitudes, form the
chief interests of human life. Go where a
man may, home is the centre to which his
heart turns. The thought of his home
nerves his arm and lightens his toil. For
that his heart yearns when he is afar off.
There he garners up his best treasures.
God has ordained for all men alike the
highest earthly happiness in providing for
all the sanctuary of home.—Dr. Channing.


138		WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
			R. S. REPORTS.

	At the General Retrenchment Meeting
in the Fourteenth Ward Assembly Rooms,
Saturday, Feb. 3, 1877, there was a very
good attendance. President Mrs. M. I.
Horne presided. After the usual exercises,
the reading of the minutes from the various
wards concluded, Mrs. Horne addressed the
assembly; her remarks were wise and judi-
cious, and appropriate to the times in which
we are living. She referred to the position
in which prayer should be offered, thought
it was always most appropriate to kneel, but
it was not at all times convenient, yet the
head should be bowed and the thoughts
concentrated and fixed upon the Lord while
the blessing has invoked. She also spoke
of partaking of the Sacrament in a proper
manner, which should certainly be observed.
Counselor Miss Snow spoke upon the sub-
jects of home-industries, the spiritual culti-
vation of the young and gave many impor-
tant and interesting ideas. Counselor Mrs.
Young spoke upon the silk-culture and its
adaptability to the climate of Utah and the
circumstances of the people; that it would
be a resource for means which could be
made profitable, if once it could become
thoroughly established. Mrs. E. S. Barney,
Mrs. W. East, Mrs. M. A. E. Watmough
spoke upon things and principles which per-
tain to the building up of the kingdom, and
many of the sisters present bore a faithful
testimony. An excellent spirit pervaded
the minds of all.

	PEOA SUMMIT Co., Jan. 27, 1877.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	The Peoa Relief Society was organized
on September 13th 1875, since which time
we have endeavored to keep pace with the
times, and follow the good advice which is
given to us through your valuable paper.
	The present officers of the Society are:
President, Mrs. Rachel M. Miles; Counsel-
ors, Mrs. Lydia Marchant, Mrs. Sarah M.
Newman; Secretaries, Mrs. Harriet M.
Marchant and Mrs. Sally A. Lee; Treasurer,
Mrs. Lauretta Palmer; Teachers, Mrs. Lydia
E. Walker, Mrs. Elizabeth Wright, Mrs.
Annie Jensen, Mrs. Annie Marchant, Mrs.
Maria Lyons, and Mrs. Martha Huffaker.
These sisters have been diligent in the dis-
charge of their duties and merit great
praise for the active part they have taken
in the society.
	The Society is in a flourishing condition
and the members, numbering thirty-four in
all, are alive to their duties, and are anxious
to forward the good cause in which they
are engaged. With the spirit of God to
guide us, and peace and unity practiced in
our every-day lives, I know that we will be
able to accomplish much good.
	We do not forget the noble move in rela-
tion to the Grain Question, and hope in
time to be able to do something in regard
to that matter; but at present we can do
nothing, having lost our grain crops this
last season by frost which has been a great
detriment to the prosperity of our settle-
ment.
	The visit of Sisters' E. Howard and E.
S. Barney was to us an enjoyable time. The
good advice they gave to the sisters in re-
lation to their duties, the encouragement
we received to live pure and holy lives,
and the benefit of the Society and the good
it would accomplish was fully dwelt upon;
all of which gave us renewed diligence in
the discharge of our duties. The visit of
these two sisters and their words will long
be remembered, and the good spirit and in-

[Column 2]
fluence which they left, will, we trust, ever
be in our minds.
	On Dec. 14, 1876, we organized a Young
Ladies' Retrenchment Association with the
following officers: President, Mrs. Jane Ann
Maxwell, Counselors, Miss Ann Eliza Pal-
mer, Mrs. Elizabeth Marchant, Miss Annie
M. Larsen, Miss Louisa Wright, and Miss
Elizabeth Maxwell; Secretary, Miss Rachel
E. Shippen; Assistant Secretary and Treas-
urer, Miss Luella M. Lee. The Association
at present, numbers twenty-three. The
young sisters feel well and are determined
to do all in their power to help the good
cause in which they are engaged, and by
living, and encouraging others to live pure
and holy lives that they may become use-
ful and honorable women in the kingdom of
our Lord.
			HARRIET MARCHANT.
							Sec'y.

	CEDAR CITY, IRON Co., Jan. 18, 1877.
EDITOR EXPONENT.
	In compliance with your request to the
Relief Societies, I feel it my duty to for-
ward a report of the steps taken by us in re-
gard to the grain question. We have not
made much progress so far. A committee
of three sisters have been appointed who
will no doubt faithfully discharge the du-
ties devolving upon them in this cause,
feeling as we all should the responsibility
of doing our utmost in complying with the
call made by the president of our church.
Our Bishop has kindly allowed us the priv-
ilege of storing what grain we can get this
year separately under our own care in the
Tithing Office, until we can build a store-
house of our own, which we intend doing
as soon as practicable. Upon the resigna-
tion of our beloved President Mrs. Whitta-
ker, whose death a few months ago was so
deeply regretted by all, and her counselors
a year ago last May; the Society was re-or-
ganized of which you were duly informed
at the time; under the present officers, viz.,
Mrs. Sarah Uric as President; Mrs. Mary
Ann Lunt as first, and Mrs. Sage Jones as
second counselor, the Society is in a pros-
perous condition, the members being alive
to their duties and trying with God's help
to forward His work to the best of their
abilities.
	Wishing prosperity to the EXPONENT and
a "Happy New Year" to all.
			JANE H. PARRY. Ass't.
							Sec'y.

		ALMY, WYOMING Ter. Jan. 1877
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	Knowing that yourself and readers feel a
kindly interest in the welfare and progress
of the women of our church, I write a few
lines to inform you that directed by proper
authority, Bro. Samuel Pike, the president
of the branch has organized a Relief So-
ciety in this place, over which sister Emma
Pike is selected to preside; Sisters Beve-
ridge and stoddard as counselors, and sister
Crowther as Secretary and Treasurer, to be
assisted by Sister Stoddard. This place is
not a permanent home for the Saints, but
the Coal Mines are here and those who
come here are only migratory; this is why
we have not had a society started before.
There are a great many families here and of
course a good number of good women
among them and we think much good can
be done by bringing them together, mak-
ing them better acquainted with each other
and developing talents of various kinds as
well as providing a fund for the safety of

[Column 3]
the poor, sick, and aged. Quite a number
intend to take your excellent paper.
	The duties of the Society are new to
most of us but we intend to do the best we
can to fulfill them.
	Desiring an interest in the prayers of our
sisters, we are,
			Respectfully Yours,
				EMMA PIKE Pres't.
			E. S. CROWTHER. Sec'y.

		OXFORD, ONEIDA Co, IDAHO TER.
							Jan. 17, 1877.

	We are organized into a branch of the
Relief Society as follows: Mrs. Mary Ann
Boice President; Mrs. Mary Black, Mrs.
Martha R. Cooper, Counselors; Mrs. Louisa
Croshaw, Secretary; Mrs. Ann Hepworth,
Treasurer. Our settlement is small conse-
quently our numbers are few, yet we wish
to be acknowledged by our beloved sisters
as co-workers with them in the cause of
Zion. Our little girls are classed off in
their labors under the tuition of Mrs. Lucy
Clements. The products of our labor
amounts to forty dollars since July last, con
sisting of carpet, quilts and wheat.
	I wish to mingle my voice with the
daughters of Zion. My ideas have been
aroused with regard to the high positions
that women are considered worthy of fill-
ing, aside from that of housewifery; espec-
ially in the Grain Movement given by our
beloved President. I exclaim in my aston-
ishment, can we become Saviors like Joseph
who was sold into Egypt, who by the sav-
ing of grain preserved his kindred from
the devastation of a famine, or is this com-
mandment given us that our brethren will
be more diligent in this direction or is it
given us so that we will retrench from ask-
ing our husbands or fathers for unecessary
gewgaws. My opinion is that it requires
all this to accomplish the one purpose of
storing grain; consequently I esteem it a
great privilege to be an active worker with
my associates in this great and wise under-
taking, and I thank my Father in Heaven
that I live and have a standing in a proph-
etic church. Although young, I can say
I have a husband constant and true, and
bright and promising little children, and
with him I desire to rear them in the way
of righteousness. This desire is with me
when I labor, when I eat, and even in my
night visions the care of my little ones is
with me, for in my surroundings is my
hope founded and in the cause of Zion is
my faith predicated.
		Your Sister etc.,
					LOUISA CROSHAW.


SAMARIA. MALAD, Jan. 22nd, 1876.
	Our Society is doing well. We had a
special meeting on Saturday last, for the
purpose of donating towards building the
Temple, and the sisters have promised one
hundred and forty six dollars, and two hun-
dred and fifteen lbs of butter to be paid
this year, and I consider they have donated
liberally. The brethren have done well too;
on Sunday, the 14th inst., they had a meet-
ing, Bishop Daniels presiding. They donated
for the Temple one thousand dollars. Forty
families are in this settlement, so I think
we have done very well, we have also
built a new meeting house this winter, so
we are progressing as fast as circumstances
will permit. On Feb. 14th we will have
been organized two years; we have forty-
two members in our Society.
	MARY D. PRUE,
		Secretary.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


TEMPLE SONG.

Shout for joy, and sing this glory!
	For the Temple now is done;
Tell all nations this glad story,
	That redemption's work's begun
For the dead and for the living.
	Who these blessings will receive,
And rejoice, that God is giving
	Pow'r eternally to live.

Hallelujah! Hallelujah!
	Praise the Father and the Son!
God of Abram, Isaac, Jacob—
	Day of joy on earth begun.
And the brightness of its dawning,
	Shows the breaking of the day,
That the time is fast approaching,
	When all sin will flee away.

Glory! glory! glory! glory!
	How our hearts with rapture swell,
That these blessings we're receiving,
	Who on earth at present dwell.
Thanks to God our Heav'nly Father,
	For His mercies kindly giv'n,
For the Priesthood, and a Temple,
	For the foretaste here of Heav'n.

Hallelujah! Hallelujah!
	Praise the Father and the Son!
God of Abram, Isaac, Jacob—
	Day of joy is just begun;
And the brightness of its dawning,
	Shows the breaking of the day,
For the time is fast approaching.
	When all sin will flee away.

		EMILY B. SPENCER.


[For the EXPONENT
PRAISE.

Give praise to Jehovah, His watchcare is over
	His people, though myriads seek to destroy.
Be prayerful, then, brother and sister and mother,
	Your time in the work of redemption employ.

God will not forsake us, but blessings await us,
	If humbly we seek for His spirit to guide;
Our cares it will lighten, our prospects 'twill brighten,
	And comfort afford us, whatever betide.

Then heed every warning, God's kingdom adorning,
	That you may be ready, when Jesus the Lord
In glory appearing, while sinners are fearing,
	To greet him, when earth is as Eden restored.

Then reigning in splendor with Christ our defender,
	We heavenly blessings will surely obtain;
If we have been seeking, God's laws to be keeping.
	The joys of Celestial life we shall gain.

	ELLEN W. WALLACE.
S. L. City, January, 1877.


[Column 1]

NO SURPLUS WOMEN.

CONCLUDED.
	It would be only a waste of time to call
the attention of our readers to these un-
happy and unsanctified marriages. Men
and women bound to each other by a civil
law which gives them certain privileges,
from which in some countries there is no
escape, and which in the sight of God, in
millions of instances there is no shadow of
doubt, are only an abomination. What can
be the result of such a union, what the pre-
natal influences which usher into the world
the helpless offspring of unholy wedlock?
Far better to remain single throughout all
time, than to entail the catalogues of sin
and crime upon successive generations.
Why should so much wonder be expressed
that wickedness increases upon the earth,
when men and women enter into the matri-
monial state with as little feeling, or conge-
niality as they would make any civil com-
pact or business arrangement? Not that
caution and prudence are not requisite, I
firmly believe they are, but there should
be PRINCIPLE implied, underlying the found-
ation of marriage, and requiring affection
of the most faithful and solid quality.
	Young people, men, or women, it matters
not of which sex, who have not something

[Column 2]

more than a sort of fascination, or attraction
for each other, and marry because their
friends think it very suitable they should
do so, will find they must cultivate an im-
mense amount of charity for each others
short-comings and foibles, and especially
should children be given to them, for they
see much more quickly through any flimsy,
or transparent covering, than parents are
apt to imagine.
	"Single blessedness" is far preferable to
unhappy married life; and to be independ-
ent and earn one's own living is much more
desirable than to eat the bread of a man
who, if he does not taunt you with depen-
dency, makes you feel the great favor he
has conferred upon you by making you his
wife;
		"O marriage! marriage! what a cnrse is thine,
		When hands alone consent, and hearts abhor."
	Summing up the whole matter in question
of No "Surplus Women," my opinion is
this, that good, wise and honorable men
should marry all the good women that are
marriageable, and love them too, such as
are lovable. In the world, where monoga-
my exists, there can only be an equal divi-
sion, for the rest what remains to be done?
A MISSION, if they will fill it nobly, honor-
ably and faithfully. And each one who
seeks it will find a fitting place, where all
her energies and capabilities may be ex-
erted wisely and for good.
	There is a great and growing necessity
for woman's work in the revolutionizing of
society. It is the prophetic influence of the
coming crisis, when woman's aid will be
needed, that is calling forth all this criti-
cism. At any given time when there is a
certain work to be performed, persons are
raised up and qualified to do that work, and
afterwards it becomes apparent that they
were eminently fitted, by some intuitive
power, for the very occasion. Of this, histo-
ry furnishes us abundant proofs. All per-
sons of quick perception may easily discern,
if they will, the presage of woman's era
now in progress; are not the leading char-
acteristics which mark the signs of the
times, giving notice that some great, radical
change is about to transpire?
	As an illustration let us quote the old
proverb, true or untrue ("this deponent
saith not"), "When more boys than girls
are born it is said to be a sign of war." If
there is a majority of single women, it is
for some special purpose; there is something
for them to do besides sitting down and be-
moaning their fate. "Causes produces ef-
fects." There must necessarily be a mighty
phalanx of women to establish "Woman's
Suffrage," Polygamy and other innovations
upon tradition and man-made customs of
past ages; an equal number would not an-
swer, for how could the rights of suffrage
and heritage be obtained, if their numbers
did not strengthen their ranks. Men have
become so steeped in intemperance and
many other vices, and infidelity has diffused
itself so thickly among mankind that the
puriyfying elements of woman's atmosphere
must penetrate into every circle, or sphere,
where her influence can be a power for good.
	Let woman hold herself sacred from the
touch or embrace of impurity—let her be so
chaste that the man she marries must come
up to her ideas of virtue and purity and the
world would soon see the result, but so long
as women will pander to the depraved tastes
of men who have no respect for honor, ex-
cept in empty words, whose lives are an un-
blushing lie—so long will woman be at a
discount. If the women of the world could
believe what is really before them, they
would speak louder and stronger and more
emphatically in their own defense.

[Column 3]

	We should be valiant in a good cause, and
the Woman's Missionary cause is assuredly
a good one, if it is filled with dignity and
discretion.
	AUNT EM.
S. L. City, Feb. 9, 1877.


EXTRACTS FROM A LETTER.

	LAIE, OAHU, SANDWICH ISLANDS,
							Dec. 27, 1876.
MRS. L. G. RICHARDS,
	Beloved Sister and friend: I now endeav-
or to answer your truly sisterly letter of
Aug. 28th.

	We are all well and doing the best we
can. There has but one of the missionaries
as yet arrived, Bro. James Keeler, who ar-
rived at Honolulu on the 14th of this
month, reaching here on the 18th. Some
forty natives young ladies, and gentlemen
gathered here and sung songs welcoming
him again to their land. The natives
thought a great deal of Bro. Keeler when
he was here with Bro. George Q. Cannon.
His Majesty the King Kalakaua, visited
our church in Honolulu, on the 17th of Dec.
Prest. W. E. Pack had the honor of preach-
ing to him. The King's object in visiting
our church was to organize Female Relief
Societies, as he is doing in other churches,
and after services were through he pro-
ceeded to attend to that business. He
wished to raise a fund to be applied to the
relief of the sick. During his remarks he
said his sympathies were with our people.
He wished them well and would like to
see them prosper. He had learned by the
Minister, Mr. Pack, that our right name
was not "Mormon" but Latter-day Saints;
said it was wrong to nick-name churches
and people, they should be called by their
right names. He was a member of the
Reverend Mr. Bishop's church, and had
been nick-named "Behopa." Said all
churches here were aiming for the same
place; they were all the same to him and
would receive his support.
	I will now tell you how nicely we en-
joyed our Christmas. We foreigners, were
serenaded by the Laie Band which consist-
ed of four tin whistles, one snare and one
bass drum, on which the brethren made tol-
erable music for amatuers. We treated
them to lemonade (some we had manufac-
tured ourselves from limes for the occasion,)
and cakes which they partook of with a
relish, being something new to them. We
sisters joined together and had a family
dinner. All seemed to enjoy themselves,
and thus passed my first Christmas on Laie
The sisters wish to be remembered to you.
Little Millie learns the la[---]ge fast; she is
a great comfort to me.

	The weather is very pleasant; Thermom-
eter 81 in the shade.
The EXPONENT comes regularly and we 
are always ready to peruse it.  You say you
know this mission will prove a blessing to 
me.  I hope and trust my Father in Heaven
will remember me and find me worthy of 
His blessings.  I know we have the pray-
ers of the Saints.  I am your sister, as ever,
JANE E. MOLEN.


A TREASURE.--A lady applying for
admission to the junior class of an Ameri-
can seminary, on being questioned by the
president as to her qualifications, replied,
"I ain't much of an arithmetiker; but I am
an elegant grammarist."
