SALT LAKE CITY, FEB. 15, 1877.


[Column 1]

COURAGE UNDER DIFFICULTIES.

	True heroism is not daunt[-]d by opposition.
No accumulation of difficul[--]es can discourage
those who possess firm determination, and a
will to succeed in any certain direction. It
gives fresh impetus and s[-]rength to those who
are earnest in their endeavors to persevere.
Timid, shrinking, men and women are easily
swerved from any given purpose; the little an-
noyances of the struggles that beset all in this
life, to them seem impassable barriers. They
wilt under the influence of ridicule, sarcasm,
or satire. Persons of superior talents and gen-
nius, often become disheartened under a little
cloud of embarrassments, or unpropitious cir-
cumstances, when if they only had a little per-
severance, a strong will and a courageous soul
to combat these opposing elements, the cloud
would have burst like a bubble, and the unpro-
pitious would have changed to the auspicious.
	There is an old proverb that in war you should
"keep your powder dry and trust in Provi-
dence." Life to most people is a continual war-
fare one difficulty after another arising to inter-
cept progress upon its journey. Keep your
courage up, never let your spirits shrink in
the encounter, however arduous or unpleasant,
and "face the music" with indomitable resolu-
tion and an unlimited amount of endurance,
asking for wisdom and guidance from "Our
Father in Heaven" in the name of One who
endured all shame passed through the most
fiery ordeal and came off "Conqueror over
death and the grave."
	If there is one thing more essential to life
than another it is true courage; without it the
brightest intellect becomes a curse instead of
a blessing. To be sure there are instances
where support is given by some one of strong-
er power or force of character, but such props
are liable to be removed; the only sure trust is
God and one's self. How necessary then in
the training of a child, in the building up, or
forming a character, that there should be a
fountain of noble courage and inbred fortitude
within. Let them know the realities of life.
and do not gloss it over with any falsity, but
as the mind matures let them meet the little
emergencies that will inevitably come, and
teach them to be brave and resolute without
being designing or malicious. This is true
philosophy for boys the world over; but it is
equally necessary for girls, and were they all
trained in childhood and girlhood to be thus
brave and self-reliant, there would not be half
so mnch whining over blasted hopes, and bro-
ken hearts. Of all the conditions of women
in the world the most deplorable state is of
those who despise working for themselves, and
who have been allowed to imbibe the idea

[Column 2]

that woman's position in life is one of de-
pendency. Mothers you are doing your daugh-
ters the greatest injustice, when you allow
them to come to years of maturity without
placing in their hands a power of some sort by
which they could, if necessary be self-support-
ing. They are not fit for wives or mothers
and if they remain single they will lack that
solidity of character which will help them to
maintain any position of responsibility or
trust. Trusting in Providence is proper and
right but were this maxim to be inculcated
without qualifying, (beg pardon) but there are
those who would never get any further than
eating, drinking and sleeping; and instinct
would be their only guide.
	What can be done by men or women them-
selves is their work, and duty points the way,
the future result, the final solution of the grand
problem must be left in the hands of Him who
doeth all things well.
	E. B. Wells


[Column 1]

HOME AFFAIRS.

	THE regular meeting of the Young La-
dies' Association of the Tenth Ward, held
Feb. 8, 1877, was one long to be remembered,
especially by the Secretary who, on that occas-
ion was the happy recipient of a large and
beautiful chromo, both chaste and elegant;
presented by the President in behalf of the
Association as a token of respect; kind, friend-
ly remarks were made by several present and
a vote of thanks tendered the Secretary and
her Assistant for their punctual attendance and
faithfulness in the past. The Secretary re-
turned her heartfelt thanks for the kind appre-
ciation of the gift and the good feeling of the
young sisters which was still more precious.
A happy peaceful spirit pervaded throughout
the meeting and all present seemed to partake
of its influence.
	DURING THE past few weeks we have receiv-
ed many pleasant calls from strangers and
travelers, some of them seemingly very anx-
ious to learn of our principles, others of our
business interests more especially. Among
these were Alph. Mullender, Representative of
Verviers, at the International Philadelphia
Exhibition, and Delegate of the Royal Bel-
gian Government. He was introduced to us by
Mr. H. C. Goodspeed of this city; Mr. J. G.
Morrison Esq., of London, England; and J.
T. Wiltshire of Sydney, New South Wales;
the last two gentlemen staid in the city sever-
al days taking items of all the most notable
features, and attended meeting and Sunday
School, and expressed much interest in the
Home Institutions. To all these gentlemen
we explained concerning the Grain Movement,
Mr. Wiltshire gave us a good description of the
way they preserve grain in Australia in im-
bedded rock. We also acknowledge the calls
of Mr. and Mrs. Frank Cole and Miss E. Al-
lison, the two ladies being writers and corres-
pondents of papers were of course particularly
entertaining in conversation. Mr. Cole seems
entirely free from the prejudice which exists
with so many against us as a community of 
people.
	S. L. CITY, Feb. 8, 1877.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	I send you a Report of the Young Ladies'
Retrenchment Association of the Thirteenth
Ward for the past year. Our meetings are
very well attended and the young ladies seem
interested in educating themselves in their re-
ligion, always willing to do whatever they are
requested to,that will aid them in being useful,
and improve them in mind or heart. We have

[Column 2]

in the Society a Temple Fund, which each
member can contribute to weekly, and as the
contribution is placed at five cents all can
reach it. In this manner we have collected
for the Temple the sum of twenty-three dol-
lars and sixty cents. Have also a quilt valued
at six dollars which we have handed into the
Relief Society, and quilt pieces amounting to
three dollars. About two months since we
commenced buying books intending to have a
library composed of a collection of our Chnrch
Books. Have succeeded in obtaining the "Life
of Brigham Young" which we are now read-
ing and expect before it is finished to be able
to get another, and so continue until we have
a Library of useful, interesting books, which
if not great in number are "priceless in value."
We have had some most excellent teachings
at times from the elder sisters who take great
interest in our improvement, and becoming
in connection with the other Young Peoples'
Societies, a strong and mighty phalanx in this
kingdom. Our President, Mrs. Elizabeth
Thomas, is devoted and unremitting in her la-
bors and exertions in this organization.
		L. A. ASHBY, Sec'y.
	SERICULTURE.—The Deseret Silk Associa-
tion met at the Fireman's Hall on Wednes-
day, the 7th inst., Mrs. Zina D. Young presid-
ing.
	Mrs. Young said she was glad to meet with
the members of the Association and talk over
those things that were for our benefit and to
adopt such measures as were thought best for
the development of the silk interest. It was
not for the benefit of a few, but the communi-
ty at large, and more especially the rising gen-
eration. She suggested that a pamphlet be
published on the manipulation of the silk
worm, with the experience of those who had
been successful in this Territory. She hoped
to be able, before long to supply the demand
at home with all kinds of sewing silk. Mrs.
Lorinda Robinson, of Farmington, would
soon have some handkerchiefs in the market
for sale.
	Mr. Paul A. Schettler exhibited some speci-
mens of silk, of home manufacture; also some
samples that he had received from Italy, the
former having been reeled at his house, by
Mrs. Candon, of Logan. He had one hun-
dred and twenty-five skeins, which weighed
fifteen pounds. It took four pounds of cocoons
to make one pound of silk. He had received
a communication from the firm of Rinalds
Kuntzel and Co , Florence, Italy, stating that
our sample of silk was first-class, the only
fault being it was a little too coarse, having
from ten to twelve threads, theirs only having
four threads, that being their standard. How-
ever it would sell very well at eleven or twelve
dollars a pound, and as far as they could learn,
by persons visiting Italy, also by newspaper
reports, we have the best climate for silk rais-
ing they know of. What was most needed was
to influence land owners to plant mulberry
trees. It would prove a gold mine to them
and the community. They offered to buy an-
nually from fifty to one hundred tho[-]sand
pounds of cocoons. Mr. P. A. Schettler [-]aid
he was in favor of purchasing the very [-]est
machinery that could be got. He had received
two ounces of silk-worm eggs of the Tuscan
race which is considered the best in Europe.
The cocoons are not so large, but the silk is of
a better quality. He had seventeen ounces of
eggs to dispose of at four dollars an ounce; al-
so some in small quantities that he would give
to children and to new beginners.
	Mrs. Barney exhibited some specimens of


silk raised and reeled by Mrs. A. K. Dunyon,
which were excellent.
	Mr. Joseph Heywood, of Gunnison, Sanpete,
enquired where to get trees and what the price
of them.
	Mr. Schettler said he had some young trees
to dispose of from three to four years old from
the seed, at ten cents a tree.
	Meeting adjourned till the first Wednesday
in March, at this place at 3 p. m.
						R. SIMPSON, Sec'y.
	THE MANUFACTURE of silk in this Territory
is being brought already to a degree of perfec-
tion in some specialities. The silk laces, veils,
handkerchiefs and scarfs, are a credit to the
department, and the rich, elegant fringe which
was manufactured for the ornamenting of the
St. George Temple, from the very elements of
Utah, is a tangible proof of the improvement
which is being made in sericulture. To those
who desire the future welfare of Utah this busi-
ness should be interesting and every exertion
should be made to introduce mulberry trees
wherever there is land which can be made
available to the growth of them. The leaves
can be gathered by children, and the cocoons
raised by persons who are not able to perform
arduous labor. In regard to buying machinery,
first of all there should be a supply of the
silk on hand, otherwise the machinery would
be a dead weight on the owyer's hands until
it could be secured. The planting of the
mulberry trees and the raising of cocoons comes
first and the machinery will be a secondary
consideration.

[Column 1]
				OBITUARY.

	DOLLY ANNIS, one of the faithful mothers
in Israel, died on the first instant, in the Six-
teenth Ward of this city of general debility
and old age. She was born June 10, 1800; be-
ing seventy six years, seven months, and
twenty-two days old at the time of her death.
Sister Annis was born in Vermont. Her
maiden name was Green. When the Gospel
of Jesus Christ first found her, she was living
with her first husband whose name was Dan-
iels, and who was the father of her children.
She received and embraced the truth, but Mr.
Daniels rejected it. In consequence of this
difference of opinion, Sister Annis suffered
much persecution; and finally for the sake of
gathering with the Saints, she left her home,
companion and children, subsequently mar-
ried Brother Annis who soon after died a
faithful member of the church. Afterwards
working her own way and paying her own ex-
penses; she came to Utah to finish her days
among the people she loved. She was a warm
friend, a true philanthropist and an energetic
advocate of the principles of truth.

EDITOR EXPONENT:
	The columns of your interesting paper, de-
voted to the encouragement of all that is good
and estimable in woman character and life,
seems to be a place most suitable for the names
of those who have been successful in leaving
behind them an unexceptionable life's record.
There are a few who live in this world in a
manner to secure the esteem and respect of all
who know them, and whose memory it is a
pleasure to cherish. Such was the life of her
whose name appears in this communication,
Mrs. Ann Cash Richards. She was the daugh-
ter of Joseph and Mary Hayward Jones, and
was born May 29, 1803, in Tilley, Shropshire,
England. She embraced the Gospel in Eng-
land in the early days of its history there, and
in 1832 took charge of the house department
in connection with the Liverpool Office, where

[Column 2]
she had an opportunity for many years to be-
come acquainted with and entertain as hostess,
many of the Elders who visited and labored in
that mission, hundreds of whom remember
the name of "Sister Cash" as one who always
took pleasure in doing what she could to ad-
minister to them the comforts of life, and en-
courage them in their labors. In 1835 she
came to this city, gathering with the saints
and leaving children and relatives behind,
where by her uniform goodness of life, without
aspirations, or aims for distinction in this world,
she secured the esteem of all who knew her.
	It was always her pleasure to do for and en-
courage the Relief Societies and all other or-
ganizations for the good and prosperity of the
saints, especially the poor among them.
	For years she was afflicted with an asthmat-
ic cough, especially during the winter months,
which of late proved too severe for her, and
the dropsy terminated her life, Jan. 30, 1877,
having experienced, especially in her early
history many trying changes and vicissitudes
incident to a life of more than seventy-three
years; and has passed away with every assur-
ance which integrity and faithfulness to the
Gospel and its ordinances can give to a worthy
sister in Christ.
	She was followed to her last resting place by
a goodly number of those true sisters and
friends whom she had esteemed in life, and to
whom she was grateful in the hour of death,
which hour to her had no sting, nor shall the
grave have victory.

—"Millennial Star," please copy.


CORRESPONDENCE.

			SODA SPRINGS Jan, 24. 1877.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	I wish to add my testimony to others who
have written on the subject, and say that the
very course the sisters are taking is an initiatory
step toward the UNITED ORDER of the king-
dom of God. The words of our beloved Presi-
dent Brigham Young to the brethren and sis-
ters are the words of life to every one who
will obey the same; the united efforts and
faith of all that take an active part in the pub-
lic enterprises which have been organized
with a view of consolidating the influences and
and exertions of this people, will be a blessing
in helping to establish upon a firm foundation
the Kingdom of God, which will "break in
pieces all other kingdoms," and in spreading
light and truth throughout the earth.
	I pray the Lord to bless the sisters in their
efforts to move forward in every good word and
work. I enjoy reading the EXPONENT very
much and desire it may prosper.
	Your humble Servant,
						LUDWIG SUHRKE
EDITORS EXPONENT:
	When a faithful sister passes away from
this earth I am glad to see it noticed in 
your paper.  I feel that the EXPONENT be-
longs especially to the sisters of Utah.
They are the ones that love to peruse its
contents, and the ones that desire to sup-
port and keep it in our midst.  I often
think what great blessings as editors you
are securing.  You are working for a pur-
pose, filling a great and glorious mission in
spreading the Gospel of Jesus Christ in this 
last dispensation.
	Who can read the EXPONENT and not 
feel that such sentiments are dictated by
honest hearts full of integrity to their God
and not ashamed of being Latter-day


Saints. I am pleased to read the commu-
nications of the Elders. The letter to 
Aunt Em is interesting and contains truths
which sooner or later will be realized. The
poetry by the same writer, expresses the
sentiments of every faithful saint when
away from their mountain home and the
society of the Saints.
	There has been a cloud of sickness and
sorrow over our city for about three months
but it has nearly passed. Those who have
not been called to mourn the loss of friends
by the terrible disease (Small Pox) have
felt sorrow in hearing of the sickness and
death of many of our good brethren and
sisters. It has carried sadness to many
hearts because we could not visit and help
them in their hour of need, and mingle our
tears with those that mourned. The last
taken away from us by that disease was
Sister Delecta Monch, and baby, wife of
Prof. Monch. She was a school-teacher,
and by her kindness and aimiability won
the love and respect of the old and the
young as well as the teachers who were as-
sociated with her. Her life seemed to hold
a bright prospect for the future but for
some wise purpose she was taken from our
midst. Every saint and friend deeply sym-
pathizes with the husband, parents and
family in their great loss which is undoubt-
edly her gain. She has paid the debt all
must pay, it is only a question of time, and
matters not when if we are only prepared.
	A. M. FRODSHAM
Ogden, Jan. 25th, 1877.


R. S. REPORT.

THREE MILE CREEK, BOX ELDER CO.,
	Jan. 27, 1877.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	I will endeavor to give a brief report of
our Society here. We were organized into
a branch of the Relief Society, July 29th,
1875, by sisters Eliza Snow, Harriet Snow
and Pres. A. O. Perry, as follows:
	Mrs. Jane Perry, President,
	"	Mary Young,
	"	Mary Perry,	Counselor ,
	"	Rebecca Thorn, Sec'y,
	"	Lucy Woodard, Assistant,
	Miss Francessa Campkin, Treasurer.
	There are about fifty members enrolled;
we meet every two weeks and enjoy a good 
degree of the Spirit of the Lord; our meet-
ings are well attended considering the
scattered condition of our little settlement.
Our Society is in a flourishing condition and
we can truly say, our Association has been
the means of uniting us together as a band
of sisters. Although we have not much
means we are blest with health and willing
to perform our part in every good work
requested by our president. I write also to
inform you of our success in tne grain
movement, we have nearly one hundred
bushels of wheat now on hand and intend
adding to it as fast as our limited means
will allow. Through the courtesy of Bro.
Richard Thorn we received storage for the
winter. We desire by the help of our
Father in Heaven to exert our energies to
secure all the grain we can, and be one
with our sisters in the organization in car-
rying out all the counsels of our leaders.
	Hoping for the success of your valuable
paper, we remain as ever your sisters.
	REBECCA THORN, Sec'y.

ITEMS ABOUT STORING GRAIN.

			MILL CREEK, Feb. 1st, 1877.
EDITOR EXOONENT:
	We as a branch of the Grain Department 
desire to unite our efforts in gathering and
storing grain. Mrs. Mary McAlister has
been elected Chairman, and Mrs. Elizabeth
Carlisle Secretary, and as we are widely
scattered a visiting committee of eight wo-
men have been appointed. We formed this
Grain Committee December 19th 1876, and
now have on hand nearly one hundred
bushels of wheat and thirteen dollars to
buy more with. The Young Ladies, Re-
trenchment Association donated eleven dol-
lars. Considering everything we think we
have made a good beginning, and pray that
we may continue to persevere. Bishop
Miller has given us great encouragement
and good counsel; he gives us a bin in his
mill for to store our grain securely, and we
can't help but go forward and do our duty,
when we have the approbation and help of
our Bishop, and having a testimony within
us that we are doing the will of our Heav-
enly Father
	Praying for the prosperity of the Grain
Department and the unity of every good
brother and sister in this Latter-day work,
I remain yours etc.
			ELIZABETH CARLISLE, Sec'y.


[Column 1]

	For the EXPONENT.
THE DECLINE OF THE DRAMA.

	It is sad for both votary and patron of the
dramatic art to note the decadence from its
primeval design, and subsequent zenith of
glory and utility, to the perverted and semi-
lethargic condition in which it is to-day.
	Could the shade of the Athenian Thespis
or the Roman Rascius appear upon the
scene and view the unappy fate of the plan
and its perfected beauty, or, could the spir-
its of the immortal Garrick, Kemble, Kean,
or others of like fame, contemplate the
degradation of the profession which the
brilliant efforts of their genius elevated to
a co-equal station with the proudest in the 
"temple of art", would they not raise a sol-
emn protest against the base perversion,
and hurl the thunders of their righteous
indignation on the heads of all who are im-
plicated in this retrogression?
	But who are to blame—what is the cause
or causes which have dragged down and
reduced nearly to the level of a vice, an
art which, carried out from the primitive
idea, would instruct, elevate, and tend to
refine all who patronized it? On this last
point, all who are acquainted with the his-
tory of the drama are united; but, alas, how
easily is the good made evil! How often
things purposed for beneficial effect, through
ignorance and mismanagemeut produce the
exact reverse!
	The press throughont the country con-
tains inquiries, speculations and commenta-
ries on the unprecedented lax condition of
our theatricals, each paper assigning var-
ious reasons for the present stagnation. To
one interested in the prosperity of the
mimic stage, these matters are of serious
import, and demand consideration. Though
many arguments are adduced, and different
opinions expressed, yet from the unsatisfac-
tory bearing of the causes severally alleged,
we cannot acquiesce in any of the general
verdicts. It may be, as some assert, that at-
tractions are insufficient to suit the public
taste, or it may be as others urge that thea-
tre rates are too high for the present string-

[Column 2]

ency in monetary matters, but these are
unsubstantial reasons for the decline of a
profession; for, why, under the same cir-
cumstance as the last, do not our school sys-
tems collapse, our litigation diminish, and
our journals go into general bankruptcy?
The design of the drama was to become as
beneficial to humanity as any of these. An
instructor, a school, where nature seeing
nature could study itself, and yet the others
flourish in respectability; the teacher, the
lawyer and the editor are admitted into the
best society, while the drama is neglected
and degraded, and the actor shunned as
though his presence were a disgrace; the
former professions in public estimation,
stand as necessary to the life of a commu-
nity, while the latter is sunken to the grade
of an amusement.
	Viewing the case in this light, which we
feel to be a correct one, the query is sug-
gested: Is not the decline of the drama due
solely to the perverted tastes of a degener-
ate age? It was not so some hundred and 
more years ago when Garrick was the s'ar
and Shakespeare the theme, nor when
John P. Kemble wore the laurel and men
admired genius and perfected art. Those
were the times when "Sensational Dramas"
and "Black Crook" extravaganzas were
comparatively unknown. Then the dis-
play of feminine semi-nakedness and the
follies and vulgarisms of brainless fops,
were hissed from the stage, and denounced
by the press; and it remained for a later
and more civilized (?) age to foster these
indecencies, and worship at the shrine of
the golden calf they erected.
	Now, the greatest attraction for the mor-
al (?) modest (?) and fastidious public, in-
stead of the drama proper, and artists of
true merit, is a blaze of calcium light and
a display of female nudity. The most suc-
cessful managers and actors are those who
pander to this depraved public appetite,
and are upheld and patronized while the
others are slighted and forsaken. Can you
wonder at the decline of the drama? Can
you wonder at the stigma which rests upon
it, and the lethargy which binds it down?
Managers, actors, and public, can you not
see what is needed?—REFORM—genuine
and thorough. It is folly to bemoan a re-
sult whose cause is perpetuated by your-
selves; dig up the roots and the branches
will die. Unite your efforts to quell this
secession—this counterfeit which shocks all
decency, degrades public morals, and
wrecks the general welfare of the profess-
ion. Let it not be said that Edwin Booth
lost his labors and fortune in a futile at-
tempt to check this downward tendency;
emulate his noble example, persevere in
the good cause, and the drama will yet rise
in name and position and free itself from
the stain which now marks its character
affd defeats the fame and prosperity of its
ambitious devotees.
	"THESPIS."
Lancaster, Pa. Jan. 14, 1877.


WOMAN'S VOICE.

	Through your columns I desire to say a
few words to your readers, and more espe-
cially to the young, for upon them will rest
a large portion of the Latter-day work. I
pray that a portion of the Holy Spirit may
accompany my words that they may reach
the hearts of those who shall read them.
	Young people, be obedient to your teach-
ers and leaders, and I pray the Lord to
bless you with wisdom and power to bear
a faithful and earnest testimony to the


cause you have espoused, before all people
whenever you may have opportunity given
you; and our heavenly Father will bless
you with more wisdom and knowledge, to
live your holy religion purely before God;
and angels will rejoice over you. I am a
mother, and I feel to offer a prayer for the
rising generation, and that they may more
fully realize the importance of the days in
which we are living. Great events are
about to transpire, for which it is necessary
we become prepared, temporally and spirit-
ually. My dear young friends, let us strive
to live so that we may be prepared to be-
hold our Redeemer with joy, for the time
of His coming draws near. O what man-
ner of a people ought we to be in name and
in works? Truly Latter-day Saints.
	JEMINA BROWN.
S. L. City, 20th Ward.


EDITOR EXPONENT:

	I think it is our duty as a band of sisters
to respond to all the calls that are made up-
on us. We have joined these associations
for the sisters, and I think we ought to obey
and respect the rules. We have now en-
tered upon a New Year, and I think we
ought to begin with the year and be deter-
mined to live better and attend all our
meetings.
	Now if we believe that by depriving
ourselves of the foolish and vain pleasures
of this life that we will gain the blessings
that are in store for the righteous, let us
begin at once, no matter if it does seem
hard; we do not live in this world for the
pleasures of this life, but to prepare our-
selves for a better and higher sphere, for
according to our works we will be reward-
ed. When God gives a commandment to
His people He expects it to be obeyed, and
if we do not obey it we cannot expect Him
to bless us, for He has not promised to do
so; but He says, "Inasmuch as my people
do my will and keep my commandments I
will pour out my blessings upon them."
Then it is our duty to live for them; it is
no use to ask His blessings unless we do
right, for we cannot serve two masters;
then let us choose the right one, and strict-
ly obey all the commandments He gives us
through His servants. God does not come
Himself when He wishes to give His peo-
ple a commandment, but He has chosen a
mouthpiece upon this earth, to whom He
reveals His mind and will concerning His
purposes. And we should prepare ourselves
to receive and obey all the commandments
that are given. Then if given to us through
the Prophet that it is not prudent to dance
round dances, surely we can live better
without them than to lose our blessings.
Then let us be determined that we will give
them up, and not say we will have our
pleasure and enjoyment, if it deprives us
of our salvation.
	From your sister in the Covenant,
				FLORILLA BURGESS.
	Goshen, Utah.


MY little Leon is now four and one-
half years old. One day at the table he
finished picking the third spare-rib bone,
and laying them all in a row, he looked up
in my face and said, "Ma, those bones are
read and accepted."		E.S.B.


TIME is a species of wealth which it is
impossible for us to hoard, but which we
may spend to good advantage.

AN INDICTMENT OF THE
			PREACHERS.

	In the light of the recent action of the
Newark Presbytery, we give an extract
from a European letter by Jane Grey Swiss-
helm.
	American preachers are wont to make a
European tour for their bronchittis, and
then come home and lecture us women on
the respect and gratitude we owe them on
account of our privileges!
	"It is Christianity," they tell us, "that
has lifted us out of slavery," and set us at
their feet to learn wisdom! Now, I beg
leave to call attention of the reverend gen-
tlemen to the fact that "Christianity had
been introduced into every country in
Europe by women, and was firmly estab-
lished in every one, before a woman sent
Columbus to look after America. That
every government in Europe, except Tur-
key, is a Christian government to-day, that
Saxony has God and Christ and Martin
Luther in her constitution; that her court
is Catholic Christian and her established
church and people Protestant Christian;
and the whole kettle-of-fish of European
governments are Christian. and that the
women are beasts of burden, or family
property prisoned in iron safes for the hon-
or and benefit of their owners, present or
prospective.
	The father guards the honor of his
daughter as an important part of his pos-
session, and sells it and her in the matri-
monial market to the highest bidder. Her
purchaser installs her stewardess of his
house, gives her the keys, and flogs her
whenever he thinks she needs a flogging.
	She is not supposed to have and inherent
virtue which would preserve her purity
without watching or the true inwardness of
fathers and husbands to teach her that the
male Christians in public places are very
dangerous animals.
	Women are not allowed to sing praises in
the houses of worship belonging to that sys-
tem of religion, which has lifted us all to
such sublime heighths of unquestioned
confidential intercourse with the shepherd
of their souls, in Catholic and Protestant
confessionals.
	These blessed Christians, in this Chris-
tian land, with its Christian government,
hitch women up with dogs and donkeys,
and set them to drawing loads through city
streets, and plows in country fields; and we
are called upon to reverence the clergy for
what Christianity has done for us, while
they exert all their influence to enforce on
the women of to-day the laws laid down for
Corinthian slaves two thousand years ago!
	No set of men on the face of God's green
earth are exerting themselves against Wo-
man's claim for common justice as are the
clergy of the United States and England—
the Christian clergy, the self-appointed
guardian of women's souls, and the sneer-
ing opponents of their right to freedom of
conscience and just compensation for their
labor.—Woman's Journal.


THE WOMEN'S WORK.

	The following copy of a letter addressed
by the President of the Centennial Board
of Finance to Mrs. E. D. Gillespie, Presi-
dent of the Centennial Executive Commit-
tee, has been received by Mrs. E. R. Snow.
Thinking it might be interesting for our
readers to know something of the apprecia-
tion of woman's work abroad, we publish
it in full.


"Mrs. E. D. Gillespie, President of the
		Women's Centennial Executive Com-
		mittee:
	"MY DEAR MADAM.—The Centennial
Board of Finance assigned to the Secretary
the duty of replying to your letter of the
29th ult., and at the same time requested
me to express to you and your associates
their feelings in contemplation of the close
of your labors in connection with the In-
ternational Exhibition. This duty I gladly
comply with.
	"This work which has been accomplishedhas placed the workers by which it was
wrought very close to the hearts of the
American people. They recognize in it a
true expression of their gratitude for the
past, their progress in the century, and the
hopes for the future which animate them in
this the Centennial year; and whilst they
never can know the individuals to whom
they are indebted for the fruits of all their
sacrifices, their perseverance and their la-
bor, there is now, and I believe will ever
continue to be, a grateful appreciation uni-
versally felt towards every actor in the
memorial scenes which have so recently
closed upon us.
	"As the Centennial Board of Finance
are very conscious of the efficient aid, and
of the steady, perservering zealous devotion
of your associates, and especially of your-
self, to the great cause in which you have
been co-workers with them, it gives me
very great pleasure to express in their be-
half their grateful acknowledgements for
all that you have done, and to assure you
that whilst in common with yourself public
approval is their only coveted reward, they
will ever bear in living remembrance all
your works, which having been entered in
a season of great darkness, were persevered
in through much adversity, until they cul-
minated in a blaze of glory. Allow me to
convey from the Centennial Board of Fi-
nance to you and your fellow laborers their
kindly greeting and their best wishes for
your prosperity and happiness.
	"Very sincerely,
		JOHN WELSH,
			President.
"Philadelphia, Dec. 18, 1876.


PEN AND SCISSORS.

	WANTED, the receipt which is given
when a gentleman pays his respects.
	LONG range practice—Twenty years' ex-
perience as a cook.
	HARD to beat—Boiled eggs.
	IF a lady can't weep for her lost hus-
band, she can at least wear watered silks.
	GOSSIP.—A child thus defines gossip: "Its
when nobody don't do nothing, and some-
body goes and tells of it."
	SCIENCE Made Easy.—Friend, to scien-
tific authority: "Doctor, how is a man to tell a
mushroom from a toadstool?" Scientific
authority: "By eating it. If you live it is
a mushroom; if you die; it is a toadstool."
	A WELL-known disciple of Izaak Walton
sent a telegram from Edinburgh to the inn-
keeper at a favorite resort of anglers, re-
questing the usual preparations to be made
for him, in the shape of dinner, bed, etc.
On reaching his destination next day he
was surprised to find no expectation of his
arrival, and nothing ready as he had desir-
ed. "Did you not get my message?" he
asked in astonishment. "Ou ay, we got a
letter; but, as it wasna in your ain hand-
writing, we paid no attention to it."


FLOWERS.

	Every human soul has germs of some
flowers within, and they would open if they
could only find sunshine and free air to ex-
pand in.
	BEAUTY is admired, talent adored, but
virtue is a woman's crown. With it the
poor are rich; without it, the rich are poor.
It walks through life upright, and never
hides its head for high or low.
	IT cannot be too often repeated that luxu-
ries not necessities, bring ruin. We are
ruined, not by what we really want but by
what we think we do; therefore never go
abroad in search of your wants, if they are
really wants, they will come home in search
of you, for he who buys what he does not
want, will soon want what he cannot buy
	IN a world like the present, one of the
grandest occupations is that of giving con-
dolence. We ought all of us to study this
holy science of imparting comfort to the
troubled. There are many who could look
around upon some of their best friends who
wish them well, and are very intelligent
and yet to be able to say to them in days of
trouble, "miserable comforters are ye all."


BOBERT FULTON.

	The collection of "Gates papers" in the
Historical Society was due to Robert Ful-
ton, who next to his steam project was an
amateur in history, and was among the
early patrons of this institution. There are
very few persons now living who remember
Robert Fulton, for he died sixty-one years
ago; but those who knew him could not soon
forget his tall form, which was more than
six feet high, his expansive brow covered
with curls of dark brown hair, and his large
and piercing eyes. His mouth was beauti-
ful and suggested eloquence. Upon the
whole, his appearance was peculiarly strik-
ing, and well worthy of the position he holds
in history. That his life was one of inces-
sant conflict scarcely need be said. He was
called "crazy Fulton," not only by the
masses, but also by men who pretended to
scientific skill. The by-word of that day
was the "Marine Smoke-jack and Fulton's
Folly." The place where the "folly" was
constructed was near Corlear's Hook, and
here the inventor endured some of his se-
verest conflicts. At last, on the 7th of Sep-
tember, 1807, he saw the Clermont start on
her trip to Albany. At that time it was
the only steamboat in existence. Fulton
was then more than forty-two years old,
and had but little more than seven years to
live. It was a peried of intense activity,
both in improving his invention and de-
fending his patent. To a lawsuit connected
with the latter his death was immediately
due. Coming from Burlington, New Jer-
sey, to this city, he was obliged to cross the
Hudson river in a row boat, and was an
hour making the trip. He became thor-
oughly chilled, and died of rapid consump-
tion in his fiftieth year. Dr. Hosack, who
attended him, exclaimed to a friend as he
returned home at midnight: "Fulton is dy-
ing. His severe cold amid the ice in cross-
ing the river has brought on inflammation
and glassites. He extended to me his gen-
erous hand, grasping mine closely, but he
could no longer speak."Fulton's body was
buried in the Livingstone vault in Trinity
Churchyard. His portrait was painted by a
daughter of his friend Thomas Addis Em-
met, and is considered a tolerable likeness.
—New York paper.


144		WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


PICTURES.

	There is a silent influence in pictures
which many of us do not understand. In
childhood, even these speechless monitors,
looking down from the wall, speak to the
heart with an eloquent meaning that often
moulds its future destiny. In manhood
they bear witness to our growth and pro-
gress, and indicate the changes of the fleet-
ing years. In old age they awaken mem-
ory, bring up the scenes and faces of by-
gone times, and link our hearts in tender-
ness to the shadows of the past.
	But in childhood their inf[-]uence is more
impressive than at any other stage of life,
because the mind is then yielding and sus-
ceptible to its surroundings; the earliest
years being passed at home, and our infant
observation bounded by the four walls of a
nursery, the pictures, if there, are among
the first subjects of delightful contempla-
tion. Imitation, as well as curiosity, is a
fundamental element in human nature, it
follows the other almost invariably. In
this relation the human mind led by inquiry
derives the rudiments for theory before re-
ducing to practice; and thus the ideal
serves the actual.
	Pictures are generally representations of
life, either fanciful or practical, and often,
if attractive, exercise more influence on an
unsophisticated mind than the anxious
teachings of a mother or guardian. In-
stances have been known where they have
marked out and determined the after life
of children whose early years were passed
in the midst of them. We give one—a
father and mother could not divine the rea-
son why both their boys should have a de-
sire to go to sea and follow the hard and
dangerous vocation of a sailor for a livli-
hood. They had not taught nor encour-
aged the idea as they could remember, be-
ing both strenuously opposed to it, but
nothing would avail to prevent the execu-
tion of the plan. One day the father was
sitting, lost in thought when his gaze
chanced to rest on two old pictures on the
wall, one a ship in full sail, the other a na-
val combat. All at once the truth flash-
ed upon his mind; these were what had in-
spired his sons with a taste for the mari-
ner's life. Subsequently the fact was ad-
mitted by the two.
	There is true philosophy to sustain this
incident, let the skeptical say what they
choose, and the philosopher who said, "Tell
me your associates and I'll tell you what
you are," evidently understood what he
was saying.
	In your homes everything should be pleas-
ant, sensible and substantial; not only the
pictures, books and furniture, but the liv-
iug occupants, for all are pictures which are
deeply impressed on the minds of children.
These pictures never perish, for our actions
words and faces remain there as witnesses
to manhood, as remembrances to age, and
as pictures whose light remains forever.
	Then let us be careful to direct the minds
of the young in a right direction, by plac-
ing pictures before them worthy of copy
and emulation; that when time shall devel-
op their latent talents and energy, they in
turn may become exemplary of our race
and bright pictures for the study of those
who follow in their footsteps.
								IAGO.


MANY seek solace for their troubles in
the gay French capital. They think the
best plaster for their wounds is the plaster
of Paris.


HOUSEHOLD HINTS.

	Marble Cake.—Light part—White sugar, 1½
cups; butter, ½ cup; sweet milk, ½ cup; soda, ½
teaspoonful; cream of tartar, 1 teaspoonful; whites
four eggs; flour, 2 ½ cups, beat the eggs and sugar
of together; mix the cream of tartar with the flour,
and dissolve the soda in the milk.
	To dress a Loin of Mutton like Venison.—Skin
and bone a leg of mutton and lay it into a stew-
pan, with a pint of water, a large onion, stuck
with a dozen cloves, and a spoonful of vinegar;
add, when it boils, a few sprigs of thyme and
parsley, and some salt and pepper; let it stew
gently three hours, and turn often. Make some
gravy of the bones, and add it at intervals to the
mutton when a r[-]quired.

WOMAN'S EXPONENT.
VOL. 5.	SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH. MARCH 1, 1877.	No. 19.


FANCY'S PANORAMA.

I see, within the shadows of past years,
	A panorama all unrolled to view,
In many places blurred with woman's tears,
	But life like in its outlines, clear and true.
Visions of wildest fancies come and go,
	And whisp'rings of some dear delicious hours;
And soft and deep and musical the flow
	Of mem'ries river murm'ring 'mid life's bow'rs
How silently we summon to our aid
	The monitors then wont to be our guide,
And where our weary feet 'mong brambles stray'd,
	And sorely in our anguish we were tried.
Stern duty was our motto, and we sought
	To tread the narrow path, and trust in God,
Braving life's battles though with perils fraught.
	And bowing meekly 'neath the chastening rod.
And brighter grew the way as years rolled on,
	And light beam'd up where all around was dark;
And well we deem'd it that some toil was done
	E'en though accomplished by a glim'ring spark.
'Tis pleasant thus to contemplate, at times,
	The pictures which remembrance conjures up,
List'ning to echoing bells of long-lost chimes,
	Though seemingly we drain the bitter cup,
Tis but in thought, and life looks brighter still
	By contrast of those ills and griefs and fears;
And humbly may we our true mission fill
	Through all the changes of the coming years.

	EMILE.
S. L. City, Feb. 26, 1877.	E. B. Wells


[Column 1]
			ADVICE TO GIRLS.

EDITOR EXPONENT: 
	Feeling a deep interest in the welfare of
the young ladies of Utah, I deem it a priv-
ilege to address them through the columns
of your valuable and interesting paper,
through which we learn of the grand im-
provements and enterprise of the sisters
from year to year.
	Young ladies you must perceive now
that you have superior advantages to im-
prove your minds and advance in the scale
of intelligence to those who have lived a
generation before you. It is necessary you
should hasten to step forward and qualify
yourselves for usefulness in life; house-
work to most young girls comes first in
every day experience; and every girl should
strive to acquire a full knowledge of all the
duties of the household; she should be
taught how to work as she is taught how to
read; there is art and skill in the proper use
of the broom; activity and cleanliness both
belong to its use; order and refinement are
required in the chamber for the arrange-
ment of the bed and furniture, but still
more varied and numerous are the duties
of the kitchen. Girls require most training
and the largest experience in the kitchen
which is a laboratory where chemicals are
compounded in the preparation of whole-
some food for the family. If there is one
thing more than another that is really nec-
essary for health it is for a woman to under-
stand how to prepare and cook food. The
necessity of industry, the obligation of
each girl to work for her living, her happi-
ness and her home, nothing is more valua-
ble than to learn to work systematically and
economically; and when extravagant fash-
ions give way as they inevitably must, to
sensible and prudent management in do-
mestic and social circles, the industrious
young woman with consistent ideas, sound
common sense, and adaptability, will hold
the front rank of the women of Utah; such

[Column 2]
will be the ones chosen for friends, wives,
and mothers, to act as counselors with the
brethren.
	Your birth and mission, young girls, is
co-equal with that of the young men, they
are your brothers, and your destiny is alike
eternal. Having been born you are not ex-
empt from the duties life involves, there-
fore study to realize the importance of the
course you pursue here, and do right in
spite of all annoyances and vicissitudes.
Life abounds with opportunities that are
within your reach of improving your minds
and enriching your hearts. Obligations
rest upon each of you for self-improvement,
this obligation in its binding force will run
parallel with your existence through all the
succeeding years of the future; then forget
not that our Father in heaven will require
you to improve, to cultivate yourselves for
it is an obligation he has imposed upon all
His children.
	My dear young ladies, I beseech you to
read history; select the best books that have
been written by the best men and women,
and you may obtain through careful train-
ing of the mind, great wisdom, and make
life a success. Choose the best society, lay
aside light reading, and when you read
sacred things and works of importance,
write on the tablets of your minds the im-
pression given, this will give you wisdom
to act well your part as wives and mothers
and to instruct your children in the paths
of virtue and happiness, and teach them to
walk in your foot prints. When you make
choice of a companion for life, select one
that is in good standing in the church and
kingdom of God, and do not accept of one
who frequents drinking saloons, and places
of that sort; or one who chooses the society
of those who do those things. Shun such
men as you would the poisonous adder, for
they will embitter your whole future exist-
ence.					D. F. Cox.
	Beaver, Feb. 4, '77.


NOTES AND NEWS.

	ON THE 28th of February, Prof. Mercy B.
Jackson delivered the valedictory address
to the students of Boston University School
of Medicine, in Tremont Temple.
	THE FREE Dormitory for women in New
York, is said to be besieged nightly by
homeless women applying for shelter. Only
destitute women of respectability are ad-
mitted. The general police captains of the
city have been furnished with tickets good
for one night's lodging, and these are dis-
tributed among the poor women whom they
deem worthy of relief.—Ex.
	And yet these are the people who talk so
much about the terrible condition of affairs
in Utah, and especially in Salt Lake City.
In that very city there are hundreds of For-
eign Missionary Boards and so forth for the
benefit of the heathen, etc. Truly ought
Charity to begin at home. In conversation
with strangers from New York, Chicago
and other large cities, we have always tak-
en especial pains to make reference to the
thorough systematic management of the
Relief Societies organized among this peo-
ple for the benefit of the sick, the aged,


and the poor, and of the intention to pro-
vide work for all who are not incapacitat-
ed for labor, instead of giving without com-
pensation. The home-industries which
this people are laboring to establish, will
eventually furnish occupation for the labor-
ers of both sexes.


THE MORAL EFFECT OF HURRY.

	To the thoughtful, the moral conse-
quences of tension and hurry are very sad-
dening; to the physician their results are a
matter of profound concern; their grave
evils come under his daily observation.
No evolution of force can take place with
undue rapidity without damage to the ma-
chine in which the transformation is effect-
ed. Express railway stock has a much
shorter term of use that reserved for
slower traffic. The law is inversely propor-
tioned. It is therefore no matter of sur-
prise that the human nervous system is no
exception to the law. The higher salubrity
of rural over urban life is not a matter of
fresh air and exercise. Rural life involves
leisure and pause in work, which are very
essential to the maintenance of the ner-
vous system in a state of due nutrition.
Unremitting spasms soon cease altogether.
	The tension of life produces weakness at
the very place where life is most needed.
The damage done to the health of the most
valuable part of the community, the best
trained thinkers, the most useful workers,
is incalculable. Work and worry, though
not proportional, are closely connected, and
an excess of the former soon entails an in-
crease in the latter beyond the limits which
the nervous system can bear with impuni-
ty, especially the condition under which
the work has to be done. The machinery
for organizing the work of a community
has to be rigid and inflexible, and in the
strain involved in bringing a changing or-
ganism into harmony with a machine, the
former must inevitably suffer.—London
Lancet.


When Garrick was beginning his career,
his great rival was Quin. Garrick was
bright and natural, Quin was formal and
pompous. It was evident that the death
struggle between the old and the new
school was about to begin. At last it was
decided that they should appeal definitely
to the public. "The Fair Penitent" was
chosen. Garrick performed Lothario and
Quin Horatio. The first act took up a full
hour, for the house was full of the advo-
cates of both schools, and both were loud
in their plaudits. At last a trivial and lu-
dicrous, but, as all must have felt, a signifi-
cant circumstance gave Garrick the victory.
In the last scene of the second act Lothario
challenges Horatio "Two hours ere noon to-
morrow I expect thee." Quin with his
usual pompous stateliness, paused so long
before he gave an answer, that some sim-
ple fellow in the gallery, really excited by
the position, cried out, "Confound you, you
coward, why don't you tell the gentleman
whether you'll meet him or no?"


There are many of the greatest deeds
done in the small struggles of life.


146		WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
			R. S. REPORTS.

	The General Retrenchment Meeting held
in the Fourteenth Ward Assembly Rooms,
Saturday afternoon, Feb. 17. was one of in-
tense interest. The Minutes of the Junior
Associations in the different wards were par-
ticularly interesting and showed the next
improvement in the young ladies. Presi-
dent Mrs. M. I. Horne expressed herself
pleased to see so large an assembly, and
said that if the numbers continued to in-
crease that it would be necessary soon to
adjourn to a larger building. She made
many other appropriate remarks on the sub-
ject of Storing Grain; spoke of the signs of
the times—of wars and rumors of wars, and
Babylon's fall, and said this people were
well aware that if Babylon fell now and the
judgments came, they were not prepared to
sustain themselves. Mrs. Bishop, Mrs. Dy-
er and others bore faithful testimonies to
the truth of the gospel. Mrs. E. S. Barney
spoke particularly on the necessity of rais-
ing mulberry trees. Mrs. Clara J. Young
testified of her enjoyment in trying to live
in the United Order, and earnestly entreated
the Latter-day Saints to prepare their minds
for it, that they might avoid the failures
that had been experienced. Said that her
interests and sympathies were with this
people, and not with the outside world.
Counselor Miss E. R. Snow spoke expressly
upon the subject of home industries; said one
great aim was to supply work for those who
desired to labor, and also to be able to make
the entire community self-sustaining. Said
that the time seemed too short for anything
aside from the absolute duties. By request
of the President, Mrs. Horne, Bro. Wm.
Smith made a few remarks on subjects con-
nected with woman's work, and woman's
influence, and the progress he had noticed
since he last met with them. He gave a
powerful testimony on the subject of heal-
ing.

				HUNTSVILLE, WEBER, Co.
								Feb. 13, '77.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	I take pleasure in writing a few lines to
you as a member of the Relief Society of

[Column 2]
Huntsville, which town is located some
twelve miles east of Ogden. It is a flour-
ishing little town with a goodly number of
inhabitants mostly from Denmark and
Sweden. Our leading men are ever alive
to the interests of Zion as also our Relief
Society. Our worthy President Mrs. M. J.
Hammond and Counselors, and sisters in
general are doing all they can in storing
up grain for the time of need. The pro-
ceeds of our last working meeting will be
turned into sheep and grain. We think it
wise to provide working material for our
elderly sisters, as many of them can spin
and knit and are very willing to assist in
whatever way they can.
	The Young Ladies' Retrenchment Society
are doing all they can in the way of making
quilts, and braiding straw, and ever mani-
fest a willingness to assist in the good
cause.
	Your interesting paper is always appre-
ciated, and hoping that it may continue to
instruct, and cheer the hearts of the sisters
is the prayer of your sister,
				ELIZABETH HAWKINS.

			S. L. CITY, Feb. 14, 1877.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	The Eighth Ward Relief Society held
their Annual meeting on the first of Feb.,
instead of Jan. the usual time, on account
of the illness of the president; There were
present at the meeting, President Mrs. Lea-
ver, and her Counselor, Mrs. Sarah Need-
ham; also Bishop Sheets, and Counselors
Brockbank and Royal B. Young. The
Bishop said we had done considerable for
the poor this year; that our receipts and dis-
bursements showed that means had been
liberally donated and disbursed to assist
those who were sick and needy, which he
was pleased to see. Said that as we had
commenced storing grain he hoped that we
would continue to persevere; he believed
that the sisters would accomplish what the
brethren had failed to do in storing grain.
Bro. Brockbank made some very excellent
remarks on "Woman's Sphere" observed
that much had been said on that question,
that it was truly a great mission she had to
perform and that great responsibilities rest-
ed upon women as wives and mothers.
Bro. R. B. Young made a few remarks; said
he was pleased to see the Relief Society do-
ing so much good, felt to sustain them;
thought there seemed to be a lively interest
taken by the sisters of the Society to
do their duty in whatever sphere they were
called to act. The sisters of our society
feel to respond to all the requirements of
those in authority over them, and to carry
out the counsel of President Young in the
Storing of Grain and in the building of
Temples; and feel willing to do all they can
in their humble way to build up the king-
dom of God upon the earth.
				E. B. FLETCHER, Sec'y.

			CANTON, ILL. Feb. 14, 1877.
EDITORS' EXPONENT:
	I thought I would inform you of our lit-
tle organization in Canton, that you may
learn of our prosperity and desire of be-
coming united with the sisters in Zion.
	We were organized into a Relief Society
by Elder D. M. Stuart, assisted by Elder
Middleton, at a conference held December
31, 1876. We meet every two weeks and
are abundantly blessed with the holy spirit,
engaging in singing, prayer and conversing
upon the pure principles of the gospel that
are calculated to exalt all those who are

[Column 3]
humble and obedient enough to receive
them.
	We read the EXPONENT in our meetings
which is truly interesting and edifying,
and it has proven to be a most valuable ar-
ticle in this vicinity in removing a great
deal of the prejudice existing in the minds
of many, who have been blinded by the
false reports that are circulated abroad by
some who have at one time known the
truth and turned from it and by others who
are totally ignorant of the Latter-day work.
We take great pleasure in reading the let-
ters from the Relief Societies in the differ-
ent settlements of Utah stating their pro-
gress and the good spirit enjoyed, and wish
we were there to listen to the wise counsels
of our sisters who are experienced.
	We are quite a distance from Zion but
we feel to exert our influence for good
though our numbers are small, and we real-
ize that we can do but little, being sur-
rounded by those who are opposed to the
doctrines we advocate, we find a great
many influences to contend with; but all
the sisters seem to be united and desirous
of doing all in their power for the good of
the cause in contributing their means for
the upbuilding of the kingdom of God, and
we trust that if we are faithful and diligent
in our efforts, we may be instrumental in
bringing some to a knowledge of the truth
of the Gospel which the world terms "Mor-
monism."
	We desire to be kindly remembered by
our sisters in Zion. Praying for the success
of your little paper and all institutions of
Improvement, I remain your co-worker in
the cause of Truth.
			SARAH WILLIAMS, Pres't.

			GRANTSVILLE, Feb. 21, 1877.
EDITOR EXPONENTS:
	The sisters of Grantsville are trying to
obey the counsel of President Young in re-
gard to storing grain. They are also trying
to retrench from everything that is likely
to injure them. The Relief and Retrench-
ment Societies are doing well. The former
has over two hundred bushels of wheat
stored away, besides being determined to
help their husbands keep all they can in
their bins at home by doing without every-
thing in the way of useless finery. In our
sewing meetings we always introduce sub-
jects of conversation pertaining to matters
of interest to us as a body of people, and
no gossip is ever allowed or introduced.
	The dancing parties out here are closed
at ten o'clock in the evening; we mothers
think this an excellent arrangement as it
will save the health of our young girls, and
very likely in many instances their lives.
	There is also a Young Men's Mutual Im-
provement Society here. This last effort
to awake our youth to a sense of their
importance in these last days, for they must
be the ones to carry off the responsibilities
of this church and kingdom, receive the
earnest prayers of every thinking mother.
	Our City Fathers are trying with all their
might to keep LIQUOR STORES out of
Grantsville, this of course receives our
hearty approval. We are all trying to do
as much good as we can, not forgetting the
Temple by any means.
	Still desiring to improve, we remain
your sisters in the Relief Society,
	Mary A. Hunter, President,
	Jane Eastham,	}	Counselors.
	Sarah Hale,		}
					M. A. HOUSE, Sec'y.


WOMAN'S  EXPONENT.		147


THE MOTHER OF WASHINGTON.*

Long hast thou slept unnoted. Nature stole
In her soft minstrelsy around thy bed,
Spreading her vernal tissues, violet gem'd,
And pearled with dews.

			She bade bright Summer bring,
Gifts of frankincense, with sweet songs of birds,
And Autumn cast his reaper's coronet
Down at thy feet, and stormy Winter speaks
Sternly of man's neglect. But now we come
To do thee homage, Mother of our chief!

Methinks we see thee as in olden time,
Simple in garb—majestic and serene;
Unmoved by pomp or circumstance—in truth.
Inflexible, and with a Spartan zeal
Repressing vice and making folly grave.
Thou deemst it not woman's part to waste
Life in inglorious sloth—to sport a while
Amid the flowers, or on the summer wave,
Then fleet, like the ephemera, away,
Building no temple in her children's hearts
Save to the vanity and pride of life
Which she had wors[---]p'd.
			For the might that cloth'd
The "Pater Patrix"—for the glorious deeds
That make Mount Vernon's tomb a Mecca shrine,
For all the earth—what thanks to thee are due,
Who 'mid his elements of being, wrought,
We know not—Heaven can tell!

			Rise, sculptured pile!
And show a race unborn who rest below,
And say to mothers, What a holy charge
Is thine—with what a kingly power this love
Might rule the fountains of the newborn mind.
Warn them to wake at early dawn, and sow
Good seed before the world hath sown her tares;
Nor in their toil decline—that angel hands
May put the sickle in, and reap for God,
And gather to his garner.
			Ye who stand
With thrilling breast to view her trophied praise,
Who nobly reared Virginia's godlike chief—
Ye, whose last thought upon your nightly couch,
Whose first at waking, is your cradled son,
What though no high ambition prompts to rear
A second Washington, or have your name
Wrought out in marble with a nation's tear
Of deathless gratitnde—yet may you raise
A monument above the stars—a soul
Led by your teachings and your prayers to God.
								L.H. SIGOURNEY.
On laying the corner-stone of her monument at Fred-
ericksburg, Virginia.


WRITTEN BY REQUEST, AND READ
	BEFORE THE YOUNG LADIES' RE-
		TRENCHMENT MEETING OF 
				THE 19 WARD.

	I heard my name put down for an essay
last Wednesday, but I think the few re-
marks I am about to read will scarcely bear
that title. I have been engaged every mo-
ment since we last met and have not much
time for writing, but I have thought much
of you all, and the motive of this little as-
sembly. I have throwm a few of my ideas
rapidly together to read to you this after-
noon, agreably to your request. Last meet-
ing a few words were dropped upon dignity
of character, and I wish to define a little
clearer what I comprehend is contained in
these three words. Some imagine pride
and exclusiveness is meant by them, but I
do not so understand them. Dignity of
character is simply self-respect, in which I
see many young girls are deficient; this
arises from thoughtlessness or a poor edu-
cation, that is in early training. One who
possesses this requsite, will be modest and
quiet in her deportment, will never be
guilty of rudeness to any one, even her
young associates; will be ever respectful to
her parents and teachers or persons older
than themselves, and consequently more
experienced. They will enter all places
quietly and with respect, especially any
place set apart for the worship of God.
They will behave in like manner during
the service, leaving the house in the same


quiet manner, and I want you to remember
that very few boys would be rude if girls
conducted themselves with grace and dig-
nity; women and girls make and control
manners in a great measure, and they also
have a mighty and powerful influence in
forming morals; you can scarcely realize in
how great a degree; but try it my young
sisters with your boy-friends, and see if
they will not respond to your good man-
ner, and respect and esteem you for them.
Try it and years hence you will remember
my words, and say I spoke truth. A lady
is not made by fine clothes, or a fine house,
by no means. She may be clothed in home-
ly apparel, and have a very humble dwell-
ing, but if she respects herself and her as-
sociates, in which consists "dignity of
character" she shows that she is a true lady;
and no one has a right to that title but
those who earn it. You need not be pre-
cise, formal or repellant. No, show that
you are a happy and contented girl by your
pleasant, free, agreable manners, and you
will then be known as a young lady possess-
ing "dignity of character."
					HANNAH T. KING.
Jan. 23, 1877.


WOMAN'S VOICE.

	MORONI, SANPETE Co., Jan. 22, 1877.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	I wish to add my testimony with that of
others in this good work that we as a band
of sisters in Utah are engaged in, for the
building up of the kingdom of our Father
in this last dispensation. I know the Lord
has revealed His will again to the human
family, to all that will obey His Gospel. We
the Sisters of Moroni feel to respond to the
counsel of our prophet, and feel thankful
that we have one who is able to warn us of
what is coming to pass. Sisters I know
what it is to be without a leader, I realized
it in Nauvoo for a short season, when our
prophet was slain in Carthage Jail; I was
an eye witness to those terrible scenes at
Nauvoo. O sisters, what a time that was
to look upon. Then we had to seek a home
in the mountains, where nobody else would
live, but thank God it was prepared for His
people. I rejoice that I have a home with
them, although I stand almost alone, only
my children with me, my husband died
when I came out from Nauvoo in 1847 but
I thank God that I am here if I am alone.
Well sisters let us do what we can in the
Grain Movement. The sisters of the Mo-
roni Relief Society are doing what they can
this year, in that direction, and if the Lord
assists and blesses us with another good
crop we will feel exceedingly thankful.
We have made a good beginning and our
Bishop is very kind and has given us a
place to store it in the Tithing Office until
we can build, and the brethren will, I hope,
help us, according to President Young's
suggestions. We feel strong as a band of
women, and hold our meetings once in two
weeks, a good spirit prevails and all seem
alive to their duties. It has been my lot to
do hard work more than writing, all my
life.
	I hope the Lord will add His blessing un
to us all; and may prosperity attend Israel,
is my prayer in the name of Jesus Amen.
						SARAH JOLLY.

yes, he leave off three times this morn-
ing."


EXTRACTS FROM THE WRITINGS OF 
	ALPHONSE DE LAMARTINE:
		HIS IDEAS OF ST. PETER'S
				AT ROME.

	St. Peter's is the work of an idea, of a
religion, of the human mind in its entire
extent at one period of the world. It is no
longer an edifice destined to contain a vile
people, which we are considering. It is an
edifice destined to contain all the philoso-
phy: all the prayers, all the grandeur, all
the thoughts of man. The walls seem to
rise and to swell out no longer in the pro-
portions of a nation, but in the proportions
of a God! St. Peter's is truly an apotheo-
sis in stone, a monumental transfiguration
of the religion of Christ. The architects
of the gothic cathedrals were sublime bar-
barians, Michael Angelo alone is a philoso-
pher in his conceptions. St. Peter's is
philosophical Christianity, from which the
divine architect banishes superstition and
shadows, and into which he introduced
space, beauty, symmetry and floods of in-
extinguishable light. The incomparable
beauty of St. Peter's at Rome, consists in
in its being a Temple which may be used
by all worshippers—a God-like Temple, if I
may venture to employ that word in rela-
tion to stone. It seems destined to clothe
the idea of God in all the splendor of which
it is capable.
	Were Christianity to perish, St. Peter's
would still remain a universal, eternal, and
rational Temple of whatsoever religion
should succeed the worship of Christ, pro-
vided that religion were of humanity and
of God. It is the most abstract Temple
which the human genius, inspired by a di-
vine idea, has ever constructed here below.
When a visitor enters it he knows not
whether he is entering an ancient or a mod-
ern Temple; no details obstruct the eye, no
symbols distract the thoughts; men of all
sects and creeds enter it with the same re-
spect. They feel that it is a Temple which
can only be inhabited by the idea of God;
and which no other idea can fill.
	Change the Priest, take away the altar,
pull down the pictures, carry off the stat-
ues, nothing is changed, it is still the
House of God, or rather St. Peter's is in
itself alone, the grand symbol and type of
that eternal christianity, which, possessing
in its morality, and in its holiness the germ
of the successive developments of the re-
ligious ideas of all ages and of all men, ex-
pands along with the human reason in pro-
portion as God develops it, holds commun-
ion with God in the light, spreads wide,
and towers aloft in proportion of the human
mind, as the latter expands unceasingly,
and collects all nati[---] into the unity of
one adoration, every [---] more and more
rational, shapes out of all divine, forms one
God alone, and out of all nations one only
humanity. Michael Angelo is the Moses of 
architcetural Catholicism, and as such he
will one day be understood. He has erect-
ed an imperishable ark for future times, a
pantheon of reason purified and rendered
divine.


A SEVERE RETORT.—Fletcher, the Bish-
op of Nismes, was the son of a tallow-
chandler.  A proud duke once endeavored
to mortify the prelate by saying, at a levee,
that he smelt of tallow; to which the other
replied: "I am the son of a tallow-chandler,
'tis true; and if your lordship had been the
same, you would have remained a tallow-
chandler all the days of your life."
