SALT LAKE CITY, MARCH 1, 1877.


EXCELLENCE.
	It is scarce expected in this probationary
sphere that any person will be perfect. Faults
and foibles seem to be the accompaniments to
whatever merit human nature is capable of at-
taining. In many different positions and pla-
ces weaknesses are more plainly apparent be-
cause the person occupies prominence, and the
whole community look on and judge, watch
and criticize. Not only is this true as regards
those who hold offices and positions of power
and trust, it is also true of editors and writers.
	It would be advisable for some of those per-
sons who are so ready to judge and condemn,
if they would for a moment, "Put themselves
in his (or her) place." It is just possible that
they might not think it such an easy task to
please everybody. Although we desire to keep
in the direct channel of purity and truth, and
pray that we may be guided right, we must
leave the success of our efforts as "Bread cast up-
on the waters;" and if we are blamed and scof-
fed and sneered at what of it? It is but a
breath, and when we are sure our motives and
efforts are to sustain those divine principles
which will lead all who obey them back into
the presence of God the Father, what need we
care if we are not understood by those who do
not see as we do? Pope said,
"Let such teach others who themselves excel,
And censure freely who have written well."
	Our paper aims to represent the true senti-
ments of the women of the church of Latter-
day Saints, in strict accordance with the spirit
and interests of the Gospel. We have no de-
sire or ambition to achieve the transient honor
or praise of the world outside of that. "Vault-
ed ambition overleaps itself." And all who
are seeking the applause of the world should
consider how changing is the tide of human
affairs.
	Humility is the safest condition for one's
self; devoid of that, even genius and talent lose
half their excellence.
	We desire not only to be just but charitable,
acting after the pattern of the Golden Rule "To
do unto others as we would wish them to do
unto us;" but we will if possible at all times
maintain the right. Our paper is not the re-
pository or receptacle of all the ramblings of
persons who have no knowledge of principle.
 We do not publish everybody's ideas and
opinions, it would be utterly inconsistent, if
we did. It is our object to disseminate such
principles as are elevating and purifying in
their nature and practise. The interest of home
institutions, organizations and improvements,
particularly among women, are what we con-
sider it our duty to represent truthfully, re-
gardless of the aspersions and criticisms of
those who would impugn our sincerity and
earnestness.
		[handwritten] E. B. Wells


REPORT OF THE DEDICATION OF
 THE KAYSVILLE RELIEF SOCIETY
 HOUSE, Nov. 12, 1876.

	Present—Presidents D. H. Wells, John Tay-
lor, and Joseph F. Smith; Bishop C. Layton
and counsel, the officers of the Society, and a
whole house full of Saints. The choir sang,
"This house we dedicate to Thee."
 Dedication prayer by Elder John Taylor as
follows:
 "O God the Eternal Father in the name
of Jesus of Nazareth we present our-
selves before thee at this time; we have assem-
bled in this place for the purpose of dedicating
this hall which has been built for a Relief So-
ciety house wherein thy daughters and hand-
maids may meet to attend to the various du-
ties devolving apon them in a quorum capac-
ity; we ask O God that thy blessing may be
with us on this occasion, and that the words
we may say may be dictated by thy spirit.
We have gathered together in this place to
dedicate and set apart this house unto thee; and
we ask that thy blessing may rest upon us.
 We dedicate this house and all its surround-
ing unto thee. The ground on which it stands
we dedicate unto the Lord of Hosts, to be holy
unto thee, the stone, the mortar, and every-
thing that enters into its composition--the
brick, the lime the sand, and all the various
articles that enter into its composition, that it
may be sacred and holy, the boards and nails
of the floor, and the joists it rests upon, and
everything connected therewith, we conse-
crate and make holy unto the Lord our God.
We dedicate the roof, the shingles, the nails,
the rafters and sheeting, and everything con-
nected therewith unto the Lord; also the ceil-
ing, the plaster, the lath and lime, and every-
thing connected therewith we sanctify and
make pure unto the Lord God of Hosts. We
dedicate the doors, the windows, the frames
and fastenings thereof, the glass the putty, the
paint, and everything that enters into the com-
position of this house to be sanctified and holy
unto thee our God; and we ask that thy spirit
may dwell here, and that pure principles may
dwell here; and when thine handmaids shall
meet to perform any duty or office, may thy
spirit be with them, to direct, enlighten, and
assist them, and whatever office or calling they
shall undertake, we ask, O God, that their acts
and sayings may be such as God and angels
will approve. We dedicate this house for the
purpose for which it was designed. May thy
daughters and handmaids feel it a delightsome
place to assemble in. Bless also the young sisters
in their organizations; may thy peace be here,
and the spirit of God to attend them in their
administration. Bless the Bishops, the Elders,
the Priests, the Teachers, and the Deacon's,
and all thy servants in the Holy Priesthood,
and whosoever has built or contributed toward
the building of this house. Bless the sisters
in their labors in providing for the poor, coun-
seling or instructing or whatsoever they may
set their hands to do.
 We now dedicate this house and ourselves
unto thee, and all who shall take part in the
labors therein. Lead them we pray thee in
the paths of life, and ultimately save us in thy
CELESTIAL KINGDOM, we ask it in the name of
Jesus. Amen."
 Remarks by Elder John Taylor; I remember
the organization of the first Relief Society in
Nauvoo, Emma Smith was president and sis-
ter Whitney, (wife of Bishop Whitney,) and
sister Cleveland were her counselors. Eliza R.
Snow was secretary and Elvira Cole treas-
urer. A good spirit prevailed, and the pro-
phet Joseph spoke on the occasion. Some time
after Emma got sorely tried. It was the time
when men were commanded to take more wives
It made us all pull pretty long faces sometimes,
it was not so easy as one might think. When
it was revealed to us it looked like the last end
of Mormonism. For a man to ask another
woman to marry him required more self-confi-
dence than we had. I will not confess the sins
of others but I will tell you some of my own.
I was riding out one day by the old grave yard
where I met Joseph. Said he to me, 'Elder
Taylor stop, look at me,' speaking of the reve-
lation lately given on CELESTIAL MARRIAGE,
he said, 'These things must be entered into or
the keys will be turned, and the church of God
go on no further.'
 There was nothing previous to that revela-
tion by which men and women could be joined
together for eternity. We had a good religion
to live by, but it would not do to die by. We
were not prepared to inherit a celestial glory
without doing the works of Abraham, for the
man is not perfect without the woman, nor
the woman without the man. No mat-
ter what I wished, there was no other expedi-
ent that would answer but to obey the com-
mandment from God. I have talked about it
since that time to senators, congressmen, lords,
nobles and congregations in the prominent
cities of the earth, but have found no one who
could say anything REASONABLE nor SCRIPTU-
AL against it. The United States Govern-
ment has not succeeded in overthrowing
it. God has sustained us, and we have come
off victorious. Many intelligent men look
upon the marital relationship as a problem
which the Mormons will solve, hoping they
will solve it successfully, because the intelli-
gent men I speak of know the nation is going
to the bad. But to return to my subject about
Emma Smith. Emma Smith, at first profess-
ed having faith in the revelation on celestial
marriage, but afterwards forsook it, using her
influence to pervert the minds of the sisters,
and set them against it. She tried it with my
wife. The sisters in these days are like some
of the wives of the patriarchs of old. There
was some trouble in their families we are told
in the Bible. The good sisters in Nauvoo said
'they believed the revelation, but—But
what? But as the Savior said, 'if it is possible
let this cup pass, but Thy will be done, no
mine.'
 My wife told me many things Emma Smith
had told her.  When I met Joseph I said, Bro-
ther Joseph, do you know that sister Emma
has been talking thus and so to my wife, and
telling her such and such things, and said that
you have denied the revelation yourself? Said
Joseph, "Brother Taylor, sister Emma would do
throne Jehovah himself, if she could, for the
accomplishment of her purposes."
 Women in these associations should not use
their position and power to pervert good; if
they do so, evil is sure to follow and come on
their own heads. When the sisters come to a
place of this kind they ought to feel the spirit
of their mission, enter into the sympathies
of the people, consider the wan[-]s of the
poor and provide for them, and n[-]ver never,
entertain harsh feelings towards on another,
but forgive as they would be forgi[-]en.  Wo-
men any more than men, have no right to
speak evil; a great deal is committed to their
care, they are required to train their child-
ren in the [-]ays of God. And what honer is 
theirs if they only seek it. We should not
let our children see in us actions, or hear
from us words that we would be ashamed of
again. When your husbands are absent you


[Column 1]

sisters should ask God's blessing that He
should lead you in the paths of life, and fur-
ther, you should lay hands on your sick child-
ren and rebuke diseases in faith and power
and God will hear you. Let us fear God and
work righteousness, and I pray that the spirit
of peace, and the blessing of God may rest
and abide with you in the name of Jesus.
Amen.
	Prest. D. H. Wells:—The Relief Society of
this place has performed a very creditable
work. The sisters are coming up towards the
mark in their sphere of usefulness, more than
they have been. They are a power in Israel,
and a great one, too. It has bren said by the
world that the women in Utah were kept in
subjection and bondage by the male sex, and
that if they were enfranchised they would
liberate themselves &c. They were enfran-
chised, and I am glad that the sisters by their
works show that they appreciate their privi-
leges. They have done exactly what I expect-
ed they would do. Our enemies thought the
women would vote down PLURAL MARRIAGE.
	The sisters are quite a power in the wards in
assisting the bishops. Some of our city bish-
ops say they would not know what to do, now,
without the aid and assistance of the sisters in
looking after the poor, and other duties; to some
it looked curious to have to ask the advice and
aid of the sisters, not only in the duties I have
mentioned, but in taking measures for the
saving and storing of grain. Why should it
be? Should a famine come the women would
have to suffer hunger as well as the men. In
saving and storing up grain the men have been
tried for years, but they have continued to let
the grain go; now we want to see if the sisters
will be more successful in storing the grain.
It is a good time now to store up for a time of
need. The grasshoppers are here, and we can-
not tell what they may do. It is wisdom that
we should treasure up our grain. The sisters
have the power to help us in doing this.
These things are within the province and law-
ful sphere of the sisters' Relief Societies. The
women are just as much interested as the men
are and their salvation and welfare are just as
much concerned. I like to see the sisters bear
their proportion of the labors of the kingdom,
that they may share in its blessings. Women
are more susceptible of receiving the truth
than men. More women in proportion
obey the gospel than men. I merely state this
as a fact which has come under my observa-
tion, and I believe that they have a more re-
fining influence in a community; they might
extend this influence in many ways. For in-
stance, the girls could resolve never to marry
a man who is addicted to vicious or degrading
habits. This would have a good tendency, I
think, to prevent drunkeness and rowdyism,
the use of tobacco. etc., although it is the duty
of our young men to abstain from these habits
without such an incentive.
	Marriage is very important. Our women's
Societies should seek to reform our young men.
It might hurt the feelings of a young sister to
risk losing the man she likes, but you may de-
pend upon it, that a man will be more apt to
reform before marriage than after. I cannot
blame a woman for leaving a man who will
continue to be a drunkard. Drunkenness
brings ruin and degradation to the family.
The drunkard destroys his family, sacrifices
house, home and interests, until, finally, he
kills himself, and leaves his family in poverty.
It is a true saying that there are many men
in the world who deserve no wife at all, and
the time will come when they will not have
wives.
	This house is a credit to the sisters of Kays-

[Column 2]

ville. I hope they will work harmoniously
together for the best purposes. Can we
really find a people on the earth who will
uphold the principles of the gospel, and bring
up their children in the nurture and admoni-
tion of the Lord, and sustain his kingdom that
it may stand and abide forever? Yes the time
has come when it will be done. With this
people and those who will spring from them,
and through their instrumentality, the Lord
will establish his kingdom and sustain it on
the earth. The children of this people who
have been trained in the light of the gospel
will bear off the kingdom, though many of
them may go to destruction, the remainder
will stand faithful to the end. In so doing
they will have to withstand temptation.
	We need to become self-sustaining, who is
better qualified than the sisters to bring this
about? The kingdom of God, like any other
kingdom, requires space, territory, and people
to be subjects of it. Building, manufacturing,
agriculture, etc., are just as much needed in it,
as in any other kingdom, and any man or wo-
man who is engaged in any of these callings
with pure motives, is just as much on a mis-
sion as if preaching the gospel. Every man,
woman and child has a mission. What was
God's purpose in bringing us here at this par-
ticular time? One purpose was for us to obtain
tabernacles for our spirits to dwell in; another
is for us to pass through the ordeal and prove
our integrity; another is to establish a holy
and righteous seed in the earth, and to sustain
those principles of salvation that God has re-
vealed and will reveal. The accomplishment
of these purposes requires determination, skill,
application and perseverance. In carrying out
some of these purposes women are indispensa-
ble; in others they can render great assistance.
It is their glorious mission. Let us go forth
then, and let this be our motto "God and His
Kingdom before all other things." The mere
fact that we are engaged in this work is a tes-
timony that we are different to all other peo-
ple.
	We are rapidly increasing in numbers and
strength in these valleys, spreading abroad
and becoming a mighty nation in the midst of
the desert, which has been made to blossom as
the rose, as the prophets foretold. Whenever
we want to find a country for saints to dwell
in we have to seek out such places as Utah
and Arizona.
	Are we growing in the principles of the gos-
pel in proportion to our increase in numbers
and substance? Are we preparing to be self-
sustaining while Babylon falls, or are we drift-
ing after Babylon? These matters belong to
the duties of Relief Societies; so does the pre-
serving and storing of the fruits and grains of
the earth for our sustenance. The men have
been and are selling all the grain to pay mer-
chants, bills, etc., should a famine come it will
be a terrible thing. We could hardly bear to
see our friends and neighbors suffer for the
want of food, and should they have to depend
on us, they will be a heavy tax on us. The in-
stinct of many animals and insects impels
them to hoard up food for winter; why should
not the human race do the same?
	God bless us all, and particularly you, my
sisters, and help you to continue in the good
work you have begun, is my prayer in the
name of Jesus. Amen.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


"I HAVE turned many a woman's head,"
boasted a young nobleman of France.
"Yes," replied Talleyrand, "away from
you."


OBITUARIES.

	DIED IN Spanish Fork, Jan. 28, 1877, after
a long and painful illness, Mrs. Jemima Jex,
wife of William Jex. She lived and died a
true Latter-day Saint.
	Died, in the Sugar House Ward, Jan. 13th,
1877, of puerperal, after an illness of ten days,
Martha Ann, beloved wife of Bishop Wm, C.
A. Smoot, daughter of Perriguene and Julia
Sessions, born Sept. 20, 1834. The deceased
came to the valley in 1847, and was married to
Wm. C. A. Smoot January 29, 1852, by Prest.
B. Young.
	She was a loving, kind and faithful wife and
mother, devoting her whole life to the welfare
of her family. Her worth cannot be too high-
ly appreciated, and her early death is deeply
mourned by her loving, bereaved husband,
children and friends, who know her worth.
	She was the mother of fourteen children—
five sons and nine daughters—three have gone
before her; the youngest, a daughter, fourteen
days old at her death, the eldest, Elder Wm.
C. A. Smoot, is on a mission to England.
	She lived and died a full believer in the Gos-
pel of Jesus Christ; attended to all the ordi-
nances which will insure her Eternal life in
the presence of her Father in Heaven, and in
the Morning of the Resurrection, when the
faithful Saints of God shall be brought forth
from their graves, clothed with immortality,
we will meet our beloved and enjoy her society
through an endless eternity.
	"She is not dead, but lives in Christ, the Res-
urrection."	M. T. S.
	Provo, Feb. 23, 1877.


EDITOR EXPONENT:
	Our Retrenchment organization has been
called to part with one of its brightest orna-
ments, by the death of our well beloved Secre-
tary, Sister Martha Ann Murray. She was the
daughter of John and Martha Baugh of this
place. Deceased was born in Salt Lake City,
January 22, 1853; her parents moving with her
to Wellsville in 1860, where she resided up to
the time of her death, which occurred on
Wednesday Jan. 24th, 1877. She was a most
exemplary young woman, a true Latter-day
Saint, an obedient daughter, an affectionate
sister, a valuable friend, a faithful wife, and
a useful member of society. For many
years our young sister held the position of
school teacher in our midst, in both day and
Sunday school, her talents and aimable bear-
ing rendering her an object of love to all who
came in contact with her. She was also a
prominent member of the Wellsville choir for
several years, and was elected Secretary of our
Retrenchment Society at its organization,
both organizations sustaining a severe loss at
her removal to a more glorious sphere of ac-
tion. Sister Murray was married Jan. 27th,
1877.
	The whole people of Wellsville followed the
corse to its last resting place and consigned it
to mother earth with many tears and regrets.
Her troubles were short on this earth, may her
happiness be eternal. A singular coincidence
in connection with the birth, marriage and
death of sister Martha, is that they all occur-
red, in the month of January.
	M. E. W.


IF a spoonful of yeast will raise fifty cents
worth of flour, how much will it take to
raise funds enough to buy a barrel with?


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]

R. S. REPORTS.

	MILL CREEK, S. L. Co., Feb. 13, 1877.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	By request of our honored President Mrs.
I. J. Walters, I will undertake to write
you a brief report of the proceedings of our
Retrenchment Association. On account of
the resignation of our former president,
we were reorganized Feb. 16, 1876: the As-
sociation now stands as follows: Mrs. I. J.
Walters, president, Misses E. Carlisle, Jan-
net Park, Sarah A. Walter s Adelade How-
ard, Amelia B. Morgan and Mrs. Clara J.
Hawley, Counselors; Misses Melinda H.
North and Mary E. Spencer, Secretaries;
Mrs. Margaret M. Spencer, Superintendent.
	We hold our meetings every two weeks
and those of the girls who attend seem to
take quite an interest in the meetings and
are united.
	In our Superintendent we have a good
and able counselor, in whom we have entire
confidence; she teaches us many good and
useful things concerning our religion,
and also how to walk in our every day lives.
Our President takes great interest in the
Association and is fully alive to her duties.
We take the EXPONENT in our Association
and read selections from it and the Church
Works in our meetings. On the 16th of
Dec. last, we had a social party, which was
conducted in Leap Year style, being man-
aged especially by the young ladies of the
Association; beginning at 3 p. m. and
closing at 11 o'clock in the evening. There
was a full attendance and all seem satisfied
at the close, having had an enjoyable time.
The proceeds of the party are to be used by
the Association to buy grain.
	On the 24th of Jan. We were favored
with a visit from Mrs. Zina D. Young, and
Mrs. E. S. Barney. They gave us excellent
instructions on various subjects. Caution-
ed the girls never to say anything against
Plural Marriage, and exhorted them to be
self-sustaining as far as possible. They also
gave us some instructions pertaining to silk
culture; thought we might just as well dress
in silk as cotton. They exhibited some
specimens of beautiful silk fringe which
had been made to use in ornamenting the
St. George Temple, also a silk handker-
chief of home manufacture, all from the ele-
ments of Utah. Referred to the Woman's
Book. Gave us some instructions upon
prayer, showing the manner in which it
should be conducted etc.; spoke against
round dancing, thought the young people
had better listen to the counsel of a man of
seventy-five years experience and profit
there-by. Upon the whole we had an
excellent meeting a good spirit having per-
vaded throughout. All went away feeling
that they had beeu more than repaid for
coming.
	Hoping that ere long we will have anoth-
er visit from the fountains head, and wish-
ing success to all institution of improve-
ment, we remain your sisters,
	ISABELLA WALTERS, Prest.
	M. H. NORTH,
	M. E. SPENCER,	Secy's.

	PLEASANT GROVE, Feb. 18, 1877.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	We held our Annual Meeting Feb. 1st,
there were a goodly number of ladies in
attendance, also Bros. Thomas Wooley,
Lewis Harvey, and James Ballenger, who
came by invitation of our President Mrs.
E. Brown.
	Opened by prayer singing and the usual

[Column 2]

reading of minutes and the annual report.
After a few remarks by Mrs. E. Brown, the
meeting was ably addressed by the brethren
named above; they spoke highly of the ef-
fort the sisters are making in saving grain.
Thought perhaps that President Young in-
tended to shame the brethren for their
slackness in obeying couusel, for many
years he has been trying to have the people
lay up the grain. Was pleased to see
the Society progressing their little mites
were accumulating to a great amount with
which they might be able to do a great deal
of good; spoke of our living in unity and
oneness and trying to keep all the command-
ments of God; gave many good counsels
that will long be remembered by all who
had the pleasure of hearing them.
	Report of the year ending Jan. 17 1877.
	Received, in sundries, donation, and div-
	edends from store..................	$	115 57 1/2
	Cash.......................................		  11 20
	Disbursed for fencing and
	plowing lots...........................		234 95
	To the poor.............................		  55 75
	For the Temple.......................		  50 00
	Organ for Meeting House......		  13 50
		Capital Stock:
	Shares in Co-op Store............		515 38
	Provo Factory.........................	118 00
	Three City lots........................	400 00
	Property in the treasury...........	  55 75
		Total				$    1080 13
	President Mrs. E. Brown then addressed
the meeting; said that through unavoidable
circumstances we did not commence saving
grain as soon as we wished to; but we have
selected our purchasing committee and
have on hand about sixty bushels of wheat
and are making arrangements to secure
more, if our Heavenly Father blesses us
with a bountiful harvest the coming season,
we will then try to buy up grain enough
that our little ones may not cry for bread;
and some of the beautiful mornings of next
summer the sisters that have agreed to
make a raid on the harvest fields and gath-
er the golden heads of grain that the harvest-
ers have dropped, will be showing by
their presence there they are earnestly en-
gaged in this great work.
	We have three city lots fenced and ready
for growing mulberry trees; some sprouts
that were planted last spring are doing well,
and we expect to plant more this coming
spring.
	May the spirit of God inspire every Lat-
ter-day Saint to do His will that they may
receive His blessings.
	MRS. F. E. STUART,
		Ass’t. Scc’y.

	Minutes of a meeting held in Logan Dec.
5, 1876, for the reorganization of the Young
Ladies' Retrenchment Association. Meet-
ing having been called to order Mra. Eliza-
beth Benson, president of the Relief So-
ciety of Logan was appointed by unanimous
vote to act as president of the meeting,
Miss Kennie Bailiff, secretary "protem."
	After the usual exercises of singing—and
prayer (by Bishop Preston) the president of
the meeting arose and said she was very
thankful that the sisters had come from S.
L. City, to visit and to reorganize the young
ladies, that she hoped all would be united
in the object before the meeting.
	Mrs. E. Howard then addressed them up-
on the subject and said that the time had
now arrived for the daughters of Zion to
turn their attention to many things pertain-
ing to the interests of the kingdom of God.

[Column 3]

	She counseled the girls especially to keep
the "Word of Wisdom" and to read good
books; gave much excellent instruction.
Miss E. R. Snow said that before proceed-
ing with the reorganization she wanted to
know whether the young ladies wished to
be organized and if so to make it manifest;
the vote being unanimous, it was then
moved, seconded and carried that Miss El-
len Ricks be appointed president, and
Misses Sarah J. Card and Caroline Olsen,
Counselors; Miss Lydia Crochett, Secretary,
Miss E. R. Snow then explained that as
the president chosen was absent, and it was
her privilege to choose her counselors her-
self, it would be wise to leave the choosing
of the other Counselors until the president
herself was present. She also advised them
to choose a Treasurer. Miss Snow then
continued her remarks by explaining the
many ways in which the young sisters
could make themselves useful and help in
building up the kingdom of God. She en-
joined upon them punctuality in attending
their meetings, and to be united together
in whatever they undertook. Mrs. Jane S.
Richards, president of the Weber Co. So-
cieties spoke very encouragingly to the
young girls, advised them to be ever kind
and obedient to their mothers and do all
they could to help and comfort them. Mrs.
Zina D. Young then spoke a few words, she
did not wish to detain them, thought the
time had been well spent, but would like
to advise the girls to set out mulberry cut-
tings and raise silk-worms, thus making a
small beginning towards the manufacture
of silk.
	The meeting closed by benediction from
Bishop Preston.
	Since the meeting mentioned above the
remaining officers have been chosen: Mrs.
Emeline Jones, and Misses Esther Leish-
man, Ruth Clarkson, Martha Bittens, Coun-
selors; Miss Julia Baliff. Ass't Sec'y., and
Miss Isabella Davidson, Treasurer.


SILK RAISING IN UTAH.

EDITOR EXPONENT:
	The interesting subject of silk raising is
of much importance; and silk may be cul-
tivated as a profitable industry to many of
the inhabitants of this community; the
mulberry tree will grow in most places in
this Territory. If the cultivation of silk
were commenced by this people generally,
an industry for the rising generation would
be created which would be a source of very
great wealth to them in the near future.
What shall be done to find employment for
the coming generation as well as the pres-
ent? The silk industry would provide
many with profitable labor; silk raising,
reeling and manufacturing would employ
many and profit the community.
	The total manufacture of silk in value, in
1874 in America, was twenty million eighty-
two thousand, four hundred and eighty-two
dollars. Would not Utah do well to make
an effort for her share of this immense in-
come? All industries have their commence-
ment, and should have their progress. The
following is said to be a report for 1874:
"Of the different branches of the silk in-
dustry, thrown and spun silk embrace
twenty-six establishments, half being
in New Jersey, with machinery of one
thousand and three horse power, one hun-
dred and twenty-nine thousand, one hun-
dred and seventy-eight throwing spindles;
the number of hands employed in 1874,
was four thousand and twenty-six; amount
of wages paid, nine hundred and forty-five


[Column 1]

thousand, seven hundred and ten dollars;
capital invested, forty-one million two hun-
dred and fifty-two thousand, one hundred
and fifty-two dollars; total product, three
million, eight-hundred and sixty-two thou-
sand, three hundred and twenty-five dol-
lars; including tram and organzine, two
million, nine hundred and eleven thousand
and fifty-five dollars; spun silk, eight hun-
dred thousand dollars; fringe silk, one hun-
dred and nine thousand, two hundred and
seventy dollars, and floss silk, fourty-three
thousand dollars; total weight of product,
five hundred and forty thousand, eight hun-
dred and four pounds."
	This extraordinary and stupendous pro-
duction had its origin, like all other indus-
tries; and other places may follow after and
progress. New Jersey, like many other
places in times past had no silk industry;
but according to preceding facts, immense
wealth has been accumulated. Tooele is a
very fine location for the silk industry, as
well as many other localities. Many of the
people in Tooele no doubt, are very favora-
ble to the cultivation of silk, and we hope
soon to see them commence in the cultiva-
tion of it.
	Salt Lake City has set a good example,
and all other places would do well to form
working committees. One acre of mulberry
trees planted two feet distant in rows and
six feet asunder will be three thousand,
four hundred trees, which method is prob-
ably as good as any (Mr. Hoag, of California,
planted as above stated, and raised two
thousand dollars worth of silk to the acre.)
	Suppose that fifty pounds of silk is raised
to the acre, and worth when reeled twelve
dollars per pound, fifty multiplied by twelve
makes six hundred dollars worth of silk.
The manufacturers will give twelve dollars
per pound reeled, and when it is manufac-
tured into silk goods is worth eighteen dol-
lars per pound, when sold to wholesale pur-
chasers then twenty per cent is added to the
eighteen dollars, which makes twenty-one
dollars and sixty cents. and then the retail
sellers mostly add about thirty per cent,
which makes five dollars and forty cents
more to the first cost, making a total of
twenty-seven dollars.
	Fifty pounds of silk sold at twelve dollars
per pound reeled, is worth a hundred dollars,
when the manufacturer's pay is added and
twenty per cent added by wholesale pur-
chasers makes one thousand and eighty
dollars, then the retailers cost added
amounts to one thousand four hundred and
four dollars. Any family or community,
who have the facilities to raise and reel and
manufacture the productions of one acre of
silk may make a satisfactory profit in its
cultivation. The question is asked is there
any money in it? Undoubtedly there is
wherever there is a market for its sale; if
one thousand dollars in silk can be raised
per acre, which is only one half that has
been accomplished in California; the manu-
facturers will add about one third for their
remuneration, which will then amount to
one thousand five hundred dollars, and add-
ing in this case sixty per cent for the whole-
sale and retail profits, will run up the amount
to about two thousand four hundred dollars.
Those who understand the wholesale and
retail charges in trade, will not think this
calculation overrated.
	 No time ought to be lost in commencing
this valuable industry. The object to be ac-
complished, is to raise, reel and manufac-
ture the silk and then all the benefit of it
will be secured.
	If Mr. Hoag's treatise on silk raising
were read, very much information would
be obtained. Improvement will continue to

[Column 2]

be made in silk as well as in all other
branches of business, until it arrives at a
good standard of perfection.
	JAMES I. STEEL
Lakeview, Tooele Co.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]

WHAT TO DO WITH OLD MAIDS.

	What 900,000 English, and 30,000 Yan-
kee old maids—"What shall become of us
poor old maids?" It does seem to me that
this subject is more asked about than acted
upon. Woman's Suffrage, Woman's Rights,
Woman's influence, Woman in office, etc.
	Now it has always been a matter of won-
der with me that man, woman, or sensible
child, should be troubled to find occupation,
in this busy, bustling world. I have always
recommended to persons out of employ-
ment to take right hold of the first thing
they saw in their way that was left undone
by some one else. But few persons like to
do this. Their habits always having been
to reach after that business which pays best,
and is easiest on the muscles. We have a
fine variety of examples of this in S. L.
City. I have, when visiting there, been
much amused at the little "world displayed"
on Main Street. Being a country woman,
I read the signs, cards and shingles, and al-
so listen with wonder to the multitude of
voices; men from every nation under hea-
ven trailing out here after the Mormons—
and I have said to myself "what went ye
out into the wilderness to see?" Eager,
anxious restless ones why are you here?
I look not at surplus women, nor surplus
children, but surplus humanity. When I
see vast unappropriated resources, and a
vast number of people not well employed;
or perhaps not employed at all, I marvel as
an American should marvel, at the leg-
islation which does not lay hold upon such
as have no means of support and regulate
their conditions and relieve the country
of vagrants and beggars, and bring in a
revenue to the government.
	Coming back to the matter, the unmar-
ried, and perhaps very sensible ladies refer-
red to. The English people we have noth-
ing to do with, and therefore need not af-
flict ourselves on their account. The sub-
ject I would discuss is our own nation of
people. As to matrimony nothing on earth
is worse than a bad marriage. Let that go
by for the time; 30,000 unmarried women
in a country where there is the most intel-
ligent class of inhabitants, is no misfortune,
In my judgment there is a finer opening
for enterprises for women now, than ever
before.
	Were I of the class referred to by Bessie
Bramble, I would surely make an effort to
induce Government to take the same inter-
est in her White children as her Black or
Red ones. I would lend my utmost ability
to the work, feeling that it was a mission
from divine authority and must succeed.
	Look through our Eastern cities and see
if Victor Hugo could not find children of
the street there as well as in Paris. Why
might not these honorable unmarried women
of the world combine together, petition
government to set them off a tract of land
and lend them at first other necessary aid
and protection. Go into the large manu-
facturing districts in the heart of this na-
tion and gather out mechanical talent, the
starving, helpless ones, possessed of bone
and sinew and muscle, who are now out of
employment, and found a colony on some of
the vast expanses of unoccupied land where
soil is fertile, the climate medium, water
plenty, and timber, perhaps coal, near at
hand. Women of intelligence and ability,
such as the unmarried women of America

[Column 2]

attain in the present day, could, with tact,
energy and their indomitable perseverance,
so manage as to divide to every class of tal-
ent its peculiar business or work, and every-
thing be done upon a systematic principle.
When Romulus founded Rome he so divid-
ed the community, the Army, Agriculture
and Arts, that they were justly proportioned
and all were defended, all were sustained,
and all were employed.
	When Solomon built the Temple and en-
riched Jerusalem, he simply so divided the
people that there was no idle surplus, and
there ought not to be in our country a sur-
plus of even one child ten years old that is
in good health.

	Why should there be a question of What
shall 30,000 unmarried ladies do, if they
have got pluck and shrewdness enough and
will work together and pioneer, by the as-
sistance government may lend them, a vast
unsettled country. Of course it will take a
strong determination and undaunted cour-
age, but they will be so engrossed in their
work, and anxious to accomplish the pur-
pose designed, that there will be no time to
bemoan their unhappy fate.
	Having obtained the consent and protec-
tion of government, gather up the desti-
tute children and take them where they
may labor and live, where they may be
taught honesty and virtue, for is it not a
disgrace and a blemish upon the escutch-
eon of this nation that there are thousands
of helpless children in this boasted land of
liberty and freedom, with its unoccupied
millions of acres of fertile lands, crying to
starving mothers for bread?
	Gather up these little innocent ones as a
hen gathereth her chickens under her
wings, and call them from degradation and
hunger to honest labor, education and hap-
piness, and of you then, will it not be said,
"Thy children shall make haste." Lift up
thine eyes and behold all these gather to-
gether and come unto thee; "Sing, O bar-
ren, thou that didst not bear, for more are
the children of the desolate than the chil-
dren of the married wife. Enlarge the
place of thy tent and stretch forth the cur-
tains of thine habitations, and make the
desolate places to be inhabited," by the vo-
taries of virtue, honor, peace and plenty,
who would otherwise, perhaps, continue in
vice, beggary or crime.
	There is a place, ladies, for your neglect-
ed talents; see if there is among you Wo-
man's Rights Advocates' statesmanship to
found a colony and return money to the
treasury by the results of your Political and
Economical ability.
	My hands are full of work (as for me I am
not surplus), and so are those of every Lat-
ter-day Saint that is living her religion, but
for the world at large no better plan than
this could be entered upon, granting that
Government would lend such patronage as
it is willing to give to fallen races.
	Share no sympathy with such benevolent
societies as give money to the poor; it en-
courages idleness and beggary, and is sim-
ply ruinous to a child's sense of morality
and honor. It implants in the minds of
children that the poor are to live by gift
and patronage from the rich. Give money
only to the sick and aged, to others the
privilege of honest labor and honest pay.
Cast out from any community waste and
extravagance, and keep God's command-
ments, observing "Six days shalt thou la-
bor," and so forth.
	These are, Mrs. Editor, the honest
thoughts and convictions of a
		COUNTRYWOMAN.
	York Station, Jan. 14, 1877.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]

ITEMS ABOUT STORING GRAIN.

	GUNNISON, Feb. 4, 1877.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	According to the instructions through the
EXPONENT, given by counsel of President
B. Young, the Relief Society of this place
has commenced to gather grain. By the
help of the Lord and our brethren, we have
at this date got three hundred and forty-
four bushels of wheat in our bin, to which
we anticipate adding weekly, monthly and
yearly, as long as the Giver of all good per-
mits plenty to abound. The brethren as
well as the sisters are wide awake to the
importance of the Grain Movement.
	Our Relief Society contemplates building
a hall and granary, to the erection of which
many of our husbands, sons and brothers
have already kindly and encouragingly of-
fered their aid; and the feeling is prevalent
that the grain gathered, and yet to be gath-
ered, is sacred to the Lord's purposes, and
can only be disbursed when the Lord's own
time comes.
	We feel, that while our husbands are
toiling yearly to raise the "staff" and nec-
essaries of life, it is but consistent, that we
should be as frugal and industrious as possi-
ble; that, far from being encumbrances, we
may be a source of genuine comfort and
strength unto them.
	The EXPONENT is ever welcome, and
ever replete with what we want.
	HELENA MADSEN, Sec'y.

	SPANISH FORK, Feb. 12, 77.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	I have watched and read with interest
the various reports of the Relief Societies,
concerning the storing of Grain; and it gives
me much pleasure to see and hear of the
success which has attended their efforts
while endeavoring to carry out the counsel
of our beloved president and father; the
success which has attended the sisters in
their laudable enterprise, is consequent, no
doubt upon their earnestness in the work,
and their faith in the Gospel and in the
counsel given; being fully assured from a
long experience, that in following and car-
rying out the counsels of the Servants of
God from time, to time, the people have
been exceedingly blessed, both spiritually
and temporally. When I read the counsel
conveyed to me through the pages of our
bright luminary the WOMAN'S EXPONENT,
I did truly rejoice, and when it was read
and proposed that we begin to lay up
grain, it was acccepted and received with
joy and thankfulness by the sisters of our
Society, and we felt to thank the Lord for
the inspiration that prompted this counsel,
knowing assuredly that famine and pesti-
lence will desolate the land, and that as
Joseph was sold and banished from his
home and kindred to bring about the pur-
poses of God in saving his brethren and
kindred, so have the Latter-day Saints, even
the seed and royal blood of Joseph, been
banished from their homes, friends and in-
heritances to work out the divine purposes
of God, who maketh even the wrath of
men to praise Him; and as Joseph filled the
granaries of Egypt, so may we fill the
granaries of Utah; thereby proving that we
are noble daughters of a noble Sire.
	We had lumber on hand that we received
for quilts and socks, and have had a bin
made, in which we have over one hundred
bushels of wheat, and still keep adding, and
intend to lay up all we can. Our Society
is in a prosperous condition, and whether

[Column 2]

we meet to work, or to exhort each other,
the spirit of the Lord is with us, and the
spirit of love and unity abounds; and es-
pecially is this spirit manifest in the Teach-
ers; at our meetings they bring in good re-
ports, no contentions or backbiting; and as
a general thing all feel well and desire the
prosperity of the Society.
	Ever praying for the welfare of Zion, I
am Yours,
	MARY FURGUSON.

	PORTERVILLE, MORGAN Co.,
		Jan. 17, '77.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	I take the privilege of conveying a few
lines to tell you how we are getting along
in this little branch. There is a good Re-
lief Society here and we are striving with
our amiable president and her counselors to
do what good we can in carrying out the
counsel given us and trying to be one with
the other societies throughout the Territory.
	In our last meeting the Grain Movement
came into consideration, and the sisters ex-
pressed their willingness to do all in their
power to secure and store away grain. We
have accepted a bin, kindly offered us by
Brother Sanford Porter, and sincerely hope
we will soon see it filled. Two of the sisters
visited around last week, and quite a num-
ber of bushels of grain were donated a foun-
dation to build upon.
	We also have a Young Ladies' Retrench-
ment Association. We are progressing
slowly, and feel that a little instruction
through your valuable paper will be kindly
accepted. We feel that our work in Re-
trenchment must be otherwise directed
than in dress, for our ball-room and Sunday
dresses are manufactured at home, that is,
generally speaking.
	Yours Truly,
	AMY A. PORTER. Sec'y

WOMAN'S EXPONENT.
VOL. 5.	SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, MARCH 15, 1877.	NO. 20.


A DREAM.

Tis strange to night, that while the wintry blast,
Bends the bare boughs, till frost-bound forests moan
In very pain; while shadows huge and grim,
Are thrown grotesque from trunk and trembling limb.
That I alone the graveyard gates have passed;
And stand within, without a thought of fear,
Though solemn sounds, and night, and death are near.

I heed not that the moonlight fitful falls
Through hurrying clouds that cross the tranquil rays;
This dismal blast might be a summer breeze,
And summer laurels crown the leafless trees,
Not more than they, this weird, wild night appal;
I wrap my arms around one cold gray stone;
And like the dead, am quiet and alone.

What if I were like these, whose breathless sleep,
Is never broken as the years go by?
What if my spirit, tired of toil and pain,
Should flit away, and not return again
What have I done that any eye should weep?
To find me lying cold and quiet here,
With helpless hands upon this self-sought bier.

Would any, lifting up these helpless hands
Bless them for deeds of love and mercy done?
Would any kiss the silent lips and say,
That for the friendless they were wont to pray,
That they from ill some erring soul had won?
Would I be missed from out life's erring band
If I had entered in the shadow land?

Or have these hands forgot to cast around,
God's blessings, that we gather by the way;
Forgot to lift the burden from the weak?
And have these scornful lips been slow to speak
Words that would heal some keen and cruel wound?
Have I been wasting gracious, golden hours,
And planting thorns, instead of fruits and flowers.

This dismal wind would tell my doubting soul
That my life's measure has been void and vain;
But when I mark that quiet, steadfast ray,
That still through storm pursues its silent way,
Fresh courage comes, and I would ask again,
That I may live to clothe in warmth and light,
The purpose, born of doubt and pain to-night.

	MILLICENT.
Emile Wells


[Column 1]

"BY THEIR FRUITS YE SHALL
KNOW THEM."

	Having heard an article read which was
published in the Woman's Journal against
"Plural Marriage," and misrepresenting
things generally, I wish, through the col-
umns of the EXPONENT, to give my sisters
a word of caution, that they may not be de-
ceived by those that appear in sheep's cloth-
ing, (speaking scripturally,) but their object
is to ingratiate themselves into favor among
the families of the saints, for the purpose
of leading away the young, and those
that cannot discern the spirit they are of.
	The article referred to, was written by
a lady brought up among the Latter-day
Saints; and after she was more than thirty
years old, became of her own free will and
choice, the fourth wife of a man in "Plural
Marriage." She rejoiced in having found
her affinity, and remarked to her intimate
friends of the congeniality that existed be-
tween herself and husband. She was cer-
tainly old enough to choose for herself, and
was happy as a plural wife, as long as she
was "the petted lamb of the flock," (as she
styles herself,) but when her husband re-
membered he had other wives that requir-
ed his attention, she came to the conclu-
sion that Polygamy was not true, and took
up her pen to write against the principle;
still claiming a man for her husband who
had three wives, (and each of them child-

[Column 2]

ren) previous to her marriage, thereby ac-
knowledging herself a plural wife; how
inconsistent!
	She also makes the assertion that the
"Woman's Exponent" does not represent
the true sentiments of the "Women of
Utah," but is edited by Brigham Young
and Eliza R. Snow, a woman on the shady
side of seventy. Is not this an insult to
the Editors as well as to the women of Zion,
when we all know that it is edited and sup-
ported by women that are true Latter-day
saints both young and old.
	With regard to President Young as edit-
or, his high and exalted position as the
leader of so great a people, gives him plen-
ty to occupy his mind, time and attention
without editing papers. It is the same with
our beloved Sister Snow, who is held in the
highest esteem by both the men and wo-
men of this community. Although she is
seventy-three years of age, her hair is not
yet gray, and she can perform more labor,
mental and physical, than the lady who makes
her age an objection could do at forty. I am
writing matters of fact—truths as they ex-
ist, without the least prejudice or misrep-
resentation.
	I myself, heard the lady say "I cannot
give up the religion of my mother, taught
me from childhood;" yet she denies one
very important principle of that religion.
	If we believe baptism for the remission
of sins, the laying on of hands for the re-
ception of the Holy Ghost, and the ordi-
nances of the house of God, also the bap-
tism for the dead, the resurrection and 
eternal judgement, we must believe in Ce-
lestial Marriage for time and eternity. The
Lord revealed the principle of the ever-
lasting Gospel through the prophet Joseph
Smith for the salvation and exaltation of
the human family, if they will accept it
and live obedient to its requirements.
	We as Latter-day Saints cannot like the
Christain world, accept one portion of the
revealed word of God and reject the others;
neither can we serve God and Mammon,
but like Joshua of old we must choose whom
we will serve. Now is the time my sisters
to choose whether we will serve the Lord
and keep His commandments, or if we will
serve Baal and go after the follies of the
world. Many spirits are in our midst, let
us try the spirits by the law and the testi-
mony, if they speak not according to this
there is no truth in them. "By their fruits
ye shall know them," was the teaching of
our Lord and Savior Jesus Christ in His
day, so also can we know them in our day.
We can judge the spirits of people by
their conversation. Let us my sisters,
serve God and keep His commandments,
never associating with those who speak
against the Lord's anointed, or the princi-
ples of the Gospel, neither bid them wel-
come to our houses; if we do the spirit of
God will become grieved and our minds
will be darkened.
	This Anti-Polygamist is very anxious to
talk to our young people, and makes the
assertion if she could call a vote from the
young people, nine-tenths of them would
give their vote against Polygamy. I am
thankful to say in this she would be woful-
ly mistaken. I have no doubt she could

[Column 3]

find a number like herself, that have apos-
tatized from the truth, that would give her
their vote, but they would be but a small
minority.
	I fear I have made my article too long,
but I am so much interested in the welfare
of our young people, I would warn them
against the cunning craftiness of those that
are trying to deceive them and lead them
from the truth.
	AUNT MARY
S. L. City, March 5, 1877.


NOTES AND NEWS

	THE ASPECT of Vesuvius is now said to
be threatening and according to the most
reliable accounts there is a tendency to an
explosion, such as occur periodically. It is
authentically stated that up to the seven-
teenth century the number of eruptions
was less than four to each hundred years.
In the eighteenth century there were twen-
ty-three, and in this nineteenth century
twenty-six have already occurred. The
mountain is decidedly restless, there is a
subdued roar from the depths, which tell
of a surging sea of lava. 
	THE BOSTON "Woman's Journal" says
Sir George Campbell of England, recently
at a public banquet, in proposing the health
of the Queen, said he was not a Woman's
Rights man in the common acceptation, but
he wished the Salic law might be so
reversed in England that woman should
hereafter occupy the throne. The senti-
ment was received with applause, in which
two members of Parliament who were pres-
ent joined. 
	THERE IS a bright prospect now that the
statue designed to commemorate the first
Centennial anniversary of American inde-
pendence will be completed: the joint work
of the French and American people. The
statue will be the representation of the
Goddess of Liberty, and will be located at
Bedloe's Island, near New York harbor,
where it may be seen by all going out or
coming in from the great Metropolis of the
United States. The statue will be one hun-
dred feet in height, the pedestal eighty
feet. The goddess will wear a crown upon
her head from which will burst out beams
of light, illuminating at night the sky and 
all around. 
	FAMINE IN CHINA- England and Amer-
ica are called upon to aid in raising funds
to assist the Chinese in this their hour of
distress. "This fearful calamity has burst
out in the province of Shan Tung in the
northern part of China, but the greatest
distress is in the prefecture of Wu Ting Fu,
a densely populous part of the country,
where the crops have wholly failed." For
three months it is said the people there
have been living on the barks and roots of
trees. Multitudes are dying of starvation.
Many persons have taken their own lives,
and some parents have even buried their
children alive. Letters have been sent to
the merchants of Shanghai, Canton and
Hong Kong, soliciting help in the strong-
est terms. American Missionaries there
have suggested the most proper methods
of sending funds.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


R. S. REPORTS.

	The General Retrenchment Meeting held
in the Fourteenth Ward Assembly Rooms,
Saturday, March 3rd, was especially inter-
esting. The day was a very stormy one, but
all who were there seemed filled with that
good spirit, which cannot fail to make glad
the heart. There were very few minutes
from Junior Associations, and the time was
therefore devoted to speaking. The Presi-
dent, Mrs. M. I. Horne, gave some of her
experiences of many years since, and relat-
ed incidents of note in the life of Joseph
Smith, which had come under her direct
observation. Counselors Mrs. Z. D. Young,
Mrs. P. Woodruff, and B. W. Smith,
all spoke upon the duties and particular
mission of the sisters. Mrs. Woodruff
spoke of her anticipated visit to St. George,
and exhorted the sisters to faithfulness and
to ask the Lord continually for wisdom.
Mrs. E. B. Wells, Mrs. W. East, Mrs. L. A.
Wells and others spoke upon those subjects
which are immediately connected with the
practical work under the supervision of the
sisters, exhorted to caution and diligence,
and to beware of false spirits. President
Mrs. M. I. Horne sang a "song of Zion."
These meetings really seem to be spiritual
feasts for those who are seeking to unite
their efforts in assisting to bring about a
universal reform, and preparing for those
great events which are about to transpire.

		TOOELE CITY, Feb. 27, 1877.
EDITOR EXPONENT:

	I take the pleasure of communicating a
few of my thoughts to you, thinking that
you are always pleased to hear from your
sisters of the Retrenchment Association.
I attended a meeting of the Young Ladies'
on the 23 inst. and was very much edified.
They have changed their meetings from
the day time to the evening, in conse-
quence of many of the girls attending
school, and meet once a week. The Y. M.
M. I. Association and the Young Ladies'
Retrenchment Association will meet to-
gether once a month, when the minutes of
both Associations of the past three meet-
ing will be held on the second of March
when we anticipate a good time. Our new
Social Hall has been so far completed so as
to admit of dancing in it; and we have had
quite a number of good dances and one
concert in it which have proved entirely
successful. We expect to have another
concert on the fifth of March, which will be
for the benefit of the hall. We cannot say
like some of your correspondents, that we
have had no round dancing since sisters E.
R. Snow, Zina D. Young, M. I. Horne and
E. Howard organized us a little over two
years ago; but I am happy to say that a few
of our sisters have kept the resolutions
sacred; not because they did not enjoy
these pleasures, but because they wished to
obey the counsel of those who are older and
wiser, that they might receive the blessing
which always follows obedience. We as a
Retrenchment Association have commenced
Storing up Grain and we hope to go on in
this good work and cast in our mites with
our sisters who are engaged in preparing
for a day of want. We have had a great
amount of good instructions from our breth-
ren and sisters of late, with regard to the
"signs of the times." I believe that we
fully appreciate the blessings and privileges
with which we are surrounded.
	Hoping that the daughters of Zion may
become more efficient helps in the kingdom
of God, is the humble desire of your sister
in the Gospel of peace.
							M. L. L.
				Corresponding Secretary.

			SLATERVILLE, Feb. 25, '77.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	The Relief Society of this place meet
regularly and a good spirit prevails; we en-
joy ourselves very much, and often receive
encouraging instruction from the brethren
who frequently visit our meetings. The
sisters take a lively interest in the perform-
ance of their duties knowing that much
good can be accomplished if their labors
are applied in the right direction, putting
their trust in God. I will not weary you
with a lengthy article at present, but per-
haps you may hear from me again in the
future. I will now conclude.
	Your sister and co-worker,
						MARY FIELD.
	PARIS, BEAR LAKE Co., March 1, '77.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	Our society is in a prosperous condition,
the members are interested in their duties
and feel determined to do all in their pow-
er to forward the good cause in which they
are engaged. I would say in relation to the
Grain Question, we are not over stocked
with that commodity in this northern re-
gion, owing to occasional frosts before our
crops mature; although as a general thing
enough is raised, and some to spare, of
good sound wheat, to furnish bread for the
community. The society has a small
amount on hand, and intend adding to that
little as fast as our limited means will al-
low, and with the help of our Heavenly
Father we will exert ourselves and secure
all the grain we can to store for future emer-
gencies, knowing as we do so that it is the
wish of our beloved President, that wheat
should be stored up for a time of need.
Pres't. C. C. Rich. Bishop Wm. Budge, and
others of the brethren, have from time to
time given us good counsel with regard to
this matter, and if we put in practice the
counsel of good men, we know we are in
the line of our duty.
	Taking into consideration the standing
of the society financially, we have dona-
ted liberally to the Temple Fund, and wish
we had been able to have done more. The
past winter has been unusually mild for this
part of the country; not more than from
eight to ten inches of snow has fallen at
one time during the season. We are now
blessed with delightful weather, and from
all appearances can commence ploughing
and putting in crops much earlier than
heretofore. Peace prevails in this settle-
ment with fair prospects of a happy future.
Hoping that we may realize our anticipa-
tions, and ever be found diligent servants
in aiding and assisting to move forward
the Lord's great work, and at last be found
worthy of the reward promised to the faith-
ful.
	I am your sister etc.
				Alice M. Rich, Sec'y.
	ANOTHER LITERARY INCORRIGBILE.—
Gustave Planche, the distinguished French
reviewer, read so much that he had seldom
time to wash his hands. One day, howev-
er, a lady with whom he was engaged to
dine succeeded in persuading him to ac-
cept a ticket for a warm bath. He took the
bath, but presented himself at dinner with
his hands still unwashed. During his im-
mersion he had read incessantly, and, hold
ing the book in his hands, had not once
dipped them in the water.

	THE SISTER.—No household is complete
without a sister. She gives the finish to
the family. A sister's love, a sister's influ-
ence—what can be more hallowed?. A sis-
ter's watchful care—can anything be more
tender? A sister's kindness—does the
world show us anything purer? Who would
live without a sister? A sister is a sort of
guardian angel in the home-circle. Her
presence condemns vice. She is the quick-
ener of good resolutions, the sunshine in the
pathway of home. To every brother she
is light and life. Her heart is the treasure-
house of confidence. In her he finds a safe
adviser, a charitable, forgiving, tender,
though often severe friend. In her he
finds a ready companion. Her sympathy
is as open as day and as sweet as the fra-
grance of flowers. We pity the brother
who has no sister, no sister's love. We feel
sorry for the home which is not enlivened by
a sisters presence. A sisters office is a no-
ble and gentle one. It is hers to persuade
to virtue, to win to wisdom's ways; gently
to lead where duty calls; to guard the
citadel of home with the sleepless vig-
ilance of virtue; to gather graces and strew
flowers around the home-altar. To be a sis-
ter is to hold a sweet place in the heart of
home. To minister in a holy office.


H﻿OW TO CALCULATE INTEREST,.
AND WHAT IT WILL DO.

	The following rules are so simple and so
true according to all business usuages, that
every banker, broker, merchant or clerk
should post them up for reference. There
being no such thing as a fraction in it, there
is scarcely any liability to error or mistake.
By no other arithmetical process can the de-
sired information be obtained by so few fig-
uers.
	Six per cent.—Multiply any given num-
ber of dollars by the number of days of in-
terest desired, separate the right hand fig-
ure and divide by six, the result is the true
interest on such sdum for such number of
days at six per cent.
	Eight per cent—-Multiply any given
amount by the number of days upon which
it is desired to ascertain the interest, and
divide by forty-five and the result will be
the interest of such for the time required
at eight per cent.
 Ten per cent.—Multiply the same as
above, and divide by thirty-six, and the re-
sult will show the rate of interest at ten
per cent.
	What it will do.—If a mechanic or clerk
saves only 2 cents per day from the time
he is twenty-one until he is three score and
ten, the aggregate with interest will
amount to $2,900; and a daily saving of
27 1/2 cents reaches the important sum of
$29,00.
	A sixpence saved daily will provide a
fund of nearly $7,00—sufficient to purchase
a good farm. There are few employees
who cannot save daily, by abstaining from
the use of cigars tobacco, liquor etc., twice or
ten times the amount of the six cent piece.
Every person should provide for old age,
and the man in business who can lay away
a dollar a day will eventually find himself
possessed of over $100,000.


AN Irish lover remarks, "It's a very
great pleasure to be alone, especially when
yer swat heart is wid ye."


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


WASHINGTON.

Land of the West! though passing brief the record of 
	"thine age,
Thou hast a name that darkens all on history's wide page!
Let all the blasts of fame ring out--thine shall be loudest
		far;
Let others boast their satellites--thou hast the planet star.
Thou hast a name whose characters of light shall ne'er de-
		part; 
'Tis stamped upon the dullest brain, and warms the cold-
		est heart;
A war-cry fit for any land where freedom's to be won.
Land of the west! it stands alone--it is thy Washington!
Rome had its Ceasar, great and brave; but stain was on
		his wreath -
He lived the heartless conqueror and died the tyrant's
		death.
France had its eagle; but its wings, though lofty they
		might soar,
Were spread in false ambition's flight, and dipped in mur-
		der's gore.
Those Hero gods, whose mighty sway would fain have
		chained the waves--
Who flashed their blades with tiger zeal, to make a world
		of slaves -
Who, though their kindred barred the path, still fiercely
		waded on;
Oh! where shall be their "glory" by the side of Washing-
		ton?
He fought but not with love of strife; he struck but to de-
		fend;
And ere he turned a people's foe; he sought to be a friend.
He strove to keep his country's right by reason's gentle
		word,
And sighed when fell injustice threw the challenge sword
		to sword.
He stood the firm, the calm, the wise, the patriot and
		sage;
He showed no deep, avenging hate--no burst of despot
		rage.
He stood for liberty and truth, and dauntlessly led on,
Till shouts of victory gave forth the name of Washington.
No ear of triumph bore him through a city filled with
		grief.
No groaning captives at the wheels proclaimed him victor
		chief;
He broke the gyves of slavery with strong and high dis-
		dain;
And cast no sceptre from the links when he had crushed
		the chain.
	He saved his land, but did not lay his soldier trappings
		down
To change them for the regal vest, and don a kingly
		crown.
Fame was too earnest in her joy--too proud of such a son,
To let a robe and title mask a noble Washington.
England, my heart is truly thine - my loved, my native
earth!
The land that holds a mother's grave, and gave that
mother birth!
Oh, keenly sad would be the fate, that thrust me from thy
		shore,
And faltering my breath, that sighed, "Farewell forever
		more!"
But did I meet such adverse lot, I would not seek to dwell
Where olden heroes wrought the deeds for Homer's song
		to tell.
Away, thou gallant ship! I'd cry, and bear me swiftly on,
But bear me from my own fair land to that of Washing-
ton.
										ELIZA COOK.


REMEMBRANCES.

	       BY BLANCHE BEECHWOOD.

		"That heart methinks
Were of strange mould which kept no cherished print
Of earlier, happier times, when life was fresh,
And love and innocence made holiday." 
	If there is any theme that never grows
monotonous, and of which we can always
call forth fresh thoughts, and tender recol-
lections it is childhood.  It is a subject that
touches the strongest hearts, and never
fails to bring some pleasant remembrance
to the most cold and barren mind. "When
I was a child," says the happy mother, the
fond father, the aged grand-parents, the
good aunts and uncles; and how they love
to take the little children in their arms and
tell them of their own child-life.  Always
some, reminiscences of "earlier happier
times" that send the life-current rushing
swifter through the veins, or make the
heart-strings ring out the echoing music of
the pleasures of the past.
 It is good to keep a living spring from
the fountain of one's childhood open,
through all future life, for it refreshes the
wanderer upon life's journey; and in many
barren, desolate places, it revives the droop-
ing spirit, and reanimates the soul with the
hopes and aspirations that were erst an in-
centive to exertion, and like the gentle
soothing zephyrs from afar renew and in-
vigorate the tired energies, and act as an
impetus to force us to the accomplishment
of the resolves with which we armed our-
selves in the spring time of life.
 Many of the greatest men and women
date back to early impressions and influen-
ces the origin of the plans and well laid
schemes that have worked out for them
places of distinction in the ranks of wealth
or fame.
 Children can never be too happy, the hap-
pier you make them, the richer, and purer
will be the fountains of their thoughts, and
the growth of high and holy feelings de-
pend in a great measure upon the influen-
ces and surroundings of one's childhood-
I do not allude to wealth, or indulgences
that are improper, for the simplest enjoy-
ments are by far the sweetest to a child.
Broken dishes, and rag-dolls, and the cast-
off and worn-out finery of grandmothers,
which abound in the garrets of old-fash-
ioned homes, made the children as happy,
and more satisfied, than the grand wax-
dolls with real hair, and eyes that open
and shut, and the elegant baby tea and din-
ner sets, which mama is so afraid the child-
ren will spoil because they cost so much
money. In this age of splendor and mag-
nificence, in the homes of the rich, every-
thing is too costly for children, and their
lives are hampered and little childish im-
pulses pent up and circumscribed by the
rules of fashionable life. "Don't make a
play house of mother's room," how often
have I heard this; how much nicer child-
ren think it is to play in mother's room
than almost anywhere in the world, and
many a mother who feels too nervous and
tired to let her children play there, and 
tired to let her children play there, and 
sends them out to drink in of other influ-
ences, would in after years give all she pos-
sessed, to have kept under her own eye
those precious ones, now gone perhaps, or
imbued with ideas which sting to the very
soul that mother, whose nerves could not
endure the noise and meriment of her own
little ones. The mother's room is the dear-
est place on earth to the little child; let
them play there, let them come there to
pour out all their little griefs and sorrows;
and as they grow older, do not send them
into the parlor or some cold formal place to
tell their joys or sorrows; but in mother's
OWN room, with the freedom of childhood
give them the full confidence of unselfish
affection.
 No matter how homely or poor the "moth-
er's room" was, the heart will instinctively
turn back to it in manhood, or womanhood,
and with that feeling of unfeigned love and
tenderness which lives forever, which is
immortal within the soul, imprinted in-
deliby in memory. In the long, weary
journey of a changeful life, there are times
and places, hours of such stolid darkness
that one has scarcely courage to look for-
ward, but it is always possible to look back
and to recall some pleasureable emotions
that bring into the heart the sunshine of 
the past, and make one feel that down in
the depths of the soul there lives a green
and precious memory of some loved or 
cherished thing which time can never
dim, or trouble destroy.
"Let the dead past bury its dead" is a
poetical phrase, and one which has become
proverbial, but there is also a "living past"
which we cannot annul, and of which a
record has been kept, and the entries made
there are in imperishable characters. That
which is good and true should live forever,
should bloom perpetually.
 No doubt locked away in the annals of
the past, are the keys which must unlock
the portals of the future, and from the ashes
of buried remembrances, of good deeds
and noble acts, there will eventually arise an
incense which will shed its fragrance
through all the eternal future. Thus will
the "living past" be all powerful to ensure
an entrance through the "golden gate" into
the Paradise of happiness. "Except ye be
as little children" were the words of our
blessed Savior; then is it not meet to pre-
serve some of the simplicity and guileless-
ness of childhood, keeping fresh and pure
the fountains of truth and recalling occa-
sionally to our remembrance the innocent
trust and implicit faith of the days "Lang
Syne?"
	S.L. City, March, 9, 1877.


CORRESPONDENCE. 

MORGAN, MORGAN Co. Feb. 22, '77
EDITOR EXPONENT
	Thinking a few items in relation to this
little settlement might be interesting to
some of your many readers; and feeling a
desire to contribute for the benefit of my
fellow beings, I ask a little space in the col-
umns of your paper. The Exponent is al-
ways a welcome visitor with me; it is fraught
with such wise counsel that has a tendency
to elevate and not degrade the human
family.
	We have had but very little snow in this
vicinity, but a great deal of sickness, es-
pecially among the children.  Diptheria
has seemed to be the epidemic; this winter
nearly every family have had more or less
of it and quite a number have died from its
effects.  For the benefit of your readers I
will give a remedy which has proved ef-
fectual whenever it has been persevered in,
especially when not accompanied by any
other disease.  On the first symptoms,
which is pains in the head and sore throat,
give a warm bath, administering cayenne
pepper in cream from one quarter to half
teaspoonful doses, every two or three hours
and apply to the throat a large poultice of
raw scraped potatoes, which should be
changed at least every half hour; admin-
ster no patent medicines, as cayenne in
such large doses generally operates suffi-
ciently on the bowels.  The cayenne when
mixed in cream can be administered to
very small children.
	The croup is another very fatal disease if
not taken in time, but if a mother will
keep on hand some common Scotch snuff,
and on the appearance of croup, saturate
well a piece of thin muslin and cover it
with plenty of the snuff and apply one
piece to the throat and another to the chest,
they need not lose their children by croup,
that is if it is done in time.
	Wishing the sisters success in every en-
terprise that tends to the salvation of our 
race, I remain your sister in the Gospel.
				HARRIET WELSH

a beautiful young lady to a gallant officer.
"No wonder, madam," he replied "since
you do so much execution unmasked."
