SALT LAKE CITY, MARCH 15, 1877.


GENUINE WORK.
 There is an innate satisfaction to be found
in the performance of real labor, mental or
physical. There is a variety of work in this
busy world and there are many ways and
means by which success in these different de-
partments is achieved.  Duty must be one in-
centive in the great labor of life, especially
when persons are brought into circumstances
which place upon them responsibilities and ob-
ligations that are uncongenial and arduous,
perhaps positively distasteful.  To do this sort
of work well, is good discipline, for it must be
entered upon so ardently and be done so thor-
oughly to ensure success, that in all probabil-
ity the desire to accomplish it effectually will
lend a force to action, and the very ardor of
the pursuit will give vigor and inspiration.
Hope of recompense is not really the best so-
lace for arduous struggling and earnest endeav-
or.  It should not in any wise be looked upon
as the main object.  If any occupation is good
enough to warrant its being undertaken at all,
it should be dealt with in the very best manner
possible and with the best grace.  Take into
consideration all the possibilities of disappoint-
ments, and then determine if possible, by skill
and perseverance to withstand them or ward
them off, and to overcome every obstacle and
hindrance.  Bring all the powers of action to
bear, and put forth the best efforts for continued
exertion, and work steadily, diligently and
faithfully. Work, constant and earnest is the
best antidote for melancholy and many other
evils which beset mankind.  Moods and ineli-
nations are but poor lame guides.  Waiting for
Proivdence or for fate to mark out some plan
for future usefulness is not good policy.  Oc-
casionally, perhaps there are strange interpo-
sitions, but man is his own architect, usually,
and can carve out his own future to a greater
or less degree according to his capacity for gen-
uine usefulness, and his untiring diligence and
faithfulness.
 In the business of life there is an immense
amount of satisfaction just from conquering
difficulties; and another pleasure consists in
discharging real obligations.  "If your heart
is in your work" is an old adage, but to throw
your heart into any sort of work you may
have to do, any clear perceptible line of duty,
would help to make the labor pleasant and  en-
tered upon by men with reluctance through
unavoidable circumstances, or contingencies and
real triumph achieved by vigorous, steady ef-
forts making that reluctance respond to the
will which resolves to succeed.
 Work is the universal mission of all, the
Savior himself inculcated this principle in all
His teachings; and employment for old and


young is a safeguard against many evils and
temptations.
	When the utmost has been done to attain a
certain point, or achieve a special success, or
solve some difficult problem, then do not go to
regretting and bemoaning if you fail, but be
sure the failure has come through something
forgotten or overlooked, something which had
not been pre-considered.  Learn to profit by
by such failures they may prove more useful
lessons in life than the most complete and sig-
nal success, for they may prepare and discip-
line the faculties and energies to accomplish
some more difficult and nobler work.
E. B. Wells


HOME AFFAIRS.

HON. GEORGE Q. Cannon arrived in this
city Tuesday evening, March 13, in good health
and spirits.  Several of the brethren went to
Ogden to meet him.
IN THE obituary of Mrs. Martha A. Murray
who died in Wellsville, a mistake occurred in
stating the year of her marriage, it should
have read 1876, instead of 1877.
ANY PERSONS writing to us under assumed
signatures, must be sure and give the Editor
their real names otherwise their articles will
not be published.
JARED O. PRATT, son of Elder Orson Pratt
of this city, and another young man named
Mathew Ingram, were accidently killed by a 
snowslide which occurred in Big Cottonwood
canon on Sunday last.
 THE NEW Secretary for Utah, Hon. Levi P.
Luckey, with his family recently arrived in
this city.  We hope his position here may be
as propitious as his name would suggest.
WE HAVE reports from many diferent Re
lief Societies and Retrenchment Associations
which we have not space for now, but wil
publish as fast as practicable.  We have also
correspondence, poetry, essays, etc. which will
all come in place in due time.

 IN THE last number of the EXPONENT, in
the minutes handed us for publication from
the Eighth Ward, a mistake was made in call-
ing Royal B. Young the Bishop's counselor.
Elder R. B. Young is a home missionary but
not counselor to Bishop Sheets.

 MRS. S. K. GREENE writes from Springdale,
Kane County, under date of March 5th:  The
weather is warm and beautiful; the almond
and apricot trees are all in bloom, and nature's
aspects are pleasant and delightful.  The peo-
ple feel to praise God for His abundant mer-
cies, and to rejoice in His blessings so richly
bestowed upon them.
 TO-DAY, President John W. Young and
some of the Twelve Apostles, started on
their journey southward, expecting to preach
in some of the southern settlements upon the
route to St. George, and to arrive at that city
in time to be present at the General Confer-
ence and Dedication of the Temple, which
will take place on the 6th of April,
 MISS E. R. SNOW and Mrs. Zina D. Young
paid a visit to Ogden on Tuesday, March 13th,
to be present at the annual meeting of the
Young Ladies Retrenchment Association. The
assembly was a very large one and the spirit
of God seemed especially manifested on the
occasion. The members seem earnest in their
desire for progression and advancement in all
things and on all subjects which pertain to the
building up of the kingdom of God.  They are
exerting a powerful influence in the cause of
reform. They will probably favor us with a
full report of their proceedings at their meet-
ing, for publication.
 THE PRESIDENTIAL question has been at
last settled and if not altogether satisfactorily,
yet the commotion and excitement  the sub-
ject seems to have subsided for a time, and
toned down to a sort of quietude.  It remains
for the future to determine what will be the re-
sult of the decision of the election.  At any
rate there is a lull, and the people have been
answered in their cry, "Let us have peace."
The Scriptures say "they shall cry peace,
peace, when there is no peace."  The country
is looking forward for a policy so settled and
equitable that its results will bring prosperity.
The people are asking for their burdens to be
lightened, currency restored, and so on, but
even the man on whom rests the heaviest re-
sponsibilities cannot do impossiblities, nor
succeed in effecting any public reform unless
he is properly sustained by those who are his 
co-workers.
 MADAME LE VERT who died recently and of
whose death we were apprised through the
"Deseret News" of last evening, was one of
those genial, pleasant, amiable women, whom
to see is to love.  Her Souvenirs of Travel is 
one of the best descriptions of country and
society on the continent we have ever read.
She was a woman well known in what the
world terms the best society.  Although she
was advanced in years at the time of her short
sojourn in Salt Lake City, yet she was charm-
ing in appearance, manners and conversation.
She was, during one period of her life, consid-
ered the most popular woman in the United
States. She was much impressed by the exer-
tions being made here by the Women of Utah
to become self-sustaining, although she was
not one of the advocates of woman's rights
among the circle in which she moved.
THE YOUNG ladies of the Twentieth Ward
Retrenchment Association had a social gath-
ering in the ward school-house on Tuesday
evening Feb. 27th. In the course of the even-
ing the president of the Association, Mrs.
Sarah Howard, became the recipient of a very
unexpected presentation.  It was her birth-
day and the secretary read an address written
for the occasion, expressing the feelings of the
members of the association, and begging her
acceptance of a birthday gift, as a small token
of their esteem and appreciation of her efforts
in their behalf.  One of the young ladies then
presented the gift  a very neat ladies work box.
So perfect was the surprise to Mrs. Howard
that she was very much impressed with such
a manifestation of affection from the young
ladies.  The evening passed off pleasantly and
was one long to be remembered, especially by
the president who has won the love and confi-
dence of her young friends and associates.
May she live many years to instruct them in
the principles of the Gospel, and that they may
ever appreciate her kind teaching and worthy
example is our earnest wish.
 SERICULTURE.--The Deseret Silk Associa-
tion met in the Fireman's Hall on the 7th
inst., Mrs. Zina D. Young presiding.
	Mrs. Young reported that the instructions on
silk raising were ready for the press and would
be printed by the time of holding our next
meeting.  She advised the members to plant
out trees and cuttings, and to use their influ-
ence to have others do so; expected to have all
the society silk reeled by the time of next
meeting.
 Very interesting remarks were also made by
Sisters Horne and Barney and others present.
Mr. P. A. Schettler said he felt greatly in-


terested in what had been said, and felt en-
couraged in what had already been done,
which was proof that all we needed was for
the people to take hold of this enterprize to
make it a success: to plant out trees and raise
the cocoons, and they could be worked right
here, and thus be giving employment to a
great many people. As soon as his trees could
be removed he would advertise in the papers.
He wished those who wanted eggs to let him
know as soon as possible. 
	Mr. P. A. Schettler and Mr. A. C. Pyper
were appointed a committee to see to the pub-
lication of the pamphlet on silk culture.
	Motioned that we change our meetings from
Wednesday until the first Friday in the month.
	Meeting adjourned until the first Friday in
April at 3 p. m.
						R. SIMPSON, Sec'y.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.		157


A PARAGRAPH FROM THE HIS-
			TORY OF JOSEPH SMITH.

		NAUVOO, ILL., Thursday, March 24, 1842.
	I attended, by request, the Relief Society 
whose object is the relief of the poor, the des-
titute, the widow and the orphan, and for the
exercise of all benevolent purposes. Mrs. Em-
ma Smith has taked the presidential chair;
Mrs. Elizabeth Ann Whitney, and Mrs. Sarah
M. Cleveland are her counselors; Miss Elvira
Cole, is treasurer, and our well-known and tal-
ented poetes Miss Eliza R. Snow, secretary. 
There was a very numerous attendance at the 
organization, of some of our most intelligent, 
humane, philanthropic and respectable ladies;
and we are well assured from a knowledge of
those pure principles of benevolence that flow
spontaneously from their humane and philan-
thropic bosoms, that with the resources they
will have at command, they will pour in oil and 
wine to the wounded heart of the distressed;
they will dry up the tears of the orphan, and
make the widow's heart to rejoice. Our la-
dies have always been signalized for their acts 
of benevolence and kindness; but the cruel
usage they have received from the barbarians
of Missouri, has hitherto prevented their ex-
tending the hand of charity in a conspicuous
manner; yet in the midst of their persecutions,
when the bread has been torn from their help-
less offspring but their cruel oppressors, they 
have always been ready to open their doors to  
the weary traveler, to divide their scanty pit-
tance with the hungry, and from their robbed
and impoverished wardrobes, to divide with 
the more needy and destitute; and now that 
they are living in a more genial soil, and
among a less barbarous people, and possess fa-
cilities that they have not heretofore enjoyed,
we feel convinced that with their concentrated
efforts, the condition of the suffering poor, of
the stranger and the fatherless will be amelior-
ated. We had the privilege of being present 
at their organization and were much pleased 
with their "modus operandi," and the good or-
der that prevailed. They are strictly parlia-
mentary in their procedings.


MINERSVILLE, BEAVER, Co.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	By request of our President, Polly Ann Ze-
briskie, I address you, to inform you that we
are still trying to honor our calling as a branch
of the Relief Society, and to do all in our pow-
er to assist the needy. We are one with our
sisters in Utah to follow out the counsel of our
honored President Brigham Young, with re-


gard to storing up grain. Our Bishop, James
McKnight, gave us the privilege of using part
of the Tithing-house, where we have had a bin
made, and commenced storing up grain
against a time of need. On the 14 of January
the sisters met and had a sewing bee and pic-
nic. The supper was tastefully arranged and
the bounties of nature supplied in abundance.
We held our annual meeting and transacted
business on Feb. 22; the books showed our so-
ciety to be in a prosperous condition. Our
Bishop attended the meeting, and gave us 
much valuable instruction pertaining to our
duties.
				P. A. ZEBRISKIE, Pres't.
						S. ROBERTS, Sec'y.


TO THE YOUNG LADIES SOCIETY.

	Sisters, I have not been as punctual in at-
tending meeting lately, as I would have
liked, circumstances having prevented me;
but my thoughts are ever with this society;
in my secret prayers I ask God to bless us,
to inspire us with a sense of our religion,
that we may interest ourselves in the pro-
gress of this glorious work. How many of
us realize the importance of studying our
religion, of seeking a knowledge for our-
selves that this is the everlasting gospel and
only true gospel on the face of the earth.
Not many, I’m afraid. Should we know
this? certainly we shonld; would you be-
lieve anything without knowing for your-
self whether it were true or not? One
might say why do you have to know it, let
me ask you what is the use of believing in
God who created all things, and what is the
use of asking him for his blessings if we
do not believe in him. But I take it for
granted that we all believe in a God, that
none of us are skeptical; and believing in
God, we are willing to obey him to the best
of our knowledge, even if that knowledge
be limited. Then my sisters it behooves us
to seek a knowledge for ourselves concern-
ing God’s will so that we can perform it to
his satisfaction; how are we going to know
his will otherwise; he has said, “If any
man lack wisdom let him ask of God who
giveth to all men liberally and upbraideth
not." He has also said "Ask and it shall
be given unto you, seek and ye shall find."
His word is unchangeable. Let us ask him
to show us things that we are greatly in
need of to-day, for did we all know for our-
selves that Mormonism, as it is termed, was
true, we would, I am certain, do more for
its progression than we have done hereto-
fore.
	We are called Daughters of Zion, are we
worthy the name? I think we have done
but little to be worthy so honorable a title.
I for one feel ashamed to think that l have
wasted the best of my days in idleness,
but now feel to thank God that I have
awakened to a sense of my duty. God never
placed any of us on the earth to grovel
along from day to day and never rise above
the level, and some hardly ever get that
far, all through their own carelessness. It
is our privilege to become wise and honor-
able women, and we will be, provided we
set our mark high, and work with a will to
attain it. Some might laugh at the idea,
thinking that we have great men enough
without having great women, but this is
not so, for who with any principle about
them whatever, will despise a noble-mind-
ed, good principled woman, and these
things constitute greatness; she will be a
blessing to any man who may chance to get
her.

[Column 2]
	We as girls do not wish to get ahead of
the boys, not at all, we only wish to be able
to help them that's all, do you think they
will dislike us for this? they would not be
looking at their own interest if they did.
Now my young sisters this society is called
the Young Ladies' Retrenchment Society,
do we fully understand the meaning of the
word Retrenchment, I think we do, but we
do not know altogether what to retrench
from; some people think it is from dress,
they are right, that is one theme, a small
one, but one of interest. We are told it is
from our ignorance and all evil ways, but
some people have got the idea that it is
dress and dress alone, and you will often
hear it said that the society is of no good
for the girls dress worse now than they ev-
er did; but this is not so for there is no girl
who attends these meetings regularly but
who tries to do better both as regards her
dress and all things that pertain to living a
good life. There are some few in Manti
who are a little extravagant in dress, are
they members of the Society? if they are
you very seldom find them at meeting, is
this the case with us? no we come here
feeling happy, feeling that we learn some-
thing, and that henceforth we will do all
the good we can and all in our power for
the advancement of the kingdom of God
and for our own improvement.
	These are my resolutions and I feel that
the other sisters feel the same, and that we
may continue to meet together and gain
knowledge, wisdom, and understanding,
and work with a will, so that in the end we
will be worthy to be called the Daughters
of Zion is my prayer.
							M. T.
	Manti, Feb. 19, 1877.


ITEMS ABOUT STORING GRAIN.

						March 3, 1877.

EDITOR EXPONENT:

	In accordance with your wishes I write
to inform you that the Relief Society of
this place have responded to the call in re-
lation to storing grain. A good spirit pre-
vails among the sisters, and all feel willing
to carry out the counsel of President Youug,
in regard to that subject. Our Bishop has
kindly offered us a place in the Tithing
Office to carefully store away our grain,
which we have gladly accepted. The sis-
ters have liberally given according to their
circumstances; and we have at present,
nearly one hundred bushels of wheat on
hand. The brethren here have a good
United Order farm, and the Bishop has
promised to give the Relief Socifety an in-
terest in it; so we hope that by the help of
our brethren, we will be able to add more
to our stock from year to year. Our be-
loved President, Mrs. E. A. Willie is ear-
nest in exhorting the sisters to become unit-
ed, and to live their religion in the truest
sense and spirit. Your excellent paper is
read with great interest, and the best wish-
es are expressed for the advancement of
every good work for we know that those
who keep the commandments of God will
receive His blessings, and those who do
not will have His judgments upon them.
	May God bless you and all who are striv-
ing to do right, is the prayer of your sister
in the Gospel.
						J. A. HUGHES.
	Mendon, Cache Co., Utah.


158		WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
			WOMAN'S VOICE.

EDITORS EXPONENT:
	A feeling of interest and a desire to do
my part with the rest of my sisters prompts
me to write a few of my feelings. Often
when meditating I have asked myself this
question, what can I do to forward the
work of God? For I have no greater desire
than to do my part and be classed with the
noble handmaids of the Almighty. When
swallowed up in the work and cares of my
household I found but little time or oppor-
tunity to go out, except on the Sabbath to
meeting, where I would hear that which
comforted and stimulated me to endure all
trials, and to perform my duties as a wife
and mother. When I was weary and my
spirits would begin to lag, I was cheered
with the thought that the Sabbath day was
drawing near, and I could again have the
privilege of listening to the servants of God,
where I knew that I would be again re-
newed to press forward.
	I have sometimes felt a little conscience-
stricken when I thought that perhaps I could
have done more, but there is no one but
mothers who know or can ever imagine the
mother's complete responsibilities; and we
are not judges of each other. A mother's
work is never done as long as her strength
holds out. When she can go no longer
Providence provides some other way; at
least, this has been my experience. It has
been my study for years to try to the best
of my ability and all the knowledge I could
gain, to train up my children in the right
way, and arrive at the best method to lead
and manage each one according to their dif-
ferent dispositions, and also how to make
the best use of what little talent God has
given me to improve upon. Since my
health has been delicate I have been going
through a pretty hard school, but I feel to
acknowledge the hand of God in all my
trials, for I can now look back and see that
I have learned a great many useful lessons;
and I thank the Lord for them as much or
more than I would had my path been more
easy and my burdens been more light, for I
have learned the worthlessness of all things
that are not controlled by the Holy Spirit.
My prayers has been continually that God
would lead me to do His will, and I feel an
inward admonition that I am now doing
just what he wants me to do to prepare my-
self, as well as those he has put under my
charge for a work which will in the future
do much more towards rolling on His cause
than I could do anywhere else. I have
never felt that I should make any great
noise in the world. The Lord intended each
one to fill a certain niche, and I know of
none at present that I could fill outside of
my own family circle. Here I find enough
to do in trying to lead and influence those
around me to live their religion, as well as
other duties which I am required to per-
form, and if I can enjoy the fruits of my
labor my joy will be very great. A little
work done well is much more worthy of
praise than a great deal badly done, and I
believe that when the Lord has something
more for me to do that it will be made
known to me, because I stand ready and
willing to do anything that he wills for me
to do. I am seeking to remove prejudice
by writing letters, and sending our papers
to my friends and kindred in the East and
West, and warning them of the terrible ca-
lamities which will overtake them like a
thief in the night, telling them that all who
will not take up sword against their neigh-
bors must needs flee to Zion for safety.
	Last winter after being sometime sick and
low-spirited, I was administered to by Sis-

[Column 2]
ters E. R. Snow and M. T. Smoot; my
health and spirits revived. I commenced
writing sketches of my own history, and I
was blessed in doing it; I was interested,
and never while occupied in that way was I
lonesome; I forgot the things around me,
so far as the world was concerned, I was
carried back, was living my life over again,
as it were, some portions of which never
failed to bring tears to my eyes, and other
scenes brought sweetest joy. This winter
I have been requested to furnish my moth-
er's life, or experience in this church, for
the Woman's Book. I have labored care-
fully and diligently in trying to remember
and gather up the most interesting items.
	I wish my sisters to know I am wide
awake in the spirit of the Gospel, and my
spirits have not been as buoyant for years
past as they have within the few past
months.
	There is a very important work for moth-
ers to do—to watch and teach their children,
especially daughters, that they do not be-
come lifted up in their hearts above other
young sisters, which is too prevalent among
the daughters of Zion. I see so much of it
that I believe I should take it for my first
text if I was a public speaker; but I can at
least preach from it at home, as I have ever
done, and I would feel very sorry if one of
my daughters should forget herself so far
as to feel above speaking or associating with
any that she had ever known who were re-
spectable. One Latter-day Saint is just as
good as another. It is the poor and the
meek who have the promise, and I wish to
be with those who will be considered worthy
when the Lord makes up his jewels, and I
also wish to keep those that the Lord has
placed under my charge in remembrance of
these things, and of their own unworthi-
ness, instead of talking about others who
may be much better than themselves, al-
though SOCIETY has condemned them.
	The best are not yet perfect, "but charity
covereth a multitude of sins." Whosoever
exalteth himself shall be abased; and he
that humbleth himself shall be exalted."
Pride generally exposes a person to many
effronts, and humility is the surest method
of gaining respect, and how true it is,
that we must become as little children, be-
fore we can enter into our Father's king-
dom, and were he not more merciful than
we, to forgive, what hope could we have of
salvation? It is the privilege of each one
to live in a way that they can know and
understand the will of God concerning
themselves. I find that my path is becom-
ing smoother since I learned to acknowledge
His hand in all things, even in the most
common occurrences of life. I know that
if I cannot at first see and comprehend the
meaning that if I wait a little all will turn
out much more satisfactory to myself than
it could if I had planned it. If all could
come to this conclusion they would find out
the truth of Jesus' words when he said,
"My yoke is easy and my burden is light."
	That we may all become of one heart and
one mind is the prayer of your sister,
					H. M. WHITNEY


FAITH, HOPE, AND CHARITY.

	READ BEFORE THE YOUNG LADIES'
			MUTUAL IM. ASS'N. 

	The three cardinal virtues. The Scrip-
tures tell us that without faith it is impos-
sible to please God. Hope is the key that
unlocks the door of happiness. While
Charity, pale meek-eyed Charity, is the
greatest of the three.


In this short essay, I shall dwell but
briefly on either of these virtues. I think
I shall endeavor to relate to you an allegory,
as near as I can, in the ancient eastern
style.
	Faith Hope, and Charity, were one day
wending their way to earth, to visit its in-
habitants when their conversation turned
on their respective value to man. "I" said
Hope. "you will both cheerfully acknowl-
edge, am of the most consequence to re-
generate man. I cheer his fallen spirits and
raise his drooping head, whisper to him of
joys to come, and teach him to bear the
present in the glad anticipation of the fu-
ture."
	When Hope had ceased speaking, Faith
gently said, "you are indeed necessary to
the happiness of man. But am I not more
necessary to him? By my aid, he can dis-
cern the future, can work miracles, and
greatest of all, can see and converse with
his Maker face to face."
	"My sisters" said Charity "your words
are full of truth. But Hope, you with
your bright eyes, gleesome voice, and rosy
lips; and Faith you too, with your mighty
power, and quiet, peaceful smile, I envy
you not. We are all blessings, but should
man possess you both, and lack my gifts,
he cannot dwell in the presence of God.
	The angel who accompanied them, sped
back to heaven with the intelligence that
Charity would win the greatest crown.
	My story will serve to illustrate my
meaning. If I were to multiply words, I
could not convey to your minds a clearer
idea of the value of these separate attri-
butes. One might perhaps live with a very
small degree of Hope and Faith, but if en-
dowed with a large portion of Charity, he
is indeed blessed. His hope of happiness
in this life may be dead, his faith but suffi-
cient to enable him to rely on God, still if
he possesses Charity, he shall know the ex-
quisite pleasure of giving, be it much or
little; he will love all God's creature's; he
will receive kindness for he will be kind
to his fellow-men; he will never have an
enemy among those who love what is good,
for he will be the friend of all such. He
will relieve the widowed, cherish the or-
phaned, and succor the oppressed and
needy when in his power to do so. Peace
shall dwell in his habitation, and content-
ment reign in his heart.
							S. Y. D.
	St. George, Feb. 16, '77.


MY DEFENCE OF "MY PURPOSE."

	My dear sisters I think you wrong me,
you say that "my purpose" is a selfish one,
that my interest extends no further than
my fireside, that in my blind selfishness for
my own, I forget the world wide work so
much needed to be done, that I ignore all
the many avenues of usefulness opening
for woman for the elevation of herself, and
am content to settle down in the home cir-
cle as if in that was constituted the height
of woman's ambition and the extent of her
sphere.
	I do not suppose that my idea of ambi-
tion or of my particular sphere would sat-
isfy every one, nor do I doubt but there are
very many ways of doing good within the
reach of all even if they do not engage in
any world-wide efforts for the common and
universal good. Certain it is that if our
practice does not accord with our theory, our
example and influence in our home circle
will not be very beneficial. Suppose I
should spend my time in passing around
from house to house consoling, exhorting


and administering to the wants of others,
while my own home is untidy and disor-
derly, things allowed to go to waste for
want of care, my children running around
neglected, growing wilder and more wick-
ed every day, really, sisters I cannot see
that this is the way for me to accomplish
the most good. It seems to me like passing
by the substance to grasp the shadow. Pa-
tiently the coral insects add atom after atom,
and mite after mite to the structure they
are rearing, until after perhaps thousands
of years it appears above the surface of the
great waters, affording homes and susten-
ance for millions of animal nature. So will
every true woman add her mite to the com-
mon stock for the common good; no matter
how humble her sphere or how small her
anticipations of success; not by overlooking
the ways and means at her feet, nor by
stepping over opportunities within her
reach, of far more certain fruition of good
results, to grasp after something away off,
something apparently more grand and no-
ble, but by doing with her might what her
hands finds to do, letting her light so shine
before men that they may glorify our Fath-
er who is in heaven." All honor to those
noble women who are willing to brave the
scorn of those who would deprive woman
of the social standing to which she has a
God given right, who toil and labor to raise
their sisters to their proper level. And
even in this respect the humblest mother
can throw in her mite, she can teach her
little boy that his sister is by no means his
inferior, that she is entitled to his highest
regard and equal in every respect to him-
self. Let every mother see to it as far as
she has influence, that her daughters have
at least equal chance of education with her
boys. My own opinion is that they ought
to have better, for are they not to be the
future Mothers in Zion? and if as is said
mothers are mostly responsible for the
moulding and shaping of the infant mind,
should not our daughters be educated men-
tally and physically with this in view?
	After all, may be my purpose is a selfish
one, for is it not for our own individual sal-
vation that we are prompted to do the best
we can? and it is impossible for us to gain
salvation for any others unless they them-
selves are willing to become acceptable with
God. At any rate I hope to labor on and
patiently await the time, when with the
Lord's blessing, I can send into the field
some more effectual workers than myself. It
is my constant care and prayer that they
may prove so.
				ESTHER A. BENNION.
	Vernon, Feb. 26, 1877.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]

BREAKING THE SABBATH DAY.

	Children you may deceive your parents,
but you cannot deceive God or your own
conscience. "Honor thy father and moth-
that thy days may be long in the land which
the Lord thy God giveth thee;" this is one
of the first commandments, it was written
by God's own finger on a table of stone, on
Mount Sinai; look again at the first word.
Honor your father and mother by obeying
them cheerfully, when in their sight, and
by your good conduct when out of their
sight. Let the children of pious parents
keep it in mind that they are the represen-
tatives of their parents; their good, moral
character shows that they have been care-
fully disciplined; yet it is too true that some
children however well trained, feel more
ashamed to be seen praying than to be
heard cursing and lying in the presence of
the wicked. Under the law of Moses; the

[Column 2]

disobedient child was to be stoned to death,
in the presence of the whole congregation;
there was no escape from this penalty un-
der the Jewish laws. The Bible in many
places speak expressly against disobedient
children. Read that awful declaration in
Proverbs, 30, 17, "The eye that mocketh at
his father, and despiseth to obey his moth-
er, the ravens of the valley shall pluck it
out, and the young eagles shall eat it." If
you are poor, resolve to be honest and up-
right, you may be rich in the purity of your
character, as the wise man has said, "a good
name is better than precious ointment."
"Go not in the ways of the transgressor,"
if you are seen in such company you will
have to bear part of the disgrace that fol-
lows bad deeds, and in this way lose the
the confidence of all good people. Select
your companions with great care; this is a
question of such importance that we should
not pass it over with a glance; says one,
"why should I be so particular about the
company I keep?" We should be particu-
lar in this matter, for if we form an ac-
quaintance with a disobedient person, we
are liable to partake of the same spirit.
	When I see some of our young people
running wild, breaking the Sabbath day,
taking the name of the Lord in vain, giv-
ing no heed to counsel from parents or
friends; what a stigma on their parents and
to society; what a disgrace to a young man
when placed in circumstances to earn a
name and honorable position in life, to
waste his time and talents disobeying the
laws of God by smoking, drinking and in
breaking the Sabbath. What young lady
of principle would encourage his example
by being in his company. The thought
arises what a vast amount of good might
be done by our young ladies if one and all
would be in earnest and use their influence
by improving in all things, and not only re-
trench, but by their conduct set the exam-
ple. Presidents and Counselors of the As-
sociations, you are the ones to set the exam-
ple for the young to pattern after in dress
and everything that pertains to these In-
situations.
	It is very pleasing to see the young ladies
of this place brave enough to manifest a
spirit of reform by taking a vote not to as-
sociate or even go forth in the dance with
those young men that use spirituous liguors.
Be determined my young sisters to live to
the Lord, thus setting an example before
the young men worthy of imitation; let
them understand that you mean what you
say. I can think of nothing so pure and
angelic as a young woman clothed with the
spirit of God; kind and affectionate to her
parents, and keeping the Sabbath day sac-
red.
	M. J. C.
Bountiful, Davis Co., Feb. 27, '77.


ITEMS ABOUT STORING GRAIN.

			HUNTSVILLE, March 3, 1877.

EDITOR EXPONENT:

	By request of our President, Mrs. Mary
J. Hammond, I send a written report of
the Relief Society of Huntsville for publi-
cation. The Society is in a prosperous con-
dition. We meet twice a month and are
united and ever ready an[-] willing to re-
spond to every call made by our president
who take great interest in striving to
bring every good and useful principle be-
fore her sisters.
	The Society numbers about one hundred
and fifty members. There are but very few


poor in our midst and they are well provid-
ed for. The Society has funds on hand
amounting to two hundred and eighty-five
dollars. We have bought a few head of
sheep and are striving to become self-sus-
taining. We have shares in the Co-opera-
tive Store amounting to one hundred and
forty dollars, and shares in the Relief So-
ciety Store in Ogden City, amounting to
thirty-eight dollars. We have a commit-
tee appointed to buy wheat and store it, to
be used in the future for the benefit of the
saints according to the counsel which may
be given through the prophet of God upon
the earth. We have a very interesting
Sunday School under the direction of El-
der Charles Wright; both old and young
seem to take great interest in attending
regularly, and in gaining useful knowledge
pertaining to our religion. The EXPONENT
is very much appreciated by those who
have subscribed for it; we are only sorry
that there are not more numbers of this
valuable paper taken in our midst.
	Praying the Lord to bless the EXPONENT,
and all who are interested in it, and hoping
you will excuse this my first attempt, I re-
main your sister, 
	MARY B. HALL,
		Sec'y


FLOWERS.

	Home is the head of the river, and an
influence, whether blessed or pernicious,
extended there will effect all its after course.
Hence the noble Frenchwoman was right,
when, in reply to the monarch's question,
"What is needed to secure the prosperity
of France?" she said, "Good mothers."
	A TRUE LADY.—Beauty and style are
not the purest passports to respectability—
some of the noblest specimens of woman-
hood the world has ever seen have present-
ed the plainest and most unprepossessing
appearance. A woman's worth is to be es-
timated by her real goodness of heart aud
the purity and sweetness of her character;
and such a woman; with a kindly disposi-
tion and a well-balanced mind and temper,
is lovely and attractive. Be her face ever
so plain and her form ever so homely, she
makes the best of wives and the truest of
mothers. She has a higher purpose in life
than to flaunt her finery in the streets, or
to gratify her inordinate vanity by attract-
ing flattery and praise from a society whose
compliments are as hollow as they are in-
sinc[-]re.


LITTLE COURTESIES.—The art of "living
together" pleasantly is promoted greatly by
the habitual exchanges of the little courtes-
ies of this life; they are never unimport-
ant, never unacceptable. Shall brothers
and sisters be less careful of the feelings of
one another than those of strangers?
Should there be, between a husband and
wife, less effort at gentleness of deport-
ment and courtesy of expression than is ex-
tended to outsiders who have no special
claims, and may never be seen again?
Shame upon any member of any family
who neglects those affections and those
suavities of deportment toward the mem-
bers of the household, even to the lowest
servant, which cannot fail to elevate the
giver, and to draw from the receiver those
spontaneous reciprocities which makes fam-
ily associations so pleasant.—The Gem.


MISS EMMA ABBOTT has made her "de-
but" in New York City, in opera, and it is
said by newspaper reporters, gives promise
of fair success.


160		WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


HYGIENIC NOTES.

	Carbolic Acid.—In a recent number of the
"Lancet," Dr. Alexander Watson recorded several
cases of small pox and scarlet fever which by the ex-
ternal application of carbolic acid met with marked
success. In the case of one patient with small-
pox, whom he saw at the period when papulae ap-
peared, he ordered an enema, and then had the
patient (a girl of eleven years) sponged all over
with carbolic acid soap suds. On the next day a
severe attack of confluent small pox was threatened,
but the child was sponged as she had previously
been, and then her whole body was painted with
carbolic acid glycerine of the British Pharmacopae.
Five grains of Dover's powder were then given to
allay irritability, and the little girl slept quietly
for several hours, when she was sponged again.
No vesicles formed, and the patient was convales-
cent in a few days. Carbolic acid was, in the
meantime, plentifully used about the room.
	Cure for Diptheria.—A small quantity of either
sulphate of iron, tannin, or chlorate of potash, put
dry upon the tongue, and allowed to dissolve
there, once in every hour, have all been found to
be most efficient remedies in the early stages of
diptheria. This terrible disease appears under
three varieties or forms. First, there is a fever,
severe pains in the head, bones and limbs, and a
very great prostration. There may be no soreness
of the throat, but on examining it, small white
specks will be noticed on the tonsils, but few or
none on the rest of the throat. These cases will
recover under any and every variety of treatment
in from four to six days. The great, but false,
reputation of many remedies and physicians is
based upon the necessarily favorable results of
these cases. Second: Large patches of false mem-
brane will be observed both on the tonsils and
back of the throat; but the glands of the neck do
not become swollen.
	The majority of these cases will also recover,
under very simple treatment, unless the disease
extends down to the windpipe, which it rarely
does. Third: True malignant diptheria, with
swelling of the glands of the neck and under the
jaw; profuse and often offensive exudations in the
mouth and throat; more or less discharge from the
nostrils. Chlorate of potash is a disinfectant as
well as a curative remedy; and if given pure and
dry upon the tongue every hour, night and day,
will rarely disappoint the practitioner. A small
dose every night may act as a preventtive reme-
dy. Sometimes tannin may be given more readily
than the chlorate of potash. The sulphate of
iron is rather more irritant, and must be given in
smaller quantities. The main points in the use of
these remedies are to give them dry upon the
tongue, and frequently and regularly both by night
and day.—Ex.


DR. Holland thinks the cure for gossip is 
culture. He says good-natured people talk 
about their neighbors only because they
have nothing else to talk about.


THE man who lives right and is right
has more power in his silence than another
has by his words. Character is like bells
which ring out sweet music, and which,
when touched accidentally, even, resound
with sweet music.


The mind grows through its ideas.
Thought is soul food, and greatness comes
of enlarged conceptions of truth. As the
barren field is profitless, so is the mind
which is barren of ideas. The one pro-
duces no bread for the world's eating; and
the other no thoughts for the world's think-
ing.


WOMAN'S RECORD.
	THE "Bosten Post" calls Miss Kate Field
a busy body indeed. At the same time she
is permanently engaged upon the staff of
the London Examiner, she is contributor to
the "Hornet, and is filling a theatrical en-
gagement in London, has recently written
a play for Herman Vezin, and a blank verse
poem which have been warmly praised.
	THE REV. Mrs. Maggie Van Cott has
been holding a series of revival meetings
in New York City, and the "Woman's
Journal" tells us that, notwithstanding the
fact that some think women have no right
to preach, she has made 1,200 converts the
past winter.
	GEORGE ELIOT is at work again on a
new novel. It is said she feels very much
disappointed that Middlemarch is consider-
ed better than "Daniel Deronda." She
herself regards this novel her master piece.
She produced her first original work 18
years ago, and the whole product of the
sale of her books is estimated at $165,000.

WOMAN'S EXPONENT.
VOL. 5.	SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, APRIL 1, 1877.		No. 21.


TO BEREAVED MOTHERS.

In Rama there was heard a voice of grief;
	Rachel bemoaned her sad and lonely lot;
Would not be comforted nor find relief,
	Her children slain, she wept that they were not.

A wailing voice is heard in Deseret,
	The babes are stricken and the mothers mourn;
Their hearts are pained, their cheeks and eyes are wet,
	For those who're not, which they have fondly borne.

Oh sisters! comfort ye! We've but to wait,
	Though for a season life seems reft of charms:
We know fair cherub forms at the bright gate,
	Will welcome us with loving, clinging arms.

We'll clasp them to our hearts to part no more,
	We'll find the same sweet lips and pure soft breath,
The same dear eyes God will in love restore,
	And in that Heavenly home there is no death.

We'll hear again their infant voices speak,
	In the glad silv’ry tones we loved so much,
And feel again on forehead, lip and cheek,
	Their kisses and their fingers' velvet touch.

How long oh Lord! how long! But hush poor heart!
	Thou would'st not question Him whose power sublime
Hath fashioned, formed and made thee what thou art!
	Thou must abide His will and wait His time.

And while you wait the noble lesson learn
	Of patience duly tried and perfect trust;
Though oft with hunger sore you wildly yearn,
	Remember still that God is wise and just.

Oh Gospel Light! most holy and serene!
	How shall they stand who know not thy fair truths!
How thy blest presence cheers death's solemn scene,
	How thy glad sounds the wounded spirit sooths!

How should we these strange trying tests endure,
	Had we no higher claim than words of men?
Did we not feel Our Father's promise sure,
	That we shall have our darlings back again.

Thank God we may confess His mighty hand
	In all the trials He directs us through,
Since He hath given us grace to understand
	His holy covenants, forever new!

Sublimest Faith! Most sacred gift of God!
	Shall they who know thee fail, or deem thee false?
Shall frail, weak woman sink beneath the rod,
	Which chastening, purifies, upholds, exalts?

What though we weep! God gave us tears to shed,
	When hearts o'er burdened burst from calm control;
The blessed Savior's heart was torn and bled,
	He wept and groaned in anguish of His soul.

In His dear name, for strength and grace we'll call,
	Nor deem the crosses we must bear too great:
The prize He offers is sufficient all—
	Oh comfort ye! my sisters—we can wait!

Let worldlings laugh our patient hopes to scorn,
	We'll not condemn; the Father judges best;
We own His power and while we truly mourn,
	Rejoice as truly in His promised rest.

Let me these humble, sacred tributes weave,
	O'er little ones so loved, so early gone!
It comforts mine, and other hearts that grieve,
	May gather courage as they struggle on.

	LULA.
Lula D. Richard


[Column 1]

WE WILL REPRESENT OURSELVES.

	In a letter published in the "Woman's
Journal," of Boston, the writer has made a
grievous mistake in representing the Mor-
mon ladies. She says "none but a few of
the ignorant sustain the doctrine of Plural
Marriage; the more intelligent class, and
especially the young ladies, would be glad
to see it abolished."
	As I have resided in S. L. City over twen-
ty years, and have been extensively ac-
quainted with all classes, I believe it is my
duty to correct such a mistake, when I see
it published to the world. I have conversed

[Column 2]

with a number of strangers, visiting the
city, who have expressed their surprise to
find so much intelligence among the women
who sustain this principle practically; the
writer of the letter to which I allude is in-
cluded among that number, and still retains
her husband's name, though so strongly
and widely proclaiming against the princi-
ple. These matrimonial alliances sanctioned
by the FIRST wife, prove that all parties
are involved in solving this wonderful
problem; which it would not take a Phila-
delphia lawyer more than six weeks to do,
provided theological evidence would be re-
ceived as testimony. And to those who
are infidel to the evidence of inspired writ-
ers we would say, we have the physiologi-
cal proofs to sustain our theory, and the
laws of nature, which should govern the
regeneration of man. And there are thou-
sands of noble, intelligent women in Utah,
who have received for themselves a knowl-
edge of the truth of the principles revealed
by Joseph Smith the martyred Prophet,
and they fear not to meet the opposition of
a frowning world, realizing that God is the
Author of revealed religion, and it is His
right to organize the plan by which the in-
habitants of the earth can be redeemed;
and He has manifested His power by in-
spiring men and women who have and will
carry out His plans at the sacrifice of all
EARTHLY honors.
	Our little EXPONENT, though in its in-
fancy, we are proud to say is the represent-
ative of truth, and is the Exponent of the
principles advocated by the Mormon wo-
men: and we recommend its moral teach-
ings and divine instructions to all who
read its pages, as truthful and elevating.	This reminds us of the thousands and
millions of dollars appropriated for the pub-
lication of literature that is worse than
nonsense, filling the minds of the rising
generation with fables and errors, that will
require years to eradicate. But this is in
keeping with the age in which we live.
The world of mankind has almost become a
swindle, and a counterfeit, politically and
religiously. As with the politician, so with
the priest—they all work for money, and
not for the interest of the people; confi-
dence is lost, and the light of truth, that
once beamed upon the patriots of former
days, is being withdrawn in consequence of
the wickedness and fraud that have been
practised upon a people who would delight
to honor their constitutional rights.
	Seeing we have digressed a little from
our subject, we will return by saying we
think it is much better to rspresent our-
selves; than to be MISREPRESENTED by oth-
ers, and we intend to let those ladies who
have been lecturing, and publishing books,
to misrepresent us to the world know, that
with the help of God in whom we trust, we
intend to represent ourselves, and leave
those in the hands of God who seek to
fight against Him. We know the wonder-
ful problem being solved by the Mormon
people is the greatest mystery of the pres-
ent age, but it only requires time to solve
itself. If it be of God who can fight against
it; it is He who has revealed it, and sus-
tained it; it was beyond the power of man
to do what has been already done, then why
fight against it? If it is not of God it will

[Column 3]

fall to the ground of itself—let it alone.
The Saviour said a good tree is known by
its fruits. My testimony is that for truth,
integrity and virtue the Mormon people
are not excelled by any people on the earth,
and we call upon honorable men and women
living among us, who are not of our faith,
to be our witnesses. If this is not true,
why are they here? If we are ignorant,
and practice degradation and crime, why
do our enemies come and make their homes
among us? There is a large amount of
room in the world, and we only occupy the
Great American Desert. I never knew a
people that honored their constitutional
rights more than the Mormons. If a Meth-
odist comes among us and shouts, no one
says why do you worship in that way. If
the Quaker comes and shakes, no true saint
asks, why do you shake? The Catholics
come and rear their lofty convent walls re-
quiring their converts to pay penance; the
Mormon goes to his own church, minds his
own business, does not even ask, why do
you pay penance to the Priest? For he has
already learned as much as the little boy
who asked his father who he paid penance
to? the father replied, "To the priest, my
son;" "But who does the priest go to?"
"To God," was the reply; "Well," said the
boy, "I will go to God, and keep my shill-
ing to buy me a Bible."
	W. E.
S. L. City, March 20, 1877.


R. S. REPORTS.

	CLARKESTON, CACHE Co.,
		Eeb. 17th, 1877.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	The First of this month the Relief Socie-
ty of Clarkeston held their Annual Meet-
ing, and a report of the past year's doings
was read, which showed that the poor had
been cared for: We have paid nine dollars
and seventy-five cents to the Sunday School,
twenty-one dollars to the Salt Lake Temple,
twenty-five dollars for a share in the Wool-
en Factory being built in Logan; we have
about forty dollars in shares in the Co-op
Store, we have also a small sheep herd, and
we have commenced to save a little wheat,
we have about thirty bushels in the bin, and
expect to get some more. Our settlement
is small; but the sisters feel willing to assist
in building up the sisters feel willing to assist
in building up the kingdom of God. Our
worthy Bishop, Bro. John Jardine, met
with us and gave us some valuable instruc-
tions. He certainly is the right man in the
right place. A number of other brethren
were there. The people here are busy get-
ting the materials together to build a new
meeting house; we expect to have it fin-
ished by next fall.
	Union and a good spirit prevail. The
health of the people is good, and we feel
that the blessings of God are with us. The
EXPONENTS come very regularly, and the
sisters are anxious to read them at all times.
Hoping that we may be found faithful to
the end, we remain your sisters in the Gos-
pel,
	MARY GRIFFIN, President.
		JANE GODFREY, Secretary.


[Column 1]
			SMITHFIELD CACHE Co.,
						March 16, 1877.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	We the Relief Society of Smithfield wish
to let you know what we are doing to help
build up Zion. We are building a neat lit-
tle adobie house to hold our metings in.
We got the walls up and the roof on last
fall, and are going to have it finished as
soon as possible now. The young men and
boys hauled the materials, and the society
had two dances and picnics for them. The
boys had a fine time and were so well
pleased that they said they would help the
society again. The brethren donated some
of the work, and we have paid the rest
with quilts made by the Relief and Re-
trenchment Societies. The Retrenchment
Association has done considerable. Our So-
ciety are doing what they can in the Grain
Movement; we have gathered up some-
thing over one hundred bushels of wheat.
Next year we intend to commence sooner,
and gather in the grain in good season, and
think we can do better than we did this
winter. We will exert ourselves early in
harvest time.
		Your sister etc.
					E. M. MERRILL.

	RIVERDALE, WEBER Co. March 8, 1877.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	Our worthy President Sister Martha A.
Bingham desired me to write to you. I
trust I may be guided in what I may say
by the spirit of the Lord. Our society is in
a more prosperous condition than it has
been; the sisters seem to be more interested
in the things of the kingdom. Our Society
is small in comparison to many others, our
numbers are about forty in all, the sisters as
a general thing feel to be one with other
Relief Societies in saving and storing grain.
We are not as well off financially as some of
our sisters, but we have a bin prepared and
some few bushels of wheat in it which we
intend adding unto as long as the Lord pros-
pers us with means and plentiful harvests.
	Your excellent paper is always welcome
with us, it is a source of pleasure and com-
fort; we have it read in our meetings occas-
ionally as the spirit directs. May the Lord
bless every honest mother and sister in Is-
rael who are striving to do His will and-
keep His commandments, is the prayer of
your sisters in the Gospel.
			M. A. Bingham, Pres't.
				H. A. CHILD, Sec'y.

				LEHI, March 1st, 1877.
EDITORS EXPONENT:
	The Relief Society of this place is not
behind the sisters of other settlements in
trying to carry out the counsel of President
Young in storing grain, though we are late
in reporting. A committee was chosen to
visit the sisters and obtain means in that
way or any other that they in their wis-
dom thought best. We are pleased to re-
port to you, that by donations, proceeds
from a party, and other means, we have on
hand more than one hundred bushels of
wheat and with the prospect of more. Not
having a bin of our own, Bishop Evans
kindly proffered us one at the Co-op store
to put it in; and to insure us from all losses
he gave us a written order for the amount,
subject to our call at any time. We expect,
if possible, to build a granary this coming
summer.
	We rejoice to know that the Lord has
counted us worthy to be co-workers in pre-

[Column 2]
paring for that time which has been predict-
ed by ancient and modern prophets, when
famine shall come upon the earth, and hun-
dreds of the inhabitants shall come to Zion
to buy bread of the (now, but not then) de-
spised Latter-day Saints.
	We also wish to aid in helping to publish
the Woman's Book. The EXPONENT is an
ever welcome and most desirable visitor.
With much esteem and kind wishes for
your prosperity and success in your laudable
endeavors to do good, I am
		Yours respectfully,
			REBECCA STANDRING,
						Secretary.

				PAYSON, March 5th, 1877.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	The sisters of Payson feel that they have
had a rich treat of late. Sunday, Feb. 4,
Bishop W. G. Young gave us two soul-stir-
ring discourses; he visited the Relief Socie-
ty and the Young Ladies' Retrenchment
Association here, imparted wise counsel to
the mothers and daughters. He talked of
the Woman's Book about to be published,
representing its interests, and canvassed
for the "Life of Brigham Young, or Utah
and her Founders." Feb. 11, Bishop Lo-
renzo Young gave us a splendid discourse
in the afternoon, President Joseph Young,
Sen., visited the Sunday School; we could
have sat for hours and listened to his wise
counsel and fatherly advice to old and
young. Monday evening, Feb. 12, Elders
Hardy and Young addressed the Young
Men's Improvement Association, after
which Mrs. Zina Young said she could bear
testimony to all that had been said; the
outside influence felt its power. She spoke
on sericulture, exhibited a specimen of
fringe that had been made for the Temple
at St. George. The feeling with us is that
we have had a feast of fat things, and say
God bless the brethren for their wise coun-
sel, and Sister Zina for comforting and
cheering us with her motherly and inspir-
ing conversation.
	We hail each number of the EXPONENT
with delight, knowing that much valuable
instruction is contained therein; we regret
that it has not a wider circulation in this
place. The sisters all join with me in love.
						M. A. HARDY.

	MORRELL, SUMMIT Co., March 15, 1877.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	In reply to your kind request to hear
from our Relief Society we herewith send
you a statement of our financial condition,
March 15, 1877.
	Am't received and made,......... $60 85
"       Disbursed..................................   16 70
"       On hand.....................................   44 15
	Our society was organized Sept. 1874,
with the following officers, Jane E. Lemon,
President; Christine Larsen and Julia
Woolstenhulme, Counselors; Jane Keeler,
Secretary; Jane E. Lemon, Treasurer; and
twenty members. We meet twice a month
and enjoy each other's society and counsel,
and feel benefitted thereby. We read the
EXPONENT with great interest and are
pleased to learn through its columns that
the mothers in Zion are awake to a sense of
their duties and are storing up the precious
grain, for we know it will be our salvation
in years to come. We ahve considered the
Grain Movement in our meetings, and
would like to give our mite to help forward
in this great work, but the frost destroyed
our grain. This being a newly settled place

[Column 3]
we have not the means at present, to go
elsewhere to purchase grain, but hope for
better times soon.
	The sisters of our society conceived the
idea of having a party and managing the
affair themselves. We decorated our school
house nicely with pictures and flowers, a
few of our sisters understand making arti-
ficial flowers and made an abundance of
them, which gave to the room an elegant
appearance. At two o'clock in the afternoon
President Mrs. J. E. Lemon opened the
dance by prayer. Mrs. Jane Keeler was
Manager, and at ten o'clock Counselor C.
Larsen dismissed. We maintain the idea
that woman's influence will not be lessened
but increased by her taking a more active
part in recreations and social enjoyments.
	In Sept. last we received a flying visit
from sisters' E. S. Barney and E. Howard.
They gave us good advice and we desire to
carry out their teachings to the best of our
ability.
		Respectfully yours,
			JANE E. LEMON, Pres't.
				JANE KEELER, Sec'y.

	MORONI, SANPETE Co., March 10, 1877.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	We have been called upon to mourn the
loss of one of the most faithful members of
our Society, Sister Caroline Jorgensen who
was accidentally kicked by a cow which
caused her death in forty-eight hours af-
terward; this occurred on the 2nd of Feb.
She assembled with us at our Society meet-
ing three days previous to the accident, and
was also at Fast meeting the day before.
Her age was sixty-eight years; she was an
accoucher and practiced here many years
and was also always ready and willing to
help and comfort the sisters in distress.
She was greatly beloved by all her brethren
and sisters, and was respected by those who
are not of our people. Her loss to us will
be very great. She was always ready with
her donations in helping to build up the
kingdom of God. Her sudden death was
quite a shock to her husband and children
and to us all. All the sisters of the ward
convened at her house to pay their tribute
of respect to her beloved remains, and all the
teams in the settlement were there to con-
vey us to her last resting place. Bishop
Bradley and other brethren spoke comfort-
ing words to the bereaved family, and to
the great congregation that had assembled;
we all felt to say dear sister rest until the
morning of the first resurrection, then you
will come forth and receive a crown of
righteousness that fadeth not away, reserv-
ed for you from the foundation of the
world—and to all those that love His ap-
pearing.
	In respect to the Grain Movement we
have two hundred bushels well stored. We
have a committee of four to look after the
grain; they are very energetic in gathering
in the "staff of life;" the brethren are very
kind in helping the sisters donate the
wheat. We are gathering all the means
we can for the Temple. Our young ladies
are making quilts and comforters, they also
wish to do something to help build up the
kingdom of God.
	Wishing success to the EXPONENT, I re-
main your sister in the Gospel.
							J. BAILEY.

Moody and Sankey are still holding forth
in Boston, Mass., and drawing, constantly,
large audiences. The new tabernacle erect-
ed for them there seats 7,000 people; and is
always well filled.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


TO THE MEMBERS OF THE
Y. M. M. I. A.

Brothers in faith, in God and the right,
Lead on, we are with you in fervor and might;
Meet ever with courage the foes of our cause,
The ruthless defilers of God and His laws.

Fear not to tell of His goodness and truth,
Fear not even now in the days of thy youth;
Think not His cross and His burthen is hard,
As thy day so thy strength, and great the reward.

Of those who enlist in the conflict begun,
Who stand by our flag 'till the battle is done,
Whose heart never falters whose arm will not yield
'Til the last foe is conquered or fled from the field.

We want not the bullet, the sword nor the knife
Not death is our object but life, ever life;
Our weapon is Truth, undaunted and brave,
Its foes to contend with, its champions to save.

Wide is the field and great is the host,
And well does the enemy guard every post;
Ignorance, falsehood, malice and pride,
With their numerous allies on every side.

But they'll vanish away and flee from the light
Of Truth, as the bright sun will banish the night,
And Faith, Hope, and Charity, sisters divine,
The Crown for the Victor in glory will twine.
	HOPE.
Sarah C. R[---]


ITEMS ON DRESS.

	I wish to address a few friendly words to
you my dear friends and sisters, and have
chosen this subject, and hope I shall give
no offence but merely express a few ideas
that were prominently brought to my mind
lately. I had a few words written and in-
tended to send them to you, but on reading
them to President Young, he opened to my
mind such a halo of light and revelation on
this subject that I could not be satisfied with
what I had written. I only wish you my
dear readers, could have been with me and
heard and seen the plainness and beauty of
the principles opened to my view. It is
undoubtedly the case that this waste of
dress is not countenanced by those whom the
Lord has placed to lead Israel We are all
human enough to enquire the reason of this.
President Young answered this question in
language which I am unable to use. In this
life we are really in possession of but one
thing. Money, houses, lands, all, all are
left behind when we go hence; and even
while here, with all our efforts and exer-
tions to keep them, with a breath they are
gone. How true the old saying, "riches
take wings." So that all that remains to us
is our TIME. This no one can rob us of;
but I am inclined to say we very often al-
low ourselves to be cheated into casting it
aside as a worthless toy. Consequently
when we spend our valuable time in mak-
ing up clothing which is elaborately trim-
med, we both waste our time and those
means which should be devoted to sending
for the poor, and in other charitable pur-
poses. Right here some one says, "Oh yes!
but Miss or Mrs. So and So have renewed
their covenants and received the ordinan-
ces of the Lord's house and other blessings,
and they dress extravagantly and wear all
the ruffles they choose. What if they do?
Because Judas betrayed Christ does it fol-
low that all the Apostles were to turn trait-
ors and forsake their God? Apply the si-
mile here. Shall we encourage a love of
dress because others do so? Most certain-
ly not. It is useless to tell a lady that gen-
tlemen prefer to see ladies dressed plainly
and neatly. They don't care a fig, they
will answer you; they dress to please them-
selves." Which translated means that
they dress to be like each other and to fol-
low the fashions of a wicked, foolish world.
But I honestly believe that if you can con-


vince a Mormon, woman's reason, that it is
wroug to put trimmings on her clothes or
dress elaborately, she will cease to do so. It
is only the result of thoughtlessness; they
do not stop to consider the matter, but go
blindly on, following in the lead of those
perhaps, who should know better, and set
a better example.
	Do you think sisters you would like to
associate with angels with these trappings
on? I suppose that you have all been prom-
ised that you should one day meet with
them, and sooner or later we must put a
stop to this folly. So let us do so now, and
be prepared to advance one step further in
the grand march to meet our Savior; if we
do, we will be thus binding Satan out of
our hearts, for this folly of dress surely
originates from him. Let us do this, and
not pass over this matter so lightly, for if
we do, we will some day be called to ac-
count for it.
	Yours with love,
		S. Y. D.


GOOD MANNERS.

	I have long been impressed with the im-
portance of the subject named above, both
in public and private life; Scripture enjoins
us to be courteous, and to be given to hos-
pitality, and the Savior himself, was a pat-
tern of gentleness, tenderness and consider-
ation towards those He associated with.
All these are principles upon which good
manners should be based; there is no doubt
the heart and intentions are sometimes
very good, when the manners are repulsive
and uncouth, and as the MASSE do not look
beyond the surface, such persons cannot feel
an injury is done to them, if they are judged
unjustly. We should allow the warm feel-
ings of the heart to expand beyond our-
selves, if we desire to be beloved and re-
spected. Good manners are a letter of in-
troduction at first sight, and the coldest
heart must be thawed by a kind and ur-
bane salutation or welcome. Lamartine, a
good and beautiful French writer says "The
Gospel is democratic, and Christianity re-
publican;" and the royal law of the same is
"Do as you would be done by;" all of which
implies the law of kindness, of course in-
cluding good manners. True politeness
springs from the heart, and requires often
a sacrifice of our own comforts, and conve-
niences. Where the spirit is loving, hum-
ble and teachable, there we find the mate-
rial from which good manners are formed;
true it is that "trifles make the sum of hu-
man things," yet the happiness of our
every day life, is made up of such minute
particles that they must be felt rather than
made palpable by language.
	I might give the routine of Educational
politene s, and would uphold such as far as
it is good; but the Home circle should be
the centre of our good manners; to our par-
ents, to our brothers and sisters, and to our
husbands let our good manners be ever ap-
parent. My sisters the grand secret is to
cultivate the heart, and the affections, not
to let the energies stagnate, and the heart
to grow cold; then the manners will
be colored accordingly and we will not see
apathy and frigidity taking the place of
more genuine qualities in ourselves, and
others. Friendship would then spring up
where now it is rarely found, and add an-
other blessing to the many by which we are
surrounded. Let us then bear in mind my
young sisters, the importance of cultivat-
ing our hearts and minds, that in all things
we may be worthy the name of saints of
the Most High; and our manners be the


INDEX of that moral and intellectual train-
ing which is necessary to maintain a per-
fect equilibrium in the balance of high in-
tellectual attainments, and advantages,
which are around the Saints of Latter-days,
that by every means, however small, we
may be assisting to bring about the time
when a people shall be prepared for the Sec-
ond coming of our Lord, and in whom God
the Father will delight to be all in all.
				HANNAH T. KING.


WOMAN'S VOICE.

	SMITHFIELD; CACHE CO.,
		March 13, 1877.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	I have often felt a desire to bear my tes-
timony to the truth of this work, through
your columns. I know this is the only true
church upon the earth. This is the stone
that Daniel saw cut out of the mountain
without hands, and kept rolling until it
filled the whole earth. So this work is roll-
ing on, and will continue to roll and crush
everything that comes in contact with it.
	We are a blessed people above all people
upon the face of the earth; we are in the
midst of the light, therefore we cannot see
its brightness. I sometimes think we don't
appreciate our blessings and privileges as
we ought. I have taken great interest in
reading the reports of the different settle-
ments on the Grain Movement; it seems all
have partaken of the right spirit—that is,
obedience to the word of the Lord through
His servant. The sisters here are not be-
hind in that great work; we have laid a
foundation, having a goodly quantity of
grain in store this year, and we will con-
tinue from year to year, until it will be
said it is enough. We are laying the foun-
dation of a great work, and out of small
things proceedeth that which is great. The
Lord requireth the heart and a willing
mind; and the willing and obedient shall
eat the good of the land of Zion in these
last days.
	Our beloved President of the Relief So-
ciety, Mrs. Morehead, is always on the
watch tower, ready to help the poor and
needy, to comfort the sick and strengthen
the weak, and render assistance wherever
it is needed.
	We have been afflicted with sickness this
winter and had to part with many of our
dear little ones, but we are not without
hope, they are ours still if we will live
faithful to the principles of life and salva-
tion, so that we shall receive our children
again; our treasure is in heaven, let our
hearts be there also. Truly this is a day of
rejoicing—a temple is built to the Most
High—an Ensign to the people, where they
will flock like doves to the windows. Let
us continue building temples, and devote
our time and interest to the building up of
the kingdom of God upon the earth, re-
trenching from every foolish fashion and
habit. Let our garments be the workman-
ship of our own hands. May the blessings of
God attend all our efforts to do good is my
prayer.
	MARTHA M. WILLIAMS.


ELIZABETH Stuart Phelps is writing up
the woman question in the form of a story.
This fanciful style of writing is oftentimes
effective of good, and many important sub-
jects are brought convincingly to the mind
of the reader in this way, which they never
would have listened to, or read, had the
book been of a more serious character.
