SALT LAKE CITY. APRIL 1, 1877.


[Column 1]

BE A TRUE WOMAN.

	"Be a woman! On to duty!
		Raise the world from all that's low,
	Place high in the social heaven
		Virtue's fair and radiant bow.
	Lend thy influence to each effort
		That shall raise our nature human,
	Be not fashion's giddy lady—
		Be a brave, whole-souled, true woman."

	There is a vast amount spoken and written
in the present day about what women should
or should not do; advice in general terms is
given profusely. It is scarcely possible to lay
down rules for guidance in the education of
girls, especially their home-education, because
it must be modified by so many conditional or
controlling circumstances, that what would
be practicable for one, would not perhaps be
possible for others. Duty is the main point,
but duty is regulated by the different surround-
ings of a person, and the varied phases of
character. To be consistent one must take all
these perceptible differences into consideration.
Woman is active in her nature, and dreads
stagnation of any description, and if girls are
trained to habits of industry and regularity in
real work of some sort it will be a good pre-
ventive against "ennui," idleness and gos-
sip. There are many young women no doubt,
who scarcely take a thought of the time they
waste. They rebel against their fate as they
call it, and long for something fresh, some-
thing sensational, anything to break the mo-
notony of life. Therefore often in place of a
reality, they accept that which calls forth
only the emotional part of their nature, and
grasp the unreal believing it will prove the
"true alchemy," when all around them are
those resources out of which might be pro-
duced a growth of attainment in excellence
in some qualification or profession, which
would develop a corresponding happiness in
proportion to the enlargement of thought,
views and comprehension.
	After school days are over for girls, and more
particularly is this the case in large families
where there are many daughters, there is a
great need of care and watchfulness in the path
of action which should be chosen to walk in.
Young women hate to be looked upon as wait-
ing to be married, and hear the observation
made "so and so are in the market," as if they
were goods and chattels to be sold at auction;
they naturally shrink from those things and
it makes them sulk and become ill-tempered
to be considered out of place because they
don't marry. They want some systematic dis-
cipline in their life and pursuits, and to be en-
couraged in turning their mind and attention
to some worthy object, and that is safest which
presents itself most forcibly as the avocation

[Column 2]

likely to develop the best powers of woman-
hood.
	The girl at home is thus portrayed (and in-
deed the picture is not overdrawn) A little
note writing, a little vase-dressing, a little
practising (music), "a little reading, a little cro-
quet, a great deal of chatter, and, more than
all, much running in and out among near
neighbors, till the power of steady improve-
ment is frittered away and no progress is
made." There is an old Latin proverb, which
translated reads thus, "Friends are the thieves
of time," yet all love to have friends; what
would this life be without them; but this prov-
erb is true to a very great extent. Every per-
son can realize this in some degree. It is an
imperative duty to be conscientious in regard
to wasting the time of another, as well as
one's own. This is a point which seems to be
very little considered, but it is of such vital
importance that it cannot be too deeply im-
pressed upon the minds of the young.
	Let us return to our subject, marriageable
girls: not girls who want to marry but who are
just launched into womanhood and have no
fixed purpose in life, and long to find avocations
and an impetus for undeveloped capacities.
Young, beautiful, innocent, sensible, possessing
all those rare and exquisite traits which blend
so harmoniously in woman when cultivated to
a high degree, with all the expectation and
hope which belongs to that period of life,
knowing there is something expected of them,
but without positive force to step forward and
carve out any special purpose alone. Parents
and friends sometimes forget that these young
women are full of vigor, and must have scope
for action, and that once turned into some prop-
er channel, they could so discipline their own
minds and characters as to effect their whole
after life for good. They want some regular,
steady employment of genuine labor, else per-
chance they drift into habits of irregularity
and try to "kill time," perhaps just through
the want of proper advise and help from those
who do not fully realize how important is the
issue at stake. Every profession and avenue
of labor that opens for woman is a blessing;
there is no occasion for waiting girls, you have
no time to wait, there is to much real work to
be done for any hands to lay idle. Your mis-
sion upon this earth is of as much consequence
as that of your brother-man, and by all avail-
able means prepare yourselves physically, mor-
ally, mentally and spiritually for the great
battle of life, and the more real strength you
have time to gather for the conflict, the more
sure and steadfast will be your onward course,
and the better will you be prepared to lend as-
sistance to others. Learn adaptability to the
times, the places and the circumstances, wher-
ever your path of duty may be, and cultivate
application and endurance of purpose, and re-
member "he who builds a pyramid builds a
great thing, but he who builds a character, a
greater."
	E. B. Wells.


HOME AFFAIRS.

	WE RETURN thanks for the "Utah Musical
Bouquet," edited by Daynes & Son. We
watch with pleasurable interest all the im-
provements which tend to develop talent
among the people. We are gratified to see so
many home productions in songs and music.
The Temperance Song, "Pearly Water, Pure
As Snow," we conscientiously recommend as
suitable for day or Sunday Schools. Too
much cannot be done to influence the young
on the subject of Temperance.
	NOTICE: Mrs. H. Strickland has recently


opened a small store one door east of Mrs.
Sloan's establishment, Second South Street.
Mrs. Strickland has a variety of fancy notions
for sale; knits ladies and gent's hosiery, on the
Lamb machine, and also carries on dress-mak-
ing. She respectfully invites the patronage of
her friends, and all who are interested in home-
manufacture.
	ON SATURDAY morning, March 24, President
D. H. Wells and wife, with some other mem-
bers of his family, and Hon. George Q. Cannon
left this city for St. George. Bishop Edwin
D. Woolley, Bishop Samuel Woolley with some
others of their family, and Prof. O. H. Riggs,
also accompanied them making up quite a
a pleasant party, they will reach St. George
Tuesday next.
	WE LEARN by letter to the Deseret News
that the company that left Salt Lake City
Monday March 19, "enroute" for St. George
were enjoying themselves vastly by the way,
holding meetings in different places and meet-
ing with old friends and acquaintances. We
noticed the sisters were doing their part in ad-
dressing meetings and giving counsel to the
Relief Societies and organizations of women.
The meeting at Meadow Creek Mrs. Phebe
Woodruff and Mrs. Phebe Snow addressed the
sisters, and at Kanab, Mrs. Zina D. Young and
Mrs. Jane S. Richards.
	THE COLUMBIA Herald of March 21, pub-
lished in Columbia, Pa., contains an interest-
ing letter from Elder A. M. Musser of this
city, in which he contrasts the institutions of
this people, religious, social and educational,
with those existing in the world. He relates
in brief the condition in which the saints
found this country, and the drivings and mob-
violence which had been the cause of the exit
of the Latter-day Saints from the borders of
civilization. Elder Musser is publishing a
pamphlet there on the belief of this people con-
taining a defense of Polygamy.


SISTERS BE IN EARNEST.

	[The article below is republished from EX-
PONENT No. 10, Oct. 15, 1876. It was written
by SPECIAL request of President Brigham
Young, and approved by him before its publi-
cation. We have been asked to furnish copies
of this article at different times, and having
no spare numbers, we decided to give it to the
public in print again. In consideration also of
its being seed-time, and that this people antic-
ipate storing all the grain they possibly can
this season, they cannot be too deeply im-
pressed with the importance of the Grain
Movement. And in the season thereof let all
the Latter-day Saints forget not to ask Him
who giveth seed time and harvest, to multiply
the measures of harvests that the Saints
throughout all the borders of the land may
have an abundance of grain to store up for
Zion.]
	At the suggestion of President Brigham
Young we would call the attention of the wo-
men of this Territory to the subject of saving
grain. It is one important item which Presi-
dent Young has labored diligently to impress
upon this people ever since their residence in
these valleys. His advice has ever been to the
brethren to cultivate the soil and let the mines,
and mining speculations alone, for the grain
was of more consequence than gold or silver.
And these people are witnesses to that fact;
particularly having experienced the scarcity of
provisions and especially of breadstuffs, in the
earlier settlement of this country.
	The Lord has blessed this people abundantly
in the few years past in their fields and in their


[Column 1]

stores of grain and provisions; and as in al-
most every other case where there is a profu-
sion of blessings, the people who receive them
become indifferent and do not appreciate them
as they ought. The people have been careless
in regard to taking care of their grain and
many other things. In many instances they
neglect to pay their tithing, and dispose of
their products in such a manner as, were it to
continue, the Lord would be sorely displeased
with them. This year grain is unusually low, yet
the grasshoppers have been visiting different
parts of the Territory, and, it is feared by
many, have laid their eggs; therefore it is
more than ever essential that such measures
be taken as will secure the grain now in mar-
ket. The Relief Societies and Co-operative or-
ganizations in which some of the women of
this Terriory are interested, have funds at
their disposal from time to time, which can be
made available to carry out President Young's
wishes in regard to buying and taking care of
the grain.
	It is in no doubt the best investment of
means that could possibly be made, for if the
prophecies of the Scriptures, both ancient and
modern, and of Joseph Smith are to be fulfill-
ed, which the Latter-day Saints profess to be-
lieve, there will be a desolating famine in the
land; and if the women of this Territory will
be wise, and hearken unto the counsel given to
them now, there is no doubt that the result
will be an eminent success. If the means now
on hand in the societies and women's associa-
tions can be turned into grain, and such meas-
ures taken to store it and preserve it from
damp and all other things that would damage,
or destroy it, as can be made practicable ac-
cording to circumstances and advantages, then
one point will be gained towards the temporal
salvation of this people in case of emergency.
	In whatever women undertake they are gen-
erally earnest, and our advice would be imme-
diately without any delay (except such as is
unavoidable) to commence to carry out Presi-
dent Young's counsel in this matter. Where
there is a will there is (almost invariably) a
way, and in this matter there should be a per-
sonal and individual as well as a general inter-
est. In order to satisfactorily accomplish any
scheme, one great object is to be in earnest; on
this earnestness depends much of the success of
whatever enterprise is undertaken. If you de-
termine to do anything of whatever name or
nature.first be sure it is the proper thing to do,
and then without losing time, bring your ener-
gies into immediate requisition and you are al-
most sure to succeed.
	The women of this Territory are capable of
carrying out President Young's counsel in this
matter; and if they do not they will be left
without excuse; he has called upon the breth-
ren until he has become wearied, and now he
appeals to the sisters. Is not this a proof of
his confidence in their desire to listen to coun-
sel? The sisters will have ample opportunity
now to prove their ability for financiering; and
it is greatly to be hoped that they will mani-
fest such earnestness in this matter as cannot
fail to accomplish the desired object.
	President Young further advises the sisters,
that they enter into such arrangements as are
applicable to their circumstances in their var-
rious districts and settlements, to build store-
houses for the storing of their grain in the sec-
tions of country as they shall divide off or ap-
portion to certain localities; also that they so-
licit donations from their husbands and the
brethren to assist them in carrying out this en-
terprise, both for the building of the store-
houses and also in obtaining grain to fill them,
and preserve it for future need.

[Column 2]

	We sincerely hope that what has been said
will be sufficient to rouse a spirit of determin-
ation in the minds of the women of Utah, to
devise and to put in practice, such measures as
shall be economical and wise, and for the gen-
eral good of all concerned.

[Column 1]
			CORRESPONDENCE.

			CENTREVILLE, March 20th, 1877.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	Believing that a few items from here touch-
ing the great work in which we are all engag-
ed will be gratifying to you, permit me to state
that we are blessed with a general time of
health in this place, with an increasing desire
made apparent to walk as near the line of duty
as is possible, when our circumstances are tak-
en into consideration. I am proud to say that
the Young Ladies Retrenchment Association
is in a flourishing condition, each and every
member thereof responding cheerfully to all
the requirements made of them, and I rejoice
to be associated with them and all such as love
and serve God in the Latt[-]r-day kingdom, and
that I have the privilege of doing something
though small to help roll it forth. A general
good feeling akin to inspiration prevails in the
Relief Society, and a desire to grow in grace,
and in the knowledge of the truth. The Young
Mens' Association is also i[-] a flourishing con-
dition.
	Praying for the success of your valuable pa-
per and every laudable endeavor in the king-
dom of God, we subscribe ourselves your sis-
ters in the Gospel,
					ELIZABETH WADOUP,
					CAROLINE DALRYMPLE.

		CLIFTON, IDAHO, March 11th, 1877.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	Thinking a few lines from this little settle-
ment would be expected, I proceed to let you
know how we get along. The sisters meet
twice a month and we enjoy a goodly portion
of the spirit of God in our meetings. Some of
the sisters as well as the brethren have renew-
ed their covenants, and all appear to be awak-
ening to a sense of their duties. We had a
visit from Bro. Hardy of Salt Lake City, and
we all enjoyed ouselves, and his instructions
have been duly appreciated by the brethren,
from the spirit manifested by them. Our Sab-
bath School and Sabbath meetings are well
attended.
	We have a Retrenchment Society organized
in which the young girls are very much inter-
ested. We called a meeting on the 1st of Feb.
to consider the Grain Movement. It was an
excellent meeting, thirteen sisters joined our
society and we number now over thirty mem-
bers. We have about thirty bushels of wheat
now in the bin and the prospect of some
more.
The EXPONENT is received and duly appre-
ciated by the sisters. We look forward with
pleasant anticipation for a visit from some of
the sisters from Salt Lake City.
				Your Sister, etc.
						JANE M. HOWELL.
EDITORS' EXPONENT:
	I hope that in writing these few lines, I may
at least be able to do myself some good, and
that it may tend to develop my own mind. I
think it is a great and eventful day in which
we live; many great things are transpiring
among the nations, and we can realize that
the "signs of the times," are manifest; this
shows me the necessity of living more faithful
before the Lord, and it leaves no time to idle

[Column 2]
away, but to be watchful and prayerful every
hour of our lives and be ready and willing at
all times to discharge to the best of our ability
every duty resting upon us to perform in this
kingdom, and I think one of the greatest du-
ties resting upon us as a band of sisters, is the
saving of our GRAIN and storing it away for a
day of need, and what a great privilege this is
granted us to bring together our small means
thus, to save ourselves and those who shall
flock to us for assistance in a day of need!
What great blessings are conferred upon us,
and still the world call us "the down trodden
women of Utah." I feel thankful to my
Heavenly Father that I live in this glorious
day, and that I have a standing in this king-
dom, and that the Lord through His priest-
hood upon the earth, thus forwarnes us of the
judgments that He is about to pour our upon
the earth. I think we are slow to hearken to
His voice. I desire to take hold with renewed
energy, of the duties resting upon me, and im-
prove the golden opportunities that are grant-
ed me for acquiring knowledge, for I know
there is a great work to be performed; and the
Lord has said that in the last days "He will
cut His work short in righteousness."
	We are desirous to be co-workers with the
brethren, and may we be true aids in the good
cause of advancing this kingdom, is my s[-]n-
cere wish for us all.
				CHRISTINE M. WARNICK.
	Pleasant Grove, March 13, 1877.

EDITOR EXPONENT:
	Our Society comprising several districts,
lying far away on either side of Morgan City,
deprived many sisters of the privilege of at-
tending meetings and of doing the good it was
in their hearts to do. Therefore, Bishop Smith
who cares for his flock with fatherly solicitude,
decided to organize a Relief Society in each
little branch of his ward; he therefore called a
general meeting of the sisters, for the purpose
of dissolving the society, and to divide the
stock on hand among the several branches.
The total stock on hand is two hundred and
eighty-eight dollars and thirteen cents.
	We met again on the 19th inst. to reorganize
the old branch of South Morgan. The elec-
tions were as follows: Mrs. Lydia Rich Presi-
dent, Mrs. Mary Ann Simmons, and Mrs. Ann
Fry Counselors; Mrs. Rawb Secretary; Mrs.
Annie Lesnell, Treasurer. Our beloved Bish-
op being very sick, E[-]ders R. Fry, W. Edding-
ton and D. Ball addressed the meeting and or-
dained the officers.
	I hope that you will hear oftener, and good
news from this corner of the vineyard, for I
feel vividly impressed that the harvest is close
at hand. And now, good-bye dear little EX-
PONENT, may thy pithy columns, impart as
much warmth and comfort of soul to every
weary head and heart, as they do to
			Your sister in the Gospel,
				ESTHER C. E. Francis.

	P. S.—Would it not be a good plan, for
every Society to keep a copy of the EXPONENT
smooth and clean, to be bound after awhile, in
a handsome volume? And so keep a Record
of every Branch of this great and glorious en-
terprise; namely, the Relief Society of Utah.
							E. C. E. F.


MRS. KING.—We are pleased to announce to
the many friends of Mrs. Hannah T. King
that she has secured the agency for Holman's
great Crown Family Bible, the most superb
edition ever published in this country.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]

FROM BEAVER TO ST. GEORGE.

	From Beaver the road is interesting af-
ter leaving the main highway, when it
takes an easterly direction through the fa-
mous Fremont-pass, gradually growing nar-
rower until the rocks are from two to three
hundred feet, perpendicular, ragged, and ir-
regular, with deep fissures where the ce-
dar and pine have thrown their firm,
strong roots and resist the fierce gales that
sweep like avenging spirits over mountain
and glen. Six miles from the entrance
some small springs are found, which sup-
ply the tired traveler with the cool refresh-
ing draught of clear, soft, water; passing
along, mountain and hill-side are covered
with the verdure of the evergreen trees
and shrubs and the simple though beauti-
ful wild flowers of which there is a great
variety, lark-spurs, roses, violets, and the
sage, in whose deep, beautiful cup the
honey-bee revels joyously until the last
leaf drops faded forever; the aspen in smal
groves here and there quivers in the
breeze, as if truly in affright (as tradition
has it) at the most solemn scene of the Sav-
ior's crucifixion.
	The road from the springs for some dis-
tance is very uneven and rocky, opening
presently into a small valley within
whose bounds the Prarie Dog, a species of
Marmot lives in comparative security and
peace, greeting the traveler at times with
his short snapping bark. Of two roads here,
one diverging to the right passes almost
directly through Bear Valley, cutting off,
it is said, some miles of rocky road, the oth-
er continuing on almost in the same direc-
tion as at first, passes over the hills reach-
ing the Sevier, some miles below the other,
crossing on its way a long stretch called
the five mile rocky hill. The road leading
through Bear Valley Cañon is most delight-
ful; the bottom lands covered in some pla-
ces with a species of wheat grass, tall and
thrifty, also the common grass which cat-
tle prefer, in abundance; tiny springs form
a small stream called Bear Creek which
flows into the Sevier a short distance be-
low the mouth of the cañon.
	Along the Sevier the country presents a
different aspect, dotted here and there with
farm houses and ranch buildings, many of
which are now deserted as the range in
many places has been completely over-
stocked with cattle. Many very thrifty
farms are seen, with beautiful meadows
yielding a large supply of hay; wheat and
corn are raised in abundance. The scenery
is grandly sublime in many places. Arriv-
ing at Panguitch, (so called from the lake
above, which provides the inhabitants with
a plentiful supply of fish and a clear stream
of soft water flowing through all parts
of the town;) here we find an abundance of
excellent butter and cheese; very few veg-
etables being raised within its limits; quite
a number of the inhabitants spend their
summers ranching, and because of this the
place has a somewhat untidy appearance.
Here a very good bridge crosses the Se-
vier about a mile from the town, the only
one in this part of the country as the stream
is forded in many places, and grows gradu-
ally smaller as the road proceeds toward
the rim of the basin, the dividing line be-
tween the northern and southern slope; as
the Sevier loses its tributaries it becomes
a bright, clear stream only the size of a
common creek; the water soft and of a de-
licious coolness issuing from a cañon on the
right, supplied with springs far up among
the pines, where those fond of fish can ex-
ercise their patience by catching, with hook
and line, the spotted mountain trout, or

[Column 2]

gather, in its season, the wild fruit which
grows here in abundance. Mr. Wm. Asa
resides at the mouth of the cañon, and trav-
elers often find rest and refreshment here,
in this solitude.
	Leaving this place a small stream on the
left flowing from springs near the rim of
the basin, is covered in spots with a curious
plant that seems to float on the water, bear-
ing small white flowers, very much resem-
bling the common plum blossom, and delic-
iously fragrant, having long vine-like stems
and feathery leaves. The road gradually
ascending to Cutter's ranch, a distance of
two miles, diverging to the right, traverses
a long swell of wire-grass land; the hill
sides along are not as steep here, and are
covered with tall pines. Cliffs of red and
white sand stone, castellated and strangely
picturesque arise here and there forming
most beautiful scenery, and one might eas-
ily imagine they were the remains of some
ruined city of Old.
	Entering the pine forest after a drive of
two or three miles, a small log cabin on a
knoll is the next sign of life and habitation,
though only a snmmer residence, the place
being used for a sheep ranch. Cattle de-
sert the grass land below during the hot
weather on account of the large fly which
is too annoying to endure. The road from
here is a delightful drive through open
glades, sunny nooks, and shady dells, grad-
ually ascending, thus bringing the traveler
to the RIM of the great basin of North
America; from whence the descent passes
through a dense pine forest interspersed
with plants and flowers whose rich perfume
floats out on the summer air, mingling with
that of pine and cedar, bringing as it were,
new life to both body and mind, and charm-
ing the eye with varied tints and changing
hues.
	MORE ANON.


[Column 1]

EXECUTIVENESS.
	There is a marvellous difference among
individuals with respect to their accom-
plishing work. Some pass a lifetime in do-
ing what amounts to nothing. They seem
born to 'putter,' to waste themselves on
trifling, weak, useless tasks; mere human
lichens are they, parasites, chips on the cur-
rent of life, drifting aimlessly. Others have
every day something to show for their
time:
	"Something accomplished, something done,
	Hath earned a night's repose."
These possess a purpose and energy, a strong
will, a clear perception, and often an active
imagination; all of which combined, render
them motors in society. Though a great
deal depends on original endowment, a
great deal also depends on training. Child-
ren who are brought up to do nothing, gen-
erally do nothing. Those who are trained
from infancy in all ways of self-help and
helpfulness to others, have the ability to
help themselves and help others. "To him
that hath shall be given." Lord Bacon says:
"There is no stand or impediment in the
wit but may be wrought out by fit studies.
If a man's wit be wandering, let him study
mathematics; for in demonstrations, if his
wit be called away never so little, he must
begin again; so every defect of mind may
have a special receipt." This last asser-
tion is as true in matters pertaining to char-
acter as in those intellectual. The purpose-
less individual may, if he will, change him-
self in this particular, by setting himself
tasks to perform, and steadily performing
them. This may be found at first laborious;

[Column 2]

and even painful; but habit renders all
things easy.
	This setting one's self tasks, however,
does not as surely develop one's powers as
having these tasks set by some one else;
and of all taskmasters, stern necessity has
the most to show for his pupils on exami-
nation day. Let no one who loves himself
repine at the commands of this stern task-
master; his mission is most beneficient.
From what rough and crude material does
he often fashion noble, accomplished, and
beneficient men and women! This he does
by laying heavy burdens on them, by sub-
jecting them to hard and cruel blows from
the hand of fortune, by compelling them to
make brick without straw, by forcing
them to develop every resource of their
power, and often every capability they pos-
sess for doing and suffering.
	It is useless for one without a definite
purpose or plan to dream of accomplishing
much. Few indeed are they upon whom
this greatness is thrust. The doers in this
life are those who intend to do; all others
are cumberers of the ground—thistles,
weeds, unfruitful figtrees destined to per-
ish. It is a very easy matter to spend day
after day and week after week in merely
going through the forms of doing, and at
the end of this lime have actually nothing
accomplished. The butterfly doubtless vis-
its as many flowers as the bee, the grass-
hopper goes over as much ground as the
ant, but how different the showings in the
two contrasted cases. A purpose animates
the bee and the ant; one moves in a "bee
line" to the accomplishment of her pur-
pose, the other works with such persistent
industry that for 3,000 years at least the ant
has been an example known and read of by
as many as have studied her ways.
	A great deal of the time of even purpose-
ful men and women is wasted for want of a
well digested plan of action. The execu-
tive individual has a pretty clear idea, first
of exactly what he wishes to accomplish,
and secondly of the manner in which
he intends to work. He does not blunder
into a thing and blunder on all the way
through it. He may not see what his last
step will be, but he sees quite clearly what
his first step must be, and then the second,
and, as he progresses, the end becomes
clear. He does not allow himself to be di-
verted or detained from his work by slight
causes, petty hindrances, trivial interrup-
tions. If one stops to follow all the little
side-paths that lead off from the main thor-
ough-fare from Joppa to Jerusalem he will
be a long time in reaching that ancient cap-
ital. The way to secure main issues is to
neglect all smaller ones that have not a di-
rect bearing on the success aimed at.—A
woman writer in the New York Tribune.—


HINTS TO WRITERS AND SPEAKERS.

	William Cullen Bryant gave the follow-
ing excellent advice to a young man who
offered him an article for the "Evening
Post:"
	My young friend, I observe that you
have used several French expressions in
your article. I think if you will study the
English language, that you will find it cap-
able of expressing all the ideas you may
have.
	I have always found it so, and in all that
I have written, I do not recall an instance
where I was tempted to use a foreign word,
but that on searching I found a better one
in my own language.
	Be simple, unaffected; be honest in your
speaking and writing. Never use a long


word when a short one will do. Call a
spade, a spade, not a well-known oblong in-
strument of manual industry; let a home
be a home, and not a residance; a place a
place, and not a locality; and so with the
rest. Where a short word will do, you lose
by using a long one. You lose in clearness;
you lose in honest expression of meaning,
and in the estimation of all men who are
competent to judge, you lose in reputation
for ability.
	The only way to shine, even in this false
world, is to be modest and unassuming.
Falsehood may be a very thick crust, but in
the course of time it will find a place to
break through. Elegance of language may
not be in the power of all of us, but sim-
plicity and straightforwardness are.
	Write much as you would speak; speak
as you think. If with your inferior, speak no
coarser than usual; if with your superior,
speak no finer. Be what you say, and within
the rules of prudence. No one was ever a
gainer by singularity of words or in pronun-
ciation. The truly wise man will speak
that no one will observe how he speaks. A
man may show a great knowledge of chem-
istry by carrying about bladders of strange
gases to breathe, but he will enjoy better
health, and find more time for business,
who lives on common air.
	Sidney Smith once remarked: After you
have written an article, take your pen and
strike out half the words, and you will be
surprised to find how much stronger it is.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


WOMAN'S VOICE.

	Sisters do we understand why we are
placed here upon this earth? Do we fully
realize the responsibility resting upon us?
The duty of woman upon this earth is to
"bear the souls of men," and prepare them-
selves for a future existence. Each per-
son has to work out his or her own salva-
tion. How necessary it then is for us to
live in obedience to the laws of God as giv-
en through the mouth of His prophet. If
we cannot live in obedience to those laws
here, how can we expect to live in heaven
and enjoy His presence. It is necessary for
us to lay a firm foundation while we dwell
here in time, that we may have something
to build upon in eternity. Teach our child-
ren by example as well as by precept, train
them with all diligence to walk in the
ways of the Lord.
	We know that light, knowledge, intelli-
gence and wisdom are ours if we seek them
diligently in humility. We also know that
the end draweth nearer and nearer, and the
hour of trial for all who dwell upon the
earth is near at hand, and will be terribly
severe; and that His work will be cut short
in righteousness.
	If we do all we can that is right, learn
all we can that will benefit us now and
hereafter, we will not have done more than
God will require of us. Let us keep the
spirit of God continually in our hearts,
having charity for each other's failings,
striving hard meanwhile, to overcome our
own short comings, and with the help of
God if we do these things, we will conquer
in the end.
	D. E. DUDLEY.
Clifton, Idaho, March 8, 1877.

	WELLSVILLE, Feb. 10, 1877.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	I will give you a few items of a general
nature respecting woman's work and pro-
gress.  In our meetings many of the sisters
young and old arise and bear faithful and


earnest testimonies to the truth of the ev-
erlasting Gospel. I believe the sisters who
covenanted to eschew round dancing have
faithfully kept their covenants inviolate.
	The majority of the people are earnestly
striving to live by every word that pro-
ceedeth out of the mouth of God, and their
subscriptions to the Logan Temple, and all
other public and private benefits, bear am-
ple testimony. A few among us, when the
bridegroom cometh, may be caught nap-
ping and our lamps devoid of oil, but the
gospel net catches of all kinds.
	Considerable sickness has prevailed among
the people inthis valley—diphtheria among
the children, but we are in the hands of
God and have full faith in His guidance.
With prayers continually for your success
in your work of love, I remain your sister
and fellow-laborer in the cause of truth, and
for the redemption of Zion.	M. E. M.


PATIENCE AND PERSEVERENCE.

	Patience is one of the powers of endur-
ance. Perseverance is an auxiliary and
these help us to be resigned to our lots in
life. If true patience characterizes our
every act, how much happier we would all
be. We should study the dispositions of
those we are associated with at home,
around our fireside first, and if little errors
are committed be patient over them. Be
patient with the dear little ones, with fa-
ther and mother and especially with the
aged who depend upon us for comfort and
support. Remember while we are patient
with those we love and respect, how others
who love us have been patient with our
weaknesses in the past, and will be, we
trust in the future, till the end of our days.
A wife and mother who is truly forbearing
at all times, thus adds greatly to the happi-
ness of her husband and makes a little
Eden for herself and children, and if sick-
ness or trouble come s in any form, let us
study how best to bear it with resignation
and strength of character. Let us help
what we can, but if it is beyond what we
can control let us not fret about it, for it
makes all around us unhappy, to see us
meeting trouble half way, it also displeas-
es God who understands what is for our
good, and only gives us trials, that through
them we may become sanctified, and if we
live in accordance with the best light we
can obtain, and bear patiently all we are
called to endure, we will be satisfied and
happy here, and prepared to dwell with the
just hereafter. Let us choose the sunny
side of life, and not glance too often at the
dark side of the picture. Life is too short
to spend it in vain regrets.
	MARY STUART.
Hyde Park, March 11, 1877


R. S. REPORTS.

	PARIS, ONEIDA Co., Feb. 18, 1877.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	I thought a few lines from this place
might be acceptable to the many readers of
your excellent paper. The Lord has seen
fit to favor the inhabitants of Bear Lake
valley with a mild winter, for which we
feel truly thankful. The little snow we
have had (which would not exceed one foot)
has mostly disappeared.
	The saints as a general thing feel well;
we have very many meetings and they are
well attended; very few parties and thea-
tres. I never knew a time when the inter-
est of the young and rising generation was
so much at heart and sought after by our


President and Bishop and those whose right
it is to look after and preside over this
stake of Zion, as at the present time. They
are ever ready with their timely advice
and counsel to young and old. We have
good week-day and Sabbath schools which
are well attended. Our Relief and Re-
trenchment meetings are excellent, Pres't.
Rich often meets with us. We have made
an effort to purchase grain, but are sorry to
say that it is but a feeble one compared
with some of the accounts we see in the
EXPONENT. Our crops last year were most-
ly cut off by the frost and it is rather a
hard matter to lay up grain where there is
none: but we mean to "try, try again," and
my faith is that "where there's a will
there's a way."
	There has been some sickness during the
winter, mostly among the children not
many deaths have occurred however, except
in Montpelier where a number of children
have died from a disease, resembling in
some respects Diphtheria. I do not consider
it incurable when taken in time. Many
people let diseases run too long without
treatment, leaving nature to perform a task
by far superior to her strength.
	Your valuable paper is read with interest
by many of the sisters and brethren too,
and for its welfare and advancement you
have our best wishes.	EMELINE RICH.


MALAD CITY, March 10, 1877.
EDITORS EXPONENT:
	As we have lately held our annual meet-
ing at this place, it may not be amiss if you
have space, to publish our report, &c., for
the year ending, Jun. 31st, 1877. We have
one hundred and sixteen members.
	Amounts collected............... $209.00
	Disbursed for different charita-
	able purposes..........——.....	102.00
	Amount on hand.................	107.00
							———
			Total....................	209.00
	The members, with but very few excep-
tions, attend their meetings regularly, and
perform other duties connected with our
holy calling. We can truly say that our
Society is in a flourishing condition; we
are trying to assist our brethren who are
called to labor in the ministry, to establish
truth and righteousness in this part of the
Lord's vineyard. Although we have con-
siderable opposition here, as the wicked are
laboring hard for the supremacy, our motto
is onward, the Lord being our helper, until
all the enemies of truth are subdued.
	We desire also to be one with our sisters
in the different settlements of Utah, to fol-
low out the counsels of our beloved Presi-
dent, Brigham Young, with regard to pur-
chasing and storing grain. Our worthy
Bishop Daniels has been kind enough to
proffer us room in his granary to store up
our wheat. A committee of four has been
appointed to do the buying, and they are
busy in the work. With the help of our
brethren, who are always ready and willing
to assist us, we mean to gather in all we
can this season, and have it secured for a
day of want.
	We hold our meetings once in two weeks
and they are well attended. We have
great cause to feel well, as everything seems
conducive to our happiness; no sign of sick-
ness: prosperity and plenty seem to abound
in all the habitations of the Latter-day
Saints in this beautiful valley. The snow
is all gone; farming and gardening have
commenced; the prospects are favorable for
a good crop this season. We have been
called upon by our bishop to do what we


can towards building the temple. We al-
ways desire to be co-workers with our breth-
ren and to respond to their calls. We have
made up a subscription list of $400.00 for
1877. Hoping that our lives may be spared
to see a temple to the Lord finished in Salt
Lake City. We feel that we have been
highly honored in being selected out from
amongst this wicked and perverse genera-
tion, to help bring about the purposes of
the Almighty in this last dispensation of
the fulness of times.
	We also consider that our responsibilities
are very great indeed, as mothers and
daughters in Israel; and our duties are
many in directing the minds of the young
in the ways of the Lord. May we all of us
at all times strive to teach and practice cor-
rect principles; may we all enjoy a greater
portion of the Spirit of the Lord, which
will yield us happiness in this world, and
salvation in the world to come, is our earn-
est desire.
	JANE PRICE, President,
	ANN DANIELS,
	ESTHER LUSK,		Couselors,
	CATHERINE DAVIS,	Secretary.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


HYGIENIC NOTES.

A SPECIFIC FOR SMALL-POX.

	Saltpetre was accidently discovered to be a
specific for this loathsome disease. It was in the
following manner:
	A middle-aged lady, the mother of six child-
ren, had often sought relief from pain in the back
by taking saltpetre and brandy. Small-pox was
prevailing in the vicinity. Mrs.——was expos-
ed to it, and contracted the disease. The premon-
itory symptoms were very violent—fever extremely
high—severe pain in the head, and excruciating
pain in the region of the kidneys.
	A physician was called during the night, but,
in doubt as to the nature of the disease, though
suspecting it to be a case of small-pox, he made
no prescription, promising to reaurn at early
dawn. Fever and pain increasing she begged her
husband to prepare for her the old preparation of
saltpetre and brandy. The latter was then not to
be had, but he crushed a piece of nitre as large as
a common white bean. This she took in a tea-
spoonful of cold water. Feeling some better the
dose was once or twice repeated. Pain soon sub-
sided and she slept well during the remainder of
the night and awakened feeling perfectly well!
When the doctor called he was greatly astonished
to see Mrs.——superintending the affairs of her
houshold. He left without making any pre-
scription. She had sixty well defined pustules in
her face; but they were but slightly inflamed and
not at all painful. The development of smallpox
on her entire person were in number and appear-
ance in keeping with those on her face.
	In due time all her children and her hnsband
were similarly affected by fever and pain in the
head and back, and received similar treatment
and the results were the same. Several families
caught the prevailing contagion, to whom——
told his story and recommended the same remedy,
and in every case the results were favorable.
	These facts came to us at first hand, and the
reader may rely upon them as exact statements
without embelishments or exaggeration. Here
were from ten to twelve cases all relieved from
pain and fever in less than an hour. The pustules
were speedily developed, but were more like the
disease in its convalescent stages than at any oth-
er period. As to the quantity given, it was not
at all definite, but the first patient, within an
hour, must have taken of nitre the bulk of three
ordinary white navy beans.
	We give these statements because we learn
from various sources that smallpox is unusually
prevalent. Physicians and cautious nurses would
do well to give nitre a fair and full trial. The


cases to which we refer were those of active labor-
ing people living in small tenements, and in the
winter season. It will be well to give the alleged
specific a fair trial. Physicians should do so and
report results.
	We cannot state from personal knowledge, but
we are under the impression that all these pa-
tients had been vaccinated in their youth.—Jour-
nal of Commerce.


MRS. Emma Malloy, of Indiana, has been
lecturing in Vermont. Mrs. Malloy is one
of those brave women who dares to speak
in unmeasured terms of hypocrisy and
pride in churches and among the religious
sects of the day. She is whole-souled in
the cause of Temperance. In one of her
lectures she quoted these words of Theodore
Cuyler—"Some people are more afraid of
soiling carpets than soiling the souls of
men.” 
	THE sweetest and most signal revenge to in-
flict upon enemies who seek to belittle our la-
bors or under-estimate our abilities, is to do
everything well, to lead irreproachable lives,
to earn popular confidence and respect, to es-
chew all but laudable undertakings, to succeed
in every act and labor. Success is the most ef-
fectual reproach to envy, malice, and unfriend-
liness.

WOMAN'S EXPONENT.
VOL. 5.	SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH. APRIL 15, 1877.	No. 22.


WELCOME TO SPRING.

O beauteous Spring! fragrant of leaf and bloom.
	Nature with myriad welcomes hails thy birth;
Thou breathest, and lo! a rich and sweet perfume,
	Rises like incense from the gladdened earth.

O gent!e spring! thy presence everywhere
	Renews the life-pulse, even in earth's cold heart,
And from its bosom, wild-buds and blossoms fair
	In rich profusion, all spontaneous start.

Whence come ye fairy-footed, gay with flowers?
	Lightly ye tripped o'er mountain hill and plain;
The song-birds herald thee from Eden's bowers,
	And, with sweetest music, usher in thy reign,

Trilling their notes till answering echoes ring
	From wood, and glen, and fountain's mellowed flow:
And mortal hearts a silent offering bring,
	And bathed in sunlight, nature's landscapes glow.

And hope springs up afresh in saddened lives,
	And clouds disperse and heaven again is clear,
And tenderest trust, and confidence revives,
	For spring has come, and beautified the year.

Thou comest a harbinger from courts above;
	That we may realize, God's promises are sure;
Thou scatterest blessings, all replete with love,
	And givest us faith, earth's trials to endure.

Hail b'essed type of Morn, whose roseate light,
	Shall bring the tidings of a holier birth—
The Resurrection, by whose power and might
	The sleeping millions will be ushered forth.
		EMILE.
S. L. City, April 13, 1877.


[Column 1]
	A TRUE TALE OF THE PAST
			AND PRESENT.

	When on the Island of Tupuai, South
Pacific Ocean, a boy was given me, when an
infant one year old. His father, a brilliant
young man, native of New York, was a
missionary companion of my husband, Eld-
er Addison Pratt. Having remained there
for several years, he married a native wo-
man from the Chain Island group; the issue
was three sons, one of which we, (my hus-
band and myself) adopted. We brought
him over the seas when he was two years
old, in 1852. In 1858 I brought him to
Utah. He was a smart, bright, boy, and I
took pride in teaching him. He remained
with me till he was sixteen, his father be-
ing absent most of the time. He then left
me without permission, and went with a
freighter to Montana. Through various
vicissitudes, he became an express rider.
On one of his trips, in 1870, he was cap-
tured by the Black Foot Indians, robbed of
the mail, stripped of all his clothes, and
left seventy miles from any habitation. He
managed to reach Ft. Hawley almost frozen
and starved; after recuperating he was in-
duced to ingage in the same business, which
he followed one year longer, and was then
captured by the Sioux Indians; he was kept
two years confined to one lodge; but after
that had more priviliges granted him; he
became so accustomed to their habits, and
not being cruelly treated, felt disposed to
remain with them until circumstances
should render it practicable for him to
make his escape. In the meantime apply-
ing himself with all diligence to study
their character, their methods of warfare,
and to know every inch of their country;
thinking that in some future day his knowl-
edge would be of service to himself and he
of service to his country. It was not until
1875 that he found an opportunity to es-

[Column 2]
cape, having been five years the protege of
the famous "Sitting Bull." We named
him "Ephraim Pratt," while away he took
the name of his real father Frank Grou-
ard; he went to a military station, and
was immediately appropriated by General
Crook; as guide and scout in his command.
His father, Mr. B. F. Grouard, seeing his
name in the papers wrote to him to ascer-
tain who he was. The mystery was then
solved he was found to be my long lost boy;
lost to me ten years. I had followed him
with my prayers, and thankful was I that
in his childhood I had taught him to be-
lieve in God and pray to Him. I address-
ed a letter to Fort Fetterman, and received
in return one of the most pathetic, penitent
and impressive letters I ever read; giving
me an account of his adventures and suffer-
ings, imploring in a most humble manner,
forgiveness for leaving me abruptly, and
saying "how dearly he had paid for his
waywardness and disobedience in leaving
so good a mother, who was always kind and
used every means to make him a good man."
The family and neighbors were excited and
moved to tears when the letter was read;
his photograph was sent, as natural as life;
a well-dressed gentleman, no look of an In-
dian about him, it is to us almost like the
resurrection of the dead. The reporters
write every thing in his praise, as a "first
class cabinet officer," "chief of the northern
scouts, braver than any Sioux," etc. His
ready brain and hands are said to be "prac-
tically invaluable." One thing which inter-
ests us very much is that he has found a
white girl among the Sioux, and from the
description given we are greatly in hopes it
is Bro. Thurstons child, stolen from Cache
Valley in 1867, and grand daughter of EId-
er Erastus Snow. My son described her in
his letter as a pale, delicate girl, apparently
about sixteen years old; the Indians are
kind to her, they keep her making bead-
work; she knows not a word of English."
He farther says, "I have twice tried to cap-
ture her, but did not succeed, I think I
shall yet accomplish it."
	I have written to Bro. Snow and received
in return his heartfelt acknowledgement;
promising his faith and prayers; and his ad-
vice to the young man was that he would
advise him to attempt nothing so hazard-
ous as her capture would be, only in the fear
of God, and by so doing, and acting with
great caution, he feels he would be sure of
success. I copied the letter and sent it to
my son. I feel to pray most earnestly that
such an event may transpire, and the dear
one, whoever she may be, restored to her
yearning parents if they still live, and most
certainly the complication of these peculiar
circumstances would constrain us to ac-
knowledge a Divine Providence in the
whole transaction, and to thank our Heaven-
ly Father for His all-preserving care. I ex-
pect my son soon to visit me.
						L. B. PRATT.
	Beaver, March 31st, 1877.


WE shall find difficulties in all great en-
terprises; if we are sure we have begun
them from God we may securely cast all
events upon his providence, which knows
how to dispose and how to end them.


NOTES AND NEWS.

	SENATOR EMERSON'S bill making women
eligible for school officers has been recon-
sidered and passed by the New York Sen-
ate.
	THE LEGISLATURE of Minnessota has
passed a bill allowing women to practice
law in all courts of the state. The vote in
the House stood sixty-three to thirty. In
the Senate twenty-six to six.

	OLIVER AMES died at his home in Eas-
ton, Mass., March 9. Mr. Ames was a lead-
ing man "in projecting and completing the
Union Pacific Railroad, and was its presi-
dent at the time of his death." He was
an upright business man and his death is a
a public loss.

	ONE OF the most novel occurrences of re-
cent date was the examination by a phren-
ologist of the head of the Rev. Mrs. Phebe
A. Hanaford. It having been decided that
she was not to occupy the pulpit in the
church where she had heretofore been sus-
tained; her adherents fixed upon this pecu-
liar plan for the establishment of her claims
to the right of the pulpit. Mr. Graham,
the phrenological expert, gave her a most
superior character in every respect, and
pronounced her superiorily qualified to hold
the position. Of course her opponents were
chagrined.

	THE BOSTON "Woman's Journal" gives a
short notice of a Woman's Temperance
meeting held in Fremont Temple, Mrs.
Mary A. Livermore presiding who made a
brief address. She was followed by other
well-known women who are laboring with
zeal and efficiency for Temperance. It was
very evident from the character of the ad-
dress, that women can speak for Temper-
ance. Why cannot they vote for it? Sim-
ply because they are women and have not
the ballot." How inconsistent it is that
women must endure all the trials of drnnken
husbands, brothers and fathers, yet they are
not in any public capacity whatever allowed
to act even for their own protection, against
their abuses. May God speed the day when
woman will be sufficiently strong by right
of law to maintain a position securely
against the abuse and misuse of power.
How is it possible in view of the many evils
which exist for any woman to fail to see
the want of woman's influence in the right
of the ballot.


BLUE STOCKINGS:—A charming club
which existed in the time of Johnson had
for its most prominent member Mr. Still-
ingfleet, who wore blue stockings, and was
so distinguished for the brilliancy and wit
of his conversation that when absent the
other members would exclaim, "we can do
nothing without blue stockings." In this
way the title of the club became established,
and the name drifted into a soubriquet for
all who affected literature. The earliest
mention of a "blue stocking" occurs in a
Greek comedy entitled "The Banquet of
Plutarch."

[Column 1]
			R. S. REPORTS.

	The General Retrenchment meetings in
the Fourteenth Ward Assembly Rooms
grow more and more interesting, and it is
observable that the Junior societies are fast
gaining in knowledge and understanding
of spiritual gifts, blessings and attainments.
The meeting on Satuaday, March 17, was
crowded to overflowing; the Spirit of God
seemed poured out in rich abundance. The
sisters who addressed the meeting dwelt
most particularly upon home industries
among women. Among the speakers were
President Mrs. M. I. Horne, Counselors
Miss E. R. Snow, Mrs, E. Howard, and
Mrs. B. W. Smith, Mrs. E. S. Barney and
many in the congregation who bore faithful
testimonies of the Latter-day Work. Mrs.
E. A. Whitney sang a Song of Zion, and
gladdened every heart in the assembly.
	On Saturday, March 31, the General
Meeting was not quite so full as usnal,
owing to the inclemency of the weather.
Pres. Mrs. M. I. Horne addressed the sis-
ters upon the duties and obligations of
mothers which rested upon them in caring
for their families, spoke upon the preserva-
tion of health and the care of children; of
home industries, and of sustaining home
institutions, that the saints might have some
resources when Babylon should fall. Coun-
selor Mrs. B. W. Smith spoke upon the sub-
jects appropriate to the times, and exhort-
ed the sisters to greater diligence. Mrs.
E. S. Barney spoke about silk-culture, the
planting out of mulberry trees, the home
manufactures of Matthews & Co. Mrs. M.
E. Watmough, Mrs. W. East and others
spoke very earnestly and impressively on
the principles of the Latter-day work.
Mrs. E. B. Wells read a paragraph from the
Book of Doctrines and Covenants, expressed
herself in regard to some particular points
of the Gospel as understood and practised
by the saints, allnded to the great work be-
ing done by the Women of Utah, under
the several organizations which exlst.

			OGDEN, March 21st, 1877.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	Our Annual meeting of the Relief Socie-
ty, which should have been held Dec. 16,
1876, was postponed until March 13, owing
to sickness caused by the Small pox, which
made many sad and sorrowful hearts, and
I trust through it we have been learned
to place a greater reliance upon our Heav-
enly Father, "who doeth all things well,"
and may we as Latter-day Saints ever be
ready and willing to put our trust in God.
	At our meeting of March 13th, we were
much pleased and gratified with the pleas-
ure of having Miss Eliza R. Snow and Mrs.
Zina D. Young with us. Meeting was con-
ducted by President Mrs. Jane S. Richards.
The Secretary read the minntes and finan-
cial report of the society.
	Miss E. R. Snow spoke in her usual unas-
suming manner, showing us that being of
the blood of Abraham, we were lawful
heirs to all the blessings promised to his
seed; that we had been gathered out from
the world, but what would it profit us
if we did not live up to what we had re-
ceived, as we were to be judged by our
works. She spoke of the many duties and
labors of the sisters, and exhorted us to be
true and faithful.
	Mrs. Z. D. Young said there were two
things she was proud of; "her religion and
her sisters," and warned us against a fault-
finding spirit; she said we have one God,

[Column 2]
one Savior, and work for one purpose, the
establishing of Zion.
	Mrs. J. S. Richards prayed our memories
might be strengthened that we might be
able to retain the good instructions we had
received and profit by them.
	Many of the sisters bore their testimony
to the Gospel work, and the spirit of God
was in our midst, and all felt blessed. The
good instructions cheered every heart. In
the evening we had a parlor entertainment
which was largely attended, and I trust
that Miss Snow and Mrs. Young were as
pleased with their visit as we were. Our
President Mrs. J. S. Richards has gone to
St. George to attend Conference.
				A. M. FRODSHAM, Sec'y.

	PARIS, BEAR LAKE Co., Feb. 18, 1877.
EDITORS EXPONENT:
	I am happy to say that our Relief Society
is in a flourishing condition and a good
spirit prevails both in it, and also among
the people in general; there is a great im-
provement here among the young. The
Young Ladies' Retrenchment Association
is exceedingly prosperous, they hold their
meetings once in two weeks, and manifest
a deep interest in trying to aid and assist
in obeying the counsel of those that are
placed to preside over them. Our Young
Men's Mutual Improvement Association is
also doing well, and a spirit of improve-
ment prevails among the rising generation.
The winter has been very mild with but
very little snow, but we have had consider-
able sickness mostly among the children,
but with good care and the skillful treat-
ment of Mrs. Emeline Rich and others, and
the faith and prayers of the faithful but few
have died. Our Society takes deep interest
in saving up grain, we think it a subject of
vital importance, and although we live in a
climate that is subject to early frosts, and
many have to buy their breadstuff from
year to year and bring it from other settle-
ments, yet we feel a determination to do
what we can to obey the voice of the good
shepherd, knowing that the Lord will not
command His people to do anything that
they cannot perform. It is through obedi-
ence we realize the greatest blessings. Let
us be wise and do all that is required of us,
that we may be prepared for every coming
event, for we know that the nations of the
earth are ripening for destruction, and the
time is near at hand when God will pour
out His judgments upon them with famine
and pestilence. Not only do we hear of war
in foreign lands, but also in our boasted land
of liberty they are divided, and the time
is near at hand when he that will not take
up his sword against his neighbor must
needs flee to Zion for safety. Then let us
be wise and prepared for these things, that
we may be counted wise stewards and be
found with oil in our lamps and numbered
among the wise virgins.
	The EXPONENT is read with deep inter-
est, and is a great advantage to us in this
isolated place, and although we cannot have
the society of our sisters that are visiting
the different settlements, yet we feel the in-
fluence of their good counsel through the
EXPONENT; but we live in hopes when
spring dawns upon us we shall be favored
with a visit from some of our worthy sis-
ters from Salt Lake.
	Praying for your success in the good
work in which you are engaged, I remain
as ever your sister in the Gospel of peace.
			MRS. JULIA P. LINDSAY.
						Pres't.  R. S.

[Column 3]
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	On the 19th of March the good people of
Ephraim had a visit from Bro. W. G.
Young, and his kinsman, John Young Jun.
son of John Young Sen. deceased, also Elders
James Works of Manti, and Reuben Alred
of Spring City. A meeting was held at
the sisters Hall at two o'clock, p. m., when
Bro. W. G. Young addressed the assem-
bly and gave many valuable instructions
both to young and old. He particularly
cautioned the young sisters against going
away from home, said they had much bet-
ter stay at home and wear home-spun and
wooden shoes. Counseled the brethren of
whom a number were present, to be ener-
getic in giving to their dwellings a more
home-like appearance. He dwelt earnestly
upon teaching children to speak the truth
at all times. Bro. Works also addressed the
meeting, spoke in a very interesting and
instructive manner, both of the past and of
the future. Bishop Petersen made a few
closing remarks, bore testimony that the
sisters had been a great help to the Bish-
ops; also of their willingness in responding
to the calls made upon them at all times.
At the close of the meeting, Sister Peter-
se[-], our President, called for a vote of
thanks to be given to the brethren, which
met with a hearty response from all.
	With best wishes for your happiness and
the success of the EXPONENT, I am your
sister, Respectfully,
			AGNES S. ARMSTRONG,
								Sec'y.

			FAYETTE, SANPETE Co.,
						March 21, 1877.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	I send you a brief report of our Relief
Society which was organized Sept. 12, 1875
by President John Bartholomew, and Elder
John Metcalf, as follows: Mrs. Jane A.
Brown, president; Mrs. Eliza R. Bartholo-
mew, and Mrs. Polly Bartholomew coun-
selors; Mrs. Charlotle E. Miller, Secretary;
Mrs. Emma Bartholomew, Assistant Sec'y.;
Mrs. Amy Miller, Treasurer. There are
twenty-one members in the society and we
meet every first Thursday in the month to
give in our reports also to receive counsel
and instruction. We also have our sewing
meetings which are well atteuded and the
spirit of God prevails in our midst. We
have not much means at our command, but
still are in a prosperous condition, the Lord
blesses us in all our undertakings to serve
Him. He also blesses us with health and
strength, and willing hearts to perform all
that is required of us.
	We feel that the storing up of grain is of
the Lord, we have on hand quite a quantity
and will add to it as fast as we can. By the
courtesy of our President John Bartholo-
mew, our wheat is stored in his granary till
we can get one built for our own use.
There is a good rock meeting house in this
place for which we, (the R. S.), have made
matts and carpets, we also purchased a
stove and the window furniture, so you see
beloved sisters, that we can always find
something to do; I contemplate introduc-
ing the silk culture.
	Last January on the 22d, Sisters Williams
and Madsen paid us a visit which was a
real feast to us; our desire ever since then
has been for the sisters who have a large
experience to visit us whenever they can
make it convenient.
	Your sisters in the Gospel,
			JANE A. BROWN, Pres't.
				AMY MILLER, Treasurer.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.		171


[For the EXPONENT.
		A PROMISE.

Mine eyes! mine eyes! rain happy tears!
For ye shall in your mortal life
Look on your Saviour, on your God.
O blessed eyes! that live to-day—
Weep all your sorrow, shade away,
Ye shall look on the living Lord;
Mine eyes! mine eyes! look only up
To heaven's pure and sinless height.
O that no vision dark, profane,
Of stained earth, might intervene
To leave its blemish-touch upon
Their mirror gaze. O blessed eyes!
Weep happy tears! let morning's pure,
And silent night's cool, holy breath
Waft far away all earthly scenes—
For thou shalt look upon thy Christ.
O might it be, that when His eyes
Read through mine own, the heart below,
They shall be clear as pebbled stream
Through wh[-]ch we see the flow.

O blessed hands! and shall ye lie
In the pure clasp of Christ's thy Lord?
Thrill, blessed hands, with holy flame,
That Christ the pure, the victor King,
O're mortal trial, mortal sin,
Who unstained passed,
Alone and weak, this all we meet,
Shall look in mortal eyes and smile,
And bless thee by His own pure touch,
O blessed hands! be pure thy touch,

O happy feet! tread lightly on
O'er thorny paths, tho' weak and sore,
For ye shall tread the blessed earth,
That Christ's own feet shall press upon.
O feet, keep firm and pure thy ways,
On dust too sacred for e'en lips
To press, your steps shall fall.

Thrill eager heart and brain and ears,
Thrill, flesh and blood, and thirsting soul,
Which ne'er shall die,
For ye shall in His presence stand,
And see His face, and hear His voice.
Fear, mortal frame! lest in the light
Of His divine and beauteous eyes,
Which read all life, ye trembling fall,
And burn away beneath their glow.
				AUGUSTA J. CROCHERON.


[Column 1]
							[For the EXPONENT.
			GOOD WORKS.

		BY BLANCHE BEECHWOOD

						For as the light
	Not only serves to show, but renders us
	Mutually profitable: so our lives,
	In acts exemplary, not only win
	Ourselves good names, but do to others give
	Matter for virtuous deeds, by which we live. 
								CHAPMAN.

	How necessary are good works to the
complete life of a Saint of God; the per-
formance of good works is something to be
desired: if our part in life be ever so low-
ly, our means of doing good for others over
so limited, with what we have in our charge
we may devote ourselves to accomplishing
some good work. If we extend to another
the comfort of loving words in the hours
of sorrow, of doubt, or temptation, we are
even in this sweet ministration doing 
deeds worthy to be engraven in the herald-
ry of good works. There are various ways
and means, by which the truly good may
earn the right to the inheritance which ac-
crues from good works. To bestow the
bounties of the earth upon the poor, is not
the greatest means of doing good works, it
is perhaps the easiest; to give without
thought, of what has been liberally dis-
bursed to us, deserves very little credit; but
to devise ways and means whereby the
poor may provide for themselves, and be- 
come happy through a competence in rec-
ompense for labor, is far more praiseworthy.
It is to some almost an ambition to be ac-
counted generous in giving, but the pru-
dent person who calculates the real benefit

[Column 2]
of a fellow-creature, will, by the ingenuity
of his brain and the magnanimity of
thought, look farther than the giving for
immediate succor, and thus perhaps invent
that which will provide labor and conse-
quently its equivalent in food for the many.
	Good works are great works in the truest
signification of the term. From the small-
est beginnings have the mightiest conse-
quences resulted. A few tiny drops of wa-
ter gush forth from the mountain side,
there has just been sufficient latent force to
propel them from the surface, they trickle
down the slope and are replenished by rain
and moisture in their course, and other
diminutive streams flow into the narrow
bed they have formed, and unite their cur-
rents together, accumulating more and
more as their path is ever onward, until
they stretch away into the vast distance
and become powerful in and of themselves,
and the great commerce of cities is borne
on their waters; thus controlling in part
the riches and substance of masses of peo-
ple; and in the final end of their course
mingling the mighty river of water with
the great abyss which holds the nations in
awe by the force of its might. Thus may
the good works of the Saints united togeth-
er, carry them into the great ocean of pow-
er, that will control the elements of life,
and establish the stupendous institutions
which are necessary to concentrate the ef-
forts of all man and womankind into one
grand and sublime purpose, blending and
harmonizing all their various powers and
attributes to make one glorious whole,
where every part and portion will assist in
forming a connecting link in the chain
which will eventually bind together all who
love God and do good works.
	We find many passages in the Holy Scrip-
tures certifying of the good works and in-
fluence of women as well as men. We are
all capable of doing good works; it is the
power of good deeds that makes one’s life
sublime. It matters not how lowly our
sphere, we make it noble by the manner in
which we live. We cannot be too particu-
lar how we order our lives. One of the 
Apostles in former days, in his exhortation
to the saints, said, “And let us consider one
another, to provoke unto love, and to good
works; not forsaking the assembling of our-
selves together.” By meeting togeth-
er and interchanging thoughts and ideas
we become in possession of a wider knowl- 
edge of human nature, and of the attributes,
powers and gifts of different persons, and
are enabled to understand what the causes
are of various individual peculiarities, and
thus our views are enlarged, our sympa-
thies increased to do good to our fellow-
creatures, and we are better prepared to
invest ourselves with that mantle of charity
which will make us more capable of living
by the golden rule of our Saviour, “Do un-
to others etc.”
	Good works, however humble, as surely
shed a sweet fragrance through the whole
life of a person, as does the violet perfume
the entire atmosphere which surrounds it.
What is it that comforts us in looking back?
It is the recollection of good deeds. It
is that we have ministered all in our power
to the welfare and true happiness of oth-
ers. These reflections are a solace in every
lonely hour.
	Let us cultivate that love which is exalt-
ing in its nature, which tends to strength-
en every good purpose. Seek to develop in
ourselves, and call out in those around us
the strong affections, out of which spring
earnest faith, which incites to good works
and heroic courage in the pathway of right;

[Column 3]
a steadfastness to withstand all temptations
to wrong-doing; these will promote the
growth of sterling integrity of character
and nobility of soul, that will enable us to
endure all the vicissi udes of life, and yet
retain the true womanly characteristics
which men are so afraid will be lost in
maintaining public positions, and institu-
tions for the higher development of wo-
man’s capabilities.
	Woman need lose none of her grace and
sweetness by making herself useful outside
the home circle, if she be sufficiently forti-
fied within. It will be too antagonistic to
her sensitive nature, to overstep the neces-
sary bounds, and go farther than duty 
prompts, for love of notoriety.
	The theory very generally conceded
places woman upon an equal footing with
man, that is, if man is stronger physically 
and mentally, woman has the advantage in
being morally and spiritually man's su-
perior.
	I had no idea of arguing equality of sex
in this article, but only to advocate good
works among women. This seems to be
an era of woman’s works, and everywhere
woman seems awakening to an interest in
all the prevailing subjects of the times. It
has been said by inspired men, that “there
will be more women saved than men,” and
their good works will be the means of their
salvation. Good works, according to the
Scriptures, is the object of our creation.
"For we are his workmanship created in
Christ Jesus unto good Works."
	Salt Lake City, April 9, 1877.


WOMEN PHYSICIANS.

	Association Hall, Fifteenth and Chestnut
streets, was thronged on the 15th of March,
by a brilliant and interested audience, it be-
ing the Twenty-fifth Annual Commence-
ment of the Women's Medical College of
Pennsylvania.
	The degrees of M. D. were conferred by
the President, T. Morris Perot, upon the
following graduates:
	Franes N. Baker, Martha M. De Graw,
Elizabeth W. Smith, and Mary A. Young,
from Pennsylvania; Josephine G. Davis,
and Henrietta B. Woolston, of New Jersey;
Caroline S. Pease and Ella M. Whitaker, of
New York, Cornelia L. Boardman, of Mass-
achusetts; Elizabeth E. L. Forbes, of Dela-
ware; Elizabeth A. Follansbee, of Califor-
nia; Romania B. Pratt, of Utah; Elvira
Bainfer and Elizabeth T. Richards of Mich-
igan, and H. Josephine Varnum, of Maine.
	The spring course of 1877 commenced on
Monday, March 19, and will continue ten
weeks. Its chief purpose is to procure for
the students of the Women's Medical Col-
lege of Pennsylvania, all the advantages
of those improved methods of study in-
troduced into medical institutions in this
country the last few years. The essential
features of these are : First, a progressive
course of study; second, an increased
amount of demonstrative teaching, with
practical work on the part of the students.
—Woman's Words.


EARLY IN the month of May the cere-
mony of unveiling the statue of Fitz
Green Halleck will take place in Central
Park, New York. Whittier will give a
poem, and William Gullen Bryant will de-
liver an address. This statue is the first
ever raised to an American poet, and will
be near those of Shakespeare and Walter
Scott.
