			Salt Lake City, Utah.

SALT LAKE CITY, APRIL 15, 1877.


"IN SEASON AND OUT OF SEASON."

	This passage of Scripture will apply to the
work now laying before women to accomplish,
and they will find that it will not do to stand
still and wait for some one else to do, but
everywhere be earnest in the work before
them. It has been often remarked that there
is too much talking among women, but in the
present day there seems to be a necessity for
it. "To do more and talk less," is another fav-
orite phrase of many, but to add works to
preaching is better. Women have many things
to do now-a-days, and it is positively neces-
sary to do a vast amount of talking, as well as
brain, and head work. Executive ability is in
good demand, but there is hardly a premium
for it.
	Talk to the purpose is perhaps applicable,
but talking of some sort has evidently got to
be done; and as men have not time to do all the
talking, women must do some of it. "In sea-
son and out of season, in the house and by the
way," in every place where good can be accom-
plished, women should be willing to bring in-
to requisition all the talents they po[-]sess to
bring about the good results for which they are
laboring. It will not do to be too sensitive to
criticism; it is very evident there is some real
work accomplished by women or the public
would not be making such a noise about the
woman cause. Wherever any material good
is effected there is always opposition. The
work now being done among women is some-
thing really wonderful, and while the real ben-
efit is apparent there is little need to care for
what "Madame Grandy" says. Above all wo-
men on the earth the Latterday-Saints should
be earnest and zealous, in season and out of
season, they have the greatest purpose in la-
boring, To lay a sure foundation for them-
selves and children, that they may not be left
without resources when the judgments of God
shall come upon the nations and when Baby-
lon shall fall.
	There is no danger of saying or doing too
much in a good cause, started in the right di-
rection. The women of Utah are moving for-
ward to establish and sustain every depart-
ment of home industry, manufacture and im-
provement, that is, or can be made practicable
for women. They not only talk, but work, and
add faith to works; and may God also add His
blessing to every earnest endeavor.
					[handwritten] E. B. Wells


[Column 1]
	GENERAL CONFERENCE AT ST.
				GEORGE
	This year a new epoch seems to have
opened in the history of the church of Jesus
Christ of Latter-day Saints, and they have
held their General Conference in a Temple
built and consecrated to God and the adminis-
trations of the holy priesthood upon the earth.
The two stands opposite each other, were oc-
cupied by those bearing the priesthood of the
order of Melchizedec and Aaron. In the first
stand sat President Brigham Young, his coun-
selors and the brethren of the Twelve and
some others. In the opposite one Bishop Ed-
ward Hunter and some other Bishops. Presi-
dent B; Young opened the conference by giv-
ing out the hymn. President D. H. Wells of-
fered the dedicatory prayer, the choir then
sang, "High on the mountain top a banner is
unfurled." The Conference was then address-
ed by Elder George Q. Cannon and President
Brigham Young. The combined choir sang
"And it shall come to pass in the last days."
	In the afternoon of the first day Elder Jos-
eph F. Smith, and President John W. Young
addressed the congregation, after which Pres-
ident Brigham Young said he desired to preach
in terms not long but loud. In all our deal we
should do unto others as we would have oth-
ers do unto us. The Choir sang "Oh praise the
Lord all ye nations."
	The second day of Conference in the morn-
ing Elder C. C. Rich and Elder Wilford Wood-
ruff addressed the people and Elder George Q.
Cannon read a long list of names of mission-
aries appointed to different localities. Some to
Europe, some to the United States and some to
Arizona, after which he made a few remarks
and President Brigham Young said to the
Conference, that the purer the Latter-day
Saints were, the greater would be the rage of
the wicked but the less would be their power
to persecute. The choir sang, "As the hart
panteth after the water brooks."
	In the afternoon of the second day of Con-
ference, after the usual regular exercises, Eld-
er George Q. Cannon read another list of
names of missionaries who were called to pro-
claim the Gospel in different parts of the world
and fourteen Elders whose mission it was to
study the Spanish language.
	It was also moved, seconded and unanimous-
ly carried that the Relief Societies throughout
the Church take a mission to raise silk and do
all in their power to clothe themselves and
their families.

[Column 2]
	Elder Cannon also presented the authorities
of the Church who were unanimously sus-
tained by vote of the Conference. Elder Frank-
lin D. Richards, and Elder Erastus Snow oc-
cupied the afternoon, at the close the choir
sang "Behold O God, our shield and look upon
the face of thine anointed."
	Sunday morning third day of Conference,
Elder Brigham Young Jr., and Elder John
Taylor addressed the congregation. President
Brigham Young spoke to the Conference on
the subject of union. He announced that the
seats of the congregation would be reve[-]sed
and Bishop Edward Hunter would administer
the sacrament. Elder George Q. Cannon pre-
sented the names of some more missionaries.
Sunday afternoon Bishop Edward Hunter first
addressed the Conference, he was followed by
Bishop Edwin D. Wooley; then President
Brigham Young said "we will adjourn this
Conference from this house to the New Taber-
nacle in Salt Lake City, commencing at 10
o'clock in the morning of October sixth next."
	The choir sung "How beautiful upon the
mountains," the congregation then arose and
sung, "The spirit of God like a fire is burning."
Benediction pronounced by President Brigham
Young asking the blessing of God upon all
there assembled, and the good and honest in
heart all over the earth; that the spirit of God
might rest down upon the Temple, that an-
gels might visit it, and all who ministered
there for the living and the dead might feel
the holy influence of the power of God.


TWO DAYS MEETING AT ST.
GEORGE.

	Previous to the General Conference a two-
days meeting was held according to appoint-
ment in the St. George Temple. President
Brigham Young, and his Counselors, eleven of


the Quorum of the Twelve, presiding Bishop,
Edward Hunter, and many High Priests, El-
ders and Bishops, and a large congregation of
the saints were there assembled to worship
God in a Temple erected to His Most Holy
Name.
	The spirit of the Lord was poured out upon
them in rich abundance, and the sermons de-
livered upon that occasion were replete with
wise instructions and counsel, suited to the
condition of the people at the present day, and
great power rested upon those who spoke and
prayed, and good order prevailed. Elder J. T.
D. McAllister was elected president of St.
George Stake of Zion by unanimous vote.
The saints in that country have had much to
endure, but the Lord is blessing them above
others, in that they have a Temple completed
into which they may enter to worship God,
and call upon Him, and where they can minis-
ter for the living and the dead.
	We are indebted to the "Deseret News" for
information in regard to these meetings, and
the Conference at St. George.


HOME AFFAIRS.

	WE HAVE received the pamphlet published
by A. M. Musser, and O. F. Whitney, in Col-
umbia, Pa. on Mormonism and particularly
explaining the principle of Plural Marriage.
We would recommend it as valuable to the
reading public of Utah as it brings many good,
and wholesome arguments in favor of that pe-
culiar institution of Marriage.
	MRS. M. E. Randall had a juvenile enter-
tainment in the 14th Ward Assembly Rooms,
Thursday and Friday evenings called The
Floral and Little Old Woman's Concert, Her
pupils who were all quite young did credit to
themselves and their training. The princi-
pal feature was the Zephyrs, which consisted
of thirty pupils, all at once chanting forth the
Lord's prayer.
	WE ARE pleased to be able to announce to
the public that the Treatise on Silk Raising is
now ready for sale. It gives those who desire
instructions, from the formation of the eggs,
through all the various stages to the
spinning of cocoons. It describes minutely
all the particulars, and the information has
been collected from the most reliable sources,
and practical experience of persons engaged in
sericulture. The Deseret Silk Association have
compiled this pamphlet very carefully for the
benefit of those who are uninformed in these
matters, and every item has been carefully ar-
ranged. We recommend it as invaluable, for
it contains the real and positive facts briefly
stated and adapted for use. All Relief So-
cieties should supply themselves with a suffi-
cient number, as they now have the special
mission of silk-raising by unanimous vote of
the General Conference. The pamphlets on
Silk Culture are for sale at the Woman's Com-
mission House. price ten cents each.
	It is now clearly demonstated that a superior
quality of silk can be made of the elements of
Utah. We were shown specimens of the most
excellent quality and colors of skein and floss


[Column 1]

silk, spun and colored by Miss Rockwood of
this city. Mrs. Robinson of Farmington is
now weaving very elegant dress silk. The
loom for weaving ribbons is nearly completed
at Bountiful and Mrs. Wadoup of that place
understands the weaving of ribbons in the
hand loom. There are many ribbon-weavers
here from Europe but most of them have been
accustomed to the machinery run by steam,
which has not yet been introduced here.
	Mrs. Susan Stringam and Miss M. A. Rock-
wood, 13 Ward are prepared to take pupils and
teach them the art of reeling the silk from the
cocoons, at $l0,00 apiece. Persons who are in-
tending to commence raising silk, would do
well to take advantage of this oppprtunity.
	AMONG THE home manufactures, we are
pleased to announce many useful chemical ar-
ticles for domestic use by R. Matthews & Co.
Every article used by them in manufacturing,
is subjected to chemical examination by Prof.
J. L. Barfoot and prepared under his direction.
They have the Bath Sand Tablet which we can
recommend as cleansing, and can be used with-
out injuring the most sensitive skin. Baby,
Toilet and Shaving soap of superior qualities,
also scouring-soap for woodwork and all house-
hold purposes. They also manufacture the Es-
sence of Jamaca Ginger and other essences,
which have been tested by many as equal
to any manufactured elsewhere. Writing fluid,
and boot-blacking of excellent quality and free
from adulterations, also waterproof paste which
by applying to boots and shoes, wet feet
may be prevented and render the leather more
pliable and endurable.
	It is to the best interest of the Latter-day
Saints to patronize all home manufactures.
	We would recommend the Relief Societies
to form Clubs and send to the Woman's Com-
mission House of this city, and get the above
articles at wholesale rates.
	THE WOMAN'S Commission House, estab-
lished in this city under the auspices of the
Relief Society, and called the Relief Society
Woman's Mercantile Association, is now doing
business in all the departments of dry goods
home productions. This enterprise has requir-
ed a great amount of indomitable energy and
perseverance on the part of those who have
been active and efficient workers in the cause;
and the sisters of the Relief Society through-
out the entire city and its vicinity should not
forget to whom they are indebted for the suc-
cess of the institution thus far. It is still in
its infancy, scarcely six months since it was
established, and it needs the practical suoport
of the people, to ensure its future success. We
sincerely hope the presidents and officers of
Relief Societies will bear in mind that this in-
stitution represents their interests; and do
whatever is in their power to sustain it, and
those who devote their time solely for the pub-
lic good without renumeration. Complaints
have been made by women in times past, that
there was no place where home made work
could be offered for sale, and fairly represented;
now let the sisters be earnest in sustaining a
place where all have equal opportunity to dis-
pose of such articles.
	SILK ASSOCIATION.—The Deseret Silk Asso-
ciation met persuant to adjournment Friday,
Apr. 2, at the Firemen's Hall.
	Mr. P. A. Schettler in the chair.
	Mr. P. A- Schettler said the season was fast
approaching for commencing to raise silk
worms, and by present appearances we would
have to begin much earlier this season than
usual, as the trees were already budding out.
He thought the interest in silk culture was

[Column 2]

progressing, as there were a great many mak-
ing inquiries and planting out trees. He had
had confidence that silk could be raised here
for many years, and now it was demonstrated
beyond a doubt, that it not only could be raised
here, but could be manufactured. He strongly
urged that the people plant out trees as that
was one of the most essential points. He said
our prospects were good for getting machinery
for spinning and twisting, and reported that
the Treatise on silk raising was ready and could
be got by applying at the Woman's Store, Old
Constitution Buildings, or of the Secretary,
second house east of Fireman's Hall. They
are ten cents per copy.
	Mr. Smith, of Bountiful, said he advised all
that could to plant out trees and cuttings. The
time would come when the people would wish
that they had planted out mulberry trees.
Silk raising and manufacture would yet form
one of the greatest sources of wealth to this
community and give many people employ-
ment. We ought to encourage our children
in it and by so doing we would be teaching
them something useful, instead of their being
left to wander about the streets in idleness.
He had planted out from five to six hundred
cuttings.
	Mrs. White of Mill Creek, said that her
daughter was learning to reel silk. She had
planted out a quantity of cuttings and was
going to plant out some trees.
	Mrs. Morgan of Mill Creek, was going to try
and raise some silk worms this season.
	Very interesting and encouraging remarks
were made by Mrs. Smith, Mrs. Higgs, Mrs.
Hardy and others, and all felt encouraged to
do their parts.
	Mr. Schettler said that to those who had
children who wished to try and raise some
worms he would give some eggs in small quan-
tities.
	Meeting adjourned until the first Friday in
May at this place, at 3 p. m.
	R. SIMPSON, Secretary

For the EXPONENT.
	TO THE RELIEF SOCIETY.

BELOVED SISTERS:
	I wish to call your attention to the mission
given to all Branches of the Relief Society by
the General Conference held in the Temple in
St. George; which mission is, THE RAISING OF
SILK. This important branch of Home Indus-
try has been long neglected, although through
the efforts of a few, sufficient proof has been
given of the practicability of its success.
	Millions of additional trees should be pro-
duced. Now is the time for setting cuttings
which should be attended to immediately, to
take advantage of this year's growth. What
the sisters cannot do with their own hands,
they may bring to pass as Solomon "built the
Temple."
					More anon.
	Your fellow-laborer in the great cause—the
establishment of Zion.
						ELIZA R. SNOW.
	Salt Lake City, April 10, 1877.


I WOULD say then, most empthatically,
"Bring forward the young woman." The
next score of years is in her hands, and in
ours only as we lift up hers.  For us it is
now the cheerful endurance of patient pil-
grimage; for the young woman it is the
struggle, step by step, for new footholds.
The future of Woman is with her, and not
with us, whose eastward falling shadows
are so rapidly lengthening.
			—Prof. Maria Mitchell.


BITTER SWEETNESS.

		"If none were sick and none were sad,
		What service could we render?
		I think if we were always glad,
		We scarcely could be tender;
		Did our beloved never need
		Our patient ministration,
		Earth would grow cold and miss indeed
		Its sweetest consolation,
		If sorrow never claimed our heart,
		And every wish was granted
		Patience would die and hope depart,
		Life would be disenchanted."

	This verse, one among many that we
find without name to indicate ownership,
struck my attention as containing a senti-
ment worthy of study and reflection to
those who have not yet learned the art of
extracting sweetness from the bitter expe-
riences of life, who have not learned to
look upon evil as a principle that is neces-
sary to happiness, and yet we all know that
the tenderest and most beautiful sentiments
of the human heart would be dormant and
unknown were they not created and nur-
tured by the hand of sorrow.
	We cannot sympathize where there is
nothing but joy, we cannot be gratified
when we do not need the patient ministra-
tion that springs from pity and forbearing
love.
	A little child leaves its mother's knee
and wanders out into the garden, a new
world to the wondering infant, everything
may be bright and beautiful, and in its joy-
ous playfulness and unrestrained frolic
mother is soon forgotten, but even while it
plays and gambols, filled with all the joy its
little heart can hold, the fair fingers are
stained, the white garments become soiled,
and then perchance the little foot strikes
some hidden rock, and there is pain, the
first thought, the first cry is for mother, a
few sweet words of sympathy, or a caress,
and in her arms the tired infant sleeps, per-
haps sobs for awhile although sheltered and
and safe, and cleansed from the impurities
of its heedless wanderings; what was it
that drove the child to its surest, never-fail-
ing refuge?—pain, and too often we fail to
remember and draw near to our Creator un-
til smitten as the little child, with a need
of care that earth has failed to give, and a
pain beyond the power of mortal to touch;
it is then that our higher nature rises up
and we reach our hearts and hands heaven-
ward, with thanks for the pain and evil that
has brought us nearer that divine love and
mercy that is ever reaching down to earth,
and searching for the stricken and down-
cast children of sorrow, and even as it nev-
er fails to comfort the hearts, and uphold
the hands stretched out for aid, so should
our hearts respond to every call for sympa-
thy and care and our hearts bestow bless-
ings, for in blessing we are blest, and in
comforting we are comforted.
							MILICENT.


THE title of Duke was introduced into
England by Edward III, but was limited to
princes of the blood royal until the reign of
Edward VI. The title became oxtinct un.
der Queen Elizabeth in 1572, but James I.
revived it again. A duke may qualify.
eight chaplains, and a duchess two.


Don't look out of railroad car windows
when they are open. The habit is exceed-
ingly injurious to the eyes, as the sharp
draft of air caused by rapid motion, badly
affects the optic nerve. Several cases of
total blindness have recently occurred in
England from this cause.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


FROM LANCASTER CITY, PA.
 It will not be my lot to record the adven-
tures of a Sinbad, a Gulliver, a Goldsmith,
nor a Humboldt; for though in a strange
country, to me, and possessed of one of
those far reaching imaginations with which
Shakespeare invests "lunatics, lovers and
poets," my conscience is not so elastic, nor
the field of observation so extraordinary, as
to permit me to startle you either by fact
or fiction. I may however tell a little of
what I see and hear during my travels, and
the relation will serve to inform the minds
of those who have never been in this sec-
tion of our country, and to refresh the
memories of all who have seen for them-
selves. In the city of Lancaster stands a
monument, a noble and imposing column of
R. I. granite, inscribed with the names of
the principal battles fought during the Re-
bellion, and surmounted by an image of the
goddess of liberty, holding a shield and a
drawn sword; it stands on a foundation
about two feet high, and at each of the four
corners is placed on a pedestal a statue, rep-
resenting respectively, the navy, infantry,
cavalry and artillery. The whole is sur-
rounded by an iron picket fence, and com-
memorates the achievements of the soldiers
of Pennsylvania during the late war.
	In the Woodward Hill Cemetery, also
sleeps the mortal remains of James Buchan-
an, the 15th President of the United States.
The man who would have exterminated a
whole people, is himself the inmate  of a
narrow chamber in the halls of death, while
that same people under the same leader-
ship as then, have grown still greater, and
stand to-day as an indisputable assurance
to all, that truth and justice will eventually
triumph in spite of anything on earth.
Here also is the tomb of Thaddeus Stevens,
the great Equal Rights man, buried in a
cemetery founded by Martin Shriener. On
his tomb is the following inscription: “I re-
pose in this quiet, secluded spot, not from
any natural preference for solitude, but find-
ing other cemeteries limited as to race, by
charter rules, I have chosen this that I
might illustrate in my death, the principles
which I advocated through a long life--
Equality of man before his Creator.” These
men's characters furnish us a contrast in
life and death, which is a good lesson on
human nature.
	This city numbers in population 27,000,
and presents on the main streets a brisk and
business like appearance; the streets and
alleys, as is common in the east, are rather
contracted, but are relieved by many fine
buildings which adorn each side and attract
the attention of all who admire architec-
ture.
 The penitentiary awoke my interest, not
from any sympathy I feel for places of that
character, but from the peculiar and antique
design on which it is built. Its ponderous
piles of masonry, castellated front, towers
and turrets, perforated with narrow iron
barred windows, forcibly suggest the me-
daeval and lance breaing ages of the past,
and the strongholds of the notorious Rob-
ber Counts of Germany.
 Pennsylvania, or this portion of it, in the
summer time, unquestionably, presents a
beautiful picture; the nature of the coun-
try, with its farms, orchards, woods, hills,
and rustic byways and turnpikes, show
that the milder seasons will clothe it in pic-
turesque loveliness and grandeur.
 In this county is found the Gap Nickel
Mine, the only one of that kind being
worked in the United States.
 Luzerne Co. differs considerably from
this region. There are located the great
coal mines which, with the iron ones, con-
tribute principally to the wealth of the
State, and to the needs of the surrounding
country. As is quite natural, everything
there is of a less romantic order, and more
crude and matter of fact; though the Lack-
awanna Valley, with the broad Susquehanna
sweeping through it, under a summer sky,
might vie with the older and more fertile
display of scenery.
 Nearly all the laboring classes are miners,
and many, from the frequent periods of
compulsory idleness, consequent to the fluc-
tuations of the market, the low wages, and
high prices of provisions, are reduced to the
depths of poverty.
 The mines and railroads are controlled by
the rich, who scruple not to use the power
in their hands, often, for the oppression of
the poor.
 It is a consolation to know that God
is no respecter of persons, and that the
time is close at hand when all will be
judged, when "many that are first shall be
last, and the last shall be first."
I heard it stated that the coal indications
in Luzerne Co. showed enough of that arti-
cle to last a population of 50,000,000–60,000
years. It is the anthracite quality, and
much superior to that found in Utah.
 A pleasing characteristic of the society in
the country towns, is that freedom and
courtesy to strangers, so common in the
west. Every one you meet salutes you
with a pleasant good morning, or, good eve-
ning, though you may never have met be-
fore. The major portion of this communi-
ty is of German birth or descent; they are,
as a rule, good, reliable citizens, and vote
the Republican ticket. The memory of
Gen. Lee's raids, during the war, has not
entirely died away.
	No more at present,
			Yours respectfully,
							O. F. W.


[Column 1]

LUCRETIA MOTT.

	Mrs. Mott has been spoken of as a
leading woman of the first century of the
Republic, but to our mind she is a leading
woman of the second, and of all the centur-
ies that are to follow. It is hard to say
where the influence of any person truly
great and good begins or ends. But it is
absolutely certain that long after younger
hands have grasped the fallen mantle, the
blessed influence which has been wrought
out in a noble life of self-abnegation, must
and does continue, through all time.
	LUCRETIA MOTT, the woman philanthrop-
ist, humanitarian, and Quaker preacher,
was born in the Island of Nantucket, Jan-
uary 3, 1793. Descended from the Cof-
fins and Macys on her father's side, and
of the Folgers on the mothe's, and through
them related to Dr. Franklin, she comes of
honorable and renowned ancestry.
	She said of herself that "she was brought
up to be useful in the family." In 1804
Mrs. Mott removed to Boston and studied
in the public and private schools there, and
subsequently she studied in the Friends
boarding-school in Duchess County, N. Y.
—becoming a teacher there when fifteen.
At eighteen, she married James Mott of
New York; removing to Philadelphia
where she has since resided, happy in the 
love, affection, and reverence of her daugh-
ter and son-in-law and their children. Her
husband died full of years and honors, in
1868.
	Regarding the work to which she has no-
bly devoted her life, Mrs. Mott has placed

[Column 2]

on record the following: "The unequal
condition of woman in society early im-
pressed my mind. Learning while at
school that the charge for the education of
girls was the same as that of boys, and
when they became teachers women received
but half as much as men for their services,
the injustice was so apparent that I resolved
to claim for myself all that an impartial
Creator had bestowed. At twenty-five,
surrounded by a little family and many
cares, I felt called to, and engaged in, the
ministry—receiving every encouragement.
Temperance Reform early engaged my at-
tention. The oppression of the working
classes by existing monopolies, and the low-
ness of wages, and I have attended many
meetings and heard their appeals with com-
passion, and a great desire for a radical
change in the system that makes the rich,
richer, and the poor poorer. The various
associations and communities tending to
a greater equality of condition have had
from me hearty God-speed." This noble
utterance none can doubt.
	To her labor in the cause of reform is
more attributable the uplifting of the
down-trodden and oppressed everywhere,
than to any other living woman.
	We visited Mrs. Mott in her own home
one day last week. The season of the year
was less propitious than it would have been
later—but we could easily understand how
charming and harmonious its associations
must be. The house, highly characteristic
of Quaker simplicity, has been modernized,
the new corresponding with the old. She
received us very kindly in the large com-
fortably furnished parlor, and after some
few minutes conversation with herself and
daughter, she invited us into her cosy lit-
tle sitting-room, sacred to her hours of read-
ing and writing and working. For the
busy hands and wonderful brain are never
idle. Indeed, in the matter of work this
venerable and beloved woman commends
by her example the lesson of industry to
every young girl who would achieve honor
and distinction. Wood fires are used in
the house which she frequently builds from
choice, deeming no occupation too humble
that is greatly done. Though at such an
advanced age, it must not be thought that
our venerable friend takes no interest in the
question of the day. Quite the contrary. Of
President Hayes' policy as now understood,
and of himself personally, she spoke with
much approbation—deprecating all efforts
to keep alive sectional feelings. We took
leave of her with much regret—tempered
with the fact that when the roses bloom
we hope to visit her again. Tender in
heart, guileless in soul, brilliant in intellect,
this wonderful woman still remains to us—
coming regularly into the city to Wednes-
day Meetings.
	Though now in her eighty-fifth year, she
is as keen of comprehension, as strong men-
tally, as in her earlier working days, the
central thought of an appreciative circle.
	Martha Coffin Wright, her sister who is
also known as a philanthropist and reform-
er, says of her: "The striking traits of Lu-
cretia's character are remarkable energy
that defies even time, unswerving conscien-
tiousness, and all those characteristics that
are summed up in the few words, "Love to
man and love to God. Mrs. Soule says:
"Birth made Victoria a queen, but her own
pure, sweet life makes Lucretia Mott a
queen—queen of a realm on which the sun
never sets—the realm of humanity. if ev-
er any woman ever 'inherited the earth' it
is this blessed Quaker woman." The treat-
ment received by the delegates to the con-


vention in 1840, in London, declared Mrs.
Mott, "brought the woman question more
into view, and an increase of interest in the
subject was the result. In this work I have
engaged heart and hand, as my labors,
travels, and public discourses evince. The
misrepresentation, ridicule and abuse heap-
ed upon this, as well as other reforms, do
not in the least deter me from my duty.
To those whose name is cast out as evil for
the truth's sake, it is a small thing to be
judged of man's judgment."—Woman's
Words.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.		175


PREJUDICE.

	There is every day ample opportunity to 
observe the manifold evils arising from
prejudice. How often do some of us allow
ourselves to speak evil or slanderous things
of those who have proved themselves
friends in the truest sense. We are too will-
ing to believe evil of our fellow beings; and
by hearing one who is bitter against anoth-
other, and has allowed prejudice to mis-
lead judgment, slander them, we fancy the
persons to be really as they were represent-
ed, and unfit associates for us, willing to be-
lieve without trying to find out the real
truth for ourselves; thus often wrongfully
prejudiced, keeping at a distance those
who are misrepresented, and committing a
grievous sin before God who has said
"judge not lest ye be judged;" How very
careful we should be never to say a word to
injure the character of any person. We
may do more harm in a moment by utter-
ing something which in reality has no
foundation, than we can atone for in a life
time. A thorough investigation should be
gone through before we are prepared to
give our opinion, and then we should be
careful in regard to speaking upon any prin-
ciple; no matter how objectionable others
may find it, we are not capable of declaring
its errors simply because others do not ad-
mire it, or cannot comprehend it. Who
have been the persecutors of the Latter-day
Saints? Not those who with unbiased
minds have studied the principles revealed
in our holy religion; if unwilling to believe,
at least they would cease to try to injure.
Our real enemies are those who through
prejudice at first have allowed their hearts
to become embittered, and perhaps aided by
a wicked disposition they were willing to
rush blindfold, not caring how much in-
justice was done by their words and acts.
While the people of the world are filled with
prejudice, should not we as saints strive to
overcome this narrow mindedness. I have
heard the remark that in days gone by,
every man was called a gentleman until
proved a rogue, but now-a-days it would
seem as if all were considered rogues until
proved to be gentlemen. We should try to
be ever just. We had better by far say
more good than deserved, than more evil
than is true. Let us guard our own
tongues, discipline our own thoughts, see
that our hearts are free from impurity, and
we will have no time to encourage evil or
listen to slander.
					MARY STUART.
	Hyde Park, March 14, 1877.


R. S. RREPORT.

EDITOR EXPONENT;
	The Young Ladies Retrenchment Asso-
ciation of the 16th ward, S. L. City, held
their third Annual Meeting Jan. 24, 1877.
Miss E. R. Suow, Mrs. P Woodruff, Mrs.


B. W. Smith, Mrs. E. Howard, and Mrs.
M. P. Young addressed the meeting, most
of them remarked that they had met with
us at our organization, and previous annu-
al meetings, and noticed the progress the
young ladies had made; spoke upon the
principles of Celestial Marriage, Word of
Wisdom, and Retrenchment and urged up-
on the young ladies the necessity of trying
to bring about a reform among their asso-
ciates and of shunning the company of in-
temperate, profligate men; spoke of the
evils of round dancing, and extrava-
gance in dress; gave much good and wise
counsel and instruction and bore faithful
testimonies to the work of God.
	Bishop F. Kesler remarked he was glad
the Bishops had helps, it was good to have
these Associations, for missionaries are
needed at home. Said we should do all we
could to stem the downward course of the
youth, and should be careful in the teaching
of our children. Alluded to the calamities
that are coming on the earth, and the rev-
elations of God concerning the redemption
of the living and the dead; and how to pu-
rify our tabernacles by shunning those
things which are forbidden. Said that Sis-
ter Snow and others were doing a good
work and they would have their reward;
gave other valuable instructions and prayed
God to bless both young and old in the name
of Jesus.
	President Miss Mary Williams then invi-
ted all the young ladies present who were
not members to meet with us and join our
society. Our meetings are held every Tues-
day at one o'clock.
	Meeting then adjourned, after which all
the officers and members, and the visitors
named above, the officers of the Relief So-
ciety, and some others, repaired to the res-
idence of Mrs. M. J. Thompson where a ta-
ble was spread with refreshments. Miss E.
R. Snow then blessed the Young Ladies
Association of this Ward and prayed that
they might prosper.
	Wishing prosperity to the Woman's pa-
per, and all other enterprises for the good
of Zion; we subscribe ourselves, your sis-
ters.
	MARY WILLIAMS, Pres't.
	EMILY S. ELLSMORE, Sec'y.


WOMAN’S WISDOM AND VIRTUE.

 Mr. Ruskin, in a recent lecture, thus
spoke of woman, as illustrated by the hero-
ines of Shakespeare:
   “The catastrophe of every play is caused
always by the folly or fault of man; the re-
demption, if there be any, is by the wis-
dom and virtue of a woman, and failing
that, there is none. The catastrophe of
King Lear is owing to his own want of
judgment, his impatient vanity, his misun-
derstanding of his children; the virtue of
his one true daughter would have saved
him from all the injuries of the others, if
he had not cast her away from him; as it is,
she all but saves him.
  “Of Othello, I need not trace the tale;
nor the one weakness of his so mighty
love; nor the inferiority of his perceptive
intellect to that even of the second woman
character in the play, the Emilia who dies
in wild testimony against his error: 'Oh,
murderous coxcomb! What should such a
fool do with so good a wife?' In Romeo
and Juliet, the wise and entirely brave
stratagem of the wife is brought to ruinous
issue by the reckless impatience of her hus-
band. In 'Winter's Tale,' and 'Cymbeline,’
the happiness and existence of two princely
households lost through long years, and


imperiled to the death by the folly and ob-
stinacy of the husbands, and redeemed at
last by the queenly patience and wisdom of
the wives. In Measure for Measure,' the
injustice of the judges, and the corrupt
cowardice of the brother, are opposed to
the victorious truth and adamantine purity
of a woman.
	In 'Coriolanus,' the mothers' counsel act-
ed upon in time, would have saved her son
from all evil; his momentary forgetfulness
of it is his ruin; her prayer, at last granted,
saves him—not indeed, from death, but
from the curse of living, as the destroyer
of his country. And what shall I say of
Julia, constant against the fickleness of a
lover, who is a mere wicked child; of He-
lena, against the petulance and insult of a
careless youth; of the patience of Hero,
the passion of Beatrice, and the calmly devo-
ted wisdom of the "unlessoned girl,' who ap-
pears among the helplessness, the blindness,
and the vindicitive passions of men, as a
gentle angel, to save merely by her pres-
ence, and defeat the worst intensities of
crime by her smile?
	Observe, further, among all the principal
figures in Shakespeare's plays, there is only
one weak woman—Ophelia; and it is be-
cause she fails Hamlet at the critical mo-
ment, and is not and cannot in her nature
be, a guide to him when he needs her most;
from that all the bitter catastrophe follows.
Finally, though there are three wicked wo-
men among the principal figures—Lady
Macbeth, Regan, and Goneril, they are left
at once to be frightful exceptions to the or-
dinary laws of life; fatal in their influence
also in proportion to the power for good
which they have abandoned. Such, in
broad light, is Shakespeare's testimony to
the position and character of woman in hu-
man life. He represents them as infallibly
faithful and wise counselors, incorruptibly
just and pure examples—strong always to
sanctify, even when they cannot save."—
Pulpit of the Day.


WHY EDITORS DO NOT COMMIT 
				SUICIDE.

 The New York correspondent of The
Cincinnati Gazette, in a recent letter says:
 While speaking of suicides as a feature
in human experience, I am led to the re-
markable fact that editorial life exhibits so
general an exception.  While we have had
instances of self-inflicted death among law-
yers, bankers and mechanics, such a case
has not occurred among the journalists in 
this city.  One may reasonably inquire
what is the cause of this exception?  They
labor intensely, and are generally ill-paid,
and they have a full share of life's hard-
ships, which they endure until their change
comes in the course of nature.  I can give
no other reason for this except that the pro-
fession is constantly busy, and works with
an approval of conscience, since it works
for public weal.  Editors, in fact, are so oc-
cupied with a range of thought embracing
puclic interest that they have little time to
think about themselves.  Walter Scott
made the remark after he had reached
bankruptcy that nothing saved him from
insanity save his habit of writing. His
mind was thus led away from the contem-
plation of his miseries.  Journalists are so
engaged in chronicling the woes of others,
that they lose sight of their own."


AN INCALCULABLE WEIGHT.—The weight
of indignation.

WOMAN'S RECORD.

	AT a recent meeting of the Citizen's Suf-
frage Association of Philadelphia, the fol-
lowing resolutions were passed:
	Resolved, the women of this nation can-
not consistently rejoice in the success of the
Republican, or deplore the defeat of the
Democrats, both parties being indifferent,
if not opposed to the advance and estab-
lishment of equal rights.
	Resolved, that the women of Philadel-
phia have no good reason to expect that
their rights will be regarded by either of 
the late candidates for Mayor, neither hav-
ing uttered a public word in their behalf.
	Resolved, that this society rejoices in the 
fact that women in England have equal
rights with men to vote, with one excep-
tion, the election of members to Parliament,
which, through the persistent efforts of wo-
men and men having that work at heart,
is likely soon to be removed.


FLOWERS.

HE whose worth speaks, needs not to
speak his worth.
	THEY are the wise whom God esteems
wise. They are are the wise whom God
makes wise.
	WARS have been a disgrace to that Chris-
tianity which the nations profess.—Lord
John Russell.
	THERE is no war among men but what
arises from some vice—from covetousness,
or ambition, or an immoderate love of glory.
—Plutarch
	WITH time and patience the mulberry
leaf becomes satin. What difficulty is
there at which a man shonld quail when a
worm can accomplish so much from the leaf
of the mulberry.
	KEEP your conscience tender—tender as
the eye that closes its lid against an atom
of dust, or as that sensitive plant which you
have seen shrink and shut its leaves not
merely from the rude touch of a finger, but
at the breath of a moth.


MISCELLANEOUS.

	INVENTIONS have taken from women the
distaff, the shuttle and the loom, only to
call her genius into wider work.
	GIRLS in their teens should learn the
worth of money and the judicious use of it.
If poor, they should earn it honestly, and
then expand it in gaining an education, or
saving it for the emergencies of the future.
				—Sarah M. Perkins.
	RUSSIAN leather is the skin of the horse
or the calf tanned with the bark of the
birch, which gives it that peculiar smell
which is so grateful to the senses, and seems
to preserve it from the attacks of insects.
	THE number of pilgrims who assembled
in 1875 at Mecca, is stated by Consul Beytz
in his commercial report on Jeddah, for that
year to have been 140,000. Of these 40,000
went by sea, and the remainder by caravans
across the continent.


PEN AND SCISSORS.

 THE warmest reception contributions
meet with from editors:—To burn them.

DRY SUBJECT.--A college professor, who


was giving a course of lectures on Egyptian
antiquities, said to his class, "This morning,
young gentlemen, I have a very dry sub-
ject. It is the Mummy."
	MANY of the great men of history have
been of short stature, from the days of that
ancient philosopher who, as the story goes,
was so diminutive that he had to carry
lead in his pockets to prevent his being
blown away. Canute the Great, for exam-
ple, was a singularly small man; Napoleon,
too, was little; Nelson had no height to boast
of; and the great Conde was short enough.
Hildebrand—Gregory the Seventh—the
mightiest of all the Popes, was also quite
a diminutive person. Then amongst men
of letters, poets, and philosophers, Mon-
taigne, the essayist, was little; so was Pope
—"a little crooked thing that asks no ques-
tions;" so was Dryden; so was Dr. Watts;
who insisted, as we all know, on the mind
being the stature of the man; and so was
Scarron, who, alluding at once to his ill
health and his little size, called himself an
"abridgement of human miseries." Will
any one, after such names as these—and
the list might be indefinitely extended—
look down on little men with disdain?

WOMAN'S EXPONENT.

VOL. 5.		SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, MAY 1, 1877.		No. 23.


For the EXPONENT.
			MENTORIAN.

	You've pass'd the hey-day term of life
	With all its day-dreams, love and strife.
	You've tested to your heart's content
	All they can offer—all they've lent,
	And find that all that they have made
	A hollow reed, on which you've played;
	And though at times a syren strain
	Has issued lulling heart and brain,
	Have you not woke from out that dream
	To wish to plunge in Lethe's stream?
	That all the past might by-gones be-
	And leave you once again all free
	To start, and take another road,
	That should conduct to an abode,
	Where love, and joy, and peace abound
	And wisdom consecrates the ground:
	You stand and take a calm look back
	Upon the glittering troubled track,
	That you have almost wildly pass'd,
	As in a mirror it is glass'd;
	It had such magic charm for you,
	You almost hate to say adieu;
	But yet "the still small voice within"
	Imploring whispers NOW begin!
	The half of life is still before you
	Yes—youth has not yet ceased to score you,
	Awake! arouse that soul of thine!
	Command her to "arise and shine!"
	The sanctum of your heart explore
	The Temple of your brain look o'er;
	Behold what God has done for you,
	And tremble should you prove untrue.
	He gave you talents, and a Soul
	With much thats noble to control;
	With much that's given to impart
	Instruction to the human heart;
	And to propel the expanding mind
	That truth and wisdom yearn to find;
	Are these not talents all untold,
	That in "a napkin" none must "fold?"
	You know all this—I need not tell
	You things you long have known so well;
	Then pardon, if I take my pen
	To brighten up your mental ken,
	And say in friendship, brother mine
	Arise! and make your mark in Time!
	That in the glorious future, you
	May stand among the pure and true
	Whose brilliant minds, and mighty soul
	Hereafter will this globe control;
	And let us strive that you and I
	In that bright galaxy may vie.
	My friendly rhyme I now will end
	Subscribing, H. T. King, your friend.
S. L. City.


[Column 1]

MEMORY'S MUSIC.

BY BLANCHE BEECHWOOD

	"It haunts me still though many a year has fled
		Like some wild melody."
	Always and ever, to the pure minded and
innocent, there is a pleasure in recalling
moments fraught with joy and happiness.
The scenes and remembrances may be of a
simple nature, perhaps of green pastures
or meadows gay with buttercups and cow-
slips, where the merry calves frisked and
gamboled and we, with hearts as light as
these thoughtless animals, participated with
our companions in sports of a scarcely
more exalted phase. Such is human weak-
ness; in looking back it is invariably some
trivial thing that recalls the sweetest and
most delightful associations. A ramble by
moonlight in some charming spot, when
our hearts were the very sanctuary of pure
and innocent feelings, ere we knew the de-
ception of the world, the music of murmur-
ing water, the moonlight upon some old

[Column 2]

ruin, may have so infused its beauty into our
souls as never to be forgotten. The com-
panions of such dear, delicious hours are
usually endeared to us even though we
should be separated from them forever; and
the vision of a face many years forgotten
will come back to us sometimes with won-
derful accuracy of delineation.
	Many are the bright pictures memory
conjures up to illuminate life's pathway,
and God has given us this faculty of call-
ing to remembrance the "light of other
days" to help us to endure our lives with
better grace. Somebody loved us in the
long ago, is the consolation of many a weary
heart, and the rapture of that love still
clings to us, for true love never dies. In
days of loneliness how pleasant it is to
think, we have had friends who loved
us for ourselves, and fully estimated all our
goodness.
	These are echoes that reverberate through
our whole lives if we do not crush them
out, and come back to us again and again,
to comfort us by their silent key notes of
music, which only speak to the inmost heart.
	The voices of little children stir up the
fountains of tenderness in the aged, because
they wake old-time remembrances. Many
curious illustrations have been given of
incidents when the recollection of some epi-
sode in life has been recalled by a similar
sound or sight to that familiar in earlier
days, acting as a spell of enchantment;
showing how susceptible are our natures to
life-long impressions, or that the effects
produced by certain pleasurable emotions
can never be wholly eradicated.
	The memories of the past are the echoes
of the soul; they come to us in our day-
dreams, they float round us in our night
visions, they are clearest in their own pecu-
liar element, as for instance we recall them,
or they haunt us, when some similar spot,
act, or scene is presented. Sometimes we
feel we have brought with us almost inde-
finable echoes of our spirit-life; transient
and exquisite, yet for the moment like a ray
of mellowed sunshine thrilling us as with
a holy touch of joy. Sacred emotions these.
Something almost too sublime to be com-
prehended by mortals. They give us the
finest touches of our being here; they im-
part spiritual grace. They whisper to us of
our divine origin, in the language of the
soul. We are not mere creatures of clay of
a finite nature. These finely-drawn but in-
controvertible truths bespeak the infinite
within us.
	Matter-of-fact people, who must have
tangible things to rely upon for proofs, tell
us these are only fancies. It is a common
way of expressing a gloriously beautiful
subject, but are they not convincing proofs
of the life and love we participated in, be-
fore we tabernacled in the flesh.


DR. Johnson was famous for disregarding
public abuse. When people criticized and
answered his pamphlets, papers, etc., he
would say, "Why, now, these fellows are
only advertizing my book; it is surely better
a man should be abused than forgotten."


Out-door relief—A breath of fresh air.


﻿NOTES AND NEWS.

	MME. BONAPARTE, who resides in Balti-
more, is now in her ninety-fourth year. She
has often expressed an ardent desire to live
until she had completed a century, and
present appearances indicate that she may.

	MISS C. A. Bassett a lady of education
and enterprise has established an Educa-
tional Bureau in Des Moines Iowa, The na-
ture of it is an educational exchange
through the mediumship of which teach-
ers may find engagements and school-offi-
cers may find teachers and Institute in-
structors.	Ex.
	MRS. Gillespie, president of the Wo-
mans Executive Committee, at a meeting
recently held in Philadelphia, said that the
entire expense of running the Woman’s
Pavilion was paid by women, and she only
regretted that they had not engaged a lady
architect to make the plan and superin-
tend the building. The expense of get-
ting up the shows at the opening and clos-
ing of the exhibition was also paid by the
ladies.—Ex.

	THE EASTERN war question still remains
unsettled, no negotiations for peace having
had any salutary effect. There are so many
conflicting reports that it is scarcely possi-
ble to form a correct idea upon the subject
at all; and at present the result is not with-
in the power of man to determine. It is
said that the relations of Russia with
America are altogether of a peaceable char-
acter. The Sultan in speaking of the Rus-
sian attitude towards Turkey is reported to
have said, “Political and financial reasons
exist demanding a termination of the pres-
ent intolerable position of uncertainty."

	JUDGING FROM the newspaper notices of
President Hayes and wife, one would be
inclined to think that a decided change in
many things would be effected by their
good but quiet example. “President Hayes
abstains from the use of all intoxicating
drinks." Then the people will assuredly
have sober decisions; and his influence
will be felt for good wherever he presides
in public, or social circles. If Mrs. Hayes
persists in dressing plainly, she will deserve
the gratitude of all good people, for her ex-
ample will in process of time make a de-
cided change in the tide of fashion, and let
us hope she will be able to settle the much-
vexed question of woman's extravagance
in dress, for the popular party.

	THE REV. Robert Collyer of Chicago, re-
cently delivered a lecture entitled “Clear
Grit.” It was in the interest of the Hos-
pital for the Cure of Chronic Diseases. One
point he said he could speak upon with au-
thority, was “that it was better to work ten
hours a day in making horseshoes, and
making good ones, than to dawdle through
the week and preach a poor sermon on Sun-
day.” He went on to summarize his ad-
dress under four heads, which he said "were
the unmistakable evidences of pure grit.”
To be able to work honestly and well; to
marry young; to raise a large family with
God’s help; and then to persevere and be
contented.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
			R. S. REPORTS.

	The General Retrenchment Meeting in the
Fourteenth Ward Assembly Rooms was one
of special interest. President Mrs. M. I.
Horne told the sisters that their labors were
but just commencing; that the Conference
minutes calling upon them by the unami-
mous vote of that august assembly, to go
into the silk business, was suggestive of
the magnitude of the work before them.
Counselor Miss E. R. Snow said she hoped
this mission given to the women would be
taken into immediate consideration by ev-
ery one who had at heart the present and
future interest of Zion; she felt it to be a
very important one. In having it conferred
by unanimous vote of the conferense, the
sisters might expect the benefit of the faith
and prayers of this great body of people:
she alluded to the means which had been
used to start the Silk Association, and
the encouragement which had been given
to the people to interest themselves in this
industry; she said that in Brigham City
they were going to have a department in
their Co-operative store for homemade silk,
and if the sisters were energetic in this di-
rection they might all have home made
silks that would be durable. Silk culture
and its present and future success was the
principal subject before the meeting. A
beautiful silk dress pattern which was man-
ufactured from the elements of Farmington
was exhibited; the first silk dress ever
make in Utah. President Mrs. M. I.
Horne then announced to the audience
Bishop Hardy and he addressed the assem-
bly; expressed the pleasnre he felt in meet-
ing the sisters and the improvement which
had been made in many respects during
the last few years; said he had never antic-
pated seeing a silk dress manufactured with-
out machinery; the sister who made the
dress presented it as a voluntary Temple do-
nation; added that it had been purchased (by
Mrs. M. M. Barrett) and the money given to
the Temple, and that lady had given the dress
to Miss E. R. Snow, who he hoped might
by the blessing of God live to wear it out.
A Testimonial or certificate had been neatly
framed and given to Mrs. Clark as a me-
mento of respect for her labors in the silk
business. He spoke of his appreciation of
the Relief Society in his ward. Elder An-
gus M. Cannon arose and said that he had
about the same feeling in arising before
that meeting as he had twenty-two years be-
fore when he addressed a congregation of
lawyers and learned men in a hall in Hart-
ford, Conn. He spoke of his great honor
and reverence for good women; that his
best and noblest feelings had been implant-
ed by his mother; said he realized the sis-
ters had in their keeping the training of
those who were to be the future defenders not
only of this church but of the country.
Miss Snow expressed her thanks and good
feelings to be the recipient of such a gift.
Counselor E. Howard spoke upon dress, ex-
travagance, economy, and the saints living
their religion. Counselor B. W. Smith
make a few remarks upon the tannery, and
Mrs. E. S. Barney upon sericulture and the
straw-hat establishment. The meeting was
one long to be remembered by all present.

	CHERRY CREEK, ONEIDA Co., IDAHO,
					March 10th, 1877.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	By reading your valued paper, and see-
ing the prosperous condition of the differ-
ent Relief Societies, we thought it in order
to represent ourselves, rather than not be
represented.

[Column 2]
	We have here a Society, presided over by
Mrs. Eleanor Dudley assisted by Mrs. Eliza-
beth Chivers and Mrs. Elizabeth Hunting
as Counselors. We number in all twenty-
one members. Our district extends four or
five miles, part of which is enrolled in Ma-
lad branch. Our Temple donations have
amounted to $140.00, given by thirteen of
our members; we hold our meetings semi-
monthly, and they are well attended. A
sociable and friendly feeling prevails in our
meetings.
	Wishing all the Societies and different
enterprises prosperity, and not forgetting
our own, we remain
	Yours respectfully,
		ELEANOR DUDLEY, President,
			SARAH A. JONES, Secretary.

		ROCKVILLE, March 26th, 1877.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	Our Relief Society is in a very prosper-
ous condition, with the following named
officers:
	Mrs. Amy H. Draper, President,
	"	Maria Smith,			} Counselors.
	"	Margaret Brundage,	}
	"	Fannie P. Slaughter, Secretary,
	"	Hannah Jennings, Treasurer.
	We have been very busy of late prepar-
ing for the brethren and sisters who are
availing themselves of the opportunity of
going into the temple to perform a work for
themselves and their dead.
	When we were called on this Southern
Mission we thought that we were unfortu-
nate indeed, but now we are beginning to
think that we have been highly favored in
being permitted to live near the Temple.
Our Society has donated some towards
building thi[-] Temple, and we are now go-
ing to donate some towards the erection of
the Sanpete Temple.
	I have noticed in the EXPONENT that a
great deal is being done and said with re-
gard to the GRAIN MOVEMENT; I have felt
highly pleased when reading of the success
of the sisters in saving and storing grain,
for I believe it to be a very important step.
We have not commenced doing anything
towards gathering grain yet, but we are be-
ginning to agitate the matter some.
	Ever praying for the prosperity of the
EXPONENT and every other good under-
taking, I remain
	Your sister in the Gospel,
			FANNIE SLAUGHTER,
						Secretary.

	BEAR RIVER CITY, March 30th, 1877.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	I will give you a brief report of the Re-
lief Society in this place. The Society con-
sists of eighty-nine members, and we have
our meetings semi-monthly. We are doing
our best to carry out counsel in the way of
buying wheat; we have bought over one
hundred bushels of wheat already, and we
are exerting ourselves to raise means with
which to buy more. We own $303.00 capital
stock in the Co-op Store, on which we
draw interest. We help the poor, and visit
the sick, and are trying to be more united,
and live our religion to the best of our abili-
ty. We have a good and faithful President
in Mrs. Christine Albertsen, and her Coun-
selors, Mary C. Neely and Anna Hansen,
are faithful co-workers with her; Karen
Christensen is Treasurer.
	Wishing for the success of the EXPO-
NENT, I remain
		Your sister in the Gospel,
					MARY HANSEN,
							Secretary.

[Column 3]
	TOKERVILLE, KANE Co., March 2, 1877.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	By request of our worthy president Mrs.
Elizabeth Kleinman, I address you to in-
form our co-laborers in the kingdom of God
that we are trying to honor our calling as a
Relief Society and do all in our power to
assist the needy and comfort the afflicted
either by word or deed. General good
health prevails here, with the exception of
a few bad colds, which are incidental to this
season of the year.
	We were called to part with one of our
most respected sisters last August, Mrs.
Annie Jackson; she was a dutiful member
in the Society and served as Secretary as
long as her health would permit. She was
greatly esteemed by all who knew her.
	We read the reports of the different or-
ganizations in the settlements STORING
GRAIN, but we have none to store up as
our facilities here do not admit of its pro-
duction; we depend entirely on the north-
ern part of the Territory for breadstuff.
	I am sorry to say we have no Young La-
dies Retrenchment Association organized
yet, but we live in hopes that we may have
ere long. The St. George Temple is now
finished for which we should all feel very
thankful; there are many people going forth
to receive their blessings, which fills our
hearts with joy and gratitude. Our Society
made about forty yards of carpet for the
Temple. Our financial affairs are as follows:
we have a twenty-five dollar share in the
Washington factory, and a thirty dollar
share in this Co-op Store; carpet and cush-
ions which we donated to the meeting house
valued at eight dollars and twenty-five
cents; donated to a needy family, a quilt
valued at seven dollars; to the St. George
Temple twenty-nine dollars; to the Wash-
ington factory, eight dollars and forty
cents; sundries on hand, sixty-nine dollars
and thirty cents; cash on hand, four dollars
and fifty cents.
	We have had refreshing rains lately and
the people are planting their gardens; the
trees are also in bloom.
	Praying for the prosperity of our breth-
ren and sisters in this Latter-day work, I
remain yours etc.
			SARAH SPILSBURY, Sec'y.

	SUGAR HOUSE WARD, Apr. 7, 1877.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	I enclose you a brief report of a meeting
of the Relief Society held in the Society
Hall, Feb. 7th 1877, President Mrs. S. B.
Gibson presiding. After the usual exerci-
ses of singing and prayer the financial re-
port for the year 1876 was read. Amount of
donations received eighty-seven dollars
and fifty cents; We have also on hand
about fifty bushels of wheat, and a consider-
able quantity of flour which we hope to keep
adding to as long as the years of plenty
last.
	On March 23, the Sisters of the Relief So-
ciety had a social party to help in getting
means to finish their new Hall. They had
a splendid time all feeling to appreciate the
efforts of the committee to make it a suc-
cess. The proceeds of the party amounted
to forty-eight dollars and seventy-five cents.
	With kind regards I am yours
				Respectfully,
					SARAH WHEELER,
							Sec'y.

WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


THE WOMEN OF UTAH.

	LINES SUGGESTED ON HEARING THEY
	WERE ABOUT PUBLISHING A BOOK CALL-
	ED "THE WOMEN OF MORMONDOM."
The women of Utah they are going to tell
Of their hardships, and griefs and virtues as well?
They have toiled long and nobly, have fought the good
	fght,
Against sin and Satan, and to maintain the right.
In Missouri when the saints withont mercy were driv'n
And ties that were closest in anguish were riven,
The sisters were heroines, unflinching and brave,
Though their fondest and dearest were laid in the grave.
And many a mother when the tempest was wild,
In the storm without shelter, gave birth to her child,
Like the mother of Jesus as we often have read,
Who was glad in a manger to make her poor bed.
And in this freer country where churches abound
Little pity for Latter-day Saints has Been found.
The women of Utah, yes, their lives should be told,
And placed upon record in letters of gold.
Those mothers in Israel, (some now pass'd away,)
They have borne well the burden and heat of the day,
And their names should be honor'd while time shall en-
	dure
Their reward in the future, awaiting, is sure.
Talk of heroes that fought and the victories won
The mem'ries emblazoned on tablets—in song,
They fought but for country and they won the proud day
And rose from oppression, and a tyrants dread sway.
But the women of Utah a victory will claim
That will place their rank foremost in annals of fame;
Forc'd to leave home and friends to these mountains to
	fly; 
God will watch over them, while His wrath passes by
Their deeds are on record and remember'd will be
In this world through all time, and in eternity.
But my pen it would fail, and these pages would swell
If their self-denial and worth I should tell.
The women of Utah let their names be enrolled
On the brightest escutcheons the future shall hold
And their mem'ry forever engraven shall stand
'Mong the noblest and fairest of every land. 
	C. DANIELS.
Provo Jan. 22, 1877,


A LEAF FROM A JOURNAL.

One day I searched an ancient cabinet
And found a musty book; I have it yet—
A journal of the good year'twenty-nine,
Kept by a long departed friend of mine;
And from its leaves grown brown with age,
I take a story and a single page:

	"Roses, oh Roses,
		Handing passion-red,
		Crimson warmth o'cried,
		Thus hath he said,
			'Wait, wait, I come!'

	"Violets, oh Violets,
		Guileless wonder hearts so true,
		Faithful little souls of blue,
		I whisper it to you:
			'Wait, wait, he comes!'

	"Daisies, oh Daisies,
		Snow-white things of purity,
		A little patience teach me.
		I blush to whisper thee:
			'Wait, wait, he comes!'

	"Wil!ow, oh Willow.
		Willow drooping tenderly,
		Hear me softly say to thee,
		These words, his words to me:
			'Wait love, I come!'

	"Heart, oh heart,
		Bless this dear, dear day
		That brings from far Bombay,
		These words that to thee say:
			'Wait love, I come!"

This old-time love-song beareth date
Of June, the month of roses and of fate,
The twentieth day;
	And just below, one sees
The story's end, in words like these:
"In flowery, far-away Bombay,
Died—lit June, the twentieth day."
	GEORGE H. PICARD.


A LADY asked her servant how the mus-
tard pot had become cracked. The reply,
made with all gravity, was that shc did not
know; but supposed it was owing to the
mustard being so strong.


MUSICAL AND DRAMATIC NOTES.

 The Boston Music Hall was open for its
Annual Concert Apr. 11, 1877. The enter-
tainment was quite a remarkable one in
character, and some of the most famous ar-
tists America can boast participated in it;
the programme was miscellaneous and as
Boston is the metropolis of America in mu-
sic, it will not be out of place to reprint the
bill in full, as it might be a model for other
musicals.

l. Hungarian suite, op. 16........Hofmann
			Orchestra.
2. Aria, “Cara fu solo a me,”
			Aiceste..................Gluck
		Miss Annie Louise Cary.
3. Concerto, No. 4, G-major, op. 53.
		...........................Beethoven
Mme. Annette Essipoff and orchestra.
4. Mad scene from “Hamlet,” 
		...........................A Thomas.
	Miss Clara Louise Kellogg.
5. Largo (adapted By J. Helmsberger),
		new...........................Handel
 For violin, violas, harp, great organ, and
solo violin, by Mr.S. E. Jacobson [first time
in America].
6. Aria,"O don fatale,” Don Carlos,
			.............................Verdi
		Miss Annie Louise Cary.
7. Solo piano, 'Rhapsodie Hongroise,
		No. 2................................Liszt.
	Mme. Annette Essipoff.
8. Rolero, Sicilian Vespers.........Verdi.
	Miss Clara Louise Kellogg.
9.Waltz, Autumn roses...........Strauss.
			Orchestra.
10. Duet, Quis est homo...........Rossini.
Misses C. L. Kellogg, and, A. L. Cary.
11. March and chorus, Tannhauser
		...................................Wagner.
		Orchestra and great organ. 
	We have read the reviews of this enter-
tainment in the Boston papers and publish
here some extracts:
	“Madame Essipoff was heard in the best
music which she has ever played in Bos-
ton.  *  *  The technical difficulties of the
composition were simply forgotten under
Madame Essipoff's treatment.  *  We do
not think it would be possible to find a bet-
ter interpreter than Madame Essipoff of
such a fiery product of Listz’s peculiar gen-
ius.
 “Miss Carey was in wonderful voice, and
sang Gluck’s “Cara fu solo a me” with grand
fulness of tones and appropriate gravity, of
style.” Upon being recalled she gave
Beethoven’s Arietta, “In gusta tombo,”—
“O don fatale” from Verdi’s Don Carlos.”
She also gave “Summer Evening Memor-
ies." Miss Kellogg interpreted Ophelia’s
mad scene with genuine dramatic life and
discrimination.  Miss Kellogg also display-
her vocal fluency and her exact and easy
skill as an “executante” in the Rolero from
“Sicilian Vespers.” In response to an im-
perative ENCORE she sang “The Little
Shepherdess.” Madame Essipoff politely
yet firmly declined to repeat any of her
numbers though repeatedly called back to
the stage.
 ANNA E. Dickinson made her debut the
second of April at the Eagle Theatre, New
York.
 MME, Ristori gave a performance at the
Apollo Theatre in Rome recently, for the,
benefit of the Working Men’s Co-operative
Society. The piece was “Elizabetta d' In-
ghilterra,” and the great actress was ably
seconded by the Morelli Company, which
came expressly from Siena for the repre-
sentation.
 MME. Adelina Patti has appeared with
the greatest success in “Semiamide," a part
the lady has not heretofore sustained in
Europe. 
 AT Liszt's recent concert at Vienna, the
city of Vienna presented him with the
large gold “Salvator medal.” The Viennese
critics dwell on the piano-forte performances
of Dr. Liszt as equal to those of his best pe-
riod. The instrument was covered with
crowns and flowers. He accompanied Mme.
Gomperz-Bettelheim in the Scotch airs of
Beethoven.


ABOUT THE "LITERATI."

	SCRIBNER & Co. announce for the com-
ing season, a monograph upon Charlotte
Bronte including letters by her which have
never before appeared in book form.
	MRS. Macguid will shortly publish a ser-
ies of papers to be designated "Among the
Yorkshire Abbeys:" they will appear in one
of the English magazines.
	How literary men and women write their
books and study is always a matter of inter-
est: here is a sketch of some noted authors.
Mr. Trollope asserts that he does all his
writing before breakfast. Mr. Tom Taylor
that all his literary work was performed be-
fore official hours in the morning. H. J.
Byron says he only writes two hours in a
day, but he takes credit for thinking a
great deal. Mr. Hepworth Dixon literally
writes from morning to night. George El-
liot is at her desk from six to seven hours a
day.Mrs.Henry Wood writes every day until
dinner time. Farjeon, on being asked "how
many hours a day he wrote," replied "all
the time" which is asserted to be very
nearly true. The late Lord Lytton said
that in two hours daily great things in lit-
erature might be done. Scott never on his
working days wrote less than four or five
hours, and he always sat down to his desk
primed. There was no affectation of lei-
sure about the authors of Pendennis and
David Copperfield, aud Belzac would lock
himself up for weeks together and wrote
hour after hour at a stretch. Miss Braddon
has been in the habit of writing from ten
to sixteen hours a day, a couple of years
ago her labor was so incessant that it turned
her brain and she had to lay down her pen
for many months in the middle of a novel.
		Ex.
	HERBERT Spencer's opinion of George
Eliot is that she is "the greatest woman
that ever lived."
	"THE Two Americas," is the title of a
new book by Major Sir Rose Lambert Price
Bart. The critics denounce the book as an-
ti-republican in the strongest sense, and
abounding in slang which is decidly unbe-
coming to the British aristocracy. Here is
a sample of his sentiments. "To universal
suffrage all the existing ills in all existing
republics, can be distinctly traced."


Good nature is the best feature in the finest
face. Wit may win admiration, judgment
command respect, knowledge attention,
beauty inflame the heart with love, but
good nature has a more powerful effect, it
adds a thousand attractions to the charms
of beauty, and gives an air of beneficence
to the homliest face.
