SALT LAKE CITY, MAY 1, 1877.


[Column 1]
		REVERIES OF A WOMAN.

					"These can lie,
	Flatter and swear, deprave, inform,
	Smile and betray: make guilty men, then beg
	Their forfeit lives, to get the livings; cut
	Men's throats with whisperings; sell to gaping suitors
	The empty smoke that flies about a palace."

	Perchance some one reads this title and
apostrophizes thus: O, some love tale long past,
recalled by a winter's fire to amuse the fancy.
You never were mere mistaken, dear reader.
Women's reveries are often prophetic as well
as their dreams, and there is more in them
than some people give them credit for know-
ing. But to come back to the subject. "The
times are out of joint" so it would seem, as
much now as when Shakespeare lived (and
Shakespeare lives to-day mere truly than many
living men) for men rave madly of blood and
ruin and desolation, without counting the
cost. It would be wiser if THEIR reveries
were more sober. Assuredly they must be
dreaming. When men concoct schemes of
ruin and desolation they should consider
the fearful consequences.
	It is not a trifling matter to seek to disfran-
chise a vast body of people, whose worst fault
is that they have been prospered and become a
power in the land; who have wrested the desert
lands from their barrenness, and by assiduous
toil have overcome almost superhuman difficul-
ties. Driven out from civilization, the Latter-
day Saints crossed the sandy plains amidst
savages and wild beasts, women and children
too, encountering every peril with the courage
and heroism of those Pilgrim Fathers whose
descendants many of them were, and for what?
That they might seek out an abiding place,
where, free from molestation of their enemies,
they might dwell in peace and gather around
them their families, and teach their children
the laws of God, and the commandments of
heaven as revealed to them. These are their
offences. For these things they are now ar-
raigned by self-styled judges, who condemn
them to the severest penalties of the law, and
beseech Congress with ten thousand lies in
their mouths to wipe out this Mormon people,
if it must be done at the point of the bayonet.
	Gentlemen, if such you be, if you have hu-
man blood in your veins, by all that you hold
sacred, stop, before you go further. Why do
you seek the lives of this people? What would
you with them? Have you not come here and
found homes? Have you any injustice to com-
plain of at the hands of this people, whom you
so foully abuse and misrepresent? Why are
you here? Was there not room for you in oth-
er states and territories? Was there not scope
for your marvelous imaginations? If this peo-
ple are so low, so miserable, so degraded, why

[Column 2]
did you come? They were happy without you;
this city was not defiled with the many curses
which infest it now, ere you came. This peo-
ple were abundantly capable of maintaining
themselves and opening up continually new
facilities and industries; their homes were the
abodes of peace and coutentment; their chil-
dren were taught to reverence God and not pro-
fane His holy name. How is it to-day, under
the influence of the outside element, of the
civilization of the nineteenth century? Very
different indeed.
	The Mormons left their homes in Ohio, in
Missouri, in Illinois; they bid adieu to their
friends, and for their religion traversed the
desert wilds, giving up all they had heretofore
held dear in the world, They planted towns
and villages on their way, that have since
grown and flourished and been converted into
homes for thousands of people. They came to
this barren, desolate country, where no white
man had ever lived, came here with their little
helpless ones, and commenced anew. Women
and children will not hold their peace and see
their rights again jeopardized. The voice of
woman will be lifted against you who are seek-
ing their destruction. Let them alone, leave
them to themselves. Far better would it be for
you to hide your heads in shame, and seek in
all humility to make reparations for the wrongs
already done this people, than to seek to stir
up strife and hatred against them. What class
of men are ye that are thus howling and rag-
ing? "Verily the heathen rage and the people
imagine a vain thing." Are ye men who fear
God, and dare to talk of ruin and blood to wo-
men and innocent children.
	"Vengeance is mine saith the Lord," have
you forgotten these words, or do you not ac-
knowledge the words of God? Remember that
in this day and age it is so ordained, that wo-
men speak and represent their own cause. The
women of Utah are abundantly able to repre-
sent the cause they have espoused, and they
will, and who shall hinder them? The Mor-
mon women have been misrepresented, and
have suffered reproach and calumnies without
having the means to contradict them; but now,
thanks to the liberality of the age as regards
woman, she is permitted to have a voice in her
own affairs and represent her own cause. The
Mormon women are the ones who are engaged
in solving some of the most intricate social and
moral problems, and it would be wise if the
world could have the patience to wait and see
the result.
	The Mormon women trust in God, they have
implicit faith in His promises; they are sincere
and earnest in living practical lives, they are
capable of great self-sacrifice; they believe Gcd
has spoken, and, true to the divinity of their
natures, they are ready to honor Him and
obey His commandments.
	How mistaken the world are, when they rep-
resent Mormon women in bondage to men.
There is no greater freedom than the Gospel
gives to woman. And it is this that makes
Mormon women conscious of their power.
They have suffered and become strong; expe-
rience has matured them, and given them a
higher order of attainment than a mere men-
tal education. Have not the enemies of this
people caused suffering enough to women and
children? Have not the hardships already en-
dured in times past, the recital of which has
often brought tears from the eyes of strangers,
given you sufficient evidence that you cannot
eradicate from this people the principles for
which they have endured so much? Let them
alone; if they are wrong, time will eventually
show them the error; if they are right, how
vain are all your efforts. The charity which

[Column 3]
the Saviour taught would not admit of this
bitter spirit of animosity.
	God is for the weak: He defends the innocent.
He will not suffer those who trust in Him to
be dismayed; besides He has set up His king-
dom never to be thrown down. The women
are the majority in the land; they will not let
their claims to justice and the rights of citizen-
ship be ruthlesly set aside. God is for woman: he
ever has been; Jesus revealed Himself to woman
after His resurrection. Woman in this age has
suffered much, and, like unto Sarah, some have
given to God in sacrifice their heart's best offer-
ing, and God will remember them. Cease to per-
secute the saints of God, for if you do not God
will come out from His hiding-place and will
vex the nations. You think the Mormons an
insignificant body of people; they are mighty
in spirit, and their women are united in one
grand solid phalanx to help build up Zion, to
sustain their own institutions, educational and
social, and wo be to him who dares to lift his
hand against Zion.
	[handwritten] E. B. Wells


HOME AFFAIRS.

	A SPECIAL TRAIN left the city at 6 a. m.,
Friday, April 27th, with a large party of breth-
ren and sisters, to meet President Brigham
Young and company at York. They returned
at half-past one p.m., the party seem univer-
sally to be in good health and excellent spirits.
The Latter-day Saints and the many friends of
President Brigham Young will be glad to know
he arrived at home safe and well. The Presi-
dent has preached several times on the way.
The party spent Sunday at Richfield, where
they held meetings. Presidents Brigham
Young, John W. Young, D. H. Wells, Elders
J. F. Wells and Charles Burton addressed the
congregation. The company arrived at Manti on
Tuesday last. The same afternoon the brethren
visited the hill where it is designed to erect a
Temple and held meeting in the evening. Pre-
sident D. H. Wells and Elders J. F. Wells ad-
dressed the people. Wednesday morning held
meeting again, President John W. Young and
Elder George Gibbs were the speakers. At
noon they repaired to the Temple site, laid off
the ground and dedicated it, President Brigham
Young offered the dedicatory prayer and turn-
ed over the first sod; the company then drove
on as far as Moroni, where they passed the
night, thence direct for home.
	There has been a vast amount of preaching
done during the journey by the First Presidency
the Twelve Apostles and by the young Elders
in the interest of the Y. M. M. I. Association.
The sisters who went south have also spoken in
the Relief Societies in many of the settlements.
There is the same good feeling expressed by
each and every one in referring to the Temple
and the Conference. And to the beloved Pres-
ident of this people and his party we can
heartily say, Welcome, Welcome Home.
	MUSTARD.—The best quality of mustard can
be raised in Utah, it has been satisfactorily test-
ed. The Woman's Commission House would
gladly recommend a good article of homemade
mustard. How absurd for Latter-day Saints to
import thousands of pounds of this article
when a better quality can be manufactured for
home consumption. Brethren and sisters,
raise some mustard and let it be put into the
stores for sale.
	THE SALT Lake Theatre was opened three
nights last week, Miss Adelaide Neilson, cele-
brated for her beauty and dramatic art, per-
formed Juliet in "Romeo and Juliet," Rosa-
lind in "As you like it," and Julia in "The


[Column 1]

Hunchback." Miss Neilson received the
greatest applause from the audiences here, and
her beauty and many attractions win admira-
tion Eastand West. She played with feeling,
her voice is melodious, her manner quiet and
unaffected, her dress magnificent.
	WE RECEIVED through the courtesy of a
friend the first number, issued April 1, of a
new paper established by women. It is called
"Woman's Words" and is to be issued month-
ly in Philadelphia: price $1.00 per year; Mrs.
Juan Lewis, publisher, 625 Walnut Street. It
is designed to promote the interest of women
throughout the United States, without regard
to sect or party: and upon such liberal ground
it cannot fail to meet with hearty appreciation
from those who desire the promotion of uni-
versal good. We find this number contains
much excellent matter, and many items of in-
terest relative to woman's progression in litera-
ture, science and art. It does not affect the
extreme on the woman's rights question, but a
proper and fitting medium upon all the points
discussed in its columns.
	ANNUAL MEETING of the R. A. in the First
Ward, S. L. City. This meeting convened at
the residence of Mrs. Mary W. Musser, April
4, 1877. The financial report of the Society
during three years was read by Mrs. Alice
Fletcher, Treasurer. This report alone, clearly
shows the energy, perseverance and executive
ability of the officers and members, and that
the object of their ambition is a laudable one,
viz., to assist in improving themselves and
others, and help to establish the kingdom of
God upon the earth.
	The meeting was presided over by President
Mrs. Sarah Graham, who has been earnest and
energetic in her endeavors to assist the young
ladies in this good work. Elder J. Booth and
A. Steel both spoke very encouragingly to the
Society, and assured them that a decided im-
provement was manifest in many ways. Mrs.
Marion Chase, President of the Relief Society
of that ward, addressed them in a manner cal-
culated to impress them with the greatness of
the work in which they had enlisted. Allud-
ed to the temporal as well as spiritual matters
which were pending. Mrs. Steel, Schnitar
and Brown and several of the young sisters
expressed themselves on the principles of the
Gospel, the great work now being performed
by women, the necessity of union and the de-
sire they had to do all in their power towards
sustaining home industries and aiding in every
good undertaking. Mrs. Sarah Graham made
some excellent remarks in regard to the oppor-
tunities afforded the young ladies for improve-
ment, expressed great pleasure in being asso-
ciated with them and meeting together and
helping each other. Some business was tran-
sacted, a vote of thank given to the Secretary,
Assistant Secretary and Treasurer, and a uni-
versal good feeling pervaded the meeting.
	THE SPECIAL mission given to the Relief So-
cieties by the vote of the Conference, viz.
sericulture, should receive the earliest careful
attention; certainly the first movement to be
made is the arranging to have mulberry trees
grown, sufficient to supply food for large quan-
tities, of worms. It is to be hoped that the
sisters will take hold of this branch of indus-
try with the energy and perseverance they
have proven themselves capable of, when called
upon to undertake anything requiring faith,
prayer and executive ability. Do not be
afraid you will have too much to do; overwork
is not from so much being done, but it is
crowding off until the necessity comes, and
then hurrying; method in the performance of
work is the antidote for this sort of thing.

[Column 2]

When people learn to systematize their labor,
and all concerned in any department will
agree, when there are no idlers who leave their
share of work for another to perform, the aggre-
gate proportion that suffer from over-work will
be greatly reduced. "Hurry and High Press-
ure" are evils to be avoided. If the sisters will
concentrate their efforts (and no doubt they
will) they can accomplish all that can be de-
sired in this direction; for it will not only be a
source of profit, and bring wealth into the
community, but it will be one of the best ave-
nues for true charity, that which gives labor
to the poor, and makes them self-sustaining,
instead of non-producers. Mr. Jennings has,
we understand, donated $200 towards buying
machinery. There has already been consider-
able success achieved through the untiring
perseverance of some of the sisters, and when
the united exertions of thousands are brought
to bear in this direction no doubt the results
will be abundantly satisfactory.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.		181


THE OBJECT OF YOUNG LADIES'
			ASSOCIATIONS.

	It is a difficult matter to have some under-
stand the nature of our Retrenchment Asso-
ciations. A young lady remarked to me the
other day that she did not see what benefit it
was, for those who attended the meetings did
not retrench.
	Let us reflect a few moments. Is there noth-
ing we need to retrench except our dress? Let
us examine our minds, morals and principles,
our daily life, the object of our being here upon
the ea[-]th, and see which needs our attention
most.
	God says in a revelation, given Dec. 27, 1832,
37th paragraph, page 106, Doctrine and Cove-
nants:  "Therefore cease from all your light
speeches; from loud laughter; from all your
lustful desires; from all your pride and light-
mindedness, and from all your wicked doings.
		See that you love one another.
Cease to be idle; cease to be unclean; cease to
find fault one with another; cease to sleep
longer than is needful; retire to thy bed early,
that ye may not be weary; arise early, that
your minds may be invigorated; and above all
things, clothe yourelves with the bonds of
charity as with a mantle, which is the bond of
perfectness and peace; pray always, that ye
may not faint until I come."
	It is our duty and privilege to have pure and
intelligent minds. It is necessary then to learn
of the things of God, to store our minds with
useful knowledge, to think and reflect what is
His will concerning us. Are we aiding our
fellow creatures? Are we cultivating purity,
charity, benevolence and those virtues which
are essential to our happiness and which will
fit us for the high and responsible positions we
may be called to fill? Are we sincere and val-
iant in doing the will of God? Can we see any
good resulting from our actions and words?
Are we seeking to live daily the covenants we
have made? Do we love one another? Are
we retrenching from our light-mindedness and
light speeches? Are we seeking to know of
the will of the Lord concerning us? Do we
seek the society of those who are pure and up-
right? Do we daily retrench from what we
may consider small evils? If we have not
gained this perfectness we certainly are, in
these societies, seeking to gain it. I would say
to those who do not attend our meetings, and
who make such remarks, that we wish to pro-
gress step by step; to gain knowledge, be char-
itable, cultivate amiable dispositions, intelli-
gent, pure minds, good morals and principles,
be practical and obedient and improve the


time given us here. These are the robes we
are seeking to invest ourselves in-these are 
the noble objects of our Retrenchment Asso-
ciations.
							M. H.


STORING GRAIN.

WELLSVILLE, CACHE Co., Feb. 18 '77.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
 We wish to inform you that the sisters
in Wellsville feel to respond to the call,
of President Young in regard to storing up
wheat.  Our worthy Bishop, Wm. H.
Maughan with several of the brethren were
present at our last monthly meeting; and
our Bishop gave us some excellent instruc-
tions in regard to STORING GRAIN, as well
other good and wholesome counsel in try-
ing to be self-sustaining and the implic-
it obedience the Lord requirs of us.  Oth-
ers of the brethren encouraged us to go on
in doing good; our president, Ellen Whit-
on exhorted the sisters to be one with the
Priesthood in all things that we may be co-
workers together.  Counselor Margaret
Woodward and several others bore faithful
testimonies to the truth of the gospel, and
manifested a sincere desire to be united in
all good works.  We have commenced stor-
ing up wheat since our last meeting, and
esteem it a great blessing and privilege to
have a prophet to lead us, that we may es-
cape, by listening to counsel, the impending
calamities that will befall the disobedient.
Success to the Exponent, we peruse its
pages with great interest.
              LUCY HASLAM, Sec'y.
        ELIZABETH MAUGHAN, Treas.


THOUGHTS ABOUT RETRENCHMENT.

	These are a few of my thoughts, sisters.
We were organized into societies to re-
trench from everything that is evil; not
only in extravagance of dress, but every
thing that is not of the Lord. We are coun-
seled not to backbite nor speak evil of each
other or our neighbors; and we are not to 
slander or use bad language, whether in
company or at home, and always and every-
where be modest, gentle, kind and polite,
and treat everybody with due respect both
old and young. Girls, I think prayer is one
of the greatest blessings we can enjoy; by
faith and prayer the sick are healed; and I
believe the plan of healing as taught by the
gospel is better than any other way that has
been introduced; and girls, if any of you
are in trouble first call on the Lord and He
will hear and answer your intercession.
	My young sisters let us now go forth and 
help to build up the kingdom of God that
we may come forth in the morning of the
First Resurrection, among the Saviors on
Mount Zion.
				CLARA E. SNEDAKER.
	Mill Creek, Apr. 4. 1877.


THE Empress of Japan visited the shools,
factories, and public offices at Kioto recent-
ly. Great interest was taken in the visits,
this being the first occasion of any Japanese
Empress taking part in public proceedings.
Eastward the star of empire takes its way,
perhaps, for woman."—Ex.
	From the above and many similar occur-
ences, which have and are transpiring from
time to time, it is easy to perceive there is 
a new era opening for woman; and that she
is gradually partaking of the spirit and 
feeling which permeates throughout the 
length and breadth of the land.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]

CORRESPONDENCE.

	PARADISE, CACHE CO., Apr. 8, 1877.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	Your valuable paper comes regularly to
hand and is a most welcome visitor. For
women I consider it the most useful and
interesting paper published, and think it
should be in the household of every Latter-
day Saint for the wives, mothers and daugh-
ters to read and profit by; and if the male
members read it also it, cannot fail to in-
struct them, for its teachings are the pure
principles of the gospel written by inspira-
tion of the spirit of God. When I reflect
upon the perfect order of the Kingdom of
God, the beauty, harmony and unity of its
various organizations, extending even to
the women, and to the young people of
both sexes, uniting and binding all togeth-
er in one interest, that of both temporal
and spiritual salvation. I often wonder
what more evidence any reflecting mind
can require to substantiate the divine ori-
gin of this work. I take great pleasure in
reading the several reports of the Relief
Societies, in learning of the good which
they have done and are doing, but most es-
pecially was I impressed with reading the
article on STORING GRAIN, republished
from No. 10, not having read it before, as I
did not take the EXPONENT at that time
which was my loss; I fully endorse the in-
instructions given, and shall endeavor to 
carry them out to the best of my ability
as soon as circumstances will permit.
	Ever praying for the welfare of Zion, I
remain your sister in the gospel,
	LYDIA B. REMINGTON,
		Prest. R. Society.

	TOOELE, TOOELE Co., March 15, 1877
EDITORS EXPONENT:
	As spring has come we are very busy
putting in the grain and we hope the Lord
will bless us with an abundant harvest, so
that we can fill our bins for a future day.
Brother Micklejohn has kindly given us the
use of one of his wheat bins until we can
get one of our own. The sisters have no-
bly and liberally donated; we have at pres-
ent, over one hundred bushels of wheat,
and with the help of our Heavenly
Father, we will still keep storing it up
for a day to come; and we feel that through
His blessings upon our labors the coming
season, we will be able to more than double
the amount that we now have on hand. We
held a silk meeting on the 10th of the
month, to take into consideration the plant-
ing out of mulberry trees, and to encour-
age the culture of silk for we know that it
will lay the foundation for a good work in
the future, and we know that it is our duty
to do all that lays in our power to help
build up the kingdom.
	I know that this is the true church and
kingdom of God, and that it will never be
thrown down or given to another people;
it will stand forever. It came not from
man nor from the will of man, but from
God our eternal Father, it is His cause and
His work and we must not think that the 
name of a saint is going to save us, for we
will all be judged by the deeds done in the
body. When I think of this it causes my
heart to rejoice and fills me with gratitude
to my Heavenly Father; and I pray that
we may be kept faithful to the end of our
days.
	Success to the EXPONENT and to all good
enterprises.
	A. M. TATE.

[Column 2]

	PROVO, APRIL 11, 1877.
EDITOR EPXONENT:
	It becomes my sad duty to acquaint your
readers that death has taken from our ranks
one of the noblest women that have graced
our society with their presence, in the
death of Mary Ann (Yeager) Whipple of
this city: deceased was born Nov. 1, 1822,
in Philadelphia Penn., received the gospel
June, 1844, in Philadelphia, emigrated to
Utah in 1850, was married the same year
to Edson Whipple, they assisted in the set-
tlement of Iron County with Prest. George
A. Smith's Company in 1852; after helping
to establish the new settlement there, they
removed to Provo where she resided filling
the position of first Counselor to the Presi-
dent of the Relief Societyof the Second Ward
until her death which occurred March 26th
1877 from incipient Small Pox and erysipi-
las, together with consequent nervous ex-
citement in attending her husband's son
Heber, through his illness. Sister Whip-
ple was one of the heroines of the small af-
fairs of life, who pass unnoticed, but the
self sacrifice and devotion to duty, made
her truly beloved and respected by all who
were acquainted with her virtues, her
death under the attending circumstances of
the infectious disease, precluded the paying
of respect by the usual gathering for funer-
al services, * but on the night appointed
for the removal of her remains to their si-
lent resting place, by the side of her hus-
band's son, there were many wakeful ears,
upon which the noise of the wheels of the 
hearse fell with mournful sound, and many
tears fell at the sad and dreary parting with
one so esteemed and worthy of the love of
those her friends who mourned her loss, but
yet comforted with the knowledge that the
Father of all, will not overlook this our sis-
ter when he makes up His jewels, but will
recompense her for all her self sacrafice and
toil during her probation here.
	Deceased leaves four children to mourn
her loss, one, William M. Whipple, is assist-
ing in the settlement of Arizona.
	Respectfully,
	M. WRIDE, Sec.
		Second Ward R. S.
	The City authorities with their board
of health, together with our city Marshal,
J. W. Turner, are entitled to the thanks
and commendations of our citizens for their
prompt and decisive action in keeping the
disease from spreading further among our
community.

	KANOSH, MILLARD Co.,
		April 3rd, 1877.
EDITOR EXPONENT;
	On the 20th of last month we were visit-
ed by some of the sisters who were on their
way to St. George, and we had a very in-
teresting meeting, which was open to all,
and there was a good attendance.
	The meeting was called to order by the
President, Mrs. Adelia Kimball, and after
the usual exercises Mrs. Zina D. Young ad-
dressed the assembly; she spoke of the
Relief Society Organization in Nauvoo;
gave us the order of its arrangement and
said that it was organized for the benefit of
the poor, and those that required aid. Said
the Releif Societies should at all times be
willing to administer to the wants of those
who were suffering and required comfort
and sympathy, but be sure that sympathy
was given in the proper way. Be sure and 
have the Spirit of God at all times, and
then counsel would be given wisely. En-
treated the sisters to refrain from speaking
evil of each other, to be careful how they

[Column 3]

talk before their children, as their minds
are young and they remember the teachings
of their mothers; to cultivate a spirit of
kindness and forgiveness. She spoke of 
the EXPONENT and hoped the sisters would
encourage the taking of it, as it was good,
wholesome reading. Hoped the sisters
would not speak against the principle of
plural marriage, as those in it could scarce-
ly realize the blessings they are in posses-
sion of, and they knew nothing of the out-
sidworld, if they did they would not 
suffer themselves to talk against it. Spoke
of the necessity of becoming self-sustain-
ing, and entreated her sisters in every
way possible to sustain and manufacture all
they could. In regard to the silk culture,
it was now time to plant out the cuttings of
mulberries; showed some silk specimens
produced in the north. Spoke of the pure-
ness of our silk in compaaison to that in-
termixed with other trash, which was
manufactured abroad. Said we all had the
art and capacity to make ourselves self-sus-
taining and a source of wealth to other
countries; hoped the people would take an
interest in the silk culture; spoke of the 
expense President Young had incurred in
assisting to start this branch of home indus-
try, and thought the sisters should take an
interest in it. Spoke on the Grain Move-
ment, and of obedience. She also made
some remarks on the Woman's Book now
in the press, which was written by the
man who wrote the "Life of Brigham
Young;" hoped the sisters would patronize
it. Spoke to the young boys on temper-
ance, exhorting them to abstain from to-
bacco and liquor, and to aim high, cultivate
their minds, and if they would remain
faithful could attain to greater excellence
and power eventually than Queen Victoria's
children. She represented the advantages
arising to the young girls from the Re-
trenchment Organizations, and that all
should endeavor to carry out the counsel of
the servants of God.
	Mrs. Jane S. Richards said she always had
a testimony to bear, and if we lived up to 
the principles taught us we would always
have something good to say. She thought
outside of the Gospel there was no pleasure.
Spoke upon faith in cases of sickness, and
said the Saints should never be without
consecrated oil; told them not to run after
doctors, but to exercise their faith; spoke
of the good things that had been said dur-
ing the day, and added that if we could
only remember them we would be more apt
to do our duty.
	Mrs. Young then spoke of the Woman's
Commission Store in Salt Lake City, said
the home industry was represented there;
invited the sisters to visit it, it was OUR
store—the Sisters of Zion's store—and hoped
the sisters would help the President of the 
Kanosh Relief Society.
	Mrs. Marian Pratt made a few very in-
teresting remarks in conslusion.
	We had a splendid time and a good spirit
prevailed. Our Society is improving and 
all the sisters seem anxious to do their duty.
With regard to the Grain Movement, the 
sisters have truly done what they could;
those who had no wheat put in molasses
and corn and flour and peaches, and some
has already been exchanged for wheat. By
this means we hope to get in a considerable
amount of grain. We are always pleased
to read so many good reports in the EXPO-
NENT, and especially with regard to the
Grain Movement. The sisters in the Re-
lief Societies have really manifested an
earnest zeal, which does them great credit.
We are having quite an early spring in this
part of the Territory; everything seems


forward; the peach trees are in full bloom
and if the frosts only keep off, there will be
a great quantity of fruit.
 Praying the Lord will bless all the efforts
of His faithful saints, I remain,
Your sister in the gospel of peace,
                              A, PAXTON,
                              Secretary.

			MOUND FORT, March, 8th 1877.
EDITORS EXPONENT:
	The Mound Fort Relief Society was or-
ganized with twenty-six members, on the
29th of January, 1874, by Miss E. R. Snow,
assisted by Mrs. Zina D. Young, of Salt
Lake City, and Mrs. Jane S. Richards of
Ogden. The Society now numbers thirty-
seven.
	We hold semi-monthly meetings which,
considering our scattered condition, are
well attended. The sisters feel well, take
an interest in all matters of a public and 
general nature, which tend to the develop-
ment of good, rejoice in talking to each
other on the principles of the gospel, and 
delight in devising means and laboring to
assist the poor, and to build up the kingdom
of God.
	Our Financial Report up to January 29,
1877, shows the following:
Receipts in cash, merchandise, and
	sundry articles.............................. $87.27
Disbursements.....................................   62.59
On hand................  ..............................    24.68
	Towards purchasing silk machinery sev-
eral sisters have subscribed, in the aggre-
gate $7.00, and they intend to make up
their respective amounts to $5.00 shares.
	The EXPONENT is taken by quite a num-
ber of our Society, and read with great in-
terest. Ever praying the Lord to bless
you in your labors, we remain
		Your sisters in the Gospel
		Susan Moore, President,
		Mary Farr,		} Counselors.
		Annie Taylor,	}
		Annie D. Taylor, Secretary,
		Mary C. Barker, Treasurer.

		BOUNTIFUL, March 31, 1877.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	It is now some time since you heard from
us so I thought a few lines would not be out
of place. Miss E. R. Snow, Mrs. Zina D.
Young, E. S. Barney, and Presindia Kim-
ball, visited us last fall; they spoke on the
truths of the Gospel and home manufacture;
showed the necessity of sustaining all
branches of home industry so that we may
become a self-sustaining people, and when
Babylon falls, we may not be left without
resources. They brought a good spirit in
in our midst, since then our meetings have
kept on increasing, and the spirit of God
has been with us; we have been greatly
blessed in many ways; we have been able
to put the roof on our Relief Society Hall,
and have also got the flooring laid, so that
we can have the use of it. We are endeav-
oring to carry out President Young's coun-
sel, by STORING GRAIN; the sisters seem to
have the spirit of their mission, and have
freely responded to the call made of them.
We are also interested in the silk culture
and hope to see a great many trees set out
this spring, and some silk worms raised;
we are having a ribbon loom made which
will soon be ready for use, hoping that it 
will be an inducement to the sisters to raise
the silk.
	I wish to say a word in favor of the 
Young Ladies Retrenchment Association.
They have a faithful president in Mrs. Su-
san Grant, and hold their meetings every

[Column 2]
two weeks. They are now editing a paper
called the Young Ladies Companion, which
is both instructive and interesting; they 
also read good selections when they assem-
ble together, and most of them seem to
take great interest in this Latter-day work,
and are faithful in attending their meet-
ings. 
		Yours &c.,
			MARY E. CARTER, Sec'y.

EDITOR EXPONENT:
	On the evening of Tuesday, March 20th,
a surprise birthday party was tendered Mrs.
Elizabeth Stickney, President of the Santa-
quin and First President of the Spring
Lake Relief Societies and the teacher of 
our precinct school,—by the misses of her
school. A number of her friends were in-
vited and all who participated seemed to 
enjoy themselves. She was the recipient
of several presents.
	The Relief Society here, under the pres-
idency of Mrs. Elizabeth Broadbent is 
steadily growing and all the sisters seem to
take a lively interest in helping to make
the Society a blessing. We have not been
able yet to lay up much GRAIN, but expect
the present year to make a good start in
this direction.
							D. E. B.
	Spring Lake, March 21st, 1877.


SUPERFLUOUS MEN.
	I claim no originality for my caption.  It
was suggested, of course, by Mrs. Liver-
more's "Superfluous Women," but aside
from the qualifying adjective, her "Super-
fluous Women" and my "Superfluous Men"
have nothing in common.
 The world would be the loser if those su-
perfluous women, mostly earnest, helpful
workers in the quiet walks of life, should
suddenly drop out of it, while the men, I
have in mind, are superfluous not on ac-
count of their number, but their character,
they are superfluous in the sense that they
are useless, a hindrance rather than a help
to society and the world would be better oft
without them.
 1. Of these I will mention first the loaf-
ers.  This is a large class comprising many
grades from the tramp to the genteel loafer
who belongs to a club and smokes the best
Havannas, but they all have the same gen-
eral characteristics.  They are lazy, they
have no visible means of support and like
the man who, being brought before the Po-
lice Court and interrogated as to his busi-
ness, replied that his wife was a dress-mak-
er, a large number are dependent on hard-
working, self-sacrificing wives, mothers and
sisters. They twirl their canes and their
mustaches they gossip, they flirt, they ex-
pectorate, they stand on the street corners,
they frequent drinking saloons, and all
places of high and low resort, they gamble,
they bet, they talk politics, they vote (oh
yes early and often), but their one distin-
guishing characteristic is, their aversion to
any kind of honest industry.  According
to all rules of political economy this large
class of non-producers are entirely superflu-
ous.  They add nothing to the wealth or
well-being of the nation but stand outside
all its great needs and interests.
 2.  Habitual liquor drinkers and confirmed
drunkards.  While there is life there is
hope is doubtless true of drunkards as of
invalids, but it seems to require almost a
miracle to eradicate the appetite for strong
drink when once firmly established.  Think
of the demoralizing influence of this loath-
some mass of humanity, the misery of
wives and families, the poverty, the desti
tution, the crime, the little children continu-
ally brought into being by these wretched
fathers to inherit their morbid appetites
and diseased constitutions!
 3.  Closely allied to these are the licen-
tious, the debauchees, the moral lepers of
community, whose touch is pollution whose
breath is pestilential and who, besides their
every day pernicious influence are entail-
ing upon future generations the horrible
results of their vicious lives.
 If the first class mentioned are superflu-
ous these are far worse.  The first may be
compared to barnacles on the ship of state
—the last two are leeches drawing the life-
blood from the social body and poisoning
the whole circulation; taken together, they
are the drags and dregs of our civilization.
Could they all be suddenly blotted out of
existence by a series of discriminating
earthquakes or accidents, what an incubus
would be removed from society how would
the atmosphere be purified, and progress
and reform make giant strides towards a
millennial age!
	4.Again, there is a multitude of blatant
pot-house politicians, demagogues and of-
fice-seekers, filibusters and the like who
are a superfluity in the body politic.  Could
we be rid of them also, we should speedily
have a united and prosperous people, an
unexceptionable civil service, a model Re-
public and a modern Utopia with all the
modern improvements including Impartial
Suffrage!
					A. E. DICKENSON. 
Woman’s Journal.


ALFRED TENNYSON.

			Alfred Tennyson is to-day the
monarch, the sovereign of English verse.

	It began a new era in English verse when
Alfred came caroling out of Lincolnshire.
and since [-]e has devoted his life exclu-
sively to his calling. Having once discov-
ered the divine gift in his wallet he has
never neglected or slighted his charge.
Tennyson as a poet adds industry to inspir-
ation. Not running to publishers with
quickly created verses, he leaves them out
until he has given them the virtue of the
fullest posssible excellence. His motto has
ever been "Perfection or Silence."
	With some writers poetry is an employ-
ment, with Tennyson it is an existence.
Tennyson's knowledge is extensive and
great. In science he keeps fully abreast of
the times; he is a master of philosophy as
well as poesy; and in botany he is almost
without a peer. His knowledge is exact.
As well as Huxley he is the very genius of
verification. It is Tennyson's custom af-
ter writing a poem, to have it put into type,
carefully corrected and then put away for
years, until the time comes when the au-
thor sees fit to have it published. His
egotism has often been condemned but it is
magnificent. There is nothing small or
mean about it. He is always altering his
verses, but ever for the better. Willis
once said in that habitually felicitous man-
ner of his, that a literary reputation could
only be built up, like the walls of Jerusa-
lem, with a trowel in one hand to plaster
in friends, and a sword in the other to
smite down foes. But Tennyson has been
as a rule, supremely scornful and regardless
of all his critics.—"The Pulpit of the Day."


"EXPERIENCE" says Jean Paul Ritcher,
"is the best schoolmaster, but the school-fees
are somewhat heavy."


184		WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


A SCRIPTORIAL DRAMA.

				SELECTED.

	The scriptorial drama of "Joseph and his
Brethren," says the "Northern Echo," has 
been performed in the hall of the Mechan-
ics' Institute, Darlington, before a large and
appreciative audience, by an amateur com-
pany of Primative Methodists. The scrip-
torial narative has been divided into nine
scenes, which were gone through with
much spirit by the performers, who were
all dressed in character. Pharaoh's throne,
and crown, Joseph's coat of many colours,
Bejamin's sack with the king's cup, to-
gether with the necessary Oriental cos-
tumees, were all represented with remark-
able fidelity. At intervals, between the
scenes, appropriate pieces of music were
rendered with much vigor by the brethren,
who, whether singing or acting, entered
heart and soul into their respective roles.
The representation was received with much
favor, and it will shortly be repeated.


SCIENTIFIC NOTES.

	IT is said that the United States Weather
Bureau is ahead of any in the world in the
accuracy of its forecasts.

	ANOTHER cure running race with that of
blue glass, is now doing wonderful work
in France. It is termed the metallotherapy
and electric homoepathy: it consists of the
application of metallic plates or cylinders—
gold, copper, or iron. It is stated some
very remarkable cures of paralysis have
been affected.

	Scientists assert that a system of tele-
graphy originated long since in Africa
among the negroes; it has been in opera-
tion from time immemorial on the west
coast of Africa in Cameroons county. The
instrument is called the "illeembic," and
through sounds produced upon striking it,
the natives converse across miles of Terri-
tory with ease and fluency as if standing
face to face, the noises constituting a com-
plete language, as intelligible to the opera-
tor as speech. The method is telephonic
and the signals are read by sound and not 
sight.

	THE great engineer of the Suez, M. De
Lesseps, made a calculation in regard to
turning the great Desert of Sahara into a
sea, a manufactured ocean. The French
marines discussed the matter, and consider-
able excitement was caused by it. Time,
however, has elicited a cooler calculation,
and Prof. Maury (who is, we believe, con-
sidered an authority on such questions)
says it "would not impoverish the seas
proper, no more than a man is impoverished
by a dollar dropped into the contribution
box." Science waits the "denouement."

	SCRIBNER'S Monthly describes a new
form of gas stove, designed to give warm
fresh air to a room, and provide for getting
rid of the products of combustion by means
of the chimney. It consists of two upright
cylinders, enclosed one within the other;
the outer cylinder is open at the bottom,
and is connected by means of a pipe with
the chimney. The inner cylinder is con-
nected at the bottom with a fresh air box or
pipe, and takes air from out-of-doors, and is
open at the top. The gas is burned in the
outer cylinder, and serves to heat both the
outer walls and the inner cylinder, through
which air is drawn from out of-doors, and
sent warmed into the room."


PEN AND SCISSORS.

 DIOGENES on being asked,  "Of which
beast is the biting the most dangerous?"
answered,  "If you mean wild beasts, 'tis
the slanderer; if tame, the flatterer."
	"LET every man" said Sydney Smith,
"be occupied, and occupied in the high-
st employment of which his nature is
capable, and die with the consciousness that
he has done his best."  If the highest em-
ployment is not to be found in our daily av-
ocations, let us seek it in our leisure.
 THE affection which shrinks from pub-
licity, seeks to be invisible, and avoids cer-
emony, is more true and deep than that
which finds its solace in that outward dis-
play which invites the comment of the
world at large.

WOMAN'S EXPONENT.
VOL. 5		 SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, MAY 15, 1877.  No. 24.﻿


RUSTIC WARBLINGS.

The birds are building their pretty nests,
	And sounding their love notes clear and sweet;
The bees are busy and nothing rests;
	Nature seems dancing with fairy feet.

Dear little birds, you may chipper and sing,
	While art and industry your home secures,
As if for your sakes alone came Spring—
	But I know a love tale as sweet as yours.

Beautiful spring-time! cheery and mild,
	With silvery showers and radiant sun:
I could frolic once more like a joyous child,
	Only for work that must be done,

I have thoughts to think, almost too dear
	To lock in my bosom, or to unfold:
I will pencil them down on the paper here,
	And who shall know if they're weak or bold?

I know somebody good and true,
	Whose manly heart is just and kind:
If his eyes are not of the "deepest blue,"
	And his hair "so beautiful!" never mind.

Although not a favorite with the girls,
	Who puff their dresses and flaunt and flirt,
And simper and gossip and shake their curls;
	Although in courting he's no expert;

Yet, children around him feel no neglect,
	The poor are blest from his ample store,
He treats the aged with great respect,
	He is good to his mother—what man does more?

He can laugh as heartily as the best,
	And greatly enjoys a pleasant joke;
But he makes the Sabbath a day of rest;
	He drinks no wine and does not smoke.

Indeed my hero has virtues rare,
	He is free from fashion's unhallowed taint,
He has good, sound sense, he has learning fair,
	He's a model man, he's a brave young saint. 

He loves and his love he dares confess,
	Like the most and best of moral men;
Some young lady may whisper "yes!"
	And she'll be somebody's darling then.

Dear little birdies, sing love tales,
	Irrespective of listening guests;
It is happy May and love prevails,
	So chipper and whistle and build your nests.

My heart is o'erflowing with gladness too,
	My dream of love is so sweet and pure;
I wish I could warble it forth like you,
	But now I must go to my work "for sure."

		WAMSUSIE.
May, 1873.


[For the EXPONENT.
OUR DUTIES AS DAUGHTERS
OF ZION.
"Let us then be up and doing
With a heart for any fate."
 The above are the words of a poet, and
let us put them into practice, and see if we
cannot accomplish more good than we are
now doing.
 Every little while, my dear sisters, we
hear of missionaries being called to go to
different parts of the world to preach the
gospel, others to make new settlements;
and many other duties to perform that
would not be advisable for us to step for-
ward and do.  But for all this we can do
thousands of things that we are not very
diligent in performing, where we would
not be counted stepping over the thresh-
hold of modesty and propriety.  If we can-
not go to foreign countries and preach to
the heathens, if we cannot go and make
new settlements, and help to widen the
borders of Zion, if we cannot quarry, polish
and lay up rock for the temple, we need
not be idle, God never designed that we
should work in this way; but He has given
us our portion of work, and it is for us to
step forward as brave sisters and do it;
we can encourage the brethren in being
faithful to the principles of their religion
and help to build up the kingdom of God.
 We can learn to raise silk, make our own
dresses, ribbons, hats, bonnets, flowers,
trimmings, laces, gloves, and many other
things too numerous to mention, that would
save a great expense and what we save, give
as a donation to a temple, or for some other
noble work that will bless the people of
God.
 By hard study, diligence and persever-
ance, we may qualify ourselves as school-
teachers, clerks, cashiers, telegraphs opera-
tors and fill positions of honor and trust.
Then let us be faithful and let it not be
said that men are kept from other duties
to fill positions that we might occupy to our
credit.
 Let us step forward as brave volunteers
in this great work and take upon ourselves
the responsibilites of accepting situations
in our schools, our stores and telegraph offi-
ces, etc.  Let us be straightforward and
firm in our dealings, gentle and loving in
our governing, courteous and polite in our
manners, lively interesting and instructive
in our conversation ,constant and confiding
in our friendships, and love all that is good,
noble and exalting, ever striving to help
one another, and keep the commandments
of God our heavenly Father.
 We should learn to be our own doctors,
by having faith in the power of the Priest-
hood, and have power to heal the sick by
the laying on of hands.
 We should set an example that will be
worthy of following, and let our every act
be our recommendation into good society.
Encourage the brethren in their works, and
strengthen them in the faith by our pray-
ers.
					MAY B. EUNO.
Fairfield, April 25.


NOTES AND NEWS.

	VASSAR COLLEGE is doing a good work
for future generations by giving opportuni-
ties to women of obtaining not only a high-
er education for themselves but these wo-
men will bequeath to posterity the impress
of their work.
	A MONTHLY magazine is published in
London called the English Woman's Re-
view, which is devoted to social and indus-
trial questions. The "Woman's Journal"
says, it is a perfect mine of facts and infor-
mations on the Woman question.
	THE FRENCH Societi du Deoit des Fum-
mes" held an interesting meeting lately in
Paris in the Salle Lax, which was presided
over by M. Armard Duportal, Deputy for
Toulouse; supported by M. Brisson, mem-
bers of the Municipal Counsel of Paris.
One of the questions brought before the
meeting and upon which papers were read
and speeches were made was "la recherche


de la paterniti." The French law forbids
every attempt to make known the paterni-
ty of illegitimate children. Statistics were
presented showing the number of these
fatherless children, who either die in the
first months of their existence, or live to
people the prisons and galleys.—Ex.
	It seems fully time that women were per-
mitted to speak in their own defense where
such cruelty and oppression exists to
wards the unfortunate. If the women of
America obtain Universal Suffrage it is
probable they may in many ways assist wo-
men of other countries in the protection of
their liberties and rights. Would such laws
as these exist if woman was allowed a voice
in the matter? No! the love of offspring is
too strong with woman, and she bears too
much the impress of divinity in her na-
ture.


NATIONAL WOMAN'S SUFFRAGE
			CONVENTION.

	The Tenth Annual Convention of the
National Woman's Suffrage Association,
will be held in Masonic Hall, corner 6th,
Ave. and 23d St. New York, May 24, 1877.
	"The great effort of the National Wo-
man's Suffrage Association this year, will
be for the 16th amendment which shall
specifically declare woman's right to the
ballot. The right of petition is woman's
only recognized method of influencing leg-
islation; a million names to the 45th Con-
gress would have great moral influence up-
on Congress and the nation. The United
States already has three classes of voters,
the black man, naturalized male citizens,
and amnestied rebels; each of these has
right to the ballot under United States au-
thority. Though by decision of the Su-
preme Court, in case of Virginia L. Minor,
of Missouri, all women were remanded to
the States, yet as the National Government
had power to invest with the ballot in the
above instances, it has like power to en-
franchise woman.
	"Let us therefore meet and discuss the
best methods of attaining this great object."
	The circular is signed by Matilda Joslyn
Gage, Ch. Ex. Com., and the information is
respectfully given also that all contributions
to this convention should be sent to Ellen
C. Sargent, 1733 De Sales St., Washington,
D. C. Letters should be addressed to Mrs.
Gage, Fayetteville, N. Y.

much as the best actions, for they contain

	PERSONS of extended views—Panorama
painters.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
			R. S. REPORTS.

	The General Retrenchment Meeting
which convenes semi-monthly in the Four-
teenth Ward Assembly Rooms was crowded
to overflowing on Sat. April 28. President
Mrs. M. I. Horne made some remarks up-
on labor and its reward; the return of the
brethren and sisters from the Temple, and
the subject of Storing Grain. Said that as
war was now declared on the Eastern conti-
nent the sisters could see the wisdom
of this mission; thought the sisters ought
to do all in their power to be wise virgins.
Counselor Mrs. P. Woodruff said she re-
joiced to to be with the sisters again, but
greatly enjoyed her visit to the Temple and
with the settlements on the journey. Said
the people south were united and were will-
ing to devote themselves to the kingdom
of God. Mrs. Margaret P. Young referred
to the pure enjoyment she had in the Tem-
ple, and the good influence which seemed
to pervade its surroundings and associations,
and the gratitude and thankfulness she felt
at all times in reflecting upon her visit.
Counselor Mrs. E. Howard, said she felt
the spirit of God poured out upon the hand-
maidens of the Lord; thought it was time
the sisters stopped depending upon Baby-
lon, and should make more sacrifices to-
wards building Temples. She felt it was
necessary to retrench from everything
impure and foolish and prepare our hearts
to enter the Temple of God. Mrs. Martha
B. Young made an earnest exhortation to
the sisters; Mrs. Higgs and Mrs. East testi-
fied to the spirit of God that was manifest.
Mrs. East particularly exhorted the sisters
to offer up their sacrifices to God, and lay
all upon the altar, that they might secure
the great reward promised and not fall
short.
		SPANISH FORK, Apr. 28, 1877.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	The second annual reunion of the Young
Ladies Retrenchment Association of this
place was held in the City Hall on Monday,
Apr. 2nd. The exercises commenced at 2
o'clock p. m., by singing and prayer.
Speeches, songs, recitations and original
compositions, together with the reading of
the Annual Review by the editor Miss Alice
Jex; interspersed with sweet strains of mu-
sic by Messrs. Thomas Hitchens, John Mor-
rison, and Miss Emma Isaacs; constituted
the afternoon's entertainment.
	Bishop Geo. D. Snell expressed his pleas-
ure at seeing so many present; spoke of the
advantages to be derived from our Annual
Review; as it enabled us to see the progress
made in the year past: spoke of the necessi-
ty of union and adherence to the rules and
regulations of the society: exhorted the sis-
ters to make their own fashions, and not
follow those of the world.
	Mrs. M. J. Morrison's remarks were full
of kindly feeling and expressions of the
pleasure she felt in having the opportunity
of doing good, and hoped to be able to hon-
or the mission assigned her as president of
the association; she also bore a strong tes-
timony to the healing power which she had
seen made manifest in a recent experience;
spoke of the changes that had taken place
in the society since their last reunion. She
concluded by expressing her desire that as
a society they might be more in earnest in
doing the will of our Heavenly Father, that
they might more fully enjoy His blessings.
	Miss Jane Powel spoke upon the subject
of charity, hoped the sisters would culti-
vate it in their daily lives. Bro. Geo. H.
Brimhall, Prest. of the Y, M. M. I. A.,

[Column 2]
made a short address; he was much pleased
to see the energy and diligence displayed
by the sisters.
	Bishop Snell presented the following offi-
cers for the ensuing year. President, Mrs.
M. J. Morrison; Counselors, Misses M. J.
Thomas, Maria Finch, Elizabeth Martell,
Mary Beckstrom, Clara Conrad—Miss
Jane Robertson was accepted as counselor
in the place of Mrs. Jane Reese, who re-
signed on account of ill health. Superin-
tendent Mrs. Jane Powell; Treasurer, Miss
Jane Robertson; Secretary, Miss Hannah
Snell; Ass't. Secretaries, Misses S. Warren
and Emma Evans; Corresponding Sec'y.,
Mrs. Hannah Cornaby; all of whom were
unanimously sustained.
	Intermission for one hour, during which
time about one hundred and fifty persons
sat down to tables bountifully spread with
picnic which was very tastefully arrang-
ed by a committee previously appointed.
The evening exercises were of the same
character as those of the afternoon. Bish-
op Snell, in connection with other brethren,
blessed and set apart the officers of the so-
ciety after which each officer was called up-
on to express their feelings and they all
expressed their willingness to perform the
various duties assigned them to the best of
their ability.
	The Treasurer read the financial report for
the two past years which showed that the
sum of fifty-seven dollars and forty cents
had been collected, and a disbursement of
forty dollars and fifteen cents, leaving a
balance in hand of seventeen dollars and
twenty-five cents. A five cent Temple
fund, donations received weekly, is in suc-
cessful operation in the association to which
the members contribute. A library has al-
so been started to which books are added
by donation and purchase.
	The second anniversary of the Society
was a time of real pleasure, and ended with
expressions of mutual good feeling and a
determination in the future to be more
zealous of good works.
				HANNAH CORNABY,
							Cor. Sec'y.

			WALLSBURG, Apr. 18, 1877.
EDITORS EXPONENT:
	By request of our worthy President, I
take the privilege of writing a few lines to
let you know how our society is getting
along in this little place; we hold our meet-
ings twice a month and a spirit of peace
and union prevails in our meetings; our
society is small compared with many oth-
ers, about forty members in all; the sisters
feel to be united with other sisters in carry-
ing out the counsel of President Young;
our place is small, and our means limited,
but we intend to do all we can in STORING
GRAIN, and have about forty bushels safely
stored.

		BEAVER CITY, March 28, 1877.
EDITOR EXPONENT;
	We have had quite a spiritual feast of
late listening to the teachings of the breth-
ren and sisters that were on their way to
the General Conference at St. George. The
company of the Twelve Apostles held two
days meeting here which was highly ap-
preciated by the saints of Beaver and other
adjoining settlements. On the first even-
ing after their arrival Elder Joseph F.
Smith gave a very interesting lecture to
the Young Gentlemen and Ladies Associa-
tion, on the rise and progress of the Mor-
mon Church. The following evening Mrs.
Zina D. Young, Mrs. Phebe Woodruff and

[Column 3]
others addressed us, gave much valuable
instruction concerning our temporal as well
as our spiritual welfare; urging us as a com-
munity to be self-sustaining and patronize
home manufactures; they also gave excel-
lent advice to the young; in fact all the
meetings were good and interesting and we
felt to thank the brethren and sisters for
their many valuable instructions.
	Wishing success to your valuable paper,
I remain your sister,
						M. J. Shipp,
							Ass't. Sec'y.
	I also send you the Petition of the ladies
of Beaver, which were three hundred in
number, for publication if you feel so dis-
posed.
	PETITION TO THE HONORABLE MAYOR
	AND CITY COUNCIL OF BEAVER CITY,
	UTAH TERRITORY.

	We the undersigned lady residents and
citizens of Beaver, beg respectfully to rep-
resent to your honorable body, that there
are seven liquor saloons in our city and that
the great amount of spiritous liquors sold
has a demoralizing effect upon the youth of
our community. We consider intemperance
the bane of crime in all civilized countries
where the sale of liquor is not kept under
proper control or prohibited altogether.
The latter we would fain ask at your hand,
but understanding as we do, that the late
Governor Woods refused to sign the amend-
ment to our City Charter with the power
to prohibit; we simply and humbly ask that
you restrain as far as practicable the bane-
ful demoralizing traffic, and raise the li-
cense fee to a figure that will only make
it profitable for medicinal purposes. We
would also ask for the sake of our sons and
brothers that the ordinance forbidding to
sell to "minors under sixteen years of
age;" be made to read twenty-one years
of age; and that the police authorities
be instructed to see that the law is rigid-
ly enforced and give such other relief as
your honorable body deem for the best
good of the youth of our growing com-
munity, and as in duty bound your peti-
tioners will every pray.

			LAKE VIEW, Feb. 28, 1877.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	I send you a few words from our little
place, to let you know how we are getting
along with our meetings. We have com-
bined the Young Men's Meetings and our
Retrenchment Meetings together and the
young men take charge of the meetings
one Sunday night and the young ladies the
next; we all enjoy the spirit of the Lord,
all get up and bear a strong testimony to
the work of God, and the holy spirit rests
upon us in our meetings, and we all take
an active part in making them interesting
and we know that it is our duty to help
bear off the kingdom of God. How proud
our parents would be to have their sons and
daughters grow up to be mighty men and
women in the church of Jesus Christ; and
if we do not improve ourselves while we
have a chance we will have to endure the
consequences, for we will never have the
opportunity to come back and retrace our
footsteps, either before or after we pass
away from this earth. I never felt more
like doing good than I do at the present
time, and we trust that all the young men
and women may persevere in the good
cause.
					J. G. MARTIN.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


EVER THE CHILD "ROSABELL."

Searching ways and haunts of childhood,
	Down its lanes, o'er meadows green,
Through the orchard, field und wildwood,
	For a wreath to crown my Queen.
Ling'ring fondly by the brooklet,
	Rippling through its mossy dell;
Culling flowers by its waters.
	To bedeck fair "Rosabell.

Emblem of a true affection,
	Breathing of a love sublime,
Reaching through the years eternal,
	Of a sinless, deathless clime.
In the dewy morn of childhood,
	Liveth ever, Rosabell"
Why she never merged from girlhood,
	There's no mortal tongue can tell.

There she lives a fairy creature,
	Bright and joyous as of yore,
Sunshine gilding every feature,
	Time can never reach her more.
Turning o'er historic pages
	Pressing back the weight of years,
All the agony of ages,
	Joys and sorrows, hopes and fears,

Sunny life of bud, and blooming,
	Like genil ruled by charmed ring,
Come to me with love's adorning,
	Voices from a far off spring.
Clear blue eyes to mine upturning,
	Rosy lips breathe loving words,
Fragrant as the air of Eden,
	Musical as song of birds.

Bud and bloom of nature's glory,
	Love-light twining "immortelle,"
Fairy tale and simple story,
	Crown the brow of "Rosabell."

Salt Lake City.
	Affectionately inscribed to E. B. W.
		By Sister LU.


[For the Exponent.
		A FEW REFLECTIONS.

	One hundred years have passed away
since our grandsires fought and bled, and
suffered almost every privation to assist in
establishing a Constitution which guaran-
teed unto all, "Liberty and Equal Rights"
—that all who wished might come and wor-
ship God according to the dictates of their
own consciences, and rest secure under
the Nation's broad escutcheon: Our fore-
fathers were filled with a heaven-born hope
that their children from generation to gen-
eration would appreciate and sustain these
inalienable rights bequeathed to them by
their Veteran Fathers.
	It was the spirit of God which inspired
Columbus to search out this contintent,—and
by the same spirit and power our forefath-
ers, although they may not have realized it,
fought and gained the victory, that a Free
Republic might be established and a land
and people prepared that God might again
reveal Himself to His children, and set up
His kingdom never more to be thrown
down.
 What has transpired in this short vista of
time. For a few years the people were
blessed and happy, although there were a few
wars and schisms, yet those who held the
reigns of government, and those who ad-
ministered the same with many others,
stood nobly and firmly for right regardless
of party or sect, and America was free and
blessed above all other nations.
	About the middle of the century the
Lord began to reveal the Gospel and to re-
store His Holy Priesthood again to man.
Then Satan began to stir up the people to
persecute those who believed in direct rev-
elation from God revealed through Joseph
Smith, the prophet whom God raised up in
this the last dispensation to restore the Gos-
pel taught by Jesus and the Apostles, and
also by the ancient prophets; these prophets
when speaking of the corruption of Baby-
lon in the last days, said, "Come out of her,
Oh! my people that ye partake not of her
plagues," and that a people should gather
from every nation under heaven to a land
prepared for them.  When these things be-
gan to come to pass, prejudice sprung up 
among those who administered the laws of 
the land and they disregarded the claims of 
justice; and it was not long before mobs
combined here at home, in our boasted land
of liberty to destroy the Saints of God.
The thought makes the blood of old '76,
that runs through my veins, boil with in-
dignation and shame, that I, a child of the 
Republic with many others whose grand-
sires had fought and died to bequeath to 
their children rights and a law to protect
them in their religious views and the exer-
cise of religious liberty, were compelled at
the point of the bayonet to walk barefooted
through the crusted snow, cruelly cutting
our feet, so much so, that the path made by
us was a trail of blood.  Ah! methinks if 
the fathers of our glorious Republic and 
framers of the Constitution could have
viewed the scene, their very ashes which
have lain mouldering for years must have
been stirred in their graves.  Not only did 
this great wrong exist among the minor
officers and administrators of the laws
of the land, but those who stood at 
the head of the nation were embittered
with the same prejudices.  When the saints
appealed for justice and redress, to Presi-
dent Van Buren--his reply was "Gentle-
men your cause is just but I can do nothing
for you."  Well might liberty blush and re-
tire to the mountains, and justice hang its
head in shame, because there was no Exec-
utor; and thus were the Saints driven from
city to city and from state, to state and in-
to the Great Americcan Desert which has 
indeed been made "to blossom as the rose."
Still have our enemies continued to send
their shafts of venom after us.  They think
themselves better provided with a pretext
than our former persecutors were, for they
have now a great HOBBY HORSE on which to 
ride called "polygamy," and one after an-
other of their immaculate politicians ride
to Congress with a bill to be passed against
the Mormons.  But strange to say ever
one who has gone with this HOBBY HORSE
gets worsted and are politically killed.  This 
is in fulfilment of a portion of Scripture.
The Lord says in speaking of the enemies
of His people that He will "put a hook in 
their jaws."
They do not realize the hand of God but 
walk in darkness, the blind leading the 
blind, and corruption and bribery prevail in 
high places, seeking to deprive people of 
the rights guaranteed by our beloved Con-
stitution.
Oh! how my soul is pained when I see
the rights and privileges of citizenship
trampled upon, and changed to suit the ca-
price of men who would sell themselves
and the liberties of their fellow-beings for 
money and for office.  Stooping to become
partizans regardless of the oaths they have
taken pledging themselves before high
heaven and all men to maintain inviolate
the Constitution and its relations to the 
rights of the people, whose cause they re-
resent.
In their zeal to do away with the system
of polygamy they seem confused in their 
plans some proposing one plan and some
another.  I would say to you gentlemen
this is because you do not understand what 
you are dealing with; hundreds and thou-
sands of your predecessors have been in the 
same sad dilemma; because as phyicians are
sometimes puzzled so are you to understand
the nature of the object you are contend-
ing against.  Grant, in his version of it, calls
it a twin relic of barbarism, or a loathsome
ulcer and like a quack Doctor, has been ex-
perimenting during his presidential career,
but has neither killed or checked its growth.
To be continued.


SILK CULTURE.

	As the time is fast approaching to begin
raising the silk worm, I am daily interroga-
ted, "How shall we proceed in silk raising?"
For the benefit of those wishing to engage
in the business, I will make a few remarks
in regard to it, founded on my own experi-
ence,which has heretofore proved success-
ful.
	In the first place we must be alive to our
business. Now the first thing to be done is
to secure silk worm eggs before the weath-
er gets too warm, to avoid them hatching,
and before the leaves are far enough ad-
vanced to begin feeding. In order to avoid
their hatching, place them in a cool dry
place or cellar, if not damp, in a box of any
kind, to preserve them from the mice,
with a goodly portion of gimlet holes bored
in the ends and sides to admit of the circu-
lation of air, there to remain until wanted
to hatch. From the first to the 15th of June
bring the eggs from your cool repository,
and spread them upon a newspaper upon a
table. Leave them in a cool room. It is
best not to expose them too suddenly to
warmth that would hatch them. Leave
them in a cool room a day or two, then take
them to a warmer room, and spread on a
table as before. In a few days they will
begin to hatch. They commence early in
the morning and continue hatching until
near noon.
	Now your labor commences. As soon as
they hatch, they start off in search of food.
Pluck your tenderest leaves, about the size
of a quarter of a dollar, leaving the tip end
and one small leaf. Cut the leaves in halves,
lay them on the worms, where they are the
thickest. When they are all on the leaf, if
it is not full of worms, place it in another
spot, and so on until the leaves are full.
Have another paper near the one you have
your eggs upon. Then take a pen-knife,
and take hold of the edge of the leaf full of
little worms, placing them on the paper
you previously laid on the table, taking
them up one by one as they fill up, lay them
side by side, until all that day's hatching
are taken care of. Now gather some ten-
der leaves, laying one on the top of anoth-
er, say from five to one dozen, according to
the number of worms. Lay them on a
board and cut them with a sharp knife,
about an eighth of an inch wide, and cross-
ways the same. Then sprinkle them light-
ly over the young worms; be careful not to
put on too many, as the worms will attack
the first leaf they come in contact with.
If there is more than enough for them the
top leaves will wilt, and dry up before the
worms can get to the top. Repeat this op-
eration every two hours. If the worms do
not come up it is a sure sign that they
have had too much feed, and are unable to
get up. Then you must feed lighter. Ov-
er-feeding is a great fault in the first stage
and must be avoided. Continue in this
way each day during hatching, taking care
to keep each day's hatching by themselves.
	Any person wishing silk-worm eggs can
be accommodated by addressing the under-
signed.
	MRS. ANN K. DUNYON,
		Draperville, Salt Lake Co.
—Deseret News, May 1st, 1876.
